Document 1 of 500

It doesn't matter how this Toronto byelection shakes out - every scenario is some kind of bad for Justin Trudeau's Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doesnt-matter-how-this-toronto-byelection-shakes/docview/3071280912/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+doesn%27t+matter+how+this+Toronto+byelection+shakes+out+-+every+scenario+is+some+kind+of+bad+for+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Matt Gurney: There's a byelection in the riding of Toronto-St. Paul's on Monday - typically a pretty safe red seat. I'll start with a couple of confessions. First, I normally hate talking about byelections. I think they're usually stories we just use to fill airtime and column inches and don't really mean much. You can draw conclusions from 338 simultaneous elections - more, now, I guess - but not much from one at a time. But I think this one matters because there's no good-news scenario for the Liberals here. They either lose a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad, or they barely hold a seat they shouldn't ever lose, and that's bad. Or they win it, and everyone is shocked and relieved, and that's a kind of bad, too. So I'd welcome your thoughts on that. But also, confession two. I'm taking time off next week, and I'm not going to be around to say much about whatever happens. I'll just read your reaction.
Susan Delacourt: I spent some time in Toronto-St. Paul's earlier this month and my sense was that it was looking pretty bad for the Liberals. Lawn signs may not be an accurate predictor, but on the streets where I was roaming, the blue ones far outnumbered the red ones. There is no sugar-coating that. I talked to someone who had been knocking on doors, who reported many people saying that Justin Trudeau had to go. My colleague Tonda MacCharles did her own reconnaissance and aptly painted that bleak picture in a story last weekend. One thing that surprised me in that story was the Conservative strategist who said they still expected to lose Toronto-St. Paul's. That sounded like spin to me, laying the groundwork for Conservatives to claim in victory that Pierre Poilievre had somehow defied the odds. Last time I checked, the odds were massively in favour of a Conservative win. They're 20 per cent ahead in the latest Abacus poll, for heaven's sake. I'm not buying the suggestion that they're the underdogs in this byelection.
MG: That's interesting. I honestly don't have a prediction. I read the polls so, yeah, I get it. But I always assume that in Canadian politics, the least interesting thing is usually the most likely thing. And the least interesting thing, I think, would be the Liberals hanging on by any margin. "Red seat stays red, film at 11." But I have friends in that riding and they agree entirely with your view and Tonda's. As for Conservatives I've talked to, I think caution has been morphing gradually into an expectation that yeah, they'll win. And that in turns leads to a realization they're going to have to defend the damn riding in a year or so.
SD: I am not making any predictions either. It's perhaps a reflection of how long I've been in this crazy business, but I don't see Toronto-St. Paul's as a Liberal stronghold. It was a Carolyn Bennett stronghold; she owned that constituency from the moment she decided to give up her job as a popular family physician and jump into politics in 1997. But I also remember it as Barbara McDougall's riding in the 1980s, a solid blue Progressive Conservative bastion in Brian Mulroney's era. I'm intrigued by the caution you heard from Conservatives. It tells me Poilievre's team is far more attached to the idea of playing offence than defence in the next year.
MG: There's a whole crop of homeowning (if they're wealthy!), job-holding, kid-having voters who have only seen a Liberal hold that riding, and it's in a pretty red part of the city. I understand it's a riding that can go blue, but it only really goes blue if the blue team is basically coming as close to running the board as it can reasonably ever hope. And like I said above, every scenario is bad for the Liberals. I don't know how the summer is going to go at party headquarters, but it might be awkward at times.
SD: When I interviewed Trudeau earlier this year, I asked him who he consults in tough times. He said, "Define 'tough times,' because that sounds like 'Monday' to me." That will be this Monday, for sure. Actually, though, I've been thinking about another quote, from Bill Clinton, who famously said that Democrats need to fall in love, while Republicans need to fall in line. When I spoke to some people in Toronto-St. Paul's, they said there's no Poilievre-mania out there - the kind of enthusiasm one might expect for a leader with those soaring poll numbers. But maybe Conservatives don't need voters to fall in love, they just need them to fall in line. And that does mean Toronto-St. Paul's is a test for them, too.
MG: Two summers ago, I observed a palpable shift in attitudes toward the Liberals in my own Toronto riding, which is just a few minutes from Toronto-St. Paul's. I know the polling didn't begin to move until about a year after that, but I felt something happening sooner. I'd be watching my daughter play soccer and some other parent would randomly confide in me, "I usually vote Liberal but I'm done with Trudeau." And I'd just sort of smile and nod and try to return my attention to the game, but that kept happening. I know that anecdotes aren't data, but after like the fifth time this happened, I began to realize that something was afoot. And maybe a perfect way of putting it is that people the Liberals count on, especially here in the Centre of the Universe, fell out of love. And then about a year later, they started falling in line. And the polls spotted that.
SD: That's the on-the-ground intelligence I don't have. Interesting. I know you're off next week, but I may try to lure you into a private chat so we can dissect what it all means when the dust settles on Monday night.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a rally in Montreal on June 19, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Voters

People: Gurney, Matt; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Alberta NDP party set to choose new leader to replace Rachel Notley

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alberta-ndp-party-set-choose-new-leader-replace/docview/3071320958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+NDP+party+set+to+choose+new+leader+to+replace+Rachel+Notley&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alberta NDP will officially name its new leader Saturday afternoon, concluding a closely-watched competition to replace outgoing leader Rachel Notley.
There are four contenders in the running. Former health minister Sarah Hoffman, former justice minister Kathleen Ganley and Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse are all sitting MLAs, while former Calgary mayor Naheed Nenshi is a high profile party outsider.
This is the provincial party's first leadership competition since it's metamorphosis from a handful of MLAs to a political force elected to government in 2015, ending the Progressive Conservative's four-decade run. The hotly contested race has spurred debate about the nature of progressive politics on the prairies to whether or not to cut ties with the federal party, as well as to how best take on Premier Danielle Smith.
The race also boosted the membership in the party to swell past 85,000 members, making it one of the largest provincial political bodies in the country.
The leader means the party will say goodbye to Notley as leader. The daughter of Grant Notley, another NDP leader, she led the party to its historic win, and while in office, raised the province's minimum wage, cut child poverty, boosted labour standards and introduced more accessible child-care options.
But after two consecutive losses to the United Conservatives, she announced in January that she would step down.
In her last speech as leader on Friday, Notley encouraged cheering delegates to be ready to rally around whoever replaces her and to fight to win again in 2027.
"So many of us made immense sacrifices to do this work, and today we live in an Alberta that is better for it," Notley said in her final address as leader at Calgary's BMO Centre.
"We can say together: it was worth it."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Alberta NDP leadership candidate Naheed Nenshi, centre, makes an closing statement as fellow candidates, left to right, Gil McGowan, Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse, Sarah Hoffman, and Kathleen Ganley look on during a leadership debate in Calgary, Alta., Saturday, May 11, 2024. Results of the leadership race are to be announced Jun. 22.

Document 3 of 500

The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/science-centre-is-latest-casualty-doug-fords/docview/3071083709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Science+Centre+is+the+latest+casualty+of+Doug+Ford%27s+policy+of+neglect&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A failing common to successive Ontario governments in recent decades is the unconscionable neglect that has seen provincial jewels fall into decay.
Both Ontario Place and the Ontario Science Centre - the latter closed for good Friday after a report that said its roof was in danger of collapsing - were, in their heydays, popular venues that celebrated energy and adventure, knowledge and possibility.
That they would have to be renovated and updated over time was inevitable.
That they should have been, in the case of Ontario Place, shuttered and left to rot, and in the case of the Science Centre, poorly maintained and maligned, shames those who have led the province.
It's galling to know that governments in Ontario failed to make the necessary investments to maintain and renew prized provincial assets.
Premier Doug Ford, in particular, did nothing to reverse this decay. As the Star reported, Ontario's auditor general said in December that maintenance projects on the Science Centre were deferred and repeatedly denied funding, including 42 such projects since 2017, the year before the Ford government took power.
Presumably, Ford shed few tears at the closure, using it to justify a planned transfer of the Science Centre to Ontario Place as part of a major redevelopment on the waterfront that will include a massive, privately owned Therme spa.
Ford's proposed move has been controversial, with allegations of arbitrary decision-making that denied public consultation.
Local residents have also said the closure of the Science Centre - designed by the late Raymond Moriyama and opened in 1969 at Don Mills and Eglinton avenues - marks the loss of one of the few attractions in the area.
The latest engineering report said the concrete used in construction of the Science Centre was nearing the end of its lifespan. To upgrade the roof would cost from $22 million to $40 million and take about two years or more, it said.
So Ford let the Science Centre decay and then, when it became dangerous, immediately shut down a facility he already wanted closed.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma promptly touted the "eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility" on the waterfront.
An earlier report by Ernst & Young said the Science Centre was "in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical maintenance costs."
That report said it would cost $600 million more to repair and upgrade the existing Science Centre than to build a new one. It should never have gotten to that point.
After six years of government by Ford and his team, with continual impulsive decisions, pet projects and a cadre of too-close-by-half insiders, it's hard not to see the case of the Science Centre as emblematic of Ford's approach to infrastructure: allowing decay of prized public places and taking the opportunity his neglect created to justify new for-profit developments he wanted all along.
But much is lost along the way, as today's sad news again shows.
"For more than five decades, the Ontario Science Centre has been a beloved landmark and an integral part of our community and our province," said the centre's CEO Paul Kortenaar.
"Our building itself has been part of the experience and a cherished space for generations of visitors, sparking wonder and curiosity about science and the world around us."
But for Doug Ford, the Science Centre appears merely to be someplace that we used to love.
If ever there was an example of knowing the price of everything and the value of nothing, Ontario's mishandling of these assets may be it.
CAPTION: Workers erect gates and fences around the Ontario Science Centre on Friday after the province abruptly announced its closure.

Subject: Science

Title: The Science Centre is the latest casualty of Doug Ford's policy of neglect

Document 4 of 500

Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh are their own worst enemies. Here's how they can use the summer to turn things around

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-pierre-poilievre-jagmeet-singh-are/docview/3071083543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Jagmeet+Singh+are+their+own+worst+enemies.+Here%27s+how+they+can+use+the+summer+to+turn+things+around&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As MPs scurried out of Ottawa's oppressive heat this week for what is likely the last summer break before the next federal election - the fixed election is set for October 2025, but it's difficult to see NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh supporting the Liberals' next budget - many were looking forward to hitting the barbecue circuit and taking time to recharge. But for the parties, the reprieve from the daily to and fro on Parliament Hill is a crucial time for a reset. And they badly need it.
The Liberals
What happens to the Grits this summer may be determined by what happens Monday evening in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Liberal MPs will be hitting the refresh button on Elections Canada's website for results, but not everyone will be wishing for a big win. Some MPs, who've been too scared to tell Prime Minister Justin Trudeau what they're really hearing at the doorstep, hope a defeat or near defeat gives Trudeau pause about continuing, and encourages other Liberals to call for new leadership.
But reports from the field suggest the Conservatives aren't pulling out their vote. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre may not want to give the Grits a reason to dump their leader.
Toronto-St. Paul's is a Liberal stronghold and should never have been in question. But Trudeau's unpopularity, a desire for change, and a significant Jewish population in the riding dissatisfied by the Liberals' approach to the war in Gaza has put the riding in play. Expectations were lowered to such a point that any win may be spun by those around Trudeau as a decisive victory.
The prime minister is lucky no leadership candidate is actively working to shove him out the door. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney waits patiently in the wings. He's had conversations with Trudeau about joining the Liberal team, and his arrival would offer the Grits a jolt of energy. Many Liberals are hungry for a realignment towards the centre, and believe Carney can bring them there.
If Carney is serious about a run, he should announce his plans before the dozens of Liberal MPs who are contemplating retirement make decisions they'll regret.
Whether Carney joins Trudeau or waits to pick up the pieces after a Liberal defeat, the Liberals' biggest problem is that they've lost the drive that brought them to power in 2015. When CBC journalist David Cochrane asked the prime minister this week why he wanted a fourth term, Trudeau's response was, "Oh, it's continuing to meet the moment for Canadians." That's not a pitch. Who and what are the Liberals fighting for? They have the summer to find the answer.
The New Democrats
If Singh wants to be prime minister, the NDP leader needs to shift its strategy.
The party's reasoning for signing a supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals in 2022 was to demonstrate to progressive voters that a vote for the NDP wasn't a wasted vote, and that one did not need to vote Liberal to get big things done. With 25 (now 24) MPs, it has delivered on that goal. Dental care and free contraceptives and free diabetes medication - if they get rolled out before the next election - will have been accomplished thanks to the NDP.
But this pitch doesn't work when facing the prospect of a majority Conservative government.
Singh's challenge is twofold. First, he must create space between himself and Trudeau. The Conservatives have brilliantly branded the Trudeau government as the "NDP-Liberal government," cementing in the mind of voters the idea that the NDP is not a change option. Singh must wrestle the mantle back from the Conservatives.
His second challenge is one to which the party seems to have given little thought. If the NDP wants to capture the "anyone but Pierre Poilievre" vote from the Liberals, Singh must be seen to be prime ministerial. That's not an easy task for a leader who has never run anything, who has been abandoned by a quarter of his caucus, and who has only one MP with cabinet experience. Singh must build a team and show a maturity that convinces voters he is ready to govern.
The Conservatives
With a 20-point lead in public opinion polls, the Conservatives are poised to win a majority at a scale not seen since Brian Mulroney's 1984 government .
There is a palpable appetite for change. Abacus Data reports more than 80 per cent of survey respondents want to see the Liberals replaced. Over the past year, the "change" vote has coalesced around the Conservatives.
The only person standing in the way of the party's success is its leader. Poilievre is a bright, partisan and talented communicator, but he can also be very polarizing.
This week, in an interview with CTV, for example, Poilievre repeatedly insulted Montreal Mayor Valerie Plante, calling her "incompetent" and "radical." Poilievre may disagree with Plante's policies and think her too left-wing, but Montrealers still elected her - twice. Should the future prime minister be publicly insulting the mayor of Canada's second-largest city? Poilievre sought to define himself last summer before the Liberals framed him up, but he is now hurting his own rebranding efforts. Is he the leader shown in Conservative advertising who talks about building a country together? Or is he a leader who prioritizes waging battles against ideological opponents and further sowing division in the country?
There are 12 weeks left until MPs are back in Ottawa. That's plenty of time for the parties and the leaders to reset - if they choose to.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an announcement at Northumberland High School in Alma, N.S. on June 20, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; State elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Political advertising

Dentists reject idea of Tory meddling: Liberals claim that Conservatives are fuelling opposition to new care plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dentists-reject-idea-tory-meddling/docview/3070998654/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dentists+reject+idea+of+Tory+meddling%3A+Liberals+claim+that+Conservatives+are+fuelling+opposition+to+new+care+plan&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The heads of several dental groups are rejecting a Liberal minister's framing that the federal Conservatives are to blame for oral health providers' opposition to aspects of Ottawa's dental care plan.
The dismissal of Health Minister Mark Holland's comments, which were made earlier this week, are the latest source of friction between Canada's dental associations and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government over the rollout of what's been deemed a "historic" dental coverage program.
Several dental association presidents told the Star that the Conservative party has nothing to do with their outstanding questions over implementing the plan, while Liberal and NDP messaging around the program do.
"There has been zero pressure, really, from the Conservatives on us in any kind of fashion that would result in the low uptake of dentists registering for the program," said Dr. Robert Wolanski, president of the British Columbia Dental Association.
"The misinformation that has been provided has been provided almost solely by ... the Liberal government and the NDP," Wolanski said, of a program that was born out of the minority Liberals' parliamentary alliance with the New Democrats.
During a news conference on Tuesday, Holland suggested that the country's dental associations were "actively seeking" to oppose the plan because the Conservative party was against its success and was "calling these dental associations and screaming at them and getting angry at them."
"If you're a conservative party, (and) you want to run against taking dental care away, fine. But calling around and bullying and creating all of this negativity, you know, I think that's what the underlying force is," Holland said.
Both the Canadian Dental Association (CDA) and the Conservative party have denied the minister's claims, which Holland says are rooted in fears that Pierre Poilievre would be at a political disadvantage should the program be viewed as a success.
Wolanski said provincial and territorial dental associations have had "very little" contact with the federal Conservatives over the past two years, and that their health critic, Stephen Ellis, only reached out to them in April to have conversations Wolanski characterized as "inquisitive, open and respectful."
The meetings mostly saw Ellis ask questions about how the program works and dentists' concerns, Wolanski said.
While dental groups support the goals of the program - which is open to seniors and will expand to include eligible adults with disabilities and children under the age of 18 on June 27 - they say they still have questions about language in the plan's agreements, patient privacy and the claims process, among other concerns.
They've also taken issue with messaging that suggests the program is entirely free and offered to any Canadian for any service at no cost.
Holland has said he's never misguided Canadians about how the program actually works, and told the Star earlier this month that after many talks with dental groups and attempts to rectify their concerns, he no longer understands what their issues are.
The minister said Tuesday that more than 200,000 seniors have accessed care under the plan and that "a little over 40 per cent" of oral health providers have signed up to deliver the program.
But it is the politicization of the issue, Wolanski said, that's a new cause for concern.
"The politics, to be honest, have been frustrating for us because that's not how ... our minds work. We're about patients, not politics," he said.
"There were claims of bullying and things like that. I've spoken to every association president across the country, and none of us have experienced that."
Dr. Jenny Doerksen, head of the Alberta Dental Association, said Holland is simply engaging in "campaign tactics."
"We are not here to be part of this political game that they're playing," she said.
The new president of the Ontario Dental Association, Dr. Maneesh Jain, told the Star that dental groups meet with parties of all political stripes. He said Holland's comments took him "by surprise" given the positive conversations he's had with the minister's office.
CREDIT: Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Subject: Conservatism; Oral hygiene; Dental care; Associations; Dentists; Political parties

Document 6 of 500

Looking for summer festival fun not far from the GTA? Take these music road trips

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/looking-summer-festival-fun-not-far-gta-take/docview/3070998589/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Looking+for+summer+festival+fun+not+far+from+the+GTA%3F+Take+these+music+road+trips&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the third-biggest concert market in North America, Toronto has had trouble sustaining a weekend destination music festival, like Governors Ball in New York, Lollapalooza in Chicago, or Osheaga in Montreal.
It's not for lack of trying: from V Fest on Toronto Island to Field Trip at Fort York to Bestival in Woodbine Park, there have been many attempts over the past two decades to stage mega-fests in this city, but none have stuck around long enough to become annual fixtures that attract music fans from around the world.
Of course, Toronto is hardly lacking for multi-artist summer events, though these tend to take the form of club-hopping discovery experiences (Canadian Music Week, NXNE), neighbourhood gatherings (Beaches Jazz Fest), week-long citywide programming loosely affiliated by branding (Toronto Jazz Fest, Luminato) and selective genre-focused affairs (like the EDM weekender Veld).
For whatever reason, Torontonians seem largely resistant to the concept of hanging out with thousands of fellow music lovers all day in an open field, bouncing between stages to take in an eclectic array of sounds, and passing out on a blanket from sunstroke or too much beer. This either makes us the least fun or most sensible city on the continent.
But if that authentic outdoor summer-festival experience is what you crave, there are several options just outside the GTA to help fill that Coachella-sized void. And most of these events offer the opportunity to see popular artists in more intimate and unique environments than you might experience in Toronto, with less traffic hassle.
Born & Raised
Dates: July 5-6
Location: Montebello Park, St. Catharines
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $121 per day
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Born & Raised launched two years ago in the quaint inner-city environs of Montebello Park to celebrate St. Catharines' two biggest musical exports: screamo heroes Alexisonfire and folk-rock institution City and Colour, both of which feature Dallas Green on lead vocals and guitar. The two acts will once again headline separate days on bills that nicely contrast and complement one another: City and Colour perform on July 5 with Canadian indie-rock favourites Metric and Sloan, and Alexisonfire will turn the rose-lined park into a raging mosh pit on July 6 alongside fellow early-2000s survivors the Used and electro-punk phenom Ekkstacy, among others.
Mariposa Folk Festival
Dates: July 5-7
Location: J.B. Tudhope Memorial Park, Orillia
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $149-$189 for day passes, $249 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Mariposa has endured for over five decades by recognizing that folk music isn't merely an acoustic-based sound, but a mode of straight-from-the-heart storytelling that transcends genre. So while this year's lineup is hardly lacking for seasoned troubadours like Bruce Cockburn and William Prince, it also accommodates the rustic indie rock of Band of Horses and Okkervil River, Americana ensemble Old Crow Medicine Show, DIY prankster B.A. Johnston, and Canadian rap royalty like Maestro Fresh Wes and Shad.
Because Beer
Dates: July 12-14
Location: Pier 4 Park, Hamilton
Approximate drive time from Toronto: One hour
Ticket price: $45 for day passes (includes two beer tastings), $99 for weekend passes (includes six beer tastings)
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Over the past decade, Because Beer has functioned primarily as a craft-brew tasting event with a small ancillary music tent that has more often served as the background soundtrack to boozy revelry rather than a festival focal point. But this year, organizers are exploding the format like a shaken-up can of PBR. This newly expanded three-day version of Because Beer doubles as an all-star showcase for some of Ontario's premier punk and indie acts, including PUP, the soon-to-be-retired Tokyo Police Club, Daniel Romano's Outfit and Toronto's own Billy Talent - whom you'll be able to see here for way cheaper and in a much cozier and picturesque waterfront setting than the other Southern Ontario festival they're headlining this summer (more on that later).
Hillside Festival
Dates: July 19-21
Location: Guelph Lake Island, Guelph
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price: $75-$95 for day passes, $189-$209 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Since 1984, Hillside has served as the rare music festival that encourages attendees to pack bathing suits. Located on the shores of the Guelph Lake conservation area, Hillside is less a concert event than an off-the-grid retreat into an Edenic hippie haven, complete with a green-roofed main stage, vegan-food vendors and late-night drum circles. And that carefree vibe is reflected in the anything-goes programming: At no other festival will you find British folk-guitar master Richard Thompson alongside D.C. punk-jazz ensemble the Messthetics, West African supergroup Benin International Musical and synth-pop party-starter Rich Aucoin.
Riverfest Elora
Dates: August 16-18
Location: Bissell Park, Elora
Ticket price: $85-$95 for day passes, $155-$550 for weekend passes.
Approximate drive time from Toronto: One hour and 45 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes, at the nearby Elora Gorge
What to expect: Beginning as a backyard party back in 2009, Riverfest Elora has gradually grown into the sort of event that can lure A-list alt-rock acts up to the banks of the Grand River for exclusive appearances. This year's lineup is topped by busker-punk pioneers the Violent Femmes and indie-folk icons Fleet Foxes. Further down the bill are several other internationally acclaimed artists - like raucous rockers Bully, violin-wielding R&B queen Sudan Archives and Jurassic 5 alumni Chali 2na and Cut Chemist - you won't see at any other Southern Ontario venue this summer.
Boots and Hearts
Dates: August 8-11
Location: Burl's Creek, Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices: $89.99-$249.99 for day passes, $329.99-$669.99 for weekend passes
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Where Burl's Creek's genre-agnostic Wayhome Festival (2015-2017) failed to become our very own Bonnaroo North, Boots and Hearts has absolutely thrived at the same site since 2012 by being ruthlessly precise in hitting its target demographic: country music lovers. This year's edition once again corrals some of Nashville's biggest guns - including Jason Aldean, Thomas Rhett and Cody Johnson - but it also acknowledges that the sound of modern country owes more to Rob Thomas than Johnny Cash, which explains the appearance by the former's '90s alt-pop band, Matchbox Twenty.
All Your Friends Fest
Dates: August 23-24
Location: Burl's Creek, Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices: $119-$269 for day passes, $249-$475 for weekend passes
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect: Where flagship festivals like Coachella have experienced a dip in popularity south of the border, nostalgia-driven niche events - like the '80s-goth gathering Cruel World and the 2000s-indie celebration Just Like Heaven - have become hot tickets. In that spirit, the inaugural All Your Friends Fest will function as a high-school reunion for pop-punk-lovin' Millennials, with a lineup - Fall Out Boy, Jimmy Eat World, Dashboard Confessional, the All-American Rejects - that resembles the T-shirt wall at Hot Topic. (And if you miss Billy Talent at Hamilton's Because Beer, here's your other chance to see them in action this summer.)
Artpark
Dates: Summer concert season runs until September 7
Location: Lewiston, N.Y.
Ticket prices: Varies according to event
Approximate drive time from Toronto: Two hours and 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect: Artpark isn't a festival, but a rather pastoral, non-corporatized venue alternative (complete with interactive sound-sculpture garden!) to Budweiser Stage, located a stone's throw over the border in Lewiston. Its outdoor 10,000-capacity concert bowl - essentially a steep grassy hill - often hosts tours that don't make their way up into Canada: This summer's area exclusives include the Flaming Lips, Gin Blossoms, Stray Cats and Blues Traveler.
CAPTION: Sloan plays Born & Raised in St. Catharines on July 5.St. Catharines' Born & Raised festival hosts Metric on July 5.Maestro Fresh Wes will perform at the Mariposa Folk Festival on July 7.Toronto punks PUP will play Because Beer in Hamilton.Bully will rock Riverfest Elora in Bissell Park.Rob Thomas and Matchbox Twenty are coming to the country-themed Boots and Hearts festival at Burl's Creek.Wayne Coyne and his Flaming Lips headline Artpark on July 22.Riverfest Elora, seen here in 2019, will host Violent Femmes and Fleet Foxes this summer.
CREDIT: Stuart Berman Special to the Star

Document 7 of 500

Are 'no name' and 'Compliments' wines coming to your supermarket? Private label wines could arrive soon - and Ontario's wineries are worried

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/are-no-name-compliments-wines-coming-your/docview/3070998566/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+%27no+name%27+and+%27Compliments%27+wines+coming+to+your+supermarket%3F+Private+label+wines+could+arrive+soon+-+and+Ontario%27s+wineries+are+worried&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's Saturday night. The in-laws are coming for dinner.
As you stroll the supermarket aisles, pondering which wine to serve with the main course, a few bottles catch your eye. Should you buy that juicy Napa Cabernet Sauvignon? The Aussie shiraz with the kangaroo on the label? (They complained about that one last time.)
What about this new one, with a fancy, end-aisle display: President's Choice Select Merlot. Why not? It's gotten decent reviews and the price seems right.
That scenario could soon become reality: The Ontario government is in the middle of consultations with industry groups that could see supermarkets such as Loblaws, Metro, and Sobeys and big-box stores like Costco and Walmart selling private label wines as soon as 2026.
It might be revolutionary for Ontario consumers, but not everybody's happy. Ontario wineries and grape growers worry private label wine could hammer their sales, as retailers give their house brands a push. And it's a valid concern: In many European countries, private label wine sales account for well over a third of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for nearly three-quarters of the market. Even in wine powerhouse nations like France and Italy, private label accounts for a third of the market.
Will Ontario supermarkets jump at the chance to sell their own brands of wine? They're not saying yes just yet, at least not publicly: Requests for comment to Loblaw and Sobeys were directed to the Retail Council of Canada, while Metro declined to comment and neither Walmart nor Costco responded to requests for comment in time for the Star's deadline.
But they're not saying no, either.
"Private label could prove useful, but it will depend on the overall policy mix because there are many moving parts," said Michelle Wasylyshen, a spokesperson for the Retail Council of Canada, which includes the major supermarkets as members.
Making the situation even worse for Ontario wineries, supermarket house brands might not even be made from local grapes, according to Richard Linley, president of Ontario Craft Wineries.
"Do we want Ontario to be a captive market for a handful of large producers selling low-cost bulk wine from foreign countries? We would argue that we should be standing up for our own domestic industry," said Linley in an email.
Linley would like to see "guardrails" introduced by the provincial government that would help protect existing wineries. For example, the province could allow Ontario wineries to open up more off-site stores to showcase their own products.
And in fact, the province says guardrails - though it didn't define them - are under discussion now.
"The government will continue to consult with other market participants on the issue, including considering the appropriate guardrails if government introduces private label products no sooner than 2026," Emily Hogeveen, spokesperson for Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, told the Star.
If private label wine sales are allowed, Linley would like to make sure that only wines bearing the Vintner's Quality Alliance Ontario logo would be allowed, meaning they're made from grapes grown in Ontario and meet specific quality benchmarks.
"The government has been clear in saying that retail expansion will not come at the expense of small, local producers and we are counting on them to deliver on this promise," said Linley. "If private label is to be brought in at a future date, they should consider sourcing 100 per cent Ontario VQA first."
The head of the Grape Growers of Ontario, which supplies many Ontario wineries who don't have their own vineyards - or don't produce enough grapes on their own - said the potential move toward private label sales comes at a pivotal time for the industry.
With an extra 8,000 or so outlets able to sell alcohol across the province later this year, liberalized alcohol sales could be a huge boon to Ontario producers, said Debbie Zimmerman. But done wrong, it could be dangerous, she added.
"The province has given us this golden opportunity to shape Ontario products for the future," said Zimmerman. "If we don't get this right, and they don't get this right, then all this wonderful work that's been done will be wasted."
Even if they had to meet VQA Ontario standards, private label wines could still spell trouble for the Ontario wine industry, Zimmerman said: After all, churning out bulk wines for supermarket labels isn't exactly a high margin business.
"I think everybody's a little cautious about private label because they see what could potentially happen. ... It becomes a commodity. It doesn't become an important value that is Ontario," said Zimmerman.
A source familiar with international trade rules and federal government policy said requiring private label wines to be 100 per cent Ontario product would likely be challenged by foreign producers.
In other jurisdictions where private label sales are allowed, they command a big share of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for a staggering 72.8 per cent of the market. In France and Spain, private label wines make up roughly a third of the market. In Alberta, private label wines now account for 10 to 15 per cent of the province's total wine market, and make up anywhere from 30 to 40 per cent of wine sales at Costco, according to estimates from industry insiders. In B.C., the country's other big wine-producing province, private label wine sales aren't allowed, either in grocery stores or big box retailers.
Whether it's wine, cookies or breakfast cereal, there's a basic reason retailers push sales of their own house brands, said Deleo De Leonardis, a retail veteran who was the vice-president for the Sobeys private label business between 2012 and 2017.
"Better margin is a big part of it, obviously," said De Leonardis, who is now CEO of a company which runs authenticity tests on ingredients for food companies and retailers. "The profit margins you can't scoff at. They're usually manufactured at a lower cost, and all of the middle men are out. You're not spending money with a big marketing campaign. You're not spending the big bucks on marketing to launch a brand like Coca Cola."
De Leonardis said a retailer's gross margin on a typical national brand cereal or other packaged product is around 25 per cent. For private label products, that rises to 35, 40 per cent or even more, she said.
While a better profit margin is a big part of private label's appeal for retailers, so is having something that other stores don't, said De Leonardis.
"The big prize is differentiation. Because that product is unique to your store, you're building up a demand and value for your store brand. And it differentiates you from your competitor," said De Leonardis.
Sometimes, the differentiator is almost solely cost, as in the case of Charles Shaw wines sold at Trader Joe's in the U.S. Better known as Two Buck Chuck for its initial selling price, it's not exactly top shelf stuff, De Leonardis acknowledged.
"Was it the best alcohol? No. If you had a good palate, you maybe had to hold your nose as you drank it," said De Leonardis. But other private label wines are actually pretty good, De Leonardis said, pointing to Costco's house brand Kirkland Signature, which sells several different wines, including sought after wines such as Italy's prized Barolo.
With Ontario's minimum pricing for alcohol products, we're unlikely to ever see something like Two Buck Chuck here. But for some brands, the differentiator isn't just price, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson. It all depends, she said, on what strategy a retailer is pursuing for its other private label products, pointing to Costco's Kirkland private label products and PC products at Loblaws.
"Kirkland is different than President's Choice. People would expect Kirkland to be cheaper than they would PC. Because Presidents Choice has spent a lot of time building that brand," said Hutcheson, managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group.
And that overall brand strategy, said Hutcheson, could play a role in where the retailers source their wines. A private label whose strategy is focused on price would be more likely to source their wine wherever they can find it. A Canadian company might be more likely to seek out a VQA supplier, argued Hutcheson.
"If it's a Canadian brand like Loblaws I think it would make sense for them to do it with a Canadian vineyard, versus saying the PC Black Label is from the Loire."
As for whether private label wines are likely to sell well in Ontario if the government allows it, De Leonardis has little doubt.
"If private label wines are able to offer good quality at competitive prices ... I can't see why consumers wouldn't flock to it."
CAPTION: Debbie Zimmerman, head of the Grape Growers of Ontario, was cautiously optimistic about recent moves liberalizing the sale of alcohol. "The province has given us this golden opportunity to shape Ontario products for the future. If we don't get this right, and they don't get this right, then all this wonderful work that's been done will be wasted."The government is in consultation with industry groups that could see supermarkets such as Loblaws, Metro, and Sobeys and big-box stores like Costco and Walmart selling private label wines.

Document 8 of 500

Ontario considers revisions to data: Municipalities say undercounting homes by CMHC costs them millions in funding

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-considers-revisions-data/docview/3070998472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+considers+revisions+to+data%3A+Municipalities+say+undercounting+homes+by+CMHC+costs+them+millions+in+funding&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is considering revising its tallies of how many homes are built in cities and towns across the province, after some complained that undercounting has cost them millions in provincial funding.
As Premier Doug Ford's government attempts to get 1.5 million homes built by 2031, it has assigned annual housing targets to 50 municipalities and promised extra funding to those who exceed or get close to them.
To qualify for money under the Building Faster Fund, which can be spent on housing-enabling infrastructure, municipalities need to have hit at least 80 per cent of their target of housing starts as calculated by the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation.
But the Ontario's Big City Mayors group says there are discrepancies between the CMHC data and their own internal counts, and for four municipalities that were close to qualifying for funding it meant losing out on $23.3 million.
The town of Oakville has records of 2,701 housing starts in 2023, but the CMHC reported 1,752, which put the Greater Toronto Area municipality at 76 per cent of the province's target and it therefore narrowly missed qualifying for Building Faster Fund money.
"We have building inspectors who inspect every poured foundation," Oakville Mayor Rob Burton said.
"We keep records of those inspections: address, description, date, the whole deal. So CMHC has offered many stories over the last six months that have changed from time to time about how they do it and my submission is they're not doing it right when our documented evidence is there to contradict them."
The town of Ajax says CMHC missed counting 324 units in an apartment building. While the CMHC acknowledged the error and said it would include the units in the 2024 counts, it still means the town just missed out on qualifying for $4 million through the building fund, a spokesperson said.
The CMHC said in a statement its tracking involves site visits and it stands by the data.
"We work with all municipalities on an ongoing basis to produce the most accurate and objective data based on our methodology," the federal Crown corporation wrote. "All data is verified prior to monthly publication to ensure that no revisions or retroactive changes are needed afterwards."
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra's office said this week that the government is discussing with the CMHC how to improve data and is working with the Association of Municipalities of Ontario to evaluate the concerns and "determine if there is a need for revisions or remedies."
Calandra acknowledged at a legislative committee hearing earlier this month that at the same time the province was engaged in a dispute with the federal government over how it was counting affordable homes built in Ontario, he was hearing concerns from municipalities about the province's own tracking of their housing progress.
"We should be able to better track not only across municipalities, not only affordable housing, but as we've heard through some of the criticism of the BFF funding and how CMHC tracks shovels in the ground, I think we also have to do a better job of how do we accumulate that data from our municipal partners," he said.
Ontario has not yet met any of its annual targets toward its goal of 1.5 million homes, though it came very close last year after it started counting long-term-care beds.
Calandra has previously said he is considering counting student residences and retirement homes, and the big city mayors are asking him to confirm if that will happen, and if transitional housing will count.
The housing starts data discrepancies are just the latest concern from municipalities with the province's system for tracking and rewarding housing progress. They say housing starts shouldn't be the metric at all, no matter how the CMHC counts them.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Homes under construction in Milton in 2023. The Ontario's Big City Mayors group says there are discrepancies between the CMHC data on housing starts and their own internal counts.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 9 of 500

Much work to do on the BBQ circuit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/much-work-do-on-bbq-circuit/docview/3070997546/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Much+work+to+do+on+the+BBQ+circuit&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As MPs scurried out of Ottawa's oppressive heat this week for what is likely the last summer break before the next federal election - the fixed election is set for October 2025, but it's difficult to see NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh supporting the Liberals' next budget - many were looking forward to hitting the barbecue circuit and taking time to recharge. But for the parties, the reprieve from the daily to and fro on Parliament Hill is a crucial time for a reset. And they badly need it.
The Liberals
What happens to the Grits this summer may be determined by what happens Monday evening in the Toronto-St. Paul's byelection. Liberal MPs will be hitting the refresh button on Elections Canada's website for results, but not everyone will be wishing for a big win. Some MPs, who've been too scared to tell Prime Minister Justin Trudeau what they're really hearing at the doorstep, hope a defeat or near defeat gives Trudeau pause about continuing, and encourages other Liberals to call for new leadership.
But reports from the field suggest the Conservatives aren't pulling out their vote. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre may not want to give the Grits a reason to dump their leader.
Toronto-St. Paul's is a Liberal stronghold and should never have been in question. But Trudeau's unpopularity, a desire for change, and a significant Jewish population in the riding dissatisfied by the Liberals' approach to the war in Gaza has put the riding in play. Expectations were lowered to such a point that any win may be spun by those around Trudeau as a decisive victory.
The prime minister is lucky no leadership candidate is actively working to shove him out the door. Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney waits patiently in the wings. He's had conversations with Trudeau about joining the Liberal team, and his arrival would offer the Grits a jolt of energy. Many Liberals are hungry for a realignment toward the centre, and believe Carney can bring them there.
If Carney is serious about a run, he should announce his plans before the dozens of Liberal MPs who are contemplating retirement make decisions they'll regret.
Whether Carney joins Trudeau or waits to pick up the pieces after a Liberal defeat, the Liberals' biggest problem is that they've lost the drive that brought them to power in 2015. When CBC journalist David Cochrane asked the prime minister this week why he wanted a fourth term, Trudeau's response was, "Oh, it's continuing to meet the moment for Canadians." That's not a pitch. Who and what are the Liberals fighting for? They have the summer to find the answer.
The New Democrats
If Singh wants to be prime minister, the NDP leader needs to shift its strategy.
The party's reasoning for signing a supply and confidence agreement with the Liberals in 2022 was to demonstrate to progressive voters that a vote for the NDP wasn't a wasted vote, and that one did not need to vote Liberal to get big things done. With 25 (now 24) MPs, it has delivered on that goal. Dental care and free contraceptives and free diabetes medication - if they get rolled out before the next election - will have been accomplished thanks to the NDP.
But this pitch doesn't work when facing the prospect of a majority Conservative government.
Singh's challenge is twofold. First, he must create space between himself and Trudeau. The Conservatives have brilliantly branded the Trudeau government as the "NDP-Liberal government," cementing in the mind of voters the idea that the NDP is not a change option. Singh must wrestle the mantle back from the Conservatives.
His second challenge is one to which the party seems to have given little thought. If the NDP wants to capture the "anyone but Pierre Poilievre" vote from the Liberals, Singh must be seen to be prime ministerial. That's not an easy task for a leader who has never run anything, who has been abandoned by a quarter of his caucus, and who has only one MP with cabinet experience. Singh must build a team and show a maturity that convinces voters he is ready to govern.
The Conservatives
With a 20-point lead in public opinion polls, the Conservatives are poised to win a majority at a scale not seen since Brian Mulroney's 1984 government.
There is a palpable appetite for change. Abacus Data reports more than 80 per cent of survey respondents want to see the Liberals replaced. Over the past year, the "change" vote has coalesced around the Conservatives.
The only person standing in the way of the party's success is its leader. Poilievre is a bright, partisan and talented communicator, but he can also be very polarizing.
This week, in an interview with CTV, for example, Poilievre repeatedly insulted Montreal Mayor Valerie Plante, calling her "incompetent" and "radical."
Poilievre may disagree with Plante's policies and think her too left-wing, but Montrealers still elected her - twice. Should the future prime minister be publicly insulting the mayor of Canada's second-largest city?
Poilievre sought to define himself last summer before the Liberals framed him up, but he is now hurting his own rebranding efforts. Is he the leader shown in Conservative advertising who talks about building a country together? Or is he a leader who prioritizes waging battles against ideological opponents and further sowing division in the country?
There are 12 weeks left until MPs are back in Ottawa. That's plenty of time for the parties and the leaders to reset - if they choose to.
CAPTION: With the next election now in sight, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and other party leaders should each take the summer to consider what they plan to offer voters when they return to Ottawa in the fall, Althia Raj writes.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia Raj OPINION

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Election results; State elections; Voters; Prime ministers; Political advertising

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 10 of 500

Are private label wines coming to Ontario?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-private-label-wines-coming-ontario/docview/3070997361/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+private+label+wines+coming+to+Ontario%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's Saturday night. The in-laws are coming for dinner.
As you stroll the supermarket aisles, pondering which wine to serve with the main course, a few bottles catch your eye. Should you buy that juicy Napa Cabernet Sauvignon? The Aussie shiraz with the kangaroo on the label? (They complained about that one last time.)
But what about this new one, with a fancy, end-aisle display: President's Choice Select Merlot. Why not? It's gotten decent reviews and the price seems right.
That scenario could soon become reality: The Ontario government is in the middle of consultations with industry groups that could see supermarkets such as Loblaws, Metro and Sobeys and big-box stores like Costco and Walmart selling private label wines as soon as 2026.
It might be revolutionary for Ontario consumers, but not everybody's happy. Ontario wineries and grape growers worry private label wine could hammer their sales, as retailers give their house brands a push. And it's a valid concern: In many European countries, private label wine sales account for well over a third of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for nearly three-quarters of the market. Even in wine powerhouse nations like France and Italy, private label accounts for a third.
Will Ontario supermarkets jump at the chance to sell their own brands of wine? They're not saying yes just yet, at least not publicly: Requests for comment to Loblaw and Sobeys were directed to the Retail Council of Canada, while Metro declined to comment and neither Walmart nor Costco responded to requests for comment in time for the Star's deadline.
But they're not saying no, either.
"Private label could prove useful, but it will depend on the overall policy mix because there are many moving parts," said Michelle Wasylyshen, a spokesperson for the Retail Council of Canada, which includes the major supermarkets as members.
Making the situation even worse for Ontario wineries, supermarket house brands might not even be made from local grapes, according to Richard Linley, president of Ontario Craft Wineries.
"Do we want Ontario to be a captive market for a handful of large producers selling low-cost bulk wine from foreign countries? We would argue that we should be standing up for our own domestic industry," Linley said in an email.
Linley would like to see "guardrails" introduced by the provincial government that would help protect existing wineries. For example, the province could allow Ontario wineries to open up more off-site stores to showcase their own products.
And in fact, the province says guardrails - though it didn't define them - are under discussion now.
"The government will continue to consult with other market participants on the issue, including considering the appropriate guardrails if government introduces private label products no sooner than 2026," Emily Hogeveen, spokesperson for Ontario Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, told the Star.
If private label wine sales are allowed, Linley would like to make sure that only wines bearing the Vintner's Quality Alliance Ontario logo would be allowed, meaning they're made from grapes grown in Ontario and meet specific quality benchmarks.
"The government has been clear in saying that retail expansion will not come at the expense of small, local producers and we are counting on them to deliver on this promise," Linley said. "If private label is to be brought in at a future date, they should consider sourcing 100 per cent Ontario VQA first."
The head of the Grape Growers of Ontario, which supplies many Ontario wineries who don't have their own vineyards - or don't produce enough grapes on their own - said the potential move toward private label sales comes at a pivotal time for the industry.
With an extra 8,000 or so outlets able to sell alcohol across the province later this year, liberalized alcohol sales could be a huge boon to Ontario producers, said Debbie Zimmerman. But done wrong, it could be dangerous, she added.
"The province has given us this golden opportunity to shape Ontario products for the future," said Zimmerman. "If we don't get this right, and they don't get this right, then all this wonderful work that's been done will be wasted."
Even if they had to meet VQA Ontario standards, private label wines could still spell trouble for the Ontario wine industry, Zimmerman said: After all, churning out bulk wines for supermarket labels isn't exactly a high margin business.
"I think everybody's a little cautious about private label because they see what could potentially happen ... It becomes a commodity. It doesn't become an important value that is Ontario," said Zimmerman.
A source familiar with international trade rules and federal government policy said requiring private label wines to be 100 per cent Ontario product would likely be challenged by foreign producers.
In other jurisdictions where private label sales are allowed, they command a big share of the market. In Norway, private label wines account for a staggering 72.8 per cent of the market.
In Alberta, private label wines now account for 10 to 15 per cent of the province's total wine market, and make up anywhere from 30 to 40 per cent of wine sales at Costco, according to estimates from industry insiders. In B.C., the country's other big wine-producing province, private label wine sales aren't allowed, either in grocery stores or big box retailers.
Whether it's wine, cookies or breakfast cereal, there's a basic reason retailers push sales of their own house brands, said Deleo De Leonardis, a retail veteran who was the vice-president for the Sobeys private label business between 2012 and 2017.
"Better margin is a big part of it, obviously," said De Leonardis, who is now CEO of a company which runs authenticity tests on ingredients for food companies and retailers. "The profit margins you can't scoff at. They're usually manufactured at a lower cost, and all of the middle men are out. You're not spending money with a big marketing campaign. You're not spending the big bucks on marketing to launch a brand like Coca Cola."
De Leonardis said a retailer's gross margin on a typical national brand cereal or other packaged product is around 25 per cent. For private label products, that rises to 35, 40 per cent or even more, she said.
While a better profit margin is a big part of private label's appeal for retailers, so is having something that other stores don't, said De Leonardis.
"The big prize is differentiation. Because that product is unique to your store, you're building up a demand and value for your store brand. And it differentiates you from your competitor," said De Leonardis.
Sometimes, the differentiator is almost solely cost, as in the case of Charles Shaw wines sold at Trader Joe's in the U.S. Better known as Two Buck Chuck for its initial selling price, it's not exactly top shelf stuff, De Leonardis acknowledged.
"Was it the best alcohol? No. If you had a good palate, you maybe had to hold your nose as you drank it," said De Leonardis. But other private label wines are actually pretty good, De Leonardis said, pointing to Costco's house brand Kirkland Signature, which sells several different wines, including sought after wines such as Italy's prized Barolo.
With Ontario's minimum pricing for alcohol products, we're unlikely to ever see something like Two Buck Chuck here. But for some brands, the differentiator isn't just price anyway, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson. It all depends, she said, on what strategy a retailer is pursuing for its other private label products, pointing to Costco's Kirkland private label products, or PC products at Loblaws.
"Kirkland is different than President's Choice. People would expect Kirkland to be cheaper than they would PC. Because Presidents Choice has spent a lot of time building that brand," said Hutcheson, managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group.
And that overall brand strategy, said Hutcheson, could play a role in where the retailers source their wines. A private label whose strategy is focused on price would be more likely to source their wine wherever they can find it. A Canadian company might be more likely to seek out a VQA supplier, argued Hutcheson.
"If it's a Canadian brand like Loblaws I think it would make sense for them to do it with a Canadian vineyard, versus saying the PC Black Label is from the Loire."
As for whether private label wines are likely to sell well in Ontario if the government allows it, De Leonardis has little doubt.
"If private label wines are able to offer good quality at competitive prices ... I can't see why consumers wouldn't flock to it."
CAPTION: Ontario wineries and grape growers worry private label wine could hammer their sales, as retailers give their house brands a push.The Ontario government is in the middle of consultations with industry groups that could see the introduction of private label wines as soon as 2026.Wine Marketing Assoc. of Ontario
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star

Subject: Wines; Marketing; House brands; Profits; Supermarkets; Retail stores; Presidents; Alcohol; Liquor; Wineries & vineyards

Location: Italy; Norway

Music road trips

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/music-road-trips/docview/3070997287/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Music+road+trips&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For the third-biggest concert market in North America, Toronto has had trouble sustaining a weekend destination music festival, like Governors Ball in New York, Lollapalooza in Chicago, or Osheaga in Montreal.
It's not for lack of trying: from V Fest on Toronto Island to Field Trip at Fort York to Bestival in Woodbine Park, there have been many attempts over the past two decades to stage mega-fests in this city, but none have stuck around long enough to become annual fixtures that attract music fans from around the world.
Of course, Toronto is hardly lacking for multi-artist summer events, though these tend to take the form of club-hopping discovery experiences (Canadian Music Week, NXNE), neighbourhood gatherings (Beaches Jazz Fest), weeklong citywide programming loosely affiliated by branding (Toronto Jazz Fest, Luminato) and selective genre-focused affairs (like the EDM weekender Veld).
For whatever reason, Torontonians seem largely resistant to the concept of hanging out with thousands of fellow music lovers all day in an open field, bouncing between stages to take in an eclectic array of sounds, and passing out on a blanket from sunstroke or too much beer. This either makes us the least fun or most sensible city on the continent.
But if that authentic outdoor summer-festival experience is what you crave, there are several options just outside the GTA to help fill that Coachella-sized void. And most of these events offer the opportunity to see popular artists in more intimate and unique environments than you might experience in Toronto, with less traffic hassle.
Born & Raised
Dates July 5-6
Location Montebello Park,
St. Catharines
Approximate drive time
from Toronto Two hours
Ticket price $121 per day
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect Born & Raised launched two years ago in the quaint inner-city environs of Montebello Park to celebrate St. Catharines' two biggest musical exports: screamo heroes Alexisonfire and folk-rock institution City and Colour, both of which feature Dallas Green on lead vocals and guitar. The two acts will once again headline separate days on bills that nicely contrast and complement one another: City and Colour perform on July 5 with Canadian indie-rock favourites Metric and Sloan, and Alexisonfire will turn the rose-lined park into a raging mosh pit on July 6 alongside fellow early-2000s survivors the Used and electro-punk phenom Ekkstacy, among others.
Mariposa Folk Festival
Dates July 5-7
Location J.B. Tudhope Memorial
Park, Orillia
Approximate drive time
from Toronto Two hours
Ticket price $149-$189 for day
passes, $249 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect Mariposa has endured for over five decades by recognizing that folk music isn't merely an acoustic-based sound, but a mode of straight-from-the-heart storytelling that transcends genre. So while this year's lineup is hardly lacking for seasoned troubadours like Bruce Cockburn and William Prince, it also accommodates the rustic indie rock of Band of Horses and Okkervil River, Americana ensemble Old Crow Medicine Show, DIY prankster B.A. Johnston, and Canadian rap royalty like Maestro Fresh Wes and Shad.
Because Beer
Dates July 12-14
Location Pier 4 Park, Hamilton
Approximate drive time
from Toronto One hour
Ticket price $45 for day passes (includes two beer tastings), $99 for weekend passes (includes six beer tastings)
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect Over the past decade, Because Beer has functioned primarily as a craft-brew tasting event with a small ancillary music tent that has more often served as the background soundtrack to boozy revelry rather than a festival focal point. But this year, organizers are exploding the format like a shaken-up can of PBR. This newly expanded three-day version of Because Beer doubles as an all-star showcase for some of Ontario's premier punk and indie acts, including PUP, the soon-to-be-retired Tokyo Police Club, Daniel Romano's Outfit and Toronto's own Billy Talent - whom you'll be able to see here for way cheaper and in a much cosier and picturesque waterfront setting than the other southern Ontario festival they're headlining this summer (more on that later).
Hillside Festival
Dates: July 19-21
Location Guelph Lake Island,
Guelph
Approximate drive time
from Toronto: Two hours
Ticket price $75-$95 for day passes, $189-$209 for weekend passes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect Since 1984, Hillside has served as the rare music festival that encourages attendees to pack bathing suits. Located on the shores of the Guelph Lake conservation area, Hillside is less a concert event than an off-the-grid retreat into an Edenic hippie haven, complete with a green-roofed main stage, vegan-food vendors and late-night drum circles. And that carefree vibe is reflected in the anything-goes programming: At no other festival will you find British folk-guitar master Richard Thompson alongside D.C. punk-jazz ensemble the Messthetics, West African supergroup Benin International Musical and synth-pop party-starter Rich Aucoin.
Riverfest Elora
Dates Aug. 16-18
Location Bissell Park, Elora
Ticket price $85-$95 for day passes, $155-$550 for weekend passes.
Approximate drive time
from Toronto One hour
and 45 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes, at the
nearby Elora Gorge
What to expect Beginning as a backyard party back in 2009, Riverfest Elora has gradually grown into the sort of event that can lure A-list alt-rock acts up to the banks of the Grand River for exclusive appearances. This year's lineup is topped by busker-punk pioneers the Violent Femmes and indie-folk icons Fleet Foxes. Further down the bill are several other internationally acclaimed artists - like raucous rockers Bully, violin-wielding R&B queen Sudan Archives and Jurassic 5 alumni Chali 2na and Cut Chemist - you won't see at any other southern Ontario venue this summer.
Boots and Hearts
Dates Aug. 8-11
Location Burl's Creek, Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices $89.99-$249.99 for day passes, $329.99-$669.99 for weekend passes
Approximate drive time from
Toronto Two hours, 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect Where Burl's Creek's genre-agnostic Wayhome Festival (2015-2017) failed to become our very own Bonnaroo North, Boots and Hearts has absolutely thrived at the same site since 2012 by being ruthlessly precise in hitting its target demographic: country music lovers. This year's edition once again corrals some of Nashville's biggest guns - including Jason Aldean, Thomas Rhett and Cody Johnson - but it also acknowledges that the sound of modern country owes more to Rob Thomas than Johnny Cash, which explains the appearance by the former's '90s alt-pop band, Matchbox Twenty.
All Your Friends Fest
Dates Aug. 23-24
Location Burl's Creek,
Oro-Medonte
Ticket prices $119-$269 for day
passes, $249-$475 for weekend
passes
Approximate drive time from
Toronto Two hours, 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? Yes
What to expect Where flagship festivals like Coachella have experienced a dip in popularity south of the border, nostalgia-driven niche events - like the '80s-goth gathering Cruel World and the 2000s-indie celebration Just Like Heaven - have become hot tickets. In that spirit, the inaugural All Your Friends Fest will function as a high-school reunion for pop-punk-lovin' Millennials, with a lineup - Fall Out Boy, Jimmy Eat World, Dashboard Confessional, the All-American Rejects - that resembles the T-shirt wall at Hot Topic. (And if you miss Billy Talent at Hamilton's Because Beer, here's your other chance to see them in action this summer.)
Artpark
Dates Summer concert season
runs until Sept. 7
Location Lewiston, N.Y.
Ticket prices Varies according to event
Approximate drive time from
Toronto Two hours, 15 minutes
Can I camp out there? No
What to expect Artpark isn't a festival, but a rather pastoral, non-corporatized venue alternative (complete with interactive sound-sculpture garden!) to Budweiser Stage, located a stone's throw over the border in Lewiston. Its outdoor 10,000-capacity concert bowl - essentially a steep grassy hill - often hosts tours that don't make their way up into Canada: This summer's area exclusives include the Flaming Lips, Gin Blossoms, Stray Cats and Blues Traveler.
CREDIT: Stuart Berman Special to the Star

Title: Music road trips
