Document 12 of 500

Iconic attraction closes for good: Popular educational centre shuttered after engineers find the roof was in danger of collapsing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/iconic-attraction-closes-good/docview/3070997283/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Iconic+attraction+closes+for+good%3A+Popular+educational+centre+shuttered+after+engineers+find+the+roof+was+in+danger+of+collapsing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Science Centre has been abruptly closed for good after a new engineering report found the roof is in danger of collapsing.
In a stunning move Friday afternoon - first reported by the Star - the Ministry of Infrastructure announced the iconic 1969 Don Mills building designed by the late Raymond Moriyama will no longer be structurally sound within a few months.
That meant Friday was the last day of operations for the once-popular tourist attraction that successive Progressive Conservative and Liberal governments have allowed to deteriorate for years.
"The actions taken today will protect the health and safety of visitors and staff at the Ontario Science Centre while supporting its eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility," said Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
About 250 workers - including 200 members of the Ontario Public Service Employees' Union - will be affected but they are not immediately losing their jobs.
Closure of the facility comes against the backdrop of Premier Doug Ford's controversial decision to move the science museum to Ontario Place as part of the redevelopment of the lakeside park, which includes a privately owned Therme water spa.
Political opponents charged Ford wanted the 55-year-old building to become unusable so he could more easily relocate the science centre to the waterfront by 2028.
But the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report found the air-filled concrete used in its construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life.
While the report said the building isn't unsafe right now, the risk of rain or snow buildup could make it so sooner rather than later because it would corrode the reinforcing steel underneath.
To that end, it must be vacated by Oct. 31.
The lightweight aerated concrete used in the construction has "the potential to degrade due to water infiltration," the review said.
One roofing panel is in the "critical" category, another six per cent of the panels are "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" while the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
"A significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
Rimkus recommended all such roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two or more years.
"These estimates are incomplete and subject to change," the report warned, noting the diagnosis was made based on visual inspections of all three science centre buildings and did not involve any "intrusive investigation."
Ford's government, which has been heavily criticized over its changes to the science centre, moved quickly due to safety concerns for children, parents and staff.
"Any risk of collapse is too much risk," said a senior official, who was not authorized to speak publicly before the announcement.
The science centre's board made the difficult decision late Thursday.
About 2,000 children scheduled to attend day camps this summer at the science centre will have relocated activities at a nearby public school. Their parents will be fully refunded within 30 days and given free programming at the school.
Science centre members, who pay annual fees ranging from $175 to $1,000, will be reimbursed on a pro-rated basis.
A wedding scheduled there for Saturday has been moved to another provincially owned facility and the bride and groom refunded.
The building's condition is worse than the government thought as recently as last November.
An earlier report released by Infrastructure Ontario, in relation to the planned move of the science centre to Ontario Place, warned the ravine-straddling building "is not sustainable under status quo conditions and is in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical building maintenance costs."
That 62-page report by consulting firm Ernst & Young was dated March 8, 2023 but not revealed until November, when it was presented as a "business case" for the controversial move. The report said it would cost $600 million more to fix up the old science centre than to build a new one.
As the Star broke the news Friday, it was initially business as usual as the science centre, with the main parking lot about three-quarters full and the bus lot jammed with school buses bringing students for the last week of school trips.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) and Floyd Ruskin, co-chair of Save Ontario's Science Centre, held a news conference just outside the main entrance as workers put up fences to close off the parking lots.
Shamji questioned the reports calling for the science centre's abrupt closing, saying he had been speaking at conference inside earlier in the day.
"What we're seeing happening right now at the science centre is so emblematic of how Doug Ford treats public infrastructure," said the Liberal MPP.
"Whether it's the way he underfunds health care or education, he allows it to fall apart and dismantle it, and then uses it to justify transfers to support his wealthy donors and friends, and to advance for-profit private interests like private spa companies," he said.
Ruskin noted "there's been over 50,000 people that have signed a petition not only to keep the science centre here on Don Mills Road but also to renew it, restore it and reinvest in it, because this government has refused to fund the maintenance."
"We don't want a science centre (at Ontario Place) with 18 per cent less exhibition space and also no interactive or immersive exhibits," he said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "closing a world-class science and cultural institution is heartbreaking, especially when we know the government's real motive here is to justify Ford's mega-spa vanity project" at Ontario Place.
"The government could have invested in revitalizing the science centre, but instead it's using our public money to concoct a sham business case against this important community hub," said Stiles.
The Star broke the news to Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16 Don Valley East), who represents the ward and has for months been searching for ways to keep the science centre open at that location or, failing that, replace it with something beneficial for the community.
"I'm devastated by the news. I was literally talking to two folks from Flemingdon Park last night who said how important the science centre is to their children's learning," said Burnside.
"There was always a part of me that thought the reports of the structural issues were overstated and could be addressed for a lot less money. It looks like the board of the science centre has decided that's not the case," he said.
"A lot of parents in the area rely on day camps and after-school programs. It is a neighbourhood improvement area with a dearth of opportunities and facilities for the folks of Flemingdon. This was such a wonderful resource, which is why none of us wanted it to close," the councillor said.
"The problem now is that because it's closing immediately, any other plans to fill that gap aren't in place."
Ontario's auditor general said in December that "deferred maintenance projects that were at risk of critical failure have been repeatedly denied funding" at the science centre, including 42 such project since 2017, the year before the Ford government took power.
The auditor also noted attendance had peaked 10 years ago, at 920,000 annual visitors, and had fallen more than 12 per cent before the COVID-19 pandemic hit, in part because exhibits "were not always evaluated for their effectiveness."
With files from Raju Mudhar
and David Rider
CAPTION: Workers erect fencing around the Ontario Science Centre Friday after the province announced it was closing for safety reasons.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie AND Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau With files from Raju Mudhar and David Rider

Document 13 of 500

Scotland's strategy is saving lives: 2018 policy increased prices, led to a 15% reduction in alcohol-related deaths in just three years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scotlands-strategy-is-saving-lives/docview/3070997236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Scotland%27s+strategy+is+saving+lives%3A+2018+policy+increased+prices%2C+led+to+a+15%25+reduction+in+alcohol-related+deaths+in+just+three+years&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One frosty winter morning, Tom Bennett awoke on the cold ground with a bare foot frozen in the snow.
The night before, the 33-year-old man had been too drunk to tie his shoelaces and one of his boots had fallen off. He was homeless and he knew that alcohol was killing him. But he was too sick to care.
"It was just a horrible place to be," said Bennett from his family home near Glasgow.
"I lost multiple jobs, multiple relationships. I lost all my dignity, self-respect and self-esteem. I nearly died on many occasions."
Today, Bennett, 47, is working to help others emerge from the same kind of darkness at a charity that helps those in the throes of addiction to heal. His journey reflects both alcoholism's toll and Scotland's transformative efforts to curb tragedy.
As Ontario moves to make alcohol more affordable and accessible, Scotland's policies are advancing in the opposite direction.
Ontario officials say they are fulfilling a 2018 election promise to increase "choice and convenience for shoppers and support Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry."
But Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. While in Ontario, alcohol-related admissions have risen by a third and deaths by almost half, according to the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction.
While Scots officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
As in many countries, alcohol is central in Canada's social and economic life. Over three quarters of Canadians aged 15 and older report drinking alcohol, according to Statistics Canada. And booze sales contribute $26 billion annually to the national economy.
But new research is showing that drinking alcohol is more dangerous than previously thought. And alcohol's collective harms are vast because the number of people who drink is so high.
While Scottish officials say that hard-hitting measures are necessary to lessen tragic outcomes, health experts fear Ontario's approach makes tragic outcomes inevitable.
Scotland has an age-old drinking problem. For years, the country suffered more alcohol-related deaths - from conditions such as liver disease - than much of the rest of Europe. In 2003, Scots were dying from drink at a rate three times higher than the rest of Britons.
Bennett grew up deep in this tragic alcohol culture. His affluent family viewed alcohol - as many Scots did - as a fundamental part of Scottish life.
In Bennett's community, drinks flowed at the many festivals that hail ancient Celtic traditions. People drank to celebrate, to mourn, to relax and to unwind after work. Bennett enjoyed "wee nips" with his parents from the age of 10 and was buying liquor with friends from the age of 15.
"Alcohol was everywhere," Bennett said with emotion. "And cheap."
Some hard drinks cost less than soft drinks at the local store, he recalled.
"Why buy pop, when you could get 'off your face' for the same money on cheap booze?"
In those days, if you wanted to escape from the despair of drink, you were often on your own.
Bennett remembers a lonely night in jail, shaking violently in severe withdrawal, desperate for a change.
He was ignored. There was no doctor. He was released back to the frigid streets the following day.
"I was not somebody who was having two bottles of wine. ... I was going through seven or eight litres of wine a day ... and two or three bottles of spirits," he recalled as two of his three daughters chatted in the next room.
"I had nowhere to go. I didn't know what to do."
Six months later, Bennett found the remarkable strength to recover from his 15-year dependence with the support of his family and a residential program.
He says he feels lucky to be alive.
"I have known so many people who weren't so lucky .... and have gone on to die," he said.
Today fewer Scots are dying because the influences that led to Bennett's misery are being transformed by Scottish government policy.
Scotland has cut alcohol-related hospital admissions by 40 per cent and deaths by almost half. It won the inaugural European Award for Reducing Alcohol Harm in 2016 and has been recognized by the United Nations for its initiatives tackling alcohol's affordability, availability and attractiveness.
It did not happen easily.
When the Scottish government started its drive to reduce alcohol's downfalls, in the early 2000s, it looked to Canada for advice.
Among its consultants was Tim Stockwell, a professor of psychology at the University of Victoria and director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research. Stockwell and his team had studied British Columbia and Saskatchewan alcohol price increases between 2002 and 2009 and found that a 10 per cent rise in average minimum prices reduced alcohol-related hospital admissions by 18 per cent two years later.
Scottish officials asked Stockwell about Canadian alcohol pricing approaches.
"I told them that we do it terribly," he said in a video interview.
Canada's alcohol pricing policy has stemmed less from public health concerns and more from desires to protect local producers and increase government revenue, Stockwell said.
Even so, the evidence Stockwell had studied about the benefits of increased prices was convincing. So Stockwell helped develop the Scottish policy and assisted over eight years to put it into action. It took two years to pass the legislation through the Scottish Parliament and six years to fight legal challenges by the alcohol industry in Scottish, European and United Kingdom courts.
The key part of Scotland's landmark policy was aimed at reducing drinking by introducing minimum unit prices to make drinking more expensive. Raising minimum prices is the most effective way of reducing alcohol's harms, Stockwell and other experts say. High prices cut the consumption of heavy drinkers - who are often partial to dirt-cheap booze - and young people, who can only drink what's low-cost.
At the beginning of 2018, the price of a beer in Scotland was about $1.00, according to public health data - the target Premier Doug Ford promised in his 'buck-a-beer' election slogan that year.
But a month before Doug Ford won a majority in Ontario, Scotland enacted a minimum price of 50 pence (C$0.87) for a unit of pure alcohol (10 mL) that meant a minimum of about $1.75 Canadian for a can of beer, $8.75 for a bottle of wine and $24.50 for a bottle of spirits, according to Dr. Peter Rice, one of the founders of the Scottish Health Action on Alcohol Problems, a medical think tank that has campaigned for change since 2006.
The new policy increased the prices of almost half the liquor on sale and led to a 15 per cent reduction in alcohol-related deaths in just three years. Last April, the Scottish Parliament unanimously voted to renew the policy and approved an increase to 65 pence per unit ($1.14).
"The pandemic hit us hard," Rice said in a phone interview. But with minimum unit pricing "we have still been able to stem the tide."
Alongside its pricing strategy, Scotland reduced the legal limit of alcohol for drivers to 50 mg per 100 mL of blood from 80 mg - the level in Ontario. This change was bolstered by a forceful media campaign that increased public opposition to drinking and driving. "It's not one for the road," a campaign slogan ran. "It's none for the road."
Scotland was also the first country to introduce "alcohol brief interventions." This involves training medical professionals to have short, structured conversations with people who abuse alcohol about their habits and how to access help.
Scotland still tolerates wide alcohol availability in supermarkets and small stores, something that flies in the face of what experts say is second only to pricing on the list of most important ways to reduce alcohol harms: reducing access.
Alex Collop sees first-hand the effects of easy access on low-income families in his work for Circle, a charity that supports families affected by alcohol and drugs.
Alcohol addiction leads to lower incomes, housing insecurity, domestic abuse and violence, Collop said from Circle's cosy meeting room in the heart of Pilton, one of Edinburgh's least-privileged communities.
"It is easier to buy alcohol than healthy food here," Collop said, noting that the stores beside a nearby pub called the Ferry Boat include a gambling house, a pizza parlour and a liquor store.
In his role as an alcohol counselling manager, Bennett also advocates for making alcohol tougher to get. He found it treacherous to go shopping when he came out of a residential rehab and was trying to stay sober.
"I was going to the supermarket for essential domestic items like toilet paper or washing-up liquid. And on the other side of the aisle.... hundreds and hundreds of bottles of whiskey, vodka, gin and everything else under the sun," he said in a video posted online.
Although alcohol is readily available in Scotland, high minimum unit prices prevent retailers from competing for customers with bargains or multi-buy promotions, something that experts worry will transpire in Ontario.
Timothy Naimi, a professor at the University of Victoria and the current director of the Canadian Institute for Substance Use Research, gives Ontario a failing grade on its plan to make alcohol more available.
"Ontario was already doing poorly" in not protecting the public from alcohol harms, Naimi said. But the government's planned tripling of retail outlets will "decimate its performance."
British Columbia research, led by Stockwell, showed that every 20 per cent increase in the number of private retail stores was associated with a three per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This is mainly because increased retail competition drives prices down, Stockwell said. He added that large chains that can buy cheaply in bulk also often use alcohol as a loss leader to bring in customers who will then pay more for food and other higher-margin items.
"It's like a double public health whammy - cheap alcohol and expensive fruit and veg."
A recent study in Finland estimated that allowing alcohol sales in grocery stores, without minimum prices, would lead to a 22 per cent increase in hospital admissions and a nine per cent increase in alcohol-related deaths.
This could play out similarly in Ontario, where current minimum legal price limits are two and half times lower than levels that would curb consumption, according to University of Victoria research.
"It might not sound like a big step," Rice said, "but our experience is that moving alcohol into the grocery market will have many repercussions."
Scottish and Canadian experts questioned Ontario's decision to expand alcohol convenience especially while its hospitals and emergency rooms are unable to meet current demand. Ontario already has 320,000 alcohol-related hospital visits annually - a number bound to rise vastly with more retail access, according to the Canadian Alcohol Policy Evaluation project.
Leslie Buckley, chief of the addictions division at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, told the Star that she hopes Ontario will introduce alcohol brief interventions, like those in Scotland.
Ontario's direction "is also a sign that we need more health promotion around all substances, talking about the harms and making it clearer for people," Buckley said.
And as Ontario pursues its current path, Scotland's success mitigating tragedies is being recognized even in some of its oldest institutions of drink.
On a drizzly Tuesday afternoon, the Ferry Boat pub in Pilton was bustling. Four old-timers clutched pints around a wobbly table as they hooted with laughter. They said they have been meeting at the Ferry Boat almost every afternoon since the 1960s.
"We used to cut school to drink when we were nine years old," said Tommy, a retired fisherman.
"It's the working man's pastime," said David, a retired chef. "We would come in here after a shift and stay all night. Sometimes I slept here."
Even so, the regulars are not surprised to hear that fewer of their countrymen are dying of drink.
"It is too expensive now," said Alistair, 68, a retired painter. "Now I can only afford eight beers a night and I usually make it home."
CAPTION: In 2018 Scotland became the first country to introduce minimum unit pricing for drinks. Ontario Premier Doug Ford pretends to drink from a beer can at a May announcement saying the province is speeding up the expansion of alcohol sales.Jane Barlow PA via the Associated PressChristopher Katsarov The Canadian press file photo
CREDIT: Katharine Lake Berz Special to the Star

Document 14 of 500

'We all need to be screaming': TTC board sounds alarm on aging Line 2 trains

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-all-need-be-screaming-ttc-board-sounds-alarm/docview/3070785577/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+all+need+to+be+screaming%27%3A+TTC+board+sounds+alarm+on+aging+Line+2+trains&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TTC riders could be stuck with declining service and a huge repair bill unless the federal government comes through soon with money to replace Toronto's aging Line 2 subway trains, according to the transit agency.
At a meeting of the TTC board Thursday, agency staff warned that the fleet serving its main east-west route is about 18 months from the end of its service life and is in urgent need of replacement.
After hearing the presentation, the board voted to draft a media campaign that would ask riders to pressure their MPs to have Ottawa match the city's and province's contributions for a new fleet. The two lower levels of government have each pledged $758 million toward the $2.3 billion purchase of 55 trains.
"At this point we all need to be screaming," said Coun. Dianne Saxe (Ward 11, University-Rosedale), who moved the motion.
"This is something where we need everyone who cares about the TTC and everyone who needs the TTC to pester your MP," said Saxe, noting that Toronto's more-than 20 federal Liberal representatives play a critical role in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government.
"We desperately need the money," Saxe said. "We have to have the cars."
TTC CEO Rick Leary said the Scarborough RT is a cautionary tale about what happens when vehicles are operated past their intended life. Even before a July 2023 derailment forced the the TTC to decommission that line, it was notorious for breakdowns and unreliable service.
"We don't want to have that type of (situation) to happen to Line 2," said Leary, speaking to reporters shortly before his shock resignation Thursday. He said he was hopeful Ottawa will commit the funding by the end of the year.
Line 2 is an essential part of the TTC subway network, and is responsible for more than 400,000 daily boardings. Staff told the board that the line's trains will start reaching the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.
Premier Doug Ford agreed to provide provincial funding of nearly $760 million for the replacement fleet as part of the "new deal" reached with Mayor Olivia Chow last November, but made it conditional on Trudeau's government matching that amount.
The province has its own interest in the purchase. Metrolinx, Ontario's transit agency, is hoping to piggyback on the TTC's order to buy 15 new trains for the Scarborough and Yonge North subway extensions.
TTC staff told the board the agency needs to secure the federal portion by March 2025 at the latest so that it can place the order in time to have new trains arrive by 2030. But despite "productive discussions" with Infrastructure Canada about a new federal transit fund, no firm pledge has been received.
A spokesperson for Infrastructure Canada didn't return a request for comment by deadline Thursday.
If the money doesn't arrive by early next year, the TTC says it will have to undertake a $1.4-billion life-extension overhaul to keep the trains operational for another 10 years, and perform maintenance on the Line 2 signalling system that is 70 years old in some sections. The signals are scheduled to be replaced with a modern Automatic Train Control (ATC) system, but it would only work with new trains.
The refurbishment option would only delay the need to replace the fleet, staff warned, and new trains would be required by 2036. Together the cost of the life-extension program, repair work and new trains would push the cost of the delayed replacement to more than $4.1 billion. Meanwhile, riders stuck on old trains would face a "degraded customer experience" and more unreliable service.
To pay for the higher costs of the delayed train purchase, the TTC would have to re-allocate funding from important programs like ATC.
Diverting funding from other projects to keep old trains in service "doesn't help with the growth of the city, the reliability of the system itself," Leary said. "It's a bad use of public money."
CAPTION: The trains serving Line 2 Bloor-Danforth will start reaching the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.

Document 15 of 500

Change the name of Yonge-Dundas Square or spend the money elsewhere

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/change-name-yonge-dundas-square-spend-money/docview/3070724651/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Change+the+name+of+Yonge-Dundas+Square+or+spend+the+money+elsewhere&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sankofa Square doesn't work
Chow and allies move ahead with renaming Yonge-Dundas Square, June 18
I don't mind renaming Yonge-Dundas Square, but why not name it instead after an important Black Torontonian? Perhaps William Hubbard or Thornton Blackburn, or both? Hubbard-Blackburn Square has a nice ring to it, and in time it might even come to be referred to as "The Hub." The short street along the south side of the square, currently called Dundas Square, could then be renamed Sankofa Place. Let's honour a Torontonian.
I. J. Shapiro, Toronto
The issue that is upsetting the public is not the name. It was the failure of the city to not conduct the widespread public consultation on the name that was promised at the beginning of the renaming process. Rather than extensive public consultation, a small select group chose the name that was quickly and quietly presented to city council for approval with the support of Mayor Olivia Chow who had decided to short-circuit the promised naming process.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
It is estimated that changing the name of Yonge-Dundas Square will cost close to $1 million. This is only the beginning. Changing subway stations' names comes next. I suggest an easy cost-effective solution to the Dundas problem. Pay a top-rated student a reasonable stipend to search the history books and find a woman named Dundas who made an exemplary contribution to Canadian culture, and then add some public service announcements highlighting her achievements. Problem solved.
Karen Riehm, Toronto
I am not a supporter of renaming Dundas Square. Giving it a new name that means nothing upon first blush to most people will not benefit the growth or attraction of this site for future gatherings and events. The old name for me was about a spot on a certain street corner, so it was good that I knew where it was. Nothing mattered about the man it may have been in reference to.
Norm Ferguson, Richmond Hill
The money can be used elsewhere
Why not take the $1 million for a proposed name change for that ugly pile of concrete called Yonge-Dundas Square and instead honour the contributions of African-Canadian citizens by creating community programs in disadvantaged neighbourhoods for Black youth. It seems to me a much better use of limited city funds than renaming a square that is already an embarrassment to downtown Toronto. Maybe someday the name Sankofa Square can be used to rename something all Torontonians can be proud of.
Elaine Minsky, Toronto
Per Bank may listen but he will not hear
The Loblaws boycott organizer recently had coffee with the company CEO. Their meeting didn't go as you would think, June 15
When the Loblaws CEO Per Bank declares he "gets" where Emily Johnson is "coming from," he is being honest. He is not an idiot. Idiots do not get paid $22 million a year, unless their father owns the company. While he "gets it," nothing will change because above all else Loblaws is capitalism at its finest. After Bank met Johnson he said, "I really liked her. She listened to me. I listened to her."
Then she was the main course at the next Weston dinner.
Joking aside, Bank will listen because he is professional, what he won't do is hear her. The cash registers are just too loud. What I sincerely hope is that Johnson keeps on her original chosen path of boycotting. That seems to get billionaire DNA in a tizzy.
Rochelle Carrière Hatton, Sudbury
Fashion takes consideration
Trudeau's kitschy socks. Ford's ugly shoes. Social media's fashion Guy ranks Canadian politicians' style, June 13
Is any Ontarian or Canadian affected by Premier Doug Ford's taste in shoes, or that of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in socks? Let them have their choices, but no, they are not a fundamental statement about how they see themselves or the world. Such a shallow matter should only be an issue in the fashion industry, one that has negatively affected and wrought havoc on the psyche of too many, particularly young women. Let us at least leave this fluff out of our political commentary.
Mike Brown, Burlington
Wasn't it former Prime Minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau who said "style is the man himself?" One only has to recall the summer summit in Ottawa in 1981. A photo of Trudeau shows him standing next to U.S. President Ronald Reagan. Reagan wore a dark, conservative, navy suit, a sharply-pressed, starched white shirt, white pocket puff and red and blue rep tie. Trudeau wore a light beige suit jacket, an open-collar-striped shirt and a red rose in his lapel. Yes, "style is the man himself." It matters.
Robert Douglas Bodnar, Toronto
We need home care workers
This housing experiment for older adults has changed lives. Here's how it could also save Ontario millions, June 15
Kudos to reporter Moira Welsh for sharing the story of Community Wellness Hubs as one means of enabling the infirmed to age in confidence at home. However, the essential missing element is a sufficient complement of health care workers willing to enter the home care sector. That won't happen until the provincial government levels the playing field and grants home care workers the same legal rights and working conditions as health care workers in other sectors. Until then, workers in home care will experience the same isolation and anxiety as the patients they care for.
Rabbi Shalom Schachter, Toronto
Hockey excluded women for a long time. That has consequencesSteady decline in youth hockey participation in Canada raises concerns about the future of the sport, June 12
This Associated Press story quotes former NHL player Matt Herr saying "(hockey) was everybody's first choice, and now there's all these different choices and we've got to make sure we're still everybody's first choice." That's the myth and, perhaps, is a core reason why hockey is in trouble. Hockey was not the first choice of girls and women. Not because we didn't want to play, but because we were not allowed to play. Only now is there pro-women's hockey in Canada with no thanks to the NHL. Those of us excluded from the game as young people often do not embrace it for our children. We experienced hockey as an exclusive male-only domain with a dose of often toxic masculinity. Herr should think about that when devising strategies to make hockey everybody's first choice.
Ann Peel, Toronto
CAPTION: Olivia Chow's executive committee voted Tuesday to move ahead with the new name Sankofa Square for Yonge-Dundas Square.

Document 16 of 500

Ford government closing Ontario Science Centre today after report found roof in danger of collapsing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-closing-ontario-science-centre/docview/3070724633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+closing+Ontario+Science+Centre+today+after+report+found+roof+in+danger+of+collapsing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Science Centre has been abruptly closed for good after a new engineering report found the roof is in danger of collapsing.
In a stunning move Friday afternoon - first reported by the Star - the Ministry of Infrastructure announced the iconic 1969 Don Mills building designed by the late Raymond Moriyama will no longer be structurally sound within a few months.
That meant Friday was the last day of operations for the once-popular tourist attraction that successive Progressive Conservative and Liberal governments have allowed to deteriorate for years.
"The actions taken today will protect the health and safety of visitors and staff at the Ontario Science Centre while supporting its eventual reopening in a new, state-of-the-art facility," said Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
Some 250 workers - including 200 members of the Ontario Public Service Employees' Union - will be affected but they are not immediately losing their jobs.
Closure of the facility comes against the backdrop of Premier Doug Ford's controversial decision to move the science museum to Ontario Place as part of the redevelopment of the lakeside park, which includes a privately owned Therme water spa.
Political opponents charged Ford wanted the 55-year-old building to become unusable so he could more easily relocate the science centre to the waterfront by 2028.
But the 52-page Rimkus Consulting Group engineering report found that the air-filled concrete used in construction - similar to that in more than 100 now-closed British schools built in the same era - is nearing the end of its life.
While the report said the building isn't unsafe right now, the risk of rain or snow buildup could make it so sooner rather than later because it would corrode the reinforcing steel underneath.
To that end, it must be vacated by Oct. 31.
The lightweight aerated concrete used in the construction has "the potential to degrade due to water infiltration," the review said.
One roofing panel is in the "critical" category, another six per cent of the panels are "high risk," nine per cent are "medium risk" while the remaining 84 per cent are "low risk."
"A significant snow or rain ... could exceed the reduced load-carrying capacity of the distressed panels, placing them at an increased risk of sudden collapse," the report said.
Rimkus recommended all such roof panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two or more years.
"These estimates are incomplete and subject to change," the report warned, noting the diagnosis was made based on visual inspections of all three science centre buildings and did not involve any "intrusive investigation."
Ford's government, which has been heavily criticized over its changes to the science centre, moved quickly due to safety concerns for children, parents and staff.
"Any risk of collapse is too much risk," said a senior official, who was not authorized to speak publicly before the announcement.
The science centre's board made the difficult decision late Thursday.
About 2,000 children scheduled to attend day camps this summer at the science centre will have relocated activities at a nearby public school. Their parents will be fully refunded within 30 days and given free programming at the school.
Science centre members, who pay annual fees ranging from $175 to $1,000, will be reimbursed on a pro-rated basis.
A wedding scheduled there for Saturday has been moved to another provincially owned facility and the bride and groom refunded.
The building's condition is worse than the government thought as recently as last November.
An earlier report released by Infrastructure Ontario, in relation to the planned move of the science centre to Ontario Place, warned the ravine-straddling building "is not sustainable under status quo conditions and is in operational crisis due to a failing structure with mounting critical building maintenance costs."
That 62-page report by consulting firm Ernst & Young was dated March 8, 2023 but not revealed until November when it was presented as a "business case" for the controversial move, stating it would cost $600 million more to fix up the old science centre than to build a new one.
The material in question - known as reinforced autoclaved aerated concrete or RAAC - was a popular choice in educational, institutional and industrial buildings from the mid-1950s to the mid-1970s, and was marketed as a "go-to" product because of its light weight and thermal insulation capacities that also made it a good sound and fire barrier, the report said.
Made of an aerated blend of sand, Portland cement and aluminum powder, the product has since "raised concern amongst building managers and owners worldwide due to structural issues," the report added.
"As a building material, RAAC has an overall reduced robustness compared to steel decks or traditional concrete, making it more susceptible to damage from impact and raising the risk of sudden failure .... owners and facility managers are often not aware of the problems ... until there is physical evidence of a failure or deterioration," it said.
"The consequences of RAAC failure include potential building damage and risk to public safety."
Infrastructure Ontario CEO Michael Lindsay said the government concluded it "would require significant investment and a vacant facility to remediate."
As the Star broke the news Friday, it was initially business as usual as the science centre, with the main parking lot about three-quarters full and the bus lot jammed with school buses bringing students for the last week of school trips.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) and Floyd Ruskin, co-chair of Save Ontario's Science Centre, held a press conference just outside the main entrance, as workers put up fences to close off the parking lots.
Shamji questioned the reports calling for the science centre's abrupt closing, saying he had been speaking at conference inside earlier in the day.
"What we're seeing happening right now at the science centre is so emblematic of how Doug Ford treats public infrastructure ..." said the Liberal MPP.
"Whether it's the way he underfunds health care or education, he allows it to fall apart and dismantle it, and then uses it to justify transfers to support his wealthy donors and friends, and to advance for-profit private interests like private spa companies," he said.
Ruskin noted "there's been over 50,000 people that have signed a petition not only to keep the Science Centre here on Don Mills Road but also to renew it, restore it and reinvest in it, because this government has refused to fund the maintenance."
"We don't want a science centre (at Ontario Place) with 18 per cent less exhibition space and also no interactive or immersive exhibits," he said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "closing a world-class science and cultural institution is heartbreaking, especially when we know the government's real motive here is to justify Ford's mega-spa vanity project" at Ontario Place.
"The government could have invested in revitalizing the science centre, but instead it's using our public money to concoct a sham business case against this important community hub," said Stiles.
The Star broke the news to Coun. Jon Burnside (Ward 16 Don Valley East), who represents the ward and has for months been searching for ways to keep the science centre open at that location or, failing that, something beneficial for the community.
"I'm devastated by the news. I was literally talking to two folks from Flemingdon Park last night who said how important the science centre is to their children's learning," said Burnside.
"There was always a part of me that thought the reports of the structural issues were overstated and could be addressed for a lot less money. It looks like the board of the science centre has decided that's not the case," he said.
"A lot of parents in the area rely on day camps and after school programs. It is a neighbourhood improvement area with a dearth of opportunities and facilities for the folks of Flemingdon. This was such a wonderful resource which is why none of us wanted it to close," the councillor said.
"The problem now is that because it's closing immediately, any other plans to fill that gap aren't in place."
Ontario's auditor general said in a December annual report looking in part at the science centre found "deferred maintenance projects that were at risk of critical failure have been repeatedly denied funding," including 42 such project since 2017, the year before the Ford government took power.
The auditor also noted attendance at the science centre peaked 10 years ago, at 920,000 annual visitors, and had fallen more than 12 per cent before the COVID-19 pandemic hit in part because exhibits "were not always evaluated for their effectiveness."
- With files from Raju Mudhar and David Rider

TTC CEO Rick Leary resigns + Rebel News claims ownership of truck with Islamophobic messages

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, June 21 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the TTC CEO's resignation, a truck displaying Islamophobic messages around the GTA and U of T's protest encampment back in court.
DON'T MISSTTC CEO Rick Leary has resigned
After seven long years captaining the transit agency of Canada's largest city, TTC head Rick Leary has announced his resignation. He dropped the bombshell announcement at a surprise press conference on Thursday. The 61-year-old Boston native said his work with the TTC had been "the most rewarding experience of my four decades in public transit." Advocates are already on the lookout for improvements in the next leader of the TTC - some are calling for a safer environment for workers, while others want a "renewal" of the city's transit system, Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia report. Here's what Leary's departure means for the transit agency.
Miss something? Just two weeks ago, Leary oversaw an 11th-hour deal with the transit agency's largest union, averting a strike that would have stopped the city in its tracks.What we know: The sudden resignation comes just eight months after the TTC board voted to investigate Leary over allegations of workplace misconduct. The allegations have not been proven.Next station: TTC staff are raising alarm bells - again - over the aging Line 2 subway trains, with the board voting for an ad campaign to pressure Ottawa to boost their contribution to a new fleet.Rebel News claimed ownership of a truck broadcasting Islamophobic messages
Far-right media outlet Rebel News is taking ownership of a digital-ad truck displaying Islamophobic messages. The brightly lit truck, with screens on its sides, has been spotted around the GTA, raising concerns about inciting hatred towards the city's Muslim community, the Star's Raju Mudhar reports. Rebel News founder Ezra Levant told the Star that the ad campaign was paid for by an anonymous group that is opposed to pro-Palestinian protests, justifying it as "free speech." Toronto police said its hate crime unit is investigating. Here's what else we know about the truck.
Context: Hate crimes in the city rose 47 per cent in 2023, nearly doubling the 10-year average amidst tension sparked in the city from the war in Gaza. Antisemitic acts made up more than a third of all reported hate crimes in 2023, while seven per cent were reported as Islamophobic.Word from Amira Elghawaby: "This clear incitement to hate Muslims is deeply worrisome given the ongoing violence that our communities continue to experience," Canada's Special Representative on Combating Islamophobia posted on X."The rights of the protesters must prevail." Lawyers are pushing back against the U of T's bid to clear the protest encampment
The fate of the pro-Palestinian encampment on U of T's downtown campus has been playing out in a Toronto courtroom this week, with lawyers for the protestors making their arguments on Thursday. The protest has been polarizing, to say the least. Protestors and supporters have said the encampment - which demands that the university divest from Israel - is a form of free expression and protest amidst the war in Gaza. Detractors and the university have called the encampment intimidating, with some claiming it as being antisemitic. The Ontario judge overseeing the case said he would decide on the case within a week. Here's how the case has played out so far.
Protestors say: The 200-tent encampment has a right to stay, protected by the Charter. They said all are welcome, so long as they maintain respectful behaviour.U of T says: Protestors are trespassing on the university's private property and that the encampment has left community members feeling unsafe.Context: The encampment was first set up on May 8. The university took protestors to court in a bid to remove them and their tents after Toronto police said they wouldn't intervene without a court order.WHAT ELSE
The Justin Trudeau's government is losing its momentum on Indigenous reconciliation, leaders say - and they're worried a Conservative government would be worse."It is indisputable that the defendants' reports slandered." A judge has allowed former Liberal MP Han Dong's defamation lawsuit against Global News to proceed.A court has granted Justin Trudeau's government an extension to fix the "lost Canadians" citizenship rules - with a catch.Doug Ford is urging Ottawa to match the U.S. by placing tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese EVs.After nine election victories, a Toronto MP known for his straight talk and wit says he won't run again.A Brampton firefighter has admitted to murdering his wife and then staging an elaborate, clumsy coverup for police."We've been managing the decline." The TDSB approved a balanced budget, but trustees aren't happy.A veteran Toronto police officer has been charged with the sexual assault of subordinates.Toronto is still under an extreme heat warning. Here's when it's expected to end.Heading to the beach this weekend? Check the water quality before you go."Toronto has an amazing opportunity." As the WNBA heads north, player Natalie Achonwa and reporter Kayla Grey discuss what it should look like.Elisapie practically grew up at the post office in her tiny Nunavik village. Now, the Inuk singer's face is on a Canada Post stamp.POV
For the Blue Jays, doubling down on George Springer and Kevin Kiermaier is a rally killer.

JUNE 20, 2024: Revered Canadian actor Donald Sutherland has died at the age of 88 following a long bout with an undisclosed illness. His passing was announced by his son, actor Kiefer Sutherland. "Never daunted by a role, good, bad or ugly. He loved what he did and did what he loved, and one can never ask for more than that," Kiefer posted on X. "A life well lived." Here's how the star of such films as "M*A*S*H" and "The Hunger Games" is being remembered.

Document 18 of 500

Liberals lose steam on reconciliation: Indigenous leaders worry a Poilievre government could be worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-lose-steam-on-reconciliation/docview/3070415212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+lose+steam+on+reconciliation%3A+Indigenous+leaders+worry+a+Poilievre+government+could+be+worse&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nine years after Justin Trudeau came to power campaigning on a new relationship with Indigenous people, Indigenous leaders say his government's once considerable rate of progress is slowing - and they are worried about that momentum reversing if the Conservatives topple the Liberals in the next election.
As they mark National Indigenous Peoples Day, leaders of three national organizations say the reconciliation agenda from Trudeau's 2015 platform has led to greater advancements on Indigenous priorities than previous governments.
Most importantly, they feel greater recognition of their self-government authority and believe there are stronger ties with Ottawa. But Indigenous leaders are also concerned about "going backwards," losing those relationships should a Conservative government take power in the next year, and are currently taking steps to make sure they don't lose their seat at the table.
"Hopefully, with all the changes in this country over the last 15 years or so, it's not possible to be a governing party in this country and still hold those same beliefs or treat First Nations, Inuit and Métis rights holders and rights-holding institutions in the way that the previous government did," said Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami (ITK) president Natan Obed. "But you never really can tell."
How the Trudeau government
has pursued reconciliation
Since 2015, the Liberal government has pursued a strong Indigenous mandate, including promising to end all boil-water advisories by 2021, an inquiry on Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women, Girls and Two-Spirit People (MMIWG2S), and fulfilling the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's 94 calls to action, including investing more than $30 billion in Indigenous priorities.
Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak told the Star earlier this year the "trajectory" of Canadians' thoughts on Indigenous people and reconciliation is changing, which she credits to the work done by the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and court settlements like on child welfare.
In October, the Federal Court approved a $23-billion settlement over child welfare discrimination. Then in February, the Supreme Court upheld legislation affirming Indigenous self-governance on child welfare over provincial jurisdiction.
Woodhouse Nepinak said it's important "for people to start speaking about the truth of the history of this country, it's not all cosy and it's not all perfect and First Nations people have been at the brunt of that."
Having worked with ITK and Inuit organizations since 2002, Obed has experienced several successive Liberal and Conservative governments. He said the Trudeau government has "transformed" its relationship with the Inuit.
ITK has seen "incredible advancements in their relationship," including the creation of a Crown-Inuit Partnership agreement, co-development of major legislation like the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) Act and forming relationships with more than 20 government ministers.
Métis National Council (MNC) president Cassidy Caron said there has been more progress in the past nine years "than we ever have in our history for the Métis nation," including the signing of its Canada-Métis Nation Accord and "innovative" co-developed legislation with child welfare.
Getting stuck in 'incremental
positive change'
The Liberal government has also been criticized for falling short on some promises. In the past year, government reports showed a lack of progress on closing long-standing socio-economic gaps between Indigenous and non-Indigenous peoples, particularly around housing, health care and policing.
Obed said the ongoing challenge with the federal government is "incremental positive change versus transformative change, and we're stuck in incremental positive change in a number of our different file areas." And while the ITK has positive relationships with some federal departments, others "still seem to be able to do whatever it is that they want with funds, including or excluding Inuit in the process."
After this year's federal budget was tabled in April, Woodhouse Nepinak called on the government to make a new commitment to reconciliation with First Nations people, saying Indigenous people are no longer a priority for the government.
Obed and Caron echoed the national chief's renewed calls for reconciliation. Caron said "the reconciliation agenda that was promised in 2015 has gone a little stale," and "a deep reflection" on what should be on a reconciliation agenda moving forward is needed.
"They respect our processes and have a willingness to work with us. However, it's not as full steam as it was nine years ago, even five years ago."
The cabinet ministers responsible for the Indigenous file aren't surprised by that response.
"When you are existing as the colonial partner in a colonial country, I think it's fair to expect all kinds of feelings and observations about the speed of transformation, whether it's fast enough, whether it's enough," Indigenous Services Canada Minister Patty Hajdu told the Star.
Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree said the agenda set in 2015 was "super ambitious" and Canada's journey with reconciliation is going to be multi-generational.
"We're still far from being in the ideal situation, but we have changed the trajectory of where this country needs to go and we are going in that direction. Each and every year, we're advancing on a range of issues," he said, adding reconciliation is a "one-way street" going only forward.
A turn back toward
the Harper years?
With a federal election on the horizon, memories of the previous Conservative government, which saw one of the largest political movements by Indigenous people in decades with the Idle No More movement, has given Indigenous leaders cause for concern.
Obed said the approach taken by the Harper government to "discredit democratic functions of Indigenous Peoples" was something that "set back the work of our organizations, in some cases, by a generation."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been making efforts to change his party's relationship with Indigenous people; in 2008, he said Indigenous people needed to learn the value of hard work and cast doubt on compensating residential school survivors for their trauma, comments for which he later apologized. He now supports searching for unmarked graves at the sites of former residential schools, and announced his support for a First Nations Resource Charge, which would allow First Nations to levy taxes on resource projects on their lands, something he called "a First Nations-led solution to a made-in-Ottawa problem."
Caron said she has good working relationships with the Conservative party's critics on the Indigenous file, and has met with the opposition leader. But both Caron and Obed said they do not want to "move backward" by losing the voices they gained through bilateral meetings the AFN, MNC and ITK established with the Liberal government in 2016.
"I think the only way that we continue to move forward is having open and honest relationships and conversations, and if we can't even be at the table with whatever government is in power, that would set us back," Caron said.
Conservative party spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said Poilievre has prioritized meeting with Indigenous leaders, and members of his team "will continue to engage with First Nations, Inuit and Métis" and work "toward real economic reconciliation and support them in taking back control of their money, their resources, and their lives."
Reconciliation on the ballot
Hajdu said reconciliation will be kept on the agenda in the next election campaign and hopes "all parties understand that reconciliation is the right thing to do and it's the smart thing to do."
Her priority for the next year is getting the long-awaited Indigenous water legislation through the House. Hajdu also wants to see more on economic reconciliation through the Indigenous Loan Guarantee Program and including Indigenous people in resource development.
Anandasangaree said housing, closing the infrastructure gap, ending the MMIWG2S crisis and self-government through co-developed legislation and modern treaties will continue to be his priorities.
"These are things that we need to continuously work toward, and every government that comes after us - it could be in a year, it could be in 20 years or 30 years - need to continue on this direction and if anything, every government needs to double their efforts," he said.
Obed and Caron said implementing UNDRIP Act's action plan is another priority, including an Indigenous Human Rights Tribunal, which would serve as an accountability mechanism for UNDRIP's implementation.
The MNC is also focusing on the self-government agreements for Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, despite the delays and controversy they have encountered through Bill C-53.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with AFN National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak, left, and president of the Métis National Council Cassidy Caron after his remarks at the Indigenous History Month reception in Ottawa on Monday. Caron said there has been more progress in the past nine years "than we ever have in our history for the Métis nation."Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Joy SpearChief-Morris Toronto Star

Subject: Legislation; Child welfare; Native North Americans; Tribunals & commissions; Inuit; Reconciliation; Political parties; Political campaigns

Document 19 of 500

Justin Trudeau's government is losing its momentum on Indigenous reconciliation, leaders say - and they're worried a Conservative government could be worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-government-is-losing-momentum-on/docview/3070412284/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+government+is+losing+its+momentum+on+Indigenous+reconciliation%2C+leaders+say+-+and+they%27re+worried+a+Conservative+government+could+be+worse&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Nine years after Justin Trudeau came to power campaigning on a new relationship with Indigenous people, Indigenous leaders say his government's once considerable rate of progress is slowing - and they are worried about that momentum reversing if the Conservatives topple the Liberals in the next election.
As they mark National Indigenous Peoples Day, leaders of three national organizations say the reconciliation agenda from Trudeau's 2015 platform has led to greater advancements on Indigenous priorities than previous governments.
Most importantly, they feel greater recognition of their self-government authority and believe there are stronger ties with Ottawa. But Indigenous leaders are also concerned about "going backwards," losing those relationships should a Conservative government take power in the next year, and are currently taking steps to make sure they don't lose their seat at the table.
"Hopefully, with all the changes in this country over the last 15 years or so, it's not possible to be a governing party in this country and still hold those same beliefs or treat First Nations, Inuit and Métis rights holders and rights-holding institutions in the way that the previous government did," said Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami (ITK) president Natan Obed.
"But you never really can tell."
How the Trudeau government has pursued reconcilliation
Since 2015, the Liberal government has pursued a strong Indigenous mandate, including promising to end all boil-water advisories by 2021, an inquiry on Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women, Girls and Two-Spirit People (MMIWG2S), and fulfilling the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's 94 calls to action, including investing more than $30 billion in Indigenous priorities.
Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak told the Star earlier this year the "trajectory" of Canadians' thoughts on Indigenous people and reconciliation is changing, which she credits to the work done by the Truth and Reconciliation Commission and court settlements like on child welfare.
In October, the Federal Court approved a $23-billion settlement over child welfare discrimination. Then in February, the Supreme Court upheld legislation affirming Indigenous self-governance on child welfare over provincial jurisdiction.
Woodhouse Nepinak said it's important "for people to start speaking about the truth of the history of this country, it's not all cosy and it's not all perfect and First Nations people have been at the brunt of that."
Having worked with ITK and Inuit organizations since 2002, Obed has experienced several successive Liberal and Conservative governments. He said the Trudeau government has "transformed" its relationship with the Inuit.
ITK has seen "incredible advancements in their relationship," including the creation of a Crown-Inuit Partnership agreement, co-development of major legislation like the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP) Act and forming relationships with more than 20 government ministers.
Métis National Council (MNC) president Cassidy Caron said there has been more progress in the past nine years "than we ever have in our history for the Métis nation," including the signing of its Canada-Métis Nation Accord and "innovative" co-developed legislation with child welfare.
Getting stuck in 'incremental positive change'
The Liberal government has also been criticized for falling short on some promises. In the past year, government reports showed a lack of progress on closing long-standing socio-economic gaps between Indigenous and non-Indigenous peoples, particularly around housing, health care and policing.
Obed said the ongoing challenge with the federal government is "incremental positive change versus transformative change, and we're stuck in incremental positive change in a number of our different file areas." And while the ITK has positive relationships with some federal departments, others "still seem to be able to do whatever it is that they want with funds, including or excluding Inuit in the process."
After this year's federal budget was tabled in April, Woodhouse Nepinak called on the government to make a new commitment to reconciliation with First Nations people, saying Indigenous people are no longer a priority for the government.
Obed and Caron echoed the national chief's renewed calls for reconciliation. Caron said "the reconciliation agenda that was promised in 2015 has gone a little stale," and "a deep reflection" on what should be on a reconciliation agenda moving forward is needed.
"They respect our processes and have a willingness to work with us. However, it's not as full steam as it was nine years ago, even five years ago."
The cabinet ministers responsible for the Indigenous file aren't surprised by that response.
"When you are existing as the colonial partner in a colonial country, I think it's fair to expect all kinds of feelings and observations about the speed of transformation, whether it's fast enough, whether it's enough," Indigenous Services Canada Minister Patty Hajdu told the Star.
Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree said the agenda set in 2015 was "super ambitious" and Canada's journey with reconciliation is going to be multi-generational.
"We're still far from being in the ideal situation, but we have changed the trajectory of where this country needs to go and we are going in that direction. Each and every year, we're advancing on a range of issues," he said, adding reconciliation is a "one-way street" going only forward.
A turn back toward the Harper years?
With a federal election on the horizon, memories of the previous Conservative government, which saw one of the largest political movements by Indigenous people in decades with the Idle No More movement, has given Indigenous leaders cause for concern.
Obed said the approach taken by the Harper government to "discredit democratic functions of Indigenous Peoples" was something that "set back the work of our organizations, in some cases, by a generation."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been making efforts to change his party's relationship with Indigenous people; in 2008, he said Indigenous people needed to learn the value of hard work and cast doubt on compensating residential school survivors for their trauma, comments for which he later apologized.
He now supports searching for unmarked graves at the sites of former residential schools, and announced his support for a First Nations Resource Charge, which would allow First Nations to levy taxes on resource projects on their lands, something he called "a First Nations-led solution to a made-in-Ottawa problem."
Caron said she has good working relationships with the Conservative party's critics on the Indigenous file, and has met with the opposition leader .
But both Caron and Obed said they do not want to "move backwards" by losing the voices they gained through bilateral meetings the AFN, MNC and ITK established with the Liberal government in 2016.
"I think the only way that we continue to move forward is having open and honest relationships and conversations, and if we can't even be at the table with whatever government is in power, that would set us back," Caron said.
Conservative party spokesperson Sebastian Skamski said Poilievre has prioritized meeting with Indigenous leaders, and members of his team "will continue to engage with First Nations, Inuit and Métis" and work "toward real economic reconciliation and support them in taking back control of their money, their resources, and their lives."
Reconciliation on the ballot
Hajdu said reconciliation will be kept on the agenda in the next election campaign and hopes "all parties understand that reconciliation is the right thing to do and it's the smart thing to do."
Her priority for the next year is getting the long-awaited Indigenous water legislation through the House. Hajdu also wants to see more on economic reconciliation through the Indigenous Loan Guarantee Program and including Indigenous people in resource development.
Anandasangaree said housing, closing the infrastructure gap, ending the MMIWG2S crisis and self-government through co-developed legislation and modern treaties will continue to be his priorities.
"These are things that we need to continuously work towards, and every government that comes after us - it could be in a year, it could be in 20 years or 30 years - need to continue on this direction and if anything, every government needs to double their efforts," he said.
Obed and Caron said implementing UNDRIP Act's action plan is another priority, including an Indigenous Human Rights Tribunal, which would serve as an accountability mechanism for UNDRIP's implementation.
The MNC is also focusing on the self-government agreements for Alberta, Saskatchewan and Ontario, despite the delays and controversy they have encountered through Bill C-53.
Obed said ITK plans to continue with its tuberculosis elimination plan, which Ottawa has previously pledged to fund. ITK has asked for $131 million over seven years.
"We don't want the legacies of successive governments to make outrageous promises that then aren't kept," he said.
"I think that is the direct threat that reconciliation faces."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks with AFN National Chief Cindy Woodhouse, left, and president of the Métis National Council Cassidy Caron, right, after his remarks at the Indigenous History Month reception in Ottawa on June 17, 2024.

Full text: Sankofa Square doesn't work
Chow and allies move ahead with renaming Yonge-Dundas Square, June 18
I don't mind renaming Yonge-Dundas Square, but why not name it instead after an important Black Torontonian? Perhaps William Hubbard or Thornton Blackburn, or both? Hubbard-Blackburn Square has a nice ring to it, and in time it might even come to be referred to as "The Hub." The short street along the south side of the square, currently called Dundas Square, could then be renamed Sankofa Place. Let's honour a Torontonian.
I.J. Shapiro, Toronto
The issue that is upsetting the public is not the name. It was the failure of the city to not conduct the widespread public consultation on the name that was promised at the beginning of the renaming process. Rather than extensive public consultation, a small select group chose the name that was quickly and quietly presented to city council for approval with the support of Mayor Olivia Chow who had decided to short-circuit the promised naming process.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
It is estimated that changing the name of Yonge-Dundas Square will cost close to $1 million. This is only the beginning. Changing subway stations' names comes next. I suggest an easy cost-effective solution to the Dundas problem. Pay a top-rated student a reasonable stipend to search the history books and find a woman named Dundas who made an exemplary contribution to Canadian culture, and then add some public service announcements highlighting her achievements. Problem solved.
Karen Riehm, Toronto
I am not a supporter of renaming Dundas Square. Giving it a new name that means nothing upon first blush to most people will not benefit the growth or attraction of this site for future gatherings and events. The old name for me was about a spot on a certain street corner, so it was good that I knew where it was. Nothing mattered about the man it may have been in reference to.
Norm Ferguson, Richmond Hill
Why not take the $1 million for the proposed name change and instead honour the contributions of African-Canadian citizens by creating community programs in disadvantaged neighbourhoods for Black youth. It seems to me a much better use of limited city funds than renaming a square that is already an embarrassment to downtown Toronto. Maybe someday the name Sankofa Square can be used to rename something all Torontonians can be proud of.
Elaine Minsky, Toronto
Loblaws CEO may listen, but
he will not hear
The Loblaws boycott organizer recently had coffee with the company CEO. Their meeting didn't go as you would think, June 15
When the Loblaws CEO Per Bank declares he "gets" where Emily Johnson is "coming from," he is being honest. He is not an idiot. Idiots do not get paid $22 million a year, unless their father owns the company. While he "gets it," nothing will change because above all else Loblaws is capitalism at its finest. After Bank met Johnson he said, "I really liked her. She listened to me. I listened to her." Then she was the main course at the next Weston dinner. Joking aside, Bank will listen because he is professional, what he won't do is hear her. The cash registers are just too loud. What I sincerely hope is that Johnson keeps on her original chosen path of boycotting. That seems to get billionaire DNA in a tizzy.
Rochelle Carrière Hatton,
Sudbury
Fashion takes consideration
Trudeau's kitschy socks. Ford's ugly shoes. Social media's fashion Guy ranks Canadian politicians' style, June 13
Is any Ontarian or Canadian affected by Premier Doug Ford's taste in shoes, or that of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau in socks? Let them have their choices, but no, they are not a fundamental statement about how they see themselves or the world. Such a shallow matter should only be an issue in the fashion industry, one that has negatively affected and wrought havoc on the psyche of too many, particularly young women. Let us at least leave this fluff out of our political commentary.
Mike Brown, Burlington
Wasn't it former prime minister Pierre Elliot Trudeau who said "style is the man himself?" One only has to recall the summer summit in Ottawa in 1981. A photo of Trudeau shows him standing next to U.S. president Ronald Reagan. Reagan wore a dark, conservative, navy suit, a sharply pressed, starched white shirt, white pocket puff and red and blue rep tie. Trudeau wore a light beige suit jacket, an open-collar-striped shirt and a red rose in his lapel. Yes, "style is the man himself." It matters.
Robert Douglas Bodnar, Toronto
We need workers for home care
This housing experiment for older adults has changed lives. Here's how it could also save Ontario millions, June 15
Kudos to reporter Moira Welsh for sharing the story of Community Wellness Hubs as one means of enabling the infirmed to age in confidence at home. However, the essential missing element is a sufficient complement of health-care workers willing to enter the home care sector. That won't happen until the provincial government levels the playing field and grants home care workers the same legal rights and working conditions as health-care workers in other sectors. Until then, workers in home care will experience the same isolation and anxiety as the patients they care for.
Rabbi Shalom Schachter,
Toronto
Hockey excluded women for
a long time
Steady decline in youth hockey participation in Canada raises concerns about the future of the sport, June 12
This Associated Press story quotes former NHL player Matt Herr saying "(hockey) was everybody's first choice, and now there's all these different choices and we've got to make sure we're still everybody's first choice." That's the myth and, perhaps, is a core reason why hockey is in trouble. Hockey was not the first choice of girls and women. Not because we didn't want to play, but because we were not allowed to play. Only now is there pro-women's hockey in Canada with no thanks to the NHL. Those of us excluded from the game as young people often do not embrace it for our children. We experienced hockey as an exclusive male-only domain with a dose of often toxic masculinity. Herr should think about that when devising strategies to make hockey everybody's first choice.
Ann Peel, Toronto
