TTC board raises alarm on aging Line 2 trains

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-board-raises-alarm-on-aging-line-2-trains/docview/3070411852/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+board+raises+alarm+on+aging+Line+2+trains&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TTC riders could be stuck with declining service and a huge repair bill unless the federal government comes through soon with money to replace Toronto's aging Line 2 subway trains, according to the transit agency.
At a meeting of the TTC board Thursday, agency staff warned that the fleet serving its main east-west route is about 18 months from the end of its service life and is in urgent need of replacement.
After hearing the presentation, the board voted to draft a media campaign that would ask riders to pressure their MPs to have Ottawa match the city's and province's contributions for a new fleet. The two lower levels of government have each pledged $758 million toward the $2.3 billion purchase of 55 trains. "At this point we all need to be screaming," said Coun. Dianne Saxe (Ward 11, University-Rosedale), who moved the motion.
"This is something where we need everyone who cares about the TTC and everyone who needs the TTC to pester your MP," said Saxe, noting that Toronto's more-than 20 federal Liberal representatives play a critical role in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government.
"We desperately need the money," Saxe said. "We have to have the cars."
TTC CEO Rick Leary said the Scarborough RT is a cautionary tale about what happens when vehicles are operated past their intended life. Even before a July 2023 derailment forced the TTC to decommission that line, it was notorious for breakdowns and unreliable service.
"We don't want to have that type of (situation) to happen to Line 2," said Leary, speaking to reporters shortly before his shock resignation Thursday. He said he was hopeful Ottawa will commit the funding by the end of the year.
Line 2 is an essential part of the TTC subway network, and is responsible for more than 400,000 daily boardings. Staff told the board that the line's trains will start reaching the end of their 30-year service life in 2026.
Premier Doug Ford agreed to provide provincial funding of nearly $760 million for the replacement fleet as part of the "new deal" reached with Mayor Olivia Chow last November, but made it conditional on Trudeau's government matching that amount.
The province has its own interest in the purchase. Metrolinx, Ontario's transit agency, is hoping to piggyback on the TTC's order to buy 15 new trains for the Scarborough and Yonge North subway extensions.
TTC staff told the board the agency needs to secure the federal portion by March 2025 at the latest so that it can place the order in time to have new trains arrive by 2030. But despite "productive discussions" with Infrastructure Canada about a new federal transit fund, no firm pledge has been received.
A spokesperson for Infrastructure Canada didn't return a request for comment by deadline Thursday.
If the money doesn't arrive by early next year, the TTC says it will have to undertake a $1.4-billion life-extension overhaul to keep the trains operational for another 10 years, and perform maintenance on the Line 2 signalling system that is 60 years old in some sections. The signals are scheduled to be replaced with a modern Automatic Train Control (ATC) system, but it would only work with new trains.
The refurbishment option would only delay the need to replace the fleet, staff warned, and new trains would be required by 2036. Together the cost of the life-extension program, repair work and new trains would push the cost of the delayed replacement to more than $4.1 billion. Meanwhile, riders stuck on old trains would face a "degraded customer experience" and more unreliable service.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Toronto MP McKay says he won't run again

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/toronto-mp-mckay-says-he-wont-run-again/docview/3070403620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+MP+McKay+says+he+won%27t+run+again&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Inside the Liberal caucus meeting on Wednesday, John McKay was having a bit of a Tom Sawyer moment.
The veteran MP had just told his colleagues that, after 27 years and nine straight election wins in his Scarborough riding, he would not run again in the next federal campaign. That uncorked a flow of praise from his fellow Liberals, including from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who recalled how McKay staged a session on the mechanics of being a parliamentarian after Trudeau was first elected in 2008.
"You know, it's sort of like going to your own funeral," McKay said over the phone, echoing the scene from the classic Mark Twain novel in which townspeople grieve for the boys who aren't really dead.
"People say such nice things about you, you start to wonder whether that's really you," McKay said. "I guess when you survive as many years as I have survived, you achieve a certain status in caucus and people may think you're wiser than you actually are.
"But I had some very nice things said about me, and I appreciate it."
Known around Parliament Hill for his straight talk and wry wit - when asked how he feels about his upcoming retirement, McKay deadpanned: "I'm a white male; I don't have feelings" - McKay is the latest Liberal MP to step down or decide not to run again in the next election scheduled for the fall of 2025. That includes a host of long-serving MPs and a bevy of former cabinet ministers, like Carolyn Bennett, David Lametti and Omar Alghabra, all of whom are quitting at a time when national polls place Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives in a commanding lead over Trudeau and the incumbent Liberals.
But while McKay acknowledged the situation is "not good" for his party, he said the Liberals' political fortunes had almost no impact on his decision to retire from federal politics. It's more that he is at the right "age and stage" to call it quits. He's 76,has five young grandchildren, and his wife, Carolyn Dartnell.
McKay informed Trudeau of his plans more than a year ago, but held off going public until he figured out who might run in his long-held Liberal riding of Scarborough-Guildwood. Turns out, since fellow Toronto Liberal MP Michael Coteau is losing his neighbouring riding of Don Valley East in the redrawing of federal electoral boundaries, he will shift over and vie for McKay's old seat, McKay said.
Coteau, a former Ontario provincial cabinet minister, confirmed the arrangement to the Star, explaining that he was in a "very awkward place" and happy to have found a place to run.
McKay was first elected when Jean Chrétien was prime minister, during the federal government's drive to slash costs to balance the budget. He said that example of fiscal discipline stuck with him. He was also deeply impacted by encounters with figures like Roméo Dallaire, whose eloquence about the horrors of the Rwanda genocide have stuck with McKay.
As an MP who never entered cabinet, McKay said he found a way to leave a mark through private members' bills - legislation that comes from parliamentarians who aren't part of the sitting government. McKay's list includes a law passed in 2004 that forced tobacco companies to produce "firesafe" cigarettes; the so-called "Better Aid Bill" of 2006, which was designed to ensure foreign aid goes to poverty reduction; and most recently a law he co-sponsored that came into effect this year, to force companies to submit annual reports on whether there is forced labour in their supply chains.
CAPTION: Liberal MP John McKay, seen here in 2019, has announced he will not seek re-election in his Scarborough riding.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Document 23 of 500

Ford urges Trudeau to hike tariffs on EVs: Follow U.S. lead to curb cheap imports coming from China

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-urges-trudeau-hike-tariffs-on-evs/docview/3070403575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+urges+Trudeau+to+hike+tariffs+on+EVs%3A+Follow+U.S.+lead+to+curb+cheap+imports+coming+from+China&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is urging Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to impose stiff tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese electric vehicles to protect Ontario's nascent industry from being swamped.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said Thursday.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said. "Unless we act fast, we risk Ontario and Canadian jobs."
In Westville, N.S., Trudeau was noncommittal but stressed "we will continue to be there to protect Canadian workers and Canadian industry."
"Doug Ford and I - the Ontario government and the government of Canada - have worked closely together over the past years to build one of the strongest electric vehicle manufacturing ecosystems in the world here in Canada," the prime minister said.
"And you don't just have to take our word for it - Bloomberg recently ranked Canada number one in the developing EV battery supply chain in the world, ahead even of China," said Trudeau.
"We're watching closely what the Americans and other allies have done. We actually had significant conversations about this last week when I joined together with other G7 leaders in Italy to talk about global economic issues."
Ford's intervention comes as pressure is mounting on Ottawa to get in line with U.S. President Joe Biden on taxing Chinese-made EVs, which can be cheaper than domestic vehicles.
While Canada currently has a six percent tariff on Chinese EVs, Ottawa gives buyers - like those who buy Teslas made in Shanghai for the Canadian market - a $5,000 rebate.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Trudeau and Ford on the EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy, said Canada must act.
"While the U.S. protected their market with a serious tariff, most people don't know four out of five cars made in Canada are actually sold to Americans," said Volpe.
"We have the moral obligation to return the favour and protect the Canadian market for U.S. imports from these Chinese Trojan horses," he said Thursday.
Trudeau's government appears torn between competing pressures to boost EV use to meet climate-change targets and the need to protect Canadian jobs. Ottawa has decreed that by 2035 all new light-duty vehicles must be zero-emission.
In Ottawa, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said "it's a very live discussion that we're having with many colleagues, including (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne and others."
"There are trade-offs one way or the other - and we're looking into this," Guilbeault said Wednesday.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng emphasized "Canada and the United States have a very integrated auto supply chain."
"We also have ... a lot of really amazing Canadian auto workers, so it's an issue that we are taking very, very seriously and I will have something to say about that very shortly," said Ng. "For Canada, what's really important is ... trade that is underpinned by the rules of trade. Canada can't be a backdoor for products that are unfairly traded."
Ford pointed out that "over the last four years, Ontario has a secured $43 billion worth of investments in electric vehicle and battery manufacturing, securing hundreds of thousands of good, well-paying jobs."
"This has been an all-hands-on-deck achievement, working side-by-side with the federal government and our private-sector labour partners," the premier said.
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief Mark Ramzy Toronto Star With files from Stephanie Levitz

Document 24 of 500

After nine election victories, a Toronto MP known for his straight talk and wit says he won't run again

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/after-nine-election-victories-toronto-mp-known/docview/3070785571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+nine+election+victories%2C+a+Toronto+MP+known+for+his+straight+talk+and+wit+says+he+won%27t+run+again&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Inside the Liberal caucus meeting on Wednesday, John McKay was having a bit of a Tom Sawyer moment.
The veteran MP had just told his colleagues that, after 27 years and nine straight election wins in his Scarborough riding, he would not run again in the next federal campaign. That uncorked a flow of praise from his fellow Liberals, including from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who recalled how McKay staged a session on the mechanics of being a parliamentarian after Trudeau was first elected in 2008.
"You know, it's sort of like going to your own funeral," McKay said over the phone with the Star, echoing the scene from the classic Mark Twain novel in which townspeople grieve for the boys who aren't really dead.
"People say such nice things about you, you start to wonder whether that's really you," McKay said. "I guess when you survive as many years as I have survived, you achieve a certain status in caucus and people may think you're wiser than you actually are.
"But I had some very nice things said about me, and I appreciate it."
Known around Parliament Hill for his straight talk and wry wit - when asked how he feels about his upcoming retirement, McKay deadpanned: "I'm a white male; I don't have feelings" - McKay is the latest Liberal MP to step down or decide not to run again in the next election scheduled for the fall of 2025. That includes a host of long-serving MPs and a bevy of former cabinet ministers, like Carolyn Bennett, David Lametti and Omar Alghabra, all of whom are quitting at a time when national polls place Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives in a commanding lead over Trudeau and the incumbent Liberals.
But while McKay acknowledged the situation is "not good" for his party, he said the Liberals' political fortunes had almost no impact on his decision to retire from federal politics. It's more that he is at the right "age and stage" to call it quits. He's 76, has five young grandchildren, and his wife - as she put it herself when she joined the Star's interview with McKay on Thursday - has been a "political widow for 27 years."
"I didn't believe it until it happened," Carolyn Dartnell said of her husband's intention not to run again. "He has been musing about it for a while."
Indeed, as McKay explained, he informed Trudeau of his plans more than a year ago, but held off going public until he figured out who might run in his long-held Liberal riding of Scarborough-Guildwood. Turns out, since fellow Toronto Liberal MP Michael Coteau is losing his neighbouring riding of Don Valley East in the once-per-decade redrawing of federal electoral boundaries, he will shift over and vie for McKay's old seat, McKay said.
"I know Michael and admire him greatly, and I think he's a terrific candidate and knows the riding well - and lives in it," McKay said. "So things all worked out."
Coteau, a former Ontario provincial cabinet minister, confirmed the arrangement to the Star, explaining that he was in a "very awkward place" and happy to have found a place to run.
McKay was first elected when Jean Chrétien was prime minister, during the federal government's drive to slash costs to balance the budget. He said that example of fiscal discipline stuck with him, and has marked his political views ever since. He was also deeply impacted by encounters with figures like Roméo Dallaire, whose eloquence about the horrors of the Rwanda genocide have stuck with McKay.
As an MP who never entered cabinet, McKay said he found a way to leave a mark through private members' bills - legislation that comes from parliamentarians who aren't part of the sitting government. McKay's list includes a law passed in 2004 that forced tobacco companies to produce "fire-safe" cigarettes; the so-called "Better Aid Bill" of 2006, which was designed to ensure foreign aid goes to poverty reduction; and most recently a law he co-sponsored that came into effect this year, to force companies to submit annual reports on whether there is forced labour in their supply chains.
"I sometimes feel sympathy for the cabinet ministers who essentially get marching orders" from the Prime Minister's Office, McKay said. "The so-called, much-maligned backbencher can actually have an impact that is certainly of significance."
CAPTION: Liberal MP John McKay, seen here in 2019, has announced he will not seek re-election in his Scarborough riding.

Doug Ford urges Justin Trudeau to match the U.S. and place tariffs on 'artificially cheap' Chinese EVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-urges-justin-trudeau-match-u-s-place/docview/3069990330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+urges+Justin+Trudeau+to+match+the+U.S.+and+place+tariffs+on+%27artificially+cheap%27+Chinese+EVs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is urging Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to impose stiff tariffs on "artificially cheap" Chinese electric vehicles to protect Ontario's nascent industry from being swamped.
"I'm calling on the federal government to immediately match or exceed U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports, including at least a 100 per cent tariff on Chinese electric vehicles," Ford said Thursday.
"Taking every advantage of low labour standards and dirty energy, China is flooding the market with artificially cheap electric vehicles," the premier said.
"Unless we act fast, we risk Ontario and Canadian jobs."
In Westville, N.S., Trudeau was noncommittal but stressed "we will continue to be there to protect Canadian workers and Canadian industry."
"Doug Ford and I - the Ontario government and the government of Canada - have worked closely together over the past years to build one of the strongest electric vehicle manufacturing ecosystems in the world here in Canada," the prime minister said.
"And you don't just have to take our word for it - Bloomberg recently ranked Canada number one in the developing EV battery supply chain in the world, ahead even of China," said Trudeau.
"We're watching closely what the Americans and other allies have done. We actually had significant conversations about this last week when I joined together with other G7 leaders in Italy to talk about global economic issues."
Ford's intervention comes as pressure is mounting on Ottawa to get in line with U.S. President Joe Biden on taxing Chinese-made EVs, which can be cheaper than domestic vehicles.
While Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs, Ottawa gives buyers - like those who purchase Teslas made in Shanghai for the Canadian market - a $5,000 rebate.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, who has worked closely with Trudeau and Ford on the EV strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy, said Canada must act.
"While the U.S. protected their market with a serious tariff, most people don't know four out of five cars made in Canada are actually sold to Americans," said Volpe.
"We have the moral obligation to return the favour and protect the Canadian market for U.S. imports from these Chinese Trojan horses," he said Thursday.
Trudeau's government appears torn between competing pressures to boost EV use to meet climate-change targets and the need to protect Canadian jobs. Ottawa has decreed that by 2035 all new light-duty vehicles must be zero emission.
In Ottawa, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said "it's a very live discussion that we're having with many colleagues, including (Industry) Minister (François-Philippe) Champagne and others."
"There are trade-offs one way or the other - and we're looking into this," Guilbeault said Wednesday.
International Trade Minister Mary Ng emphasized "Canada and the United States have a very integrated auto supply chain."
"We also have ... a lot of really amazing Canadian auto workers, so it's an issue that we are taking very, very seriously and I will have something to say about that very shortly," said Ng.
"For Canada, what's really important is ... trade that is underpinned by the rules of trade. Canada can't be a backdoor for products that are unfairly traded."
Ford pointed out that "over the last four years, Ontario has a secured $43 billion worth of investments in electric vehicle and battery manufacturing, securing hundreds of thousands of good, well-paying jobs."
"This has been an all-hands-on-deck achievement, working side-by-side with the federal government and our private-sector labour partners," the premier said.
"We can never take our progress for granted. Our workers are the best in the world. As governments, we need to do everything in our power to protect their jobs and the paycheques they take home," he said.
"Now's the time to work with our U.S. partners to deepen and strengthen homegrown, U.S.-Canada supply chains. Now's the time to protect good, hard-earned Ontario and Canadian jobs by matching U.S. tariffs on Chinese imports."
Five weeks ago, Biden announced he would increase its existing tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent.
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau look over a vehicle along an assembly line at an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, Ont. on April 25, 2024.

Document 26 of 500

Crombie, Liberals gear up for election: Ford's Tories have signalled the vote could come early

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/crombie-liberals-gear-up-election/docview/3069818743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Crombie%2C+Liberals+gear+up+for+election%3A+Ford%27s+Tories+have+signalled+the+vote+could+come+early&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Premier Doug Ford gearing up for an early election, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is urging her party to get ready.
"As we move into a campaign phase, I want you to know that we have a strong new team in place to help us prepare for that fight," Crombie wrote in an email to party members Wednesday.
She announced Chad Walsh and Genevieve Tomney, key players on her successful leadership bid, will be the Liberals' campaign directors.
"With Chad's deep organizing experience and network, and Gen's immense skill set in strategy, digital engagement, and communications, we will be ready for anything Doug Ford throws at us," the Liberal leader said.
"I also want to share that Tom Allison will now move into a role as a campaign chair, with additional co-chairs to be announced over the summer," she said.
The move heralds generational change at the highest level of the Liberal campaign.
Allison - a respected veteran who ran the winning leaderships of Crombie last December, Steven Del Duca in 2020 and Kathleen Wynne in 2013 - is passing the torch to Walsh, a skilled political organizer, and Tomney, a well-regarded former journalist.
"As we move into the summer months, I want you all to know that this will be a busy season for Ontario Liberals," wrote Crombie.
"While Doug Ford adjourned the legislature early to give himself a longer summer vacation, Ontario Liberals will be organizing and getting ready to fight an election - be that in 2026 or any time sooner," continued the Liberal leader.
The Star reported May 27 that Ford is considering an election call before the scheduled June 2026 vote over concerns about cuts a future federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might impose.
Worried that Poilievre might scrap or dramatically scale back the electric-vehicle strategy the provincial Progressive Conservatives have developed in co-operation with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, he is considering a spring 2025 vote.
Ford also would like to face voters while he has a big poll lead over the Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," a PC insider, speaking confidentially to discuss strategy, said last month.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Leadership

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

As Doug Ford contemplates early Ontario election, Bonnie Crombie makes moves to ready the Liberals

Document 28 of 500

Province, 4 First Nations sign deal to build roads: Project would improve access to Ring of Fire region

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-4-first-nations-sign-deal-build-roads/docview/3069494338/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province%2C+4+First+Nations+sign+deal+to+build+roads%3A+Project+would+improve+access+to+Ring+of+Fire+region&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four First Nations have signed a deal with Ontario for new roads, other infrastructure projects and skills training as the province lays a foundation for plans to mine the mineral-rich Ring of Fire region.
The province said Animbiigoo Zaagi'igan Anishinaabek, Aroland First Nation, Ginoogaming First Nation and Long Lake #58 First Nation will see their roads that connect to the provincial highway system fortified and renewed.
The roads, about 300 kilometres east of Thunder Bay, Ont., will support critical mineral and resource development, Premier Doug Ford said.
"These are all-season roads that will support First Nations communities, built by First Nations workers," Ford wrote in a statement on Tuesday.
The province signed letters of confirmation with Kenogamisis Investment Corporation,and Minodahmun Development, the former owned by those four First Nations and the latter owned by three of them.
The province is investing in the area, known as Greenstone, as part of its long-term strategy to mine the area and regions north into the Ring of Fire.
Greenstone Gold Mines, one of the largest open-pit mines in the country, is set to officially open later this summer.
The province is trying to create an end-to-end manufacturing chain for electric vehicle batteries and it sees northern Ontario's Ring of Fire region as a prime source for the critical minerals needed.
Two other communities, Webequie First Nation and Marten Falls First Nation, have signed deals with the province to handle the environmental assessments of three proposed roads into the Ring of Fire.
Several First Nations both inside and outside the Ring of Fire region have said mining on their traditional territories cannot occur without their prior informed consent.
Building up Greenstone is a priority for the province, Indigenous Affairs Minister Greg Rickford said. It is an area he has dubbed the "corridor to prosperity."
As part of the deal with the four First Nations, the province will spend $2 million to build the Migizi Plaza Rest Stop, which will serve as a hub for the nearby communities. The province will also spend $1.9 million to provide training to secure jobs for mineral development.
"Building on the success of today's announcement we believe we are in the best position any government has ever been to build out the corridor to prosperity for the benefit of isolated First Nation communities north of us and a real prospect of developing critical minerals in the Ring of Fire," Rickford said.
"We are laying the foundation for Greenstone to become the new centre of gravity for mining, in partnership with First Nations."
The Ontario Provincial Police also needs a new detachment in the area as its current location is needed for the gold mine. The province said it will work to relocate the police station.
The four First Nations welcomed the news, although one chief said its participation was under duress.
"Minodahmun's Indigenous workforce development program is crucial for empowering our people with the skills and opportunities to build meaningful careers, strengthen our communities and ensure our members are participating in projects on our lands," said Ginoogaming First Nation Chief Sheri Taylor.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Exploration in the Ring of Fire is based out of Noront Resource's Esker Camp in the James Bay Lowlands. Ontario is trying to create an end-to-end manufacturing chain for electric vehicle batteries and it sees the Ring of Fire region as a prime source for the critical minerals needed.Noront Resources
CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 29 of 500

With the polls stacked against him, here's why Justin Trudeau still thinks he can win the next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/with-polls-stacked-against-him-heres-why-justin/docview/3069493927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+the+polls+stacked+against+him%2C+here%27s+why+Justin+Trudeau+still+thinks+he+can+win+the+next+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is no doubt acutely aware of just how much Liberals need a morale boost right now.
Several weeks ago, the prime minister reportedly told his cabinet that winning in 2025 is definitely still a possibility, which many Liberals - not just ministers - are desperate to believe.
On Monday evening, in an exclusive interview with CBC's Power and Politics, Trudeau said something similar in his conversation with host David Cochrane.
Asked whether he could be the factor holding back Liberal fortunes in the next election, Trudeau said. "Canadians are not in decision mode right now."
He added: "What you tell a pollster, if they ever manage to reach you, is very different from the choice Canadians end up making in an election campaign."
True, this is another version of the stock politician's answer about the only poll that matters is on election day. But fundamentally it reflects what Trudeau sincerely believes - that when Canadians have a chance to really stare down the choice between this prime minister and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, they'll stick with a progressive government.
Intriguingly, Trudeau said he and some of his fellow G7 leaders had a talk last week in Switzerland about the challenges facing incumbent governments. What a discussion that would have been. If there was any time to be a fly on the wall, that would be one of them: British prime minister Rishi Sunak fighting for his life in the current U.K. election; French president Emmanuel Macron in the midst of his own snap election to fight back against the rise of the right in recent European elections. No doubt they all talked about who Joe Biden is up against in the U.S. election in November.
I'm trying to imagine what Trudeau had to say about his own battles with Poilievre in this context. All the prime minister told Cochrane was that he came out of those conversations knowing he wasn't alone.
"Everywhere, people are struggling with high inflation, with cost-of-living issues, with interest rates, with housing challenges, child-care challenges, all these things," Trudeau said. The fact that "we are doing better than these countries doesn't make a difference to someone who can't pay for their groceries."
It would have been interesting to hear, too, what Trudeau told Macron about the risky gambit the French president has made with his own future - forcing France into what our PM calls "decision mode" sooner rather than later.
There's not much chance Trudeau would follow that example, as things now stand. One gets the sense, listening to Liberals talk privately and publicly, that they feel they need all the time they can get before next fall - whether that's to get Canadians feeling better about the country or for some of that frustration feeding the Conservative poll numbers to burn itself out.
Trudeau's appearance on Power and Politics itself was unusual - unusual enough, in fact, that when word went out on Monday he was showing up on the set of the popular show for political junkies, there was a flurry of speculation about whether this was the announcement that the PM was quitting.
This is Ottawa's favourite guessing game right now and Trudeau, despite his protestations that he's staying on, doesn't seem to mind that he's keeping people guessing. Personally, I haven't ruled out any possibility still, though all I've heard from him are the reasons he is staying on to fight the next campaign.
All prime ministers say that, privately and publicly, up until the minute they announce they're leaving.
It did occur to me, though, that the mere fact of Trudeau doing that interview at mid-year (normally the prime minister does CBC interviews at year end) may be some kind of portent, whether that's a changed communication strategy or knocking off some items on a to-do list before summer.
Trudeau and the Liberals are doing their own polling, and they may well be seeing different numbers than the ones being churned out for public consumption. The latest Abacus poll shows Trudeau mired in deep unpopularity and the Liberals continuing to trail the Conservatives by 20 points. The latest Nanos polling has that gap a little smaller, at 14 percentage points.
But all of them seem to point to the reality that if Canadians were in "decision mode" right now, the Liberals would end up on the wrong side of that choice.
Cochrane asked Trudeau, usefully, what he wanted to accomplish with a fourth term that he hadn't yet done in his first three. The prime minister talked about "continuing to meet the moment," which is an acknowledgment that his government has had to do a lot of reacting to events since 2015.
Back in the early days, Liberals talked about "hope and hard work." What Trudeau told CBC on Monday, essentially, is that hope is hard work right now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes his way to a cabinet meeting on Parliament Hill on June 18, 2024 in Ottawa.

Subject: Canadians; Prime ministers

People: Trudeau, Justin

PM still thinks he can win an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-still-thinks-he-can-win-election/docview/3069493882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+still+thinks+he+can+win+an+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau is no doubt acutely aware of just how much Liberals need a morale boost right now.
Several weeks ago, the prime minister reportedly told his cabinet that winning in 2025 is definitely still a possibility, which many Liberals - not just ministers - are desperate to believe.
On Monday, in an exclusive interview with CBC's "Power and Politics," Trudeau said something similar in his conversation with host David Cochrane. Asked whether he could be the factor holding back Liberal fortunes in the next election, Trudeau said, "Canadians are not in decision mode right now."
He added: "What you tell a pollster, if they ever manage to reach you, is very different from the choice Canadians end up making in an election campaign."

But fundamentally it reflects what Trudeau sincerely believes - that when Canadians have a chance to really stare down the choice between this prime minister and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, they'll stick with a progressive government.

If there was any time to be a fly on the wall, that would be one of them: British prime minister Rishi Sunak fighting for his life in the current U.K. election; French president Emmanuel Macron in the midst of his own snap election to fight back against the rise of the right in recent European elections. No doubt they talked about who Joe Biden is up against in November's U.S. election.
I'm trying to imagine what Trudeau had to say about his own battles with Poilievre in this context. All the prime minister told Cochrane was that he came out of those conversations knowing he wasn't alone.
"Everywhere, people are struggling with high inflation, with cost-of-living issues, with interest rates, with housing challenges, child-care challenges, all these things," he said. The fact that "we are doing better than these countries doesn't make a difference to someone who can't pay for their groceries."
It would have been interesting to hear, too, what Trudeau told Macron about the risky gambit the French president has made with his own future - forcing France into what our PM calls "decision mode" sooner rather than later.
There's not much chance Trudeau would follow that example, as things now stand. His appearance on Power and Politics itself was unusual - unusual enough, in fact, that when word went out on Monday he was showing up on the set of the popular show for political junkies, there was a flurry of speculation about whether this was the announcement that the PM was quitting.
This is Ottawa's favourite guessing game right now and Trudeau, despite protestations that he's staying on, doesn't seem to mind that he's keeping people guessing. Personally, I haven't ruled out any possibility still, though all I've heard from him are the reasons he is staying on to fight the next campaign. All prime ministers say that, privately and publicly, up until the minute they announce they're leaving.
It did occur to me, though, that the mere fact of Trudeau doing that interview at mid-year (normally the prime minister does CBC interviews at year end) may be some kind of portent, whether that's a changed communication strategy or knocking off some items on a to-do list before summer.
Trudeau and the Liberals are doing their own polling, and they may well be seeing different numbers than the ones being churned out for public consumption. The latest Abacus poll shows Trudeau mired in deep unpopularity and the Liberals continuing to trail the Conservatives by 20 points. The latest Nanos polling has that gap a little smaller, at 14 percentage points.
But all of them seem to point to the reality that if Canadians were in "decision mode" right now, the Liberals would end up on the wrong side of that choice.
Cochrane asked Trudeau, usefully, what he wanted to accomplish with a fourth term that he hadn't yet done in his first three. The prime minister talked about "continuing to meet the moment," which is an acknowledgment that his government has had to do a lot of reacting to events since 2015.
Back in the early days, Liberals talked about "hope and hard work." What Trudeau told CBC on Monday, essentially, is that hope is hard work right now.
CAPTION: Justin Trudeau sincerely believes that when voters are faced with the choice to elect Pierre Poilievre, they'll stick with him, writes Susan Delacourt.

Document 31 of 500

Taxpayers could spend $6B more than firms on plants: Auto executive says watchdog missed factors in its tally of public subsidies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/taxpayers-could-spend-6b-more-than-firms-on/docview/3069488177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Taxpayers+could+spend+%246B+more+than+firms+on+plants%3A+Auto+executive+says+watchdog+missed+factors+in+its+tally+of+public+subsidies&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's EV-building strategy could cost taxpayers some $6 billion more than the private companies themselves are investing, the federal parliamentary budget watchdog says.
A new report released Tuesday by parliamentary budget officer Yves Giroux says a total of $46.12 billion in private investment across the nascent electric vehicle supply chain has been announced.
The PBO estimates the total public support exceeds that - pegging capital and operating subsidies offered by federal and provincial governments at up to $52.45 billion, which is $6.33 billion or 14 per cent higher.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said the parliamentary officer has inflated the figures by including $38.64 billion in federal and provincial production subsidies that are only a cost to government if the companies manufacture products in Canada. Plus, that economic activity creates jobs and generates new tax revenue, he said.
"I'd be embarrassed to put future production subsidies into this equation after having been schooled repeatedly on how they actually work," said Volpe, who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford on the EV push that is a cornerstone of Ontario's industrial strategy.
"These reports might be more useful if they tried consulting the industry they repeatedly report about," said Volpe.
The PBO looked at the announcements of 13 projects since 2020, mostly in Ontario - where Honda, Stellantis-LG and Volkswagen are among the largest - along with the Northvolt project in Quebec.
Those have drawn the biggest government subsidies to match U.S. incentives and to kick-start "clean energy" manufacturing in Canada.
Overall, Ottawa is paying the bigger public portion, which means provinces need a willing federal partner to ensure continuity in funding for scaling up an EV supply chain industry here to keep pace with the U.S.
"We estimate federal government support to be up to $31.4 billion (60 per cent) and provincial government support to be up to $21.1 billion (40 per cent)," said the report.
So far, the Trudeau government has placed big bets on the EV manufacturing future in Canada, while federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been, at best, noncommittal.
A spokesperson for Poilievre did not directly address the question Tuesday. Sebastian Skamski in an emailed reply criticized Trudeau for handing out "multi-billion dollar taxpayer funded payouts to giant, profitable, multinational companies with absolutely no guarantees for Canadian jobs."
It said Conservatives under Poilievre would make sure taxpayer dollars are "used wisely" and taxpayer-funded jobs are given to Canadians, not foreign replacement workers.
Poilievre has previously asked the PBO to analyze the costs to taxpayers, and been critical of projects that drew temporary foreign workers to set up complex technical machinery and processes.
The federal support comes via investment tax credits ($2.5 billion) at the front end of projects, and once plants are up and running and producing EV batteries and parts, via production subsidies, which are based on actual output.
The PBO estimates production subsidies will cost the federal government $24.36 billion, which he said includes foregone corporate income tax revenue.
The federal government is providing $4.4 billion in construction and other support as well.
On the provincial side, the watchdog said production subsidies will cost $14.28 billion, along with $6.7 billion in construction and other support.
The federal Liberal government and Ford's provincial Tories have argued they are making strategic investments to match incentives provided by U.S. President Joe Biden to lure EV auto and parts manufacturers stateside.
On Tuesday, the premier was in remote Greenstone, 1,200 kilometres northwest of Toronto, to announce partnerships with Animbiigoo Zaagi'igan Anishinaabek, Aroland First Nation, Ginoogaming First Nation and Long Lake #58 First Nation.
"I'm thrilled to announce that we've signed a new historic agreement with the four First Nations in the Greenstone area to support long-term growth and prosperity in northern Ontario," said Ford, who wants minerals mined in the north to be used in EV batteries manufactured in southern Ontario.
"This renewed partnership provides funding to drive economic development to build and upgrade the roads that improve access to critical mineral and resource development opportunities and to help connect First Nations communities to essential services," he said.
At Queen's Park, Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli's office said the PBO report "reinforces the significant long-term benefits the Stellantis-LG Energy Solutions and Volkswagen investments will bring to the province."
"It also acknowledges that it only analyzed a fraction of the benefits gained from the investments," said Vanessa De Matteis, Fedeli's press secretary.
"These projects will create tens of thousands of good-paying direct and indirect jobs and community infrastructure benefits, like improvements to roads, highways, and police and fire services, as we establish an end-to-end electric vehicle supply chain right here in Ontario."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 32 of 500

Trudeau minister accuses Tories of 'bullying' dental associations into rejecting national program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-minister-accuses-tories-bullying-dental/docview/3069619071/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+minister+accuses+Tories+of+%27bullying%27+dental+associations+into+rejecting+national+program&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are "bullying" Canada's dental associations into opposing the Trudeau government's national dental care plan, Health Minister Mark Holland said Tuesday.
He said the associations are suddenly "dealing with rabid Conservatives" who fear the success of the program would benefit the governing Liberals - and that's why they continue to oppose the plan despite having had their concerns addressed by the government.
"If you're a conservative party and you want to vote against this, OK," he said. "But calling around and bullying and creating all of this negativity, I think that's what the underlying force is."
Holland did not provide evidence for his claim.
A spokesperson for Poilievre denied the accusations, calling his comments "unhinged."
"It is Canada's dentists, the ones who deliver care to Canadians right across our country, who are calling out this NDP-Liberal government for delivering a broken dental program and breaking their promises to Canadians," Sebastian Skamski said in a statement.
The Canadian Dental Association and the Ontario Dental Association did not immediately reply to the Star's requests for comment on Holland's allegation.
Dental associations across the country have slammed the rollout of the plan, despite their support for its purpose, for what they say are significant administrative burdens in delivering it.
Holland previously said he didn't "even know what their issues are anymore" after the Star reported that the heads of all but one of the country's national, provincial and territorial dental associations had yet to sign on to the coverage program.
On Tuesday, he doubled down on his criticism as he upped the pressure on the dental associations to get on board with the plan.
"We've had constructive conversations with the dental associations, but it's time to move on," he said.
"If you ask the dental associations, 'What's the problem? Is it how long it takes to do it?' No. 'Is it how much you're getting paid?' No. 'Is it interaction with the program ...?' No."
Slightly more than 40 per cent of oral health professionals in Canada have signed up so far, and more than 200,000 seniors have already received care through the program, Holland said. So far, certain seniors are eligible, and dental coverage will expand to children and people with a disability tax credit on June 27.
The Liberal government has addressed administrative concerns by eliminating the contract to participate in the program effective July 8, implementing a 48-hour deadline for reimbursing dentists, and fixing privacy issues, leaving only minor concerns outstanding, he said.
But Holland suggested that because Conservatives are "calling these dental associations and screaming at them and getting angry at them," they remain opposed to the plan.
"If they can tell you, you know, what the big outstanding issue is, because I'd love to know, because I'd love to fix it," he said. "But I'm in a frustrated position now where I don't really understand what their issue is."
CAPTION: Health Minister Mark Holland responds to a question during a news conference in Ottawa on June 18, 2024.

Subject: Bullying; Dental insurance; Dentists

Business indexing term: Subject: Dentists

Robust race for mayor is a good omen for the future growth of Mississauga

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/robust-race-mayor-is-good-omen-future-growth/docview/3069199958/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Robust+race+for+mayor+is+a+good+omen+for+the+future+growth+of+Mississauga&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Robust race is a good omen
Good luck, Mississauga. With your new mayor, you're going to need it, June 14
On reading the above convoluted opinion from Star contributor Ajay Sharma on Mississauga's newly elected mayor Carolyn Parrish, I was puzzled about his motivation for making unfounded comments. This "university lecturer and researcher who studies municipal politics" could have done better.
It is a credit to Mississauga that, while Parrish was voted mayor, three other mayoralty candidates also secured a respectable number of votes - and they committed to support the victor. This is a good omen for the future growth of Mississauga.
As a long-term resident of Mississauga, I cannot agree with Sharma that "to say this is a victory for Mississauga and its future is simply not true." In 2014, Bonnie Crombie successfully replaced our iconic mayor "Hurricane Hazel." It is befitting that after 10 great years as mayor, "Brave" Bonnie is being replaced by "Confident Carolyn!"
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
How long it will take to become really sick of Pierre Poilievre?This isn't just another byelection in Toronto, June 15
Those in the riding of St. Paul's who are "sick, really sick of Justin Trudeau," ask yourselves how long it will take you to become really sick of Pierre Poilievre. The federal Conservatives will weaken, not advance the urgent battle to limit climate change, they will pull back on Truth and Reconciliation initiatives, which will eventually lead to renewed blockades and hostility, and they will weaken gun control, even though the scourge of antisemitism has recently included shots fired at a synagogue and Jewish community centre. Poilievre will, of course, not talk about any of this. Nor may voters recall just how well the Trudeau government shepherded Canada through the pandemic, giving us one of the most minimized death tolls in the developed world. Many may be in the mood to break up with their feminist boyfriend, but waiting in the wings is a wolf in sheep's clothing who squints when he looks you in the eyes. And did no one tell the dental hygienist who can't bear to look at Trudeau about the new dental care program?
Ron Charach, Toronto
No real alternative to Trudeau
Most people agree Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is past his best-buy date. But what are the alternatives? Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre has never had a real job outside politics. He proffers no policies just personal attacks and vacuous statements such as "common sense revolutions" a la Mike Harris, who destroyed education and other social supports. We look south and despair of common sense. The hard-working class listens to populist statements from politicians because they despair ... but these politicians do them no favours.
John Bullick, Mississauga
Capital gains tax boost is pure age discriminationThe rich say boosting the capital gains tax will hurt productivity, but it's just not true. Time to do a little myth-busting, June 15
Jim Stanford wrote about all the somewhat mythical benefits of the increased capital gains tax rates. I would say balderdash. The biggest flaw: the discriminatory effects on aging seniors. I have approximately 25 years to move things around such that the $250,000 floor/ceiling might never get reached. However, a senior in their 90s does not have nearly the same flexibility and that is pure age discrimination. There were no carve-outs in the law to handle such cases. That is not fair to the group that has collectively contributed more to the taxman than any other demographic over their lifespan. Fleecing granny is bad form to say the least. We get upset when we learn of scammers using the most senior among us as marks, we should be equally outraged when it is the federal government. The tax problems for those who die unexpectedly or quickly from a disease or accident are even worse. Senior citizens vote in the highest numbers, and I doubt they will ignore such an indignation.
William Blake Jacobs, North York
How can the public not see these officers in a bad light?Police officers involved in 401 crash declined interview, didn't submit notes: SIU, June 13
Four human beings, one a three-month-old baby boy, perished in a fiery, head-on collision after a wrong-way high-speed chase that was initiated by the Durham Police Force.
But two police officers have refused to provide their notes or to be interviewed by the SIU's investigative unit. These two officers, when on duty, drive vehicles stating their intention to "serve and protect." These two officers receive salaries from the public's taxes to perform that stated "serve and protect" duty.
By refusing to co-operate with the SIU's investigation into this horrific loss of innocent lives, can the public be blamed for viewing these officers as less than willing to "serve and protect," and more, rather than less, willing to conceal the facts pertaining to their possible irresponsible involvement in this tragedy?
Gary Kostin, Mississauga
Let's expand on the Halton program and soonThis housing experiment for older adults has changed lives. Here's how it could also save Ontario millions, June 15
This Halton Community Housing program sounds like a much-needed alternative to the "bleed you dry until you die" for profit retirement residences being built all across the GTA. Hopefully this type of housing program will be expanded into more areas soon.
Kim Jones, Penetanguishene
Thank Shania
Shania Twain performs, fires up the Edmonton crowd before Game 4 of the Stanley Cup Final, June 15
After musician Shania Twain's great performance in Edmonton, if the Oilers do come back to win the final series, they should call hockey's greatest trophy "The Shania Cup!" "It only goes UP! from here..."
Terry Toll, Campbell's Bay, Que.
CAPTION: In a hotly contested vote, Carolyn Parrish was elected mayor of Mississauga, replacing Bonnie Crombie who left to become leader of the Ontario Liberal party.

Document 34 of 500

Canada's EV strategy could cost taxpayers $6B more than private companies are investing, watchdog says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-ev-strategy-could-cost-taxpayers-6b-more/docview/3069199930/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+EV+strategy+could+cost+taxpayers+%246B+more+than+private+companies+are+investing%2C+watchdog+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada's EV-building strategy could cost taxpayers some $6 billion more than the private companies themselves are investing, the federal parliamentary budget watchdog says.
A new report released Tuesday by Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux says a total of $46.12 billion in private investment across the nascent electric vehicle supply chain has been announced.
The PBO estimates the total public support exceeds that - pegging capital and operating subsidies offered by federal and provincial governments at up to $52.45 billion, which is $6.33 billion or 14 per cent higher.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, said the parliamentary officer has inflated the figures by including $38.64 billion in federal and provincial production subsidies that are only a cost to government if the companies manufacture products in Canada.
Plus, that economic activity creates jobs and generates new tax revenue, he said.
"I'd be embarrassed to put future production subsidies into this equation after having been schooled repeatedly on how they actually work," said Volpe, who has worked closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford on the EV push that is a cornerstone of Ontario's industrial strategy.
"These reports might be more useful if they tried consulting the industry they repeatedly report about," said Volpe.
The PBO looked at the announcements of 13 projects since 2020, mostly in Ontario - where Honda, Stellantis-LG, and Volkswagen are among the largest - along with the Northvolt project in Quebec.
Those have drawn the biggest government subsidies to match U.S. incentives and to kick-start "clean energy" manufacturing in Canada.
Overall, Ottawa is paying the bigger public portion, which means provinces need a willing federal partner to ensure continuity in funding for scaling up an EV supply chain industry here to keep pace with the U.S.
"We estimate federal government support to be up to $31.4 billion (60 per cent) and provincial government support to be up to $21.1 billion (40 per cent)," said the report.
So far, the Trudeau government has placed big bets on the EV manufacturing future in Canada, while federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has been, at best, noncommittal.
Poilievre has previously asked the PBO to analyze the costs to taxpayers, and been critical of projects that drew temporary foreign workers to set up complex technical machinery and processes.
The federal support comes via investment tax credits ($2.5 billion) at the front end of projects, and once plants are up and running and producing EV batteries and parts, via production subsidies, which are based on actual output. The PBO estimates production subsidies will cost the federal government $24.36 billion, which he said includes foregone corporate income tax revenue.
The federal government is providing $4.4. billion in construction and other support as well.
On the provincial side, the watchdog said production subsidies will cost $14.28 billion, along with $6.7 billion in construction and other support.
The federal Liberal government and Ford's provincial Tories have argued they are making strategic investments to match incentives provided by U.S. President Joe Biden to lure EV auto and parts manufacturers stateside.
On Tuesday, the premier was in remote Greenstone, 1,200 km northwest of Toronto, to announce partnerships with Animbiigoo Zaagi'igan Anishinaabek, Aroland First Nation, Ginoogaming First Nation and Long Lake #58 First Nation.
"I'm thrilled to announce that we've signed a new historic agreement with the four First Nations in the Greenstone area to support long-term growth and prosperity in northern Ontario," said Ford, who wants minerals mined in the north to be used in EV batteries manufactured in southern Ontario.
"This renewed partnership provides funding to drive economic development to build and upgrade the roads that improve access to critical mineral and resource development opportunities and to help connect First Nations communities to essential services," he said.
At Queen's Park, Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli's office said the PBO report "reinforces the significant long-term benefits the Stellantis-LG Energy Solutions and Volkswagen investments will bring to the province."
"It also acknowledges that it only analyzed a fraction of the benefits gained from the investments," said Vanessa De Matteis, Fedeli's press secretary.
"These projects will create tens of thousands of good-paying direct and indirect jobs and community infrastructure benefits, like improvements to roads, highways, and police and fire services, as we establish an end-to-end electric vehicle supply chain right here in Ontario."
Editor's note: This is an updated version to clarify that the PBO report indicates government costs are higher than the announced investments by the companies.
CAPTION: Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Girouxestimatesthe total government support for EV manufacturing will be 14 per cent higher than announced.

Subject: Manufacturers; Supply chains; Economic development; Manufacturing; Tax revenues; Subsidies; Electric vehicles; Native North Americans; Roads & highways

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Parrish%27s+victory+a+win+for+status+quo%2C+Opinion%2C+June+14+Debunking+capital+gains+tax+myths%2C+Opinion%2C+June+15+Officers+refuse+to+participate+in+probe%2C+June+13+This+isn%27t+just+another+byelection%2C+June+15+An+alternative+to+isolation+and+anxiety%2C+June+15+Shania+Twain+performs%2C+fires+up+the+Edmonton+crowd+before+Game+4+of+the+Stanley+Cup+Final%2C+June+15&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Robust race
is a good omen
Parrish's victory a win for status quo, Opinion, June 14
On reading the above convoluted opinion from Star contributor Ajay Sharma on Mississauga's newly elected mayor Carolyn Parrish, I was puzzled about his motivation for making unfounded comments.
This "university lecturer and researcher who studies municipal politics" could have done better.
It is a credit to Mississauga that, while Parrish was voted mayor, three other mayoralty candidates also secured a respectable number of votes - and they committed to support the victor. This is a good omen for the future growth of Mississauga.
As a long-term resident of Mississauga, I cannot agree with Sharma that "to say this is a victory for Mississauga and its future is simply not true." In 2014, Bonnie Crombie successfully replaced our iconic mayor "Hurricane Hazel." It is befitting that after 10 great years as mayor, "Brave" Bonnie is being replaced by "Confident Carolyn!"
Rudy Fernandes, Mississauga
Capital gains tax boost is pure age discrimination
Debunking capital gains tax myths, Opinion, June 15
Jim Stanford wrote about all the somewhat mythical benefits of the increased capital gains tax rates. I would say balderdash. The biggest flaw: the discriminatory effects on aging seniors.
I have approximately 25 years to move things around such that the $250,000 floor/ceiling might never get reached. However, a senior in their 90s does not have nearly the same flexibility and that is pure age discrimination. There were no carve-outs in the law to handle such cases. That is not fair to the group that has collectively contributed more to the taxman than any other demographic over their lifespan. Fleecing granny is bad form to say the least.
We get upset when we learn of scammers using the most senior among us as marks, we should be equally outraged when it is the federal government.
The tax problems for those who die unexpectedly or quickly from a disease or accident are even worse. Senior citizens vote in the highest numbers, and I doubt they will ignore such an indignation.
William Blake Jacobs,
North York
How can we not see these officers in a bad light?
Officers refuse to participate in probe, June 13
Four human beings, one a three-month-old baby boy, perished in a fiery, head-on collision after a wrong-way high-speed chase that was initiated by the Durham Police Force.
But two police officers have refused to provide their notes or to be interviewed by the SIU's investigative unit.
These two officers, when on duty, drive vehicles stating their intention to "serve and protect." These two officers receive salaries from the public's taxes to perform that stated "serve and protect" duty.
By refusing to co-operate with the SIU's investigation into this horrific loss of innocent lives, can the public be blamed for viewing these officers as less than willing to "serve and protect," and more, rather than less, willing to conceal the facts pertaining to their possible irresponsible involvement in this tragedy?
Gary Kostin, Mississauga
How long will it take to be really sick of Poilievre?
This isn't just another
byelection, June 15
Those in the riding of St. Paul's who are "sick, really sick of Justin Trudeau," ask yourselves how long it will take you to become really sick of Pierre Poilievre.
The federal Conservatives will weaken, not advance the urgent battle to limit climate change, they will pull back on Truth and Reconciliation initiatives, which will eventually lead to renewed blockades and hostility, and they will weaken gun control, even though the scourge of antisemitism has recently included shots fired at a synagogue and Jewish community centre.
Poilievre will, of course, not talk about any of this.
Nor may voters recall just how well the Trudeau government shepherded Canada through the pandemic, giving us one of the most minimized death tolls in the developed world. Many may be in the mood to break up with their feminist boyfriend, but waiting in the wings is a wolf in sheep's clothing who squints when he looks you in the eyes.
And did no one tell the dental hygienist who can't bear to look at Trudeau about the new dental care program?
Ron Charach, Toronto
There's no real alternative to PM
Most people agree Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is past his best-buy date. But what are the alternatives?
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre has never had a real job outside politics.
He proffers no policies just personal attacks and vacuous statements such as "common sense revolutions" a la Mike Harris, who destroyed education and other social supports. We look south and despair of common sense.
The hard-working class listens to populist statements from politicians because they despair ... but these politicians do them no favours.
John Bullick, Mississauga
Let's expand on Halton program
An alternative to isolation and anxiety, June 15
This Halton Community Housing program sounds like a much-needed alternative to the "bleed you dry until you die" for-profit retirement residences being built all across the GTA. Hopefully this type of housing program will be expanded into more areas soon.
Kim Jones,
Penetanguishene, Ont.
Thank Shania
Shania Twain performs, fires up the Edmonton crowd before Game 4 of the Stanley Cup Final, June 15
After musician Shania Twain's great performance in Edmonton, if the Oilers do come back to win the final series, they should call hockey's greatest trophy "The Shania Cup!" "It only goes UP! from here."
Terry Toll, Campbell's Bay, Que.

Document 36 of 500

Hospital receives $1.2B cash infusion: Ottawa helping fund new facility to serve mainly First Nations people in northern Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hospital-receives-1-2b-cash-infusion/docview/3069039670/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hospital+receives+%241.2B+cash+infusion%3A+Ottawa+helping+fund+new+facility+to+serve+mainly+First+Nations+people+in+northern+Ontario&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada will provide funding to complete construction of a new hospital in northern Ontario that will primarily serve First Nations people following weeks of pressure from the federal NDP, First Nations leadership and the Ford government.
On Monday, the federal government announced $1.2 billion in funding for the Weeneebayko Area Health Authority (WAHA) Hospital Redevelopment Project. The new facility will replace the Weeneebayko General Hospital, located on Moose Factory Island on the south coast of James Bay.
"This is the completion of a project that has been long-standing and I'm very thrilled to be able to commit the $1.2 billion to Ontario so they can complete the project," Indigenous Services Canada Minister Patty Hajdu told reporters Monday in Ottawa.
Hajdu said the federal government has provided $200 million to the project since 2018.
However, when the federal budget was released in April, there was no mention of the money needed to complete the project, prompting calls for Ottawa to uphold its commitment to First Nations and see the project through.
On May 14, Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones posted a letter on X expressing the province's "disappointment" in the federal government's "unwillingness to support the redevelopment of WAHA."
And, in a letter sent to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau last month, federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh along with NDP MPs Charlie Angus and Lori Idlout criticized the government's decision "to walk away on a commitment to build a proper hospital in the James Bay region."
Hajdu said funding the project has always been a priority for the federal government but the challenge has been an issue of payment schedules with the project's contractors, Pomerleau Inc., and questions about "the nature of the payment contracts" to the Ontario government. The province has committed nearly $1.3 billion to the project.
"I think, for me, we really saw a change when we were able to engage directly with the contracting company directly. So that gave, I think, the federal government the clarity on the $1.2-billion ask," she said.
Last month, Moose Cree First Nation Chief Peter Wesley went to Ottawa to call on the federal government to follow through with a previous commitment to fund the new hospital development, stating the region currently lacks "quality and culturally appropriate health care."
"It seems like we always have to, as First Nations, we're seemingly always having to hold press conferences or new rallies and what not just to get our treaty partners to the table to recognize their obligations under the treaty," Wesley told the Star on Monday.
It was a "huge relief," he said, for the community and its neighbours to know that "the project's not going to stop and it's not going to go by the wayside."
Welsey said he has invited federal and provincial government partners to come to Moose Cree Nation and see the state of their current hospital.
Hajdu said the federal government will continue to provide funding to support the maintenance of the current building providing health-care services to the 12,000 people living in the isolated communities in the area.
"I know that we have a huge gap in this country that we have to try and close when it comes to infrastructure, health, housing for First Nations people and it's pleasing and it gives us hope when we see commitments like today happen," said Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak, who joined Hajdu during the Ottawa news conference.
The redevelopment project includes a culturally appropriate health campus in nearby Moosonee. Moose Factory Island will have a hospital, Elder care lodge along with a visitor hostel, staff accommodations and a new ambulatory care centre.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Joy SpearChief-Morris Toronto Star
