Document 37 of 500

Critics call out 'flip-flop' after Ford scraps digital wallet plans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/critics-call-out-flip-flop-after-ford-scraps/docview/3069039617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critics+call+out+%27flip-flop%27+after+Ford+scraps+digital+wallet+plans&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's failure to deliver on a promise to allow Ontario driver's licences and health cards in smartphone digital wallets is yet another PC policy "flip-flop," opponents charge.
As first revealed by the Star, the Progressive Conservatives have put the digital push on the "back burner" due to political and privacy concerns about adding government-issued identification to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets.
Tory insiders, speaking confidentially to discuss internal deliberations, conceded "our base hates the idea so it's a tough sell" - even though Ford himself had felt it would be a consumer-friendly change.
"This government can't seem to get anything right," NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Monday.
"It's all flip-flops and reversals with these guys," said Stiles, referring to other Tory policy U-turns such as cancelling the Greenbelt land swap that's now under investigation by the RCMP.
Liberal MPP Adil Shamji (Don Valley East) said abandoning the digital wallet suggests "Doug Ford's government is driven by the well-connected, rich insiders who pay for lobbyists and polls to set government policy.
"This policy reversal is just another example of governing by polling rather than principles," said Shamji.
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) said the episode speaks to the inability of the Tories to deliver on what matters to Ontarians.
"Whether it's buck-a-beer or digital driver's licenses, this government has a serious case of misplaced priorities," said Clancy. "If the premier wants to make life easier for Ontarians, I would suggest taking action to help the 2.3 million people without a family doctor, or to start building more homes that the average person can actually afford."
The Star reported Thursday the Tories were backing off on an initiative that seemed like a priority when then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy first announced it in October 2020.
Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement Minister Todd McCarthy's office said "the government currently does not have an update on its digital strategy but will provide new information as soon as it becomes available."
"The ministry continues to extend digital access to government services for those who wish to use them," said McCarthy's press secretary Matthew D'Amico.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Title: Critics call out 'flip-flop' after Ford scraps digital wallet plans

Trudeau government to fund completion of new hospital in northern Ontario

Welsey said he has invited federal and provincial government partners to come to Moose Cree Nation and see the state of their current hospital.
Hajdu said the federal government will continue to provide funding to support the maintenance of the current building providing health-care services to the 12,000 people living in the isolated communities in the area.
"I know that we have a huge gap in this country that we have to try and close when it comes to infrastructure, health, housing for First Nations people and it's pleasing and it gives us hope when we see commitments like today happen," said Assembly of First Nations National Chief Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak, who joined Hajdu during the Ottawa press conference.
The Weeneebayko General Hospital was first established in 1950 as a tuberculosis sanatorium, but in recent years had fallen into a state of disrepair.
The redevelopment project includes a culturally appropriate health campus in nearby Moosonee. Moose Factory Island will have a hospital, Elder care lodge along with a visitor hostel, staff accommodations and a new ambulatory care centre.
CAPTION: Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu rises during question period on June 17, 2024 in Ottawa.

Document 39 of 500

Campus grapples with lack of rooms for new students: Hundreds placed on wait-list for beds as Guelph accused of 'over-recruitment'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/campus-grapples-with-lack-rooms-new-students/docview/3068703097/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Campus+grapples+with+lack+of+rooms+for+new+students%3A+Hundreds+placed+on+wait-list+for+beds+as+Guelph+accused+of+%27over-recruitment%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Families are furious and accusing the University of Guelph of a "bait and switch" and a "cash grab" after the school boosted enrolment when there weren't enough residence beds, leaving more than 1,300 incoming first-year students scrambling to find a place to live.
The situation has caused such an uproar that even the mayor of Guelph has weighed in, accusing the university of "over-recruitment" and calling the situation "completely unacceptable" - but the school says it was caught off-guard after more students than expected accepted admission offers for this fall.
"I am quite disappointed with what may occur if solutions are not immediately found, not only for the impacts to first-year students but to the greater Guelph community with the planned over-recruitment tactic employed by the university ... the ripple effects will be considerable and compounding for our community for years to come," Mayor Cam Guthrie said in a public rebuke of the school.
"It is completely unacceptable."
The university said it does not guarantee a spot in residence for first-year students, but parents say school representatives - at the universities fair, during tours, and in offers of admission - repeatedly indicated there would be no problem accommodating everyone, and touted a housing report it commissioned that said the same.
"It feels almost like bait and switch," said James Clarke, whose daughter is near the bottom of the wait-list for a spot in residence.
While the university says it began communicating with families in April about possible bed shortages, dozens of parents who contacted the Star said it wasn't until last week that it was confirmed.
"They then came out at the beginning of June and said, 'Hey, we've got a waiting list of people up into the 1,300s,'" added Clarke. "Kids have already turned down other universities ... they have no other choice."
His wife, Dayna Stringer, travelled to Guelph with a real estate agent friend on Friday to find a place for their daughter, as a number of families worry about bidding wars and outrageous rents given that landlords have the upper hand.
A Toronto-area mother said she had to sign a 12-month lease at $1,750 a month for a room off campus - or $21,000 over the next year - with food and other costs on top of that. It's so far beyond what the family had budgeted that a trip planned for next year had to be cancelled.
By comparison, residence rooms at Guelph cost from $6,400 to $10,500 per school year, plus meal-plan fees. And residence spots are determined by lottery.
The university said that at this time last year, it had 5,143 acceptances, and this year that jumped to 7,849. Last year, however, some residence beds were left empty - though the year prior there was also a wait-list.
For this fall, the school has again leased a former hotel, is turning some two-person apartments into three, continues to look for extra space elsewhere, and says some beds may become available in January. Even then, it will be short about 1,000 beds.
Gwen Chapman, Guelph's provost and vice-president (academic), said the university did plan for higher enrolment numbers - in part to boost the school's budget amid stalled funding from the province on top of a tuition cut and freeze - but "by April we were starting to see a bit of the higher acceptance rate and recognized that we might not be able to accommodate everyone in residence ... so we communicated that quite clearly to students and their families."
She said the school will provide "a number of supports through our student experience team" to help first-year students adjust to living off-campus while making them feel a part of campus life.
"We certainly empathize with the situation," she said. "We recognize it's not what they wanted - it's not what we want. We would like to be able to accommodate all of the students who would like to be in residence, but this is where we are now."
Ontario Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, who represents the riding, said "what Guelph is trying to do is increase student enrolment to cover the fact that they're facing significant operating deficits" because of provincial underfunding.
The university, however, "has a responsibility to students and parents to figure out the accommodation and to be very open and transparent about what's available, what's not available," he added.
In a statement to the Star, provincial Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop said, "I fully expect the University of Guelph to be active in helping students and families find a solution."
Her spokesperson, Brooke Campbell, added that "this is a unique situation to the University of Guelph. Publicly assisted universities in Ontario are legally autonomous institutions with full responsibility for both academic and administrative matters."
Nate Broughton, who is a part of Guelph's student government but not speaking on behalf of it, said he's trying to create a task force to address the issues that the university will face with the influx of students.
The university "needs to prepare the campus for these students - there needs to be faculty hired to teach all of these students. All of these support services on campus ... these things need to really be bumped up to support this volume of students."
Chapman said the additional tuition revenue will be shared with faculties to help them serve larger numbers of students.
However, "the library is already packed, and it's hard to even find space. We're wondering about the quality of education, or are they now going to have many online classes, which will not work for my son," said Toronto mom Jenny Blaauw, who has a daughter at Guelph and a son set to start there this fall. He's about 650 on the wait-list.
"When you let in that many people, I don't have any confidence that they have the infrastructure to support it," she said.
For families who live farther afield, finding accommodation has become a huge end-of-year stress when they should be focusing on and celebrating high school graduation.
Sam Gaulin's daughter is 1,186 on the wait-list, and "we're 900 kilometres away - I'm in Kapuskasing, northern Ontario. We're freaking out quite a lot" about living accommodations, especially given her daughter's needs because of a medical condition.
"I'm genuinely very disappointed in their entire lack of communication; they knew this was coming," she said. "I'm not a rocket scientist, but if you have 1,000 rooms and you accept 2,000 kids, you know you're going to have a problem."
Her daughter made a last-minute application to another university and is waiting to hear back.
"This is just the most terrible feeling," added Deanna Nearing, whose daughter is 391 on the wait-list.
However, "a little bit of a silver lining, I guess, is that there are going to be so many students living off campus that it will be a massive group, and maybe there will be some way to advocate for them and have something positive," she added.
Stringer likened the search for a place to "an episode of 'The Hunger Games,'" and said the school "put its financial well-being over the students' well-being - this is what really gets me."
"This was a disaster," said parent Kim Ackerman. "If anything could come out of this year it is that next year, parents and students are not going through this - that they fix it, because this isn't how a kid should start university."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Parents & parenting; Families & family life; Students; Colleges & universities; College campuses; University admissions; Residence requirements

Document 40 of 500

Justin Trudeau's kitschy socks Doug Ford's ugly shoesSocial media's fashion Guy ranks Canadian politicians' style

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/justin-trudeaus-kitschy-socks-doug-fords-ugly/docview/3068702722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+kitschy+socks+Doug+Ford%27s+ugly+shoesSocial+media%27s+fashion+Guy+ranks+Canadian+politicians%27+style&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You might not know his name, but if you've scrolled through social media at all, you've probably seen Derek Guy's scathing takes on menswear and fashion.
Guy, who goes by the handle @dieworkwear, regularly takes to X, formerly Twitter, with sprawling threads full of critiques of menswear, often taking aim at politicians and public figures.
"Sometimes people ask me, do I think clothes make a difference in politics? I don't think anyone should be judged on clothes in terms of their inner character," Guy, a U.S.-based fashion blogger, told the Star. "I don't think you should ever think that someone is stupid or immoral or lazy or something based on their clothes."
But for politicians, clothes can end up distracting from what they are trying to say, or when they want to lay low, draw more attention to them, Guy said.
Here are five male Canadian politicians, ranked by Guy on their fashion sense:
1. Justin Trudeau:
'Just stop wearing the socks'
Even though the prime minister tops out Guy's list for best dressed, part of Trudeau's wardrobe draws Guy's ire.
It's the socks.
He, throughout various points in talking to the Star, called them: "kitschy," "ugly," and "ridiculous-looking."
It wasn't that long ago that Trudeau was knighted "the most stylish politician" by GQ and named in Vanity Fair's list of best-dressed men. But, as in his polling numbers, Trudeau has fallen from grace.
The socks started off as a playful way for the newly elected prime minster to signal that he was different from past politicians. His "sock diplomacy" (as labelled by the Guardian) would make headlines, like his pride-themed "Eid Mubarak" socks or Star Wars-themed socks for May 4.
"They are just such incredible poor taste," Guy said. "What they telegraph is, 'This is like my personality, I'm the guy with the quirky socks.'"
You can get away with the unconventional socks "when you're super, super old," Guy said - but, for Trudeau, "There's still life in this dude."
Suits are designed to point toward the face, from the V-shape of the suit jacket, up through the tie and shirt collar, Guy explained.
"The outfit should support you in the context of tailored clothing."
The socks, he said, "drag the eye downwards, and then you create two competing points of attention."
"It's a shame to see some beautiful suits ruined by somebody's ugly socks."
Besides the socks though, Guy said, "You can nitpick certain things, but his tailoring fits pretty well." However, the occasional navy sneaker paired with a suit, he said, "That's corny."
2. Jagmeet Singh:
Braving the double-breasted
It's no surprise that NDP leader Jagmeet Singh lands second on Guy's list. Singh has been known for his sharp fashion sense - even coming under fire when his taste gets a little expensive. According to Toronto Life, Singh prefers bespoke suiting made by his tailor in New Delhi.
Like Trudeau, Singh was applauded for his fashion sense in GQ Magazine in 2016, with a full-blown photo shoot in his iconic double-breasted suit.
"I love that he's willing to wear a double-breasted," Guy said, adding that too many men are afraid to venture into the more rare variation of the suit jacket.
In one shot of Singh in the House of Commons, Guy suspects Singh would be better suited "if the middle button (on his double-breasted coat) was lower just a little bit." The coat itself, he said, "is beautiful," but if the button was just a bit lower "you get more of a V-shaped taper and you elongate the lapel line."
One outfit of Singh's that Guy did not take as well was from the 2023 NDP Convention in Hamilton. In it, Singh wears a suit jacket, dressed down with a white shirt and (what appear to be) black jeans.
When men with tailored clothing try to dress down, "while you can sometimes wear a tailored jacket with jeans, it's always better to do it with a sport coat," he added.
It can take practice to spot the difference between a sport coat and a jacket, Guy said, so he recommends going into a store and buying a sport coat specifically for more casual occasions. Sport coats should be paired with jeans, but "don't wear them with lowrise skinny jeans," he warned, wear them with midrise, straight leg jeans to avoid a "weird silhouette."
3. Doug Ford: 'Ugly shoes'
Ontario Premier Doug Ford earned Guy's most neutral review, landing him in the middle of this list.
Ford wears reasonably "well-tailored suits," but the biggest recommendation Guy had for Ford: suspenders.
"If you wear belted trousers, or side tabs, your pants have a tendency to slip down throughout the day," Guy said, noting that Ford's hem was often collapsing.
"Many men nowadays are reluctant to wear suspenders, for good reason. They're kind of old-timey," he added, but it would stop the collapsing hem. Otherwise, he recommended Ford get his pants cuffed.
Ford's biggest fashion faux pas, however, was his "ugly shoes."
"He has very bad taste in shoes," Guy said. "The shoes look so bad because one, the design is ugly, but two, the leather is not very good."
"I would recommend that he just go to a traditional shoe store."
4. Pierre Poilievre: 'We all know
that you're a politician'
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made a conscious effort to rebrand his fashion sense last summer, dropping his glasses and suit-and-tie regiment for the occasional dressed down T-shirt and aviators.
"It's not very inspiring," Guy said as his first impression of Poilievre's dress.
"When I see this kind of stuff I just think rich dad who went to an expensive store and bought some clothes."
Poilievre gets points for wearing a sport coat to dress down, but his jackets also tend to be too small, according to Guy. "I can tell that if he fastened it, it would pull. I think he would look better in a larger jacket."
"I personally think he would look better in a fuller cut. It's a little more traditional ... (but) these kind of short jackets are often not flattering."
One look that didn't sit well with Guy was Poilievre's pairing of a Henley shirt with a sport coat at an "axe the tax" press conference in St. John's in October 2023.
"There's nothing wrong with wearing a Henley, but you want to keep Henleys to work wear - so pair it under a flannel shirt or a denim western shirt, trucker jackets," Guy recommended.
"It's essentially an old-timey form of underwear. Not something you pair with a sport coat."
Especially for dressing more casually, Guy said, a light blue button-up shirt or a long-sleeve polo would work better.
"I also just think that politicians should not try so hard to look dressed down," he added. "We all know that you're a politician. No one thinks that you're going out into the field and shovelling stuff with everybody else."
"You don't have to dress down with a Henley, because no one thinks that you're a working man. You're a politician."
5. John Tory: An 'odd choice'
If Guy has a poster-child for poorly dressed politicians, it's former Toronto mayor John Tory. He has repeatedly used a photo of Tory, taken at his resignation press conference, to illustrate what Guy considers a poor combination of a formal top, tight pants and sneakers.
"There's a lot going on here," Guy said of Tory's infamous outfit. "The thing that stands out most is the absurdity between the top and bottom half of his outfit."
The skinny jeans combined with the tailored jacket means "you see a very distinctive top block (and) a very distinctive bottom block."
Guy wasn't familiar with the context of the photo or the significance of the press conference (where Tory resigned as mayor after the Star revealed he had a relationship with a staffer).
As a Toronto mayor in his 60s, "wearing skinny jeans with dress sneakers is just a very odd choice, especially for an occasion where you're resigning for a scandal."
Bonus: David Rider, Toronto Star
senior politics reporter
In the photo that Guy often posts of Tory, the Star's senior politics reporter, David Rider, is pictured in the background intently taking notes. Rider joked on X that he is "sighing with relief that nobody is rating my clothes" every time the photo gets posted.
So, naturally, we asked Guy to rate Rider's outfit.
Guy said Rider should lose the red T-shirt in favour of a white shirt, or "if he wanted to really make more of a style statement" wear a white Henley. Rider's dress belt is also too thin and Guy said the longtime city hall bureau chief should wear a 1.5 inch belt, which is more casual, instead.
"But, he looks better, to me, than all of the other men that we previously discussed."
"As a rumpled fly on the wall," Rider said. "I dodged a bullet every time Guy and his followers eyed Tory's silhouette. Having now survived Guy's crosshairs, I am thrilled with a win for what I'll dub Reporter Utility Casual."
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Subject: Press conferences; Menswear; Clothing; Athletic shoes; Politics; Politicians

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 41 of 500

Shouldn't cyclists have to get a licence to ride in Toronto?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/shouldnt-cyclists-have-get-licence-ride-toronto/docview/3068702720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shouldn%27t+cyclists+have+to+get+a+licence+to+ride+in+Toronto%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cycling etiquette should be taught
I am an 85-year-old Toronto resident who walks, cycles and drives. I have been using my bike to shop on Danforth Avenue since I became a senior. I felt very safe prior to the introduction of the bike lanes, but they have allowed for speeding cyclists and motorized bikes. I choose the narrow back lanes behind the stores if they are available. I just returned from Cambridge, U.K., where I witnessed groups of young school age children being supervised and taught how to properly use the road and cycle lanes. This made me think about cycling education here. Shouldn't all cyclists have a licence to ride on public streets and insurance? Should cyclists carry identity and be able to prove they have read the relevant road legislation and know to follow the rules of the road? Should bikes have plates?
M. Noble, Toronto
Why do some cyclists believe that they can race along at excessive speeds on a shared walking/biking path? My husband and I often walk the path at Marie Curtis Park for exercise and enjoyment of nature. We also occasionally ride our bikes there too. This week, we were walking and a cyclist silently sped past without warning, nearly hitting us. This is a frequent occurrence. When we indicated to him that it would be nice to have been given warning with the bell, his response was, "No, I don't need a bell, you need to watch." The only way we could do that would be to walk backwards! Cyclists wanting respect need to show respect.
Rita Bearss, Etobicoke
Don't forget Walkerton
Ontario is a 'world leader' in wastewater surveillance for COVID. The province's decision to close testing sites will end that, experts say, June 5
Peril is right! Have Ford's Conservatives lost their minds with deliberate amnesia? Have they forgotten what happened the last time a Conservative government cut water testing in Walkerton, Ont.? At least seven people died. More than 2,300 became very sick, many were hospitalized and many had to go on dialysis and suffer for the rest of their lives. The Ford government is trying to solve a revenue problem of their own creation. Wastewater testing is the most efficient, low-cost way of gathering disease information. Cutting it is very short sighted, completely unacceptable and, in my opinion, wilfully negligent.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
Premier Doug Ford and his government are terminating the funding for the wastewater program, where test results were proven to be extremely helpful in the fight against the COVID-19 pandemic. There is indeed a bright side to the future potential suffering through the next pandemic - you will be able to buy beer at your local convenience store. Thank you, Premier Ford.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
We could all use some financial literacy training
Why do we need financial literacy in schools? Because too many adults can't answer these 10 essential questions, June 5
I have a confession. I am 81 years old, have an honours degree in commerce, spent 34 years working at a financial institution in various levels and have been retired for 26 years. I administer my own investment assets, am reasonably affluent and am self-sufficient. I took this test and got six out of 10 wrong. We need to teach our children financial literacy because knowing how to cash a cheque is no longer enough.
Don McMullin, Stoney Creek, Ont.
We need to ask hard questions
Is there still a place in the political landscape for reasoned argument and well thought out policy, or are Canadians happy to accept candidates for leadership based on a popularity contest among equally uninformed, misinformed and unqualified people appealing for voter support via promises of treats like "buck-a-beer" or the use of nonsensical slogans like "axe-the-tax?" They are treating us like children! We need to raise our expectations.
When a person running for a political position knocks on my door, I want to hear an intelligent explanation of policy positions, based on facts. We deserve legislators who are smart, very well educated, and well informed on the law and the constitution. Is that too much to ask? We are in a climate crisis right now. Anyone running in an election must also demonstrate a solid understanding of the science of climate change and be ready to offer proposals to address that.
Teresa Ganna Porter, Newmarket
We need to take care of ourselves
Pierre Poilievre's vision for Canada: Heaven for the very rich and squat for everyone else, June 13
Columnist Linda McQuaig raises alarm bells about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's vision of Canada. She asks if we want a "bare bones, U.S.-style safety net ... with the rest of the population forced to provide for themselves and their families in the marketplace?" Yes, that is what living in a democracy entails. Her vision is a socialist state that eventually decays to where families have no money and no government to help them. The only way a state can maintain a healthy social safety net is wealth creation, not the wealth redistribution she envisions. Recent data shows the effects of her vision, with Canada predicted to have slow per capita GDP growth. In addition, her call for more spending, including the recently introduced pharmacare and dental care programs, shows an inclination to prove Einstein's definition of insanity if she thinks they will turn out different than the current state of health care. Canadian families deserve the right to take care of themselves, with the government only intervening to help those who need it.
Alan McDonald, Trenton, Ont.
Shop in a mall, not online
I was a mall rat in my teens, but today our kids ask their parents to order stuff online for them. Amazon purchases are skyrocketing. This offers instant gratification, but with a huge carbon footprint. Shopping malls are not seeing the buyers they had in previous years, and that means our young teenagers are not getting their first jobs working in retail. Is the in-person retail market a thing of the past? Nope, if you look at shops on Don Mills. It has potential, as does the brand new mall at The Well. Shopping malls are not a thing of the past. We just need to advertise, reinvent and offer an experience versus a simple click on your phone. I am heading to The Well to shop!
Mary Hart, Toronto
CAPTION: Cyclists make their way along the Don Valley Parkway as part of the 2023 Mattamy Homes Bike for Brain Health event.

Document 42 of 500

Boxing ghosts of foreign foes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/boxing-ghosts-foreign-foes/docview/3068702715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Boxing+ghosts+of+foreign+foes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Eighty years ago, cipher clerk Igor Gouzenko walked out of the Soviet embassy in Ottawa with a briefcase full of documents and a plan to defect. When he finally got an audience with the RCMP, he revealed the most extensive espionage and influence campaign Canada had ever seen.
Gouzenko's defection spurred a series of investigations, a public inquiry, and a media frenzy. Revelations of how Moscow sought to steal secrets and infiltrate Ottawa gripped the nation and woke Ottawa up to new realities of the Cold War.
There was a time in this country when the government, having been infiltrated by Soviet spies, owned up to being duped. Facing pressure from Parliament, Ottawa named names, even when it was embarrassing.
That doesn't happen anymore. It's time for that to change.

Earlier this month, the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) released its report into foreign interference. The report revealed what our spy agencies have known for some time: Foreign governments have attempted to finance and sway our politicians in an effort to achieve their own geopolitical ends.
The specifics of those plots, however, remain classified, secret to all but a select few. There have been angry calls, from opposition politicians and the media, to release the names, but the Trudeau government, hiding behind secrecy rules, has so far refused.
All that obfuscation and secrecy has led to an escalation of mutually assured rhetoric. Columnists have warned of "traitor MPs." Sitting politicians have alleged journalists in the mainstream media are "on the take" for foreign powers. A pall, meanwhile, has been cast over all politicians with ties to other countries - particularly ethnically Chinese and Indian MPs. Another MP, themselves a second-generation immigrant, says it stokes fears that immigrant politicians have divided loyalties. "It's bull--," they said.
Over the past week, I've spoken to a half dozen staffers, organizers and members of parliament from both the Liberals and Conservatives, all of whom have significant experience working with communities targeted by foreign influence campaigns. Every single one is concerned that sitting Canadian politicians are, at the very least, too cosy with foreign governments. Several mentioned the same names, all off the record, again and again. But what I also heard was a sense of unease, a feeling like paranoia is prompting us to whip out treason allegations a little too quickly.

Last week, May was allowed to consult the classified edition of the report. (All party leaders willing to obtain top secret security clearance can see the full, unredacted version. Thus far, only Pierre Poilievre has refused.) When we spoke, May was careful not to reveal specifics about what she learned. What she did tell me is that there are "very few" current MPs named in the report. "I'm not concerned, at all, that I'm sitting with traitors," she said. NDP leader Jagmeet Singh had a different take after reading the report on Thursday, telling reporters: "I am more alarmed today than I was yesterday." There are "a number of MPs" who helped foreign governments to Canada's detriment," he continued, "and still others who benefited from foreign meddling."
So now we've got dueling perspectives from those who know. Those who don't know, meanwhile, are writing fiction. So we're left boxing ghosts, suspecting that every Chinese or Indian political activist might be a turncoat. What's more, at a time of declining trust in media, we've got an allegation that China is responsible for "direct engagement with Canadian media executives and journalists" but we've got no idea which media we ought to be looking at skeptically.
So what should we do? At the very least, it is time for a conversation about what is, and isn't, OK for a Canadian politician to do when it comes to interacting with foreign states and foreign actors.
When, for example, does consulting with foreign diplomats cross into "proactively provid(ing) confidential information," as NSICOP puts it? What should MPs who have good relations with a foreign government do when that government goes rogue and starts, say, ordering assassinations on Canadian soil? How can politicians tell the difference between community organizers - the kind crucial to every party in every election and leadership race in this country - and active proxies for a foreign government?

These are crucial questions with fuzzier answers than you might imagine. It is a largely unspoken reality that the network of foreign embassies, consulates and high commissions in Canada is there to dig up information and advance geopolitical agendas. That is not, necessarily, nefarious. Canadian missions abroad do the same thing.
But the work of these missions can also be malignant. Years ago, over pints in an Ottawa pub, Kirill Kalinin tried to convince me to run a hit job on Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland's grandfather. Kalinin, a staffer in the Russian embassy, was particularly active in making friends and influencing people in the capital. I always disclosed Kalinin's lobbying efforts, as did some of my colleagues - but his stories had a habit of popping up in left-wing blogs across the country. The government revoked Kalinin's credentials in 2018 and booted him from the capital.
The point is, the line between legitimate diplomacy and nefarious interference isn't always black and white, and the more heavily redacted reports we get, the harder it becomes for even the most informed Canadians to know what's what.
Consider one inscrutable, much redacted, section of the NSICOP report. It detailed "India's alleged interference in a Conservative Party of Canada leadership race." Some jumped at the idea that what was being described was India leaning a finger on the scale to help a particular candidate.
But reporting in the Indian community suggests the opposite is true, that the meddling was actually organized to hurt a particular candidate: Patrick Brown. Sources I spoke to confirmed that, in general, the Indian consulate actively opposed Brown's candidacy.
Baaz, a Substack-based news outlet for the Sikh and Punjabi diaspora, reported last year on a meeting between the Indian consulate in Toronto and one of the MPs backing Brown during the 2022 Conservative Party leadership race. The consulate made clear that Brown had betrayed Indian Prime Minister Nahendra Modi by aligning with Sikh separatists in Brampton, where he is mayor, and therefore "must be defeated in the leadership race, and that the MP should retract their support for him," Baaz reported.
We can't know whether that interaction is described within the NSICOP report. I asked Brown if he wanted to comment on the NSICOP report, he wrote back that he wasn't "interested in revisiting the federal leadership (race) of 2022."
But our politicians are more resilient to this meddling than we may give them credit for. Per Baaz, the MP approached by the Indian consulate, passed on the warning to Brown but opted not to act on the threat. A former staffer relayed a similar anecdote, where the Indian embassy contacted Brown - then leader of the Ontario Progressive Conservative party - to demand he disqualify a Sikh nomination candidate. Brown, multiple sources told me, declined.

Just because that effort was unsuccessful, though, doesn't mean future efforts are doomed to fail. As our country welcomes more people born abroad, and as illiberalism marches on worldwide, we need to be clear-eyed about the fact that governments will lean on their diaspora to try and sway our government. The only solution is transparency.
So what's the answer? First, shining a light on this kind of subterfuge is a defensive tactic in its own right. Any reports of this kind should be made public by default going forward. The government also needs to follow through on its plans for a foreign influence registry. We also need to put on the record the actual tactics being deployed. There's no reason we can't do that in a way that will still allow our spy agencies to operate
Perhaps even more critically, default transparency could push CSIS to be more careful about the intelligence it reports up the chain. Just because there is an intelligence assessment suggesting that an MP may have colluded with a foreign power doesn't mean it actually happened. But, as the old journalism adage goes, intelligence services need to show not tell. They need to reveal in detail, like their American and British partners regularly do, what kind of meddling is happening and with whom.
In the end, the only institution that can fix these problems is Parliament. In short, MPs have to start naming names. It's the best way to guard ourselves against meddling foreign powers and the only way the public will ever again be convinced to trust their elected representatives.
Justin Ling is a freelance
investigative journalist
based in Montreal.
CREDIT: Justin Ling Contributor Justin Ling is a freelance investigative journalist based in Montreal.;

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3068702684/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Province%27s+%27world+leader%27+status+at+risk%2C+June+6+How+financially+literate+are+you%3F%2C+June+8+Poilievre%27s+vision+cause+for+alarm%2C+McQuaig%2C+June+13&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cycling etiquette should be taught
I am an 85-year-old Toronto resident who walks, cycles and drives. I have been using my bike to shop on Danforth Avenue since I became a senior. I felt very safe prior to the introduction of the bike lanes, but they have allowed for speeding cyclists and motorized bikes. I choose the narrow back lanes behind the stores if they are available. I just returned from Cambridge, U.K., where I witnessed groups of young school age children being supervised and taught how to properly use the road and cycle lanes. This made me think about cycling education here. Shouldn't all cyclists have a licence to ride on public streets and insurance? Should cyclists carry identity and be able to prove they have read the relevant road legislation and know to follow the rules of the road? Should bikes have plates?
M. Noble, Toronto
Why do some cyclists believe that they can race along at excessive speeds on a shared walking/biking path? My husband and I often walk the path at Marie Curtis Park for exercise and enjoyment of nature. We also occasionally ride our bikes there too. This week, we were walking and a cyclist silently sped past without warning, nearly hitting us. This is a frequent occurrence. When we indicated to him that it would be nice to have been given warning with the bell, his response was, "No, I don't need a bell, you need to watch." The only way we could do that would be to walk backwards! Cyclists wanting respect need to show respect.
Rita Bearss, Etobicoke
Don't forget Walkerton
Province's 'world leader'
status at risk, June 6
Peril is right! Have Ford's Conservatives lost their minds with deliberate amnesia? Have they forgotten what happened the last time a Conservative government cut water testing in Walkerton, Ont.? At least seven people died.
More than 2,300 became very sick, many were hospitalized and many had to go on dialysis and suffer for the rest of their lives. The Ford government is trying to solve a revenue problem of their own creation. Wastewater testing is the most efficient, low-cost way of gathering disease information. Cutting it is very short sighted, completely unacceptable and, in my opinion, wilfully negligent.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
Premier Doug Ford and his government are terminating the funding for the wastewater program, where test results were proven to be extremely helpful in the fight against the COVID-19 pandemic. There is indeed a bright side to the future potential suffering through the next pandemic - you will be able to buy beer at your local convenience store. Thank you, Premier Ford.
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
We could all use some financial literacy training
How financially literate
are you?, June 8
I have a confession. I am 81 years old, have an honours degree in commerce, spent 34 years working at a financial institution in various levels and have been retired for 26 years. I administer my own investment assets, am reasonably affluent and am self-sufficient. I took this test and got six out of 10 wrong.
We need to teach our children financial literacy because knowing how to cash a cheque is no longer enough.
Don McMullin,
Stoney Creek, Ont.
We need to ask hard questions
Is there still a place in the political landscape for reasoned argument and well thought out policy, or are Canadians happy to accept candidates for leadership based on a popularity contest among equally uninformed, misinformed and unqualified people appealing for voter support via promises of treats like "buck-a-beer" or the use of nonsensical slogans like "axe the tax?" They are treating us like children! We need to raise our expectations.
When a person running for a political position knocks on my door, I want to hear an intelligent explanation of policy positions, based on facts. We deserve legislators who are smart, very well educated, and well informed on the law and the constitution. Is that too much to ask? We are in a climate crisis right now. Anyone running in an election must also demonstrate a solid understanding of the science of climate change and be ready to offer proposals to address that.
Teresa Ganna Porter,
Newmarket
We need to take care of ourselves
Poilievre's vision cause
for alarm, McQuaig, June 13
Columnist Linda McQuaig raises alarm bells about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's vision of Canada. She asks if we want a "bare bones, U.S.-style safety net ... with the rest of the population forced to provide for themselves and their families in the marketplace?" Yes, that is what living in a democracy entails. Her vision is a socialist state that eventually decays to where families have no money and no government to help them. The only way a state can maintain a healthy social safety net is wealth creation, not the wealth redistribution she envisions.
Recent data shows the effects of her vision, with Canada predicted to have slow per capita GDP growth. In addition, her call for more spending, including the recently introduced pharmacare and dental care programs, shows an inclination to prove Einstein's definition of insanity if she thinks they will turn out different than the current state of health care. Canadian families deserve the right to take care of themselves, with the government only intervening to help those who need it.
Alan McDonald, Trenton, Ont.
Shop in a mall, not online
I was a mall rat in my teens, but today our kids ask their parents to order stuff online for them. Amazon purchases are skyrocketing. This offers instant gratification, but with a huge carbon footprint. Shopping malls are not seeing the buyers they had in previous years, and that means our young teenagers are not getting their first jobs working in retail. Is the in-person retail market a thing of the past? Nope, if you look at Shops at Don Mills. It has potential, as does the brand new mall at the Well. Shopping malls are not a thing of the past. We just need to advertise, reinvent and offer an experience versus a simple click on your phone. I am heading to the Well to shop!
Mary Hart, Toronto

Document 44 of 500

Alcohol licence applications now open: Convenience stores could start selling beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink options in September

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alcohol-licence-applications-now-open/docview/3068702502/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alcohol+licence+applications+now+open%3A+Convenience+stores+could+start+selling+beer%2C+cider%2C+wine+and+ready-to-drink+options+in+September&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Corner stores can now apply for a licence to sell alcohol as part of what the Ontario government has described as "the largest expansion of consumer choice and convenience since the end of prohibition."
With a self-service online application, a $500 annual fee and approval - which could take up to six weeks - convenience stores will be able to start selling beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink options as of Sept. 5. Too late for a last-minute summer blowout but not soon enough for some who have been advocating for such a move since the mid-1980s.
"Our members look forward to participating in a modern and open beverage alcohol marketplace," said Anne Kothawala, president and CEO of the Convenience Industry Council of Canada. "We commend the (Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario) for being very helpful and transparent about the application process."
Stores have to be under 4,000 square feet to be eligible, as well as meet basic requirements around selling food products and training of staff. Licences will be granted by the AGCO.
With additional changes on the horizon for other types of retailers, the province has said that by October, every convenience, grocery and big-box store in the province that wants to sell alcohol will be able to do so. However, the ease of the process has raised concerns.
Daniel Myran, a physician and Canadian research chair in social accountability at the University of Ottawa, told the Star that key health points have not been well discussed or considered as part of the extensive and rapid rollout.
"The outlined retail licensing process has an extremely low barrier for entry and leaves no opportunity for community consultation or input," said Myran, who co-authored a paper in the Canadian Medical Association Journal last April warning that such an expansion will lead to greater alcohol use and harm.
Alcohol currently results in 17,000 deaths in Canada each year, according to the paper, which highlighted international evidence that suggests privately operated stores "confer greater potential risk than government-operated stores, owing to longer hours of sale and less attention to preventing sales to underage or intoxicated people."
Kothawala noted, however, that the government's decision to fast-track licences to convenience stores that already sell lottery tickets is proof of the "convenience industry's stellar track record as responsible retailers of age-gated products."
The government has said it expects 8,500 new stores will embrace the opportunity to sell alcohol, although Kothawala said the uptake from her members would depend on the outcome of ongoing discussions with the government over distribution, taxes and fees.
Here's what those convenience store owners will need to know and do starting Monday:
Who can apply?
Eligible convenience stores that have 4,000 square feet or less of enclosed retail floor space. Storage areas, stairs, washroom space and outdoor areas where products or services aren't offered to the public must be excluded from the measurement.
Do I have to sell food?
At least half of the retail space must be dedicated to selling food, which can include prepared items that may be eaten on-site. Stores have to offer at least five of the following types of products: canned foods, frozen foods, dry goods, fresh fruit, fresh vegetables, meat or meat alternatives, dairy or dairy alternatives, non-alcoholic beverages, baked goods and snack foods.
Who cannot apply?
Stores that don't meet the above food requirements; stores that publicly identify as a pharmacy (although a pharmacy can be located inside); retail space that is not fully enclosed, so no kiosks or stands; and stores located in First Nations communities that have decided not to allow alcohol retail stores.
Where do I apply?
Online, with the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario.
What will it cost?
The annual fee for a convenience store licence is $500.
What rules will I have to follow?
Alcohol products have to be on display in one aisle or area of the store. Inventory not for sale must be kept in a secure spot not accessible to the public. Stores can sell booze seven days a week but only between the hours of 7 a.m. and 11 p.m. and cannot offer samples. At least 20 per cent of beer, 20 per cent of ciders and 10 per cent of wine on display must be produced by small breweries, cideries and wineries and there are rules on the maximum alcohol content per type of beverage. Convenience stores, like all establishments in Ontario, cannot sell booze to anyone under the age of 19 or to anyone who appears intoxicated, and stores must post their licence in a conspicuous place as well as a warning that drinking alcohol during pregnancy can cause harm.
How long until I get approval?
If a convenience store owner already has an AGCO licence or registration because, for example, they sell lottery tickets, a licence to sell alcohol will likely be issued within days; new applicants, due to AGCO having to review eligibility, can expect to wait three to six weeks. Store owners will be notified of approval via email.
What happens after that?
Convenience stores are only able to sell alcohol purchased through a business account with the Liquor Control Board of Ontario. On approval of a licence, the AGCO notifies the LCBO which then connects with each store to guide them through the ordering process. The time it takes to order and receive product is six to eight weeks.
Will my staff need training?
Anyone who is handling alcohol products has to be 18 years or older and complete training. At the moment, the only AGCO-approved option is Smart Serve, which is done online and takes about four hours. The AGCO says it has launched an "open call" for liquor retail training programs and will make an announcement when that becomes available.
Anything else?
Convenience stores will be able to deliver products for a fee as long as the order comes from the store's inventory. However, once a customer is done with their bottles of wine or beer, they'll have to go elsewhere to return their empties: Convenience store licensees are not required to accept them or provide refunds under the Ontario Deposit Return program.
CAPTION: With additional changes on the horizon for other types of retailers, Doug Ford has said that by October, every convenience, grocery and big-box store in the province that wants to sell alcohol will be able to do so.Steve Russell Toronto Starfile photo
CREDIT: Janet Hurley Senior Writer

Document 45 of 500

LCBO workers deliver overwhelming vote to strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lcbo-workers-deliver-overwhelming-vote-strike/docview/3068590237/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+deliver+overwhelming+vote+to+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers have voted overwhelmingly in favour of a strike in a record vote on Saturday.
In its biggest strike vote ever, 8,060 LCBO members - 86 per cent of the union's membership - cast ballots and 97 per cent voted in favour of job action, according to the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents around 10,000 LCBO workers in retail stores and warehouses.
"This is our supermajority of LCBO workers in action," the bargaining team, chaired by Colleen MacLeod, said in a press release. "This record turnout will send a strong message to Doug Ford and the LCBO that we aren't backing down in this fight. We're fighting for a strong future for the LCBO."
The release said the bargaining team will return to the table this week and added, "So far the LCBO has refused to bargain. Now, we'll tell them (and Doug Ford) exactly what they need to offer to avoid strike action."
The bargaining team also added that they will provide an update on bargaining and their strike timeline on Monday at 7:30 p.m.
The OPSEU delivered a notice to bargain in January, before the LCBO's contract expired on March 31.
"We have hundreds of organizers across the province, in retail and warehouses, gearing up for what we know is going to be, a significant battle there," said OPSEU president J.P. Hornick said in January. "They also have their wage reopener after Bill 124 has been found, for the second time, to be unconstitutional."
Ontario's Bill 124, which capped public sector salary hikes to one per cent a year for three years, was in February found by Ontario's Court of Appeal to have violated collective bargaining rights for workers, including nurses and teachers.
Hornick had said a major goal for OPSEU is to fight back against Ford's efforts to privatize the LCBO and alcohol sales.
"What we've seen with Ford is these attempts to ... keep nibbling around the edges of all of the public services," Hornick said.
CAPTION: LCBO workers have overwhelmingly voted in favour of strike action in a record vote on Saturday.

Document 46 of 500

corrections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/corrections/docview/3068548867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=corrections&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A June 11 article about the high absenteeism rate at the Toronto District School Board reported an incorrect figure for the board's annual replacement cost to cover staff sick leaves in 2022-23. The replacement costs amounted to $131 million of the TDSB's $213 million overall sick leave cost, not $106 million as originally published in the board's report.
A June 14 opinion article about Mississauga electing Carolyn Parrish mayor contained errors. It said that Parrish is Mississauga's third mayor. In fact, she is the fifth mayor since Mississauga was incorporated as a city in 1974. In addition, the article incorrectly stated that in 2018 Premier Doug Ford used the notwithstanding clause to override a judicial decision that prevented him from reducing the size of city council. In fact, he threatened to use the clause.

Title: corrections

Document 47 of 500

Is Alberta ready for Nenshi's pop of orange?: Former Calgary mayor's bid for provincial NDP leadership has seen party memberships boom

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-alberta-ready-nenshis-pop-orange/docview/3068547677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Alberta+ready+for+Nenshi%27s+pop+of+orange%3F%3A+Former+Calgary+mayor%27s+bid+for+provincial+NDP+leadership+has+seen+party+memberships+boom&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In those times when a truck-sized pipe shatters, throttling the water supply to over a million people - let's say, in Calgary, where it happened this past week - it pays to have a few extra socks.
"I've managed to avoid doing laundry the entire time because I have an incredibly deep sock drawer, which is full of purple socks that people have given me over the years," says Naheed Nenshi, speaking from home in between campaign stops. Since the beginning of his political career over a decade ago, the former mayor of Calgary has made a point of dressing to match his best-of-both-worlds political philosophy, the purple socks and purple shirts and purple ties signifying the commingling of Liberal red and Conservative blue, and a wearer who has refused to swear allegiance to either.
But in recent months his sartorial struggles have expanded to adding a pop of orange to his purple. He searched the unusual colour combination online and ordered items to be delivered. Someone gave him a tie they found at Value Village. He asked a competitor's daughter, an accomplished beader, to craft a pair of custom cufflinks.
The orange, of course, represents his first foray into party politics as he seeks to replace Rachel Notley as the head of Alberta's NDP party. He entered the race in March following several years of political retirement - he's been consulting, public speaking and, he notes, making money - and months of local speculation. He announced weeks after his competitors but still landed firmly, most have assumed, squarely in the lead. Already his star power has helped rewire the party's base of support in the prairie province, with membership numbers exploding past 85,000 - making the party, Nenshi argues, one of the biggest political organizations in the country - and throwing up new nodes of support, particularly in Calgary and rural Alberta, far from the party's traditional Edmonton stronghold.
With a little under a week until the vote closes and the results are announced on June 22, Nenshi says that more than 60 per cent of those have already voted.
The race has landed on the national radar thanks, in large part, to Nenshi. The loquacious one-time business professor - not for nothing is his longtime slogan "politics in full sentences" - rocketed to prominence when he was first elected in 2010, first for being the first Muslim mayor of a major Canadian city, then for his unflappable stickhandling through the devastating floods of 2013 and his outspoken progressivism in a province usually pigeonholed as the opposite.
But it's also teeing up a reckoning on the future of prairie progressivism. While Alberta has long been stereotyped as a conservative bastion, the exact flavour of right-wing sentiment has changed considerably in recent years, with Danielle Smith more populist and combative than what came before.
Now it's the other side of the aisle that is a having a very public conversation about what it wants to be. The ballot question for many seems to be: is it more important to stick to the party roots, or put out a welcome mat for a wider swath of Albertans? Is this about ideology, or about taking on Smith?
But his campaign is not a clear slam dunk. Also in the race are three sitting members of the legislature; all women with more experience in the party and the provincial arena. Sarah Hoffman was a former health minister and deputy premier under Notley, Kathleen Ganley is a former justice minister and lawyer from Calgary, while Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse is a relative political newcomer, having been elected last year, but has held leadership roles in several Indigenous organizations.
There's no question that the spectre of Smith looms over the race. Nenshi has said repeatedly that he thinks Smith - one of the country's most right-wing leaders, with a sweeping agenda on everything from fighting Ottawa to restricting heath care for transgender youth - poses an "existential threat" to Alberta.
In a twist that, were it fictional, would seem too on the nose, they were once classmates at the University of Calgary in the early '90s. (The class was a mock first minister's conference; Nenshi was Quebec, Smith was the media. The professor? None other than the titan of Alberta politics and former premier Peter Lougheed.) It was a heady time at the dawn of the Reform Party when young politicos were clustering in Calgary, including Ezra Levant, Preston Manning and a "very quiet grad student" named Stephen Harper.
Nenshi remembers Smith as friendly, outgoing and much more politically mainstream, but argues her time as a columnist introduced her to more extreme views. Nowadays, neither would be accused of pulling their punches, and Nenshi was known during his time on council for veering into spats with colleagues. (He also recently raised eyebrows by comparing Smith's United Conservative Party (UCP) MLAs to "monkeys on the other side.")
When asked about whether he could get a little salty, Nenshi responded that, "you know, being a bit salty is me." He'll never be shy about calling out the UCP, he added, but there is a line. "I've never been in opposition before. And certainly the temptation in opposition, is just to fight all the time. And I think, though, that Albertans are also looking for an actual alternative, a positive alternative."
One of Nenshi's more unexpected defenders here has been Jeromy Farkas, a conservative-leaning former councillor who frequently, and publicly, butted heads with Nenshi as mayor. Farkas, who has no affiliation with the NDP, wrote a letter to the Calgary Herald to push back against arguments that Nenshi is not a team player.
"He can be frustrating, he has an ego, he really likes to be at the centre of things. He isn't quite as smart as he thinks," Farkas said in a call with the Star. "But he always made the time for me, he sat down with me at least once a month to break bread, to listen," he said. Farkas then lost the mayor race to succeed Nenshi, after which he says many of his "fair-weather friends" ran for the exit.
Nenshi, on the other hand, "had every reason to gloat," he says. "But he stepped up really meaningfully to be able to support me and everything that I wanted to do next."
The prospect of Nenshi unleashed on the provincial stage may also force the conservatives, who have never been fans, to up their game. "I think in the UCP, there's simultaneous salivation at the thought of finally getting the chance to beat him," says Farkas. "But I'd say that the more savvy campaigners also realize that he's a force to be reckoned with - as long as he can keep that ego in check."
Though Nenshi faces a different environment than what he might have been used to at Calgary City Hall. When he was first elected to office, Barack Obama was still in office, and the world hadn't yet grappled with the changes forced by a Trump presidency, a global pandemic or a rise in misinformation.
"The question that I asked myself over and over, when I jumped into this race is, 'is my style of politics out of style now? Am I completely out of fashion, in this very divisive world?'"
But he says he's been heartened by the sheer number of people who have bought memberships or turned out to hear his ideas.
The current toxicity has also encouraged him to dig his heels in deeper on some core principles. When he first won, he had mixed feelings about being lauded as Canada's first Muslim mayor, rather than for his ideas or his campaign, though he turned it into a story about a country that embraces pluralism.
"But I also think that multiculturalism, diversity and pluralism are under significant threat right now, and we have to speak out in favour of those issues, regardless of where we stand - and that's something I will continue to do."
In some ways, the race represents a coming of age for the Alberta NDP. Their last leadership race was a decade ago, when Notley, then one of just four NDP MLAs in the Alberta legislature, won with roughly 3,500 votes, in a competition largely overshadowed by the Progressive Conservative's own leadership race just weeks before. Of course, Notley, herself the daughter of a NDP leader, would go on to do what many thought impossible, leading the party to victory in 2015.
But part of the recipe for success was a conservative movement that had fractured into competing parties, allowing Notley's orange wave to come up the middle. After Jason Kenney stitched the two sides back together, the NDP were defeated four years later.
Math would seem to dictate that the party needs new members if they want to be competitive next time, which has raised the question ofwhether Nenshi's pop of orange represents a genuine ideological conversion. He first dipped a toe into party politics right before the last election with his first-ever endorsement, and while he threw his weight behind the NDP, it read a little lukewarm.
In an opinion piece at the time, he noted that he didn't think either Notley or Smith had been "particularly great" at being premier; dismissing the Notley years as a "lost opportunity" when it came to moving the needle on housing or mental health. But despite his criticisms of Notley's accomplishments, he also called her a "essentially a decent person who is trying to do the right thing." He does not feel the same way about Smith. And therein lies the key.
But Nenshi pushes back against the idea that appealing to more people means changing the party's DNA. Being an NDP believer on the prairies means being a bit more "pragmatic," as he puts it. Outsiders often seem surprised at the party's general approval of pipelines, for example. The local conservatives, Nenshi says, have abandoned the space occupied by the Albertans who aren't socially conservative, who like public services but don't want to waste money.
"My challenge now, if I am successful next week, is to help more Albertans see that the party is actually them, and that more Albertans need to see themselves in the NDP," he says. "It's not about changing the party."
CAPTION: Alberta NDP leadership candidate Naheed Nenshi's star power has helped rewire the party's base of support in the province, with membership numbers exploding past 85,000 and throwing up new nodes of support in Calgary and rural Alberta.Rachel Notley is endorsed by Nenshi in the run-up to the Alberta general election in May 2023.The last provincial leadership race was a decade ago, when Notley, then one of just four NDP MLAs in the Alberta legislature, won with roughly 3,500 votes.Jeff McIntosh The Canadian Press file photos
CREDIT: Alex Boyd Toronto Star

Document 48 of 500

LCBO workers deliver overwhelming vote to strike: Union points to a lack of bargaining and privatization as key issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lcbo-workers-deliver-overwhelming-vote-strike/docview/3068547567/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=LCBO+workers+deliver+overwhelming+vote+to+strike%3A+Union+points+to+a+lack+of+bargaining+and+privatization+as+key+issues&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LCBO workers have voted overwhelmingly in favour of a strike in a record vote on Saturday.
In its biggest strike vote ever, 8,060 LCBO members - 86 per cent of the union's membership - cast ballots and 97 per cent voted in favour of job action, according to the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents around 10,000 LCBO workers in retail stores and warehouses across the province.
"This is our supermajority of LCBO workers in action," the bargaining team, chaired by Colleen MacLeod, stated in a press release Saturday.
"This record turnout will send a strong message to Doug Ford and the LCBO that we aren't backing down in this fight. We're fighting for a strong future for the LCBO."
The release said the bargaining team will return to the table this week and added, "So far the LCBO has refused to bargain. Now, we'll tell them (and Doug Ford) exactly what they need to offer to avoid strike action."
The bargaining team also added that they will provide an update on bargaining and their strike timeline on Monday at 7:30 p.m.
The OPSEU delivered a notice to bargain in January, before the LCBO's contract expired on March 31.
"We have hundreds of organizers across the province, in retail and warehouses, gearing up for what we know is going to be, a significant battle there," said OPSEU president J.P. Hornick said in January.
"They also have their wage reopener after Bill 124 has been found, for the second time, to be unconstitutional."
Ontario's Bill 124, which capped public sector salary hikes to one per cent a year for three years, was in February found by Ontario's Court of Appeal to have violated collective bargaining rights for workers, including nurses and teachers.
Hornick had said a major goal for OPSEU is to fight back against Ford's efforts to privatize the LCBO and alcohol sales.
"What we've seen with Ford is these attempts to ... keep nibbling around the edges of all of the public services," Hornick said.
CREDIT: Abhiraj Lamba Toronto Star

Subject: Liquor; Collective bargaining

Business indexing term: Subject: Collective bargaining

Document 49 of 500

Toronto Star, Narwhal win top journalism prize for Greenbelt investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-star-narwhal-win-top-journalism-prize/docview/3068465804/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+Star%2C+Narwhal+win+top+journalism+prize+for+Greenbelt+investigation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Star and the Narwhal have been awarded the top journalism prize in Canada for their investigation into the Ontario government's now-reversed decision to open up the Greenbelt to development. Gov. Gen. Mary Simon presided over a ceremony at Rideau Hall on Friday naming the Greenbelt investigation as the recipient of the 2023 Michener Award for Public Service Journalism.
"This is an incredible honour and a testament to the lasting impact of dogged, focused reporting that holds power to account," said Anne Marie Owens, the Star's editor-in-chief.
The winning work was a collaborative effort involving Star reporters Noor Javed, Robert Benzie, Brendan Kennedy and Sheila Wang, as well as freelancer Charlie Pinkerton and Narwhal reporter Emma McIntosh. The 18-month project was overseen by investigations editor David Bruser and deputy investigations editor Jesse McLean.
"You present the facts and you inspire Canadians to ask questions and to hold our society to a higher standard," said Simon during her introductory remarks.
"The journalists and the newsrooms that support them deserve our thanks for taking on the tough issues, exposing wrongdoing, giving voice to the voiceless, and improving the quality of life for all Canadians," said Michener Awards Foundation vice-president James Baxter.
In 2022, the Star and the Narwhal broke the story about connections between developers who stood to benefit from having property they owned removed from the Greenbelt, a swath of land around the GTA that is protected from development. Their estimated profits would have topped $8.28 billion.
Throughout 2023, the Star and the Narwhal - both independently and together - continued to report on the story, revealing how developers had attended Premier Doug Ford's daughter's wedding; exposing a "gold rush" on Greenbelt land; and unmasking the so-called Mr. X, the well-connected consultant promised a $1-million bonus to get land out of the Greenbelt.
Following damning reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner, as well as the resignation of two cabinet ministers and two political staffers, Ford reversed his government's plans to open up the Greenbelt and apologized to Ontarians.
Speaking at the ceremony on Friday, Javed said this moment revealed the importance of journalism in the public interest.
"This was not just a win for our respective publications, but a win for journalism in itself. It reminded us of the power of public service journalism. It reminded us that good investigative journalism can still wake up a public that seems more and more detached from the work that we do. And it reminded us that together, the media and an engaged public can be a powerful force for change."
The Star and the Narwhal's work was selected from among six of the most important pieces of public service journalism in the country, published by the Globe and Mail, the Montreal Gazette and The Canadian Press, and broadcast by the CBC and Radio-Canada.
The Star and the Narwhal's Greenbelt coverage also won a National Newspaper Award and a Digital Publishing Award.
Established in 1971 by then governor general Roland Michener, the Michener Award honours outstanding and unbiased public service journalism. It is widely considered the top journalism honour in Canada. This is the Star's sixth Michener prize, the last being in 2013 for coverage of former mayor Rob Ford's crack-smoking scandal.
CAPTION: The Star and the Narwhal spent 18 months investigating the Ford government's now-reversed decision to open portions of Greenbelt land to development.

Subject: Journalism; Scandals; Awards & honors

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Star; NAICS: 513110

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 50 of 500

Naheed Nenshi has launched his political return. Is Alberta ready for him and his purple socks?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/naheed-nenshi-has-launched-his-political-return/docview/3068450457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Naheed+Nenshi+has+launched+his+political+return.+Is+Alberta+ready+for+him+and+his+purple+socks%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In those times when a truck-sized pipe shatters, throttling the water supply to over a million people - let's say, in Calgary, where it happened this week - it pays to have a few extra socks.
"I've managed to avoid doing laundry the entire time because I have an incredibly deep sock drawer, which is full of purple socks that people have given me over the years," says Naheed Nenshi, speaking from home in between campaign stops. Since the beginning of his political career over a decade ago, the former mayor of Calgary has made a point of dressing to match his best-of-both-worlds political philosophy, the purple socks and purple shirts and purple ties signifying the commingling of Liberal red and Conservative blue, and a wearer who has refused to swear allegiance to either.
But in recent months his sartorial struggles have expanded to adding a pop of orange to his purple. He searched the unusual colour combination online and ordered items to be delivered. Someone gave him a tie they found at Value Village. He asked a competitor's daughter, an accomplished beader, to craft a pair of custom cufflinks.
The orange, of course, represents his first foray into party politics as he seeks to replace Rachel Notley as the head of Alberta's NDP party.
