The loquacious one-time business professor - not for nothing is his longtime slogan "politics in full sentences" - rocketed to prominence when he was first elected in 2010, first for being the first Muslim mayor of a major Canadian city, then for his unflappable stickhandling through the devastating floods of 2013 and his outspoken progressivism in a province usually pigeonholed as the opposite.

Sarah Hoffman was a former health minister and deputy premier under Notley, Kathleen Ganley is a former justice minister and lawyer from Calgary, while Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse is a relative political newcomer, having been elected last year, but has held leadership roles in several Indigenous organizations.

In a twist that, were it fictional, would seem too on the nose, they were once classmates at the University of Calgary in the early '90s. (The class was a mock first minister's conference; Nenshi was Quebec, Smith was the media. The professor? None other than the titan of Alberta politics and former premier Peter Lougheed.) It was a heady time at the dawn of the Reform Party when young politicos were clustering in Calgary, including Ezra Levant, Preston Manning and a "very quiet grad student" named Stephen Harper.
Nenshi remembers Smith as friendly, outgoing and much more politically mainstream, but argues her time as a columnist introduced her to more extreme views. (He also recently raised eyebrows by comparing Smith's United Conservative Party (UCP) MLAs to "monkeys on the other side.")
When asked about whether he could get a little salty, Nenshi responded that, "you know, being a bit salty is me." He'll never be shy about calling out the UCP, he added, but there is a line. "I've never been in opposition before. And certainly the temptation in opposition, is just to fight all the time. And I think, though, that Albertans are also looking for an actual alternative, a positive alternative."
One of Nenshi's more unexpected defenders here has been Jeromy Farkas, a conservative-leaning former councillor who frequently, and publicly, butted heads with Nenshi as mayor. Farkas, who has no affiliation with the NDP, wrote a letter to the Calgary Herald to push back against arguments that Nenshi is not a team player.
"He can be frustrating, he has an ego, he really likes to be at the centre of things. He isn't quite as smart as he thinks," Farkas said in a call with the Star. "But he always made the time for me, he sat down with me at least once a month to break bread, to listen," he said. Farkas then lost the mayor race to succeed Nenshi, after which he says many of his "fair-weather friends" ran for the exit.
Nenshi, on the other hand, "had every reason to gloat," he says. "But he stepped up really meaningfully to be able to support me and everything that I wanted to do next."
The prospect of Nenshi unleashed on the provincial stage may also force the conservatives, who have never been fans, to up their game. "I think in the UCP, there's simultaneous salivation at the thought of finally getting the chance to beat him," says Farkas. "But I'd say that the more savvy campaigners also realize that he's a force to be reckoned with - as long as he can keep that ego in check."
Though Nenshi faces a different environment than what he might have been used to at Calgary city hall. When he was first elected to office, Barack Obama was still in office, and the world hadn't yet grappled with the changes forced by a Trump presidency, a global pandemic or a rise in misinformation.
"The question that I asked myself over and over, when I jumped into this race is, 'is my style of politics out of style now? Am I completely out of fashion, in this very divisive world?'" But he says he's been heartened by the sheer number of people who have bought memberships or turned out to hear his ideas.
The current toxicity has also encouraged him to dig his heels in deeper on some core principles. When he first won, he had mixed feelings about being lauded as Canada's first Muslim mayor, rather than for his ideas or his campaign, though he turned it into a story about a country that embraces pluralism.
"But I also think that multiculturalism, diversity and pluralism are under significant threat right now, and we have to speak out in favour of those issues, regardless of where we stand - and that's something I will continue to do."
In some ways, the race represents a coming of age for the Alberta NDP. Their last leadership race was a decade ago, when Notley, then one of just four NDP MLAs in the Alberta legislature, won with roughly 3,500 votes, in a competition largely overshadowed by the Progressive Conservative's own leadership race just weeks before. Of course, Notley, herself the daughter of a NDP leader, would go on to do what many thought impossible, leading the party to victory in 2015.
But part of the recipe for success was a conservative movement that had fractured into competing parties, allowing Notley's orange wave to come up the middle. After Jason Kenney stitched the two sides back together, the NDP were defeated four years later.
Math would seem to dictate that the party needs new members if they want to be competitive next time, which has raised the question of whether Nenshi's pop of orange represents a genuine ideological conversion. He first dipped a toe into party politics right before the last election with his first-ever endorsement, and while he threw his weight behind the NDP, it read a little lukewarm.
In an opinion piece at time, he noted that he didn't think either Notley or Smith had been "particularly great" at being premier; dismissing the Notley years as a "lost opportunity" when it came to moving the needle on housing or mental health. But despite his criticisms of Notley's accomplishments, he also called her a "essentially a decent person who is trying to do the right thing." He does not feel the same way about Smith. And therein lies the key.
But Nenshi pushes back against the idea that appealing to more people means changing the party's DNA. Being an NDP believer on the prairies means being a bit more "pragmatic," as he puts it. Outsiders often seem surprised at the party's general approval of pipelines, for example. The local conservatives, Nenshi says, have abandoned the space occupied by the Albertans who aren't socially conservative, who like public services but don't want to waste money.
"My challenge now, if I am successful next week, is to help more Albertans see that the party is actually them, and that more Albertans need to see themselves in the NDP," he says. "It's not about changing the party."
CAPTION: Alberta NDP leadership candidate Naheed Nenshi listens to a question during a leadership debate in Calgary, Alta., on May 11, 2024.

The traditional office is toast. Why corporations need to embrace hybrid and remote workplaces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/traditional-office-is-toast-why-corporations-need/docview/3068450456/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+traditional+office+is+toast.+Why+corporations+need+to+embrace+hybrid+and+remote+workplaces&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a source in the federal government leaked news that starting in September the number of days federal government workers would have to go into the office would increase to three days a week from two, Premier Doug Ford said: "Three days is a good start."
It's part of a broader push by some organizations to get people into the office for more days a week, such as UPS demanding all staff return to the office for the full Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 schedule.
Yet such top-down, full-time in the office mandates fly in the face of definitive proof that the traditional Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 is the worst of all worlds. It's based on a meta-analysis paper by researchers from Stanford University, University of Chicago, and the Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México on productivity in hybrid, remote, and in-office settings.
Hybrid work models, which combine remote and in-office activities, offer a balanced approach that has been shown to enhance both productivity and job satisfaction. This model allows for flexibility, enabling employees to work remotely while also capitalizing on the benefits of face-to-face interactions for certain types of work that benefit from direct engagement, such as brainstorming sessions and complex project discussions.
Research has consistently found that hybrid workers exhibit an increase in productivity of five per cent to 13 per cent and report greater job satisfaction. For example, one randomized control trial demonstrates that productivity in these settings is improved by about four per cent. Self-assessments by hybrid workers corroborate these findings, with three to 5 per cent increases in productivity. The international findings are similar, with positive reports from around the world.
Some employees can become disengaged
The picture for fully remote work is more complex. Using traditional, office-centric approaches to remote work introduce significant hurdles that can undermine efficiency. In such cases, communication difficulties and a lack of immediate feedback can slow down project progress, reduce collaboration, and diminish the quality of outputs.
Another major concern in remote work environments is the propensity for employees to become disengaged from their work tasks, if supervisors aren't using best practices and lack training in managing remote teams. The distractions of a home environment can lead to procrastination and a phenomenon colloquially known as "shirking from home." This issue is particularly acute for workers who may lack intrinsic motivation or whose tasks are boring.
For example, consider a study by economists from MIT and the University of California, Los Angeles observed two groups made up of 235 data-entry workers - one working from home, the other in the office - in Chennai, India. They hired these workers for eight weeks and compared the productivity of both groups. Their finding? Low-skilled contract workers hired for a two-month gig working from home proved 18 per cent less productive, based on net typing speed, than those working from the office.
By contrast, an earlier study by scholars at Stanford looked at 250 call centre employees of a sizable multinational corporation, split into two groups: those working remotely and those commuting to an office. Their prevailing assumption was that employees at home might indulge in leisure - sleeping or binge-watching television - instead of performing their duties. Contrary to these expectations, the researchers uncovered a startling 13 per cent surge in productivity among remote workers. The differences might stem from the fact that the latter study focused on permanent staff invested into their careers at the multinational company, compared to the short-term contract workers evaluated in the former study.
The evidence does show that fully remote work undermines collaboration and learning, unless you adopt best practices for remote work management. One study looked at software engineers at a Fortune 500 company with a main campus split across two buildings.
Remote work offers much better cost savings
Engineers stationed in the same building as their entire team benefitted from receiving 22 per cent more online feedback compared to those with teammates in different buildings. The researchers concluded that the lack of such feedback indicates that working remotely undermines collaboration and learning.
Still, no question that remote work offers much better cost savings. Remote employees require no office space and can be hired at lower wages. Specifically, consider FlexJobs' 2024 Work-From-Anywhere survey ofover 4,000 U.S. professionals. The findings revealed a significant willingness among workers to trade financial gain for geographic flexibility: in order to secure the ability to work from anywhere, 26 per cent of respondents would accept a five per cent reduction in salary, while 24 per cent indicated they would agree to a pay decrease ranging from 10 to 15 per cent. This data underscores a shift in priorities, where location independence increasingly outweighs traditional compensation structures in the professional landscape.
So overall, even with a more complicated picture of productivity, organizations may get a higher return on investment from remote workers. In other words, the reduction in productivity per employee - compared to hybrid work - would in most cases be overcome by the reduced cost of each employee. Of course, the negative impact on collaboration and learning needs to be kept in mind and addressed.
In any case, the debate is clearly between a flexible hybrid model or a fully remote model. The traditional office-centric model is toast. That's even before the fact that blanket return to office mandates, especially for full-time in-office work, harm productivity by decreasing employee engagement.
Hybrid work definitely offers higher productivity than office-centric work. Productivity in remote work is more nuanced and context-dependent, with negative implications for learning and collaboration, unless organizations adopt best practices - however, fully remote offers major cost savings.
Either hybrid or fully remote work is better than office-centric work, and there's never a reason to adopt a Monday-Friday, 9-5 way of working.
Gleb Tsipursky is CEO of the future-of-work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and a freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is based in Columbus, Ohio. Follow him on X: @Gleb_Tsipursky
CAPTION: Either hybrid or fully remote work is better than office-centric work, writes Gleb Tsipursky, and there's never a reason to adopt a Monday-Friday, 9-5 way of working.

Subject: Work at home; Best practice; Hybrid work; Job satisfaction; Collaboration; Cost control; Employees; Wages & salaries; Productivity

Business indexing term: Subject: Work at home Best practice Hybrid work Job satisfaction Cost control Employees Wages & salaries Productivity

Document 52 of 500

This isn't just another byelection in Toronto - it might reveal the future for Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre. Here's what's at stake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-isnt-just-another-byelection-toronto-might/docview/3068450412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+isn%27t+just+another+byelection+in+Toronto+-+it+might+reveal+the+future+for+Justin+Trudeau+and+Pierre+Poilievre.+Here%27s+what%27s+at+stake&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a fascinating political petri dish under the microscope by parties, pollsters and pundits alike.
Candidates and campaign volunteers will tell you what really matters to voters in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection is: housing, the high cost of groceries and staples, or how to tackle acts of rising antisemitism in a midtown riding that has the fifth largest Jewish population in the country.
But ask voters on the street, and they'll tell you what really matters is Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre.
It's a vague - or a visceral - feeling that the prime minister has overstayed his welcome or that they don't know if they should trust the opposition leader with the reins of power.
"I legitimately don't know yet," replied John Carthy, strolling with a friend on Yonge Street, when asked this week how he's leaning. "I would like to not vote for Trudeau. And I don't see a very good option, so that's the thing."
Many don't even know there's a byelection happening, yet advance voting for the June 24 byelection started June 14.
For Liberals and Conservatives, it's a head-to-head race where an outright loss or a steep drop in the Liberal vote share would mean essentially the same thing: embarrassment for Trudeau in fortress Toronto, and a taste of possible victory to come for Poilievre.
The Liberal and Conservative candidates have opened campaign offices right across the street from each other near Yonge and St. Clair.
Besides being pretty convenient as a hub from which to deploy volunteers, it's an in-your-face political flex.
They've called in as many resources as they can and are scrambling to identify party supporters to go vote. Turnout is always a challenge in byelections. In this one, it's crucial.
Incumbent governments often say byelections are meaningless - that what really counts is the general election result. And yet Prime Minister Trudeau has shown up at every riding where a byelection has been called since he's been in office.
What's different in this one is that the Liberal stronghold of the past three decades is clearly no longer the safe seat it was expected to be even just six or eight weeks ago.
National polls show a 20-point lead across the country for Poilievre's Conservatives. Abacus Data's latest poll, shared with the Star, shows if anything, Trudeau's approval numbers among decided voters dropped in the past week to 22 per cent - his lowest in the nine years since he took office in 2015 - while the Poilievre-led Conservatives are up two points, to 42 per cent.
So the question is whether Toronto-St. Paul's holds for the party led by an unpopular prime minister, or is an inflection point for Trudeau to contemplate leaving, as some have suggested.
For most of the past 30 years, the riding was Liberal, represented by Carolyn Bennett, a well-known physician and MP who stepped down in December after 26 years in the House of Commons.
The main candidates to replace her are all rookies, but they have political organizing experience or good connections.
Leslie Church, a lawyer and longtime senior Liberal aide who quit as Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff last fall to make her run in Toronto-St. Paul's, is a new face battling unsubtle taunts she's an outsider. An Alberta native and mother of three, she and her husband came to Toronto 22 years ago when she went to law school. They were closing a home purchase this week in the riding, because win or lose, Church told the Star, she was returning from Ottawa to live in Toronto after their kids finish the school year.
Church's main competition is Conservative Don Stewart, whose website boasts he and his two daughters "have been proud to call St. Paul's their home since 2016. His roots in the riding stretch back more than 100 years." Stewart, who has an engineering and business background, worked for a year with the firm owned by Jenni Byrne, Poilievre's key campaign adviser.
NDP candidate Amrit Parhar is a 31-year-old program director with the Institute for Change Leaders, who helps train and run collective action campaigns on social justice issues.
The Green Party candidate is Christian Cullis, a constituency organizer for city councillor Dianne Saxe.
The race is crowded. It has the dubious distinction of having the most candidates ever on a ballot. Elections Canada said 84 people have registered. They are mostly part of an organized effort to promote electoral reform that calls itself the Longest Ballot Committee. The elections agency has been forced to print a long two-column paper ballot.
So it's a compelling race even if the stakes weren't so high. And the political spin is dizzying.
Senior Liberal party officials, who spoke on condition they not be identified, said byelections are always chances for voters to send a message to incumbent governments, and that turnout in byelections is never great, so they do not take the result for granted.
The Conservatives say that's deliberate spin on the Liberals' part, to preposition what is certain to be a drastic drop in Liberal support relative to the last election and will end up being a harbinger for Trudeau's future.
A senior Conservative source, who the Star agreed not to identify, said the Liberals' move to increase taxes on capital gains will help the Tories with a certain segment of voters in the riding. It includes affluent Forest Hill and other well-off neighbourhoods. And the Liberals' attempt to "straddle both sides" of the Middle East issue may play well for Poilievre's party, which has taken a harder line in supporting Israel, and accuses Trudeau of "betraying" Canada's ally.
But the Conservative insider said the riding won't swing.
"There is a zero-per-cent chance we're winning this," the source said.
"I've seen the voter ID numbers. We're doing better than we ever have, but we never targeted this riding ... the party would never have put resources into it."
Rather, the Conservative said the result will be a cold shower for the Liberals, just how the sharp drop in Conservative vote share was in a 2013 byelection in Brandon-Souris, Manitoba. The party went from winning that by a 38-point spread in 2011 to winning it by a slim margin of two percentage points, ahead of the 2015 election where the Harper-led government lost to Trudeau's Liberals.
The senior Conservative said the Liberals' margin in this byelection won't be "that close," but it will clearly signal other GTA ridings are in play for the Conservatives, and make Liberals sweat.
"I think that what they're worried about is that this election happens, they go down 15 points in the polls or something, and their caucus members start to look at each other and say, yeah, we're f---ed."
More than a dozen cabinet ministers have gone into the riding to stump for Church. One told the Star she has a "50-50 chance" of winning. Another said the party will still win, but it is dangerously close. Some Liberals fear it could be lost outright.
The Liberals could be spinning that they're worried in order to ensure their supporters are motivated to get out . The Conservatives could be spinning that they expect to lose so Liberal supporters get complacent, and stay home.
What's clear is the fight's afoot. Poilievre, and his deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, have been on the hustings with Stewart, who appears to be winning the sign war in the riding.
What's also telling is that neither Church nor Stewart would agree to allow a Star reporter to accompany them as they went door-to-door to solicit votes.
When the reporter walked into Stewart's campaign office, the candidate spoke only for a couple of minutes, saying he had "no time" to talk, wouldn't let a reporter go door-knocking, and quickly left, heading into a back office.
Church, and her campaign staff, said it was a question of respecting "voter privacy" that they won't let reporters tag along, but she sat down for an interview where she acknowledged a fatigue with Trudeau at the door, saying because he is "front and centre on every issue, people know him."
It's an "entry point into conversations" where Church said she stresses what the Liberals "have done and how we've got more to do" to expand housing, daycare, and to improve safety around Jewish schools and synagogues.
"It's about pulling people back to remember why they have voted progressive or why they have voted Liberal as long as they have."
"I think the ballot question is who do you trust," Church said.
The trust question is definitely in play.
The NDP's Parhar, who campaigned Friday with NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, said in an interview they are focused on the affordability crisis and housing. She pitches the NDP as the only party that can be trusted to "fix" what it says are "failures" of the Trudeau government.
The Greens agree affordability and housing top the list of voter concerns, followed by antisemitism. Cullis campaign manager John Weston said of all the campaigns he's worked, this is "the most times where I've seen people crying at the door." He suggests voters are looking for alternatives to Trudeau and Poilievre.
For voters like Sharon, 45, a freelance contractor for a brand management company who works from home and gave only her first name, the byelection represents a tough choice.
"I voted Liberal almost my whole adult life," she said, but this election reminds her of when former Liberal Premier Kathleen Wynne was running against Doug Ford and the whole province was "so anti-Kathleen that you couldn't see the party."
She's become "a bit more curious" about the NDP, saying "I would never vote Conservative because I don't relate to the Conservative party. But I'm very on the fence right now in terms of who I would vote for."
"But I feel like if maybe Justin Trudeau backed away...?" she said, trailing off.
Another voter, who said she's a millennial mom, said she has no doubts.
"I don't care who it is, I'll just vote for the Conservatives because I just don't want Trudeau. I just hate him. I can't even look at his face. I absolutely cannot stand him. I want to leave Canada because of him," she said, to her friend's surprise.
"I'm a dental hygienist and my patients come in and talk about this stuff all the time and everyone seems to feel that it's time for a change. That's my read."
Voters can cast ballots Friday through Monday at advance polls, and election day is June 24.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.

Subject: Antisemitism; Polls & surveys; Electoral reform; Conservatism; Candidates; Voters; Prime ministers; Elections; Voter behavior

Document 53 of 500

Star wins top journalism award for Greenbelt probe: Co-investigation with the Narwhal gets Michener prize

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/star-wins-top-journalism-award-greenbelt-probe/docview/3068372611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Star+wins+top+journalism+award+for+Greenbelt+probe%3A+Co-investigation+with+the+Narwhal+gets+Michener+prize&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto Star and the Narwhal have been awarded the top journalism prize in Canada for their investigation into the Ontario government's now-reversed decision to open up the Greenbelt to development.
Gov. Gen. Mary Simon presided over a ceremony at Rideau Hall on Friday naming the Greenbelt investigation as the recipient of the 2023 Michener Award for Public Service Journalism.
"This is an incredible honour and a testament to the lasting impact of dogged, focused reporting that holds power to account," said Anne Marie Owens, the Star's editor-in-chief.
The winning work was a collaborative effort involving Star reporters Noor Javed, Robert Benzie, Brendan Kennedy and Sheila Wang, as well as freelancer Charlie Pinkerton and Narwhal reporter Emma McIntosh. The 18-month project was overseen by investigations editor David Bruser and deputy investigations editor Jesse McLean.
"You present the facts and you inspire Canadians to ask questions and to hold our society to a higher standard," said Simon during her introductory remarks.
"The journalists and the newsrooms that support them deserve our thanks for taking on the tough issues, exposing wrongdoing, giving voice to the voiceless, and improving the quality of life for all Canadians," said Michener Awards Foundation vice-president James Baxter.
In 2022, the Star and the Narwhal broke the story about connections between the Ford government and developers who stood to benefit from having property they owned removed from the Greenbelt, a swath of land around the GTA that is protected from development. Their estimated profits would have topped $8.28 billion.
Throughout 2023, the Star and the Narwhal - both independently and together - continued to report on the story, revealing how developers had attended Premier Doug Ford's daughter's wedding; exposing a "gold rush" on Greenbelt land; and unmasking the so-called Mr. X, the well-connected consultant promised a $1-million bonus to get land out of the Greenbelt.
Following damning reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner, as well as the resignation of two cabinet ministers and two political staffers, Ford reversed his government's plans to open up the Greenbelt and apologized to Ontarians.
Speaking at the ceremony on Friday, Javed said this moment revealed the importance of journalism in the public interest.
"This was not just a win for our respective publications, but a win for journalism in itself. It reminded us of the power of public service journalism. It reminded us that good investigative journalism can still wake up a public that seems more and more detached from the work that we do. And it reminded us that together, the media and an engaged public can be a powerful force for change."
The Star and the Narwhal's work was selected from among six of the most important pieces of public service journalism in the country, published by the Globe and Mail, the Montreal Gazette and The Canadian Press, and broadcast by the CBC and Radio-Canada.
The Star and the Narwhal's Greenbelt coverage also won a National Newspaper Award and a Digital Publishing Award.
Established in 1971 by then governor general Roland Michener, the Michener Award honours outstanding and unbiased public service journalism. It is widely considered the top journalism honour in Canada. This is the Star's sixth Michener prize, the last being in 2013 for coverage of former mayor Rob Ford's crack-smoking scandal.
CAPTION: Emma McIntosh, left, a reporter with the Narwhal, and Noor Javed, a reporter with the Toronto Star, react after they are announced as the winners of the 2023 Michener Award on Friday at Rideau Hall.PATRICK DOYLETHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Toronto Star

Document 54 of 500

This isn't just another byelection: Trudeau and Poilievre aren't on ballot, but results in Toronto-St. Paul's could reveal their futures

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/this-isnt-just-another-byelection/docview/3068372578/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+isn%27t+just+another+byelection%3A+Trudeau+and+Poilievre+aren%27t+on+ballot%2C+but+results+in+Toronto-St.+Paul%27s+could+reveal+their+futures&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a fascinating political petri dish under the microscope by parties, pollsters and pundits alike.
Candidates and campaign volunteers will tell you what really matters to voters in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection is: housing, the high cost of groceries and staples, or how to tackle acts of rising antisemitism in a midtown riding that has the fifth largest Jewish population in the country.
But ask voters on the street, and they'll tell you what really matters is Justin Trudeau and Pierre Poilievre.
It's a vague - or a visceral - feeling that the prime minister has overstayed his welcome or that they don't know if they should trust the opposition leader with the reins of power.
"I legitimately don't know yet," replied John Carthy, strolling with a friend on Yonge Street, when asked this week how he's leaning. "I would like to not vote for Trudeau. And I don't see a very good option, so that's the thing."
Many don't even know there's a byelection happening, yet advance voting for the June 24 byelection started June 14.
For Liberals and Conservatives, it's a head-to-head race where an outright loss or a steep drop in the Liberal vote share would mean essentially the same thing: embarrassment for Trudeau in fortress Toronto, and a taste of possible victory to come for Poilievre.
The Liberal and Conservative candidates have opened campaign offices right across the street from each other near Yonge and St. Clair.
Besides being pretty convenient as a hub from which to deploy volunteers, it's an in-your-face political flex.
They've called in as many resources as they can and are scrambling to identify party supporters to go vote. Turnout is always a challenge in byelections. In this one, it's crucial.
Incumbent governments often say byelections are meaningless - that what really counts is the general election result. And yet Prime Minister Trudeau has shown up at every riding where a byelection has been called since he's been in office.
What's different in this one is that the Liberal stronghold of the past three decades is clearly no longer the safe seat it was expected to be even just six or eight weeks ago.
National polls show a 20-point lead across the country for Poilievre's Conservatives. Abacus Data's latest poll, shared with the Star, shows if anything, Trudeau's approval numbers among decided voters dropped in the past week to 22 per cent - his lowest in the nine years since he took office in 2015 - while the Poilievre-led Conservatives are up two points, to 42 per cent.
So the question is whether Toronto-St. Paul's holds for the party led by an unpopular prime minister, or is an inflection point for Trudeau to contemplate leaving, as some have suggested.
For most of the past 30 years, the riding was Liberal, represented by Carolyn Bennett, a well-known physician and MP who stepped down in December after 26 years in the House of Commons.
The main candidates to replace her are all rookies, but they have political organizing experience or good connections.
Leslie Church, a lawyer and longtime senior Liberal aide who quit as Chrystia Freeland's chief of staff last fall to make her run in Toronto-St. Paul's, is a new face battling unsubtle taunts she's an outsider. An Alberta native and mother of three, she and her husband came to Toronto 22 years ago when she went to law school. They were closing a home purchase this week in the riding, because win or lose, Church told the Star, she was returning from Ottawa to live in Toronto after their kids finish the school year.
Church's main competition is Conservative Don Stewart, whose website boasts he and his two daughters "have been proud to call St. Paul's their home since 2016. His roots in the riding stretch back more than 100 years." Stewart, who has an engineering and business background, worked for a year with the firm owned by Jenni Byrne, Poilievre's key campaign adviser.
NDP candidate Amrit Parhar is a 31-year-old program director with the Institute for Change Leaders, who helps train and run collective action campaigns on social justice issues.
The Green Party candidate is Christian Cullis, a constituency organizer for city councillor Dianne Saxe.
The race is crowded. It has the dubious distinction of having the most candidates ever on a ballot. Elections Canada said 84 people have registered. They are mostly part of an organized effort to promote electoral reform that calls itself the Longest Ballot Committee. The elections agency has been forced to print a long two-column paper ballot.
So it's a compelling race even if the stakes weren't so high. And the political spin is dizzying.
Senior Liberal party officials, who spoke on condition they not be identified, said byelections are always chances for voters to send a message to incumbent governments, and that turnout in byelections is never great, so they do not take the result for granted.
The Conservatives say that's deliberate spin on the Liberals' part, to preposition what is certain to be a drastic drop in Liberal support relative to the last election and will end up being a harbinger for Trudeau's future.
A senior Conservative source, who the Star agreed not to identify, said the Liberals' move to increase taxes on capital gains will help the Tories with a certain segment of voters in the riding. It includes affluent Forest Hill and other well-off neighbourhoods. And the Liberals' attempt to "straddle both sides" of the Middle East issue may play well for Poilievre's party, which has taken a harder line in supporting Israel, and accuses Trudeau of "betraying" Canada's ally.
But the Conservative insider said the riding won't swing.
"There is a zero-per-cent chance we're winning this," the source said.
"I've seen the voter ID numbers. We're doing better than we ever have, but we never targeted this riding ... the party would never have put resources into it."
Rather, the Conservative said the result will be a cold shower for the Liberals, just how the sharp drop in Conservative vote share was in a 2013 byelection in Brandon-Souris, Manitoba. The party went from winning that by a 38-point spread in 2011 to winning it by a slim margin of two percentage points, ahead of the 2015 election where the Harper-led government lost to Trudeau's Liberals.
The senior Conservative said the Liberals' margin in this byelection won't be "that close," but it will clearly signal other GTA ridings are in play for the Conservatives, and make Liberals sweat.
"I think that what they're worried about is that this election happens, they go down 15 points in the polls or something, and their caucus members start to look at each other and say, yeah, we're f-ed."
More than a dozen cabinet ministers have gone into the riding to stump for Church. One told the Star she has a "50-50 chance" of winning. Another said the party will still win, but it is dangerously close. Some Liberals fear it could be lost outright.
The Liberals could be spinning that they're worried in order to ensure their supporters are motivated to get out. The Conservatives could be spinning that they expect to lose so Liberal supporters get complacent, and stay home.
What's clear is the fight's afoot. Poilievre, and his deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, have been on the hustings with Stewart, who appears to be winning the sign war in the riding.
What's also telling is that neither Church nor Stewart would agree to allow a Star reporter to accompany them as they went door-to-door to solicit votes.
When the reporter walked into Stewart's campaign office, the candidate spoke only for a couple of minutes, saying he had "no time" to talk, wouldn't let a reporter go door-knocking, and quickly left, heading into a back office.
Church, and her campaign staff, said it was a question of respecting "voter privacy" that they won't let reporters tag along, but she sat down for an interview where she acknowledged a fatigue with Trudeau at the door, saying because he is "front and centre on every issue, people know him."
It's an "entry point into conversations" where Church said she stresses what the Liberals "have done and how we've got more to do" to expand housing, daycare, and to improve safety around Jewish schools and synagogues.
"It's about pulling people back to remember why they have voted progressive or why they have voted Liberal as long as they have."
"I think the ballot question is who do you trust," Church said.
The trust question is definitely in play.
The NDP's Parhar, who campaigned Friday with NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, said in an interview they are focused on the affordability crisis and housing. She pitches the NDP as the only party that can be trusted to "fix" what it says are "failures" of the Trudeau government.
The Greens agree affordability and housing top the list of voter concerns, followed by antisemitism. Cullis campaign manager John Weston said of all the campaigns he's worked, this is "the most times where I've seen people crying at the door." He suggests voters are looking for alternatives to Trudeau and Poilievre.
For voters like Sharon, 45, a freelance contractor for a brand management company who works from home and gave only her first name, the byelection represents a tough choice.
"I voted Liberal almost my whole adult life," she said, but this election reminds her of when former Liberal Premier Kathleen Wynne was running against Doug Ford and the whole province was "so anti-Kathleen that you couldn't see the party."
She's become "a bit more curious" about the NDP, saying "I would never vote Conservative because I don't relate to the Conservative party. But I'm very on the fence right now in terms of who I would vote for."
"But I feel like if maybe Justin Trudeau backed away...?" she said, trailing off.
Another voter, who said she's a millennial mom, said she has no doubts.
"I don't care who it is, I'll just vote for the Conservatives because I just don't want Trudeau. I just hate him. I can't even look at his face. I absolutely cannot stand him. I want to leave Canada because of him," she said, to her friend's surprise.
"I'm a dental hygienist and my patients come in and talk about this stuff all the time and everyone seems to feel that it's time for a change. That's my read."
Voters can cast ballots Friday through Monday at advance polls, and election day is June 24.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau have both been campaigning for their party's candidate in the Toronto-St. Paul's federal byelection, a riding which has long been a Liberal stronghold.Sean KilpatrickTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Subject: Antisemitism; Conservatism; Electoral reform; Polls & surveys; Candidates; Voters; Prime ministers; Voter behavior; Elections

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 55 of 500

"It's bull----:" How Ottawa insiders really feel about Justin Trudeau's handling of the foreign interference probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bull-how-ottawa-insiders-really-feel-about-justin/docview/3068370883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%26quot%3BIt%27s+bull----%3A%26quot%3B+How+Ottawa+insiders+really+feel+about+Justin+Trudeau%27s+handling+of+the+foreign+interference+probe&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Eighty years ago, cipher clerk Igor Gouzenko walked out of the Soviet embassy in Ottawa with a briefcase full of documents and a plan to defect. When he finally got an audience with the RCMP, he revealed the most extensive espionage and influence campaign Canada had ever seen.
Gouzenko's defection spurred a series of investigations, a public inquiry, and a media frenzy. Revelations of how Moscow sought to steal secrets and infiltrate Ottawa gripped the nation and woke Ottawa up to new realities of the Cold War.
There was a time in this country when the government, having been infiltrated by Soviet spies, owned up to being duped. Facing pressure from Parliament, Ottawa named names, even when it was embarrassing.
That doesn't happen anymore. It's time for that to change.
'Traitor MPs' and journalists 'on the take'
Earlier this month, the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians (NSICOP) released its report into foreign interference. The report revealed what our spy agencies have known for some time: Foreign governments have attempted to finance and sway our politicians in an effort to achieve their own geopolitical ends.
The specifics of those plots, however, remain classified. The names of the implicated politicians, the exact races affected, even some of the countries responsible for the covert dirty tricks are still secret to all but a select few. There have been angry calls, from opposition politicians and the media, to release the names, but the Trudeau government, hiding behind secrecy rules, has so far refused.
All that obfuscation and secrecy has led to an escalation of mutually assured rhetoric. Columnists have warned of "traitor MPs." Sitting politicians have alleged journalists in the mainstream media are "on the take" for foreign powers. A pall, meanwhile, has been cast over all politicians with ties to other countries - particularly ethnically Chinese and Indian MPs. Green Party leader Elizabeth May calls it the "Joseph R. McCarthy look under your desk for a traitor MP contest." Another MP, themselves a second-generation immigrant, says it stokes fears that immigrant politicians have divided loyalties. "It's bull----," they said.
Over the past week I've spoken to a half dozen staffers, organizers, and members of parliament from both the Liberals and Conservatives, all of whom have significant experience working with communities targeted by foreign influence campaigns. Every single one is concerned that sitting Canadian politicians are, at the very least, too cozy with foreign governments. Several mentioned the same names, all off the record, again and again. But what I also heard was a sense of unease, a feeling like paranoia is prompting us to whip out treason allegations a little too quickly.
A little light treason, between friends
Last week, May was allowed to consult the classified edition of the report. (All party leaders willing to obtain top secret security clearance can see the full, unredacted version. Thus far, only Pierre Poilievre has refused.) When we spoke, May was careful not to reveal specifics about what she learned. What she did tell me is that there are "very few" current MPs named in the report. "I'm not concerned, at all, that I'm sitting with traitors," she said. NDP leader Jagmeet Singh had a different take after reading the report on Thursday, telling reporters: "I am more alarmed today than I was yesterday." There are "a number of MPs" who helped foreign governments to Canada's detriment," he continued, "and still others who benefited from foreign meddling."
So now we've got dueling perspectives from those who know. Those who don't know, meanwhile, are writing fiction. So we're left boxing ghosts, suspecting that every Chinese or Indian political activist might be a turncoat. What's more, at a time of declining trust in media, we've got an allegation that China is responsible for "direct engagement with Canadian media executives and journalists" but we've got no idea which media we ought to be looking at skeptically.
So what should we do? At the very least, it is time for a conversation about what is, and isn't, OK for a Canadian politician to do when it comes to interacting with foreign states and foreign actors.
When, for example, does consulting with foreign diplomats cross into "proactively provid[ing] confidential information," as NSICOP puts it? What should MPs who have good relations with a foreign government do when that government goes rogue and starts, say, ordering assassinations on Canadian soil? How can politicians tell the difference between community organizers - the kind crucial to every party in every election and leadership race in this country - and active proxies for a foreign government?
Diplomats and dirty tricks
These are crucial questions with fuzzier answers than you might imagine. It is a largely-unspoken reality that the network of foreign embassies, consulates and high commissions in Canada is there to dig up information and advance geopolitical agendas. That is not, necessarily, nefarious. Canadian missions abroad do the same thing.
Last year, a diplomat from a friendly nation asked me to breakfast, hoping I could help their country's government make sense of Ottawa's Indo-Pacific Strategy. I happily offered the same insights I would have offered publicly: The strategy is an incomprehensible mess. This kind of diplomacy is routine, and often mutually helpful.
But the work of these missions can also be malignant. Years ago, over pints in an Ottawa pub, Kirill Kalinin tried to convince me to run a hit job on Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland's grandfather. Kalinin, a staffer in the Russian embassy, was particularly active in making friends and influencing people in the capital. I always disclosed Kalinin's lobbying efforts, as did some of my colleagues - but his stories had a habit of popping up in left-wing blogs across the country. The government revoked Kalinin's credentials in 2018 and booted him from the capital.
The point is, the line between legitimate diplomacy and nefarious interference isn't always black and white, and the more heavily-redacted reports we get, the harder it becomes for even the most informed Canadians to know what's what.
Consider one inscrutable, much redacted, section of the NSICOP report. It detailed "India's alleged interference in a Conservative Party of Canada leadership race." Some jumped at the idea that what was being described was India leaning a finger on the scale to help a particular candidate.
But reporting in the Indian community suggests the opposite is true, that the meddling was actually organized to hurt a particular candidate: Patrick Brown. Sources I spoke to confirmed that, in general, the Indian consulate actively opposed Brown's candidacy.
Baaz, a Substack-based news outlet for the Sikh and Punjabi diaspora, reported last year on a meeting between the Indian consulate in Toronto and one of the MPs backing Brown during the 2022 Conservative Party leadership race. The consulate made clear that Brown had betrayed Indian Prime Minister Nahendra Modi by aligning with Sikh separatists in Brampton, where he is mayor, and therefore "must be defeated in the leadership race, and that the MP should retract their support for him," Baaz reported.
We can't know whether that interaction is described within the NSICOP report. I asked Brown if he wanted to comment on the NSICOP report, he wrote back that he wasn't "interested in revisiting the federal leadership [race] of 2022."
But our politicians are more resilient to this meddling than we may give them credit for. Per Baaz, the MP approached by the Indian consulate passed on the warning to Brown but opted not to act on the threat. Brown was ultimately disqualified from the race for, seemingly, unrelated reasons. A former staffer relayed a similar anecdote, where the Indian embassy contacted Brown - then leader of the Ontario Progressive Conservative Party - to demand he disqualify a Sikh nomination candidate. Brown, multiple sources told me, declined.
Transparency above all
Just because that effort was unsuccessful, though, doesn't mean future efforts are doomed to fail. As our country welcomes more people born abroad, and as illiberalism marches on worldwide, we need to be clear-eyed about the fact that governments will lean on their diaspora to try and sway our government. The only solution is transparency.
So what's the answer? First, shining a light on this kind of subterfuge is a defensive tactic in its own right. Any reports of this kind should be made public by default going forward. The government also needs to follow through on its plans for a foreign influence registry - a system to track agents of foreign powers operating in Canada. We also need to put on the record the actual tactics being deployed. There's no reason we can't do that in a way that will still allow our spy agencies to operate
Perhaps even more critically, default transparency could push CSIS to be more careful about the intelligence it reports up the chain. One ex-staffer told me that they reviewed intelligence about foreign meddling that was just "garbage." Just because there is an intelligence assessment suggesting that an MP may have colluded with a foreign power doesn't mean it actually happened.
But, as the old journalism adage goes, intelligence services need to show not tell. They need to reveal in detail, like their American and British partners regularly do, what kind of meddling is happening and with whom.
In the end, the only institution that can fix these problems is Parliament. MPs need to tackle this themselves. In short, they have to start naming names. It's the best wayto guard ourselves against meddling foreign powers and the only way the public will ever again be convinced to trust their elected representatives.
CAPTION: A blockbuster report on foreign interference by the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, chaired byLiberal MP David McGuinty, has cast a pall over Parliament Hill.
CREDIT: Justin Ling Contributor

Document 56 of 500

Business case to move science centre cost nearly $1 million: Critics decry price tag as an expensive sham to justify private spa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/business-case-move-science-centre-cost-nearly-1/docview/3068369327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Business+case+to+move+science+centre+cost+nearly+%241+million%3A+Critics+decry+price+tag+as+an+expensive+sham+to+justify+private+spa&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario awarded nearly $1 million in contracts to various companies to write a business case on moving the Ontario Science Centre to Ontario Place on Toronto's waterfront, The Canadian Press has learned.
The $925,075 in contracts, an amount disclosed through a freedom-of-information request, is a fraction of the province's overall $215-billion budget.
However, it is still far too large for a report that seemed designed to give the Progressive Conservatives cover for a deal with European company Therme to build a privately owned spa at Ontario Place as it redevelops the waterfront attraction, critics said.
"Spending a million dollars to cook up a sham business case is ridiculous beyond words," NDP Leader Marit Stiles wrote in a statement. "There is more than enough evidence that the business case was nonsense and simply a justification to build a publicly funded mega parking lot for Therme."
Liberal critic Adil Shamji, whose riding is home to the science centre, said it is "outrageous" that Premier Doug Ford spent $1 million of public money on consultants "to tell him what he wanted to hear."
"Doug Ford was always going to find a way to move forward with his nonsensical plan to relocate the Ontario Science Centre," Shamji wrote in a statement. "Blowing $1 million of taxpayer money on consultants was just a way to make his business case appear more legitimate."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said spending that much on the business case doesn't make sense.
"It's a really unjustifiable expense for an unjustifiable decision right now," he said.
Stiles, Schreiner and many others have been fighting Therme's waterpark and spa planned for Ontario Place, and the business case suggested that relocating the Ontario Science Centre there from its current location in east Toronto could help temper that criticism.
"Securing a publicly owned cultural anchor, such as the OSC, could be an important addition to counter negative perceptions of the commercialization and privatization of this unique waterfront public asset," the consultants wrote in the business case.
A report in December from the auditor general found that a proposal in the spring of 2023 to government decision makers to relocate the science centre, including the business case, noted that a "sitewide parking solution" was needed to meet Ontario's lease obligations with Therme.
It proposed that new parking be integrated with the new science centre building "in order to dispel public/stakeholder concerns relating to cost and impact on the environment," the auditor wrote.
The business case itself was "incomplete," the auditor general said, including not factoring in financing or incremental parking costs.
Ford agreed in a deal with the City of Toronto this year to consider moving the parking to nearby Exhibition Place, but no decision has yet been made.
The report concluded that relocating the science centre to Ontario Place would save about $250 million over 50 years - largely because the new building will be half the size.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Document 57 of 500

Corporations need to embrace hybrid and remote workplaces

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/corporations-need-embrace-hybrid-remote/docview/3068369310/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corporations+need+to+embrace+hybrid+and+remote+workplaces&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When a source in the federal government leaked news that starting in September the number of days federal government workers would have to go into the office would increase to three days a week from two, Premier Doug Ford said: "Three days is a good start."
It's part of a broader push by some organizations to get people into the office for more days a week, such as UPS demanding all staff return to the office for the full Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 schedule.
Yet such top-down, full-time in the office mandates fly in the face of definitive proof that the traditional Monday-to-Friday, 9-5 is the worst of all worlds. It's based on a meta-analysis paper by researchers from Stanford University, University of Chicago and the Instituto Tecnológico Autónomo de México on productivity in hybrid, remote and in-office settings.
Hybrid work models, which combine remote and in-office activities, offer a balanced approach that has been shown to enhance both productivity and job satisfaction. This model allows for flexibility, enabling employees to work remotely while also capitalizing on the benefits of face-to-face interactions for certain types of work that benefit from direct engagement, such as brainstorming sessions and complex project discussions.
Research has consistently found that hybrid workers exhibit an increase in productivity of five per cent to 13 per cent and report greater job satisfaction. For example, one randomized control trial demonstrates that productivity in these settings is improved by about four per cent. Self-assessments by hybrid workers corroborate these findings, with three to 5 per cent increases in productivity. The international findings are similar, with positive reports from around the world.
Some employees can
become disengaged
The picture for fully remote work is more complex. Using traditional, office-centric approaches to remote work introduce significant hurdles that can undermine efficiency. In such cases, communication difficulties and a lack of immediate feedback can slow down project progress, reduce collaboration and diminish the quality of outputs.
Another major concern in remote work environments is the propensity for employees to become disengaged from their work tasks, if supervisors aren't using best practices and lack training in managing remote teams. The distractions of a home environment can lead to procrastination and a phenomenon colloquially known as "shirking from home." This issue is particularly acute for workers who may lack intrinsic motivation or whose tasks are boring.
For example, consider a study by economists from MIT and the University of California, Los Angeles observed two groups made up of 235 data-entry workers - one working from home, the other in the office - in Chennai, India. They hired these workers for eight weeks and compared the productivity of both groups.
Their finding? Low-skilled contract workers hired for a two-month gig working from home proved 18 per cent less productive, based on net typing speed, than those working from the office.
By contrast, an earlier study by scholars at Stanford looked at 250 call centre employees of a sizable multinational corporation, split into two groups: those working remotely and those commuting to an office. Their prevailing assumption was that employees at home might indulge in leisure - sleeping or binge-watching television - instead of performing their duties. Contrary to these expectations, the researchers uncovered a startling 13 per cent surge in productivity among remote workers. The differences might stem from the fact that the latter study focused on permanent staff invested into their careers at the multinational company, compared to the short-term contract workers evaluated in the former study.
The evidence does show that fully remote work undermines collaboration and learning, unless you adopt best practices for remote work management. One study looked at software engineers at a Fortune 500 company with a main campus split across two buildings.
Remote work offers
much better cost savings
Engineers stationed in the same building as their entire team benefitted from receiving 22 per cent more online feedback compared to those with teammates in different buildings. The researchers concluded that the lack of such feedback indicates that working remotely undermines collaboration and learning.
Still, no question that remote work offers much better cost savings. Remote employees require no office space and can be hired at lower wages. Specifically, consider FlexJobs' 2024 Work-From-Anywhere survey of over 4,000 U.S. professionals. The findings revealed a significant willingness among workers to trade financial gain for geographic flexibility: in order to secure the ability to work from anywhere, 26 per cent of respondents would accept a five per cent reduction in salary, while 24 per cent indicated they would agree to a pay decrease ranging from 10 to 15 per cent.
This data underscores a shift in priorities, where location independence increasingly outweighs traditional compensation structures in the professional landscape.
So overall, even with a more complicated picture of productivity, organizations may get a higher return on investment from remote workers. In other words, the reduction in productivity per employee - compared to hybrid work - would in most cases be overcome by the reduced cost of each employee. Of course, the negative impact on collaboration and learning needs to be kept in mind and addressed.
In any case, the debate is clearly between a flexible hybrid model or a fully remote model. The traditional office-centric model is toast. That's even before the fact that blanket return to office mandates, especially for full-time in-office work, harm productivity by decreasing employee engagement.
Hybrid work definitely offers higher productivity than office-centric work. Productivity in remote work is more nuanced and context-dependent, with negative implications for learning and collaboration, unless organizations adopt best practices - however, fully remote offers major cost savings.
Either hybrid or fully remote work is better than office-centric work, and there's never a reason to adopt a Monday-Friday, 9-5 way of working.
Gleb Tsipursky is CEO of the
future-of-work consultancy Disaster Avoidance Experts and a freelance contributing
columnist for the Star. He is based in Columbus, Ohio. Follow him on X: @Gleb_Tsipursky
CREDIT: Gleb Tsipursky Contributing Columnist

Subject: Work at home; Best practice; Hybrid work; Job satisfaction; Collaboration; Cost control; Employees; Wages & salaries; Productivity

Families are fuming as Guelph U residence wait-list tops 1,300 - sending students scrambling to find housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/families-are-fuming-as-guelph-u-residence-wait/docview/3068465789/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Families+are+fuming+as+Guelph+U+residence+wait-list+tops+1%2C300+-+sending+students+scrambling+to+find+housing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Families are furious and accusing the University of Guelph of "bait and switch" and a "cash grab" after the school boosted enrolment when there weren't enough residence beds, leaving more than 1,300 incoming first-year students scrambling to find a place to live.
The situation has caused such an uproar that even the mayor of Guelph has weighed in, accusing the university of "over-recruitment" and calling the situation "completely unacceptable" - but the school says it was caught off-guard after more students than expected accepted admission offers for this fall.
"I am quite disappointed with what may occur if solutions are not immediately found, not only for the impacts to first-year students but to the greater Guelph community with the planned over-recruitment tactic employed by the university ... the ripple effects will be considerable and compounding for our community for years to come," Mayor Cam Guthrie said in a public rebuke of the school.
"It is completely unacceptable."
The university said it does not guarantee a spot in residence for first-year students, but parents say school representatives - at the universities' fair, during tours, and in offers of admission - repeatedly indicated there would be no problem accommodating everyone, and touted a housing report it commissioned that said the same.
"It feels almost like bait-and-switch," said parent James Clarke, whose daughter is near the bottom of the wait-list for a spot in residence.
While the university says it began communicating with families in April about possible bed shortages, dozens of parents who contacted the Star said it wasn't until last week that it was confirmed.
"They then came out at the beginning of June and said 'hey, we've got a waiting list of people up into the 1,300s,'" added Clarke. "Kids have already turned down other universities ... they have no other choice."
His wife, Dayna Stringer, travelled to Guelph with a real-estate agent friend on Friday to find a place for their daughter, as a number of families worry about bidding wars and outrageous rents given landlords have the upper hand.
A Toronto-area mother said she had to sign a 12-month lease at $1,750 a month for a room off campus - or $21,000 over the next year - with food and other costs on top of that. It's so far beyond what the family had budgeted that a trip planned for next year had to be cancelled.
By comparison, residence rooms at Guelph cost from $6,400 to $10,500 per school year, plus meal plan fees. And residence spots are determined by lottery.
The university said that at this time last year, it had 5,143 acceptances, and this year that jumped to 7,849. Last year, however, some residence beds were left empty - though the year prior there was also a wait-list.
For this fall, the school has again leased a former hotel, is turning some two-person apartments into three, continues to look for extra space elsewhere, and says some beds may become available in January. Even then, it will be short about 1,000 beds.
Gwen Chapman, Guelph's provost and vice-president (academic), said the university did plan for higher enrolment numbers - in part to boost the school's budget amid stalled funding from the province on top of a tuition cut and freeze - but "by April we were starting to see a bit of the higher acceptance rate and recognized that we might not be able to accommodate everyone in residence ... so we communicated that quite clearly to students and their families."
She said the school will provide "a number of supports through our student experience team" to help first-year students adjust to living off-campus while making them feel a part of campus life.
"We certainly empathize with the situation," she said. "We recognize it's not what they wanted - it's not what we want. We would like to be able to accommodate all of the students who would like to be in residence, but this is where we are now."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner, who represents the riding, said "what Guelph is trying to do is increase student enrolment to cover the fact that they're facing significant operating deficits" because of provincial underfunding.
The university, however, "has a responsibility to students and parents to figure out the accommodation and to be very open and transparent about what's available, what's not available," he added.
In a statement to the Star, Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop said, "I fully expect the University of Guelph to be active in helping students and families find a solution."
Her spokesperson, Brooke Campbell, added that "this is a unique situation to the University of Guelph. Publicly assisted universities in Ontario are legally autonomous institutions with full responsibility for both academic and administrative matters."
Nate Broughton, who is a part of Guelph's student government but not speaking on behalf of it, said he's trying to create a task force to address the issues that the university will face with the influx of students.
The university "needs to prepare the campus for these students - there needs to be faculty hired to teach all of these students. All of these support services on campus ... these things need to really be bumped up to support this volume of students."
Chapman said the additional tuition revenue will be shared with faculties to help them serve larger numbers of students.
However, "the library is already packed, and it's hard to even find space. We're wondering about the quality of education, or are they now going to have many online classes, which will not work for my son," said Toronto mom Jenny Blaauw, who has a daughter at Guelph and a son set to start there this fall. He's about 650 on the wait-list.
"When you let in that many people, I don't have any confidence that they have the infrastructure to support it," she said.
For families who live further afield, finding accommodation has become a huge end-of-year stress when they should be focusing on and celebrating high school graduation.
Sam Gaulin's daughter is 1,186 on the wait-list, and "we're 900 kilometres away - I'm in Kapuskasing, northern Ontario. We're freaking out quite a lot" about living accommodations, especially given her daughter's needs because of a medical condition.
"I'm genuinely very disappointed in their entire lack of communication; they knew this was coming," she said. "I'm not a rocket scientist, but if you have 1,000 rooms and you accept 2,000 kids, you know you're going to have a problem."
Her daughter made a last-minute application to another university and is waiting to hear back.
"This is just the most terrible feeling," added Deanna Nearing, whose daughter is 391 on the wait-list.
However, "a little bit of a silver lining, I guess, is that there are going to be so many students living off campus, that it will be a massive group, and maybe there will be some way to advocate for them and have something positive," she added.
Stringer likened the search for a place to "an episode of The Hunger Games," and said the school "put its financial well-being over the students' well-being - this is what really gets me."
"This was a disaster," added parent Kim Ackerman. "... If anything could come out of this year is that next year parents and students are not going through this - that they fix it, because this isn't how a kid should start university."
CAPTION: Dayna Stringer, left, and her daughter Charlotte Clarke, with realtor Michelle Martin are among the hundreds of people now scrambling to find student housing after the University of Guelph notified many first years students that they are on a wait-list for residence.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, June 14 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Jagmeet Singh's reaction to the foreign interference report, Toronto's condo surplus and the Pearson gold heist.
DON'T MISS"Traitors to the country." Jagmeet Singh says top-secret foreign interference report confirms MPs' "criminal" behaviour
After reading an unredacted, top-secret report on foreign interference, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh told reporters he's "more concerned than ever" by the allegations that MPs aided foreign governments' meddling in Canadian politics. "In short, there are a number of MPs who have knowingly provided help to foreign governments, some to the detriment of Canada and Canadians," he said. "... Some of this behaviour absolutely appears to be criminal and should be prosecuted." However, Singh could not disclose whether those elected officials are currently sitting, Raisa Patel reports - nor would he be naming any of the MPs noted in the report. Take a look at what he saw.
ICYMI: Singh came to a dramatically different conclusion than Green Leader Elizabeth May after reading the report, who previously told reporters she was "relieved" by the findings and that she believes no sitting MPs have "shown disloyalty" to Canada.Wait, what? Singh also confirmed, for the first time in such clear terms, that he was the target of foreign interference himself - although he would not offer any details.More: The NDP leader took aim at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's refusal to read the report, given other revelations that Chinese and Indian officials allegedly interfered in more than one Conservative leadership race. "He doesn't want to know and that is deeply troubling."Toronto has a record number of homes for sale. Here's why they aren't selling - despite a housing crisis
In the thick of Toronto's housing crisis, there is suddenly a glut of homes for sale that nobody wants, Diana Zlomislic reports. As the number of Torontonians facing homelessness swells to nearly 11,000, a record number of condos - generally the most affordable form of housing - have saturated the housing market. Most are tiny units built for investors, rather than larger homes suitable for families. "In many ways, families have been kind of shut out of the condo market," a real estate expert said. "It's been dominated by investors so the price per square foot is very high." Is there any way out of Toronto's housing hell?
By the numbers: As of the end of May, Greater Toronto had 8,183 apartment units on the market - the highest number for any month in recent history. Meanwhile, pre-construction condo sales are down 74 per cent from the 10-year average.Word from the city: A spokesperson for Toronto said there's nothing the city can do to stem the construction of small, investor-geared units. While it has encouraged developers to think of young and growing families, it can't force them to build larger suites, they said.He was sweating and nervous while showing police the warehouse where Canada's biggest gold heist took place - investigators think they know why
Simran Preet Panesar was sweating bullets and refused to make eye-contact with police as he led investigators on a tour of a Pearson Airport-area warehouse - the scene where the biggest gold heist in Canadian history had unfolded just days earlier. A year later, police think they know why. Shortly after the tour, the Air Canada manager quit his job and would disappear entirely, Peter Edwards reports. Now, the 31-year-old has been named as one of the two outstanding suspects in the heist; investigators believe both have fled overseas, with Panesar thought to have returned to his birthplace of India. How was this allowed to happen?
More: The other missing suspect, Arsalan Chaudhary, 42, formerly of Mississauga, is thought to be hiding in Dubai, which has no extradition treaty with Canada. He's alleged to have facilitated the movement of the missing gold.Wait, what? Seven of the nine suspects in the heist have been arrested - and yet, the chances of recovering the full $24 million worth of stolen gold bars and cash are "virtually nil." Here's why just $90,000 worth of gold has been recovered.WHAT ELSE
