A promise by Premier Doug Ford s government to include ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/promise-premier-doug-ford-s-government-include/docview/3067798982/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+promise+by+Premier+Doug+Ford+s+government+to+include+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A promise by Premier Doug Ford's government to include Ontario driver's licences and health cards on smartphone digital wallets is on hold indefinitely due to political and privacy concerns, the Star has learned.
"It's now on the back burner," said an insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal Progressive Conservative machinations.
"Our base hates the idea so it's a tough sell," said the official, noting Ford himself had backed the digital push because he saw it as a consumer-friendly measure that would make life more convenient for Ontarians.
But some Tory supporters are deeply skeptical about having government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets - even though the province's COVID-19 vaccination cards were digitized in 2021.
"They're worried about being tracked (by the government), so privacy was an issue," said a second PC source.
Officially, the Tories are tight-lipped about a scheme that was a priority when then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced it in October 2020.
"The government currently does not have an update on its digital strategy but will provide new information as soon as it becomes available," said Matthew D'Amico, press secretary to Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement Minister Todd McCarthy.
"The ministry continues to extend digital access to government services for those who wish to use them. The government will provide updates when available," D'Amico said in an email.
Three years and eight months ago, Bethlenfalvy, who is now finance minister, unveiled an ambitious digital strategy to improve Ontarians' access to services at a time when many were forced to work from home due to the pandemic.
"We need to adapt to Ontario's new reality. People are 100 per cent ready," he said at the time as he touted an 18-page "COVID-19 action plan for a people-focused government."
"The government will consult with industry on how the province could introduce a secure digital identity for Ontarians by the end of 2021, including the strong privacy protections that will make sure you only share what is needed when it's needed," Bethlenfalvy said on Oct. 19, 2020.
"You'll need to keep a driver's license or health care card in a real wallet, at least for a little while," he said, conceding that "making things easier for a customer - or in this case a citizen - is actually quite difficult."
"Starting with privacy, that's paramount. We have to bat 1.000 on those metrics there."
Mindful that millions of Ontarians were already storing their credit cards, loyalty points cards, flight boarding passes, sports and concert tickets, the Tories aggressively sold the proposal in 2020.
"Verified, digital information about you, such as the information found on your health card, driver's licence and birth certificate, can be securely stored in a digital wallet on your smartphone and conveniently used to prove your identity to access services when required," according to the government's action plan.
This would "provide new and improved digital health solutions to front-line care personnel, so they can rapidly and securely access a patient's health records from anywhere and from any device," the plan said.
Sources say that as recently as last summer Ontario government officials were meeting with Apple in Cupertino, Calif., to determine how to add driver's licences to iPhone wallets.
The minister overseeing the digital transformation at the time was Kaleed Rasheed, a former BlackBerry executive who introduced the digitized vaccination card and the successful Verify Ontario app that restaurants and other businesses used to check vaccine certificate QR codes at the height of COVID-19 restrictions.
Rasheed was forced from PC caucus over the Greenbelt land-swap scandal last September after he made what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with top Ford aides and a developer.
The Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP, who now sits as an Independent MPP and wants to return to the Tory fold, declined to comment when contacted this week.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 61 of 500

This is the real cause of teacher burnout and recruitment problems

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-real-cause-teacher-burnout-recruitment/docview/3067798937/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=This+is+the+real+cause+of+teacher+burnout+and+recruitment+problems&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Many factors feed teacher burnout
TDSB employees' use of sick days is well above average. Now the board is taking aim at its 'pervasive absenteeism,' June 11
In addition to all the other causes of stress, as a retired teacher, I am sure that the politicization of our schools contributes to the burnout teachers are experiencing. There was a time when you did not bring your particular politics into your classroom. Now I read about political stripes being shared in the classroom without any thought to how that would affect other teachers or students in the classroom. Is it any wonder teachers are quitting and leaving what is no longer a profession that welcomes everyone?
Maxanne Ezer, Toronto
Thank you for printing the letters from teachers that describe the unacceptable, often dangerous classroom behaviour that exists in today's schools. We have four educators in our large extended family, following a legacy of proud teachers who contributed to creating many fine citizens of our country. We now hear stories of disturbing classroom disruptions that are discouraging to say the least. The current crop of students exists in a different universe than those even a decade ago. Whether distracted by cellphones, in crowded classrooms or terrified by bullying, young people are now disadvantaged from early grades onward. No wonder we can't recruit school teachers today, and when we do, they can't last through a year without taking the maximum of sick days to deal with stress and anxiety from the threats of violence, hurled expletives and distractions of video games on phones preventing students from absorbing the curriculum.
Marion Bartlett, Singhampton, Ont.
A classroom is not comparable to most workplaces. Adult hygiene is significantly better than that of young children. Blowing and wiping one's nose, recognizing nausea before throwing up, toileting accidents and unguarded coughing are rare in adult workplaces, but common in elementary schools. Nor do adults usually need to be reminded to wash their hands or have to eat in crowded lunchrooms. Most offices and factories don't seat workers in close proximity to each other all day either. Classrooms are crowded, germy places. Few have air conditioning or windows that open beyond ten centimetres. Teachers get sick working in these conditions. As well, if their symptoms mimic COVID, they have been encouraged to stay home. Most teachers don't misuse sick days. They have more sick days than other professions because of the higher exposure to illness in their work places.
Joan McGoey, Toronto
The Star has reported Toronto District School Board has too high a rate of teacher absenteeism. The article goes on to imply that the public not worry because task forces are working on solutions to recruitment and retention of teachers. However, the underlying cause of the absences is well stated by the president of the Elementary Teachers of Ontario: "Working conditions giving rise to injury and illness of teachers; poor ventilation, heat stress, violent incidents, increased workload, diminishing resources, diminishing student supports." How these conditions are being addressed should be the main story.
Nanci Goldman, Toronto
Boards could find teachers if they improved recruitment
Calling the process of hiring for the TDSB slow is charitable to say the least. A more appropriate description would be glacial. I am a retired teacher from a board in eastern Ontario and I began my application process to supply for the board 14 months ago after I moved to Toronto. Two months ago I was recommended to hire and two weeks ago I was confirmed as a supply teacher. I have submitted requested information within the imposed deadlines but I'm still unable to take jobs because I haven't been able to register on the board's online supply listing service. No one can tell me why. I know from speaking with education colleagues that principals and vice principals are taking over classes because there are not even emergency supply teachers available. This crisis isn't new. Since the pandemic, schools have been struggling to ensure teachers and support staff are in place at the beginning of the school day. Our students deserve better.
Melanie Dickie, Toronto
Subsidizing beer over provincial parks
I am an avid camper and motorcyclist, with a passion for the outdoors. I love getting outside, riding my motorcycle and exploring federal and provincial parks. Unfortunately, Ontario always gets labelled as boring or not as scenic compared to other provinces that have mountains and the ocean, but I disagree. The endless lakes and trails to explore in Ontario are, in my opinion, unmatched anywhere else in Canada. That is why I cringe when I am forced to spend $70 for a single night at a provincial park, camping (happily) on the ground. I am perplexed by the decisions made by our current premier, who choose to waste tax dollars on subsidizing private industries instead of subsidizing our greatest treasure, our natural resources. Why do we focus on breaking contracts, building private underground parking lots and changing the colour of license plates, all while neglecting the spaces we have set aside to explore and appreciate? I don't blame Parks Canada or Ontario Parks. Both have had budget reductions and have needed to supplement the growing cost of operating these massive ecological and economical power plants. But at a certain point, when does the cost become too much for the average Ontario family to afford? Get back to nature. Premier Doug Ford should better fund the provincial park system.
Jack Carver, East Gwillimbury
Baseball letter too harsh
A baseball to the head is no laughing matter, June 8
The letter writer may be a bit harsh here. The woman who was injured by the baseball was tended to by medical staff, yes, but made her own decision to continue watching the game. She herself submitted photos on social media. If she was really upset, she could have turned down the Topps card offer, the free tickets for another game and the signed baseball from Bo Bichette. I understand concerns regarding fan injuries, but this was probably not a good example to use to make your point.
Brad Ing, Markham
CAPTION: The Toronto District School Board's reliance on emergency replacement personnel to supervise classes soared this year. The practice has raised concerns.

Full text: Many factors lead to high teacher burnout
TDSB employees' use of sick days is well above average. Now the board is taking aim at its 'pervasive absenteeism,' June 11
In addition to all the other causes of stress, as a retired teacher, I am sure that the politicization of our schools contributes to the burnout teachers are experiencing. There was a time when you did not bring your particular politics into your classroom.
Now I read about political stripes being shared in the classroom without any thought to how that would affect other teachers or students in the classroom. Is it any wonder teachers are quitting and leaving what is no longer a profession that welcomes everyone?
Maxanne Ezer, Toronto
Thank you for printing the letters from teachers who describe the unacceptable, often dangerous, classroom behaviour that exists in today's schools.
We have four educators in our large extended family, following a legacy of proud teachers who contributed to creating many fine citizens of our country. We now hear stories of disturbing classroom disruptions that are discouraging to say the least.
The current crop of students exists in a different universe than those even a decade ago. Whether distracted by cellphones, in crowded classrooms or terrified by bullying, young people are now disadvantaged from early grades onward.
No wonder we can't recruit school teachers today and, when we do, they can't last through a year without taking the maximum of sick days to deal with stress and anxiety from the threats of violence, hurled expletives and distractions of video games on phones preventing students from absorbing the curriculum.
Marion Bartlett,
Singhampton, Ont.
A classroom is not comparable to most workplaces. Adult hygiene is significantly better than that of young children. Blowing and wiping one's nose, recognizing nausea before throwing up, toileting accidents and unguarded coughing are rare in adult workplaces, but common in elementary schools.

Document 63 of 500

Gas plants must stay, new energy minister confirms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gas-plants-must-stay-new-energy-minister-confirms/docview/3067797215/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gas+plants+must+stay%2C+new+energy+minister+confirms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's new energy minister says the fast-growing province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels, even as concerns about climate change mount.
"I cannot underscore this enough," Stephen Lecce said Thursday at the Darlington nuclear plant where the next phase of construction is beginning on a small modular reactor.
"We will not pursue an ideological path that will deny some forms of energy when we need all of them to help fuel our economy."
Lecce was appointed head of the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification a week ago when Premier Doug Ford shuffled his cabinet and adjourned the legislature for an unusually long summer break - until Oct. 21. MPPs had been scheduled to return Sept. 9.
The government has faced criticism for a growing reliance on natural gas-fuelled power plants to keep the lights on at times of peak demand.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Lecce, who spent the past several years as minister of education, is "disconnected from the reality Ontario is facing."
"Ramping up expensive, dirty gas plants makes no sense when people are facing an affordability crisis and a climate emergency," Schreiner added in a statement.
Statistics from the province's Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO), which manages the needs of the electricity system on a day-to-day basis, show use of natural-gas-fired power plants last year was up 26 per cent from 2022 and at the highest usage level since 2012.
As recently as 2017, Ontario's electricity grid was 96-per-cent free of carbon emissions, but that has now fallen to 87 per cent in a trend environmental groups warn is the wrong direction.
Lecce said his mandate in the new ministry is to "scale up our energy using all forms of nuclear and natural gas and renewables" in what he called "an all-of-the-above approach to build for the future" as the province's population and industries grow rapidly.
That industrial growth includes new electric vehicle battery plants for Stellantis in Windsor, Volkswagen near St. Thomas and Honda in Alliston, in addition to new electric arc furnaces for steelmakers.
Last month, the IESO contracted for new power supply from 10 battery storage facilities and three natural gas and biogas plants amid surging electricity demand.
Battery storage projects can charge during off-peak hours and shoot energy into the grid as needed during peak times, such as hot summer afternoons and evenings.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 64 of 500

Parrish's victory a win for status quo

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parrishs-victory-win-status-quo/docview/3067797211/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parrish%27s+victory+a+win+for+status+quo&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On Monday, voters in Mississauga elected Carolyn Parrish as the third mayor since the city became a city in the 1970s. In her acceptance speech, a jubilant Parrish told supporters "Today we celebrate not just a victory at the polls, but a victory for the future of our city."
As far as slogans go, it seemed rather benign. It's not. And therein lies the problem for anyone interested in municipal politics in Ontario.
Less than 26 per cent of eligible voters cast a ballot in the Mississauga byelection. Of those, just 31 per cent picked the winning candidate. So what did 31 per cent of less than 26 per cent of Mississauga voters demand?
They demanded a mayor who refused to attend all-candidates' debates. They demanded a mayor who did not provide a costed platform, who seemed unclear on whether she supports a land transfer tax, and whose "Plan for Mississauga" is best described as pablum.
Parrish's supporters will no doubt say her decades in municipal and federal politics speak for themselves. But put your phone down and try to name a single thing Parrish has accomplished in all those years. (And no, stomping on a George W. Bush doll and tearing up a picture of Hazel McCallion don't count.)
You might see this as a cruel indictment of Mississauga's newest mayor. I assure you it is not. My antipathy has less to do with Parrish than it does with the state of municipal politics in this province more generally.
Parrish's victory and her "vision" are symptomatic of two conditions plaguing municipal politics, not just in Ontario but across Canada: Low voter turnout and engagement. She was able to win by saying little and promising less because voters have been conditioned not to care about municipal politics. And honestly, I don't blame them.
Think about it: When was the last time you participated in a raucous debate on an actual issue under municipal control, like land-use planning, water treatment plants or road salt contracts? When was the last time you felt like your vote in a local election had a tangible impact on your day-to-day life?
That feeling of futility is no accident. That's the way the system is designed to work. The provincial government doesn't want to cede the kind of power municipalities would need to make actual, tangible changes. So instead of real progress we get politicians, like Parrish, who promise a new day every four years then settle in to do very little at all.
During an election campaign, candidates may extol the virtues of municipal government and its critical position in Canada's democratic system. But in functional and constitutional terms, cities are an afterthought in this country.
Toronto residents know this very well. In 2018, Premier Doug Ford used the notwithstanding clause to override a judicial decision that prevented him from reducing the size of city council. More recently, the premier scrapped his plan to dissolve the Region of Peel, a move that would have made Mississauga, Brampton and Caledon stand-alone municipalities. Hardly a victory for local democracy.
That kind of heavy-handed meddling in city affairs is nothing new. Years ago, when Mike Harris was premier, I had a meeting with a senior figure at the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing.
They told me the prevalent view at Queen's Park and in the bureaucracy was that municipal governments should run like clockwork, delivering services and largely staying out of the way. "Then why bother voting?" I asked. A political handler standing off to the side offered me a wry smile. The point was that under the heavy thumb of the province, municipal votes don't matter much at all.
As a university lecturer and a researcher who studies municipal politics, I can give you several academic explanations as to why this situation persists. But the real explanation is power. The provinces have it. They don't want to give it up. For them, low voter turnout and engagement in municipal elections are ideal.
Parrish is Mississauga's newest mayor. For her and her supporters, it was an absolute victory, even if it came with a tiny plurality on a ballot overstuffed with mostly unqualified candidates. Nonetheless, they should be proud. Decades of public service have led Parrish to this point. However, to say this is a victory for Mississauga and its future is simply not true. The same statements were made when Bonnie Crombie replace McCallion as mayor in 2014. However, this time, even fewer people voted. Ultimately, this mayoral byelection in Mississauga is a victory for the government of Ontario and the status quo.
Ajay Sharma is a university
lecturer in political science, focusing on local government and the environment.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ajay Sharma Contributor;

Document 65 of 500

End encampments? Talk to our premier

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/end-encampments-talk-our-premier/docview/3067797190/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=End+encampments%3F+Talk+to+our+premier&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Encampments have existed in Toronto for more than 100 years: from the self-made tenement structures that populated the lost neighbourhood of The Ward - now the Eaton Centre - to Tent City of the early 2000s, encampments have been a pervasive element of our city life.
But since the pandemic, with the rise of tents in city parks amid a housing and affordability crisis, encampments are no longer temporary, or constrained: whether we like it or not, they're here for the long haul and in every corner of the city. Until enough housing and shelter exists for everyone who needs it, this is simply a fact.
This is also to say that without adequate, safe, accessible and decent housing and shelter options that people would willingly choose, removing encampments does nothing to address homelessness. It just shifts the issue to another park or ravine.
Across the major encampment evictions Toronto executed in 2021, not a single person gained housing; 30 per cent of people moved elsewhere outdoors; and 60 per cent of people who accepted shelter returned outdoors within weeks. Enforcement does nothing to help someone find a home. It also inevitably entails significant harm.
Unhoused people deserve a better way.
On June 26, city council will consider an update to its encampment protocol, which prioritizes engagement with residents in lieu of enforcement. While the protocol is not without its caveats, it's both important and necessary that the city is formally recognizing that defaulting to harming people for problems inflicted upon them by decades of bad policy is neither humane, compatible with human rights, or effective at reducing homelessness.
That said, even if the protocol were unassailable, even the noblest of intentions are meaningless without the resources to back them up.
Encampments have long been left to municipalities to deal with without support from other levels of government. Yet, encampments are also a feature across Canada, and in Ontario, they pervade every community, whether urban, suburban, or rural.
Ontario is inundated with encampments. This isn't just a Toronto issue.
Yet, it's only in the past year that the focus on encampments as a strictly municipal problem has tentatively begun to shift: with urging from the Office of the Federal Housing Advocate for a federal encampment strategy. This has yet to happen, despite an August deadline. The federal government has at least announced $250 million in funding for encampment support - but only if the provinces match this contribution.
To date, we haven't heard a word from Premier Doug Ford about encampment funding. Moreover, we don't even have a provincial strategy to combat homelessness, despite housing and homelessness being primarily its jurisdiction.
It's clear that any spending by the province on homelessness is doing little to address its root causes, which is why the Ontario NDP recently tabled a private member's bill to establish a Homelessness Task Force of front-line workers, advocates and unhoused people to make recommendations on an intelligent, co-ordinated and effective strategy.
Infuriatingly, there has been no response from the Ford government on bringing the experts who know the solutions to homelessness to the table. Meanwhile, encampments in Ontario continue to grow.
In considering an updated encampment protocol, Toronto is also asking other levels of government to do their part. This includes requesting that the province fulfil the cost-matching requirement to unlock federal funds for encampments, adequately fund shelters, fund access to housing - including supportive housing and rent supplements - and enact a regional homelessness strategy.
These are imperative provincial contributions if we hope to reduce encampments and homelessness more broadly and the city is well within its rights to ask for these resources.
Council can and should vote to adopt the updated protocol and simultaneously demand that the Ford government step up.
Otherwise, without provincial supports, any encampment protocol developed by Toronto - or any municipality in Ontario - is just words on a paper.
Diana Chan McNally is a
front-line worker supporting
homeless people. Follow her
on X: @Diana_C_McNally
CAPTION: City parkshave seen a rise in tent encampments amid ahousing and affordability crisis. And to date, we haven't heard a word from Premier Doug Ford about encampment funding, writes Diana Chan McNally.Richard Lautens Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Diana Chan McNally Contributor Diana Chan McNally is a front-line worker supporting homeless people. Follow her on X: @Diana_C_McNally;

Document 66 of 500

'Our base hates the idea': Insiders say politics and privacy concerns halted Doug Ford's promise to put driver's licences and health cards on digital wallets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/our-base-hates-idea-insiders-say-politics-privacy/docview/3068180466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Our+base+hates+the+idea%27%3A+Insiders+say+politics+and+privacy+concerns+halted+Doug+Ford%27s+promise+to+put+driver%27s+licences+and+health+cards+on+digital+wallets&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A promise by Premier Doug Ford's government to include Ontario driver's licences and health cards on smartphone digital wallets is on hold indefinitely due to political and privacy concerns, the Star has learned.
"It's now on the back burner," said an insider, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal Progressive Conservative machinations.
"Our base hates the idea so it's a tough sell," said the official, noting Ford himself had backed the digital push because he saw it as a consumer-friendly measure that would make life more convenient for Ontarians.
But some Tory supporters are deeply skeptical about having government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets - even though the province's COVID-19 vaccination cards were digitized in 2021.
"They're worried about being tracked (by the government), so privacy was an issue," said a second PC source.
Officially, the Tories are tight-lipped about a scheme that was a priority when then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced it in October 2020.
"The government currently does not have an update on its digital strategy but will provide new information as soon as it becomes available," said Matthew D'Amico, press secretary to Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement Minister Todd McCarthy.
"The ministry continues to extend digital access to government services for those who wish to use them. The government will provide updates when available," D'Amico said in an email.
Three years and eight months ago, Bethlenfalvy, who is now finance minister, unveiled an ambitious digital strategy to improve Ontarians' access to services at a time when many were forced to work from home due to the pandemic.
"We need to adapt to Ontario's new reality. People are 100 per cent ready," he said at the time as he touted an 18-page "COVID-19 action plan for a people-focused government."
"The government will consult with industry on how the province could introduce a secure digital identity for Ontarians by the end of 2021, including the strong privacy protections that will make sure you only share what is needed when it's needed," Bethlenfalvy said on Oct. 19, 2020.
"You'll need to keep a driver's license or health care card in a real wallet, at least for a little while," he said, conceding that "making things easier for a customer - or in this case a citizen - is actually quite difficult."
"Starting with privacy, that's paramount. We have to bat 1.000 on those metrics there."
Mindful that millions of Ontarians were already storing their credit cards, loyalty points cards, flight boarding passes, sports and concert tickets, the Tories aggressively sold the proposal in 2020.
"Verified, digital information about you, such as the information found on your health card, driver's licence and birth certificate, can be securely stored in a digital wallet on your smartphone and conveniently used to prove your identity to access services when required," according to the government's action plan.
This would "provide new and improved digital health solutions to front-line care personnel, so they can rapidly and securely access a patient's health records from anywhere and from any device," the plan said.
Sources say that as recently as last summer Ontario government officials were meeting with Apple in Cupertino, California, to determine how to add driver's licences to iPhone wallets.
The minister overseeing the digital transformation at the time was Kaleed Rasheed, a former BlackBerry executive who introduced the digitized vaccination card and the successful Verify Ontario app that restaurants and other businesses used to check vaccine certificate QR codes at the height of COVID-19 restrictions.
Rasheed was forced from PC caucus over the Greenbelt land swap scandal last September after he made what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about a 2020 trip to Las Vegas with top Ford aides and a developer.
The Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP, who now sits as an Independent MPP and wants to return to the Tory fold, declined to comment when contacted this week.
CAPTION: Then-treasury board president Peter Bethlenfalvy announced plans in October 2020 tohave government-issued identification added to Apple Wallets or Google Wallets.

Subject: COVID-19 vaccines; Digitization; Smartphones; Privacy; Scandals; Immunization; Identification documents

Is Canada ready for another Trump presidency? + protest 'bubble zones' spark tensions in Toronto city counci

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-canada-ready-another-trump-presidency-protest/docview/3067423039/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Canada+ready+for+another+Trump+presidency%3F+%2B+protest+%27bubble+zones%27+spark+tensions+in+Toronto+city+counci&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, June 13 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the prospect of another Trump administration, the war in Gaza reaches Toronto city council and the ballooning costs of a long-delayed affordable housing project.
DON'T MISSIf you thought Trump 1.0 was crazy, brace yourself for Trump 2.0. Here's where top officials say Canada is vulnerable
If Canadians thought Donald Trump 1.0 was crazily chaotic, they can expect Trump 2.0 to be crazily methodic - about all the things, Tonda MacCharles writes. For starters, that includes his vow to impose broad 10 per cent tariffs on all imports, his insistence that allies pony up billions more in defence and his promise to secure U.S. borders and deport illegal immigrants. All that became clear during a Toronto conference that drew more than 200 influential players to discuss the Canadian-U.S. relationship. The other clear message? Canadian leaders must brace for the very real prospect of another Trump win come November. Here's what was discussed.
More: Top diplomats from both countries downplayed the impact of another Trump administration on bilateral trade, defence, security and migration. And yet, Doug Ford admitted "American protectionism" keeps him up at night.Even more: But it was Steve Verheul, the lead Canadian negotiator during the previous NAFTA talks, that said the quiet part out loud: we're not ready for the prospect of a Trump presidency or the likelihood his trade advisers will want to reopen the trade pact - and Canada needs to step up its game.Martin Regg Cohn's take: On stage at the conference, Ford preached on love between the two nations - we are too interconnected economically to disconnect from one another politically, he argued. But unrequited love only goes one way at any time.A careful détente kept the war in Gaza from fracturing Toronto council. A move to create "bubble zones" threatened that peace
For months, fierce clashes raged between some city councils, Queen's Park and Parliament over how to respond to the Israel-Hamas war and the climbing death toll in Gaza. Insiders tell the Star's Alyshah Hasham and David Rider that Toronto has mostly stuck to private conversations and behind-the-scenes advocacy to avoid a blowout that may further divide an already-tense city - but a motion to have the province introduce protest-free "bubble zones" around places of worship and faith-based schools and daycares at the last council session threatened to shatter the careful détente. The emotional discussion pit Torontonians' fears over a failure to respond to rising antisemitism against people's right to protest; take a look at the outcome.
What we know: Although the motion ultimately failed by a narrow margin, Toronto's city manager has been tasked with creating an "action plan" that allows people to access places of worship without intimidation while protecting the right to protest. Go deeper: Throughout the war, Mayor Olivia Chow and her council have walked a tightrope between both sides, sticking to motions that garner majority support instead of provoking dissent. "The first rule of debating the Middle East is not to debate the Middle East," Coun. James Pasternak told the Star. "A real tragedy." The cost of the delayed Willowdale housing project has hit $36 million
After years of delays and challenges, the budget for a Willowdale development intended to provide quick and low-cost housing for low-income Torontonians has more than doubled, the Star has learned. A new report about the city-led project at 175 Cummer Ave., to be debated at the city's planning committee today, recommends councillors approve the new cost - expected to shoot up from an earlier estimate of $14.6 million to $36.3 million, according to a confidential attachment confirmed by the Star. "It's just a real tragedy," said a former councillor who championed the project. "This was completely unnecessary, that it would cost this much money, that it would take this long." Here's why.
Wait, what? The higher costs were attributed to a combination of escalating charges for already planned work as a result of the extensive delays, on top of additional work needed due to holdups to zoning approvals. But the city hasn't publicly stated what that extra work is.Background: Announced in 2021 as part of a modular housing initiative, the 59-unit project was meant to be built quickly and cheaply; but while four of the program's five projects have since welcomed tenants, Cummer has been beset with opposition from politicians and residents.WHAT ELSE
"Our democracy is on the line." The focus on bad-actor MPs misses the bigger problem with foreign meddling, the security committee chair warns.Subject officers won't submit notes or interviews in SIU probe of highway 401 wrong-way crash that killed four.It's more expensive to buy a Toronto home now than in January - even after the Bank of Canada's rate cut.Pearson airport screening officers vote to strike, adding to looming travel headaches this summer.Canada's plan to overhaul its temporary foreign worker program has employers concerned.Canada moves into the top five countries for refugee claims.The Beer Store is now allowed to sell lottery tickets. Can that save it?This busy Spadina streetcar route will be replaced by buses until the end of the year.Pierre Poilievre is giving "rich dad," Doug Ford needs suspenders. What "Menswear guy" has to say about Canadian politicians' fashion sense."Canada, the NBA title is yours." Five years ago today, the Raptors won the championship. Why the road back could be shorter.Canadian Opera Company's top leader abruptly departs in surprising mid-season shakeup.Kensington Palace shut down a Kate Middleton cancer treatment rumour.POV
Pierre Poilievre's vision for Canada: Heaven for the very rich and squat for everyone else.CLOSE-UP

Over 1.5 million pilgrims arrived in Mecca as of Tuesday, with officials expecting the number of people this year to exceed the more than 1.8 million worshippers in 2023.

Document 68 of 500

'Expanding gas infrastructure to heat buildings today is like investing in video rental stores 15 years ago'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/expanding-gas-infrastructure-heat-buildings-today/docview/3067423038/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Expanding+gas+infrastructure+to+heat+buildings+today+is+like+investing+in+video+rental+stores+15+years+ago%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The natural gas system must stop expanding in order for Canada to meet its climate goals and to avoid saddling customers with rising costs as people switch to electric heat, according to a new report by the Canadian Climate Institute."Expanding gas infrastructure to heat buildings today would be like investing heavily in a chain of video rental stores 15 years ago," said Jason Dion, senior research director at the institute and one of the authors of the report. "Energy systems need to plan for the reality that is arriving on our doorstep. The smart approach to protect consumers and ensure affordable, reliable energy in the future is to grow the electricity system - not lock in more dependence on gas." The report comes on the heels of a controversial move by the government of Premier Doug Ford to overrule the Ontario Energy Board's decision to end the subsidization of gas hookups in new buildings. The OEB had ruled that gas hookups were artificially cheaper than electricity hookups because of the practice of allowing the cost to be spread out over 40 years, instead of being paid upfront, and that gas consumption would likely drop significantly in that time, leaving fewer customers to pay for aging pipeline infrastructure.The Ford government said it was unacceptable to make new houses more expensive by adding the full $4,400 gas hookup fee.The dispute is emblematic of a problem that spreads across Canada: that utility regulators aren't taking climate change and emissions reductions targets into account in their decision making and business as usual risks "costly dead-end pathways," said Saachi Gibson, a research director at the climate institute."The sector is stuck," she said.For Canada to achieve net zero emissions by 2050, gas use will have to drop by 81 to 98 per cent in buildings, the report found. Electric heat (including heat pumps and baseboard heating), which now account for 34 per cent of heating systems, will need to more than double to 86 per cent over the next two and a half decades, with the remaining 14 per cent of systems (mostly in Alberta) powered by a hybrid of gas and electricity.Yet natural gas systems continue to expand. Thousands of kilometres of new distribution pipelines have been built over the last decade and hundreds of thousands of new customers have been added, the report found. This is because gas utilities don't make money by selling gas, which they don't mark up. Instead, they get a guaranteed rate of return on their investments in pipelines and other infrastructure."They have a direct economic incentive to pursue continued growth of gas infrastructure and new customers, even if the long-term usage case is uncertain," said Dion. This incentive structure needs to change, Dion said, pointing to Quebec, where electrical utilities and gas companies work together to provide hybrid heat, and Massachusetts, where regulators are required to consider other options before approving the replacement of end-of-life gas pipelines.To better align climate goals and utility infrastructure decisions, provinces need to legislate emissions reductions targets and empower utility regulators to take them into account, the report said."Provincial governments should stop treating gas system expansion as the default option, and equip regulators to consider alternatives," said Kate Harland, the research lead on the report. Cities and states around the world have started to ban gas hookups in new buildings, saying that electrifying heat is such a large undertaking, with millions of buildings that need to be transitioned, that the first step must be to stop adding new fossil fuel heating. These gas bans have passed in Vancouver, Montreal, New York state and Germany. But in Ontario, natural gas customers pay fees to cover new pipelines and network expansion. As more and more people switch to electric heating, those left on the system will have to pay more to maintain the infrastructure.While Ontario has a plan to expand electricity generation, including new nuclear plants, gas plants, grid-scale batteries and renewables, there is no similar long-term vision for the natural gas system."It's our hope that (Ontario) will halt the continued expansion of the gas network in the interests of ratepayers over the long term," said Dion.
CAPTION: The report on natural gas follows the Ford government's overruling of the Ontario Energy Board's decisionto end the subsidization of gas hookups in new buildings.

Subject: Infrastructure; Electricity; Emissions; Climate change; Heating; Natural gas

Document 69 of 500

Justin Trudeau's kitschy socks. Doug Ford's ugly shoes. Social media's fashion Guy ranks Canadian politicians' style

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-kitschy-socks-doug-fords-ugly/docview/3067422643/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+kitschy+socks.+Doug+Ford%27s+ugly+shoes.+Social+media%27s+fashion+Guy+ranks+Canadian+politicians%27+style&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You might not know his name, but if you've scrolled through social media at all, you've probably seen Derek Guy's scathing takes on menswear and fashion.
Guy, who goes by the handle @dieworkwear, regularly takes to X, formerly Twitter, with sprawling threads full of critiques of menswear, often taking aim at politicians and public figures.
"Sometimes people ask me, do I think clothes make a difference in politics? I don't think anyone should be judged on clothes in terms of their inner character," Guy, a U.S.-based fashion blogger, told the Star. "I don't think you should ever think that someone is stupid or immoral or lazy or something based on their clothes."
But for politicians, clothes can end up distracting from what they are trying to say, or when they want to lay low, draw more attention to them, Guy said.
Here are five male Canadian politicians, ranked by Guy on their fashion sense:
1. Justin Trudeau: 'Just stop wearing the socks'
Even though the prime minister tops out Guy's list for best dressed, part of Trudeau's wardrobe draws Guy's ire.
It's the socks.
He, throughout various points in talking to the Star, called them: "kitschy," "ugly," and "ridiculous-looking."
It wasn't that long ago that Trudeau was knighted "the most stylish politician" by GQ and named in Vanity Fair's list of best-dressed men. But, as in his polling numbers, Trudeau has fallen from grace.
The socks started off as a playful way for the newly elected prime minster to signal that he was different from past politicians. His "sock diplomacy" (as labelled by the Guardian) would make headlines, like his pride-themed "Eid Mubarak" socks or Star Wars-themed socks for May 4.
"They are just such incredible poor taste," Guy said. "What they telegraph is, 'This is like my personality, I'm the guy with the quirky socks.'"
You can get away with the unconventional socks "when you're super, super old," Guy said - but, for Trudeau, "There's still life in this dude."
Suits are designed to point toward the face, from the V-shape of the suit jacket, up through the tie and shirt collar, Guy explained.
"The outfit should support you in the context of tailored clothing."
The socks, he said, "drag the eye downwards, and then you create two competing points of attention."
"It's a shame to see some beautiful suits ruined by somebody's ugly socks."
Besides the socks though, Guy said, "You can nitpick certain things, but his tailoring fits pretty well." However, the occasional navy sneaker paired with a suit, he said, "That's corny."
2. Jagmeet Singh: Braving the double-breasted
It's no surprise that NDP leader Jagmeet Singh lands second on Guy's list. Singh has been known for his sharp fashion sense - even coming under fire when his taste gets a little expensive. According to Toronto Life, Singh prefers bespoke suiting made by his tailor in New Delhi.
Like Trudeau, Singh was applauded for his fashion sense in GQ Magazine in 2016, with a full-blown photo shoot in his iconic double-breasted suit.
"I love that he's willing to wear a double-breasted," Guy said, adding that too many men are afraid to venture into the more rare variation of the suit jacket.
In one shot of Singh in the House of Commons, Guy suspects Singh would be better suited "if the middle button (on his double-breasted coat) was lower just a little bit." The coat itself, he said, "is beautiful," but if the button was just a bit lower "you get more of a V-shaped taper and you elongate the lapel line."
One outfit of Singh's that Guy did not take as well was from the 2023 NDP Convention in Hamilton. In it, Singh wears a suit jacket, dressed down with a white shirt and (what appear to be) black jeans.
When men with tailored clothing try to dress down, "while you can sometimes wear a tailored jacket with jeans, it's always better to do it with a sport coat," he added.
It can take practice to spot the difference between a sport coat and a jacket, Guy said, so he recommends going into a store and buying a sport coat specifically for more casual occasions. Sport coats should be paired with jeans, but "don't wear them with lowrise skinny jeans," he warned, wear them with midrise, straight leg jeans to avoid a "weird silhouette."
3. Doug Ford: 'Ugly shoes'
Ontario Premier Doug Ford earned Guy's most neutral review, landing him in the middle of this list.
Ford wears reasonably "well-tailored suits," but the biggest recommendation Guy had for Ford: suspenders.
"If you wear belted trousers, or side tabs, your pants have a tendency to slip down throughout the day," Guy said, noting that Ford's hem was often collapsing.
"Many men nowadays are reluctant to wear suspenders, for good reason. They're kind of old-timey," he added, but it would stop the collapsing hem. Otherwise, he recommended Ford get his pants cuffed.
Ford's biggest fashion faux pas, however, was his "ugly shoes."
"He has very bad taste in shoes," Guy said. "The shoes look so bad because one, the design is ugly, but two, the leather is not very good."
"I would recommend that he just go to a traditional shoe store."
4. Pierre Poilievre: 'We all know that you're a politician'
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made a conscious effort to rebrand his fashion sense last summer, dropping his glasses and suit-and-tie regiment for the occasional dressed down T-shirt and aviators.
"It's not very inspiring," Guy said as his first impression of Poilievre's dress.
"When I see this kind of stuff I just think rich dad who went to an expensive store and bought some clothes."
Poilievre gets points for wearing a sport coat to dress down, but his jackets also tend to be too small, according to Guy. "I can tell that if he fastened it, it would pull. I think he would look better in a larger jacket."
"I personally think he would look better in a fuller cut. It's a little more traditional ... (but) these kind of short jackets are often not flattering."
One look that didn't sit well with Guy was Poilievre's pairing of a Henley shirt with a sport coat at an "axe the tax" press conference in St. John's in October 2023.
"There's nothing wrong with wearing a Henley, but you want to keep Henleys to work wear - so pair it under a flannel shirt or a denim western shirt, trucker jackets," Guy recommended.
"It's essentially an old-timey form of underwear. Not something you pair with a sport coat."
Especially for dressing more casually, Guy said, a light blue button-up shirt or a long-sleeve polo would work better.
"I also just think that politicians should not try so hard to look dressed down," he added. "We all know that you're a politician. No one thinks that you're going out into the field and shovelling stuff with everybody else."
"You don't have to dress down with a Henley, because no one thinks that you're a working man. You're a politician."
5. John Tory: An 'odd choice'
If Guy has a poster-child for poorly dressed politicians, it's former Toronto mayor John Tory. He has repeatedly used a photo of Tory, taken at his resignation press conference, to illustrate what Guy considers a poor combination of a formal top, tight pants and sneakers.
"There's a lot going on here," Guy said of Tory's infamous outfit. "The thing that stands out most is the absurdity between the top and bottom half of his outfit."
The skinny jeans combined with the tailored jacket means "you see a very distinctive top block (and) a very distinctive bottom block."
Guy wasn't familiar with the context of the photo or the significance of the press conference (where Tory resigned as mayor after the Star revealed he had a relationship with a staffer).
As a Toronto mayor in his 60s, "wearing skinny jeans with dress sneakers is just a very odd choice, especially for an occasion where you're resigning for a scandal."
Bonus: David Rider, Toronto Star senior politics reporter
In the photo that Guy often posts of Tory, the Star's senior politics reporter, David Rider, is pictured in the background intently taking notes.
Rider joked on X that he is "sighing with relief that nobody is rating my clothes" every time the photo gets posted.
So, naturally, we asked Guy to rate Rider's outfit.
Guy said Rider should lose the red T-shirt in favour of a white shirt, or "if he wanted to really make more of a style statement" wear a white Henley. Rider's dress belt is also too thin and Guy said the longtime city hall bureau chief should wear a 1.5 inch belt, which is more casual, instead.
"But, he looks better, to me, than all of the other men that we previously discussed."
"As a rumpled fly on the wall," Rider said. "I dodged a bullet every time Guy and his followers eyed Tory's silhouette. Having now survived Guy's crosshairs, I am thrilled with a win for what I'll dub Reporter Utility Casual."

Ontario's new energy minister rejects 'ideological' push to scrap natural gas-fired power plants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-new-energy-minister-rejects-ideological/docview/3067422642/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+new+energy+minister+rejects+%27ideological%27+push+to+scrap+natural+gas-fired+power+plants&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's new energy minister says the fast-growing province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels even as concerns about climate change mount.
"I cannot underscore this enough," Stephen Lecce said Thursday at the Darlington nuclear plant where the next phase of construction is beginning on a small modular reactor.
"We will not pursue an ideological path that will deny some forms of energy when we need all of them to help fuel our economy."
Lecce was appointed head of the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification a week ago when Premier Doug Ford shuffled his cabinet and adjourned the legislature for an unusually long summer break - until Oct. 21. MPPs had been scheduled to return Sept. 9.
The government has faced criticism for a growing reliance on natural gas-fuelled power plants to keep the lights on at times of peak demand.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said Lecce, who spent the last several years as minister of education, is "disconnected from the reality Ontario is facing."
"Ramping up expensive, dirty gas plants makes no sense when people are facing an affordability crisis and a climate emergency," Schreiner added in a statement.
Lecce said his mandate in the new ministry is to "scale up our energy using all forms of nuclear and natural gas and renewables" in what he called "an all-of-the-above approach to build for the future" as the province's population and industries grow rapidly.
That industrial growth includes new electric vehicle battery plants for Stellantis in Windsor, Volkswagen near St. Thomas and Honda in Alliston in addition to new electric arc furnaces for steelmakers.
CAPTION: Stephen Leccewas appointed head of Ontario's Ministry of Energy and Electrification a week ago when Premier Doug Fordshuffled his cabinet.

Journalism would help voter turnout in Mississauga

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/journalism-would-help-voter-turnout-mississauga/docview/3067271380/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Journalism+would+help+voter+turnout+in+Mississauga&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lack of journalism got Parrish elected
Playing it safe was not the key factor in Carolyn Parrish's victory as mayor of Mississauga. In fact, it is likely the opposite. Mississauga, a city of more than 700,000, is practically a media-free zone with no local newspaper, radio or television service of its own. In that information vacuum, there was no place for more information about Parrish, including her recalled gaffes, which her opponents' flyers could not highlight. As local newspapers disappear in Kirkland Lake (where I grew up) and New Liskeard (where I once taught), we need to remind ourselves that democracy itself is at risk where information about significant choices is unavailable to the citizens who must decide.
Ab Dukacz, Mississauga
How did May get access?
Elizabeth May says a former MP should be investigated for foreign interference - but saw no evidence traitors are sitting in the Commons, June 11
You don't know how comforted I am at Green Party Leader Elizabeth May's pronouncement that only one item in the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians' most recent report needs to be addressed. Admittedly though, I am shocked May even was given access to the report while Trudeau's government refuses to provide it to all Canadian voters. She may be a party leader, but the Greens represent such a small percentage of the vote in 2021. Why would any informed person take account of her views? The Star's energy would be better spent demanding Trudeau release the report to all!
Bill Fearn, Toronto
Grounds for change
Toronto zoning rules, an espresso machine and an anonymous complaint: Why this shop owner says her neighbourhood store is at risk, June 12
The article about a small business selling coffee shows that our Titanic-sized zoning bylaws and process to appeal are so out of date. It reminds me of a story my father told me about Yorkville in the 1960s. The Penny Farthing opened its doors in 1963 and decided to put a couple of chairs on the terrace to serve coffee. The owners were told to immediately remove the tables and chairs. Like the Finch Store forbidden to serve coffee, the Penny Farthing was not allowed to serve coffee on a boulevard because of the zoning bylaws. What has changed in over 60 years in Toronto? Yet the zoning bylaws allow 60 storey and higher condominiums downtown, where height restrictions were once enforced. I think it is time how we rethink our zoning bylaws.
Astra Burka, Toronto
Wow! Here we have a city, with cars disappearing left right and center, shootings, fights, gridlock, tenants being evicted, retirement homes being closed, and the list goes on, but city hall seems focused on a woman selling coffee is breaking a bylaw. Shocking! How desperate times must be in the small-minded bureaucratic machine that this is even given any energy. Our premier Doug Ford goes on about Ontario being open for business, but God help the small businessperson trying to eke out a living. Shame on the anonymous snitch.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Bridges are 'less than charming' up close
Boring and unremarkable? Not these Toronto landmarks - if you know where to look: Micallef, June 11
I read columnist Shawn Micallef's opinion piece on Toronto bridges. It is an interesting perspective, and true that when driving, you miss the majesty of our bridges. None of them are iconic suspension type, like you see in Montreal or Vancouver. I spend a lot of time in the valleys while mountain biking, and get up close and personal with them and I can tell you that while they are nice from a distance, they are less than charming up close. The new Port Lands bridges are indeed charming, but standard municipal design light posts were tacked on, like an afterthought. The poles disturb the graceful arcs of the bridges. It would be good to know if that was the plan all along or not. And to add some insult, standard issue perforated tube posts for no-parking signs, and homely steel guard rails mar the interior. In Toronto, whimsy and grace are always knocked down a notch by boilerplate municipal regulations. A little more flexibility for artful infrastructure would be helpful.
Norm Girndt, Toronto
Joly on the right track
Just how far should Canada go in helping Ukraine? Mélanie Joly says she wants to keep Vladimir Putin in the dark, June 11
"I think Putin has no red line ... I think we should keep that strategic ambiguity. We should not be clearly showing what are our red lines to Russia," have got to be the most intelligent words Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly has ever uttered. It makes good sense not to tell your enemies what your plans are.
Hank Bangild, Port Colborne
We pick sides because Toronto is inaccessible
You live east? I live west. In Toronto's gridlock that means we can't be friends, June 9
Since I was a kid in the 1960s, we took the Gardiner Expressway to get across town. I am now 64 so you can imagine all the trips over the years. I recall being 16 and on my last lesson as a Young Drivers of Canada student having the driving instructor ask me to take the Jameson Avenue ramp to the Gardiner westbound lanes to get home. We all know it's a very short onramp. I am still proud of that moment almost 50 years later. But things have changed. A month or so ago, Toronto Public Library offered free admission to many of Toronto's most interesting public buildings on the south side of the city, but getting there entailed fighting city traffic and construction. Forget it. Today the Gardiner is a buzzkill and after taking it,I have to do a wellness check-in with myself to ensure that I am in a healthy frame of mind before carrying on with my day.
Donna Brickus-Hollett, Etobicoke
Shake off Trump
Taylor Swift, please save the free world: endorse Joe Biden and stop Donald Trump: Menon, June 11
For months I've been telling friends that Taylor Swift is the most potent force against U.S. presidential-candidate Donald Trump. Glad to see columnist Vinay Menon is on the bandwagon. She should do some extra concerts in crucial states and donate the proceeds to the Biden campaign. Let's have a "Trumpquake" and shake him off!
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
CAPTION: Carolyn Parrish celebrates her victory to become the new mayor of Mississauga on Monday night at the Mississauga Convention Centre.

Document 72 of 500

Beer Store can apply to sell lottery tickets: Deal with Ontario allows retailer to expand its offerings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beer-store-can-apply-sell-lottery-tickets/docview/3067271236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Beer+Store+can+apply+to+sell+lottery+tickets%3A+Deal+with+Ontario+allows+retailer+to+expand+its+offerings&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Could lottery tickets help the Beer Store improve its luck now that Premier Doug Ford has cleared the way for beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed drinks to be sold in corner stores starting Sept. 5?
With some experts predicting the increased competition means Beer Stores could go bust before long, the fine print in the deal announced three weeks ago does allow them to apply for licences to sell Lotto 6/49 and other lottery tickets.
But officials at the Beer Store won't say if that's the plan and are keeping mum on what else they might sell under an agreement that says "the province shall not prohibit TBS (the Beer Store) from selling any other product, including lottery tickets" except for other forms of beverage alcohol, cannabis or tobacco.
The Beer Store has pledged to maintain "a competitive retail footprint" and is "planning to adapt and thrive" by expanding its fleet of vehicles and warehousing to deliver beer to thousands of convenience and other stores while running its bottle and can deposit return service until at least 2031.
Ontario Lottery and Gaming would not comment Wednesday on whether the Beer Store - which is majority owned by Molson Coors and Labatt with Sleeman holding a minority interest - has applied to sell lottery tickets.
"OLG products are currently not available for sale at LCBO or Beer Store locations," spokesperson Tony Bitonti said in a statement.
He noted the Crown corporation is "constantly evaluating opportunities to expand points of distribution to better serve our customers and fulfil our mandate from government. We evaluate opportunities on a case-by-case basis."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said allowing Beer Stores to sell lottery tickets appears to be a contradictory policy decision by the premier and his Progressive Conservative government.
"He's now taking reliable business away from the very convenience stores he said he was trying to help," she said.
Crombie has called the deal to put beer and wine in corner stores earlier than planned at the end of 2025 a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" because it will cost taxpayers anywhere from $225 million - the amount being paid to the Beer Store - to a total of $1 billion in other fees and lost revenues.
The Ontario Convenience Stores Association said its members have "some concerns" about the possibility of losing ticket sales, but are more worried Beer Stores could become like convenience stores.
"It's a big issue," president Kenny Shim said Wednesday.
"I'm sure we can work with the government on it," he added, noting he would raise questions as to why the Beer Store should be able to retain its role as a warehousing and delivery service if that is the case.
While the possibility of selling lottery tickets is widely seen as an olive branch from the government as convenience store beer sales begin shortly after Labour Day and in big box stores by Halloween, industry observers have cautioned there are no magic solutions for the Beer Store as the place to buy suds.
"I don't think there's any question that their days as a retail entity are numbered," beer author and consultant Stephen Beaumont told the Star's Josh Rubin.
A move into other products - such as salty snacks or other foods to wash down with a lager or ale - could be tricky given those products are widely available in supermarkets and other outlets that already are or will soon be selling beer.
"What are they going to add? Gourmet chips? Another food element? But then they'd have to be more unique than the convenience store," said Lisa Hutcheson, a managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group, in an interview with Rubin last month.
The "ancillary list" of products the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario now allows Beer Stores to sell is limited to beer-related clothing, hats, sandals, drinking glasses, bar ware, aprons, draught supplies, beverage holders, earrings, lighters, playing cards, key chains and fobs, umbrellas, towels, posters and patio lights.
It would have to be amended to allow sales of other products and merchandise, an industry source said.
CAPTION: The Beer Store has pledged to maintain "a competitive retail footprint" and is "planning to adapt and thrive" by expanding its fleet of vehicles and warehousing to deliver beer.Nick Lachance Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 73 of 500

Doug Ford thinks love will soothe Donald Trump. He better be right

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-thinks-love-will-soothe-donald-trump-he/docview/3067271121/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+thinks+love+will+soothe+Donald+Trump.+He+better+be+right&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time for Canada to start worrying about Donald Trump again.
With election day a mere 144 days away, the clock is ticking toward a comeback for the once and future U.S. president. If the vote were held today, he'd win.
That's the latest verdict from David Axelrod, the celebrated strategist who guided Barack Obama to the presidency before Trump moved in. Axelrod's sobering analysis set the table for a day of gloom, doom and déjà vu at a Canada-U.S. conclave of politicians, diplomats and financiers in Toronto on Tuesday.
Bottom line: If you are hedging against risk, hold tight and huddle together.
Axelrod isn't predicting the outcome on Nov. 4 with certainty, merely talking about today's trend lines with sagacity. The polls are not moving in the right direction and the battleground states are not lining up behind President Joe Biden's re-election.
"We are a deeply polarized country," he mused, but that's only the half of it. The bigger problem is that Democrats are disconnected from disgruntled voters, while Trump is disrupting and reconnecting.
Against that backdrop, Premier Doug Ford waded in to the debate.
As a disrupter in his own right, he delivered a populist pep talk to counter the pessimism. Like many of the other speakers - Axelrod aside - Ford opted for the audacity and tenacity of hope.
Trump may well win, yet all is not lost.
The premier made the case for calm, soothing the unsettled Bay Street crowd at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel. As a sweetener, he gave out his mobile number from the stage, as he is wont to do, inviting business moguls at the annual Eurasia Group summit to call or text if there's trouble.
What would he say from the privacy of his phone? Likely the same message delivered onstage all day:
Even if Trump disrupts, the show must go on, two-way trade must carry on, both sides must soldier on to keep the peace. We are too interconnected economically to disconnect from one another politically.
First, love will conquer all.
"I love the U.S.," Ford kept repeating. "I love the United States of America."
Second, have faith.
"May God bless the people of Ontario and Canada, and may God bless the people of America," he concluded.
But good faith only goes so far in bad times. Unrequited love only goes one way at any time.
If Trump returns to the White House, will he once again threaten our shared trade, as he did once before by ripping up NAFTA? Ottawa managed to salvage the old free trade deal, but it is due to be revisited at the five-year mark - which would come due in 2026 during a second Trump presidency.
Perhaps the most reassuring words emanated from the two ambassadors, from our two countries, who took the stage together and largely sang from similar diplomatic playbooks, opting for hope over hype. Despite the media scare stories, the trade deal is not slated for full renegotiation, merely a routine review, they both declared.
"There's no intention in the United States to renegotiate the agreement," argued America's top envoy to Ottawa, David Cohen, adding pointedly: "If it ain't broke, don't fix it."
That may be music to the Ontarians old enough to remember that same saying emanating from a previous premier, the late Bill Davis. The problem is that (like Davis) Cohen wouldn't be in a position to offer such sage advice to Trump if he took power, given that he is a time-limited Biden appointee.
Canada's ambassador to Washington, Kirsten Hillman, echoed her counterpart's view, stressing that both sides understand their "symbiotic relationship" attracts record trade and investment to each country. The larger bilateral relationship is bigger than any president or prime minister.
"Whether we have a Democratic or Republican White House, historically we've always needed to make sure that we have strong ties" at all levels, Hillman said. That means both houses of Congress but also state houses, city halls, labour unions and business.
Ford, for his part, remains unfazed. Long before he became a political disrupter, prior to his populist campaigns, the premier was in sales - which is all about relationships.
The premier talked about his meeting earlier that day with his Illinois counterpart, Governor J.B. Pritzker - a Democrat with whom he could do business. Pritzker followed up with a similarly upbeat message.
Beyond the boosterism, the recurring theme from these two political leaders, like the two ambassadors before them, is that the broader relationships matter - economic, diplomatic, political - when seeking stability. But disruption has a way of destabilizing those relationships.
Hearing Ford reassure his financial audience, it was hard to ignore how much of his own success story has also been based on disruption, over and over again. If Trump returns to power, brace for more of what came before - and start worrying about it anew.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford addresses the U.S.-Canada Summit, in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Subject: Political campaigns; State elections; Populism; Diplomatic & consular services

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J

Title: Doug Ford thinks love will soothe Donald Trump. He better be right
