Document 74 of 500

Canada should gird for Trump victory: Top officials call for co-ordinated plan to protect interests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-should-gird-trump-victory/docview/3067262651/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+should+gird+for+Trump+victory%3A+Top+officials+call+for+co-ordinated+plan+to+protect+interests&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Canadians thought Trump 1.0 was crazily chaotic, they should expect Trump 2.0 will be crazily methodic. About all of the things.
That includes his vow to impose broad 10 per cent tariffs on all imports - something Canada is unlikely to escape - his insistence that allies like Canada pony up billions more on defence; and his vow to secure U.S. borders and deport illegal immigrants, for starters.
That was a clear message delivered from the stage and behind the scenes at a conference in Toronto that drew more than 200 influential Canadian and American players from across a range of political, business and policy sectors to discuss the state of the Canada-U.S. relationship.
The other clear message?
Canadian political, business and labour leaders across all levels of government need to engage in an accelerated, more co-ordinated approach to the very real prospect of a Donald Trump win in the U.S. presidential election.
Sure, there was lots of talk about how strong cross-border ties are, how the relationship is vital to economic prosperity in both countries, and how stable U.S. democracy is even as President Joe Biden's re-election bid struggles to counter Trump's return.
Top diplomats for each country downplayed the looming impact of another Trump administration on bilateral trade, defence, security and migration.
Yet Premier Doug Ford admitted what keeps him up at night is "American protectionism."
The gathering, organized by the Eurasia Group and BMO Financial Group, had the feel of an international summit where the hallway chatter and bilateral meetings were where the players exchanged more candid views.
In private rooms two floors above the fancy speeches and snacks, Canadian cabinet ministers Mélanie Joly, François-Philippe Champagne and Mary Ng met with state governors. On the sidelines, Canada's ambassador to Washington Kirsten Hillman spoke with a senior U.S. official connected to the Biden re-election campaign and a former Trump administration official, and current and former ambassadors and premiers pressed the flesh.
Surprisingly, it was Steve Verheul, the lead Canadian negotiator during NAFTA talks that led to the current Canada-U.S.-Mexico trade agreement, who said the quiet part out loud.
We're not ready for the prospect of a Trump administration or the likelihood his trade advisers like Robert Lighthizer will want to reopen the trade pact, not just review it as scheduled in 2026, and Canada needs to up its game.
The groundwork to enlist private and public sector allies in Canada needed to start "sooner than before now," Verheul said. In an interview, he said dispute resolution mechanisms, auto sector and supply managed dairy industries would once again be on the table.
"We are entering a sprint stage from now until mid-next year" when U.S. positions and deadlines will solidify and kick in, Verheul said. A lot has changed since the last negotiation, and any trade talks now must emphasize national and economic security and supply chain resilience - factors political leaders on Republican and Democratic sides of the aisle prioritize.
Christopher Liddell, a deputy chief of staff in Trump's first administration, said Trump is "laser focused on trade imbalances" and China is first on his target list. Trade with Canada and Mexico may not be a day-one issue, "but it's going to be pretty high on the agenda."
He expects Trump will move quickly in the first six months to execute campaign positions and will announce his transition planning team soon who should be seen as responsibly planning for a return to power that seems increasingly likely.
Jason Kenney, the former Alberta premier who was Canada's longest-serving immigration minister, warned that Trump's "commitment to massively increase removals of people who are foreign nationals" means "hundreds of thousands or potentially millions" of people who have overstayed visas or never had one could head north to "take a second crack at the Canadian asylum system."
It could "overwhelm an already teetering" refugee determination system here that faces backlogs at the same time that record-high inflows of immigrants, international students and temporary foreign workers have put pressure on housing costs, and threatened the Canadian consensus that immigration is a good thing.
There's no question it's a touchy subject.
Hillman, in an earlier panel, emphatically rejected a suggestion by John Baird, another former Conservative cabinet minister, that Canada's border is a weak point or risk for the relationship.
Hillman said the renegotiated Safe Third Country agreement ensures asylum seekers must claim refugee status in the first country they arrive in - Canada or the U.S. - and border guards turn back anyone who tries to game the system and use a back door into the other country. Hillman called it a "strong common commitment to the security of asylum seekers, but also to the manageability of our border when it comes to human movement."
U.S. Ambassador David Cohen echoed that. "The overwhelming problem we have to manage is a southern border problem, not a northern border problem."
When it came to defence spending - a long-standing point of friction - Cohen, as well as U.S. Sen. Chris Coons, an influential lawmaker, are diplomatic.
Foreign Affairs Minister Joly was grilled over Canada's defence spending plan, which still has not committed formally to the NATO target of two per cent of GDP. Striking a defensive note, she asked if Politico's editor-at-large Matthew Kaminski had even read the latest update that commits to raising Canada's military budget to 1.76 per cent of the overall economy, and to buying new subs, which she said would put Canada on a "path" to two per cent.
Coons, a Democrat from Delaware, diplomatically threw Joly an assist: "What matters most is the trajectory."
But Liddell underscored defence spending is a long-standing demand of Trump's, and Ottawa should expect him to force the issue.
Even formal central banker Mark Carney, a long-rumoured possible aspirant for the Liberal leadership, weighed in on the need for Canada to make explicit its defence commitment.
"Canada must hold our full weight in NATO. This is no longer an option. It's an imperative," he said.
Carney said in order to ensure Canada remains "the essential trading partner" of the U.S., "we're going to need a new plan," one that builds on this country's critical minerals, stable banking, financial and agri-food industries, and builds on a nascent AI sector, and addresses lagging productivity.
If Trump wins, Eurasia Group president Ian Bremmer also flagged it would shift momentum away from American subsidies for Biden's green energy transition, with knock-on effects on the EV manufacturing industry and climate action policies in both countries.
U.S. political strategist David Axelrod, a former chief adviser to President Barack Obama, said the presidential race is incredibly tight but if a vote were held today, Trump would win it and he is far from convinced Biden can still turn the tide.
All of which means allies like Canada should not count on the luxury of time being on their side.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

Document 75 of 500

Ford thinks love will soothe Trump: But good faith only goes so far in bad times, with the great disrupter poised to return

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-thinks-love-will-soothe-trump/docview/3067262575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+thinks+love+will+soothe+Trump%3A+But+good+faith+only+goes+so+far+in+bad+times%2C+with+the+great+disrupter+poised+to+return&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time for Canada to start worrying about Donald Trump again.
With election day a mere 144 days away, the clock is ticking toward a comeback for the once and future U.S. president. If the vote were held today, he'd win.
That's the latest verdict from David Axelrod, the celebrated strategist who guided Barack Obama to the presidency before Trump moved in. Axelrod's sobering analysis set the table for a day of gloom, doom and déjà vu at a Canada-U.S. conclave of politicians, diplomats and financiers in Toronto on Tuesday.
Bottom line: If you are hedging against risk, hold tight and huddle together.
Axelrod isn't predicting the outcome on Nov. 4 with certainty, merely talking about today's trend lines with sagacity. The polls are not moving in the right direction and the battleground states are not lining up behind President Joe Biden's re-election.
"We are a deeply polarized country," he mused, but that's only the half of it. The bigger problem is that Democrats are disconnected from disgruntled voters, while Trump is disrupting and reconnecting.
Against that backdrop, Premier Doug Ford waded in to the debate.
As a disrupter in his own right, he delivered a populist pep talk to counter the pessimism. Like many of the other speakers - Axelrod aside - Ford opted for the audacity and tenacity of hope.
Trump may well win, yet all is not lost.
The premier made the case for calm, soothing the unsettled Bay Street crowd at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel. As a sweetener, he gave out his mobile number from the stage, as he is wont to do, inviting business moguls at the annual Eurasia Group summit to call or text if there's trouble.
What would he say from the privacy of his phone? Likely the same message delivered onstage all day:
Even if Trump disrupts, the show must go on, two-way trade must carry on, both sides must soldier on to keep the peace. We are too interconnected economically to disconnect from one another politically.
First, love will conquer all.
"I love the U.S.," Ford kept repeating. "I love the United States of America."
Second, have faith.
"May God bless the people of Ontario and Canada, and may God bless the people of America," he concluded.
But good faith only goes so far in bad times. Unrequited love only goes one way at any time.
If Trump returns to the White House, will he once again threaten our shared trade, as he did once before by ripping up NAFTA? Ottawa managed to salvage the old free trade deal, but it is due to be revisited at the five-year mark - which would come due in 2026 during a second Trump presidency.
Perhaps the most reassuring words emanated from the two ambassadors, from our two countries, who took the stage together and largely sang from similar diplomatic playbooks, opting for hope over hype. Despite the media scare stories, the trade deal is not slated for full renegotiation, merely a routine review, they both declared.
"There's no intention in the United States to renegotiate the agreement," argued America's top envoy to Ottawa, David Cohen, adding pointedly: "If it ain't broke, don't fix it."
That may be music to the Ontarians old enough to remember that same saying emanating from a previous premier, the late Bill Davis. The problem is that (like Davis) Cohen wouldn't be in a position to offer such sage advice to Trump if he took power, given that he is a time-limited Biden appointee.
Canada's ambassador to Washington, Kirsten Hillman, echoed her counterpart's view, stressing that both sides understand their "symbiotic relationship" attracts record trade and investment to each country. The larger bilateral relationship is bigger than any president or prime minister.
"Whether we have a Democratic or Republican White House, historically we've always needed to make sure that we have strong ties" at all levels, Hillman said. That means both houses of Congress but also state houses, city halls, labour unions and business.
Ford, for his part, remains unfazed. Long before he became a political disrupter, prior to his populist campaigns, the premier was in sales - which is all about relationships.
The premier talked about his meeting earlier that day with his Illinois counterpart, Governor J.B. Pritzker - a Democrat with whom he could do business. Pritzker followed up with a similarly upbeat message.
Beyond the boosterism, the recurring theme from these two political leaders, like the two ambassadors before them, is that the broader relationships matter - economic, diplomatic, political - when seeking stability. But disruption has a way of destabilizing those relationships.
Hearing Ford reassure his financial audience, it was hard to ignore how much of his own success story has also been based on disruption, over and over again. If Trump returns to power, brace for more of what came before - and start worrying about it anew.
CAPTION: Donald Trump speaks at a rally in Las Vegas on Sunday. Even if he returns to the White House and again threatens free trade with Canada, the show must go on, Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday, suggesting we are too interconnected economically to disconnect from one another politically.Madeline CarterTribune news service
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Full text: Lack of local journalism got Parrish elected
Playing it safe was not the key factor in Carolyn Parrish's victory as mayor of Mississauga. In fact, it is likely the opposite. Mississauga, a city of more than 700,000, is practically a media-free zone, with no local newspaper, radio or television service of its own. In that information vacuum, there was no place for more information about Parrish, including her recalled gaffes, which her opponents' flyers could not highlight. As local newspapers disappear in Kirkland Lake (where I grew up) and New Liskeard (where I once taught), we need to remind ourselves that democracy itself is at risk where information about significant choices is unavailable to the citizens who must decide.
Ab Dukacz, Mississauga
How did May
get access to secret report?
Elizabeth May says a former MP should be investigated for foreign interference - but saw no evidence traitors are sitting in the Commons, June 11
You don't know how comforted I am at Green Party Leader Elizabeth May's pronouncement that only one item in the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians' most recent report needs to be addressed. Admittedly, though, I am shocked May even was given access to the report while Trudeau's government refuses to provide it to all Canadian voters. She may be a party leader, but the Greens represent a small percentage of the vote in 2021. Why would any informed person take account of her views? The Star's energy would be better spent demanding Trudeau release the report to all!
Bill Fearn, Toronto
Coffee kerfuffle
is grounds for change at city hall
Toronto zoning rules, an espresso machine and an anonymous complaint, June 12
The article about a small business selling coffee shows that our Titanic-sized zoning bylaws and process to appeal are so out of date. It reminds me of a story my father told me about Yorkville in the 1960s. The Penny Farthing opened its doors in 1963 and decided to put a couple of chairs on the terrace to serve coffee. The owners were told to immediately remove the tables and chairs. Like the Finch Store forbidden to serve coffee, the Penny Farthing was not allowed to serve coffee on a boulevard because of the zoning bylaws. What has changed over the past 60 years in Toronto? Yet the zoning bylaws allow 60-storey and higher condominiums downtown, where height restrictions were once enforced. I think it is time how we rethink our zoning bylaws.
Astra Burka, Toronto
Wow! Here we have a city with cars disappearing left, right and centre, shootings, fights, gridlock, tenants being evicted, retirement homes being closed, and the list goes on - but city hall seems focused on a woman selling coffee who is breaking a bylaw. Shocking! How desperate times must be in the small-minded bureaucratic machine that this is even given any energy. Premier Doug Ford goes on about Ontario being open for business, but God help the small business person trying to eke out a living. Shame on the anonymous snitch.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Bridges are 'less than charming' up close
Boring and unremarkable? Not these Toronto landmarks - if you know where to look: Micallef, June 11
I read Shawn Micallef's column on Toronto bridges. It is an interesting perspective, and true that when driving, you miss the majesty of our bridges. I spend a lot of time in the valleys while mountain biking, and get up close and personal with them and I can tell you that while they are nice from a distance, they are less than charming up close. The new Port Lands bridges are indeed charming, but standard municipal-design light posts were tacked on, like an afterthought. The poles disturb the graceful arcs of the bridges. It would be good to know if that was the plan all along or not. And to add some insult, standard-issue perforated tube posts for no-parking signs, and homely steel guard rails mar the interior. In Toronto, whimsy and grace are always knocked down a notch by boilerplate municipal regulations. A little more flexibility for artful infrastructure would be helpful.
Norm Girndt, Toronto
Joly is taking right approach with Putin
Just how far should Canada go in helping Ukraine? Mélanie Joly says she wants to keep Vladimir Putin in the dark, June 11
"I think Putin has no red line ... I think we should keep that strategic ambiguity. We should not be clearly showing what are our red lines to Russia," have got to be the most intelligent words Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly has ever uttered. It makes good sense not to tell your enemies what your plans are.
Hank Bangild,
Port Colborne, Ont.
We pick sides because Toronto is inaccessible
You live east? I live west. In Toronto's gridlock that means we can't be friends, June 9
Since I was a kid in the 1960s, we took the Gardiner Expressway to get across town. I am now 64, so you can imagine all the trips over the years. I recall being 16 and on my last lesson as a Young Drivers of Canada student having the instructor ask me to take the Jameson Avenue ramp to the Gardiner westbound lanes to get home. We all know it's a very short on-ramp. I am still proud of that moment almost 50 years later. But things have changed. A month or so ago, Toronto Public Library offered free admission to many of Toronto's most interesting public buildings on the south side of the city, but getting there entailed fighting city traffic and construction. Forget it. Today the Gardiner is a buzzkill.
Donna Brickus-Hollett,
Etobicoke
Shake off Trump
Taylor Swift, please save the free world: endorse Joe Biden and stop Donald Trump: Menon, June 11
For months I've been telling friends that Taylor Swift is the most potent force against U.S. presidential candidate Donald Trump. Glad to see columnist Vinay Menon is on the bandwagon. She should do some extra concerts in crucial states and donate the proceeds to the Biden campaign. Let's have a "Trumpquake" and shake him off!
Peter Bradley, Mississauga

Document 77 of 500

Gas system expansion must stop, report says: Canadian Climate Institute urges regulators to change energy incentives to meet emissions goals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gas-system-expansion-must-stop-report-says/docview/3067261367/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gas+system+expansion+must+stop%2C+report+says%3A+Canadian+Climate+Institute+urges+regulators+to+change+energy+incentives+to+meet+emissions+goals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The natural gas system must stop expanding in order for Canada to meet its climate goals and to avoid saddling customers with rising costs as people switch to electric heat, according to a new report by the Canadian Climate Institute.
"Expanding gas infrastructure to heat buildings today would be like investing heavily in a chain of video rental stores 15 years ago," said Jason Dion, senior research director at the institute and one of the authors of the report. "Energy systems need to plan for the reality that is arriving on our doorstep. The smart approach to protect consumers and ensure affordable, reliable energy in the future is to grow the electricity system - not lock in more dependence on gas."
The report comes on the heels of a controversial move by the government of Premier Doug Ford to overrule the Ontario Energy Board's decision to end the subsidization of gas hookups in new buildings.
The OEB had ruled that gas hookups were artificially cheaper than electricity hookups because of the practice of allowing the cost to be spread out over 40 years, instead of being paid upfront, and that gas consumption would likely drop significantly in that time, leaving fewer customers to pay for aging pipeline infrastructure.
The Ford government said it was unacceptable to make new houses more expensive by adding the full $4,400 gas hookup fee.
The dispute is emblematic of a problem that spreads across Canada: that utility regulators aren't taking climate change and emissions reductions targets into account in their decision making and business as usual risks "costly dead-end pathways," said Saachi Gibson, a research director at the climate institute.
"The sector is stuck," she said.
For Canada to achieve net zero emissions by 2050, gas use will have to drop by 81 to 98 per cent in buildings, the report found. Electric heat (including heat pumps and baseboard heating), which now account for 34 per cent of heating systems, will need to more than double to 86 per cent over the next two and a half decades, with the remaining 14 per cent of systems (mostly in Alberta) powered by a hybrid of gas and electricity.
Yet natural gas systems continue to expand. Thousands of kilometres of new distribution pipelines have been built over the past decade and hundreds of thousands of new customers have been added, the report found. This is because gas utilities don't make money by selling gas, which they don't mark up. Instead, they get a guaranteed rate of return on their investments in pipelines and other infrastructure.
"They have a direct economic incentive to pursue continued growth of gas infrastructure and new customers, even if the long-term usage case is uncertain," said Dion.
This incentive structure needs to change, Dion said, pointing to Quebec, where electrical utilities and gas companies work together to provide hybrid heat, and Massachusetts, where regulators are required to consider other options before approving the replacement of end-of-life gas pipelines.
To better align climate goals and utility infrastructure decisions, provinces need to legislate emissions reductions targets and empower utility regulators to take them into account, the report said.
"Provincial governments should stop treating gas system expansion as the default option, and equip regulators to consider alternatives," said Kate Harland, the research lead on the report.
Cities and states around the world have started to ban gas hookups in new buildings, saying that electrifying heat is such a large undertaking, with millions of buildings that need to be transitioned, that the first step must be to stop adding new fossil fuel heating.
These gas bans have passed in Vancouver, Montreal, New York state and Germany. But in Ontario, natural gas customers pay fees to cover new pipelines and network expansion. As more and more people switch to electric heating, those left on the system will have to pay more to maintain the infrastructure.
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Infrastructure; Electricity; Emissions; Climate change; Heating; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Document 78 of 500

Pierre Poilievre is giving 'rich dad,' Doug Ford needs suspenders. What 'Menswear guy' has to say about Canadian politicians' fashion sense

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-is-giving-rich-dad-doug-ford/docview/3067510511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+is+giving+%27rich+dad%2C%27+Doug+Ford+needs+suspenders.+What+%27Menswear+guy%27+has+to+say+about+Canadian+politicians%27+fashion+sense&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You might not know his name, but if you've scrolled through social media at all, you've probably seen Derek Guy's scathing takes on menswear and fashion.
Guy, who goes by the handle @dieworkwear, regularly takes to X, formerly Twitter, with sprawling threads full of critiques of menswear, often taking aim at politicians and public figures.
"Sometimes people ask me, do I think clothes make a difference in politics? I don't think anyone should be judged on clothes in terms of their inner character," Guy, a U.S.-based fashion blogger, told the Star. "I don't think you should ever think that someone is stupid or immoral or lazy or something based on their clothes."
But for politicians, clothes can end up distracting from what they are trying to say, or when they want to lay low, draw more attention to them, Guy said.
Here are five male Canadian politicians, ranked by Guy on their fashion sense:
1. Justin Trudeau: 'Just stop wearing the socks'
Even though the prime minister tops out Guy's list for best dressed, part of Trudeau's wardrobe draws Guy's ire.
It's the socks.
He, throughout various points in talking to the Star, called them: "kitschy," "ugly," and "ridiculous-looking."
It wasn't that long ago that Trudeau was knighted "the most stylish politician" by GQ and named in Vanity Fair's list of best-dressed men. But, as in his polling numbers, Trudeau has fallen from grace.
The socks started off as a playful way for the newly elected prime minster to signal that he was different from past politicians. His "sock diplomacy" (as labelled by the Guardian) would make headlines, like his pride-themed "Eid Mubarak" socks or Star Wars-themed socks for May 4.
"They are just such incredible poor taste," Guy said. "What they telegraph is, 'This is like my personality, I'm the guy with the quirky socks.'"
You can get away with the unconventional socks "when you're super, super old," Guy said - but, for Trudeau, "There's still life in this dude."
Suits are designed to point toward the face, from the V-shape of the suit jacket, up through the tie and shirt collar, Guy explained.
"The outfit should support you in the context of tailored clothing."
The socks, he said, "drag the eye downwards, and then you create two competing points of attention."
"It's a shame to see some beautiful suits ruined by somebody's ugly socks."
Besides the socks though, Guy said, "You can nitpick certain things, but his tailoring fits pretty well." However, the occasional navy sneaker paired with a suit, he said, "That's corny."
2. Jagmeet Singh: Braving the double-breasted
It's no surprise that NDP leader Jagmeet Singh lands second on Guy's list. Singh has been known for his sharp fashion sense - even coming under fire when his taste gets a little expensive. According to Toronto Life, Singh prefers bespoke suiting made by his tailor in New Delhi.
Like Trudeau, Singh was applauded for his fashion sense in GQ Magazine in 2016, with a full-blown photo shoot in his iconic double-breasted suit.
"I love that he's willing to wear a double-breasted," Guy said, adding that too many men are afraid to venture into the more rare variation of the suit jacket.
In one shot of Singh in the House of Commons, Guy suspects Singh would be better suited "if the middle button (on his double-breasted coat) was lower just a little bit." The coat itself, he said, "is beautiful," but if the button was just a bit lower "you get more of a V-shaped taper and you elongate the lapel line."
One outfit of Singh's that Guy did not take as well was from the 2023 NDP Convention in Hamilton. In it, Singh wears a suit jacket, dressed down with a white shirt and (what appear to be) black jeans.
When men with tailored clothing try to dress down, "while you can sometimes wear a tailored jacket with jeans, it's always better to do it with a sport coat," he added.
It can take practice to spot the difference between a sport coat and a jacket, Guy said, so he recommends going into a store and buying a sport coat specifically for more casual occasions. Sport coats should be paired with jeans, but "don't wear them with lowrise skinny jeans," he warned, wear them with midrise, straight leg jeans to avoid a "weird silhouette."
3. Doug Ford: 'Ugly shoes'
Ontario Premier Doug Ford earned Guy's most neutral review, landing him in the middle of this list.
Ford wears reasonably "well-tailored suits," but the biggest recommendation Guy had for Ford: suspenders.
"If you wear belted trousers, or side tabs, your pants have a tendency to slip down throughout the day," Guy said, noting that Ford's hem was often collapsing.
"Many men nowadays are reluctant to wear suspenders, for good reason. They're kind of old-timey," he added, but it would stop the collapsing hem. Otherwise, he recommended Ford get his pants cuffed.
Ford's biggest fashion faux pas, however, was his "ugly shoes."
"He has very bad taste in shoes," Guy said. "The shoes look so bad because one, the design is ugly, but two, the leather is not very good."
"I would recommend that he just go to a traditional shoe store."
4. Pierre Poilievre: 'We all know that you're a politician'
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made a conscious effort to rebrand his fashion sense last summer, dropping his glasses and suit-and-tie regiment for the occasional dressed down T-shirt and aviators.
"It's not very inspiring," Guy said as his first impression of Poilievre's dress.
"When I see this kind of stuff I just think rich dad who went to an expensive store and bought some clothes."
Poilievre gets points for wearing a sport coat to dress down, but his jackets also tend to be too small, according to Guy. "I can tell that if he fastened it, it would pull. I think he would look better in a larger jacket."
"I personally think he would look better in a fuller cut. It's a little more traditional ... (but) these kind of short jackets are often not flattering."
One look that didn't sit well with Guy was Poilievre's pairing of a Henley shirt with a sport coat at an "axe the tax" press conference in St. John's in October 2023.
"There's nothing wrong with wearing a Henley, but you want to keep Henleys to work wear - so pair it under a flannel shirt or a denim western shirt, trucker jackets," Guy recommended.
"It's essentially an old-timey form of underwear. Not something you pair with a sport coat."
Especially for dressing more casually, Guy said, a light blue button-up shirt or a long-sleeve polo would work better.
"I also just think that politicians should not try so hard to look dressed down," he added. "We all know that you're a politician. No one thinks that you're going out into the field and shovelling stuff with everybody else."
"You don't have to dress down with a Henley, because no one thinks that you're a working man. You're a politician."
5. John Tory: An 'odd choice'
If Guy has a poster-child for poorly dressed politicians, it's former Toronto mayor John Tory. He has repeatedly used a photo of Tory, taken at his resignation press conference, to illustrate what Guy considers a poor combination of a formal top, tight pants and sneakers.
"There's a lot going on here," Guy said of Tory's infamous outfit. "The thing that stands out most is the absurdity between the top and bottom half of his outfit."
The skinny jeans combined with the tailored jacket means "you see a very distinctive top block (and) a very distinctive bottom block."
Guy wasn't familiar with the context of the photo or the significance of the press conference (where Tory resigned as mayor after the Star revealed he had a relationship with a staffer).
As a Toronto mayor in his 60s, "wearing skinny jeans with dress sneakers is just a very odd choice, especially for an occasion where you're resigning for a scandal."
Bonus: David Rider, Toronto Star senior politics reporter
In the photo that Guy often posts of Tory, the Star's senior politics reporter, David Rider, is pictured in the background intently taking notes.
Rider joked on X that he is "sighing with relief that nobody is rating my clothes" every time the photo gets posted.
So, naturally, we asked Guy to rate Rider's outfit.
Guy said Rider should lose the red T-shirt in favour of a white shirt, or "if he wanted to really make more of a style statement" wear a white Henley. Rider's dress belt is also too thin and Guy said the longtime city hall bureau chief should wear a 1.5 inch belt, which is more casual, instead.
"But, he looks better, to me, than all of the other men that we previously discussed."
"As a rumpled fly on the wall," Rider said. "I dodged a bullet every time Guy and his followers eyed Tory's silhouette. Having now survived Guy's crosshairs, I am thrilled with a win for what I'll dub Reporter Utility Casual."
CAPTION: You can get away with the unconventional socks "when you're super, super old," Derek Guy said - but, for Justin Trudeau, "There's still life in this dude."Singh was applauded for his fashion sense in GQ Magazine in 2016, with a full-blown photo shoot in his iconic double-breasted suit.Doug Ford wears reasonably "well-tailored suits," but the biggest recommendation Guy had for Ford was suspenders.Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre made a conscious effort to rebrand his fashion sense last summer.Former mayor John Tory has faced criticism for his sartorial choices from Guy.How did some prominent Canadian politicians fare in the crosshairs of a men's fashion critic?How did some prominent Canadian politicians fare in the crosshairs of a men's fashion critic?

Document 79 of 500

If you thought Trump 1.0 was crazy, brace yourself for Trump 2.0. Here's where top officials say Canada is vulnerable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-thought-trump-1-0-was-crazy-brace-yourself/docview/3067510442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+thought+Trump+1.0+was+crazy%2C+brace+yourself+for+Trump+2.0.+Here%27s+where+top+officials+say+Canada+is+vulnerable&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-If Canadians thought Trump 1.0 was crazily chaotic, they should expect Trump 2.0 will be crazily methodic. About all of the things.
That includes his vow to impose broad 10 per cent tariffs on all imports - something Canada is unlikely to escape - his insistence that allies like Canada pony up billions more on defence; and his vow to secure U.S. borders and deport illegal immigrants, for starters.
That was a clear message delivered from the stage and behind the scenes at a conference in Toronto that drew more than 200 influential Canadian and American players from across a range of political, business and policy sectors to discuss the state of the Canada-U.S. relationship.
The other clear message?
Canadian political, business and labour leaders across all levels of government need to engage in an accelerated, more co-ordinated approach to the very real prospect of a Trump win in the U.S. presidential election.
Sure, there was lots of talk about how strong cross-border ties are, how the relationship is vital to economic prosperity in both countries, and how stable U.S. democracy is even as President Joe Biden's re-election bid struggles to counter Donald Trump's return.
Top diplomats for each country downplayed the looming impact of another Trump administration on bilateral trade, defence, security, and migration.
Yet Premier Doug Ford admitted what keeps him up at night is "American protectionism."
The gathering, organized by the Eurasia Group and BMO Financial Group, had the feel of an international summit where the hallway chatter and bilateral meetings were where the players exchanged more candid views. In private rooms two floors above the fancy speeches and snacks, Canadian cabinet ministers Mélanie Joly, François-Philippe Champagne and Mary Ng met with state governors. On the sidelines, Canada's ambassador to Washington Kirsten Hillman spoke with a senior U.S. official connected to the Biden re-election campaign and a former Trump administration official, and current and former ambassadors and premiers pressed the flesh.
Surprisingly, it was Steve Verheul, the lead Canadian negotiator during NAFTA talks that led to the current Canada-U.S.-Mexico trade agreement, who said the quiet part out loud.
We're not ready for the prospect of a Trump administration or the likelihood his trade advisers like Robert Lighthizer will want to reopen the trade pact, not just review it as scheduled in 2026, and Canada needs to up its game.
The groundwork to enlist private and public sector allies in Canada needed to start "sooner than before now," Verheul said. In an interview, he said dispute resolution mechanisms, auto sector and supply managed dairy industries would once again be on the table.
"We are entering a sprint stage from now until mid-next year" when U.S. positions and deadlines will solidify and kick in, Verheul said. A lot has changed since the last negotiation, and any trade talks now must emphasize national and economic security and supply chain resilience - factors political leaders on Republican and Democratic sides of the aisle prioritize.
Christopher Liddell, a deputy chief of staff in Trump's first administration, said Trump is "laser focused on trade imbalances" and China is first on his target list. Trade with Canada and Mexico may not be a day-one issue, "but it's going to be pretty high on the agenda."
He expects Trump will move quickly in the first six months to execute campaign positions and will announce his transition planning team soon who should be seen as responsibly planning for a return to power that seems increasingly likely.
Jason Kenney, the former Alberta premier who was Canada's longest-serving immigration minister, warned that Trump's "commitment massively to increase removals of people who are foreign nationals" means "hundreds of thousands or potentially millions" of people who have overstayed visas or never had one could head north to "take a second crack at the Canadian asylum system."
It could "overwhelm an already teetering" refugee determination system here that faces backlogs at the same time that record-high inflows of immigrants, international students and temporary foreign workers have put pressure on housing costs, and threatened the Canadian consensus that immigration is a good thing.
There's no question it's a touchy subject.
Hillman, in an earlier panel, emphatically rejected a suggestion by John Baird, another former Conservative cabinet minister, that Canada's border is a weak point or risk for the relationship.
Hillman said the renegotiated Safe Third Country agreement ensures asylum seekers must claim refugee status in the first country they arrive in - Canada or the U.S. - and border guards turn back anyone who tries to game the system and use a backdoor into the other country. Hillman called it a "strong common commitment to the security of asylum seekers, but also to the manageability of our border when it comes to human movement."
U.S. Ambassador David Cohen echoed that. "The overwhelming problem we have to manage is a southern border problem, not a northern border problem."
When it came to defence spending - a long-standing point of friction - Cohen, as well as U.S. Sen. Chris Coons, an influential lawmaker, are diplomatic.
Foreign Affairs Minister Joly was grilled over Canada's defence spending plan which still has not committed formally to the NATO target of two per cent of GDP. Striking a defensive note, she asked if Politico's editor-in-chief had even read the latest update which commits to raising Canada's military budget to 1.76 per cent of the overall economy, and to buying new subs, which she said would put Canada on a "path" to two per cent.
Coons, a Democrat from Delaware, diplomatically threw Joly an assist: "What matters most is the trajectory."
But Liddell underscored defence spending is a long-standing demand of Trump's, and Ottawa should expect him to force the issue.
Even formal central banker Mark Carney, a long-rumoured possible aspirant for the Liberal leadership, weighed in on the need for Canada to make explicit its defence commitment.
"Canada must hold our full weight in NATO. This is no longer an option. It's an imperative,' he said.
Carney said in order to ensure Canada remains "the essential trading partner" of the U.S., "we're going to need a new plan," one that builds on this country's critical minerals, stable banking, financial and agri-food industries, and builds on a nascent AI sector, and addresses lagging productivity.
If Trump wins, Eurasia Group president Ian Bremmer also flagged it would shift momentum away from American subsidies for Biden's green energy transition, with knock-on effects on the EV manufacturing industry and climate action policies in both countries.
U.S. political strategist David Axelrod, a former chief adviser to President Barack Obama, said the presidential race is incredibly tight but if a vote were held today, Trump would win it and he is far from convinced Biden can still turn the tide.
All of which means allies like Canada should not count on the luxury of time being on their side.
CAPTION: Former President Donald Trump speaks at a rally at Sunset Park on June 9, 2024, in Las Vegas.

Subject: Strategic materials; Trade agreements; Political leadership; Diplomatic & consular services; Noncitizens; Political campaigns; Defense; Presidential elections; Budgets; Immigration

Document 80 of 500

The Beer Store is now allowed to sell lottery tickets. Can that save it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/beer-store-is-now-allowed-sell-lottery-tickets/docview/3067510325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Beer+Store+is+now+allowed+to+sell+lottery+tickets.+Can+that+save+it%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Could lottery tickets help The Beer Store improve its luck now that Premier Doug Ford has cleared the way for beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed drinks to be sold in corner stores starting Sept. 5?
With some experts predicting the increased competition means Beer Stores could go bust before long, the fine print in the deal announced three weeks ago does allow them to apply for licences to sell 6/49 and other lottery tickets.
But officials at The Beer Store won't say if that's the plan and are keeping mum on what else they might sell under an agreement that says "the province shall not prohibit TBS (The Beer Store) from selling any other product, including lottery tickets" except for other forms of beverage alcohol, cannabis or tobacco.
The Beer Store has pledged to maintain "a competitive retail footprint" and is "planning to adapt and thrive" by expanding its fleet of vehicles and warehousing to deliver beer to thousands of convenience and other stores while running its bottle and can deposit return service until at least 2031.
Ontario Lottery and Gaming would not comment Wednesday on whether The Beer Store - which is majority owned by Molson Coors and Labatt with Sleeman holding a minority interest - has applied to sell lottery tickets.
"OLG products are currently not available for sale at LCBO or Beer Store locations," spokesperson Tony Bitonti said in a statement.
He noted the Crown corporation is "constantly evaluating opportunities to expand points of distribution to better serve our customer and fulfil our mandate from government. We evaluate opportunities on a case-by-case basis."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said allowing Beer Stores to sell lottery tickets appears to be a contradictory policy decision by the premier and his Progressive Conservative government.
"He's now taking reliable business away from the very convenience stores he said he was trying to help," she said.
The Ontario Convenience Stores Association said its members have "some concerns" about the possibility of losing ticket sales, but are more worried Beer Stores could become like convenience stores.
"It's a big issue," president Kenny Shim said Wednesday.
"I'm sure we can work with the government on it," he added, noting he would raise questions as to why The Beer Store should be able to retain its role as a warehousing and delivery service if that is the case.
While the possibility of selling lottery tickets is widely seen as an olive branch from the government as convenience store beer sales begin shortly after Labour Day and in big box stores by Halloween, industry observers have cautioned there are no magic solutions for The Beer Store as the place to buy suds.
"I don't think there's any question that their days as a retail entity are numbered," beer author and consultant Stephen Beaumont told the Star's Josh Rubin.
A move into other products - such as salty snacks or other foods to wash down with a lager or ale - could be tricky given those products are widely available in supermarkets and other outlets that already are or will soon be selling beer.
"What are they going to add? Gourmet chips? Another food element? But then they'd have to be more unique than the convenience store," Lisa Hutcheson, a managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group said in an interview with Rubin last month.
The "ancillary list" of products the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario now allows Beer Stores to sell is limited to beer-related clothing, hats, sandals, drinking glasses, bar ware, aprons, draught supplies, beverage holders, earrings, lighters, playing cards, key chains and fobs, umbrellas, towels, posters and patio lights.
It would have to be amended to allow sales of other products and merchandise, an industry source said.
CAPTION: The Beer Store won't say if there is a plan to sell lottery tickers and it's silent on what else it might sell under an agreement with the province that allows the sale of other products.

Document 81 of 500

Win could mean harmony in Peel: Mayors confident they can come together to create a stronger region

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/win-could-mean-harmony-peel/docview/3066738970/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Win+could+mean+harmony+in+Peel%3A+Mayors+confident+they+can+come+together+to+create+a+stronger+region&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The three mayors of Peel Region, including Mayor-elect Carolyn Parrish, are confident they can work together to achieve good outcomes for residents - a situation she and her counterparts said hasn't always been the case.
In her comments on Monday after winning the Mississauga mayoral election, Parrish said she believes the region will be stronger with three mayors "that actually get along."
She alluded to disagreements between Caledon, Mississauga and Brampton in the past, while underscoring her good working relationship with Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown and Caledon Mayor Annette Groves.
"I think this time we're going to sit down peacefully and settle everything," Parrish said.
At the top of the list is a plan for the region to get their "fair share" of funding for social services, which Parrish said will be her first ask when she meets with Premier Doug Ford.
She said it's an area where the city is getting "about 60 per cent of what our population deserves."
That assertion is borne out by the numbers: A report commissioned by the Metamorphosis Network, an organization representing more than 100 non-profits in Peel Region, said the province is shortchanging the region more than $850 million annually for community and social services.
Adjusted for inflation, the report found that when it comes to services such as housing, child care, mental-health support and youth programs, the region received an average of $578 per resident less than other regions with populations of more than 500,000, including Toronto, York, Durham and Halton.
Both Groves and Brown said their communities have faced similar challenges, whether it's poverty, homelessness, or funding for child welfare.
"We have many people using our food banks. We have many young people couch surfing ... we have our own challenges where we are facing some struggles," Groves said.
"I think it's very exciting that the three mayors are coming together and its three mayors understand the needs of Peel Region," she added.
She said while there was some "turmoil" between Caledon and Mississauga in the past, she's optimistic about a more harmonious relationship going forward.
Brown said with "extraordinary" growth in Peel Region, a collective approach is necessary to tackle issues such as an overstrained shelter system. He added that a lack of funding for social issues such as addiction can trickle down to public safety. "When there's poverty, it makes people more vulnerable to being lured into organized crime," he said.
Brown said in the past, the relationship in the region was at times adversarial and even "toxic," especially during the proposed dissolution of Peel Region into single-tier municipalities.
He said Parrish has the willingness to confront difficult issues, but also to work collaboratively.
"She doesn't mince words. And so I think we'll be a great team together highlighting some of these issues."
Gurpreet Malhotra, Metamorphosis Network Mississauga co-chair, said the funding disparity for social services has had a demonstrable impact on residents.
"Because we've had such poor investment in our health-care system and in our human services systems ... What that means is worse health outcomes," he said, citing high rates of diabetes which disproportionally affect racialized people.
He said a collaborative approach is needed to see results.
"That translates to happier children, less isolated seniors and families that are stronger," Malhotra said.
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said it's committed to building "a more responsive, efficient and person-centred social assistance system."
"We look forward to working with the newly elected mayor on the important issues impacting Mississauga and Peel Region."
CREDIT: Omar Mosleh Toronto Star

Subject: Mayors; Funding; Social services

Location: Mississauga Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Parrish, Carolyn

Title: Win could mean harmony in Peel:   Mayors confident they can come together to create a stronger region

Document 82 of 500

Playing it safe got Carolyn Parrish elected. That's not the kind of mayor her city needs right now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/playing-safe-got-carolyn-parrish-elected-thats/docview/3066738724/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Playing+it+safe+got+Carolyn+Parrish+elected.+That%27s+not+the+kind+of+mayor+her+city+needs+right+now&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Carolyn Parrish has just won the safest job in Canadian politics.
The last time an incumbent Mississauga mayor lost an election was 1978. In that time, we've seen six different sitting Prime Ministers and five different sitting Ontario Premiers lose elections. Meanwhile, the only two people before Parrish to hold the Mississauga mayor's job in the last 45 years (like Parrish, both famously plain-spoken women) barely even faced a serious competitor. If history is a guide, it is like a U.S. Supreme Court seat: yours for life, or at least as long as you want it.
And though it appeared at first - and even in the home stretch as polls narrowed somewhat - to be an unusually competitive election by the historic standards of Canada's seventh-largest municipality, Parrish also won it by pursuing a "safe," front-runner-style campaign. She skipped all the debates except one, and in the home stretch of the campaign mostly appeared at events in private settings where a friendly crowd could be assured.
A safe campaign for a supersafe job. Turned out it worked.
The thing now is that in leadership, it may not be time to play it safe in Mississauga. It appears to me that for decades, regional growth and a supply of developable land meant that it was easy for the city's government leaders to promise (and deliver) low taxes and low population density, allowing developers to pay the way as they built out a prototypical sprawling, car-centric suburban community.
But recently it has run out of land to develop and housing affordability has become a crisis there, as it has in Toronto and across much of the rest of the province. The wide roads are now congested with traffic, and the city is still playing catch-up to have the kind of transit system that allows people to consider alternatives to driving. Belatedly, the late mayor Hazel McCallion began concerted efforts to build up downtown-style urban areas to serve as both residential and commercial hubs.
By the accounts of many people there, as reported by my colleague Noor Javed during the campaign, Mississauga grew up as a suburb and is now in the throes of trying to grapple with big-city problems. And a lot of the old formulas won't work to address them. The issues facing Mississauga - housing, affordability, crime (including especially car theft) and traffic - are also Toronto's issues, and Brampton's, and Vaughan's, and Pickering's.
They are regionwide issues. While the past decade of local politics has seen Mississauga yearning for independence from its regional associations (a dream at first granted and then snatched away by Premier Doug Ford), the solutions to big problems are ones that are going to be best developed in co-ordination not just with Peel regional neighbours but with other cities across the Greater Toronto Area.
During the campaign, it seemed like on some of the big issues there was a drive from many candidates to push back against the need for change. Some pledged a new era of tax freezes, campaigned against bike lanes as an alternative to car travel, began flying the NIMBY flag against new housing development with the old "protect stable communities" mantra.
This is understandable: you would imagine that people moved to Mississauga, or stayed there, or considered it beloved, because they liked the kind of place it has been. It makes sense they would rally to preserve it that way.
Except that these issues have arisen because Mississauga has outgrown the kind of place it was. The big issues in the campaign came to the forefront because the status quo isn't working any more. So the policies that produced the status quo won't fix it.
From what I can tell, in her "peek-a-boo" campaign, Parrish somewhat resisted the populist opposition to change, even if details of her actual positions weren't crystal clear. For instance, on the issue of a tax freeze, she called the idea "pure folly," pointing to the example of Toronto's financial mess that results from that kind of attitude. She promises that building more housing and addressing affordability are her top priorities. In the wake of her victory, she was promising a future for the city that would be "brighter" and "more inclusive."
It's time to see if, having played it safe to win one of the safest jobs there is, she's ready to lead boldly, in a way Mississauga now seems to need.
CAPTION: Mississauga mayor-elect Carolyn Parrish is congratulated by former city mayor Bonnie Crombie at Parrish's victory party Monday night.

Ontario Place challenge rejected: Court deals setback to group fighting province's redevelopment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-place-challenge-rejected/docview/3066738701/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Place+challenge+rejected%3A+Court+deals+setback+to+group+fighting+province%27s+redevelopment&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An Ontario court has rejected a legal challenge by a citizens group fighting the development of a private spa at Ontario Place.
A panel of the Divisional Court ruled Tuesday that Ontario Place for All's request that the Ford government conduct an environmental assessment of the West Island, where the spa is being built, "cannot succeed" due to new legislation.
The government introduced the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act in the legislature in November 2023, one week after Ontario Place for All (OP4A) applied for a judicial review of the decision to exempt the redevelopment of the West Island at Ontario Place from the Environmental Assessment Act. The new legislation received royal assent in December.
The Rebuilding Ontario Place Act exempts the West Island from the Environmental Assessment Act "and therefore nullifies the remedies sought in the application," the court's decision said.
Norm Di Pasquale, co-chair of Ontario Place for All Inc., a not-for-profit organization with 30,000 supporters, argues the legislation aims to strip Ontario Place of all rights and protections, including the Environmental Bill of Rights and the Heritage Act.
"It sets a very terrible precedent for the future of our Ontario public institutions," he said.
"If they've done this here, what is going to stop them from doing this elsewhere?" Di Pasquale said.
The Austria-based company Therme plans to create a $350-million water park and spa on the West Island. Provincial taxpayers would fund the construction of a 2,100-space underground parking garage as part of the project. The plan involves relocating the Ontario Science Centre and expanding Live Nation's concert theatre.
In its application submitted late last year, OP4A argued the plan would involve destroying 840 trees and vegetation in an internationally recognized landscape.
The move, the group claimed, would also contour and fill the lagoons and small waterways, destroying 36,000 square metres of aquatic habitat.
The Ford government responded to the application with a motion to quash the action, but that effort was dismissed in April by a different judge, who argued the extent of the Environmental Assessment Act's application to a public redevelopment project is "an important question of public interest." The decision was lauded as a win by OP4A.
A request to Ontario's Ministry of Infrastructure for comment on Tuesday's decision was deferred to Jack Fazzari, press secretary for Attorney General Doug Downey.
"As this matter is in the appeal period, it would be inappropriate to comment," Fazzari wrote in an email.
A spokesperson for Premier Doug Ford's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Tuesday's decision was a massive blow to his group's efforts, said Di Pasquale. He said the group is reviewing the decision with its legal team to determine its next steps.
"We are obviously really disappointed. We were hoping for a different result today."
Chris Glover, MPP for Spadina-Fort York, whose electoral district includes Ontario Place, echoed that sentiment in a statement Tuesday, saying it was "appalling" the government used its majority to "retroactively change the law and push through this corrupt deal."
Vowing to continue the fight, the NDP critic for democratic reform said other action is being taken over the controversial redevelopment, including an auditor general's review, a separate legal court case and the "immense people power" against the project.
With files from Francine Kopun and Emily Fagan
CREDIT: Calvi Leon Toronto Star With files from Francine Kopun and Emily Fagan

Document 84 of 500

I spoke our language for those who could not

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-spoke-our-language-those-who-could-not/docview/3066738655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+spoke+our+language+for+those+who+could+not&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: May 28 was a historic day in Ontario. I finally spoke in my language, in Queen's Park, which has been heard on this land for tens of thousands of years. Our people say, since time began.
May 28 was the first time I was able to speak my language, Anishininiimowin, in the settler government, the government that says it speaks for all of us. Yet it has not.
I spoke for all of those First Nations people who were punished and abused for speaking their language. From the time the settlers made their way to our lands and unjustly and unlawfully, claimed them as theirs under the false pretence of terra nullius - the idea from Christianity that this land belonged to no one, so it was free for the taking.
But this land we stand on, this land took care of us, it nurtured our families for generations and generations.
I spoke for all of the First Nations children, who were stolen away from their lands, their families and communities, from everything they knew. These children were frightened, they were lonely, and they were taken away to places where they were told they could not speak unless they spoke English.
Once at these so-called schools, usually hundreds of miles away from home, they were separated from their siblings, and I know these children were terrified. They were afraid to speak because if they did, they'd be beaten. They could not express themselves; they couldn't show who they truly were.
For most of these children their language was taken away, beaten out of them, and so were their spirits, who they are, their dreams and hopes, their love and their language.
I spoke for all those children, for those who survived, who made it home to parents they could no longer talk to because the children had lost their language.
I spoke for those children who became adults, living under a foreign system, one that abused us and stole us from our very identities.
I spoke for the thousands of First Nations children who never made it home from these Indian residential schools. For the thousands who died in Indian hospitals or tuberculosis sanatoriums.
I spoke for all our ancestors who never made it home.
I spoke for those children in one of our languages because I am one of those children.
They are me. I am them. Our spirits speak as one.
Our children are meant to grow up with our languages, laughing and joking with our parents, grandparents and friends. Our children are meant to learn our ways of life from the land that gives us our language.
I was lucky. I had my summers. I had my time on the land with my family. My beautiful mother, and my late father. I was thinking of him. My sisters, brothers and I had our most cherished memories with our parents, living by the lakes and rivers of our lands, learning to fish, to hunt, to trap. This kept the language alive in us. We are lucky. We know many did not have this opportunity.
May 28 was a day to reflect, to remember, and to celebrate - a day when we began to turn a page in Ontario's dark history.
After all of this time, for more than 150 years in Ontario, since this legislature existed, has it been forbidden for me or any other First Nations person to speak our language on this land in this legislature?
I hope that this moment of change encourages others to learn their language and that all other Indigenous languages survive.
Our people need our languages; they are medicine.
We need our Indigenous languages in the curriculum, taught in schools, and included in the history books and literature classes. We need Indigenous language classes besides English and French. This is the road to a just society.
Sol Mamakwa, from Kingfisher Lake
First Nation, is a survivor of the Indian
residential school system and is the MPP
for Kiiwetinoong.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa holds an eagle feather in the Ontario legislature last month as he spoke his language, Anishininiimowin. It was the first time in history that an Indigenous language was used in the chamber.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Sol Mamakwa Contributor Sol Mamakwa, from Kingfisher Lake First Nation, is a survivor of the Indian residential school system and is the MPP for Kiiwetinoong.;

Document 85 of 500

Ford, Illinois governor unite on trade and traffic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-illinois-governor-unite-on-trade-traffic/docview/3066738632/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%2C+Illinois+governor+unite+on+trade+and+traffic&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stuck in traffic? Premier Doug Ford says he feels your pain.
As he signed a new memorandum of understanding to boost the $29 billion in annual trade with Illinois Gov. J.B. Pritzker in Toronto on Tuesday, Ford commiserated about the logjams.
"There's one thing I can tell the folks here what we have in common, really, gridlock traffic," said the premier, who worked in Chicago while running a division of his family's label business.
"Believe me, I lived years going down to Chicago downtown on the Stevenson or I-90 and you try to go on the Don Valley parking lot or the Gardiner, same situation. We're jammed," he said, referring to major expressways in the Windy City and here.
"We built up around the highways, but we're expanding that."
That's an apparent reference to both the proposed 52-kilometre Highway 413 connecting Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan and the province's uploading of the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway from the City of Toronto last November.
Pritzker, the wealthy scion of the family that owns the Hyatt hotel chain, hailed Ontario as his state's "No. 1 trading partner."
"Chicago and Toronto have a lot in common. We not only share a similar set of values between the two cities and of course between Ontario and Illinois, but also there is a tremendous amount of trade," he said.
With Ford set to visit Chicago on a trade mission, Pritzker joked: "I'd be happy to take you to a Blackhawks game to show you what real hockey looks like."
Deadpanned the premier, a long-suffering Toronto Maple Leafs fan: "I can't even argue that point."
As part of the new arrangement between Ontario and Illinois, the jurisdictions will establish a "procurement co-operation council as a forum for sharing information on procurement policies and to advance opportunities for suppliers in each jurisdiction."
That's designed to make the supply chains in the province and state even more efficient, reducing roadblocks for businesses.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 86 of 500

Mississauga needs a bold Parrish

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mississauga-needs-bold-parrish/docview/3066738429/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mississauga+needs+a+bold+Parrish&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Carolyn Parrish has just won the safest job in Canadian politics.
The last time an incumbent Mississauga mayor lost an election was 1978. In that time, we've seen six different sitting prime ministers and five different sitting Ontario premiers lose elections. Meanwhile, the only two people before Parrish to hold the Mississauga mayor's job in the last 45 years (like Parrish, both famously plain-spoken women) barely even faced a serious competitor. If history is a guide, it is like a U.S. Supreme Court seat: yours for life, or at least as long as you want it.
And though it appeared at first - and even in the home stretch as polls narrowed somewhat - to be an unusually competitive election by the historic standards of Canada's seventh-largest municipality, Parrish also won it by pursuing a "safe," front-runner-style campaign.
She skipped all the debates except one, and in the home stretch of the campaign mostly appeared at events in private settings where a friendly crowd could be assured.
A safe campaign for a supersafe job. Turned out it worked.
The thing now is that in leadership, it may not be time to play it safe in Mississauga. It appears to me that for decades, regional growth and a supply of developable land meant that it was easy for the city's government leaders to promise (and deliver) low taxes and low population density, allowing developers to pay the way as they built out a prototypical sprawling, car-centric suburban community.
But recently it has run out of land to develop and housing affordability has become a crisis there, as it has in Toronto and across much of the rest of the province. The wide roads are now congested with traffic, and the city is still playing catch-up to have the kind of transit system that allows people to consider alternatives to driving. Belatedly, the late mayor Hazel McCallion began concerted efforts to build up downtown-style urban areas to serve as both residential and commercial hubs.
By the accounts of many people there, as reported by my colleague Noor Javed during the campaign, Mississauga grew up as a suburb and is now in the throes of trying to grapple with big-city problems. And a lot of the old formulas won't work to address them. The issues facing Mississauga - housing, affordability, crime (including especially car theft) and traffic - are also Toronto's issues, and Brampton's, and Vaughan's, and Pickering's.
They are regionwide issues. While the past decade of local politics has seen Mississauga yearning for independence from its regional associations (a dream at first granted and then snatched away by Premier Doug Ford), the solutions to big problems are ones that are going to be best developed in co-ordination not just with Peel regional neighbours, but with other cities across the Greater Toronto Area.
During the campaign, it seemed like on some of the big issues there was a drive from many candidates to push back against the need for change. Some pledged a new era of tax freezes, campaigned against bike lanes as an alternative to car travel, began flying the NIMBY flag against new housing development with the old "protect stable communities" mantra.
This is understandable: You would imagine that people moved to Mississauga, or stayed there, or considered it beloved, because they liked the kind of place it has been. It makes sense they would rally to preserve it that way.
Except that these issues have arisen because Mississauga has outgrown the kind of place it was. The big issues in the campaign came to the forefront because the status quo isn't working any more. So the policies that produced the status quo won't fix it.
From what I can tell, in her "peek-a-boo" campaign, Parrish somewhat resisted the populist opposition to change, even if details of her actual positions weren't crystal clear. For instance, on the issue of a tax freeze, she called the idea "pure folly," pointing to the example of Toronto's financial mess that results from that kind of attitude. She promises that building more housing and addressing affordability are her top priorities. In the wake of her victory, she was promising a future for the city that would be "brighter" and "more inclusive."
It's time to see if, having played it safe to win one of the safest jobs there is, she's ready to lead boldly, in a way Mississauga now seems to need.
CAPTION: Mississauga Mayor-elect Carolyn Parrish is congratulated by former mayor Bonnie Crombie at Parrish's victory party Monday night. Mississauga is now grappling with big-city problems, and a lot of the old formulas won't work to fix them, Edward Keenan writes.RichardLautensToronto Star
