Document 87 of 500

Group fighting Ontario Place spa dealt legal blow in fight against Doug Ford government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/group-fighting-ontario-place-spa-dealt-legal-blow/docview/3066918884/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Group+fighting+Ontario+Place+spa+dealt+legal+blow+in+fight+against+Doug+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An Ontario court has rejected a legal challenge by a citizens' group fighting the development of a private spa at Ontario Place.
A panel of the Ontario Divisional Court ruled Tuesday that Ontario Place for All (OP4A)'s request that the Ford government conduct an environmental assessment of Ontario Place's West Island, where the spa is being built, "cannot succeed" due to new legislation.
The government introduced the Rebuilding Ontario Place Act (ROPA) to the legislature in November 2023, one week after OP4A applied for a judicial review of the decision to exempt the redevelopment of the West Island at Ontario Place from the Environmental Assessment Act. It received royal assent in December.
ROPA exempts the West Island from the Environmental Assessment Act "and therefore nullifies the remedies sought in the application," the decision said.
Norm Di Pasquale, co-chair of Ontario Place for All Inc., a not-for-profit organization with 30,000 supporters, argues that the legislation aims to strip Ontario Place of all rights and protections, including the Environmental Bill of Rights and the Heritage Act.
"It sets a very terrible precedent for the future of our Ontario public institutions," he said. "If they've done this here, what is going to stop them from doing this elsewhere?"
The Austria-based company Therme plans to create a $350-million water spa on the West Island. Provincial taxpayers would pay for the construction of a 2,100-space underground parking garage as part of the project. The plan also involves an expanded Live Nation concert theatre, but it would require the Ontario Science Centre to relocate.
In its application submitted late last year, OP4A argued that the plan would involve destroying 840 trees, vegetation, and is in an internationally recognized landscape. The move, the group claimed, would also contour and fill the lagoons and small waterways, destroying 36,000 square metres of aquatic habitat.
The Ford government responded to the application with a motion to quash the action, but that effort was dismissed in April by a different judge, who argued the extent of the Environmental Assessment Act's application to a public redevelopment project is "an important question of public interest." The decision was lauded as a win by OP4A.
A request to Ontario's Ministry of Infrastructure for comment on Tuesday's decision was deferred to Jack Fazzari, press secretary for Attorney General of Ontario Doug Downey.
"As this matter is in the appeal period, it would be inappropriate to comment," Fazzari wrote in an email.
A spokesperson for Ontario Premier Doug Ford's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Tuesday's decision was a massive blow to his group's efforts, said Di Pasquale. He said the group is reviewing the decision with its legal team to determine next steps.
"We are obviously really disappointed. We were hoping for a different result today."
Chris Glover, MPP for Spadina-Fort York, whose electoral district includes Ontario Place, echoed that sentiment in a statement Tuesday, saying it was "appalling" that the government used its majority to "retroactively change the law and push through this corrupt deal."
Vowing to continue the fight, the NDP critic for democratic reform said other action is being taken to oppose the controversial redevelopment, including an Auditor General's review, a separate legal court case and the "immense people power" against the project.
With files from Francine Kopun and Emily Fagan
CAPTION: The Austria-based company Therme plans to create a $350-million water spa on the West Island.

Subject: Judicial reviews

Document 88 of 500

Peel mayors plan to put regional 'turmoil' behind them after Carolyn Parrish win

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/peel-mayors-plan-put-regional-turmoil-behind-them/docview/3066918198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Peel+mayors+plan+to+put+regional+%27turmoil%27+behind+them+after+Carolyn+Parrish+win&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The three mayors of Peel Region, including new mayor-elect Carolyn Parrish, are confident they can work together to achieve good outcomes for residents - a situation she and her counterparts said hasn't always been the case.
In her comments on Monday after winning the Mississauga mayoral election, Parrish said she believes the region will be stronger with three mayors "that actually get along."
She alluded to disagreements between Caledon, Mississauga and Brampton in the past, while underscoring her good working relationship with Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown and Caledon Mayor Annette Groves.
"I think this time we're going to sit down peacefully and settle everything," Parrish said.
At the top of the list is a plan for the region to get their "fair share" of funding for social services, which Parrish said will be her first ask when she meets with Premier Doug Ford.
She said it's an area where the city is getting "about 60 per cent of what our population deserves."
That assertion is borne out by the numbers: a report commissioned by The Metamorphosis Network, an organization representing more than 100 non-profits in Peel Region, said the province is shortchanging the region more than $850 million annually for community and social services.
Adjusted for inflation, the report found that when it comes to services such as housing, child care, mental health support and youth programs, the region received an average of $578 per resident less than other regions with populations of more than 500,000, including Toronto, York, Durham and Halton.
Both Groves and Brown said their communities have faced similar challenges, whether it's poverty, homelessness, or funding for child welfare.
"We have many people using our food banks. We have many young people couch surfing ... we have our own challenges where we are facing some struggles," Groves said.
"I think it's very exciting that the three mayors are coming together and its three mayors understand the needs of Peel Region," she added.
She said while there was some "turmoil" between Caledon and Mississauga in the past, she's optimistic about a more harmonious relationship going forward.
Brown said with "extraordinary" growth in Peel Region, a collective approach is necessary to tackle issues such as an overstrained shelter system. He added that a lack of funding for social issues such as addiction can trickle down to public safety.
"When there's poverty, it makes people more vulnerable to being lured into organized crime," he said.
Brown said in the past, the relationship in the region was at times adversarial and even "toxic," especially during the proposed dissolution of Peel Region into single-tier municipalities.
He said Parrish has the willingness to confront difficult issues, but also to work collaboratively.
"She doesn't mince words. And so I think we'll be a great team together highlighting some of these issues."
Gurpreet Malhotra, Metamorphosis Network Mississauga co-chair, said the funding disparity for social services has had a demonstrable impact on residents.
"Because we've had such poor investment in our health-care system and in our human services systems ... What that means is worse health outcomes," he said, citing high rates of diabetes which disproportionally affect racialized people.
He said a collaborative approach is needed to see results.
"That translates to happier children, less isolated seniors and families that are stronger," Malhotra said.
A spokesperson for the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said it's committed to building "a more responsive, efficient and person-centred social assistance system."
"We look forward to working with the newly elected mayor on the important issues impacting Mississauga and Peel Region."
CAPTION: Peel Region mayors say they are optimistic about their relationship now that Carolyn Parrish has been elected mayor of Mississauga.

Subject: Mayors; Funding; Social services

Carolyn Parrish to be Mississauga's next mayor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/carolyn-parrish-be-mississaugas-next-mayor/docview/3066558778/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carolyn+Parrish+to+be+Mississauga%27s+next+mayor&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a hard-fought and contentious race that looked increasingly close in the weeks leading up to election day.
With 99 per cent of polls reporting, Parrish had received 43,370 votes compared with 34,839 votes for her nearest rival, Ward 2 Coun. Alvin Tedjo.
Former provincial Liberal cabinet minister and Ward 7 Coun. Dipika Damerla was in third place with 27,053 votes, trailed by Ward 1 Coun. Stephen Dasko who had 22,340 votes.
In May, Parrish saw a comfortable lead over Damerla shrink from the double digits to just five per cent, according to poll results, suggesting her decision to pull out of mayoral debates in the final stretch may have dissuaded some voters from casting their ballot for her.
This year, advance voter turnout increased by 42 per cent compared to the first three days of advance polling in the 2022 municipal election, according to a May 27 news release. In total, more than 24,000 voters participated in advance voting.
More to come.
"Your voices have been heard and together we will build a brighter, more inclusive future for our city," Parrish said.
Parrish has served as both an MP and councillor in Mississauga and said she was confident she she would work with other mayors in the region to deliver results for residents.
"The region is going to be stronger now because we're going to have three mayors who actually get along," she said, referencing past municipal relations with the mayors of Caledon and Brampton.
"She puts in the effort, she works the best she can. I've been to city hall meetings and sat in the chamber and I can see the determination Carolyn has," Sookraj said.
Among the people who congratulated Parrish on her win were Premier Doug Ford, who said he was looking forward to working with her to build "a stronger Mississauga and a stronger Ontario", as well as Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, who showed up at the Convention Centre and said Parrish would be "a formidable mayor ... a very strong voice and a very strong leader for Mississauga".
Asked what she hopes for her legacy as mayor, Parrish said she was aspiring for "good government. Reasonable taxes. Happy people."
CAPTION: Carolyn Parrish, the new mayor of Mississauga, celebrates her victory at the Mississauga Convention Centre. A new mayor of Mississauga was elected Monday night to replace Bonnie Crombie who left to become the leader of the Ontario Liberal party.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, June 11 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on an allegedly Israel-backed "Islamophobic" misinformation campaign, Mississauga's new mayor-elect and trouble brewing in the new Don River Valley.
DON'T MISSThe Trudeau government raised concerns with Israel about its "Islamophobic" misinformation campaign
Ottawa says it raised concerns directly with Israel regarding allegations of a "co-ordinated" and "Islamophobic" misinformation campaign that targeted Canadians online, Stephanie Levitz, Alex Ballingall and Mark Ramzy report. The federal government revealed this to the Star after being asked about a report in a prominent Israeli newspaper alleging the Israeli state hired a private firm to sway public opinion in Canada and the U.S. regarding the ongoing war in Gaza. While a Global Affairs spokesperson said they were unable to "decisively attribute" the campaign to a state actor, the agency corroborated "elements" of the allegations. Here's what that means.
ICYMI: Israeli newspaper Haaretz reported that Israel hired a private political campaign firm called STOIC to allegedly create fake news sites and "hundreds" of social media accounts. These spread pro-Israeli stories about sexual assaults committed by Hamas, alleged links between Hamas and UN groups in the Gaza strip and other "deeply Islamophobic and anti-immigrant" content targeting Canada.Word from Israel: The Israeli embassy in Ottawa did not respond to the Star's request for comment. The Israeli government has denied involvement in the alleged campaign.Meanwhile: Hamas said one of its commanders was killed in a clash with Israeli forces, while Israel said four of its soldiers were killed in a Rafah explosion. Here's the latest on the war.Carolyn Parrish will be Mississauga's next mayor
Former MP and city councillor Carolyn Parrish is set to become Mississauga's next mayor after winning a hard-fought and contentious mayoral byelection, Omar Mosleh reports. "Your voices have been heard and together we will build a brighter, more inclusive future," Parrish said after her Monday night electoral win. She thanked voters for their trust, noting her top priorities as mayor included building more housing, increasing affordability and reducing crime - especially car thefts. Meet Mississauga's new mayor-to-be.
By the numbers: Parrish won the race with 43,494 votes. Her nearest rival, Ward 2 Coun. Alvin Tedjo, garnered 35,005 votes, according to the city's unofficial election results.Context: The Mississauga election - the first in five decades without a clear frontrunner or incumbent - was triggered after former mayor Bonnie Crombie stepped down in January to lead the Ontario Liberal party.Filling the new Don River valley was all going according to plan - then it sprang a leak
The operation to fill the new Don River Valley was all going according to plan - until workers noticed a leak. A crack has formed in one of the three massive concrete walls separating the new river valley from surrounding bodies of water, and water was gushing through, threatening to jeopardize the $1.35-billion flood protection project in the Port Lands. While the walls will eventually come down, connecting the river valley with the lakes, its river bed must first be filled and stabilized at a carefully controlled rate, a spokesperson for Waterfront Toronto told the Star's Mahdis Habibinia. Here's why - and what happens when things go awry.
Background: The mammoth Port Lands reclamation project, including the naturalization of the mouth of the Don River, is among North America's largest infrastructure projects and has been nearly a decade in the making.Why it matters: Experts say the project is "one to celebrate" as it strives to return a derelict and polluted district to its green roots, including functional wetlands capable of absorbing carbon emissions and excess floodwater.WHAT ELSE
The capital gains tax is controversial for the Liberals - and it's about to become a big test for Pierre Poilievre.Doug Ford has quietly disbanded the ministry overseeing a $1 billion Queen's Park renovation."I see a lot of ghosts." Community members share memories as they mourn the loss of the historic St. Anne's church.A man has admitted to killing a nursing student on a TTC bus - but says he wasn't criminally responsible at the time.TDSB employees' use of sick days is well above average. Doug Ford commiserates with Illinois governor over one common pain: 'gridlock traffic'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-commiserates-with-illinois-governor/docview/3066460243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+commiserates+with+Illinois+governor+over+one+common+pain%3A+%27gridlock+traffic%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stuck in traffic? Premier Doug Ford says he feels your pain.
As he signed a new memorandum of understanding to boost the $29 billion in annual trade with Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker in Toronto on Tuesday, Ford commiserated about the logjams.
"There's one thing I can tell the folks here what we have in common, really, gridlock traffic," said the premier, who worked in Chicago while running a division of his family's label business.
"Believe me, I lived years going down to Chicago downtown on the Stevenson or I-90 and you try to go on the Don Valley parking lot or the Gardiner, same situation. We're jammed," he said, referring to major expressways in the Windy City and here.
"We built up around the highways, but we're expanding that."
That's an apparent reference to both the proposed 52-km Highway 413 connecting Highway 401 in Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan and the province's uploading of the Don Valley Parkway and the Gardiner Expressway from the City of Toronto last November.
Pritzker, the wealthy scion of the family that owns the Hyatt hotel chain, hailed Ontario as his state's "number one trading partner."
"Chicago and Toronto have a lot in common. We not only share a similar set of values between the two cities and of course between Ontario and Illinois, but also there is a tremendous amount of trade," he said.
With Ford set to visit Chicago on a trade mission, Pritzker joked: "I'd be happy to take you to a Blackhawks game to show you what real hockey looks like."
Deadpanned the premier, a long-suffering Toronto Maple Leafs fan: "I can't even argue that point."
As part of the new arrangement between Ontario and Illinois, the jurisdictions will establish a "procurement co-operation council as a forum for sharing information on procurement policies and to advance opportunities for suppliers in each jurisdiction."
That's designed to make the supply chains in the province and state even more efficient, reducing roadblocks for businesses.
CAPTION: File photo: Ontario Premier Doug Ford.

Subject: Traffic; Roads & highways

Location: Chicago Illinois; United States--US; Illinois

Full text: Ontario students and teachers
are suffering
The person leading your child's classroom may be a parent, grandparent or lunchroom supervisor - not an actual teacher, June 8
In the article, an honest principal confesses that an emergency replacement staff person could face "massive chaos, behavioural issues, kids hurting each other or themselves, massive vandalism." As a recently retired elementary school teacher with the Toronto District School Board (TDSB), I was on sick leave for more than three months after a grade three student pushed me down on the floor and smashed my head into the terrazzo tiles injuring my rotator cuff, causing a massive head concussion and trauma. The principal never did anything. My daughter, who is now an educational assistant with the York District School Board, is off sick for three months after being beaten up by a student. The anonymous principal in your article is right when he says "the kids are unwell." Many do not agree with Minister of Education Stephen Lecce when he says his government is paying more for education than anytime in history. Former premier Mike Harris decimated Ontario's education system with the funding formula and no government since, including Premier Doug Ford's, has ever addressed that. Our students and our education staff are suffering as a result.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
TDSB teachers are taking an average of 20 sick days a year? Others take an average of 16? When I taught, five days was considered iffy! Whether justified by job stress or not, it indicates we have a huge problem in our schools. The rudeness, fractious behaviour, chaos and violence that are found today, even in early elementary grades, started long before COVID-19. Nor is it simply a money problem. My childhood classes in several schools, serving mostly low-income families, were packed with multiple grades to a classroom, with portables two years after schools opened, with no ESL teachers for the many immigrants of the period and with no specialized help for those with learning disabilities. Yet they were safe, orderly and civilized - probably because teachers were in control.
Anita Dermer, Toronto
The answer to our education woes is woven throughout the article: class size matters! If we are to provide students with educational supports and appropriate well-being measures, Ontario can no longer stuff 32 kindergarten students in a room or give 39 grade seven and eight students a teacher with double curriculum duty. Saying that the province has a "24.5 student to teacher ratio" is incredibly deceiving. Class size maximums need to be firmly held at 25 students per class. Providing anything less is unhealthy, unsafe and undeserving for both teachers and students and makes every day an emergency.
Kelly Crosthwaite, Toronto
The citizens of Toronto have no idea of how close to bursting at the seams our secondary school system is at. We cannot throw money at the education system and expect it to fix itself. There are a multitude of reasons beyond the lack of money and teachers that continue to contribute to its decline. Our teachers face a monumental task to help get our children ready for the next levels of life, whether it is post-secondary education or the job force. They have already managed staggering classroom numbers for a long time. Now in 2024 we've piled on duties that began with social media and pandemic issues and end with the unmotivated child that can't depend on adequate child-rearing from a parent who's own plate is full with the problems surviving the harsh realities of living. Teachers were never meant to be so many things to so many students of huge class sizes.
Daniel Kowbell, Mississauga
Buy a vowel
to say thanks for the memories
Pat Sajak says goodbye to "Wheel of Fortune," June 7
Reflecting on host Pat Sajak's goodbye to "Wheel of Fortune," I think of the words of Cole Porter: "It was great fun, but it was just one of those things."
JoAnn Lee Frank, Clearwater, Fla.
Legislators who won't legislate
Ford swaps education and energy ministers in cabinet shuffle, promotes MPP who resigned as minister in Greenbelt scandal, June 6
Premier Doug Ford just gave MPPs seven extra weeks away from the legislature, for a total of 19 weeks. That is over one third of the year that MPPs won't be legislating! So much for increased productivity in Ontario.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
If the MPPs are returning to the legislature on Oct. 21, instead of just after Labour Day, does that mean they will accept less pay and save all of us taxpayers a bit of money that could be spent on health care or education? I doubt it.
Ken Daley, Georgetown
Appreciate the little things
A beloved 200-year-old tree at Queen's Park had to come down. It's back now - sort of, June 8
"When life gives you lemons, make lemonade" is an idiom never more true after reading the article. Apparently, the reason for the chop of the magnificent oak trees in Queen's Park is unknown, but happily, talented artist Robert Jarvis rescued the wounded wood and created a remarkably beautiful bench for all to admire and rest upon. There are so many sad and truly important issues in this world on which to focus, but sometimes a feel good story like this must be recognized and celebrated - beauty and purpose from the ashes of loss. Thank you to reporter Reanna Julien for adding some joy to our day.
Ginny Moeser, Kawartha Lakes
What a wonderful, thoughtful and delightful story. It reminded me of a book I read to my primary school students through the years called the "The Giving Tree." No matter how often I read it, it always brought a tear to my eye. Even though the book has been divisive in recent years, it carries a message of unconditional love, which is so important for young people to hear and understand, especially as we commemorate and remember all those who sacrificed their lives 80 years ago, so we could live in freedom and sit, rest and build relationship upon this magnificent oak bench.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Good luck to new editor-in-chief
Nicole MacIntyre named Toronto Star editor-in-chief as Anne Marie Owens steps away, June 10
Just read about journalist Nicole MacIntyre's appointment as editor-in-chief of the Star. I am impressed that she (and owner Jordan Bitove) wants to make Toronto better. I am pleased to read that. I hope she will also see the paper accommodate more positive stories. There is so much negativism in the air these days I look forward to more positivity. This is a good news story.
David Kirk, Orangeville
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Students; Classrooms; Books; Elementary schools; Teachers; Education; Class size; School boards

Document 93 of 500

Rishi Sunak skipped a D-Day memorial to continue his own disastrous campaign. 'What's next: Punching Judi Dench?'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rishi-sunak-skipped-d-day-memorial-continue-his/docview/3066238949/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rishi+Sunak+skipped+a+D-Day+memorial+to+continue+his+own+disastrous+campaign.+%27What%27s+next%3A+Punching+Judi+Dench%3F%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The knock against artificial intelligence is that it doesn't yet have the ability to think strategically across a number of tasks. And so perhaps it's fitting that Rishi Sunak wants to be known as the "AI prime minister."
The Conservative election campaign in Britain remains a strategic and tactical mess. It's more Dunkirk than it is D-Day, a fact reinforced last week when Sunak skipped out on the key elements of the 80th anniversary commemorations with veterans and other world leaders in Normandy in favour of a political interview in Britain. "What's next," a former Tory operative messaged me, "punching Judi Dench? Pushing David Attenborough down the stairs?"
That Sunak gaffed is no surprise. The knock on Sunak has long been that he's "bad at politics." But to see that badness in full view during a campaign is still breathtaking. The newspapers are now filled with reports of discord within Tory campaign HQ, with some pining under anonymity for a midrace change of horse. Arise, Lord (David) Cameron of Chipping Norton?
Adding insult to these many injuries is the fact there is a new entrant in the race to become British prime minister: Nigel Farage. Sensing Tory weakness, Farage has thrown his hat back into the ring, taking over control of the Reform U.K. campaign and running for a seat in Parliament in a downtrodden corner of Essex.
As a result, Sunak now has to guard his right flank as well as his left, where Labour leader Keir Starmer still holds a 20-plus point advantage. Many Tories now expect Reform to start outpolling their outfit. Mutiny awaits.
The D-Day gaffe certainly blows a gaping hole in Tory plans to shore up their core vote among the elderly. You can't gear the first two weeks of a campaign toward courting seniors only to then turn your back on the greatest of their generation. It's the kind of thing that gets noticed. Especially by those already tempted by Farage. Indeed, reports from Tory canvassers is that it was coming up everywhere on the doorstep.
Meanwhile, Starmer's Labour Party is shoring up its advantage by promising a five-year freeze on the three major taxes in Britain - income tax, national insurance and VAT - thus cauterizing the usual wound of not being trusted with the economy.
Starmer is still not an impressive performer - even Sunak matched him in their first head-to-head debate - but at least he's not filling up his own feet with lead.
The confluence of Farage and a steady Labour campaign has some Conservatives expecting the worst. The words "Canada" and "1993" are now being put about by disgruntled Tories and the media, alike. And while the Conservatives should avoid going down to a handful of seats, as their Progressive Conservative cousins did in Canada, they are now looking at shedding over 200 MPs in one go. This will be Sunak's legacy.
As a cabinet minister relayed to me on the day the surprise election was called, this was Sunak's best shot of uniting the party behind him. Faced with the prospect of another long, hot summer of channel crossings and slow economic growth, Sunak was going to steal the march and stamp his authority on events. Well, reality has clapped back.
There are now just over three weeks left in the campaign. The Tory manifesto is expected later this week and will be the last chance to set things down a more favourable track. If the launch doesn't go well and, let's face it, on current form, nobody should expect that it will, then more will break ranks. It could soon be every man and woman for themselves.
But the Tories should resist the urge to panic or split apart. They now face the grim choice of hanging together or hanging separately. There will be another day. There always is. The aftermath of decline can be renewal, not obsolescence. The retreat at Dunkirk in 1940 enabled the invasion of Normandy four years later.
It might be grim now for the Tories, but a party that sticks together has more of a chance of renewing itself than one that goes down fighting like rats in a sack.
CAPTION: Britain's Prime Minister and Conservative Party leader, Rishi Sunak (third from right), Conservative prospective parliamentary candidate, Jeremy Quin (second from right) and Sussex police and crime commissioner, Katy Bourne (fourth from right) meet with Neighbourhood Watch representatives in Horsham, south of London on Monday during a campaign event in the build-up to the U.K. general election on July 4.
CREDIT: Andrew MacDougall Contributor

Subject: Conservatism; Older people; Prime ministers

Location: Canada; United Kingdom--UK

People: Sunak, Rishi; Dench, Judi

Title: Rishi Sunak skipped a D-Day memorial to continue his own disastrous campaign. 'What's next: Punching Judi Dench?'

Document 94 of 500

Greenbelt fight is far from over

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenbelt-fight-is-far-over/docview/3066238784/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greenbelt+fight+is+far+from+over&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's hard to imagine what happened in Doug Ford's mind when he decided that the stench from the Greenbelt fiasco had dissipated enough to welcome back Steve Clark, the second-most-responsible person behind the debacle - and as house leader no less.
Maybe he thought the public would believe it was a different Steve Clark, who just looked and sounded like the old one?
Or maybe he saw the May 22 Abacus Data poll numbers, which showed his Ontario PCs were up by 13 points over the Liberals. But he may have not looked hard enough: undecideds were up to 28 per cent, and increase of seven per cent.
For many people, however, their opinion was formed nine months ago, when the wrong guy resigned. Clark took a bullet for Ford. But few of us thought it was a rubber bullet and that less than a year later Clark would be back in the cabal.
For those of us watching Ford with one eye and the Greenbelt with the other, this is merely the latest proof that the Greenbelt battle is far from over. After Ford was forced to retreat, the PCs let the public enjoy what they rightly deserved: democracy. Then in no time, their attack on the Greenbelt resumed.
One by one, they have been pursuing new measures like the development of Highway 413 and the Bradford Bypass, two highways that will destroy hundreds of Greenbelt acres in Halton, York and Peel regions and in Simcoe County.
The new approach is "Death to the Greenbelt by a Thousand Cuts," instead of the previous attempt at one big slash.
Ford also knows that the RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal is still ongoing, so it's no wonder he is talking about holding an early election.
This all means that two right things need to happen extremely fast - because as we have seen, this government will do the wrong thing even faster.
First, the RCMP must release its review before the next election, which could be as early as next spring, so that Ford and Clark and everyone else possibly implicated can be judged by voters.
And second, the Greenbelt must be expanded.
The public won the last battle because the premier was told to keep the Greenbelt promise. But now we can see, by this Clark move and others, that his promises are empty, so it's time for us to take over and make one.
It's time for all of us to make a new Greenbelt promise and pledge to do everything we can to make the Greenbelt even larger.
From the time you began reading this oped, Ontario has lost nearly one more acre of irreplaceable farmland to urbanization, gravel mining and other developments (350 acres a day to be exact). The Ford government is in support of it all. And while they want more people to move here, more people means growing more food - which means growing the Greenbelt.
The Greenbelt must be expanded in order to continue to meet Ontario's growing agricultural, climate and nature needs, encourage appropriate housing construction within the existing urban boundaries, and once and for all, tell this government that no means no.
And while the RCMP ultimately may tell them that, we can't sit back and wait for it. (Although the surreal but entirely possible prospect of Clark having to resign twice for the same thing would be unique in Canadian political history.)
The premier is clearly putting the pieces and players together for another Greenbelt attack. The people of Ontario need to remind him who the buck really stops with, by acting instead of reacting, and demanding more Greenbelt, not the cuts he tried to give us once. As the song says, we won't get fooled again.
Burkhard Mausberg has
worked on Ontario's Greenbelt for over two decades and is
the author of "The Greenbelt - Protecting and Cultivating
a Great Ontario Treasure."
CREDIT: Burkhard Mausberg Contributor Burkhard Mausberg has worked on Ontario's Greenbelt for over two decades and is the author of "The Greenbelt - Protecting and Cultivating a Great Ontario Treasure.";

Subject: Responsible persons

Business indexing term: Subject: Responsible persons

Document 97 of 500

Teachers taking 20 sick days 'indicates we have a huge problem in our schools'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teachers-taking-20-sick-days-indicates-we-have/docview/3066238669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teachers+taking+20+sick+days+%27indicates+we+have+a+huge+problem+in+our+schools%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Watering down education
The person leading your child's classroom may be a parent, grandparent or lunchroom supervisor - not an actual teacher, June 8
In the article, an honest principal confesses that an emergency replacement staff person could face "massive chaos, behavioural issues, kids hurting each other or themselves, massive vandalism." As a recently retired elementary school teacher with the Toronto District School Board (TDSB), I was on sick leave for more than three months after a grade three student pushed me down on the floor and smashed my head into the terrazzo tiles injuring my rotator cuff, causing a massive head concussion and trauma. The principal never did anything. My daughter, who is now an educational assistant with the York District School Board, is off sick for three months after being beaten up by a student. The anonymous principal in your article is right when he says "the kids are unwell." Many do not agree with Minister of Education Stephen Lecce when he says his government is paying more for education than anytime in history. Former premier Mike Harris decimated Ontario's education system with the funding formula and no government since, including Premier Doug Ford's, has ever addressed that. Our students and our education staff are suffering as a result.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
TDSB teachers are taking an average of 20 sick days a year? Others take an average of 16? When I taught, five days was considered iffy! Whether justified by job stress or not, it indicates we have a huge problem in our schools. The rudeness, fractious behaviour, chaos and violence that are found today, even in early elementary grades, started long before COVID-19. Nor is it simply a money problem. My childhood classes in several schools, serving mostly low-income families, were packed with multiple grades to a classroom, with portables two years after schools opened, with no ESL teachers for the many immigrants of the period and with no specialized help for those with learning disabilities. Yet they were safe, orderly and civilized - probably because teachers were in control.
Anita Dermer, Toronto
The answer to our education woes is woven throughout the article: class size matters! If we are to provide students with educational supports and appropriate well-being measures, Ontario can no longer stuff 32 kindergarten students in a room or give 39 grade seven and eight students a teacher with double curriculum duty. Saying that the province has a "24.5 student to teacher ratio" is incredibly deceiving. Class size maximums need to be firmly held at 25 students per class. Providing anything less is unhealthy, unsafe and undeserving for both teachers and students and makes every day an emergency.
Kelly Crosthwaite, Toronto
The citizens of Toronto have no idea of how close to bursting at the seams our secondary school system is at. We cannot throw money at the education system and expect it to fix itself. There are a multitude of reasons beyond the lack of money and teachers that continue to contribute to its decline. Our teachers face a monumental task to help get our children ready for the next levels of life, whether it is post-secondary education or the job force. They have already managed staggering classroom numbers for a long time. Now in 2024 we've piled on duties that began with social media and pandemic issues and end with the unmotivated child that can't depend on adequate child-rearing from a parent who's own plate is full with the problems surviving the harsh realities of living. Teachers were never meant to be so many things to so many students of huge class sizes.
Daniel Kowbell, Mississauga
Legislators not legislating
Ford swaps education and energy ministers in cabinet shuffle, promotes MPP who resigned as minister in Greenbelt scandal, June 6
Premier Doug Ford just gave MPPs seven extra weeks away from the legislature, for a total of 19 weeks. That is over one third of the year that MPPs won't be legislating! So much for increased productivity in Ontario.
Michael Gilmor, Ajax
If the MPPs are returning to the legislature on Oct. 21, instead of just after Labour Day, does that mean they will accept less pay and save all of us taxpayers a bit of money that could be spent on health care or education? I doubt it.
Ken Daley, Georgetown
Appreciate the little things
A beloved 200-year-old tree at Queen's Park had to come down. It's back now - sort of, June 8
"When life gives you lemons, make lemonade" is an idiom never more true after reading the article. Apparently, the reason for the chop of the magnificent oak trees in Queen's Park is unknown, but happily, talented artist Robert Jarvis rescued the wounded wood and created a remarkably beautiful bench for all to admire and rest upon. There are so many sad and truly important issues in this world on which to focus, but sometimes a feel good story like this must be recognized and celebrated - beauty and purpose from the ashes of loss. Thank you to reporter Reanna Julien for adding some joy to our day.
Ginny Moeser, City of Kawartha Lakes
What a wonderful, thoughtful and delightful story. It reminded me of a book I read to my primary school students through the years called the "The Giving Tree." No matter how often I read it, it always brought a tear to my eye. Even though the book has been divisive in recent years, it carries a message of unconditional love, which is so important for young people to hear and understand, especially as we commemorate and remember all those who sacrificed their lives 80 years ago, so we could live in freedom and sit, rest and build relationship upon this magnificent oak bench.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Thanks for the memories
Pat Sajak says goodbye to "Wheel of Fortune," June 7
Reflecting on host Pat Sajak's goodbye to "Wheel of Fortune," I think of the words of Cole Porter: "It was great fun, but it was just one of those things."
JoAnn Lee Frank, Clearwater, Fla.
Good luck, Nicole
Nicole MacIntyre named Toronto Star editor-in-chief as Anne Marie Owens steps away, June 10
Just read about journalist Nicole MacIntyre's appointment as editor-in-chief of the Star. I am impressed that she (and owner Jordan Bitove) wants to make Toronto better. I am pleased to read that. I hope she will also see the paper accommodate more positive stories. There is so much negativism in the air these days I look forward to more positivity. This is a good news story.
David Kirk, Orangeville
CAPTION: The Toronto District School Board's reliance on emergency replacement personnel to supervise classes soared this year. The practice has raised concerns.

Document 98 of 500

Premier is taking it back to 'axe the tax'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/premier-is-taking-back-axe-tax/docview/3066238646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Premier+is+taking+it+back+to+%27axe+the+tax%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're wondering what keeps Doug Ford awake at night, how he spends every waking moment during the day, I can now reveal his most frantic and fantastical obsession.
No, it's not the housing crisis, nor the health-care crisis, not even the beer boondoggle - each of which is dragging the province down. None of the above comes close.
"Axe the tax" is his mantra above all else.
Every sinew and synapse of his being is devoted to the federal carbon levy. It consumes him and he consumes it - chewing it over with unrivalled rumination and regurgitation.
"Axe the tax" is his call to arms.
In the six years since Ford first ran for premier, he's been running relentlessly on that slogan. It predates "buck-a-beer" and has outlasted everything since.
"Axe the tax" is the bumper sticker stuck on his forehead.
But will it stick? Or will it wear off - diluted by repetition, co-opted by rival Tories, overtaken by events?
After all, it is now the core theme for Ford's federal Conservative cousin, Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre, as he pre-positions for a national election campaign later next year. Can Ford compete and still get a piece of the "axe the tax" action?
Just watch him. And hear him pre-empt Poilievre.
Today - no less than in 2018 - the slogan soaks up more of Ford's breath and bandwidth than any other issue in Ontario. Back then, he talked it up to win his first election as premier.
Now, he is banking on it to win his third term in power, sooner rather than later. Ford is toying with an early election call in 2025 - one year ahead of the fixed date mandated by law - to make the most of the country's anti-tax animus before Poilievre bites off too much of it.
Ford's sloganeering dominated the Ontario legislature for months before a summer break was announced last week. If you search through the transcripts of Ontario's legislative body, you will be inundated with "job-killing carbon tax" verbiage that is a testament to the Progressive Conservative game plan.
One after another, loyal Tories are assigned to read from a prepared script, posing their planted questions for PC government ministers to hit out of Queen's Park. The ritual is as predictable as it is pointless.
Sam Oosterhoff was the latest backbencher to rise up from his seat in the legislature and ask how the premier would put Ottawa in its place.
"What is our government doing to ensure that we are fighting this job-killing, expensive carbon tax and putting more money back into the pockets of the hard-working people?" the MPP demanded, before claiming, improbably, that voters "are flabbergasted that queen Crombie is committed to bringing forward yet another carbon tax."
Queen Crombie? That would be a reference to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, whom Ford's Tories persist in calling "queen of the carbon tax."
It is a peculiar appellation and allegation, for Crombie only took over the opposition Liberals late last year - several years after Ford took power and presided over the imposition of the federal carbon levy for consumers, and ordered the introduction of a provincial carbon levy for industrial users. The irony is that while Crombie cannot logically or chronologically be connected to the levy, Ford's provenance as "king of Ontario's carbon levy" is an inconvenient fact.
For it was Ford who cancelled Ontario's little-known cap and trade system, conceived of by conservative economists who view it as a more efficient way for the market to put a price on pollution. By doing so, he automatically triggered the federal carbon backstop, designed to ensure that every province does its bit to keep Canada on track to meet its international obligations to restrain global warming.
In 2022, the Ontario government launched its own homegrown tax that charges big polluters $65 per tonne of carbon they emit, rising to $80 a tonne on Jan. 1 - which is, naturally, passed on to consumers (unlike the federal carbon levy, which is fully rebated to most families). But why let contradictions get in the way of campaigns?
Oosterhoff was merely the last on a long list of dozens of backbenchers reading from Ford's script, and he did as he was told. There was no good answer for so bad a question that day, but there was an optimal outcome for Oosterhoff mere hours after he fought the good fight last Thursday.
Ford elevated the 26-year-old loyalist to cabinet as "associate minister for energy." Henceforth, he will be one of the premier's chief mouthpieces for his undying six-year-old crusade. As King of the Carbon Tax, Ford now has another of the faithful in his record-breaking 36-member cabinet, ready to fight a new campaign with an old battle cry.
If they win the day next year with "axe the tax," an updated rallying cry will naturally follow: "The levy is dead! Long live the King!"
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Title: Premier is taking it back to 'axe the tax'

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 11, 2024

Section: News

Document 99 of 500

Parrish prevails in bid to succeed Crombie: Former councillor, MP beats rivals in mayoral byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parrish-prevails-bid-succeed-crombie/docview/3066238609/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parrish+prevails+in+bid+to+succeed+Crombie%3A+Former+councillor%2C+MP+beats+rivals+in+mayoral+byelection&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former Mississauga councillor and MP Carolyn Parrish will be the city's next mayor.
It was a hard-fought and contentious mayoral race that looked increasingly close in the weeks leading up to election day.
"Your voices have been heard and together we will build a brighter, more inclusive future for our city," said Parrish, after winning the mayoral byelection on Monday night.
According to the city's unofficial election results, Parrish received 43,494 votes compared with 35,005 votes for her nearest rival, Coun. Alvin Tedjo (Ward 2).
Parrish said she was confident she could work with other mayors in Peel Region to deliver results for residents.
"The region is going to be stronger now because we're going to have three mayors who actually get along," she said, referring to her counterparts in Brampton and Caledon.
Former provincial Liberal cabinet minister and Coun. Dipika Damerla (Ward 7) was third with 27,119 votes. Coun. Stephen Dasko (Ward 1) received 22,408 votes.
In May, Parrish saw a comfortable lead over Damerla shrink from double digits to just five per cent, according to poll results, suggesting her decision to pull out of mayoral debates in the final stretch may have dissuaded some voters from casting their ballot for her.
This year, advance voter turnout increased by 42 per cent compared to the first three days of advance polling in the 2022 municipal election. In total, more than 24,000 voters participated in advance voting.
"She puts in the effort. She works the best she can. I've been to city hall meetings and sat in the chamber and I can see the determination Carolyn has," Parrish supporter and Mississauga resident Hardat Sookraj said at the election night celebration at the Mississauga Convention Centre.
The election, the first in five decades in Mississauga without a clear front-runner or incumbent, was triggered when former mayor Bonnie Crombie stepped down in January to serve as Ontario Liberal party leader.
Pollsters said the top issues on voters' minds were housing, affordability and crime. A plan to install bike lanes along a five-kilometre stretch of Bloor Street also emerged as a divisive issue after Damerla pledged to reopen the project if she won.
That prompted criticism from some fellow councillors who said the move undermined council's decisions and incited "anger and hate."
A member of a residents' association said they were concerned about lane reductions for drivers, while a cycling advocacy group said the issue highlighted how Mississauga was struggling to reconcile its "suburban past and urban future."
Parrish's announcement in mid-May that she wouldn't be participating in future debates was a turning point that appeared to create a dent in her cosy lead. Parrish said she made the decision after she was "personally threatened." That came a week after she made comments at an all-candidates debate that critics labelled transphobic and discriminatory toward refugee claimants.
Parrish said some of her first orders of business would be to unite council members and obtain more money for social services from the province.
Among the people who congratulated Parrish on her win was Premier Doug Ford, who said he was looking forward to working with her to build "a stronger Mississauga and a stronger Ontario."
Bonnie Crombie told Parrish supporters Monday night that she would be "a formidable mayor ... a very strong voice and a very strong leader for Mississauga."
Asked what she hopes to achieve as her legacy as mayor, Parrish said she was aspiring for "good government. Reasonable taxes. Happy people."
On Monday, voters also determined who would fill the Ward 5 council seat left vacant by Parrish in March when she resigned to run for mayor. Natalie Hart, the general manager of the Malton BIA, won with just over 3,700 votes.
CREDIT: Omar Mosleh Toronto Star

Subject: Election results; Local elections; Mayors; Councils; Voters; Debates; Voter behavior

People: Parrish, Carolyn; Crombie, Bonnie

Document 102 of 500

Ford disbands ministry of legislative affairs: Department had been tasked with oversight of $1-billion renovation of legislature building

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-disbands-ministry-legislative-affairs/docview/3066238565/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+disbands+ministry+of+legislative+affairs%3A+Department+had+been+tasked+with+oversight+of+%241-billion+renovation+of+legislature+building&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has quietly disbanded the ministry overseeing the $1-billion renovation of the Legislative Assembly building at Queen's Park.
Lost amid last week's major cabinet shuffle was the dissolution of the ministry of legislative affairs that had been run by Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
As first disclosed by the Star, Ford also stripped Calandra of his duties as government house leader after the premier was blindsided by Speaker Ted Arnott's April ban on the Palestinian kaffiyeh in the legislature. The premier felt the house leader should have warned him the Speaker was banning the black and white scarves because they are "political" garments.
The planned retrofit of the building on Queen's Park Circle - dating back to 1893 and affectionately known as "the pink palace" - is supposed to begin in 2027 and will take years to complete.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, already overseeing the delayed $1.5-billion renovation of the sprawling Macdonald Block office complex, as well as the controversial Ontario Place regeneration project, assumes responsibility for the legislature's repairs.
Surma spoke candidly this spring about the challenges of modernizing the 53-year-old Macdonald Block at the corner of Bay and Wellesley streets, across from Queen's Park. Some 6,000 bureaucrats and political aides are supposed to work there, but they are marooned in rented offices around the city because there's no timeline for them to return to the legislative precinct.
While the Macdonald Block remains in limbo, Ford's dismantling of the ministry of legislative affairs creates further uncertainty.
Calandra had been a firm hand overseeing the Queen's Park Restoration Secretariat created in March 2023 to renovate the Legislative Assembly.
That secretariat, with help from an all-party legislative committee, is currently in talks with potential private landlords for a new, temporary legislature that's expected to be built in an office building near Queen's Park.
The government is negotiating to rent 500,000 square feet of space so the temporary accommodation would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is important because Ford, Arnott, Official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work in the building.
The Star has a four-person bureau in the assembly where other major media outlets such as CBC, CTV, the Globe and Mail, the Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada, among others, also have offices.
There is an urgency to the project - the government has warned "critical building systems such as plumbing, heating, electrical and IT services, are at or beyond end of life and at risk of total failure."
On Parliament Hill, the Centre Block, which houses the Peace Tower and House of Commons, has been under renovation for several years and is not expected to reopen until 2031 at a cost of $5 billion. MPs use a temporary Commons in the neighbouring West Block.
Unwinding the ministry of legislative affairs was but one part of Ford's cabinet reshuffle that also saw Stephen Lecce moved from education to the renamed Ministry of Energy and Electrification in a swap with Todd Smith.
Steve Clark, who resigned from cabinet last September over the premier's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, will be government house leader. But he will not sit in cabinet or face opposition questions during question period, which resumes Oct. 21.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 103 of 500

We were punished for speaking our languages and banned from using them in the legislature. My speech turned a page

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-were-punished-speaking-our-languages-banned/docview/3066238477/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+were+punished+for+speaking+our+languages+and+banned+from+using+them+in+the+legislature.+My+speech+turned+a+page&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: May 28 was a historic day in Ontario. I finally spoke in my language, in Queen's Park, which has been heard on this land for tens of thousands of years. Our people say, since time began.
May 28 was the first time I was able to speak my language, Anishininiimowin, in the settler government, the government that says it speaks for all of us. Yet it has not.
I spoke for all of those First Nations people who were punished and abused for speaking their language. From the time the settlers made their way to our lands and unjustly and unlawfully, claimed them as theirs under the false pretence of terra nullius - the idea from Christianity that this land belonged to no one, so it was free for the taking.
But this land we stand on, this land took care of us, it nurtured our families for generations and generations.
I spoke for all of the First Nations children, who were stolen away from their lands, their families and communities, from everything they knew. These children were frightened, they were lonely, and they were taken away to places where they were told they could not speak unless they spoke English.
Once at these so-called schools, usually hundreds of miles away from home, they were separated from their siblings, and I know these children were terrified. They were afraid to speak because if they did, they'd be beaten. They could not express themselves; they couldn't show who they truly were.
For most of these children their language was taken away, beaten out of them, and so were their spirits, who they are, their dreams and hopes, their love and their language.
I spoke for all those children, for those who survived, who made it home to parents they could no longer talk to because the children had lost their language.
I spoke for those children who became adults, living under a foreign system, one that abused us and stole us from our very identities.
I spoke for the thousands of First Nations children who never made it home from these Indian residential schools. For the thousands who died in Indian hospitals or tuberculosis sanatoriums.
I spoke for all our ancestors who never made it home.
I spoke for those children in one of our languages because I am one of those children.
They are me. I am them. Our spirits speak as one.
Our children are meant to grow up with our languages, laughing and joking with our parents, grandparents and friends. Our children are meant to learn our ways of life from the land that gives us our language.
I was lucky. I had my summers. I had my time on the land with my family. My beautiful mother, and my late father. I was thinking of him. My sisters, brothers and I had our most cherished memories with our parents, living by the lakes and rivers of our lands, learning to fish, to hunt, to trap. This kept the language alive in us. We are lucky. We know many did not have this opportunity.
May 28 was a day to reflect, to remember, and to celebrate - a day when we began to turn a page in Ontario's dark history.
After all of this time, for over 150 years in Ontario, since this legislature existed, has it been forbidden for me or any other First Nations person to speak our language on this land in this legislature?
I hope that this moment of change encourages others to learn their language and that all other Indigenous languages survive.
Our people need our languages; they are medicine.
We need our Indigenous languages in the curriculum, taught in schools, and included in the history books and literature classes.
We need Indigenous language classes besides English and French. This is the road to a just society.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa holds an eagle feather as he stands in the Ontario legislature on May 28, 2024, and speaks in his language for the first time in history that Indigenous language has been used in the provincial legislature to ask a question of the government.
CREDIT: Sol Mamakwa Contributor

Document 104 of 500

Doug Ford quietly disbands ministry overseeing $1B Queen's Park renovation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-quietly-disbands-ministry-overseeing-1b/docview/3066558752/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+quietly+disbands+ministry+overseeing+%241B+Queen%27s+Park+renovation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has quietly disbanded the ministry overseeing the $1-billion renovation of the Legislative Assembly building at Queen's Park.
Lost amid Thursday's major cabinet shuffle was the dissolution of the ministry of legislative affairs that had been run by Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra.
As first disclosed by the Star, Ford also stripped Calandra of his duties as government house leader after the premier was blindsided by Speaker Ted Arnott's April ban on the Palestinian kaffiyeh in the legislature. The premier felt the house leader should have warned him the Speaker was banning the black and white scarves because they are "political" garments.
The planned retrofit of the building on Queen's Park Circle - dating back to 1893 and affectionately known as "the pink palace" - is supposed to begin in 2027 and will take years to complete.
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, already overseeing the delayed $1.5-billion renovation of the sprawling Macdonald Block office complex as well as the controversial Ontario Place regeneration project, assumes responsibility for the legislature's repairs.
Earlier this spring, Surma spoke candidly about the challenges modernizing the 53-year-old Macdonald Block at the corner of Bay and Wellesley Streets, across from Queen's Park.
Some 6,000 bureaucrats and political aides are supposed to work there, but they are marooned in rented offices throughout the city because there's no timeline for them to return to the legislative precinct.
"This has been a very difficult time to build," the infrastructure minister said in April, noting "unforeseen challenges," including the COVID-19 pandemic, had led to rising costs.
"There are cost overruns. Fixing the issues that we faced is of great importance because these are taxpayer dollars."
While the Macdonald Block remains in limbo, Ford's dismantling of the ministry of legislative affairs creates further uncertainty.
Calandra had been a firm hand overseeing the Queen's Park Restoration Secretariat created in March 2023 to renovate the Legislative Assembly.
That secretariat, with help from an all-party legislative committee, is currently in talks with potential private landlords for a new, temporary legislature that's expected to be built in an office building near Queen's Park.
The government is negotiating to rent 500,000 square feet of space so the temporary accommodation would have the same number of offices as the existing legislature.
That is important because Ford, Arnott, Official Opposition Leader Marit Stiles, dozens of MPPs, scores of journalists in the Queen's Park press gallery as well as hundreds of political aides, librarians and legislative staffers currently work in the building.
The Star has a four-person bureau in the assembly where other major media outlets such as CBC, CTV, the Globe and Mail, The Canadian Press, the Toronto Sun, CityNews, Global, Radio-Canada, among others, also have offices.
There is an urgency to the project - the government has warned "critical building systems such as plumbing, heating, electrical, and IT services, are at or beyond end of life and at risk of total failure."
On Parliament Hill, the Centre Block, which houses the Peace Tower and House of Commons, has been under renovation for several years and is not expected to reopen until 2031 at a cost of $5 billion. MPs use a temporary Commons in the neighbouring West Block.
Unwinding the ministry of legislative affairs was but one part of Ford's cabinet reshuffle that also saw Stephen Lecce moved from education to the renamed Ministry of Energy and Electrification in a swap with Todd Smith.
The 36-member executive council is now the biggest cabinet in provincial government history, though there are only nine women. Ford added four men and one woman.
Steve Clark, who resigned from cabinet last September over the premier's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, will be government house leader. But Clark will not sit in cabinet or face opposition questions during the legislature's question period that resumes Oct. 21.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as House Leader Paul Calandra answers a question during question period in the legislature at Queens Park on Oct. 19, 2023.

Document 105 of 500

MPs facing foreign interference allegations should be named, Star readers say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mps-facing-foreign-interference-allegations/docview/3066037706/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPs+facing+foreign+interference+allegations+should+be+named%2C+Star+readers+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Treason is Ottawa?
We can't name MPs in foreign interference allegations, committee chair says, June 5
When elected officials are working against our country, Canadians are entitled to know who they are and what they are doing. We need to know who we are voting for and who they are working to represent. If they are working against us, they need to be held accountable and suffer the consequences of their actions. No more secrecy. We deserve to know.
Rick Lockman, Orillia
This could be a fatal scandal for the Liberals if they do not act quickly and long before the next election. This is just another nail in their coffin. Not addressing potential treason will just push more voters toward Conservatives. I would hate to think how we Canadians will be better off with this scenario. The RCMP need to be pushed to bring this to an early conclusion. Canadians have the right to know just how deep this scandal goes.
Brian Mellor, Midland
It is possible one, or some, parliamentarians have committed treason. Investigate them, name them, fire them, charge them and, if found guilty, jail them.
Donna Devlin, Toronto
The Star should consider more
The Liberal government's approach to foreign interference? A whole lot of inaction, June 6
Why does the Star argue that in support of maintaining confidence in our democracy, the Trudeau government should act against the laws and norms of this same democracy? The Star argues that the names of suspect MPs should be released, which would contravene Canadian security law. Governments that break their own laws are the complete opposite of democratic.
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc says Canadians should trust the RCMP. This is casually brushed aside by the Star as a delay tactic. When in fact, this is what democracies are supposed to do. We do not publicly accuse someone without solid evidence. We do not grab someone off the street and detain them without due process.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
The Star has rightly reported the government's apparent inaction on this issue, pointing out how this matter is of major concern to the nation and threatens our democratic process. What I do not understand is why the same concern does not appear to be applied in relation to our antiquated electoral process. We occasionally see an article pointing out that the "first past the post (FPP)" system does not really reflect the wishes of the electorate. That is well and good when there were only two party contenders, but with three or more it is no longer fair. Is this because the party in power fears that majority governments are much less likely to happen in a proportional representation system? And are majority governments representative of the wishes of the electorate? So many of us decide not to bother voting at all when we know that our vote will not count, depending on which riding we happen to be in. Is this not enough of a concern for our democracy? I would hope that the media would press on this issue with as much concern as that expressed in relation to foreign interference.
Margarida Krause, Guelph
Seniors can't afford to retire
Canada's political leaders of all political stripes don't care that a growing number of seniors cannot afford to retire. This is evidenced at every level of government by the lack of senior-friendly policies and programming.
A substantial proportion of seniors had their savings decimated by the 2008 market crash and don't trust the whims of the stock market with their hard-earned retirement savings. Interest bearing investments offer guaranteed returns but have done nothing over the course of the past decade or more to help Canadians save for retirement. To make matters worse, the interest from these investments is fully taxable, further reducing the return.
It is universally agreed by economists that low interest rates hurt seniors. While Canada's political leaders don't officially influence the decisions of the Bank of Canada, they could use their influence to make senior-friendly changes to tax policy or create new senior-friendly programs that would allow seniors to have greater income security in retirement.
It is obvious that Canada's political leaders are more interested in playing self-serving political games than actually serving their constituents. Henry Kissinger once said, "Ninety percent of the politicians give the other ten per cent a bad reputation." It's time for the other ten per cent to step up.
Victor Goodman, Wadena, SK
All eyes on Edmonton
McDavid, Oilers ready for Stanley Cup final after long journey, June 6
The main thing this year is to watch and support the Edmonton Oilers versus the Florida Panthers. It would be nice to see a Canadian team win the Stanley Cup again for the first time in more than 30 years. This hockey team is incredible! It will be fun either way.
Norm Ferguson, Richmond Hill
Not all parents have skills to teach
Ontario is getting ready to update its home ec courses - and wants your input on what needs to change, June 7
A Star letter writers says teaching of home economics should not be done by teachers in school, but by parents at home. Unfortunately, the parents being urged by the reader to instruct their children often don't have those skills themselves. They are financially illiterate, their cooking consists of microwaving frozen supermarket meals or ordering in, and they are as likely to be able to sew as to make bobbin lace. Plus, many of them are exhausted from 40-hour work weeks and multiple-hour commutes for jobs that barely pay the rent. Teaching belongs at school.
Anita Dermer, Toronto
Don't wear red!
Toronto's red-winged blackbirds are on the attack, June 6
As a walker, I am well aware the danger of red-winged blackbirds. I am typically attacked three or four times a year. The Lower Don River Trail (currently closed) is particularly perilous. One thing I've noticed through the years is that attacks are far more likely when I'm wearing something red. It really sets them off. Perhaps the colour reminds them of competitors? Who knows. When walking alone at this time of year, don't wear red. When walking with friends, lend someone else a red baseball cap.
John Gavin, Toronto
CAPTION: Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc said allegations made in a parliamentary report on foreign interference were the result of intelligence being interpreted without the necessary context.
