Document 106 of 500

Doug Ford is again ignoring the lessons of the past

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-again-ignoring-lessons-past/docview/3066037462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+again+ignoring+the+lessons+of+the+past&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the few things we knew for sure in the earliest, most chaotic days of the COVID-19 pandemic in Ontario was that we didn't know enough. We didn't have enough data. We had no streamlined central system for sharing information on test results, outbreaks or mortality. We didn't know how to gather the knowledge we needed or how to share it - except, in many cases, by fax - between all the organizations working on the front lines.
That was 2020. Four years on, we are left to ask how many lives might have been saved had we better information, in real time, about what what was needed and what was working? It was a predictable, preventable lapse in basic pandemic preparation, one caused by successive governments of every stripe that, despite the stark warnings of SARS, repeatedly put short-term politics over public health.
But that was all in the past, right? Things have changed, right? The mammoth human, economic and social catastrophe of COVID-19 was a lesson not even the most myopic politicians could ignore. When the next pandemic comes, Ontario will be ready. We will know enough to know­ about local outbreaks and viral peaks, about infection rates and testing capacities.
We'd like to believe that. Having lived through the trauma of COVID-19, Ontarians certainly have the right to expect it. But that's not what's happening, at least not if the Ford government's truly inexplicable decision to cancel funding for provincial wastewater surveillance is any indication.
Ontario's wastewater surveillance system was one of the few true triumphs to come out of the pandemic in this province. When widespread PCR (polymerase chain reaction) testing was suspended in Ontario at the end of 2021, wastewater testing became the most accurate way to determine daily COVID cases in the province, the Star's Megan Ogilvie wrote last week. It works by monitoring wastewater for genetic viral material. Over time, it proved so effective, the province expanded the system to test for other existing or emerging plagues, including new COVID variants.
Until this week, Ontario had one of the most comprehensive wastewater testing systems in the world. Provincial researchers were monitoring 58 sites in Ontario, including big cities, small towns, First Nations and everything in between, Ogilvie reported. We were doing the right thing, the rare thing, in paying attention between pandemics, spending money now to improve our odds of success when disaster inevitably strikes later.
But now Premier Doug Ford and his government are throwing all that away. They informed researchers recently that money for the wastewater program, funded until now by Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks, would end July 31. Why? Because the federal government is modestly expanding its own wastewater surveillance network and the province wants to "avoid duplication" with the federal program, according to an official who spoke to Ogilvie.
Surely one lesson of COVID-19 is that governments must work together to confront pandemics, rather than playing jurisdictional hot potato. Moreover, the new federal network won't come close to matching the capacity of the existing provincial system. It will have fewer sites in Ontario (between five and nine total) and it won't feed data directly into Ontario institutions, like hospital networks, public health units and the provincial ministry. It is the Economy Basic version of Ontario's existing First Class system.
Cutting the Ontario wastewater surveillance network now is a betrayal of everyone who sacrificed in the fight against COVID-19. It is a mistake on its own. More troubling, it suggests we are already repeating the mistakes we made after SARS. Ford, like his predecessors, is ignoring the lessons of the past and failing the people of the future, setting us up for more fumbling and more deaths when the next pandemic comes.
CAPTION: University of Guelph engineering student Jonathan Evans holds a container of waste water collected from a sewer outside a student residence on campus. The water collected will be divided into three samples and sent to separate labs to determine if COVID-19 RNA is present.

Subject: Public health; Health surveillance; Pandemics; COVID-19

Title: Doug Ford is again ignoring the lessons of the past

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 10, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 107 of 500

Ford ignores lessons of Ontario's past at our peril

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ignores-lessons-ontarios-past-at-our-peril/docview/3065889246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ignores+lessons+of+Ontario%27s+past+at+our+peril&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the few things we knew for sure in the earliest, most chaotic days of the COVID-19 pandemic in Ontario was that we didn't know enough. We didn't have enough data. We had no streamlined central system for sharing information on test results, outbreaks or mortality. We didn't know how to gather the knowledge we needed or how to share it - except, in many cases, by fax - between all the organizations working on the front lines.
That was 2020. Four years on, we are left to ask how many lives might have been saved if we had better information, in real time, about what was needed and what was working. It was a predictable, preventable lapse in basic pandemic preparation, one caused by successive governments of every stripe that, despite the stark warnings of SARS, repeatedly put short-term politics over public health.
But that was all in the past, right? Things have changed, right? The mammoth human, economic and social catastrophe of COVID-19 was a lesson not even the most myopic politicians could ignore. When the next pandemic comes, Ontario will be ready. We will know enough to know about local outbreaks and viral peaks, about infection rates and testing capacities.
We'd like to believe that. Having lived through the trauma of COVID-19, Ontarians certainly have the right to expect it. But that's not what's happening, at least not if the Ford government's truly inexplicable decision to cancel funding for provincial wastewater surveillance is any indication.
Ontario's wastewater surveillance system was one of the few true triumphs to come out of the pandemic in this province. When widespread PCR (polymerase chain reaction) testing was suspended in Ontario at the end of 2021, wastewater testing became the most accurate way to determine daily COVID cases in the province, the Star's Megan Ogilvie wrote last week. It works by monitoring wastewater for genetic viral material. Over time, it proved so effective, the province expanded the system to test for other existing or emerging plagues, including new COVID variants.
Until this week, Ontario had one of the most comprehensive wastewater testing systems in the world. Provincial researchers were monitoring 58 sites in Ontario, including big cities, small towns, First Nations and everything in between, Ogilvie reported. We were doing the right thing, the rare thing, in paying attention between pandemics, spending money now to improve our odds of success when disaster inevitably strikes later.
But now Premier Doug Ford and his government are throwing all that away. They informed researchers recently that money for the wastewater program, funded until now by Ministry of Environment, Conservation and Parks, would end July 31. Why? Because the federal government is modestly expanding its own wastewater surveillance network and the province wants to "avoid duplication" with the federal program, according to an official who spoke to Ogilvie.
Surely one lesson of COVID-19 is that governments must work together to confront pandemics, rather than playing jurisdictional hot potato. Moreover, the new federal network won't come close to matching the capacity of the existing provincial system. It will have fewer sites in Ontario (between five and nine total) and it won't feed data directly into Ontario institutions, like hospital networks, public health units and the provincial ministry. It is the Economy Basic version of Ontario's existing First Class system.
Cutting the Ontario wastewater surveillance network now is a betrayal of everyone who sacrificed in the fight against COVID-19. It is a mistake on its own. More troubling, it suggests we are already repeating the mistakes we made after SARS. Ford, like his predecessors, is ignoring the lessons of the past and failing the people of the future, setting us up for more fumbling and more deaths when the next pandemic comes.
CAPTION: Cutting the Ontario wastewater surveillance network is a betrayal of everyone who sacrificed in the fight against COVID-19 and suggests we are already repeating the mistakes we made after SARS.Mathew McCarthyWaterloo Region Record file photo

Document 108 of 500

Trudeau is Ford's 'political sunscreen'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-is-fords-political-sunscreen/docview/3065889245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+is+Ford%27s+%27political+sunscreen%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford shocked the public Thursday when he announced a snap cabinet shuffle, shifting some of his highest-profile ministers into new jobs and bringing even more backbenchers into the executive team. The move rekindled what has been the hottest rumour in Queen's Park gossip circles for weeks, that Ford is gearing up to call an early election. But the question still remains, why would a premier with commanding majority risk it all with an early vote just halfway through his second mandate?
The conventional wisdom is that Ford and his advisers are worried about federal Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre winning power next year and quickly making massive, unpopular spending cuts. Ontario voters might then decide during a provincial election in 2026 they'd like to balance out the federal Tories with a provincial Liberal government. There's precedent for that kind of thing: Voters like to split the difference.
But in my view, Ford's fortunes are more tied to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau than they are to Poilievre. Ford and Trudeau may be joined at the hip. They love announcing new EV plants together. But Trudeau is also providing a much more valuable service to his provincial counterpart: He's absorbing all the ire from voters fed up with the status quo and looking for change.
Angry about traffic on the Gardiner? Pissed off you can't find a family doctor? Panicking about a rent increase? Odds are, you blame Trudeau, not Ford, even if all those problems are more the purview of Queen's Park than Parliament Hill.
For Ford, Trudeau is like political sunscreen: He blocks all the most dangerous rays. Right now, he's soaking up all the anger voters have over affordability, high interest rates and general economic malaise, the same anger currently swamping Prime Minister Rishi Sunak in the U.K. and weighing down President Joe Biden in the U.S.
Midway through his second term, Ford should be sagging under the weight of his accumulated choices in office. But he's not. His polling doesn't even reflect a spate of scandals and missteps: The RCMP investigation into the abandoned Greenbelt giveaway, the sell-off of Ontario Place, blatant patronage appointments and the decision to spend as much as a billion dollars just to speed up the sale of booze in corner and grocery stores.
It isn't Ford's everyman political charm that's making him bulletproof. It's Trudeau. The prime minister is taking all the fire while Ford relaxes in the trenches, safe behind his Justin-shaped shield.
If Ford does decide to call an early election, and Thursday's cabinet shuffle suggests it is a real possibility now, that will be a big reason why. He isn't afraid of running in the shadow of Pierre Poilievre. He's worried about what voters might think of him when they don't have Trudeau to blame for all their problems anymore.
For new Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie, that creates two related challenges. She has to carry the brand hangover from the federal party - for now, Trudeau and Liberal are still synonymous in most voters' minds - and she needs Ford's support to soften.
I'm biased. But I think Crombie has gotten a lot of big things right over the last six months. She's filled the party's war chest, grown the party's vote share in recent byelections and is winning the progressive primary with the NDP. But her polling numbers have stalled. Her success is being held back by an even bigger brand and personality. Not Doug Ford. Justin Trudeau.
The Ontario Liberals need to focus on tying Ford to his least popular and most controversial policies while creating some distance between the provincial and federal Liberals in voters' minds. Crombie and her team clear can't count on Ford waiting until 2026 to call an election, nor can they expect the prime minister to take a long walk in the sand this summer.
What they can do is take their cue from Thursday's cabinet change and start acting like the election is already underway.
Ashley Csanady is a Vice-
President at McMillan Vantage and a former communications staffer to Ontario Premier
Kathleen Wynne.
CREDIT: Ashley Csanady Contributing Columnist Ashley Csanady is a Vice- President at McMillan Vantage and a former communications staffer to Ontario Premier Kathleen Wynne.;

Document 109 of 500

HISTORIC CHURCH RAVAGED BY FIRE: ST. ANNE'S BLAZE DESTROYS CENTURY-OLD MURALS BY GROUP OF SEVEN ARTISTS

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/historic-church-ravaged-fire/docview/3065889144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=HISTORIC+CHURCH+RAVAGED+BY+FIRE%3A+ST.+ANNE%27S+BLAZE+DESTROYS+CENTURY-OLD+MURALS+BY+GROUP+OF+SEVEN+ARTISTS&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Don Beyers found out his church was on fire early Sunday morning, he threw on the first pair of pants he could find and raced - on foot - to the site.
But it was too late.
"Fire was already spewing out the dome at the top," Beyers said of the roof at St. Anne's Anglican Church in Little Portugal, where he has been parish priest for three years. "And I knew there was nothing we could do."
Sixteen to 18 murals of Jesus' life painted by three members of Canada's iconic Group of Seven dotted the walls and ceilings of the church, Beyers said, and were likely all reduced to rubble. The four-alarm fire erupted at the building on Gladstone Avenue, between College and Dundas streets, just before 8 a.m.
Most of the church was gone.
"I was crushed," Beyers said. "I just started to cry."
The "priceless" paintings, completed in 1923, were what Beyers called "a gem of Canadian culture."
It's "an extraordinary loss," he said later in the morning to media outside the church.
"This area means a lot to us. While this is incredibly devastating for my congregation, it's devastating for this community," he said. "I cannot express enough how far-reaching this church fire is going to be."
"We have our fingers crossed," Toronto Fire Services division commander Steven Darling said from a laneway near the still-smouldering building. "But it's highly unlikely there's anything we can save inside the building."
Toronto Fire hasn't determined the cause of the blaze, noting that the fire marshal's office and its teams will be investigating over the coming days. A spokesperson said no one was inside the church at the time of the fire and there were no reports of injuries.
In a press release Sunday evening, Toronto police announced they had opened an online portal to collect videos or photos from the public to assist investigators.
According to Beyers, the church's doors were locked and all the lights were shut off the night before.
"It's a real mystery to us how this even happened," Beyers said.
Darling said dozens of fire trucks, crews and kilometres of hose were dispatched to the site, along with an aerial tower that shot water onto the church's roof. Firefighters also entered through the locked door at the back of the church, but after about 10 minutes they had to retreat when the roof started collapsing, Darling said.
Deputy Fire Chief Jim Jessop told reporters at the scene it is possible the building could experience further collapses given its age and the volume of water used to extinguish the fire. Crews have created a "safe area" around the building as they continue to tend to the site.
Fire crews set up hoses and command stations along Dundas Street between Gladstone Avenue and Lisgar Street, where dozens of stores and street vendors were preparing to open for Do West Fest, a large street party held over the weekend. As black plumes of smoke shot into the sky, the festival's organizers weighed whether it should go ahead.
By mid-morning, fire crews had contained the blaze, downgrading it to a two-alarm fire, and moved the remaining vehicles and equipment to Dufferin Street, freeing up Dundas for the festival to proceed.
But it was an emotional scene as locals and visitors gathered in an alleyway off Gladstone to watch firefighters put out remaining hot spots at the historic church.
Laura Storey, 35, said she had "instant tears" when her partner came home after a run and broke the news of the fire.
She'd been going to St. Anne's for years, she said, most recently to a candlelight ceremony on Easter. Not long before that, she celebrated her birthday at the church during mass on Christmas Eve.
"We had plans to be out of the city today," she said, a tear falling down her cheek. "But we decided to just walk around here and feel it."
Keith Levenson, a former builder visiting from Vermont, stared in shock. He'd left his Airbnb earlier in the morning to grab a coffee when he saw the fire and found his way to a fence at the back of the church.
"It's a tragedy," he said, noticing the stained glass windows that had been blown out. "It's heartbreaking to see."
A number of local politicians arrived to show their support, including Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who represents the riding of Davenport, and local city Coun. Alejandra Bravo, who said the destroyed art is "something we cannot replace in Canada."
St. Anne's Church was founded on the present site in 1862 before its redesign in 1907, according to the Ontario Heritage Trust. The building was designated a National Historic Site in 1996 and the City of Toronto designated it under the Ontario Heritage Act in 1980. The two-storey church was built in a Byzantine style with a domed roof, which is unusual for an Anglican church and the only one of its kind in Canada.
Around mid-morning, as the reality of what was left of his church set in, Beyers grew emotional.
The first thing that came to mind when he arrived at the site, after racing from his home near Queen and Dovercourt streets, was the people who have been coming through St. Anne's doors for more than a century.
"My heart aches for them," he said. "Aches."
With files from Nathan Bawaan
CREDIT: Ilyas Hussein and Michele Henry Toronto Star With files from Nathan Bawaan

Subject: Fires; Anglican churches

Location: Canada

'Extraordinary loss': Blaze destroys historic St. Anne's Church in Toronto and its 'priceless' Group of Seven murals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/extraordinary-loss-blaze-destroys-historic-st/docview/3066105498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Extraordinary+loss%27%3A+Blaze+destroys+historic+St.+Anne%27s+Church+in+Toronto+and+its+%27priceless%27+Group+of+Seven+murals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Don Beyers found out his church was on fire early Sunday morning, he threw on the first pair of pants he could find and raced - on foot - to the site.
But it was too late.
"Fire was already spewing out the dome at the top," Beyers said of the roof at St. Anne's Anglican Church in Little Portugal, where he has been parish priest for three years. "And I knew there was nothing we could do."
That included rescuing murals of Jesus' life, painted by three members of Canada's iconic Group of Seven, he said.
The murals - about 16 to 18 of them that dotted the walls and ceilings of the church, Beyers said - were likely all reduced to rubble when a four-alarm fire erupted at the building on Gladstone Avenue, between College and Dundas Streets, in Toronto's west end just before 8 a.m.
Most of the church was gone.
"I was crushed," he said. "I just started to cry."
The "priceless" paintings, completed in 1923, were a landmark Beyers called "a gem of Canadian culture."
It's "an extraordinary loss," he said later in the morning in a media scrum outside the church.
"This area means a lot to us. While this is incredibly devastating for my congregation, it's devastating for this community," he said. "I cannot express enough how far reaching this church fire is going to have."
"We have our fingers crossed," Toronto Fire Services division commander Steven Darling said from a laneway near the still-smouldering building. "But it's highly unlikely there's anything we can save inside the building."
Toronto Fire hasn't determined a cause to the blaze, noting that the fire marshal and its teams will be investigating over the coming days. A spokesperson added that no one was inside the church at the time of the fire and there were no reports of injuries.
According to Beyers, the church's doors were locked and all the lights were shut off the night before.
"It's a real mystery to us how this even happened," Beyers said.
Darling said dozens of fire trucks, crews, kilometres of hose and an aerial tower that shot water onto the church's roof were dispatched to the site. Firefighters had also entered through the locked door at the back of the church during the blaze, but after about ten minutes, crews had to go on the "defensive" and retreat when the roof started to collapse, Darling said.
Fire deputy chief Jim Jessop told reporters at the scene it is possible the building could experience further collapses given the volume of water used to extinguish the fire and its age. Crews have created a "safe area" around the building as they continue to tend to the site.
Fire crews had set up hoses and command stations along Dundas Street between Gladstone Avenue and Lisgar Street, where about dozens of stores and street vendors were preparing to open for Do West Festival, a massive street party that took over that stretch of Dundas over the weekend. As black plumes of smoke shot into the sky, the festival's organizers were forced to grapple Sunday morning with how to remain open or whether it should close.
By mid-morning, fire crews had contained the blaze, downgrading it to a two-alarm fire, and moved the remaining vehicles and equipment to Dufferin Street, freeing up Dundas for the festival to proceed as planned.
But while the equipment shift offered some relief, there were still heavy emotions around the corner as locals and tourists alike gathered in an alleyway off Gladstone Avenue to watch firefighters put out any remaining hot spots at the historic church.
Laura Storey, 35, had "instant tears," she said, when her partner came home after a run and broke the news of the fire.
She'd been going to St. Anne's for years, she said, most recently to a candlelight ceremony on Easter. Not long before that she celebrated her 35th birthday at the church during evening mass on Christmas Eve.
"We had plans to be out of the city today," she said, a tear cresting down her cheek. "But we decided to just walk around here and feel it."
Keith Levenson, a former builder visiting from Vermont, stared in shock. He'd left his Airbnb earlier in the morning to grab a coffee when he saw the fire - and found his way to a fence at the back of the church.
"It's a tragedy," he said, noticing the stained glass windows that had been blown out. "It's heartbreaking to see."
A number of local politicians arrived to the scene to show their support, including Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who represents the riding of Davenport, and local councillor Alejandra Bravo, who said the destroyed art is "something we cannot replace in Canada and in the world."
St. Anne's Church was founded on the present site in 1862 before its redesign in 1907, according to Ontario Heritage Trust. The building was designated as a National Historic Site in 1996 and the City of Toronto designated it under the Ontario Heritage Act in 1980. The two-storey church was built in a Byzantine style with a domed roof, which is an unusual look for an Anglican church and the only of its kind in Canada.
Around mid-morning, as the reality of what was left of his church set in, Don Beyers, the parish priest, grew emotional.
The first thing that came to mind when he got to the site that morning, after racing over from his home near Queen and Dovercourt Street were the people that have been coming through St. Anne's doors for more than a century.
"My heart aches for them," he said. "Aches."

Document 111 of 500

Garage set on fire. Smashed windows. MPs and elected officials are rattled by rising intimidation and threats - and 'someone is going to get seriously hurt'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/garage-set-on-fire-smashed-windows-mps-elected/docview/3065706254/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Garage+set+on+fire.+Smashed+windows.+MPs+and+elected+officials+are+rattled+by+rising+intimidation+and+threats+-+and+%27someone+is+going+to+get+seriously+hurt%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - It seems only a matter of time before an elected official in Canada gets seriously hurt.
In March, somebody set fire to the garage at Liberal MP Brendan Hanley's Yukon home.
No one was injured in the blaze that destroyed the garage, two cars and a motorbike, but it left him "shocked," profoundly shaken, and fearful about the safety of his family, neighbours and those close to him.
Like nearly all MPs approached by the Star for this story, Hanley is reluctant to talk about the threat or details of that terrifying incident because he does not want to impede an ongoing RCMP probe.
But Hanley, a former Yukon chief medical officer of health who was elected in 2021, will say this:
"We all recognize the importance of protest and the many forms that can take. But it should never take the form of violence, and it should never encroach on someone's personal property. I think that's a line that has been crossed."
It's not about mean tweets, and it is not just online harassment anymore.
Canadian elected officials are confronting a sharp rise in physical threats and in-person intimidation, according to parliamentarians, House of Commons security officials, and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
And there's a veil of silence that surrounds it.
Vance Badawey, a Liberal MP, doesn't want to talk about the guy who came to his Port Colborne home last year, smashed the front window with a baseball bat, threatened his neighbours and chased kids down the street.
In a victim impact statement, Badawey made clear he remains angry about the impact on his family and neighbours, who were deeply rattled, and still are.
Melissa Lantsman, the Conservative MP for Thornhill and deputy leader, doesn't want to talk about the two individuals, one a woman, who have been charged with threatening her to the point that she required close police protection.
Eric Duncan, a Conservative MP for an Ontario riding near the U.S. border, doesn't want to talk about the man, recently released from police custody, who is charged with uttering threats against him, four months after the same man was also charged for threatening Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
In the past several weeks, protesters have turned up at the homes of Justice Minister Arif Virani and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly. They are not keen to talk about it, either.
"I am very concerned to be frank," said RCMP deputy commissioner Mark Flynn in an interview with the Star.
"We've seen a shift from people protesting or appearing ... at Parliament Hill, minister's offices, constituency offices, et cetera, to where we are now seeing people go to their residences and start taking actions at their residence."
Behavioural analysts at the RCMP who are tracking the trend say it is one of the "indicators of someone moving towards violence" when a harasser gets physically closer to the object of their ire.
Flynn says there's "a lot more" of it and traces the shift as having happened over "the last couple of years," a period that includes the COVID-19 pandemic, the anti-vaccine and anti-establishment movements that gave rise to the 2022 convoy protests, as well as Russia's war on Ukraine. Societal tensions have only escalated since war broke out in the Middle East.
The threats are affecting politicians in all provinces, across genders, across political party lines, and at all levels of government, said Flynn.
Individuals may claim a right to free speech, and that their actions are merely protest.
But Flynn said, in his opinion, "they are doing it to intimidate" the family and the MP - an escalation that has a corrosive effect on democratically elected representatives.
Sometimes graffiti is sprayed, or windows broken, beer bottles tossed on lawns, cars or pickup trucks waving "F--- Trudeau" flags do slow-rolls in front of homes, or family members and neighbours are taunted.
An MP or their family might spot someone sitting for hours outside their home in a parked car or truck, only to be replaced by another car doing another shift once the legal parking time is up, and before anyone can be ticketed.
An MP has had their tires slashed. There have been at least two incidents of arson the Star is aware of.
On April 1, a B.C. MP's office window was smashed by an axe-wielding protester, and a stick of baloney was shoved through the broken glass, according to NDP MP Charlie Angus, who has written about a "staggering escalation in intimidation" he has personally experienced.
Liberal MP Julie Dabrusin's constituency office was vandalized this week with red paint, simulated dead babies in blood-soaked shrouds laid at her office door, and a sign that read, "Julie your hands are red, 20000 children dead." She didn't post photos of it - "I don't want to give them any oxygen" - but someone else did.
It's the third time Dabrusin's riding office has been vandalized since Oct. 7. Dabrusin says her office had never been vandalized before the war in the Middle East broke out.
Both Dabrusin, who is Jewish, and fellow MP Omar Alghabra, who is Muslim, say they've had occasions where a person has approached them at their homes to make personal appeals in immigration cases, people Alghabra described as "desperate" and thinking an MP is their last hope.
Dabrusin and Alghabra say those cases feel different than what is happening now, with passions inflamed by the war in the Middle East.
Neither is OK.
Liberal MP Pam Damoff says she is quitting politics because of the threat and misogyny she has faced, a decision that hit home for some of her colleagues.
It prompted others to wonder if she wasn't exaggerating because polls show the Liberals have a tough fight for re-election anyway.
Both things can be true at the same time.
It is also true that this has been happening for a while now.
The street where Premier Doug Ford lives in Etobicoke was the scene of almost weekly protests during the pandemic by demonstrators opposed to lockdowns.
Trudeau has faced an unprecedented level of threat.
In July 2020, a man rammed a truck through the gates of Rideau Hall, where the governor general and prime minister live, and set out on foot with three loaded firearms and a knife before getting into a 90-minute standoff with RCMP officers.
Yet at the federal level, the rising threats have become so alarming that more than six weeks ago, an executive Commons committee approved more money for panic buttons, more sophisticated security and alarm systems at MPs' riding offices and homes, and for more sweeping open-source monitoring of the deluge of online hate and threats.
Many MPs no longer have an open-door policy at their riding offices, requiring constituents to book appointments ahead, online or by phone, fearing intimidation and harassment of staff.
Last week, Sergeant-at-Arms Pat McDonell told a Commons committee the rise in harassment of MPs, including indirect "threat behaviour" - that is intimidating but may not cross a criminal threshold of a direct threat to someone's personal safety - rose from eight cases in 2019 to 530 in 2023.
But it's worse than that.
By some accounts, the Commons has tracked more than 1,200 incidents of death threats, trespassing or vandalism to property, but it's not clear over what precise timeline, or even if that is the latest number. It is not data the Commons will publicly release, and McDonell declined to comment for this article.
Numbers aside, what it means for MPs is clear.
"Doxxing" of politicians from all parties has increased. That's when the private information of an MP or their children, including home addresses, have been published for protesters to target.
In at least one case, a person of concern showed up at the office of an MP's spouse.
In cases of a direct or feared imminent threat, the RCMP ensures close protection for a cabinet minister or MP. Usually, followup falls to local police forces.
Flynn won't talk about specific incidents, although he doesn't know if there is firm evidence to support concerns that talking about them publicly makes matters worse.
He said he agreed to speak for this article because "we as a society have to do something to combat this and ensure that as a society, we send a very clear message that this behaviour is not acceptable."
Echoing comments of RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme, Flynn said it may be time for legal reform.
He suggests lawmakers could specifically prohibit protest at elected officials' homes, but he acknowledged political decision-makers must strike a difficult balance between their security with the right to freely protest and express views.
The Trudeau government has proposed an online harms bill to toughen laws against online hate, but it has come up against sharp criticism for going too far.
Not all MPs are convinced a law to specifically protect politicians is required.
Some, like Conservative MP Michelle Rempel Garner, say, "It's not just an MP issue. It's an entire societal issue."
Other public and private figures receive threats and harassment too, and police seem unable to apply current criminal harassment laws to stop it, she said.
Rempel Garner was at the heart of one of the very first cases in Canada where a court in 2016 convicted an individual for criminal harassment and threats posted in 2013 online. She's not interested in posting about threats, or endlessly talking to media about the online intimidation she receives.
"I choose not to platform their behaviour because it detracts from my ability to do my job," she said, "and I'm not a victim."
But Rempel Garner supports anything that would help police act quickly or persuade judges to levy stiff penalties for threatening behaviour - which she suggests is the only effective deterrence.
Meanwhile, when it comes to personal security, MPs are left to cope on their own.
Reluctant to talk about the threats, some are worried they cannot protect their families while they are away at work in Ottawa. Others don't want to jeopardize ongoing police investigations, or legal proceedings that are underway. Often they are afraid talking will only make matters worse, and police have advised them just that - don't go public.
NDP MP Charlie Angus believes going public "does make it worse. You attract more threat."
"I also think MPs don't want to look weak and vulnerable, and some of the people I know, you know, they're pretty quiet. They don't pick a lot of public fights like me. And they faced serious, serious threats. But I feel like unless we keep talking about it, someone is going to get seriously hurt."
Four years ago, Angus said, "nobody threatened me."
Now the New Democrat receives threats weekly, and has been told some posts are "AI-generated" or campaigns mounted by bots - which further complicates the task for an MP who wants to track patterns of escalation.
When it comes to serious threats, even where charges are laid, Angus is dismayed at how the justice system fails to deal with it.
In 2022, a man who made bizarre QAnon-style allegations and threatened Angus was charged with public libel, an "obscure" offence, Angus said. A prosecutor settled the case, he said, after the accused agreed to a two-year restraining order, although the judge observed the offender likely deserved jail time.
Just last week, Angus was stunned to learn an unrelated criminal case involving someone who threatened him had collapsed.
A prosecutor stayed charges of uttering death threats even though Angus and his staff had documented the menacing and escalating behaviour they experienced, via phone records and voice recordings.
Yet a Crown attorney told a judge that critical police evidence had been lost.
"It's just been a sort of a black hole, and nobody had reached out to us," Angus said. "And nothing against the House of Commons, but MPs deal with all this on their own."
Angus is not running for re-election - not because of the current climate, he said, but because after 20 years in Parliament, it is time for him to move on.
He says he'll miss dealing with the public and used to love that about the job.
"I just love the availability. Everybody knows where I am and knows where I'd be. It's like the randomness of meeting people is what was always fun.
"And now with randomness, you never know what the random element is."
CAPTION: NDP MP Charlie Angus attends a news conference in Ottawa on Oct. 17, 2023.

Subject: Automobiles; Vandalism; Threats; Demonstrations & protests; Mounted police; Prime ministers; Politicians; Public officials; Pandemics

Location: Middle East

Title: Garage set on fire. Smashed windows. MPs and elected officials are rattled by rising intimidation and threats - and 'someone is going to get seriously hurt'

Document 112 of 500

China is dominating the EV market - and that means trouble no matter how Canada responds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/china-is-dominating-ev-market-that-means-trouble/docview/3065706193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=China+is+dominating+the+EV+market+-+and+that+means+trouble+no+matter+how+Canada+responds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Ever since the United States said it will slap steep tariffs on Chinese electric vehicles last month, attention has turned to whether the Trudeau government will follow suit.
It's a question experts and insiders say is fraught. One move either way could trigger costly trade tensions with economic superpowers in the U.S. or China. And the very issue itself pits the desire to protect Canada's own heavily subsidized electric vehicle (EV) industry against the pressure of the climate crisis to get more clean cars on the roads as fast as possible.
"Something has got to give," said Ian Lee, an associate professor at Carleton University's Sprott School of Business.
"We are between the proverbial very-hard rock and very-hard place."
That might explain why Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal government is still weighing its options almost a month after U.S. President Joe Biden's decision to impose the tariffs.
"We're looking at it very seriously," International Trade Minister Mary Ng said this week on Parliament Hill, when asked about the government's thinking on matching those tariffs. Referring to how tens billions of dollars in subsidies from Ottawa and provincial governments in Quebec and Ontario have helped attract automakers who are setting up EV battery and car factories in Canada, Ng suggested protecting that nascent industry is the key focus.
"We intend to build these automobiles in Canada," she said.
The crux of the situation is that, as countries try to confront the climate crisis, many - including Canada - are offering incentives to buyers and setting sales targets to ensure more vehicles on their roads are not belching pollution that causes global warming. Many more - also including Canada - are looking to supply those clean cars and generate jobs and revenues through the creation of their own EV industries.
But experts say there's one huge challenge. China is already dominating the global EV industry, thanks to an industrial policy directed from Beijing that has used the heft of the authoritarian state - along with advantages of a large domestic market, lower labour costs, and supplies of raw materials - to seed an array of EV companies that are now cranking out electric cars that are cheaper to buy than those built in Europe and the United States. Sixty per cent of new electric cars sold globally in 2023 were registered in China, according to the International Energy Agency. Chinese companies already dominate the global market for plug-in hybrids, and Shenzhen-based BYD has a growing international presence and is threatening Tesla for the biggest share of the global market in battery-operated electric vehicles, according to a February report from TrendForce, a market research firm.
"China is so far ahead of everyone else it's not even funny," Lee said.
But other countries are trying to catch up. The U.S. has devoted hundreds of billions of dollars to support manufacturing in green industries like EV manufacturing. And part of the industrial strategy is to prevent cheaper Chinese-made EVs from undercutting the market for those built in the U.S. On May 14, the White House announced it would increase its existing tariffs on Chinese EVs from 25 per cent to 100 per cent, and also crank up import taxes on a suite of EV-related products sent from China, including EV batteries and some critical minerals used to make them.
Similar considerations are reportedly afoot in the European Union, home to a deeply rooted auto sector in countries like Germany.
Canada currently has a six per cent tariff on Chinese EVs, but gives people who buy them - like purchasers of Teslas made in Shanghai for the Canadian market - a $5,000 rebate.
Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Parts Manufacturers' Association, told the Star he knows the Prime Minister's Office is grappling with this issue, too. He expects the Liberals to make a decision "soon" to effectively match the American tariffs, since he believes Washington will expect Canada to make a "reciprocal arrangement" to try and slow China's growing EV dominance over the North American auto sector.
"The U.S. decided waking up late is better than never waking up," Volpe said. "China hasn't won yet, but if we keep going the same way, they will."
A senior government source, who spoke to the Star about internal policy deliberations on condition they aren't named, said it's too early to say what Ottawa will do on the tariffs question. "We are legitimately looking at things now," they said.
The big consideration, the source explained, is the interpretation of risks. Slapping tariffs on China could cause Beijing to respond in kind. "We are a smaller country and a smaller economy than the United States," the source said. "(China) has tried to make examples of us before."
In 2019, for instance, Beijing imposed tariffs on billions of dollars worth of Canadian canola, pork and soybeans after relations collapsed over the arrest of a Chinese telecom executive on a U.S. warrant in Vancouver, following by Beijing's infamous jailing of the "two Michaels."
But there's also the risk of angering the Americans if they become wary that almost-assembled Chinese EVs can get finished in Canada and skirt the new tariff wall into the U.S. market, unless Canada matches with import taxes of its own. The government source said Ottawa isn't expecting an influx of Chinese-made EVs right now, but the Liberals expect it could happen in the future.
Volpe predicted trade tensions with China will dominate the scheduled renegotiation of the North American trade agreement in 2026. "It would be imprudent to fumble this one," he said.
Yet another consideration is Canada's target to ensure all new light-duty vehicles sold in 2035 are zero-emission, said Joanna Kyriazis, transportation lead at the policy organization Clean Energy Canada. That would be easier to achieve with more and cheaper EV options. China's BYD recently launched an EV that costs under US$10,000.
"We want and need more affordable electric vehicles," Kyriazis said.
But she acknowledged there are other important factors at play, and Ottawa needs to find a "sweet spot" for any new tariff that accomplishes three goals: appeasing Canada's trading partners, allowing more Canadians to afford EVs, and supporting the creation of a domestic EV sector. It won't be easy, she said. "We think the feds are in a really tough spot."
CAPTION: Chinese automobile manufacturer Wuling Motors displays its electric vehicle "Cloud EV" during the Indonesia International Motor Show in Surabaya on May 29, 2024.

Subject: Strategic materials; Manufacturing; Electric vehicles; Prime ministers; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Manufacturing Tariffs

Location: China; Beijing China; United States--US; Canada

Title: China is dominating the EV market - and that means trouble no matter how Canada responds

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 113 of 500

Canada weighs options over EV tariffs: China is dominating the market - and that means trouble no matter how Ottawa responds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canada-weighs-options-over-ev-tariffs/docview/3065691627/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+weighs+options+over+EV+tariffs%3A+China+is+dominating+the+market+-+and+that+means+trouble+no+matter+how+Ottawa+responds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Title: Canada weighs options over EV tariffs:   China is dominating the market - and that means trouble no matter how Ottawa responds

____________________________________________________________

Document 114 of 500

Increasing threats rattle politicians: 'Someone is going to get seriously hurt,' as elected officials confront rising intimidation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/increasing-threats-rattle-politicians/docview/3065691578/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Increasing+threats+rattle+politicians%3A+%27Someone+is+going+to+get+seriously+hurt%2C%27+as+elected+officials+confront+rising+intimidation&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It seems only a matter of time before an elected official in Canada gets seriously hurt.
In March, somebody set fire to the garage at Liberal MP Brendan Hanley's Yukon home.
No one was injured in the blaze that destroyed the garage, two cars and a motorbike, but it left him "shocked," profoundly shaken, and fearful about the safety of his family, neighbours and those close to him.
Like nearly all MPs approached by the Star for this story, Hanley is reluctant to talk about the threat or details of that terrifying incident because he does not want to impede an ongoing RCMP probe.
But Hanley, a former Yukon chief medical officer of health who was elected in 2021, will say this:
"We all recognize the importance of protest and the many forms that can take. But it should never take the form of violence, and it should never encroach on someone's personal property. I think that's a line that has been crossed."
It's not about mean tweets, and it is not just online harassment anymore.
Canadian elected officials are confronting a sharp rise in physical threats and in-person intimidation, according to parliamentarians, House of Commons security officials, and the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
And there's a veil of silence that surrounds it.
Vance Badawey, a Liberal MP, doesn't want to talk about the guy who came to his Port Colborne home last year, smashed the front window with a baseball bat, threatened his neighbours and chased kids down the street.
In a victim impact statement, Badawey made clear he remains angry about the impact on his family and neighbours, who were deeply rattled, and still are.
Melissa Lantsman, the Conservative MP for Thornhill and deputy leader, doesn't want to talk about the two individuals, one a woman, who have been charged with threatening her to the point that she required close police protection.
Eric Duncan, a Conservative MP for an Ontario riding near the U.S. border, doesn't want to talk about the man, recently released from police custody, who is charged with uttering threats against him, four months after the same man was also charged for threatening Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
In the past several weeks, protesters have turned up at the homes of Justice Minister Arif Virani and Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly. They are not keen to talk about it, either.
"I am very concerned to be frank," said RCMP deputy commissioner Mark Flynn in an interview with the Star.
"We've seen a shift from people protesting or appearing ... at Parliament Hill, minister's offices, constituency offices, et cetera, to where we are now seeing people go to their residences and start taking actions at their residence."
Behavioural analysts at the RCMP who are tracking the trend say it is one of the "indicators of someone moving toward violence" when a harasser gets physically closer to the object of their ire.
Flynn says there's "a lot more" of it and traces the shift as having happened over "the last couple of years," a period that includes the COVID-19 pandemic, the anti-vaccine and anti-establishment movements that gave rise to the 2022 convoy protests, as well as Russia's war on Ukraine. Societal tensions have only escalated since war broke out in the Middle East.
The threats are affecting politicians in all provinces, across genders, across political party lines, and at all levels of government, said Flynn.
Individuals may claim a right to free speech, and that their actions are merely protest.
But Flynn said, in his opinion, "they are doing it to intimidate" the family and the MP - an escalation that has a corrosive effect on democratically elected representatives.
Sometimes graffiti is sprayed, or windows broken, beer bottles tossed on lawns, cars or pickup trucks waving "F--- Trudeau" flags do slow-rolls in front of homes, or family members and neighbours are taunted.
An MP or their family might spot someone sitting for hours outside their home in a parked car or truck, only to be replaced by another car doing another shift once the legal parking time is up, and before anyone can be ticketed.
An MP has had their tires slashed. There have been at least two incidents of arson the Star is aware of.
On April 1, a B.C. MP's office window was smashed by an axe-wielding protester, and a stick of baloney was shoved through the broken glass, according to NDP MP Charlie Angus, who has written about a "staggering escalation in intimidation" he has personally experienced.
Liberal MP Julie Dabrusin's constituency office was vandalized this week with red paint, simulated dead babies in blood-soaked shrouds laid at her office door, and a sign that read, "Julie your hands are red, 20000 children dead." She didn't post photos of it - "I don't want to give them any oxygen" - but someone else did.
It's the third time Dabrusin's riding office has been vandalized since Oct. 7. Dabrusin says her office had never been vandalized before the war in the Middle East broke out.
Both Dabrusin, who is Jewish, and fellow MP Omar Alghabra, who is Muslim, say they've had occasions where a person has approached them at their homes to make personal appeals in immigration cases, people Alghabra described as "desperate" and thinking an MP is their last hope.
Dabrusin and Alghabra say those cases feel different than what is happening now, with passions inflamed by the war in the Middle East.
Neither is OK.
Liberal MP Pam Damoff says she is quitting politics because of the threat and misogyny she has faced, a decision that hit home for some of her colleagues.
It prompted others to wonder if she wasn't exaggerating because polls show the Liberals have a tough fight for re-election anyway.
Both things can be true at the same time.
It is also true that this has been happening for a while now.
The street where Premier Doug Ford lives in Etobicoke was the scene of almost weekly protests during the pandemic by demonstrators opposed to lockdowns.
Trudeau has faced an unprecedented level of threat.
In July 2020, a man rammed a truck through the gates of Rideau Hall, where the governor general and prime minister live, and set out on foot with three loaded firearms and a knife before getting into a 90-minute standoff with RCMP officers.
Yet at the federal level, the rising threats have become so alarming that more than six weeks ago, an executive Commons committee approved more money for panic buttons, more sophisticated security and alarm systems at MPs' riding offices and homes, and for more sweeping open-source monitoring of the deluge of online hate and threats.
Many MPs no longer have an open-door policy at their riding offices, requiring constituents to book appointments ahead, online or by phone, fearing intimidation and harassment of staff.
Last week, Sergeant-at-Arms Pat McDonell told a Commons committee the rise in harassment of MPs, including indirect "threat behaviour" - that is intimidating but may not cross a criminal threshold of a direct threat to someone's personal safety - rose from eight cases in 2019 to 530 in 2023.
But it's worse than that.
By some accounts, the Commons has tracked more than 1,200 incidents of death threats, trespassing or vandalism to property, but it's not clear over what precise timeline, or even if that is the latest number. It is not data the Commons will publicly release, and McDonell declined to comment for this article.
Numbers aside, what it means for MPs is clear.
"Doxxing" of politicians from all parties has increased. That's when the private information of an MP or their children, including home addresses, have been published for protesters to target.
In at least one case, a person of concern showed up at the office of an MP's spouse.
In cases of a direct or feared imminent threat, the RCMP ensures close protection for a cabinet minister or MP. Usually, followup falls to local police forces.
Flynn won't talk about specific incidents, although he doesn't know if there is firm evidence to support concerns that talking about them publicly makes matters worse.
He said he agreed to speak for this article because "we as a society have to do something to combat this and ensure that as a society, we send a very clear message that this behaviour is not acceptable."
Echoing comments of RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme, Flynn said it may be time for legal reform.
He suggests lawmakers could specifically prohibit protest at elected officials' homes, but he acknowledged political decision-makers must strike a difficult balance between their security with the right to freely protest and express views.
The Trudeau government has proposed an online harms bill to toughen laws against online hate, but it has come up against sharp criticism for going too far.
Not all MPs are convinced a law to specifically protect politicians is required.
Some, like Conservative MP Michelle Rempel Garner, say, "It's not just an MP issue. It's an entire societal issue."
Other public and private figures receive threats and harassment, too, and police seem unable to apply current criminal harassment laws to stop it, she said.
Rempel Garner was at the heart of one of the very first cases in Canada where a court in 2016 convicted an individual for criminal harassment and threats posted in 2013 online. She's not interested in posting about threats, or endlessly talking to media about the online intimidation she receives.
"I choose not to platform their behaviour because it detracts from my ability to do my job," she said, "and I'm not a victim."
But Rempel Garner supports anything that would help police act quickly or persuade judges to levy stiff penalties for threatening behaviour - which she suggests is the only effective deterrence.
Meanwhile, when it comes to personal security, MPs are left to cope on their own.
Reluctant to talk about the threats, some are worried they cannot protect their families while they are away at work in Ottawa. Others don't want to jeopardize ongoing police investigations, or legal proceedings that are underway. Often they are afraid talking will only make matters worse, and police have advised them just that - don't go public.
NDP MP Charlie Angus believes going public "does make it worse. You attract more threats."
"I also think MPs don't want to look weak and vulnerable, and some of the people I know, you know, they're pretty quiet. They don't pick a lot of public fights like me. And they faced serious, serious threats. But I feel like unless we keep talking about it, someone is going to get seriously hurt."
Four years ago, Angus said, "nobody threatened me."
Now the New Democrat receives threats weekly, and has been told some posts are "AI-generated" or campaigns mounted by bots - which further complicates the task for an MP who wants to track patterns of escalation.
When it comes to serious threats, even where charges are laid, Angus is dismayed at how the justice system fails to deal with it.
In 2022, a man who made bizarre QAnon-style allegations and threatened Angus was charged with public libel, an "obscure" offence, Angus said. A prosecutor settled the case, he said, after the accused agreed to a two-year restraining order, although the judge observed the offender likely deserved jail time.
Just last week, Angus was stunned to learn an unrelated criminal case involving someone who threatened him had collapsed.
A prosecutor stayed charges of uttering death threats, even though Angus and his staff had documented the menacing and escalating behaviour they experienced, via phone records and voice recordings.
Yet a Crown attorney told a judge that critical police evidence had been lost.
"It's just been a sort of a black hole, and nobody had reached out to us," Angus said. "And nothing against the House of Commons, but MPs deal with all this on their own."
Angus is not running for re-election - not because of the current climate, he said, but because after 20 years in Parliament, it is time for him to move on.
He says he'll miss dealing with the public and used to love that about the job.
"I just love the availability. Everybody knows where I am and knows where I'd be. It's like the randomness of meeting people is what was always fun.
"And now with randomness, you never know what the random element is."
CAPTION: Four years ago, nobody threatened NDP MP Charlie Angus. Now he receives threats weekly, and has been told some posts are "AI-generated" or campaigns mounted by bots - which complicates the task for an MP who wants to track patterns of escalation.Adrian Wyld The Canadian Pressfile photo

Document 115 of 500

'Young people are just fed up, but also ... numb': North Etobicoke community calls for action and resources, not awareness in wake of shooting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/young-people-are-just-fed-up-also-numb/docview/3065691535/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Young+people+are+just+fed+up%2C+but+also+...+numb%27%3A+North+Etobicoke+community+calls+for+action+and+resources%2C+not+awareness+in+wake+of+shooting&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nobody in the neighbourhood slept Sunday night after the shooting that killed two men and injured three others, said Cealene Gabriel, who lives in a public housing complex across the street from where the gunfire broke out.
"You could hear people screaming, people were running," she said. "It was just a big shock ... I didn't send my daughter to school that next day because she was terrified."
The gunfire behind North Albion Collegiate Institute took the lives of Seymour Gibbs, 46, who died Thursday, and Delroy (George) Parkes, 61, who died in hospital after the shooting, along with injuring three others. More than 50 bullets were fired in a shooting police believe was "random," and committed by two suspects who did not know the victims.
Gun violence isn't uncommon in Gabriel's area - it's seen nine shootings and guns discharged so far this year, resulting in at least six deaths, the second-highest rate out of all neighbourhoods in the GTA, according to Toronto police data. She says her neighbour once had a bullet enter their home. To stay safe, Gabriel and her family sit on the floor in front of their couch to watch TV.
As politicians speak out against this latest shooting, community members say they crave deeper action and renewed resources for youth support programs that have been underfunded in recent years.
"We do not need more awareness," said Stephen Mensah, Toronto Youth Cabinet executive director. "Our communities and the young people who are facing these realities daily need action to improve their lived experiences."
"Young people are just fed up, but also young people are numb. I think that's the problem ... we can't normalize these acts of senseless violence."
Statistics kept by Toronto Community Housing Corp., which has a nearly 160-unit lowrise housing community in Kipling-Mount Olive, hint at escalating tensions in the neighbourhood over recent years. Although their records are exclusive to their own property, meaning they don't capture incidents like the school parking lot shooting despite being a stone's throw away, the housing agency recorded 89 calls for service from the area last year.
That total, which can include anything from disputes between neighbours to parking violations and disturbances, was a jump from the 69 calls in 2022, and the highest number since at least 2020, records shared with the Star show. This year is on track to surge even higher if the current pace holds throughout 2024, with 32 calls for service from Kipling-Mount Olive from January to March alone.
Mayor Olivia Chow, when asked by the media what the city is doing to address the issue on Gun Violence Awareness day Friday, said the city has invested in a lot of safety measures and plans to invest more in youth programs. Her office declined to clarify which programs.
"It's important that we get to the root problem of gun violence, (which) in many ways is despair and disregard of life," Chow said. "All of those things, we must address collectively as a society and here at city hall."
Premier Doug Ford, in a statement posted to X on Wednesday, said he was "disgusted" by the shooting in his riding, and promised that he is "investing hundreds of millions of dollars to get more boots on the ground." His office did not respond to questions from the Star about what investments he was referring to.
Outside of North Albion Collegiate Institute on Friday, Mensah and other members of the Toronto Youth Cabinet called for a city-sponsored program to create more employment opportunities for youth, $300,000 for the Identify 'N Impact grant program for vulnerable youth to get engaged with their communities, and for the number of Community Healing Project youth crime prevention sites to double.
Chelsea Davenport, program manager for the Albion Boys and Girls Club, said she has seen the community call for change after other recent shootings in North Etobicoke - but not much has improved.
"Community members have spoken (out), they have literally cried, they've protested, they've advocated for the same issues for years," she said. "I think people are just tired of the talking, and they really need to see some changes from our leaders."
Davenport works with vulnerable youth, including about 75 each year involved in the criminal justice system ,to connect them with community programs and mentors.
The program has helped youth go from facing charges to attending college on a full scholarship. But last summer, it lost $100,000 in annual grant funding from the City of Toronto, which has caused staff and mentors to leave.
Culturally safe, free, and accessible mental health services are also needed, Davenport said.
Davenport said she talked to students who go to school near where the shooting took place, who said they saw social workers around to support students on Tuesday - but by Wednesday, they were gone. This, she feels, is emblematic of a deeper need for governments to invest in consistent support for communities struggling with gun violence. "People are even worried, what does this mean for the rest of summer, when we know violence goes up in the summer?" she said. "We've seen the same cycle occurring over and over ... People are exhausted."
With files from Victoria Gibson
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow, who joined a community rally in Rexdale last week, says it's important "that we getto the root problem of gun violence, (which) in many waysis despairand disregard of life."Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star

Document 116 of 500

Doug Ford's new appointments to his bloated cabinet are shameless - and revealing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-new-appointments-his-bloated-cabinet/docview/3065610719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+new+appointments+to+his+bloated+cabinet+are+shameless+-+and+revealing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once upon a time, Tories believed in lean, clean government.
Now, not so much.
Behold Doug Ford's new-look cabinet, not merely rebranded but expanded - bigger than ever before: 36 ministers of the Crown, each with a salary premium.
And yet, while in opposition a decade ago, Progressive Conservatives promised to cut the Ontario cabinet from 27 down to 16 ministers if they ever won government. The premier has now exceeded that erstwhile, worthwhile target by 20 ministers.
Six years ago, when he first came to power, Ford made a point of slashing the size of Toronto city hall from 47 to 25 councillors in his quest for greater efficiency. Now, you can comfortably fit all of city council into the historic cabinet room at Queen's Park, while Ford's full parade of overpaid ministers might have the fire marshal worrying about overcrowding.
How did this happen? How did the Tories overshoot from 16 ministers to 36?
Hubris.
The old apoplectic Ford would be crying "hypocrisy" were he still in opposition. But six years in power does something to some people.
It makes them shameless. They drop all pretence.
Thursday's cabinet shuffle is as revealing as it is irrelevant. The old journalistic cliché about shuffling the deck chairs on the Titanic does not apply here, because Ford's government shows no signs of sinking anytime soon (and the opposition is barely treading water).
But the barnacles creep up on you. They have a way of weighing you down.
Ford is padding his cabinet with pretenders - notably eight so-called "associate ministers" who gain a seat but sometimes bring little to the table. Do we need 26-year-old Sam Oosterhoff, who has been an MPP since age 19, as an "associate minister of energy" to help the real energy minister find his way, along with junior ministers in forestry, mines and small business?
It's not new blood, it's bloat.
Yes, Ford is riding high in public opinion polls. No, he does not imagine himself infallible - far from it, for his true talent is in setting the wrong course followed by a course correction and public apology.
The problem is that even after Ford expresses regret, he reverts. The best example of that is his rush to rehabilitate his disgraced former minister of housing, Steve Clark, by appointing him as house leader responsible for shepherding the government's entire legislative agenda.
Late last summer, facing public pressure and a police probe, Clark belatedly quit his post as the minister of municipal affairs who recklessly presided over the Greenbelt fiasco. Clark, who made a name for himself in opposition by reflexively demanding the resignations of several Liberal cabinet ministers for offences real or imagined, had long resisted calls for his own resignation until his position became indefensible.
Now, bizarrely, he is back out of the penalty box. While not yet restored to the cabinet room (lacking ministerial rank), he will be leading the way in the legislative chamber.
What happens if the RCMP probe in the Greenbelt gets in the way? There is, of course, another way: Ford can call an early election before police weigh in, confident that Clark and others won't sing from anything other than the same songbook crafted by the premier himself.
As Ford's loyal house leader, Clark won't be bringing MPPs back from their summer break - which began Thursday, just hours before the cabinet shuffle - until well into autumn. The legislature normally resumes shortly after Labour Day (the date of Clark's resignation from cabinet), but will now be away through Thanksgiving.
That has sparked speculation over prorogation - that parliamentary ritual of renewal where the government delivers a new speech from the throne proclaiming a new agenda. Just in time for an early election in 2025 - one year ahead of the fixed election date codified in law.
What could possibly justify Ford's premature pitch for a third term?
Surely not the premier's old promise to deliver more housing for a hungry market. His controversial plan to bulldoze the Greenbelt amounted to an $8.3-billion boondoggle (in windfall profits to developers until the deal was called off).
Presumably not Ford's past pledge to deliver more beer in corner stores to a thirsty market. His convoluted plan to reopen a signed contract with the Beer Store led him to authorize an alleged $1-billion beer boondoggle (in subsidies and foregone licensing fees).
These two boondoggles are twin bookends to Ford's leadership of the last year. And they are harbingers of the year(s) to come if he triggers an early and expedient election call.
"Buck-a-beer," Ford's 2018 campaign slogan, worked for a while - until it didn't. Even if "beer in corner stores" falls flat, and the Greenbelt bogs down, Ford is already reaching back to another of his perennial campaign bumper stickers:
"Axe the (carbon) tax."
That may sound as stale as old beer, and as tired as those past promises of lean and clean government. But if it sells, Ford is all for it.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens as Steve Clark, then his minister of housing, speaks during a news conference in Mississauga on Aug. 11, 2023.

Subject: Leadership; Local elections; Windfall profits

'Young people are numb': North Etobicoke community calls for action, not awareness in wake of shooting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/young-people-are-numb-north-etobicoke-community/docview/3065610670/se-2?accountid=15090
