Full text: Nobody in the neighbourhood slept Sunday night after the shooting that killed two men and injured three others, said Cealene Gabriel, who lives in a public housing complex across the street from where the gunfire broke out.
"You could hear people screaming, people were running," she said. "It was just a big shock ... I didn't send my daughter to school that next day because she was terrified."
The gunfire behind North Albion Collegiate Institute took the lives of Seymour Gibbs, 46, who succumbed to his injuries Thursday, and Delroy "George" Parkes, 61, who died in hospital after the shooting, along with injuring three others. More than 50 bullets were fired in a shooting police believe was "random," and committed by two suspects who did not know the victims.
Gun violence isn't uncommon in Gabriel's area - it's seen nine shootings and guns discharged so far this year resulting in at least six deaths, the second highest rate out of all neighbourhoods in the GTA, according to Toronto Police data. She says her neighbour once had a bullet enter their home. To stay safe, Gabriel and her family sit on the floor in front of their couch to watch TV.
As politicians speak out against this latest shooting, community members say they crave deeper action and renewed resources for youth support programs that have been underfunded in recent years.
"We do not need more awareness," said Stephen Mensah, Toronto Youth Cabinet executive director. "Our communities and the young people who are facing these realities daily need action to improve their lived experiences."
"Young people are just fed up, but also young people are numb. I think that's the problem ... we can't normalize these acts of senseless violence."
Statistics kept by Toronto Community Housing Corp., which has a nearly 160-unit lowrise housing community in Kipling-Mount Olive, hint at escalating tensions in the neighbourhood over recent years. Though their records are exclusive to their own property, meaning they don't capture incidents like the school parking lot shooting despite being a stone's throw away, the housing agency recorded 89 calls for service from the area last year.
That total, which can include anything from disputes between neighbours to parking violations and disturbances, was a jump from the 69 calls in 2022, and the highest number since at least 2020, records shared with the Star show. This year is on track to surge even higher if the current pace holds throughout 2024, with 32 calls for service from Kipling-Mount Olive from January to March alone.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, when asked by the media what the city is doing to address the issue on Gun Violence Awareness day Friday, said the city has invested in a lot of safety measures and plans to invest more in youth programs. Her office declined to clarify which programs, and if any are specific to North Etobicoke.
"It's important that we get to the root problem of gun violence, (which) in many ways is despair and disregard of life," Chow said. "All of those things, we must address collectively as a society and here at city hall."
Premier Doug Ford, in a statement posted to X on Wednesday, said he was "disgusted" by the shooting in his riding, and promised that he is "investing hundreds of millions of dollars to get more boots on the ground." His office did not respond to questions from the Star about what investments or programs this message was referring to.
Outside of North Albion Collegiate Institute on Friday, Mensah and other members of the Toronto Youth Cabinet called for a city-sponsored program to create more employment opportunities for youth, $300,000 for the Identify 'N Impact grant program for vulnerable youth to get engaged with their communities, and for the number of Community Healing Project youth crime prevention sites to double.
"These are the upstream solutions that we need to be taking to address the rise in violence we're seeing," he said.
Chelsea Davenport, program manager for the Albion Boys and Girls Club, said she has seen the community call for change after other recent shootings in North Etobicoke - but not much has improved.
"Community members have spoken (out), they have literally cried, they've protested, they've advocated for the same issues for years," she said. "I think people are just tired of the talking, and they really need to see some changes from our leaders."
Davenport works with vulnerable youth - including about 75 each year involved in the criminal justice system - to connect them with community programs and mentors that help them "reach their full potential."
The program has helped youth go from facing charges to attending college on a full scholarship. But last summer, it lost $100,000 in annual grant funding from the City of Toronto, which has caused staff and mentors to leave, limiting the capacity of Davenport's team.
"The thing that makes the biggest difference ... is our youth having a caring adult and a mentor that they can go to from a very young age," she said. "We need violence prevention to be recognized as a core service with stable and multi-year funding."
Culturally safe, free, and accessible mental health services are also needed, Davenport said, particularly in a community where many people may not otherwise have the resources to get this support.
Davenport said she talked to students who go to school near where the shooting took place, who said they saw social workers around to support students on Tuesday - but by Wednesday, they were gone. This, she feels, is emblematic of a deeper need for governments to invest in consistent support for communities struggling with gun violence.
"People are even worried, what does this mean for the rest of summer, when we know violence goes up in the summer?" she said.
"We've seen the same cycle occurring over and over ... People are exhausted."

Document 118 of 500

Cabinet shuffle reveals rifts among Conservatives, confidence amid probe: Insiders say surprise kaffiyeh ban led to Ford making house leader change

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cabinet-shuffle-reveals-rifts-among-conservatives/docview/3065585738/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cabinet+shuffle+reveals+rifts+among+Conservatives%2C+confidence+amid+probe%3A+Insiders+say+surprise+kaffiyeh+ban+led+to+Ford+making+house+leader+change&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a cabinet shuffle aimed at addressing controversies as disparate as the Greenbelt scandal and the legislature's kaffiyeh ban. Premier Doug Ford has signalled his confidence an RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion land swap imbroglio will not touch his Progressive Conservative government.
Ford also demonstrated his displeasure at being blindsided when Speaker Ted Arnott banned Palestinian kaffiyeh scarves from being worn in the legislature in April.
That's why the premier stripped house leader duties from Paul Calandra, who he felt should have warned him about Arnott's surprise move, and handed them to Steve Clark. Calandra will remain as minister of municipal affairs and housing, a post he assumed last September after Clark had stepped down at the height of the Greenbelt fiasco.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the back story of Thursday's ministerial shuffle, said the premier, who opposed the kaffiyeh ban, was unhappy at being caught flat-footed on such a divisive issue.
Arnott outlawed the black and white scarves commonly worn by pro-Palestinian protesters since the Hamas-Israel war began because he felt they break the rules against "political" garments in the chamber.
That sparked divisions inside Ford's caucus with, as the Star reported April 26, some Tory MPPs questioning the wisdom of the premier's intervention, saying "he didn't need to pick sides."
Apparently unfazed by the Greenbelt saga, the premier is putting Clark on the path back to being a full-fledged cabinet minister. He will earn $137,879 as government house leader compared to the $165,851 earned by a minister.
But unlike Calandra, the Tories' pugnacious point-man in the chamber, he won't take opposition questions during legislature's daily question period.
Ford's decision to move Stephen Lecce from education to the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification, in a swap with Todd Smith, exposed frictions between the provincial and federal Conservatives.
An attack on Lecce by rookie Conservative MP Jamil Jivani (Durham) underscored the rift between the premier and federal Tory Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Jivani, a former Ford aide at Queen's Park, took to social media to hail Lecce's departure from the ministry.
"Ontario has desperately needed a new education minister for years. I hope real policy changes are coming, so this will be remembered as a great day for all students, parents and teachers," he said Thursday night.
That prompted Nick Kouvalis, Ford's pollster and a key architect of the Tories' 2022 re-election, to fire back on social media the next day.
"I love Pierre. I love Jenni. But be careful ... Lecce is under my protection. Chose your next steps carefully ... You've had a wide berth so far because of Pierre and Jenni," said Kouvalis, referring to Jenni Byrne, Poilievre's most powerful adviser.
Lecce was the longest-serving education minister in decades, landing contracts during two contentious rounds of negotiations with unions, as well as ushering in a host of reforms to the sector, including destreaming high school courses.
Those now-eliminated applied courses were widely known to adversely impact Black and low-income students, hindering their chances of graduating or attending post-secondary school.
A back-to-basics focus under Lecce was meant to tackle parents' concerns about school boards being distracted by non-academic issues and culture wars.
During his five-year tenure, the ministry also added mandatory Black history lessons as well as Holocaust education to the curriculum, and provided funding to combat Islamophobia. Lecce even appointed a supervisor to take over the Peel school board - a move ministers rarely resort to - after the director and trustees there failed to address discrimination and dysfunction.
Cathy Abraham, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said she is looking forward to meeting with Smith.
"Right now the spotlight is on funding because we are all doing our budgets, and we're being asked to do way, way more with far less - and it's becoming a real challenge across the province," she said, adding it is encouraging that Smith's wife is a school vice-principal because he would understand "those kitchen-table conversations about the challenges."
That was echoed by Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association president Michael Bellmore, who has met with Smith and said "he's a very affable guy ... he seems to be a very quick study and I think it's fantastic that he has lived in the environment, of living with an educator and understanding the ups and downs of what folks in education do on a daily basis."
But teacher unions were skeptical.
"We don't know him at all from an education capacity, but we have talked to the Ontario Autism Coalition who did work with him and they did not find that he was helpful at all, but perhaps it will be different," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, referring to Smith's stint overseeing community and social services.
Smith, who is in France on a nuclear trade mission, said in a statement Friday he was "looking forward to ensuring our students are prepared for the careers of the future."
Opposition parties said the shuffle shouldn't distract voters from the Mounties' ongoing probe into the Greenbelt, an extended summer break of the legislature - which will not return until Oct. 21 instead of Sept. 9 - and Ford's ruminating about a snap election one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"This is a government that doesn't actually want to get any work done and doesn't want to be held to account because - guess what - this is a corrupt government," charged NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the reboot and recess are a bid to shift criticism from Ford's "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" to put beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails in more stores a year ahead of schedule.
"He's created the biggest cabinet in history, using your money, and he only included nine women," Crombie noted of the 36-member executive council.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 119 of 500

Stronach charged with sex assaults: Magna International founder denies the allegations dating back to the 1980s, his lawyer says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stronach-charged-with-sex-assaults/docview/3065585481/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stronach+charged+with+sex+assaults%3A+Magna+International+founder+denies+the+allegations+dating+back+to+the+1980s%2C+his+lawyer+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Billionaire businessman Frank Stronach has been criminally charged after a sexual assault investigation into incidents police say date back to the 1980s.
Peel police announced early Friday evening that the 91-year-old Stronach had been arrested and charged with five offences, including rape, indecent assault on a female, two charges of sexual assault, and forcible confinement. The incidents are alleged to have spanned from the 1980s until 2023.
Stronach is the founder of Magna International, an automotive parts company based in Aurora. He has been named to the Order of Canada.
"Mr. Stronach categorically denies the allegations of impropriety which have been brought against him," said Brian Greenspan, one of Canada's most high-profile criminal defence lawyers, who will be representing Stronach.
"He looks forward to the opportunity to fully respond to the charges and to maintain his legacy, both as a philanthropist and as an icon of the Canadian business community," Greenspan said Friday night.
Reached on his cellphone Friday evening, Stronach said, "No. Not arrested, I'm here at my restaurant, OK. Thanks for calling," before hanging up.
Police are appealing to members of the public to come forward if they have any relevant information or may be victims.
"He's been charged and given a court date and been released on some form of conditions," said Const. Tyler Bell, public information officer for Peel police. "Unfortunately, I cannot be specific as to what the conditions are in order to protect the privacy of the victims."
Due to the Stronach's high profile, Bell said, police were being "purposely vague," as they don't want to give any information that might identify the victims, including saying how many there might be.
"This is, obviously, a large investigation and it's very much in its infancy," said Bell. "We are being purposely vague, as it might make identifying the victim's easier, which we don't want to do, as in doing so, it could breach the integrity of the investigation."
Stronach was born in Austria in 1932, and emigrated to Canada in 1954 with only a few hundred dollars. He arrived in Montreal before eventually settling in Ontario, and proceeding on his path to becoming one of Canada's richest men.
In 1958, he started Multimatic Investments, which eventually merged with Magna Electronics in 1969. The name was changed to Magna International in 1973 and, by the 1990s, it grew into one of the largest auto parts manufacturers in the world. Stronach was at the head of the company for decades, leading to him being considered a titan in the Canadian business world.
He also briefly wrote freelance opinion columns for the Star.
Stronach was inducted into the Canadian Business Hall of Fame in 1996. Canadian Business magazine named him the 31st richest Canadian in 2017, estimating his net worth at more than $3 billion.
Recently, Stronach's business ventures include promoting a three-wheeled, electric micro-mobility vehicle called Sarit, which is an acronym for Safe Affordable Reliable Innovative Transport.
Along the way, Stronach has dabbled in other fields, like politics, where he ran as a candidate for the Liberals for the riding of York-Simcoe in 1988, but lost.
He's also become more involved with business and politics in his native Austria, including starting a political party there in the 2010s.
He has also has been involved in thoroughbred horse racing.
Stronach is scheduled to appear in court on July 8.
CAPTION: Billionaire Frank Stronach was arrested Friday and charged with five crimes, including rape, indecent assault on a female, sexual assault and forcible confinement, Peel police said.Matthias Schrader The Associated Press file photo

Document 120 of 500

Another Toronto nursing home says it will shut down, as deadline for 31 license renewals draws nearer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-toronto-nursing-home-says-will-shut-down/docview/3065585417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+Toronto+nursing+home+says+it+will+shut+down%2C+as+deadline+for+31+license+renewals+draws+nearer&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Yet another nursing home in the Toronto area is shutting down, the Star has learned, bringing the total number of closures over the past two-and-a-half years to four.
Expensive land and the high cost of retrofitting old homes to meet new standards have played a role in all four closures, with two being sold to developers and North Park Nursing Home, which will close its doors at the end of the year, shutting down due to a lack of affordable land for redevelopment and the ill health of its owners.
When North Park closes, 75 beds will be lost, in addition to the loss of 393 beds from the previous closures.
Seniors' advocates warn that the city could see further closures in the year to come as a total of 31 Toronto facilities, both for-profit and non-profit, are set to see their licences expire by June 2025, according to a Star analysis of long-term-care-home licensing data.
The province has announced redevelopment plans or new beds for most of these nursing homes, meaning they are unlikely to close. However, the fate of a handful of these home is unclear, as the Ministry of Long-Term Care has not shared any future plans for them publicly.
Jane Meadus, a lawyer with the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly, says many operators may not be willing or able to pay for costly retrofits to bring old beds up to the province's modern design standards, such as rebuilding resident bedrooms that accommodate no more than two beds and the installation of non-slip floors, to name just a few. Two long-term-care homes outside the city have already said they plan to close because they will be unable to meet the Jan. 1, 2025, deadline to install sprinkler systems.
Owners may instead seek to reap big profits by selling the land on which these homes sit - land that has vastly appreciated in value since these homes were built decades ago - says Meadus.
"A lot of owners may just say to heck with all these mandatory redevelopment requirements. It's too complicated. I'm just going to tear the building down and put up a condo," said Meadus, adding that she believes it is possible that hundreds, if not thousands, of long-term-care beds in Toronto could close when the majority of operating licences expire next year.
"Monetarily, they may feel it just doesn't make any sense if they have to spend millions on the issues related to retrofitting or rebuilding, having to find places to re-house residents during renovations or reconstruction, changes in design - meaning requiring more square footage per resident - staffing issues, and construction."
First there was Vermont Square, a 130-bed long-term-care home next to the Annex, that closed in September 2021 and was sold to developer Stafford Homes for $11 million. The same developer also bought the 218-bed Cedarvale Terrace, in Forest Hill just up the road from Casa Loma, for $32 million. Then there was Garden Court Nursing home, a 45-bed facility near Humber Bay Park, which sold in October 2022 for $5.5 million.
The potential closure of more nursing homes that do not renew their licences comes at a time when demand for long-term care beds already far outstrips supply. And any loss of beds in the city could present a significant challenge to the province's $6.4-billion plan to create 31,000 new beds and upgrade another 28,000 built to out-of-date standards by 2028.
Toronto has 12,272 long-term-care beds, as recently reported by the Star's Armine Yalnizyan, but the waiting list is more than three times that amount - nearly 38,000. The province-wide wait-list currently sits at more than 44,000.
If the nine Toronto homes for which the Star could not find redevelopment plans or announcements from the Ministry of Long-Term Care close, some 1,300 beds could be lost if the licences of these homes are not renewed (municipally owned homes are excluded because they are approved, rather than licensed).
The Star's tally is likely an undercount since the most recent publicly available data on licence expiries is five years old. But when the Star asked the Ministry of Long-Term Care to provide an updated list of all nursing homes with licences set to expire next year, the ministry refused, citing "confidentiality concerns."
Such a response rankles Tamara Daly, director of the York University Centre for Aging Research and Education, who points out that all care that takes place in long-term-care homes is publicly funded.
"The people of Ontario collectively pay for long-term care so the people of Ontario are entitled to know this information as soon as it's available," said Daly, who has studied the long-term-care industry for more than two decades. "Can you imagine if a community wasn't told until the last minute that its hospital was closing?"
She also questions whether it is in the interests of Ontario seniors to rely on a care model where more than half of the province's nursing homes are privately owned - many by real estate investment trusts and private equity firms whose priority is to make money for shareholders.
"We can't actually count on these organizations if what they're worried about is being able to fund their buildings or how much money they can earn from selling their real estate," Daly added.
A 2023-24 report published by the provincial government reports that as of March 2023, Ontario had opened 2,382 "new" beds, which included 1,228 net new beds and 1,154 older beds upgraded to modern standards. When the Star asked where these 2,382 beds were, the ministry wouldn't say and provided a link to a webpage of press releases.
Daniel Strauss, a spokesperson for then-Minister of Long-Term Care Stan Cho, who was moved out of the post in Doug Ford's cabinet shuffle Thursday, said that as of January 2024, "over 110 projects" were either completed, under construction or approved to start construction "representing over 18,000 beds." He added that the most recent provincial budget added an additional $155 million to the construction funding subsidy "which will fast-track construction to begin work by Nov. 30, 2024."
Even the loss of just one home in Toronto can cause immeasurable disruption to the lives of its residents forced to look for somewhere else to live.
Seniors' advocate Barbara Chernin witnessed firsthand the mental and physical toll the closing of Cedarvale Terrace, a for-profit nursing home at Walmer Rd. and St. Clair Ave. West, had on its residents. Chernin, who lives across the street from Cedarvale Terrace, recently helped some of the facility's residents to relocate after the home was sold to a developer. Cedarvale was scheduled to see its operating licence expire in 2025 but the Ministry of Long-Term Care allowed its owners to close down two years early. A 19-storey mixed-use building with 87 residential units is now planned for the site.
"The very idea of moving from a home close to family and friends, including support staff who cared for them, preyed on residents' minds causing more stress to their aging bodies," Chernin said. "While many reacted with anger and fear at first trying to fight being taken away from their home of many years, this soon gave way to exhaustion and hopelessness. How many examples of private long-term care and retirement homes shutting down does the government have to see before their hearts break too?"
Donna Duncan, CEO of the Ontario Long Term Care Association, which represents 70 per cent of Ontario's long-term-care homes, including private, not-for-profit and municipal facilities, said closures are related to challenges facing older homes and the barriers to redevelopment.
"No one wants to see a home close, and these decisions are not made lightly," she said.
Many homes cannot rebuild on their existing sites and this is especially true in Toronto, Duncan added, noting that barriers to building a new home include lack of access to affordable land with a footprint large enough to meet current building requirements, zoning restrictions, and land-lease problems if the homes were built on leased land. The rising costs of construction, higher interest rates for mortgages, and higher operating costs are also challenges for some homes, she said.
High land costs, in part, played a role in the decision to close North Park Nursing Home, near Keele St. and Hwy. 401, says the home's administrator Amo Nandlall. She told the Star the owners of the 75-bed for-profit facility wanted to renew their operating licence, which expires in June 2025, but were stymied in part by a lack of affordable land for redevelopment in the city. The older couple that owns the facility also took seriously ill, Nandlall said, which made continuing to operate the home impossible.
"It feels like a home because it's small and we know everybody and families know everybody," Nandlall said, adding that staff and family members are working to find alternative accommodations for residents. "It's sad. It's breaking everybody's heart."
CAPTION: "A lot of owners may just say to heck with all these mandatory redevelopment requirements. It's too complicated. I'm just going to tear the building down and put up a condo," said Jane Meadus, a lawyer with the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly.Tamara Daly, director of theYork University Centre for Aging Research and Education, questions whether it is in the interests of Ontario seniors to rely on a care model where more than half of the province's nursing homes are privately owned.Seniors' advocate Barbara Chernin, who recently helped some of the residents at Cedarvale Terrace to relocate after the home was sold to a developer, witnessed firsthand the toll the closing of the facility had on its residents. "While many reacted with anger and fear at first trying to fight being taken away from their home of many years, this soon gave way to exhaustion and hopelessness."When North Park Nursing Home, near Keele St. and Hwy. 401, closes its doors at the end of the year, 75 beds will be lost.

Subject: Nursing homes; Construction; Long term health care; Older people; REITs; Costs; Redevelopment; Sprinkler systems

A long, hard look at the mind of Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/long-hard-look-at-mind-doug-ford/docview/3065585319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+long%2C+hard+look+at+the+mind+of+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's mind
Join me on a journey inside the baffling mind of our premier, Doug Ford: Mallick, June 5
Premier Doug Ford, like his late brother Rob, is a man of seemingly average intelligence, who too often goes with gut hunches. While it's true that brilliance in a leader doesn't guarantee success - Jimmy Carter too often micro-managed, Bill Clinton used his cleverness to evade the rules, while the highly intelligent Bob Rae wasn't the most effective premier - running a province as complex as Ontario requires a certain level of intellect. When I see federal leaders weaving from French to English in debates, and taking on questions in both official languages, I marvel at the agility of their minds. One solution would be to mandate that candidates for premier be obliged to conduct one debate in French, or to take a few questions posed to them in French. Similarly, if the U.S. declared itself bilingual and mandated at least one debate in Spanish, I doubt that a pedestrian mind like Donald Trump's would be up to the task.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Some observations made as to who Doug Ford is have been blatant. His Achilles heel is, indeed, that he likes to be liked. He is not stupid, but he is uneducated and has shown little interest in science or climate change. He has had to learn, but still seems to enjoy his apparent goal of being the hero among friends. I do not find his folksy demeanor endearing; it hides a good deal of insensitivity and ignorance. His bravado and bluster also come from the privilege of his family of origin.
Anna-Lise Kear, Huntsville
As Premier Doug Ford throws taxpayer loot to the wind with free-for-all goodies, like an under-the-lake parking lot for the wealthy at the Ontario Place spa, no-cost automobile licences and beer in corner stores for those who can't wait for the new convenience, my guess is that he'd win the possible snap election by a landslide. Opposition parties seem to feel they'll be ready for Ford, but how so? The Liberals, the NDP and the Greens all need to recognize they'll be splitting the left-of-centre vote with one another. A more apt term for such an election would be "Doug versus the Dividers."
Jack Drury, Toronto
If Ontario taxpayers want to know where $225 million-$1 billion of their hard-earned tax dollars went, stop into the nearest convenience store, buy a bottle of booze/six pack, put it in a Premier Doug Ford-approved paper bag and head to the nearest park bench to cry into your beer.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
Quinto was absolutely a terrible customer
Zachary Quinto banned from Toronto restaurant: 'Terrific Spock, terrible customer,' June 3
What has been reported about the rude outburst of actor Zachary Quinto at Manita Restaurant is true. I was there dining with a client and heard Quinto display his arrogant outburst. The conclusion was that he was an entitled child. Although he did not say it, I expected his next statement would be "do you know who I am?" Frankly, nobody would care. As former Toronto Star journalist Corey Mintz wrote elsewhere, spending money doesn't buy the privilege of demeaning people who serve you. My advice is that Quinto would get better results with honey over vinegar. It pays to be humble, happy and thankful in great establishments.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
Make electric lawn mowers mandatory
We are only going to sell new electric cars in Canada by 2035 to help with climate change. Great idea! But why can I still buy a $500 gas-powered lawn mower to cut my little patch of grass? With the size of most lots, there is really no need to have lawn mowers that aren't electric. Some cities in Canada and the U.S. have made the step, and banned gas-powered lawn mowers or leaf blowers. They make for unnecessary pollution, and did I mention that the gas powered ones are really noisy? Maybe if my neighbour could only use an electric mower to cut his grass I wouldn't mind that he did it at 8:15 a.m. Saturday mornings.
Shawn Keba, Caledon
Drag could be more modest
Here's why Pride offers a place of salvation for this 'Drag Race' star, June 1
Although I firmly believe everyone has the right to live identifying as they feel most comfortable, as a woman I find the current trend of exaggerated dress of drag queens as pictured on the front page of the Culture section offensive. Generations of women have worked hard to ensure we are not thought of as only the sum of our various physical parts, yet it seems OK with the media and general society that entertainers are so provocative in public venues. I find it disrespectful. Female impersonators such as the late Craig Russell were/are talented entertainers portraying their characters in a favourable light. This current trend is neither entertaining, nor flattering, and is just bad taste.
Elizabeth Dunn, Simcoe County
CAPTION: Columnist Heather Mallick took a walk in Premier Doug Ford's mind. Star readers have thoughts.

Document 122 of 500

Cabinet shuffle is shameless - and revealing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cabinet-shuffle-is-shameless-revealing/docview/3065585251/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cabinet+shuffle+is+shameless+-+and+revealing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Once upon a time, Tories believed in lean, clean government.
Now, not so much.
Behold Doug Ford's new-look cabinet, not merely rebranded but expanded - bigger than ever before: 36 ministers of the Crown, each with a salary premium.
And yet, while in opposition a decade ago, Progressive Conservatives promised to cut the Ontario cabinet from 27 down to 16 ministers if they ever won government. The premier has now exceeded that erstwhile, worthwhile target by 20 ministers.
Six years ago, when he first came to power, Ford made a point of slashing the size of Toronto city hall from 47 to 25 councillors in his quest for greater efficiency. Now, you can comfortably fit all of city council into the historic cabinet room at Queen's Park, while Ford's full parade of overpaid ministers might have the fire marshal worrying about overcrowding.
How did this happen? How did the Tories overshoot from 16 ministers to 36?
Hubris.
The old apoplectic Ford would be crying "hypocrisy" were he still in opposition. But six years in power does something to some people.
It makes them shameless. They drop all pretence.
Thursday's cabinet shuffle is as revealing as it is irrelevant. The old journalistic cliché about shuffling the deck chairs on the Titanic does not apply here, because Ford's government shows no signs of sinking anytime soon (and the opposition is barely treading water).
But the barnacles creep up on you. They have a way of weighing you down.
Ford is padding his cabinet with pretenders - notably eight so-called "associate ministers" who gain a seat but sometimes bring little to the table. Do we need 26-year-old Sam Oosterhoff, who has been an MPP since age 19, as an "associate minister of energy" to help the real energy minister find his way, along with junior ministers in forestry, mines and small business?
It's not new blood, it's bloat.
Yes, Ford is riding high in public opinion polls. No, he does not imagine himself infallible - far from it, for his true talent is in setting the wrong course followed by a course correction and public apology.
The problem is that even after Ford expresses regret, he reverts. The best example of that is his rush to rehabilitate his disgraced former minister of housing, Steve Clark, by appointing him as house leader responsible for shepherding the government's entire legislative agenda.
Late last summer, facing public pressure and a police probe, Clark belatedly quit his post as the minister of municipal affairs who recklessly presided over the Greenbelt fiasco. Clark, who made a name for himself in opposition by reflexively demanding the resignations of several Liberal cabinet ministers for offences real or imagined, had long resisted calls for his own resignation until his position became indefensible.
Now, bizarrely, he is back out of the penalty box. While not yet restored to the cabinet room (lacking ministerial rank), he will be leading the way in the legislative chamber.
What happens if the RCMP probe in the Greenbelt gets in the way? There is, of course, another way: Ford can call an early election before police weigh in, confident that Clark and others won't sing from anything other than the same songbook crafted by the premier himself.
As Ford's loyal house leader, Clark won't be bringing MPPs back from their summer break - which began Thursday, just hours before the cabinet shuffle - until well into autumn. The legislature normally resumes shortly after Labour Day (the date of Clark's resignation from cabinet), but will now be away through Thanksgiving.
That has sparked speculation over prorogation - that parliamentary ritual of renewal where the government delivers a new speech from the throne proclaiming a new agenda. Just in time for an early election in 2025 - one year ahead of the fixed election date codified in law.
What could possibly justify Ford's premature pitch for a third term?
Surely not the premier's old promise to deliver more housing for a hungry market. His controversial plan to bulldoze the Greenbelt amounted to an $8.3-billion boondoggle (in windfall profits to developers until the deal was called off).
Presumably not Ford's past pledge to deliver more beer in corner stores to a thirsty market. His convoluted plan to reopen a signed contract with the Beer Store led him to authorize an alleged $1-billion beer boondoggle (in subsidies and foregone licensing fees).
These two boondoggles are twin bookends to Ford's leadership of the last year. And they are harbingers of the year(s) to come if he triggers an early and expedient election call.
"Buck-a-beer," Ford's 2018 campaign slogan, worked for a while - until it didn't. Even if "beer in corner stores" falls flat, and the Greenbelt bogs down, Ford is already reaching back to another of his perennial campaign bumper stickers:
"Axe the (carbon) tax."
That may sound as stale as old beer, and as tired as those past promises of lean and clean government. But if it sells, Ford is all for it.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford listens to then-housing minister Steve Clark in 2023, shortly before Clark resigned from cabinet over the Greenbelt scandal. Clark is now back as house leader, overseeing a bloated cast of ministers that is just another sign of Ford's hubris, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Cole Burston The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+hard+look+at+Ford%27s+mind%3A+Join+me+on+a+journey+inside+the+baffling+mind+of+our+premier%2C+Doug+Ford%3A+Mallick%2C+June+5+Zachary+Quinto+banned+from+Toronto+restaurant%3A+%27Terrific+Spock%2C+terrible+customer%2C%27+June+3+Here%27s+why+Pride+offers+a+place+of+salvation+for+this+%27Drag+Race%27+star%2C+June+1&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Join me on a journey inside the baffling mind of our premier, Doug Ford: Mallick, June 5
Premier Doug Ford, like his late brother Rob, is a man of seemingly average intelligence, who too often goes with gut hunches.
While it's true that brilliance in a leader doesn't guarantee success - Jimmy Carter too often micromanaged, Bill Clinton used his cleverness to evade the rules, while the highly intelligent Bob Rae wasn't the most effective premier - running a province as complex as Ontario requires a certain level of intellect.
When I see federal leaders weaving from French to English in debates, and taking on questions in both official languages, I marvel at the agility of their minds.
One solution would be to mandate that candidates for premier be obliged to conduct one debate in French, or to take a few questions posed to them in French.
Similarly, if the U.S. declared itself bilingual and mandated at least one debate in Spanish, I doubt that a pedestrian mind like Donald Trump's would be up to the task.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Some observations made as to who Doug Ford is have been blatant. His Achilles heel is, indeed, that he likes to be liked. He is not stupid, but he is uneducated and has shown little interest in science or climate change. He has had to learn, but still seems to enjoy his apparent goal of being the hero among friends.
I do not find his folksy demeanour endearing; it hides a good deal of insensitivity and ignorance. His bravado and bluster also come from the privilege of his family of origin.
Anna-Lise Kear, Huntsville, Ont.
As Premier Doug Ford throws taxpayer loot to the wind with free-for-all goodies, like an under-the-lake parking lot for the wealthy at the Ontario Place spa, no-cost automobile licences and beer in corner stores for those who can't wait for the new convenience, my guess is that he'd win the possible snap election by a landslide.
Opposition parties seem to feel they'll be ready for Ford, but how so? The Liberals, the NDP and the Greens all need to recognize they'll be splitting the left-of-centre vote with one another. A more apt term for such an election would be "Doug versus the Dividers."
Jack Drury, Toronto
If Ontario taxpayers want to know where $225 million to $1 billion of their hard-earned tax dollars went, stop into the nearest convenience store, buy a bottle of booze/six pack, put it in a Premier Doug Ford-approved paper bag and head to the nearest park bench to cry into your beer.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
Actor was absolutely a terrible customer
Zachary Quinto banned from Toronto restaurant: 'Terrific Spock, terrible customer,' June 3
What has been reported about the rude outburst of actor Zachary Quinto at Manita Restaurant is true. I was there dining with a client and heard Quinto.
The conclusion was that he was an entitled child. Although he did not say it, I expected his next statement would be "do you know who I am?" Frankly, nobody would care.
As former Toronto Star journalist Corey Mintz wrote elsewhere, spending money doesn't buy the privilege of demeaning people who serve you. My advice is that Quinto would get better results with honey over vinegar. It pays to be humble, happy and thankful in great establishments.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
Make electric lawn mowers mandatory
We are only going to sell new electric cars in Canada by 2035 to help with climate change. Great idea! But why can I still buy a $500 gas-powered lawn mower to cut my little patch of grass? With the size of most lots, there is really no need to have lawn mowers that aren't electric. Some cities in Canada and the U.S. have made the step, and banned gas-powered lawn mowers or leaf blowers. They make for unnecessary pollution, and did I mention that the gas powered ones are really noisy? Maybe if my neighbour could only use an electric mower to cut his grass I wouldn't mind that he did it at 8:15 a.m. Saturday mornings.
Shawn Keba, Caledon
Drag could be
more modest
Here's why Pride offers a place of salvation for this 'Drag Race' star, June 1
Although I firmly believe everyone has the right to live identifying as they feel most comfortable, as a woman I find the current trend of exaggerated dress of drag queens as pictured on the front page of the Culture section offensive.
Generations of women have worked hard to ensure we are not thought of as only the sum of our various physical parts, yet it seems OK with the media and general society that entertainers are so provocative in public venues. I find it disrespectful.
Female impersonators such as the late Craig Russell were/are talented entertainers portraying their characters in a favourable light. This current trend is neither entertaining, nor flattering, and is just bad taste.
Elizabeth Dunn, Simcoe County
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's folksy demeanor hides a good deal of insensitivity and ignorance, writes Anna-Lise Kear.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo

Document 124 of 500

A way to publicly name accused MPs: Members who don't sit on intelligence committee could divulge alleged collaborators

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/way-publicly-name-accused-mps/docview/3065577512/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+way+to+publicly+name+accused+MPs%3A+Members+who+don%27t+sit+on+intelligence+committee+could+divulge+alleged+collaborators&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA MPs named by top-secret intelligence as aiding foreign interference in Canadian politics continue to go unidentified by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, despite opposition demands to say publicly who they are.
But at least one constitutional expert says it's possible - and entirely in line with long-established rules - for certain MPs to simply disclose the names from the floor of the House of Commons.
Opposition parliamentarians - and even some Liberals - say not providing their names casts a shadow over all of their peers, and is patently unfair to Canadians who have a right to know.
The issue is now evolving into a partisan free-for-all.
As Trudeau's Liberals are accused of hiding the names to protect themselves, they accuse Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of deliberately refusing to accept the necessary security clearance to view the intelligence, allowing him to ignore the facts in a bid to score political points.
Politics aside, there is a distinction between MPs on the powerful security committee that published the bombshell allegations this week, and others who don't sit on the committee and learn the information through separate security briefings.
Here's a look.
Can the MPs on the committee
release the names?
The report comes from the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, known as NSICOP, which is made up of MPs and senators from all parties.
The committee's responsibilities are enshrined in law. Among the requirements: that members swear an oath they "will not communicate or use without due authority any information obtained in confidence by me in that capacity." Further, the members are all permanently bound to secrecy under the Security of Information Act.
So, if any member of the committee were to disclose publicly what they know, they could face years in prison.
This legal requirement for secrecy also extends to an area where MPs are typically shielded from prosecution: the floor of the House of Commons.
MPs have several rights deemed necessary to allow them to fully do their jobs in Parliament, known as parliamentary privileges. Among them is a right to free speech with total immunity from prosecution or civil liability.
But section 12 of the law establishing NSICOP removes that right from the MPs on the committee as it relates to those duties. If they disclose any information before Parliament, they can be prosecuted.
Whether that's constitutional might end up before Canada's top court.
In 2022, the Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled in favour of law professor Ryan Alford's argument that section 12 is unconstitutional, on the grounds that if parliamentary privilege is to be limited, it requires a constitutional amendment.
In April of this year, the Ontario Appeal court overturned that judgment. Alford told the Star he will ask the Supreme Court to hear an appeal.
In theory, MPs on NSICOP could test the law by standing up in the Commons and just saying what they know - which in turn would test the willingness of the government to prosecute them.
The MPs or senators would be disobeying the prime minister and ignoring the law, Alford said.
"It would be not merely an act of conscience," he said .
"It would be an act of civil disobedience, one that would be taken very seriously. I don't think they should be in that position, especially given the fact that if I'm correct and the Supreme Court agrees with me that that section of the statute is unconstitutional."
Could other MPs spill the beans?
The situation is entirely different for MPs who don't sit on NSICOP, according to Carleton University professor and constitutional expert Philippe Lagassé.
The law trumping parliamentary privilege for those on the committee doesn't apply to those who don't sit on it, he explained. That means, should an MP, such as the prime minister or an opposition leader, get a security briefing that includes the names of parliamentarians suspected of collaborating with hostile foreign governments, that MP could disclose the names to the public, Lagassé said.
All they'd have to do is walk into the House of Commons and say them out loud.
"Ironically enough, the only ones who can't say the names are members of NSICOP, because their legislation removes that privilege from them," he said. "If you had another parliamentarian who is not a member of NSICOP, who learned the information, then they could disclose it and be shielded from prosecution."
Asked if there is any debate about whether that's true, Lagassé said it's unambiguous.
"This is very clear," he said.
Which MPs don't sit on NSICOP
but might know the names?
It depends what they're told in top secret briefings that people like Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc receive from security officials. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has also said he's willing to get the required security clearance to have access to the intelligence so he could know what's at stake, as he has in similar past situations.
Poilievre - who has called on the government to release the names - has not been willing to take those briefings, claiming he doesn't want to accept the information if it means he can't share or act on what he knows.
Instead, the Conservatives argue LeBlanc could disclose the names under the public disclosure provisions of the Security of Information Act. The law says if someone discloses information because they are acting in the public interest, they can be spared.
But the government has suggested it is not interested in unilaterally disclosing the names.
LeBlanc told a Commons committee Thursday morning he has no intention of violating the Security of Information Act by disclosing what he knows. He has also downplayed the committee findings by suggesting that unspecified information in the report is uncorroborated.
"The idea there is a perfect list of names that is entirely reliable that should be released to the public is simply irresponsible," LeBlanc told the committee.
Alford, a professor at the Bora Laskin faculty of law, argues that too often it's only by MPs using their privilege that the government's claims of national security can be tested.
He points to the years-long battle over documents related to a security breach at Winnipeg's National Microbiology Laboratory which the government said were too sensitive to release to a House of Commons committee.
That fight went all the way to Federal Court, and ultimately a panel of judges was set up to review what could be disclosed.
They concluded the information was being withheld to protect people from embarrassment for failures of policy and implementation, not national security. The documents were released.
"If things really are so confidential, they shouldn't be received," Alford said.
Could we find the MPs' names
another way?
Another way the names could be disclosed is if charges are laid against any of the MPs alleged to have collaborated with foreign powers. David McGuinty, chair of NSICOP, told reporters this week that it's now up to the RCMP to investigate.
But the NSICOP report doesn't conclude whether what the MPs or parliamentarians were doing was illegal, noting that charges would be hard to lay given long-standing issues around how classified information can be used in judicial processes.
In a statement, the RCMP said it has not been given the entirety of the information in the report. It doesn't specify whether it means the information about MPs or something else - the report is broader than just those bombshell allegations.
But the national police force also said it won't confirm or deny it is investigating anybody.
"The RCMP must exercise significant caution with respect to public statements related to ongoing investigations," the statement says.
"An RCMP confirmation of such an investigation has the potential to cause damage to reputations prior to meeting an appropriate level of proof, or to interfere with an ongoing investigation."
CAPTION: It's unknown whether charges will be laid against any of the MPs alleged to have collaborated with foreign powers. David McGuinty, chair of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamen- tarians, told reporters this week that it's now up to the RCMP to investigate.Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Document 125 of 500

'It's breaking everybody's heart': Yet another Toronto nursing home is slated for closure, as more homes decide to shut down or sell to developers rather than pay the high cost of retro tting to meet new standards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/breaking-everybodys-heart/docview/3065577470/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+breaking+everybody%27s+heart%27%3A+Yet+another+Toronto+nursing+home+is+slated+for+closure%2C+as+more+homes+decide+to+shut+down+or+sell+to+developers+rather+than+pay+the+high+cost+of+retro+tting+to+meet+new+standards&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Yet another nursing home in the Toronto area is shutting down, the Star has learned, bringing the total number of closures over the past two-and-a-half years to four.
Expensive land and the high cost of retrofitting old homes to meet new standards have played a role in all four closures, with two being sold to developers and North Park Nursing Home, which will close its doors at the end of the year, shutting down due to a lack of affordable land for redevelopment and the ill health of its owners.
When North Park closes, 75 beds will be lost, in addition to the loss of 393 beds from the previous closures.
Seniors' advocates warn that the city could see further closures in the year to come as a total of 31 Toronto facilities, both for-profit and non-profit, are set to see their licences expire by June 2025, according to a Star analysis of long-term-care-home licensing data.
The province has announced redevelopment plans or new beds for most of these nursing homes, meaning they are unlikely to close. However, the fate of a handful of these home is unclear, as the Ministry of Long-Term Care has not shared any future plans for them publicly.
Jane Meadus, a lawyer with the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly, says many operators may not be willing or able to pay for costly retrofits to bring old beds up to the province's modern design standards, such as rebuilding resident bedrooms that accommodate no more than two beds and the installation of non-slip floors, to name just a few. Two long-term-care homes outside the city have already said they plan to close because they will be unable to meet the Jan. 1, 2025, deadline to install sprinkler systems.
Owners may instead seek to reap big profits by selling the land on which these homes sit - land that has vastly appreciated in value since these homes were built decades ago - Meadus says.
"A lot of owners may just say to heck with all these mandatory redevelopment requirements. It's too complicated. I'm just going to tear the building down and put up a condo," said Meadus, adding that she believes it is possible that hundreds, if not thousands, of long-term-care beds in Toronto could close when the majority of operating licences expire next year.
"Monetarily, they may feel it just doesn't make any sense if they have to spend millions on the issues related to retrofitting or rebuilding, having to find places to re-house residents during renovations or reconstruction, changes in design - meaning requiring more square footage per resident - staffing issues, and construction."
First there was Vermont Square, a 130-bed long-term-care home next to the Annex, that closed in September 2021 and was sold to developer Stafford Homes for $11 million. The same developer also bought the 218-bed Cedarvale Terrace, in Forest Hill just up the road from Casa Loma, for $32 million. Then there was Garden Court Nursing home, a 45-bed facility near Humber Bay Park, which sold in October 2022 for $5.5 million.
The potential closure of more nursing homes that do not renew their licences comes at a time when demand for long-term care beds already far outstrips supply. And any loss of beds in the city could present a significant challenge to the province's $6.4-billion plan to create 31,000 new beds and upgrade another 28,000 built to out-of-date standards by 2028.
Toronto has 12,272 long-term-care beds, as recently reported by the Star's Armine Yalnizyan, but the waiting list is more than three times that amount - nearly 38,000. The province-wide wait-list currently sits at more than 44,000.
If the nine Toronto homes for which the Star could not find redevelopment plans or announcements from the Ministry of Long-Term Care close, some 1,300 beds could be lost if the licences of these homes are not renewed (municipally owned homes are excluded because they are approved, rather than licensed).
The Star's tally is likely an undercount since the most recent publicly available data on licence expiries is five years old. But when the Star asked the Ministry of Long-Term Care to provide an updated list of all nursing homes with licences set to expire next year, the ministry refused, citing "confidentiality concerns."
Such a response rankles Tamara Daly, director of the York University Centre for Aging Research and Education, who points out that all care that takes place in long-term-care homes is publicly funded.
"The people of Ontario collectively pay for long-term care, so the people of Ontario are entitled to know this information as soon as it's available," said Daly, who has studied the long-term-care industry for more than two decades. "Can you imagine if a community wasn't told until the last minute that its hospital was closing?"
She also questions whether it is in the interests of Ontario seniors to rely on a care model where more than half of the province's nursing homes are privately owned - many by real estate investment trusts and private equity firms whose priority is to make money for shareholders.
"We can't actually count on these organizations if what they're worried about is being able to fund their buildings or how much money they can earn from selling their real estate," Daly added.
A 2023-24 report published by the provincial government reports that as of March 2023, Ontario had opened 2,382 "new" beds, which included 1,228 net new beds and 1,154 older beds upgraded to modern standards. When the Star asked where these 2,382 beds were, the ministry wouldn't say and provided a link to a webpage of press releases.
Daniel Strauss, a spokesperson for then-Minister of Long-Term Care Stan Cho, who was moved out of the post in Doug Ford's cabinet shuffle Thursday, said that as of January 2024, "over 110 projects" were either completed, under construction or approved to start construction "representing over 18,000 beds." He added that the most recent provincial budget added an additional $155 million to the construction funding subsidy "which will fast-track construction to begin work by Nov. 30, 2024."
Even the loss of just one home in Toronto can cause immeasurable disruption to the lives of its residents forced to look for somewhere else to live.
Seniors' advocate Barbara Chernin witnessed firsthand the mental and physical toll the closing of Cedarvale Terrace, a for-profit nursing home at Walmer Road and St. Clair Avenue West, had on its residents. Chernin, who lives across the street from Cedarvale Terrace, recently helped some of the facility's residents to relocate after the home was sold to a developer. Cedarvale was scheduled to see its operating licence expire in 2025 but the Ministry of Long-Term Care allowed its owners to close down two years early. A 19-storey mixed-use building with 87 residential units is now planned for the site.
"The very idea of moving from a home close to family and friends, including support staff who cared for them, preyed on residents' minds causing more stress to their aging bodies," Chernin said. "While many reacted with anger and fear at first trying to fight being taken away from their home of many years, this soon gave way to exhaustion and hopelessness. How many examples of private long-term care and retirement homes shutting down does the government have to see before their hearts break too?"
Donna Duncan, CEO of the Ontario Long Term Care Association, which represents 70 per cent of Ontario's long-term-care homes, including private, not-for-profit and municipal facilities, said closures are related to challenges facing older homes and the barriers to redevelopment.
"No one wants to see a home close, and these decisions are not made lightly," she said.
Many homes cannot rebuild on their existing sites and this is especially true in Toronto, Duncan added, noting that barriers to building a new home include lack of access to affordable land with a footprint large enough to meet current building requirements, zoning restrictions, and land-lease problems if the homes were built on leased land. The rising costs of construction, higher interest rates for mortgages, and higher operating costs are also challenges for some homes, she said.
High land costs, in part, played a role in the decision to close North Park Nursing Home, near Keele St. and Hwy. 401, says the home's administrator Amo Nandlall. She told the Star the owners of the 75-bed for-profit facility wanted to renew their operating licence, which expires in June 2025, but were stymied in part by a lack of affordable land for redevelopment in the city. The older couple that owns the facility also took seriously ill, Nandlall said, which made continuing to operate the home impossible.
"It feels like a home because it's small and we know everybody and families know everybody," Nandlall said, adding that staff and family members are working to find alternative accommodations for residents. "It's sad. It's breaking everybody's heart."
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star

Subject: Nursing homes; Construction; Long term health care; Older people; REITs; Costs; Redevelopment; Sprinkler systems

Document 126 of 500

Insiders say Doug Ford's cabinet shuffle reveals anger at surprise kaffiyeh ban, confidence he'll be exonerated in RCMP Greenbelt probe

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/insiders-say-doug-fords-cabinet-shuffle-reveals/docview/3065620425/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Insiders+say+Doug+Ford%27s+cabinet+shuffle+reveals+anger+at+surprise+kaffiyeh+ban%2C+confidence+he%27ll+be+exonerated+in+RCMP+Greenbelt+probe&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a cabinet shuffle aimed at addressing controversies as disparate as the Greenbelt scandal and the legislature's kaffiyeh ban.
Premier Doug Ford has signalled his confidence an RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion land swap imbroglio will not touch his Progressive Conservative government.
Ford also demonstrated his displeasure at being blindsided when Speaker Ted Arnott banned Palestinian kaffiyeh scarves from being worn in the legislature in April.
That's why the premier stripped house leader duties from Paul Calandra, who he felt should have warned him about Arnott's surprise move, and handed them to Steve Clark.
Calandra will remain as minister of municipal affairs and housing, a post he assumed last September after Clark had stepped down at the height of the Greenbelt fiasco.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the back story of Thursday's ministerial shuffle, said the premier, who opposed the kaffiyeh ban, was unhappy at being caught flat-footed on such a divisive issue.
Arnott outlawed the black and white scarves commonly worn by pro-Palestinian protesters since the Hamas-Israel war began because he felt they break the rules against "political" garments in the chamber.
That sparked divisions inside Ford's caucus with, as the Star reported April 26, some Tory MPPs questioning the wisdom of the premier's intervention, saying "he didn't need to pick sides."
Apparently unfazed by the Greenbelt saga, the premier is putting Clark on the path back to being a full-fledged cabinet minister. He will earn $137,879 as government house leader compared to the $165,851 earned by a minister.
But unlike Calandra, the Tories' pugnacious point-man in the chamber, he won't take opposition questions during legislature's daily question period.
Ford's decision to move Stephen Lecce from education to the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification, in a swap with Todd Smith, exposed frictions between the provincial and federal Conservatives.
An attack on Lecce by rookie Conservative MP Jamil Juvani (Durham) underscored the rift between the premier and federal Tory Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Jivani, a former Ford aide at Queen's Park, took to social media to hail Lecce's departure from the ministry.
"Ontario has desperately needed a new education minister for years. I hope real policy changes are coming, so this will be remembered as a great day for all students, parents and teachers," he said Thursday night.
That prompted Nick Kouvalis, Ford's pollster and a key architect of the Tories' 2022 re-election, to fire back on social media the next day.
"I love Pierre. I love Jenni. But, Jamil, be careful ... Lecce is under my protection. Chose your next steps carefully ... You've had a wide berth so far because of Pierre and Jenni," said Kouvalis, referring to Jenni Byrne, Poilievre's most powerful adviser.
Lecce was the longest-serving education minister in decades, landing contracts during two contentious rounds of negotiations with unions, as well as ushering in a host of reforms to the sector, including destreaming high school courses.
Those now-eliminated applied courses were widely known to adversely impact Black and low-income students, hindering their chances of graduating or attending post-secondary school.
A back-to-basics focus under Lecce was meant to tackle parents' concerns about school boards being distracted by non-academic issues and culture wars.
During his five-year tenure, the ministry also added mandatory Black history lessons as well as Holocaust education to the curriculum, and provided funding to combat Islamophobia. Lecce even appointed a supervisor to take over the Peel school board - a move ministers rarely resort to - after the director and trustees there failed to address discrimination and dysfunction.
Cathy Abraham, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said she is looking forward to meeting with Smith.
"Right now the spotlight is on funding because we are all doing our budgets, and we're being asked to do way, way more with far less - and it's becoming a real challenge across the province," she said, adding it is encouraging that Smith's wife is a school vice-principal because he would understand "those kitchen-table conversations about the challenges."
That was echoed by Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association president Michael Bellmore, who has met with Smith and said "he's a very affable guy ... he seems to be a very quick study and I think it's fantastic that he has lived in the environment, of living with an educator and understanding the ups and downs of what folks in education do on a daily basis."
But teacher unions were skeptical.
"We don't know him at all from an education capacity, but we have talked to the Ontario Autism Coalition who did work with him and they did not find that he was helpful at all, but perhaps it will be different," said Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, referring to Smith's stint overseeing community and social services.
Smith, who is in France on a nuclear trade mission, said in a statement Friday he was "looking forward to ensuring our students are prepared for the careers of the future."
Opposition parties said the shuffle shouldn't distract voters from the Mounties' ongoing probe into the Greenbelt, an extended summer break of the legislature - which will not return until Oct. 21 instead of Sept. 9 - and Ford's ruminating about a snap election one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"This is a government that doesn't actually want to get any work done and doesn't want to be held to account because - guess what - this is a corrupt government," charged NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said the reboot and recess are a bid to shift criticism from Ford's "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" to put beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails in more stores a year ahead of schedule.
"He's created the biggest cabinet in history, using your money, and he only included nine women," Crombie noted of the 36-member executive council.
CAPTION: Premier Doug is shown at a news conference in Ottawa on April 29, 2024.
