Document 127 of 500

Top-secret intelligence alleges some MPs aided foreign interference. This might be a way to publicly name them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/top-secret-intelligence-alleges-some-mps-aided/docview/3065620351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Top-secret+intelligence+alleges+some+MPs+aided+foreign+interference.+This+might+be+a+way+to+publicly+name+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - MPs named by top-secret intelligence as aiding foreign interference in Canadian politics continue to go unidentified by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, despite opposition demands to say publicly who they are.
But at least one constitutional expert says it's possible - and entirely in line with long-established rules - for certain MPs to simply disclose the names from the floor of the House of Commons.
Opposition parliamentarians - and even some Liberals - say not providing their names casts a shadow over all of their peers, and is patently unfair to Canadians who have a right to know.
The issue is now evolving into a partisan free-for-all.
As Trudeau's Liberals are accused of hiding the names to protect themselves, they accuse Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre of deliberately refusing to accept the necessary security clearance to view the intelligence, allowing him to ignore the facts in a bid to score political points.
Politics aside, there is a distinction between MPs on the powerful security committee that published the bombshell allegations this week, and others who don't sit on the committee and learn the information through separate security briefings.
Here's a look.
Can the MPs on the committee say the names?
The report comes from the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, known as NSICOP, which is made up of MPs and senators from all parties.
The committee's responsibilities are enshrined in law. Among the requirements: that members swear an oath they "will not communicate or use without due authority any information obtained in confidence by me in that capacity." Further, the members are all permanently bound to secrecy under the Security of Information Act.
So, if any member of the committee were to disclose publicly what they know, they could face years in prison.
This legal requirement for secrecy also extends to an area where MPs are typically shielded from prosecution: the floor of the House of Commons.
MPs have several rights deemed necessary to allow them to fully do their jobs in Parliament, known as parliamentary privileges. Among them is a right to free speech with total immunity from prosecution or civil liability.
But section 12 of the law establishing NSICOP removes that right from the MPs on the committee as it relates to those duties. If they disclose any information before Parliament, they can be prosecuted.
Whether that's constitutional might end up before Canada's top court.
In 2022, the Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled in favour of law professor Ryan Alford's argument that section 12 is unconstitutional, on the grounds that if parliamentary privilege is to be limited, it requires a constitutional amendment.
In April of this year, the Ontario Appeal court overturned that judgment. Alford told the Star he will ask the Supreme Court to hear an appeal.
In theory, MPs on NSICOP could test the law by standing up in the Commons and just saying what they know - which in turn would test the willingness of the government to prosecute them.
The MPs or senators would be disobeying the prime minister and ignoring the law, Alford said.
"It would be not merely an act of conscience," he said .
"It would be an act of civil disobedience, one that would be taken very seriously. I don't think they should be in that position, especially given the fact that if I'm correct and the Supreme Court agrees with me that that section of the statute is unconstitutional."
Could other MPs spill the beans?
The situation is entirely different for MPs who don't sit on NSICOP, according to Carleton University professor and constitutional expert Philippe Lagassé.
The law trumping parliamentary privilege for those on the committee doesn't apply to those who don't sit on it, he explained. That means, should an MP like the prime minister or an opposition leader get a security briefing that includes the names of parliamentarians suspected of collaborating with hostile foreign governments, that MP could disclose the names to the public, Lagassé said.
All they'd have to do is walk into the House of Commons and say them out loud.
"Ironically enough, the only ones who can't say the names are members of NSICOP, because their legislation removes that privilege from them," he said. "If you had another parliamentarian who is not a member of NSICOP, who learned the information, then they could disclose it and be shielded from prosecution."
Asked if there is any debate about whether that's true, Lagassé said it's unambiguous.
"This is very clear," he said.
Which MPs don't sit on NSICOP but might know the names?
It depends what they're told in top secret briefings that people like Trudeau and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc receive from security officials. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has also said he's willing to get the required security clearance to have access to the intelligence so he could know what's at stake, as he has in similar past situations.
Poilievre - who has called on the government to release the names - has not been willing to take those briefings, claiming he doesn't want to accept the information if it means he can't share or act on what he knows. Instead, the Conservatives argue LeBlanc could disclose the names under the public disclosure provisions of the Security of Information Act.
The law says if someone discloses information because they are acting in the public interest, they can be spared.
But the government has suggested it is not interested in unilaterally disclosing the names.
LeBlanc told a Commons committee Thursday morning he has no intention of violating the Security of Information Act by disclosing what he knows. He's also downplayed the committee findings by suggesting that unspecified information in the report is uncorroborated.
"The idea there is a perfect list of names that is entirely reliable that should be released to the public is simply irresponsible," LeBlanc told the committee.
Alford, a professor at the Bora Laskin faculty of law, argues that too often it's only by MPs using their privilege that the government's claims of national security can be tested.
He points to the years-long battle over documents related to a security breach at Winnipeg's National Microbiology Laboratory which the government said were too sensitive to release to a House of Commons committee.
That fight went all the way to Federal Court, and ultimately a panel of judges was set up to review what could be disclosed.
They concluded the information was being withheld to protect people from embarrassment for failures of policy and implementation, not national security. The documents were released.
"If things really are so confidential, they shouldn't be received," Alford said.
Could we find out about the MPs' names another way?
Another way the names could be disclosed is if charges are laid against any of the MPs alleged to have collaborated with foreign powers. David McGuinty, chair of NSICOP, told reporters this week that it's now up to the RCMP to investigate .
But the NSICOP report doesn't conclude whether what the MPs or parliamentarians were doing was illegal, noting that charges would be hard to lay given long-standing issues around how classified information can be used in judicial processes.
In a statement, the RCMP said it's not been given the entirety of the information in the report.
It doesn't specify whether it means the information about MPs or something else - the report is broader than just those bombshell allegations.
But the national police force also said it won't confirm or deny it is investigating anybody.
"The RCMP must exercise significant caution with respect to public statements related to ongoing investigations," the statement says.
"An RCMP confirmation of such an investigation has the potential to cause damage to reputations prior to meeting an appropriate level of proof, or to interfere with an ongoing investigation."
CAPTION: Liberal MP David McGuinty, chair of the National Security and Intelligence Committee of Parliamentarians, responds to questions from reporters before heading into a meeting of the Liberal caucus in Ottawa on June 5, 2024.

Subject: Civil disobedience; Committees; Investigations; National security; Politics; Prime ministers; Immunity from prosecution; Security clearances

People: McGuinty, David Joseph

Title: Top-secret intelligence alleges some MPs aided foreign interference. This might be a way to publicly name them

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

TTC irony, furious federal Tories and no need for home ec class

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-irony-furious-federal-tories-no-need-home-ec/docview/3065368227/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+irony%2C+furious+federal+Tories+and+no+need+for+home+ec+class&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Essential TTC irony
No quick fix for TTC strike says Ford government, as contract talks hit 'impasse' ahead of deadline, June 6
Toronto transit has a brand new definition of irony. There are huge posters along the subway walls featuring happy and grinning employees of the TTC with the slogan underneath stating "Be Essential." Seriously? The high courts have seen fit to deem the TTC as a "non-essential" service, thereby allowing a group of malcontents to disrupt both the personal and professional lives of thousands of citizens on a daily basis in the event of a strike. And yet apparently TTC workers believe they are "essential." That truly is irony.
Steve Veale, Toronto
Furious federal Tories
Behind the scenes, Conservatives are furious with one of their MPs over comments on podcast that defied Pierre Poilievre's wishes, June 3
It comes as absolutely no surprise that the desperate-for-power Pierre Poilievre Conservatives are furious at an Alberta Conservative MP for simply telling the truth about his beliefs. Like Stephen Harper before him, Poilievre must realize that many of his MPs, although certainly welcome under the Conservative tent, do not share positions on all issues as the party moves further right. Poilievre's apparent desire to censor his MPs and his distrust of the free press are all too stark and alarming. Like Donald Trump, he panders to, and only pretends to be friends with, the common folk who must remember the saying "those who do not learn history are doomed to repeat it."
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, Ont.
Home ec should be taught at home
Ontario is getting ready to update its home ec courses - and wants your input on what needs to change, June 3
I've been reading about all the new initiatives the province is tacking onto our already overburdened curriculum, like financial literary testing and reintroducing home economics, and am asking myself what is happening. Is it that the Ministry of Education simply needs to prove it has something to do? Is it that Premier Doug Ford and Minister Stephen Lecce just want to create further chaos in an already chaotic curriculum? Or is it partially that society is recognizing that the fundamental skills once passed on by family are simply not being taught at home? I go back 60 odd years, where my mother had me baking cakes in the kitchen, teaching me how to sew buttons on shirts, ironing my own clothes and even learning some rudimentary knitting skills, as well as having me save my pennies to help buy the bike I wanted. I was fully independent when I left home at 18, with no cellphone umbilical cord to save me. It's time we spend more time teaching children the basic life skills they require for when they leave home so schools don't have to waste time away from the academic skills.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Queen and Dundas demise
There's nothing left at the The Bay at Yonge and Queen, not even a good bra: Mallick, June 3
Thank you to columnist Heather Mallick for shedding some light on the sad reality facing quality shopping options in the core of the city. The flagship Bay store was the last hope for variety and value of quality merchandise after Eaton's then Nordstrom's. The Bay store in Windsor parallels the scenario (I frequent both), with a broken escalator and a broken heating system making it so hot it drives customers away. I fear a sad future for any Bay life anywhere.
Liz Haugh, Windsor
Trump and having faith
Jesus is their saviour, Trump is their candidate. Ex-president's backers say he shares faith, values, May 18
Donald Trump's faithful followers who also call themselves Christian - notably U.S. congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene - need to do a major moral rethink as to which fundamental values taught and practised by Jesus Christ they're willing to uphold or ignore, personally though especially publicly. Jesus fundamentally was about non-violence, genuine compassion and non-wealth. His teachings and practices epitomize so much of the primary component of socialism, such as do not hoard morbidly gratuitous wealth in the midst of poverty. He clearly would not tolerate the accumulation of tens of billions of dollars by individual people, especially while so many others go hungry and homeless.
While there are many Christians who have rejected Trump and his politics, regardless of his tempting conservative politics and pro-life professions, there nonetheless remains a vocal and politically active Christian element celebrating Trump conservatism.
Too many adherents of institutional Christianity - those Christians most resistant to Christ's fundamental teachings of non-violence, compassion and non-wealth - tend to insist upon creating their creator's nature in their own fallible and often angry, vengeful image; for example, proclaiming at publicized protests that "God hates" such-and-such group of people. Often being the most vocal, they make very bad examples of Christ's fundamental message, especially to the young and impressionable.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
More thoughts on D-Day
Eighty years ago, thousands of Allied soldiers died on D-Day. Their deaths are commemorated today as the price paid for preserving democratic ideals against tyranny. How sad these days that anti-democratic movements globally are on the rise, the initiative of seemingly mature adults, "leaders", who should know better. We've seen the consequences of similarly misguided initiatives in the past. As Rudyard Kipling wrote about the dead in the First World War: "If any questions why we died, tell them, because our fathers lied."
Enrico Cumbo, Toronto
In the days leading up to June 6, the 80th anniversary of D-Day, I searched online to find a commemorative event to attend in Toronto. On the Veterans Affairs website I found events in Ottawa, Halifax, Moncton and Ottawa, but nothing in Toronto. So I called Veterans Affairs. They knew of nothing either. They also said no one had asked about a Toronto commemorative event. I called my MP's office. They barely knew the significance of June 6. My Toronto councillor's office didn't pick up the phone. While in the end there was a ceremony at Coronation Park, this information should have been more readily available.
Keith Jolly, Toronto

Document 129 of 500

TTC strike or not, all appears lost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ttc-strike-not-all-appears-lost/docview/3065224128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+strike+or+not%2C+all+appears+lost&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not clear if Toronto will be shut down Friday, or for how long. Depending on negotiations, the entire city could be economically halted, socially isolated and thrown into chaos because of a strike by 12,000 of the TTC's 16,000 workers.
It's more of the usual strangulation that afflicts this city. But that's the TTC in 2024. Can't live with it, can't live without it.
It would be helpful to know what Amalgamated Transit Union 113 wanted, but it wouldn't say how much money it was asking for - it sees publicity as negotiation via media, whatever that is - which is infuriating.
Radio silence is a classic Doug Ford tactic so it's odd to see it adopted by a union that suspects the percentages might not be popular with the public. Although thanks to media decline, most people don't know what TTC workers earn right now anyway.
It's not true that TTC riders aren't sympathetic. Workers need raises. Everyone does. The union seeks improvements in job security, benefits and workplace safety, precisely what riders are seeking as well as they use a dirty, ill-maintained, badly run, slowed-down and dangerous transit system in Canada's largest city.
Public services are in decline across the country. What a grievous thing it is to see quality of life sliding so fast and so far. On Thursday, a major Calgary water main burst, leaving the city without enough water for any purpose, including drinking the stuff.
The Alberta government sent out a misspelled alert saying, "All residents and businesses must conserve water. Do not shower or bath [sic]. Do not wash dishes, or run appliances that use water." But it hadn't co-ordinated with the City of Calgary, which simply advised people to cut back.
Here's the fallout. Canadians will begin to believe that no one is in charge of the basics, like transit, water, garbage collection, park maintenance and policing of matters like auto theft and traffic control. Daily life becomes an exercise in frustration, something many people are less able to cope with emotionally in this strange era.
This month at my house saw cancelled appointments, postponed orders and deliveries, an angry contracted-out garbage collector doing public damage because he resents his job and the strange sight of TTC workers enthusiastically cleaning escalator banks at my station. (The TTC doesn't clean and polish. It was pure pre-strike theatre.)
I use Beck Taxi's terrific app out of solidarity because Uber et al has devastated the taxi industry while treating gig drivers and customers like squishmallows. But the problem is that even if you call a cab to your house during a strike, you have no guarantee you'll be able to get a cab home.
You can drive, but Toronto traffic was choked up even before a possible strike. There will be delays at the construction sites that clogged traffic in the first place because nobody can get to work. All drivers, even car poolers, are asking for trouble.
So Torontonians won't travel, inflicting further distress on small businesses hungry for customers. This, as usual, will be the hottest summer in human history so it seems as good a time as any to make everything worse.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow is a practical person, exactly the kind of grounded mayor we need. She understands that the Gardiner can't be a thin gravel road for three years. It's intolerable.
Searching for a metaphor for Toronto traffic, I have become a fan of Instagram unclogging videos, in which drains are magically cleared by public-spirited citizens armed with crowbars and giant forks.
We have to make sure Chow has the tools and money required to keep Toronto viable. Success will be costly but infinitely worth it.
Heather Mallick is a Toronto-based columnist covering current affairs for the Star. Follow her on Twitter: @HeatherMallick
CAPTION: The Amalgamated Transit Union 113 seeks improved job security, benefits and workplace safety, precisely what riders are seeking as well, as they use an ill-maintained transit system, writes Heather Mallick.Frank Gunn The Canadian Press

Document 130 of 500

Ford shuffles front ranks: Key ministers swap jobs, Clark promoted as house rises early for summer break

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-shuffles-front-ranks/docview/3065224095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+shuffles+front+ranks%3A+Key+ministers+swap+jobs%2C+Clark+promoted+as+house+rises+early+for+summer+break&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a major overhaul, Premier Doug Ford has shuffled his cabinet - swapping longtime education minister Stephen Lecce and energy minister Todd Smith - ahead of an extended summer hiatus.
Ford on Thursday ended the legislative session a week early before launching a massive revamp, moving 14 ministers and expanding his executive council to 36.
The move comes as the premier is considering an election next spring, a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 date and against the backdrop of an RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"We're at an important moment in our province's history with clear choices," Ford said in a statement.
The premier is so confident the Mounties' probe will not touch his office that he has brought back Steve Clark - who resigned as housing minister last September over the Greenbelt imbroglio - to be government house leader. However, that role will no longer be a cabinet position.
Clark takes over the job from Paul Calandra, who keeps his other duties as minister of municipal affairs and housing.
The most significant change is plugging Lecce into the rebranded Ministry of Energy and Electrification, with increased responsibilities for electric vehicle infrastructure as well as boosting Ontario's nuclear power generation fleet.
"It has been a very inspiring experience for me to learn from young people in Canada and Ontario," he told reporters after being sworn in.
Replacing one of the longest-serving education ministers in the province's history is Smith, who inherits a department that has labour peace with teachers until 2026, recently banned cellphones in schools and will require high school students to pass a financial literacy test in order to graduate, among other back-to-basics changes.
Smith was in France on a nuclear trade mission and was not at the swearing-in. His wife is a secondary school vice-principal.
Joining the enlarged cabinet is Natalia Kusendova-Bashta (Mississauga Centre), a registered nurse who worked hospital shifts during the COVID-19 pandemic emergency, as minister of long-term care, replacing Stan Cho (Willowdale), who goes to the rebranded Ministry of Tourism, Culture and Gaming with responsibility for the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp.
In a sign that the son also rises at Queen's Park, Mike Harris Jr. (Kitchener-Conestoga), the scion of former premier Mike Harris, becomes minister of red tape reduction, replacing former Milton MPP Parm Gill, who quit the legislature in January to run for Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives.
Other newcomers to cabinet are: Nolan Quinn (Stormont-Dundas-South Glengarry) who becomes associate minister of forestry and gives cabinet the eastern Ontario representation is has lacked since Clark left; Stephen Crawford (Oakville) as associate minister of mines; and Sam Oosterhoff (Niagara West) as Lecce's associate minister in energy.
Some ministries are changing their names or being split, such as Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs.
Rob Flack (Elgin-London-Middlesex) moves from associate minister of housing to minister of the renamed Ministry of Farming, Agriculture and Agribusiness, while former agriculture minister Lisa Thompson helms the smaller adjacent Ministry of Rural Affairs.
Greg Rickford (Kenora-Rainy River) heads the now-standalone Ministry of Indigenous Affairs and First Nations Economic Reconciliation and continues as northern development minister.
Vijay Thanigasalam (Scarborough-Rouge Park) shifts to associate minister of housing from associate minister of transportation. Trevor Jones (Chatham-Kent-Leamington), a former OPP officer, becomes associate minister of emergency preparedness and response under the Treasury Board Secretariat.
Former CFL star Neil Lumsden (Hamilton East-Stoney Creek) becomes minister of sport. He loses responsibility for tourism and culture that he had enjoyed since being appointed to cabinet in 2022.
Earlier in the day, the governing Progressive Conservatives took opposition parties by surprise, rising a week early and extending the summer break by six weeks to Oct. 21.
"I know that there are some out there - and all parties will agree - that (think) everyone's sitting on the beach for the next few months. That's the furthest from the truth," Ford told the legislature.
MPPs had been expected to resume their legislative duties Sept. 9.
"They can't get out of here fast enough," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, noting the unusually long break after a session that saw several key policy reversals, including the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under investigation by the RCMP.
"They obviously don't want to spend any time here being held to account."
In terms of political strategy, the extended break also sets the table for Ford to prorogue the legislature in the fall, which would allow the Tories to deliver a throne speech outlining their agenda ahead of a possible election in spring 2025.
The unusually long recess comes two weeks after the premier dropped hints of an early election before the next scheduled vote in June 2026 despite a housing shortage, overcrowded hospital emergency rooms, temporary ER closures in some towns, a shortage of family doctors and the cost of living after years of high inflation.
But Ford is enjoying a healthy lead in the latest Abacus monthly tracking poll for the Star. His Tories were at 39 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for the NDP under Stiles and nine per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Crombie said Ford was in a rush to adjourn the legislature because of the heat he's taking over what the Liberals estimate is the $1-billion cost of putting beer, wine, cider and mixed drinks into convenience stores a year ahead of schedule.
"What kind of leader prioritizes this when you can't get a family doctor ... when your grocery bill is through the roof and you're scraping to make ends meet?" said Crombie, calling the deal a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
"They're utterly failing to address the housing affordability crisis, and so I can understand why this government doesn't want to come back," Green leader Schreiner told reporters.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie, Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Emergency medical care; Emergency preparedness; Legislatures; Scandals; Tourism

Document 131 of 500

Dividing the Middle East into villains and victims will only sow deeper divisions at home

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dividing-middle-east-into-villains-victims-will/docview/3065224080/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dividing+the+Middle+East+into+villains+and+victims+will+only+sow+deeper+divisions+at+home&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One day after Doug Ford squandered an opportunity to unite people against antisemitism - blaming immigrants for recent attacks - Toronto police offered more sobering evidence of how deeply divided we are as a community.
The premier offered no information to back up his loose talk about outsiders last week. The police, by contrast, provided a more substantive breakdown of rising hate crimes by category, and the numbers tell their own story:
"Anti-Jewish (36 per cent), anti-2SLGBTQI+ (18 per cent), anti-Black (13 per cent), and anti-Muslim (seven per cent)," according to the report.
"Antisemitic occurrences increased from 65 in 2022 to 135 occurrences in 2023. Of the 135 occurrences in 2023, 130 were anti-Jewish and five were anti-Israeli occurrences. Anti-Muslim, anti-Palestinian and anti-Arab occurrences increased from 12 in 2022 to 36 occurrences in 2023. Of the 36 occurrences in 2023, 27 were anti-Muslim, eight were anti-Palestinian occurrences and one was an anti-Arab occurrence."
Against the backdrop of war in the Middle East, what's the connection between global animus and domestic antisemitism? As the report notes, "There was a 32 per cent increase in the number of reported hate crimes after Oct. 7" - a reference to the Hamas massacre of more than 1,200 Israelis, which provoked the inevitable overreaction leading to more than 36,000 Gazans dead (including combatants).
When people are dehumanized in the Middle East, we Canadians - new and old alike - have a choice to make, as I argued in my previous column. We can choose to take sides and dehumanize, or we can opt to find common ground here at home, coming together in the pursuit of peace - not hate, not hate crimes, not antisemitism, not Islamophobia.
By vilifying Zionism, by comparing it to colonialism and trying to revive the canard that Zionism equals racism, people are playing with fire. Ultimately, "incendiary rhetoric leads to incendiary violence," as Canada's Special Envoy for Antisemitism Deborah Lyons puts it.
There are competing definitions and, sometimes, extreme expressions of Zionism. But at root it amounts to self-determination for the Jewish people and the existence of Israel, an idea broadly supported by the vast majority of Jews even while they join criticism of Israeli governments or their actions.
The question is how we get from Zionism as self-determination to Zionism as exclusion and racism. How is the Zionist impulse for self-determination, which begat the Jewish state of Israel in 1948 (after the United Nations proposed partition into two states in 1947), any different than the Islamic impulse for self-determination that begat the Islamic Republic of Pakistan (at the time of partition of the subcontinent, also in 1947)?
Would today's anti-Zionists accuse the Muslims of Delhi who migrated to Karachi amid partition of being so-called white colonizers, any more than the Mizrahi Jews (of direct Middle Eastern ancestry) who found sanctuary in Israel upon expulsion from Arab countries across North Africa and the Middle East? Would those who dream of a single state "from the river to the sea" similarly demand of Pakistanis that they give up their Islamic homeland and come together in a single state, under Hindu rule, at the very time that supposedly secular India is increasingly treating its own Muslims as second-class citizens?
Would we exhort Bangladesh - known as East Pakistan until a genocidal civil war in 1971 that left millions dead - to reunite with what remains of Pakistan? Would we demand Bosnia's Muslims return to Serbian rule while reconstituting the old Yugoslavia - a one-state solution for Croatians, Slovenians and others that dissolved in bloodshed?
These are difficult questions steeped in history, complicated by geography, contaminated by bigotry. Against the backdrop of bloodshed in the Middle East, Zionism remains an easy and irresistible target for those who retreat to simple solutions and slogans.
When I reported from Gaza as a foreign correspondent in the late 1990s, with the Oslo accords and the Camp David peace negotiations still offering hope, the rejectionists in the top leadership of Hamas always had a ready response to the problem of Zionism: Jews should go back where they came from, they told me.
That's no answer for the nine million citizens of Israel, who trace their roots to the Middle East and now have nowhere else to go. It's also a non-answer for Canadian Jews to be told they should stay off campus or "Go back to Poland" because of their background and beliefs.
Dividing the Middle East into villains and victims - as if one must be either pro-Palestinian or pro-Israeli, rather than both - will only sow deeper divisions.
That's what the premier of Ontario got wrong when he drove a wedge instead of drawing people together the other day. For the answer is not to dehumanize those with whom we differ, or who seem different from us.
That said, one wonders if all those who lashed out at Ford's ill-considered remarks also called out the antisemitism on the streets of this city and across the country. Some did condemn the latest attacks, and good for them, but many others seemed far more vexed by Ford's verbal blunder than by the synagogue and school shootings that are now being normalized as yesterday's news.
A politician in power, like a preacher at the pulpit, must always avoid talking about "us versus them," or musing about "us versus the other." For in a country like ours, the "other" is us - and it is incumbent on all of us to do no harm.
If peace were so simple, if a ceasefire were so easily agreed upon by both sides, perhaps all the sloganeering and scapegoating would have worked wonders by now. But if we cannot solve all the world's wars, let us resolve at least to keep the peace here at home.
CAPTION: Demonstrators gather in support of the Jewish community on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Dec. 4, 2023.

Subject: Antisemitism; Jewish people; Hate crimes; Zionism; Peace; Muslims; Dehumanization; Truces & cease fires

Location: Middle East; Pakistan; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Dividing the Middle East into villains and victims will only sow deeper divisions at home

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 7, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3065224080

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/dividing-middle-east-into-villains-victims-will/docview/3065224080/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2024 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.

Last updated: 2024-06-07

Database: Canadian Newsstream Collection

Document 132 of 500

Commuting chaos looms over city: TTC, union both showed an optimistic shift in tone ahead of deadline for strike action

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/commuting-chaos-looms-over-city/docview/3065224015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Commuting+chaos+looms+over+city%3A+TTC%2C+union+both+showed+an+optimistic+shift+in+tone+ahead+of+deadline+for+strike+action&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Millions of Torontonians went to bed Thursday night uncertain about how they'll get to work in the morning. As the clock ticked down toward the TTC workers' midnight strike deadline, there were signs a last-minute deal to avert a crippling shutdown of the transit system was in reach.
Late into the evening, there was an optimistic shift in tone both from the TTC and Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113, which represents almost 12,000 TTC workers, after days of the two sides painting different pictures of how negotiations were going.
The union issued a statement just before 9:30 p.m. Thursday saying "further progress" was made following an earlier statement that there was "some progress."
In its hourly updates, the TTC said in a message posted to X at 9 p.m. that talks are "productive, professional and progressing" following a post earlier that it believes a "fair and affordable" deal was "within reach." That was echoed in a post at 10 p.m.
The local is seeking better job security, protection from contracting out and higher wages for front-line workers who have faced an increase in physical and verbal assaults.
"The TTC has left us with no other choice, as we still need to prioritize a new, fair agreement for our transit workers," the union's 5:30 p.m. statement said.
Local 113's collective agreement expired at the end of March, and despite negotiations taking place since February, without a deal in place they were in a legal strike position at 12:01 a.m. Friday.
While heated negotiations between TTC management and the union continued at the downtown Sheraton Hotel throughout Thursday, a few blocks north at Queen's Park a senior government source told the Star that in the event of a strike, Premier Doug Ford wouldn't immediately step in with back-to-work legislation as the province has in the past.
While a shutdown of the transit system that normally provides 2.5 million daily trips would seriously disrupt life in the city and deliver a blow to its economy, the source, who spoke privately in order to discuss internal deliberations, said Ford would only consider using potentially controversial back-to-work legislation if Mayor Olivia Chow requested it.
That would make Chow, a lifelong NDPer with close ties to labour, responsible for any strikebreaking law, and put her in the tough political position of deciding whether to go against Local 113, a major supporter of her successful 2023 election campaign.
The source also said there is "no way" Ford's Progressive Conservatives would use the notwithstanding clause to end a work stoppage at the TTC.
Further complicating the prospect of a quick legislative resolution to any job action was the fact the legislature rose for its summer recess on Thursday, sending MPPs back to their ridings across the province.
The mayor's office didn't respond to questions about whether she would request a snap provincial intervention. But speaking to reporters Thursday morning, Chow said as long as the two sides were still at the table she was confident they could reach a deal.
"If they stop listening to each other, if they stop negotiating, stop talking to each other, that's a problem. But that's not the case," she said.
The mayor added that "the Canadian way is for us to hear each other, to listen to each other. We work things out. We negotiate. We're pretty good at it."
At a Thursday morning press conference at the Sheraton, Local 113 president Marvin Alfred declared talks were at an "impasse," with managers unwilling to address concerns and avoid a systemwide shutdown.
He said he didn't know why TTC officials were talking about progress at the bargaining table when they weren't moving on key sticking points, including worker job security with other municipal transit systems in the GTA picking up passengers in Toronto under regional fare integration.
"Anything that we get, anything that we achieve (in negotiations) has strings attached - it's not authentic," Alfred said.
"We're talking but we need something to be done," he continued, vowing that "if a deal is not in place by midnight we will not be operating the (transit) vehicles."
Union members include front-line bus, streetcar and subway operators, and if they walked off the job it would force a near complete shutdown of the transit system. The exception is Wheel-Trans paratransit service, which the union and management have said will continue to operate in the event of a job action.
If TTC workers did strike, it would be the first time since 2008. In 2011, the provincial government passed legislation prohibiting TTC workers from walking out, but the Ontario Superior Court overturned it last year as violating charter rights.
Although a deal for a new contract remained out of reach late Thursday, earlier in the day TTC chair Coun. Jamaal Myers announced the union had agreed to keep the transit system running for a few hours past the midnight strike deadline, even if Local 113 declares a job action.
Myers (Scarborough North) said the arrangement would allow riders using the system late Thursday to "get to their destination safely."
In a message posted on its website, the TTC said some service would continue until regular subway service ends, between 2 a.m. and 3 a.m.
"The TTC advises that if customers need to travel after midnight, they should be prepared to make alternative plans to complete their journey," the agency said.
It added that "We remain optimistic that with talks progressing well, we can reach a fair deal that avoids service interruptions and inconvenience for our customers."
In his statement, Myers echoed that optimism, saying he was confident the union and management could reach a deal that would keep the system moving Friday.
"The TTC is committed to securing a deal that avoids any job action that will be disruptive to the lives of millions of Torontonians," he said.
CAPTION: Marvin Alfred, president of Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113, whose collective agreement expired at the end of March. Talks have been ongoing since February.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Ben Spurr, David Rider, Rob Ferguson, Kristin Rushowy and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Subject: Customer services; Workers; Legislation; Strikes; Employment security

Business indexing term: Subject: Customer services Workers Strikes Employment security

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Amalgamated Transit Union; NAICS: 813930

Title: Commuting chaos looms over city:   TTC, union both showed an optimistic shift in tone ahead of deadline for strike action

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.6

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 7, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

ISSN: 0319 0781

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3065224015

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/commuting-chaos-looms-over-city/docview/3065224015/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 133 of 500

Dehumanizing each other gets us nowhere

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dehumanizing-each-other-gets-us-nowhere/docview/3065223363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Dehumanizing+each+other+gets+us+nowhere&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

That's no answer for the nine million citizens of Israel, who trace their roots to the Middle East and now have nowhere else to go. It's also a non-answer for Canadian Jews to be told they should stay off campus or "Go back to Poland" because of their background and beliefs.
Dividing the Middle East into villains and victims - as if one must be either pro-Palestinian or pro-Israeli, rather than both - will only sow deeper divisions.
That's what the premier of Ontario got wrong when he drove a wedge instead of drawing people together the other day. For the answer is not to dehumanize those with whom we differ, or who seem different from us.
That said, one wonders if all those who lashed out at Ford's ill-considered remarks also called out the antisemitism on the streets of this city and across the country. Some did condemn the latest attacks, and good for them, but many others seemed far more vexed by Ford's verbal blunder than by the synagogue and school shootings that are now being normalized as yesterday's news.
A politician in power, like a preacher at the pulpit, must always avoid talking about "us versus them," or musing about "us versus the other." For in a country like ours, the "other" is us - and it is incumbent on all of us to do no harm.
If peace were so simple, if a ceasefire were so easily agreed upon by both sides, perhaps all the sloganeering and scapegoating would have worked wonders by now. But if we cannot solve all the world's wars, let us resolve at least to keep the peace here at home.
CAPTION: Demonstrators gather on Parliament Hill last December in support of the Jewish community. When people are dehumanized in the Middle East, we Canadians can choose to take sides and dehumanize, or we can opt to find common ground in the pursuit of peace, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Dave Chan AFP via Getty Images file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+No+quick+fix+for+TTC+strike+says+Ford+government%2C+as+contract+talks+hit+%27impasse%27+ahead+of+deadline%2C+Behind+the+scenes%2C+Conservatives+are+furious+with+one+of+their+MPs+over+comments+on+podcast+that+defied+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+wishes%2C+Ontario+is+getting+ready+to+update+its+home+ec+courses+-+and+wants+your+input+on+what+needs+to+change%2C+There%27s+nothing+left+at+the+Bay+at+Yonge+and+Queen%2C+not+even+a+good+bra%3A+Mallick%2C+Jesus+is+their+saviour%2C+Trump+is+their+candidate.+Ex-president%27s+backers+say+he+shares+faith%2C+values%2C&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Irony of TTC's 'essential' claim
No quick fix for TTC strike says Ford government, as contract talks hit 'impasse' ahead of deadline, June 6
Toronto transit has a brand new definition of irony. There are huge posters along the subway walls featuring happy and grinning employees of the TTC with the slogan underneath stating "Be Essential." Seriously? The high courts have seen fit to deem the TTC as a "non-essential" service, thereby allowing a group of malcontents to disrupt both the personal and professional lives of thousands of citizens on a daily basis in the event of a strike. And yet apparently TTC workers believe they are "essential." That truly is irony.
Steve Veale, Toronto
Federal Tories furious at MP for speaking truth
Behind the scenes, Conservatives are furious with one of their MPs over comments on podcast that defied Pierre Poilievre's wishes, June 3
It comes as absolutely no surprise that the desperate-for-power Pierre Poilievre Conservatives are furious at an Alberta Conservative MP for simply telling the truth about his beliefs. Like Stephen Harper before him, Poilievre must realize that many of his MPs, although certainly welcome under the Conservative tent, do not share positions on all issues as the party moves further right. Poilievre's apparent desire to censor his MPs and his distrust of the free press are all too stark and alarming. Like Donald Trump, he panders to, and only pretends to be friends with, the common folk who must remember the saying "those who do not learn history are doomed to repeat it."
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Teach home skills at home
Ontario is getting ready to update its home ec courses - and wants your input on what needs to change, June 3
I've been reading about all the new initiatives the province is tacking onto our already overburdened curriculum, like financial literary testing and reintroducing home economics, and am asking myself what is happening.
Is it that the Ministry of Education simply needs to prove it has something to do? Is it that Premier Doug Ford and Minister Stephen Lecce just want to create further chaos in an already chaotic curriculum? Or is it partially that society is recognizing that the fundamental skills once passed on by family are simply not being taught at home? I go back 60 odd years, where my mother had me baking cakes in the kitchen, teaching me how to sew buttons on shirts, ironing my own clothes and even learning some rudimentary knitting skills, as well as having me save my pennies to help buy the bike I wanted. I was fully independent when I left home at 18, with no cellphone umbilical cord to save me. It's time we spend more time teaching children the basic life skills they require for when they leave home so schools don't have to waste time away from the academic skills.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Queen and Dundas demise
There's nothing left at the Bay at Yonge and Queen, not even a good bra: Mallick, June 3
Thank you to columnist Heather Mallick for shedding some light on the sad reality facing quality shopping options in the core of the city. The flagship Bay store was the last hope for variety and value of quality merchandise after Eaton's then Nordstrom's. The Bay store in Windsor parallels the scenario (I frequent both), with a broken escalator and a broken heating system making it so hot it drives customers away. I fear a sad future for any Bay life anywhere.
Liz Haugh, Windsor, Ont.
Trump ignores Christ's values
Jesus is their saviour, Trump is their candidate. Ex-president's backers say he shares faith, values, May 18
Donald Trump's faithful followers who also call themselves Christian - notably U.S. congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene - need to do a major moral rethink as to which fundamental values taught and practised by Jesus Christ they're willing to uphold or ignore, personally though especially publicly. Jesus fundamentally was about non-violence, genuine compassion and non-wealth. His teachings and practices epitomize so much of the primary component of socialism, such as do not hoard morbidly gratuitous wealth in the midst of poverty. He clearly would not tolerate the accumulation of tens of billions of dollars by individual people, especially while so many others go hungry and homeless.
While there are many Christians who have rejected Trump and his politics, regardless of his tempting conservative politics and pro-life professions, there nonetheless remains a vocal and politically active Christian element celebrating Trump conservatism.
Too many adherents of institutional Christianity - those Christians most resistant to Christ's fundamental teachings of non-violence, compassion and non-wealth - tend to insist upon creating their creator's nature in their own fallible and often angry, vengeful image; for example, proclaiming at publicized protests that "God hates" such-and-such group of people. Often being the most vocal, they make very bad examples of Christ's fundamental message, especially to the young and impressionable.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
Never forget heroes in fight against tyranny
Eighty years ago, thousands of Allied soldiers died on D-Day. Their deaths are commemorated today as the price paid for preserving democratic ideals against tyranny. How sad these days that anti-democratic movements globally are on the rise, the initiative of seemingly mature adults, "leaders", who should know better. We've seen the consequences of similarly misguided initiatives in the past. As Rudyard Kipling wrote about the dead in the First World War: "If any questions why we died, tell them, because our fathers lied."
Enrico Cumbo, Toronto
In the days leading up to June 6, the 80th anniversary of D-Day, I searched online to find a commemorative event to attend in Toronto. On the Veterans Affairs website I found events in Ottawa, Halifax, Moncton and Ottawa, but nothing in Toronto. So I called Veterans Affairs. They knew of nothing either. They also said no one had asked about a Toronto commemorative event. I called my MP's office. They barely knew the significance of June 6. My Toronto councillor's office didn't pick up the phone. While in the end there was a ceremony at Coronation Park, this information should have been more readily available.
Keith Jolly, Toronto

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre

Document 135 of 500

TTC, union reach a framework settlement

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-union-reach-framework-settlement/docview/3065222773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC%2C+union+reach+a+framework+settlement&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto narrowly avoided potential commuting chaos - for now - after the TTC and the union representing its front-line workers reached a framework settlement on a new contract.
"It is a tentative deal. We just need to cross the t's and dot the i's," said TTC CEO Rick Leary.
Weeks of negotiations between Amalgamated Transit Union (ATU) Local 113 - representing 12,000 bus and subway operators, collectors, maintenance workers and more - and TTC management had stalled over wages, job security and benefits in the lead-up to an optimistic shift in tone on Thursday night.
"We are pleased to announce that we have reached a framework settlement with the TTC that allows us to put the strike on hold," ATU Local 113 said in a statement late Thursday.
"We will continue to work through details and to arrive at a fair and reasonable contract that we can recommend to our members to ratify."
"Our demands have been reasonable. We asked the TTC for assurance on job security, for protections on contracting out our jobs, for improvement in benefits for active members and pensioners," the statement continued.
"Today we finally saw action on these critical issues."
In its hourly updates, both the TTC and ATU Local 113 were signalling at progress at the bargaining table after days of painting two different pictures of how talks were going.
The union previously said its members had been frustrated by what it called a lack of "integrity" on wages and cross-boundary contracting out.
The two sides had been trying to reach a deal since February, and the union had been without a contract since March 31.
Sources previously told the Star the union had rebuffed a four-year TTC offer that would have given workers annual raises of 3.75 per cent, 3.5 per cent, 3.5 per cent and three per cent. The sources, who were not authorized to disclose the figures, said the union was seeking a three-year deal with hikes of seven, six and five per cent.
The union's previous contract, from April 2021 to March 2024, saw a wage increase of about two per cent each year for three years.
On Thursday, just hours before the deadline, a source told the Star that Premier Doug Ford would not step in quickly with back-to-work legislation, had the union gone out on strike. It could have taken weeks to meet the legal threshold of damage to the economy and the community before back-to-work legislation could be contemplated. A court decision last year struck down a previous Liberal government's law declaring the TTC an essential service.
The source also said that Ford would not have considered back-to-work legislation unless it was requested by Mayor Olivia Chow, which would have put her in the tough political position of deciding whether to go against Local 113, a major supporter of her 2023 election campaign.
Earlier Thursday, Chow told reporters at an unrelated 11 a.m. press conference that there were still "many hours to go," adding that "the Canadian way is for us to hear each other, to listen to each other. We work things out. We negotiate. We're pretty good at it."
Despite having more work-from-home options now than before the pandemic, the increased volume of commuters caused by a transit shutdown was expected to worsen the city's already terrible congestion and gridlock.
The new contract comes as the transit agency is struggling to recover from plummeting ridership caused by the pandemic. As of May this year, overall weekday boardings stood at 81 per cent of their pre-COVID levels.
With an average of 2.5 million trips a day, the TTC is the largest public transit system in Canada and the third-largest in North America.
More to come. This is a developing story.
CAPTION: The Toronto Transit Commission's largest union, ATU Local 113, represents about 12,000 front-line employees including bus, streetcar and subway operators, collectors, maintenance workers and more.

Framework deal avoids traffic chaos: Details still being worked out, but buses, streetcars and subways continue to operate as usual

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/framework-deal-avoids-traffic-chaos/docview/3065221014/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Framework+deal+avoids+traffic+chaos%3A+Details+still+being+worked+out%2C+but+buses%2C+streetcars+and+subways+continue+to+operate+as+usual&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto narrowly avoided potential commuting chaos - for now - after the TTC and the union representing its front-line workers reached a framework settlement late Thursday on a new contract.
"It is a tentative deal. We just need to cross the t's and dot the i's," said TTC CEO Rick Leary.
Specific details of the agreement will likely not be disclosed until union members have voted on it. It must get a majority vote to be ratified. The last time TTC workers went on strike in 2008 was after a tentative deal was rejected by union members.
Weeks of negotiations between Amalgamated Transit Union (ATU) Local 113 - representing 12,000 bus and subway operators, collectors, maintenance workers and more - and TTC management had stalled over wages, job security and benefits in the lead-up to an optimistic shift in tone on Thursday night.
"We are pleased to announce that we have reached a framework settlement with the TTC that allows us to put the strike on hold," ATU Local 113 said in a statement late Thursday.
"We will continue to work through details and to arrive at a fair and reasonable contract that we can recommend to our members to ratify."
"Our demands have been reasonable. We asked the TTC for assurance on job security, for protections on contracting out our jobs, for improvement in benefits for active members and pensioners," the statement continued.
"Today we finally saw action on these critical issues."
In its hourly updates, both the TTC and ATU Local 113 were signalling at progress at the bargaining table after days of painting two different pictures of how talks were going.
The union previously said its members had been frustrated by what it called a lack of "integrity" on wages and cross-boundary contracting out.
The two sides had been trying to reach a deal since February, and the union had been without a contract since March 31.
Sources previously told the Star the union had rebuffed a four-year TTC offer that would have given workers annual raises of 3.75 per cent, 3.5 per cent, 3.5 per cent and three per cent. The sources, who were not authorized to disclose the figures, said the union was seeking a three-year deal with hikes of seven, six and five per cent.
The union's previous contract, from April 2021 to March 2024, saw a wage increase of about two per cent each year for three years.
On Thursday, just hours before the deadline, a source told the Star that Premier Doug Ford would not step in quickly with back-to-work legislation, had the union gone out on strike. It could have taken weeks to meet the legal threshold of damage to the economy and the community before back-to-work legislation could be contemplated. A court decision last year struck down a previous Liberal government's law declaring the TTC an essential service.
The source also said that Ford would not have considered back-to-work legislation unless it was requested by Mayor Olivia Chow, which would have put her in the tough political position of deciding whether to go against Local 113, a major supporter of her 2023 election campaign. Earlier Thursday, Chow told reporters at an unrelated 11 a.m. press conference that there were still "many hours to go," adding that "the Canadian way is for us to hear each other, to listen to each other. We work things out. We negotiate. We're pretty good at it."
Despite having more work-from-home options now than before the pandemic, the increased volume of commuters caused by a transit shutdown was expected to worsen the city's already terrible congestion and gridlock.
The new contract comes as the transit agency is struggling to recover from plummeting ridership caused by the pandemic. As of May this year, overall weekday boardings stood at 81 per cent of their pre-COVID levels.
With an average of 2.5 million trips a day, the TTC is the largest public transit system in Canada and the third-largest in North America.
CAPTION: Passengers ride the Queen's Quay streetcar. Framework deal worked out at the last minute after long negotiations.Steve Russell Toronto Star

Document 137 of 500

Transit is broken like everything else in this city. Of course TTC workers might strike

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/transit-is-broken-like-everything-else-this-city/docview/3065467285/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Transit+is+broken+like+everything+else+in+this+city.+Of+course+TTC+workers+might+strike&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not clear if Toronto will be shut down Friday, or for how long. Depending on negotiations, the entire city could be economically halted, socially isolated and thrown into chaos because of a strike by 12,000 of the TTC's 16,000 workers.
It's more of the usual strangulation that afflicts this city. But that's the TTC in 2024. Can't live with it, can't live without it.
It would be helpful to know what Amalgamated Transit Union 113 wanted, but it wouldn't say how much money it was asking for - it sees publicity as negotiation via media, whatever that is - which is infuriating.
Radio silence is a classic Doug Ford tactic so it's odd to see it adopted by a union that suspects the percentages might not be popular with the public. Although thanks to media decline, most people don't know what TTC workers earn right now anyway.
It's not true that TTC riders aren't sympathetic. Workers need raises. Everyone does. The union seeks improvements in job security, benefits and workplace safety, precisely what riders are seeking as well as they use a dirty, ill-maintained, badly run, slowed-down and dangerous transit system in Canada's largest city.
Public services are in decline across the country. What a grievous thing it is to see quality of life sliding so fast and so far. On Thursday, a major Calgary water main burst, leaving the city without enough water for any purpose, including drinking the stuff.
The Alberta government sent out a misspelled alert saying, "All residents and businesses must conserve water. Do not shower or bath [sic]. Do not wash dishes, or run appliances that use water." But it hadn't co-ordinated with the City of Calgary, which simply advised people to cut back.
Here's the fallout. Canadians will begin to believe that no one is in charge of the basics, like transit, water, garbage collection, park maintenance and policing of matters like auto theft and traffic control. Daily life becomes an exercise in frustration, something many people are less able to cope with emotionally in this strange era.
This month at my house saw cancelled appointments, postponed orders and deliveries, an angry contracted-out garbage collector doing public damage because he resents his job and the strange sight of TTC workers enthusiastically cleaning escalator banks at my station. (The TTC doesn't clean and polish. It was pure pre-strike theatre.)
I use Beck Taxi's terrific app out of solidarity because Uber et al has devastated the taxi industry while treating gig drivers and customers like squishmallows. But the problem is that even if you call a cab to your house during a strike, you have no guarantee you'll be able to get a cab home.
You can drive, but Toronto traffic was choked up even before a possible strike. There will be delays at the construction sites that clogged traffic in the first place because nobody can get to work. All drivers, even car poolers, are asking for trouble.
So Torontonians won't travel, inflicting further distress on small businesses hungry for customers. This, as usual, will be the hottest summer in human history so it seems as good a time as any to make everything worse.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow is a practical person, exactly the kind of grounded mayor we need. She understands that the Gardiner can't be a thin gravel road for three years. It's intolerable.
Searching for a metaphor for Toronto traffic, I have become a fan of Instagram unclogging videos, in which drains are magically cleared by public-spirited citizens armed with crowbars and giant forks.
We have to make sure Chow has the tools and money required to keep Toronto viable. Success will be costly but infinitely worth it.
CAPTION: A crippling TTC strike makes sense, writes Heather Mallick. "This, as usual, will be the hottest summer in human history so it seems as good a time as any to make everything worse."

Subject: Taxicabs

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

Title: Transit is broken like everything else in this city. Of course TTC workers might strike

Doug Ford's Tories end legislative session early and extend break till October

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-end-legislative-session-early/docview/3065467249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+end+legislative+session+early+and+extend+break+till+October&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Summer is arriving a week early for MPPs - and ending six weeks later - after Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives ended the legislative session Thursday and extended the break.
"I know that there are some out there - and all parties will agree - that (think) everyone's sitting on the beach for the next few months. That's the furthest from the truth," Ford told the legislature.
"I know each and every one of you will be out in your ridings with your constituents and visiting events within your ridings, answering calls," said the premier, who is pondering a cabinet shuffle.
MPPs, who had been expected to resume their legislative duties Sept. 9, will now return to Queen's Park on Oct. 21.
"They can't get out of here fast enough," said New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, noting the unusually long break after a session that saw several key policy reversals, including the $8.28 billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under investigation by the RCMP.
"They obviously don't want to spend any time here being held to account."
In terms of political strategy, the extended break also sets the table for Ford to prorogue the legislature in the fall, which would allow the Tories to deliver a throne speech outlining their agenda ahead of a possible election in spring 2025.
The unusually long recess comes two weeks after the premier dropped hints of an early election before the next scheduled vote in June 2026 despite a housing shortage, overcrowded hospital emergency rooms, temporary ER closures in some towns, a shortage of family doctors and the cost of living after years of high inflation.
But Ford is enjoying a healthy lead in the latest Abacus monthly tracking poll for the Star. His Tories were at 39 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for the NDP under Stiles and nine per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
Crombie said Ford was in a rush to adjourn the legislature because of the heat he's taking over what the Liberals estimate is the $1-billion cost of putting beer, wine, cider and mixed drinks into convenience stores a year ahead of schedule.
"What kind of leader prioritizes this when you can't get a family doctor ... when your grocery bill is through the roof and you're scraping to make ends meet?" said Crombie, calling the deal a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
"They're utterly failing to address the housing affordability crisis, and so I can understand why this government doesn't want to come back," Green Leader Mike Schreiner told reporters.
The early rising of the house means a universally popular bill to protect children from drowning remains stalled.
Proposed by Progressive Conservative MPP Goldie Ghamari, it would require life jackets for children 12 and under while out on the water, making Ontario the first province to do so.
The bill is named Joshua's Law (Lifejackets for Life), in honour of a young boy from her Ottawa-area riding of Carleton who died in a boating accident.
"It's mandatory to have life jackets for everyone on a boat, but it's not mandatory to wear them," Ghamari said, adding she was initially "caught off guard because I didn't realize they weren't mandatory for children. I think it's just something that everyone assumed."
She said about 80 per cent of people who died in boating accidents in Canada between 2008 and 2017 weren't wearing a personal flotation device, and another five per cent were wearing one improperly.
The bill has the support of all parties, and recently passed second reading unanimously.
It is already the law in a number of U.S. states, as well as in Calgary after a bylaw there mandated life jackets for all ages when in a boat or other watercraft. Violators face a mandatory court appearance and a fine of up to $500.
Ghamari's bill would levy $200 fines against parents or guardians, but would exempt children who are competing in activities such as supervised rowing races, or when in an enclosed cabin on board.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks at a convenience store in Toronto on Dec. 14, 2023.

Police identify second man who died after shooting outside Etobicoke high school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/police-identify-second-man-who-died-after/docview/3065467229/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+identify+second+man+who+died+after+shooting+outside+Etobicoke+high+school&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Police have identified the second man to die after a shooting in Etobicoke on Sunday as Seymour Gibbs, who died in hospital where he was being treated for his injuries. They could not confirm if Gibbs, 46, had died as a result of a homicide related to his injuries until an autopsy is completed.
He is the second man to have died after the shooting behind an Etobicoke high school which left three others injured. The loss of the two men killed in the shooting has brought "unimaginable grief," the families say, along with tainting a once sacred community space.
"When is this thing going to stop? We need help," said Sophia Dunn, Gibbs' mother-in-law, when she spoke to the Star on Monday prior to his death. "It's hard to see (this violence), this can't go on ... it's not good for the community, it's very terrifying and scary."
A group of men were playing dominoes in a parking lot behind North Albion Collegiate Institute following a soccer game just before 11 p.m. on Sunday when a dark pickup truck pulled up behind the school and two people emerged, police say. They fired a volley of more than 50 bullets at the gathered men, killing Delroy "George" Parkes, 61, and seriously injuring Gibbs and three other men who police say are in their 40s to 60s.
Supt. Ron Taverner said that police have impounded a truck similar to one witnesses saw the suspects flee in after the shooting, but that investigators have yet to confirm if it is the same vehicle.
Taverner said one of the injured victims remains in the hospital.
"It may seem insignificant, but any information at all can prove to be very important in situations like this," he said, urging people to come forward with any tips.
"We are basically working around the clock to find the perpetrators here."
On Thursday afternoon, the police tape, evidence markers and other items left behind the night of the shooting had been cleared away from behind North Albion. A bouquet of flowers, wilting from the harsh sun, lay on the ground near where the group of men sat playing dominos moments before the shooting took place.
Police have maintained a presence in the neighbourhood, with two vehicles parked behind the school and others driving down the streets nearby. Taverner said this will continue for at least the next week.
"I want justice, our world was taken from us for no reason," said Heather Parkes, the wife of Parkes in comments to media on Tuesday.
Known in the neighbourhood as "Uncle George," Parkes was like family to those who knew him, his daughter Jaidyn Parkes said.
"If you needed help, he was there for you. If it was the last dollar in his pocket, he would give it to you," she said, adding that hundreds of people who knew her father have reached out since his death to share how much he meant to them.
"He was a pillar for anybody and everybody."
Parkes' family is fundraising to help pay for his funeral expenses, and have so far raised more than $13,000.
Police do not believe that the victims and suspects are known to each other. They are asking anyone in the area to reach out if they saw a dark-coloured pickup truck on Sunday at approximately 10:50 p.m.
Premier Doug Ford posted on X Wednesday to say that he is "totally disgusted" by the shootings and deaths in Rexdale, which is in his riding of Etobicoke North, and that he plans to invest "hundreds of millions of dollars to get more boots on the ground."
"My heart breaks for the victims and their loved ones," he wrote.
CAPTION: Two people are dead and three others were injured in a shooting in Etobicoke late Sunday, Toronto police say. The shooting happened just before 11 p.m. in the rear parking lot area of North Albion Collegiate Institute.Jaidyn Parkes with her father Delroy "George" Parkes, 61, who was killed in a shooting outside an Etobicoke high school on Sunday.Heather Parkes, the wife of Delroy "George" Parkes, speaks to media with her daughter Jaidyn Parkes, left, and Amy Jones, outside Toronto Police Service 23 Division in Toronto on Tuesday.
