A sad convocation for graduating U of T students

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/sad-convocation-graduating-u-t-students/docview/3065003087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+sad+convocation+for+graduating+U+of+T+students&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Think of the students
Protesters, pomp and circumstance: Here's how convocation at U of T began this year, June 4
What a sad picture on page A1. While the demonstrators want the rest of us to sympathize with the plight of the Palestinians in Gaza, and ask us to imagine ourselves in their place, they steadfastly refuse to do the same for anyone else. In refusing to decamp before the convocation ceremonies, they are blatantly ignoring the wishes of the administration and, much worse, the feelings of the graduates. After slaving through years of gruelling classes, sleep-stealing preparation for exams and amassing huge debt loads, they finally are getting their degrees. Imagine, actually no need to imagine - the pictures are clear, how lovely the graduation pictures will be with the encampment in their photos. For shame, demonstrators. Not a great way to get people onside. This poor graduating class already got cheated of part of their high school experience by COVID. Now they are being robbed of a proper convocation experience.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge
Lest we never forget
'Soldiers of remembrance' retrace the D-Day path in France. But how long will the memory live as veterans fade away? June 3
Today marks the 80th anniversary of the D-Day invasion. That morning, thousands of young soldiers from Canada, Great Britain, America and other Allied nations landed along the coast of Normandy on beaches code named Juno, Gold, Sword, Omaha and Utah. They landed, in many cases under heavy enemy fire, but did not waver. Their goal was to defeat Nazism and fascism and liberate Europe from Adolf Hitler and his henchmen. Many fell that day. Many more were wounded, but they believed what they were doing was just and right for a civilized world. "They fight to end conquest," U.S. president Franklin D. Roosevelt said at the time of the invasion. "They fight to liberate."
Much has changed in the past 80 years on our planet, but one thing still stands out. We owe the utmost gratitude and respect to that generation who on the morning of June 6 landed on the beaches at Normandy knowing that they might not survive the day. So, all of us should pause today for a few moments and thank those young soldiers, many who made the ultimate sacrifice that day so that our world would be a much better place to live for future generations. Lest we forget.
Mike LoSchiavo, Stoney Creek, Ont.
Questions about the Staples deal
Financial watchdog launches review of Ford government's ServiceOntario deal with Staples, June 3
Good for Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman for voicing her well-founded concerns over the financial arrangements to relocate nine ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores. On the surface, it sounds like a "win/win" for Ontarians. I question if the sole-sourced contract was inked clandestinely. If it's such a great deal for us, wouldn't a responsible government announce the plan and look for interested businesses like, say, Canadian Tire to bid on the contract? Extended hours for Ontario services sounds terrific, but won't that require additional staff, benefits and overtime funded by Ontario taxpayers? I look forward to reading the report by the Financial Accountability Office which will hopefully reveal the true cost to us.
Michelle Simson, Hastings, Ont.
Why not put Service Ontario counters in Beer Store outlets? The Beer Store likely needs a business boost now, with more beer offered elsewhere, and has many locations. Plus, they are open late and on weekends. Both are uniquely Ontario and have many visitors. Serious suggestion.
Paula Strasberg, North York
Standardized testing is necessary
Here's the ugly truth about Doug Ford's plan for more standardized testing in schools, June 5
Like many educators, a recent Star letter writer denigrates standardized testing, using the argument that all children learn differently. That is manifestly true, but misses why we need to have testing. Regardless of how children learn, there are certain things that we expect them to master at each grade level. Literacy and mathematics are basic skills, without which other skills are useless. So we administer a standardized test to determine individual scores, which tell how well the individual student has mastered the skills expected for his grade level or age, and determine aggregate scores across classes, schools, and school boards to indicate how well the school is imparting these necessary skills. The first of these determines whether a child needs some remedial education. This is business as usual.
Roger Pett, Pickering
Grassy Narrows news is not new
Frustrated at lack of progress, Grassy Narrows sues governments for failing to clean up mercury pollution, June 4
In the early 1970s I was attending the University of Guelph in the Fisheries and Wildlife Biology program. The complaints of the devastating health effects due to the mercury poisoning of the Ojibwe people of the Grassy Narrows reserve were known at least by some. Also known were the studies linking mercury poisoning of the people in Minamata, Japan (thus, it was referred to as "Minamata disease"). Back in the waters at Grassy Narrows, the response of the federal and provincial governments of the time was denial, as far as I was aware, and the response of the medical profession as reported in a recent article by journalist Brandi Morin was dismissive and insulting to the Ojibwe people who complained. The Star article by Morgan Sevareid-Bocknek once again puts this issue on the front page. As the article details it has been repeatedly in the media for years, with so little done. What I remember from the 70s after the poisoning was proved, was the belief that the river system would flush itself and all would be good if the natural systems were allowed to heal themselves. Now 50+ years later, the people of Grassy Narrows continue their struggle for clean drinking water, to be able to eat the fish and most importantly, control of what happens on their land. I wish them success in their legal proceedings.
David Maltby, Toronto

Document 141 of 500

Commuting chaos looms as TTC talks show no progress ahead of midnight strike deadline

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/commuting-chaos-looms-as-ttc-talks-show-no/docview/3065002730/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Commuting+chaos+looms+as+TTC+talks+show+no+progress+ahead+of+midnight+strike+deadline&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Millions of Torontonians were uncertain Thursday evening how they would make it to work in the coming days, as the TTC's largest union said it was set to walk off the job one minute after midnight and Premier Doug Ford's government signalled it wouldn't be quick to legislate them back to work.
With only hours to go until the strike deadline, Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113, which represents almost 12,000 TTC workers, issued a statement saying that as of 5 p.m. Thursday, there had been "no progress on our negotiations."
The local said it was still seeking better job security, protection from contracting out and higher wages for front-line workers who have faced an increase in physical and verbal assaults.
"The TTC has left us with no other choice, as we still need to prioritize a new, fair agreement for our transit workers. ATU Local 113 will be moving forward with strike action tomorrow at 12:01 am.," the statement said.
The union did not say that it had broken off talks, however, and the TTC said in a message posted to X just before 6 p.m. that negotiations continued.
Local 113's collective agreement expired at the end of March, and despite negotiations taking place since February, without a deal in place they were in a legal strike position at 12:01 a.m. Friday.
While heated negotiations between TTC management and the union continued at the downtown Sheraton Hotel throughout Thursday, a few blocks north at Queen's Park a senior government source told the Star that in the event of a strike, the premier wouldn't immediately step in with back-to-work legislation as the province has in the past.
While a shutdown of the transit system that normally provides 2.5 million daily trips would seriously disrupt life in the city and deliver a blow to its economy, the source, who spoke privately in order to discuss internal deliberations, said Ford would only consider using potentially controversial back-to-work legislation if Mayor Olivia Chow requested it.
That would make Chow, a lifelong NDPer with close ties to labour, responsible for any strikebreaking law, and put her in the tough political position of deciding whether to go against Local 113, a major supporter of her successful 2023 election campaign.
The source also said there is "no way" Ford's Progressive Conservatives would use the notwithstanding clause to end a work stoppage at the TTC.
Further complicating the prospect of a quick legislative resolution to any job action was the fact the legislature rose for its summer recess on Thursday, sending MPPs back to their ridings across the province.
The mayor's office didn't respond to questions about whether she would request a snap provincial intervention. But speaking to reporters Thursday morning, Chow said as long as the two sides were still at the table she was confident they could reach a deal.
"If they stop listening to each other, if they stop negotiating, stop talking to each other, that's a problem. But that's not the case," she said.
The mayor added that "the Canadian way is for us to hear each other, to listen to each other. We work things out. We negotiate. We're pretty good at it."
At a Thursday morning press conference at the Sheraton, Local 113 president Marvin Alfred declared talks were at an "impasse," with managers unwilling to address concerns and avoid a systemwide shutdown.
He said he didn't know why TTC officials were talking about progress at the bargaining table when they weren't moving on key sticking points, including worker job security with other municipal transit systems in the GTA picking up passengers in Toronto under regional fare integration.
"Anything that we get, anything that we achieve (in negotiations) has strings attached - it's not authentic," Alfred said.
"We're talking but we need something to be done," he continued, vowing that "if a deal is not in place by midnight we will not be operating the (transit) vehicles."
Union members include front-line bus, streetcar and subway operators, and if they walked off the job it would force a near complete shutdown of the transit system. The exception is Wheel-Trans paratransit service, which the union and management have said will continue to operate in the event of a job action.
If TTC workers did strike, it would be the first time since 2008. In 2011, the provincial government passed legislation prohibiting TTC workers from walking out, but the Ontario Superior Court overturned it last year as violating Charter rights.
Although a deal for a new contract remained out of reach late Thursday, earlier in the day TTC chair Coun. Jamaal Myers announced the union had agreed to keep the transit system running for a few hours past the midnight strike deadline, even if Local 113 declares a job action.
Myers (Scarborough North) said the arrangement would allow riders using the system late Thursday to "get to their destination safely."
In a message posted on its website, the TTC said some service would continue until regular subway service ends, between 2 a.m. and 3 a.m.
"The TTC advises that if customers need to travel after midnight, they should be prepared to make alternative plans to complete their journey," the agency said.
It added that "We remain optimistic that with talks progressing well, we can reach a fair deal that avoids service interruptions and inconvenience for our customers."
In his statement, Myers echoed that optimism, saying he was confident the union and management could reach a deal that would keep the system moving Friday.
"The TTC is committed to securing a deal that avoids any job action that will be disruptive to the lives of millions of Torontonians," he said.
CAPTION: Marvin Alfred, president of Amalgamated Transit Union (ATU) Local 113, said at a Thursday press conference that the TTC was not moving on key sticking points in contract talks as the 12:01 a.m. Friday strike deadline neared.

Subject: Agreements; Workers; Contract negotiations; Employment security

Document 142 of 500

Ontarians deserve justice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarians-deserve-justice/docview/3064875252/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+deserve+justice&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here is a fun stat. There is about as much chance of getting a summary hearing at Ontario's human rights court as there is of the Leafs winning the Stanley Cup each year. Which is to say, minuscule. A 1/32 chance, to be precise.
Not so fun is the alarm bells being sounded by Tribunal Watch in its newest report on the state of the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario, which says an "unprecedented 96 per cent" of all final HRTO decisions in 2023-24 were dismissals of applications for "jurisdictional or procedural" reasons, in which members of the public looking for justice did not have an opportunity to make oral submissions or attempt mediation.
This abysmal grading for the quasi-judicial body, taken together with the dilution of anti-racism directorate duties and a new bill impacting mental-health and anti-racism policies in universities, exposes how the province is quashing progress on the intrinsic human right to live without discrimination in Ontario.
In 2018, two years after the creation of the anti-racism directorate - which was itself 10 years in the making - Doug Ford came into power and swiftly made cuts to its programs and disbanded its advisory subcommittees on anti-Black racism, anti-Indigenous racism, antisemitism and Islamophobia.
At the same time, his government issued an ultimatum to universities asking them to come up with policies to protect free speech or else face funding cuts. The faux panic over "campus free speech" was imported as a wedge issue from the U.S. where students at the time were shutting down speeches on campuses by far-right provocateurs such as Milo Yiannopoulos.
In 2023, at least one advocacy group raised concerns about anti-racism strategies disappearing from the Ministry of Education website. Earlier this year the ministry forced the Toronto school board to take down an anti-racism teaching resource that called white supremacy a "structural reality" and called for the decolonization of education.
This past February, a new bill sought to again bring university practices under the Ford government's thumb. Bill 166, called the Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, is touted as a safety and support mechanism for post-secondary students. Critics say it undermines the independence of Ontario's universities by allowing the government to dictate policies on anti-racism and mental health, and that it creates more red tape rather than offering additional funding.
Last week, the Colleges and Universities minister of the same free-speech championing Ford government told media she wanted to see an encampment of students at the University of Toronto "cleaned up and dealt with."
The students are exercising their right to free expression in support of Palestinian lives. The issue of free speech, it seems, is rarely about genuine threats to free expression but about political expediency.
Within this context, it may not be surprising that backlogs at the tribunal began growing after this government came into power in 2018, but it is no less distressing. It's a backlog that has, the tribunal watchdog found, doubled in the past six years even though fewer people are now filing complaints.
Why would they file any, when they know their application is overwhelmingly likely to be dismissed?
Applicants whose cases were dismissed for "jurisdictional or procedural" reasons abandoned their applications in almost 80 per cent of cases. When the watchdog reviewed those who had abandoned complaints in 2024, it found an "overwhelming majority" were filed three or more years prior by applicants who were not represented by lawyers. Tribunal Watch is a non-partisan organization of law professors, former tribunal members, judges and civil servants.
"What this record proves," the report says, is that if the human rights tribunal keeps failing to move applications along, "it can count on a significant number of applicants giving up."
The blow will be felt most keenly among disabled people; a majority - 55 per cent - of the complaints are on grounds of disability, Tribunals Ontario data shows. Some 60 per cent of all complaints are related to employment.
The HRTO took a final decision based on merit in 40 cases in 2023-24, up from 33 decisions the year before, but down from the 110 decisions it used to take prior to 2019 annually. The tribunal finally has a full complement of adjudicators; however, the watchdog has said that "most of them were appointed without the background necessary to effectively adjudicate or mediate human rights cases."
Where in the past, a seemingly weak application would be scheduled for a summary hearing or sent to mediation, the tribunal now requires applicants who may not have lawyers to file additional written submissions addressing the legal test for discrimination, the report says. It imposed this new filing deadline after years of inactivity and many applicants didn't, or couldn't, meet it. "The HRTO has created new barriers to an accessible process."
Legislation that led to the creation of this tribunal once gave Ontarians who were discriminated against the chance to be heard and the hope they would find justice. They're now being obstructed by the tribunal itself.
One step forward on social justice, it seems, is invariably followed by two steps back.
CAPTION: Jill Dunlop, Ontario Minister of Colleges and Universities, speaks at a press conference in February. Last week, Dunlop told media she wanted to see a pro-Palestine encampment at the University of Toronto "cleaned up and dealt with." The issue of free speech, it seems, is rarely about genuine threats to free expression, but about political expediency, Shree Paradkar writes.Arlyn McAdorey THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Shree Paradkar Toronto Star OPINION

Document 143 of 500

Union, TTC differ on status of strike talks: With less than a day until the deadline, wages and job security still dominate the bargaining table

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/union-ttc-differ-on-status-strike-talks/docview/3064875193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Union%2C+TTC+differ+on+status+of+strike+talks%3A+With+less+than+a+day+until+the+deadline%2C+wages+and+job+security+still+dominate+the+bargaining+table&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the clock ticks down to Toronto's first potential transit strike in more than a decade, the chair of the TTC and the head of its largest union are painting different pictures of how close the two sides are to a deal.
Speaking to reporters on Wednesday with less than 36 hours until the strike deadline, TTC chair Coun. Jamaal Myers said talks between management and Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113 were "headed in the right direction."
"We are in a good position. Both parties remain bargaining. They both continue to bargain in good faith. And we are confident that there is a path to a deal," said Myers (Scarborough North).
As evidence, Myers cited the agreement between the union and TTC, announced Monday, to keep the Wheel-Trans service operational in the event of job action.
But hours before Myers' remarks, Local 113 president Marvin Alfred described a strike as all but certain.
"I'm not optimistic. We're going on strike on Friday. What it takes is a deal, and we don't have a deal. And we're not close enough to have any sort of optimism right now," he told Global News.
Local 113's collective agreement expired at the end of March, and its roughly 12,000 members will be in a legal strike position at 12:01 a.m. on Friday.
TTC spokesperson Stuart Green confirmed that if they walk off the job, there's nothing the agency can do to prevent a shutdown of the system, which normally provides 2.5 million trips each weekday.
"Our contingency is only around having staff available at stations to advise customers if there's no service," he said.
Green said the agency's advice to transit users is "to plan ahead."
"If people absolutely rely on service and can't work from home, we advise looking at alternatives like carpooling, cycling or other shared services. GO transit and regional services will be operating too," he said.
Sources who spoke to the Star Wednesday, and were granted anonymity to discuss sensitive negotiations, gave differing accounts of how the negotiations are proceeding.
Some said the two sides were still far apart, while others were confident a work stoppage could be averted.
One source said progress was made Wednesday on less costly items like benefits and working conditions, but agreements on bigger ticket issues like wage increases would likely go down to the wire.
"We're getting closer," the source said, predicting talks would proceed into Wednesday night.
Sources previously told the Star Local 113 was seeking a three-year deal with annual wage increases of between five and seven per cent, while the TTC had offered a four-year contract with yearly raises between three and 3.75 per cent.
Also at issue are job security and contracting out of maintenance jobs.
One of the major sticking points is the union's demand for guarantees around municipal transit agencies from outside Toronto providing local service within the city's borders, which Local 113 argues would amount to contracting out TTC service.
"We're fighting off having other agencies in the 905 region delivering Toronto transit," Alfred said Wednesday.
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford would not tip his hand on any plans to pass back-to-work legislation in the event of a TTC strike but suggested he is not pleased that the courts overruled the previous provincial government's move to declare the TTC an essential service, as urged by his late brother Rob Ford, the former mayor.
The 2011 legislation barred the TTC from striking. Last year, the Ontario Superior Court ruled it unconstitutional, opening the door to the first walkout at the transit agency since 2008.
"I respect bargaining ... they should bargain in good faith ... and let's just avoid it at all costs," Ford said, referring to a possible work stoppage.
"I love our TTC drivers and everyone who works at TTC but it's an essential service, it can shut down the economy in a big way."
Any attempt to legislate TTC employees back to work could be hampered by the provincial government's announcement Wednesday that the legislature will rise for the summer on Thursday. It would have to be recalled for an emergency session to pass a back-to-work law.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, whose party is the official Opposition and has power to slow the passage of any strike-busting law, on Tuesday replied "absolutely not" when asked if her party would support back-to-work legislation.
She says Ford could help avoid a strike by increasing provincial funding for public transit.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 144 of 500

Ford lost the plot on antisemitic shootings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-lost-plot-on-antisemitic-shootings/docview/3064875124/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+lost+the+plot+on+antisemitic+shootings&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford surely missed the mark when he blamed immigrants for the latest wave of antisemitic shootings.
But he's not the only one guilty of going off target.
A great many Canadians - new and old alike - are also missing the bigger lesson: How to bring people together while calling out hate.
Amid the ongoing controversy over Ford's faux pas, we're losing our focus over the original sins - the shootings and attacks outside Jewish schools and synagogues in Toronto, Montreal and Vancouver within a matter of weeks.
To be sure, the premier's perennial missteps are fair game. But the daily news cycle has defaulted to more of the same Ford-bashing (I too am guilty), while devoting only fleeting coverage to recurring Jew hatred (I'm guilty again).
Is anyone still talking about the bullets fired outside the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School in Toronto last month? Bullets fired at the Belz Yeshiva Ketana school in a Montreal synagogue? The firebombing of a Vancouver synagogue last week? Vandalism at the Anshei Minsk synagogue in Toronto's Kensington Market this week?
Sharing a podium with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who forcefully condemned the latest antisemitic outburst in Toronto, Ford went way off script: "You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world," he hectored.
By scapegoating the "other," the premier missed an opportunity to address the "us" - all Ontarians and all Canadians - with a teachable moment on tolerance. Let's not lose the moment.
Blaming immigrants for importing antisemitism and hatred here isn't just wrong-headed in spirit. It's wrong in fact.
For there is a long history of homegrown Jew hatred in this country. The latest bigotry by old stock Canadians and newcomers is little different.
Antisemitism comes in every skin colour and from every country of origin - including this country. The difference is that it now goes by another name.
A few decades ago in Ontario you could find legal papers that excluded land sales or rentals to anyone of "the Jewish, Hebrew, Semitic, Negro or coloured race or blood." Jews were excluded from universities and excoriated as "Hebes" or "kikes."
Today, Zionists or "Zios" are the preferred proxies for denigrating Jews, even calling for their exclusion ("No Zios on campus," as the protest slogan goes).
The old phrase "Zionism is racism," once discredited, has been reincarnated as "Zionism is colonialism" - which inevitably diminishes and denigrates most Jews. Why?
While Zionism takes many expressions, most Jews believe in its most common definition - self-determination for Jews (who have their own claims to indigenity in Israel). If you insult or isolate Zionists, treating them as adversaries, you are inevitably singling out most Jews for opprobrium and discrimination - your friends, neighbours, co-workers.
If you call yourself anti-Zionist (as opposed to merely non-Zionist) you are effectively opposed to Israel's raison d'être, which raises the question: What is to become of the Jewish state and its nine million citizens (of whom about one in five within its borders are Palestinian citizens who can vote - not to be confused with five million Palestinians proper living under Israeli occupation or control in the West Bank or Gaza).
To be sure, critics of Israel - of which I am one - are not necessarily anti-Israeli (or anti-Jewish). But a good many are so adamantly opposed to the existence of the state of Israel, for reasons of history or bigotry, that you have to ask:
Where would those millions of Jews go? Back to Poland, as some like to taunt? Here to Canada, where they feel increasingly besieged? Stay where they are in a single state where "Palestine shall be free, from the river to the sea," subsuming and consuming the Jewish state?
Israel is guilty of many sins during its long decades of occupation, although neither side is blameless about missed opportunities. After the Oct. 7 Hamas massacre of more than 1,200 Jews and the taking of hostages, Israel's overreaction and overreach transformed a just war of defence into a war without justifiable limits.
More than 36,000 Palestinians are now dead, according to the Hamas-run health authority (which does not tally armed fighters; Israel claims roughly one-third of Gaza's dead are combatants).
It is undeniably a human disaster that could and should have been avoided.
Yet today, Israel alone is accused of genocide. One doesn't see other, proven genocides protested with similar ferocity - not even the most recent ones alleged to have taken place in Ethiopia (as many as 300,000 Tigrayans dead as of 2022) and Sudan (another 2.5 million Darfuris are imperilled right now).
If Israel's sins are so bad that they don't pass the smell test, are its odours any worse than those of other offending countries? Or do Israel's excesses rise above all others on earth, for all time?
That's not whataboutism, it's an attempt to understand what it's all about in a time of tunnel vision. It's also a reminder for us to place the world's troubles in the Canadian context.
While protesters accuse Israel of committing genocide in Gaza, one still hears remarkably little about reported hate crimes here at home that target mostly Jews but also many others. More on that in my next column.
CAPTION: By blaming the "other" for antisemitic acts in Canada, Premier Doug Ford missed an opportunity to address the "us" - all Ontarians and all Canadians - with a teachable moment on tolerance, writes Martin Regg Cohn.Arlyn McAdorey The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Letters: 'Soldiers of remembrance' retrace the D-Day path in France. But how long will the memory live as veterans fade away? Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+%27Soldiers+of+remembrance%27+retrace+the+D-Day+path+in+France.+But+how+long+will+the+memory+live+as+veterans+fade+away%3F+June+3+Protesters%2C+pomp+and+circumstance%3A+Here%27s+how+convocation+at+U+of+T+began+this+year%2C+June+4+Financial+watchdog+launches+review+of+Ford+government%27s+ServiceOntario+deal+with+Staples%2C+June+3+Here%27s+the+ugly+truth+about+Doug+Ford%27s+plan+for+more+standardized+testing+in+schools%2C+June+5+Frustrated+at+lack+of+progress%2C+Grassy+Narrows+sues+governments+for+failing+to+clean+up+mercury+pollution%2C+June+4&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lest we never forget those who fought on D-Day
'Soldiers of remembrance' retrace the D-Day path in France. But how long will the memory live as veterans fade away? June 3
Today marks the 80th anniversary of the D-Day invasion. That morning, thousands of young soldiers from Canada, Great Britain, America and other Allied nations landed along the coast of Normandy on beaches code named Juno, Gold, Sword, Omaha and Utah. They landed, in many cases under heavy enemy fire, but did not waver. Their goal was to defeat Nazism and fascism and liberate Europe from Adolf Hitler and his henchmen. Many fell that day. Many more were wounded, but they believed what they were doing was just and right for a civilized world. "They fight to end conquest," U.S. president Franklin D. Roosevelt said at the time of the invasion. "They fight to liberate."
Much has changed in the past 80 years on our planet, but one thing still stands out. We owe the utmost gratitude and respect to that generation who on the morning of June 6 landed on the beaches at Normandy knowing that they might not survive the day. So, all of us should pause today for a few moments and thank those young soldiers, many who made the ultimate sacrifice that day so that our world would be a much better place to live for future generations. Lest we forget.
Mike LoSchiavo,
Stoney Creek, Ont.
Think of those graduating
from U of T
Protesters, pomp and circumstance: Here's how convocation at U of T began this year, June 4
What a sad picture on page A1. While the demonstrators want the rest of us to sympathize with the plight of the Palestinians in Gaza and ask us to imagine ourselves in their place, they steadfastly refuse to do the same for anyone else. In refusing to decamp before the convocation ceremonies, they are blatantly ignoring the wishes of the administration and, much worse, the feelings of the graduates. After slaving through years of gruelling classes, sleep-stealing preparation for exams and amassing huge debt loads, they finally are getting their degrees. Imagine, actually no need to imagine - the pictures are clear, how lovely the graduation pictures will be with the encampment in their photos. For shame, demonstrators. Not a great way to get people onside.
This poor graduating class already got cheated of part of their high school experience by COVID. Now they are being robbed of a proper convocation experience.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge
Questions about ServiceOntario in Staples deal
Financial watchdog launches review of Ford government's ServiceOntario deal with Staples, June 3
Good for Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman for voicing her well-founded concerns over the financial arrangements to relocate nine ServiceOntario centres into Staples stores. On the surface, it sounds like a "win/win" for Ontarians. I question if the sole-sourced contract was inked clandestinely.
If it's such a great deal for us, wouldn't a responsible government announce the plan and look for interested businesses like, say, Canadian Tire to bid on the contract? Extended hours for Ontario services sounds terrific, but won't that require additional staff, benefits and overtime funded by Ontario taxpayers? I look forward to reading the report by the Financial Accountability Office, which will hopefully reveal the true cost to us.
Michelle Simson, Hastings, Ont.
Why not put Service Ontario counters in Beer Store outlets? The Beer Store likely needs a business boost now, with more beer offered elsewhere, and has many locations. Plus, they are open late and on weekends. Both are uniquely Ontario and have many visitors. Serious suggestion.
Paula Strasberg, North York
Standardized testing is necessary
Here's the ugly truth about Doug Ford's plan for more standardized testing in schools, June 5
Like many educators, a recent Star letter writer denigrates standardized testing, using the argument that all children learn differently. That is manifestly true, but misses why we need to have testing. Regardless of how children learn, there are certain things that we expect them to master at each grade level. Literacy and mathematics are basic skills, without which other skills are useless.
So we administer a standardized test to determine individual scores, which tell how well the individual student has mastered the skills expected for his grade level or age, and determine aggregate scores across classes schools, and school boards to indicate how well the school is imparting these necessary skills.
The first of these determines whether a child needs some remedial education. This is business as usual.
Roger Pett, Pickering
Fight for clean water is decades in the making
Frustrated at lack of progress, Grassy Narrows sues governments for failing to clean up mercury pollution, June 4
In the early 1970s, I was attending the University of Guelph in the Fisheries and Wildlife Biology program. The complaints of the devastating health effects due to the mercury poisoning of the Ojibwe people of the Grassy Narrows reserve were known at least by some. Also known were the studies linking mercury poisoning of the people in Minamata, Japan (thus, it was referred to as "Minamata disease").
Back in the waters at Grassy Narrows, the response of the federal and provincial governments of the time was denial, as far as I was aware, and the response of the medical profession as reported in a recent article by journalist Brandi Morin was dismissive and insulting to the Ojibwe people who complained.
The Star article by Morgan Sevareid-Bocknek once again puts this issue on the front page. As the article details it has been repeatedly in the media for years, with so little done. What I remember from the '70s after the poisoning was proved, was the belief that the river system would flush itself and all would be good if the natural systems were allowed to heal themselves.
Now 50-plus years later, the people of Grassy Narrows continue their struggle for clean drinking water, to be able to eat the fish and most importantly, control of what happens on their land. I wish them success in their legal proceedings.
David Maltby, Toronto

Second man dead after shooting behind school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/second-man-dead-after-shooting-behind-school/docview/3064875113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Second+man+dead+after+shooting+behind+school&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A second man has died after being seriously injured in Sunday's shooting behind an Etobicoke school, police say.
Shortly before 11 p.m. on Sunday, a dark-coloured pickup truck pulled up behind North Albion Collegiate Institute and two men got out, shooting a torrent of bullets toward a group of 15 to 20 people, fatally wounding one man, later identified as Delroy "George" Parkes, 61, and seriously injuring four others in their 40s to 60s.
Toronto police confirmed on Wednesday that another man has died in hospital but said they could not confirm that it's a homicide and related to the shooting until an autopsy to determine cause of death is completed.
Police said they do not believe that the victims and suspects are known to one another.
More than 50 bullets were fired at the scene, Supt. Ron Taverner told a news conference Monday, he said the men were "completely innocent victims."
In addition to those killed and injured, three cars were hit, and some bullets entered the empty school building.
The victims were a regular fixture of the neighbourhood, according to residents, who said Parkes has been part of domino games and other activities behind the school for over 35 years.
The school where the shooting took place is located across the road from a public housing community that has been struggling over the last year with an increase of violent incidents, officials say. At a meeting of Toronto Community Housing Corp.'s community safety advisory subcommittee on Monday morning, the agency's programs and partnerships director Arsema Berhane noted the uptick within their Rexdale community of Kipling-Mount Olive, a lowrise complex comprised of nearly 160 two and three-bedroom townhouses and walk-up apartments.
Doug Ford said he is "disgusted" by the shootings and deaths in Rexdale recently and his "heart breaks for the victims and their loved ones."
Ford added that the government is aiming to get "more boots on the ground" by investing hundreds of millions of dollars.
Police are asking anyone who saw a dark-coloured pickup truck in the area on Sunday at approximately 10:50 p.m. to contact them.
CREDIT: Abhiraj Lamba Toronto Star

Document 147 of 500

Doug Ford isn't the only one who has fumbled on antisemitism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-isnt-only-one-who-has-fumbled-on/docview/3064874703/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+isn%27t+the+only+one+who+has+fumbled+on+antisemitism&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford surely missed the mark when he blamed immigrants for the latest wave of antisemitic shootings.
But he's not the only one guilty of going off target.
A great many Canadians - new and old alike - are also missing the bigger lesson: How to bring people together while calling out hate.
Amid the ongoing controversy over Ford's faux pas, we're losing our focus over the original sins - the shootings and attacks outside Jewish schools and synagogues in Toronto, Montreal and Vancouver within a matter of weeks.
To be sure, the premier's perennial missteps are fair game. But the daily news cycle has defaulted to more of the same Ford-bashing (I too am guilty), while devoting only fleeting coverage to recurring Jew hatred (I'm guilty again).
Is anyone still talking about the bullets fired outside the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School in Toronto last month? Bullets fired at the Belz Yeshiva Ketana school in a Montreal synagogue? The firebombing of a Vancouver synagogue last week? Vandalism at the Anshei Minsk synagogue in Toronto's Kensington Market this week?
Sharing a podium with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, who forcefully condemned the latest antisemitic outburst in Toronto, Ford went way off script: "You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world," he hectored.
By scapegoating the "other," the premier missed an opportunity to address the "us" - all Ontarians and all Canadians - with a teachable moment on tolerance. Let's not lose the moment.
Blaming immigrants for importing antisemitism and hatred here isn't just wrong-headed in spirit. It's wrong in fact.
For there is a long history of homegrown Jew hatred in this country. The latest bigotry by old stock Canadians and newcomers is little different.
Antisemitism comes in every skin colour and from every country of origin - including this country. The difference is that it now goes by another name.
A few decades ago in Ontario you could find legal papers that excluded land sales or rentals to anyone of "the Jewish, Hebrew, Semitic, Negro or coloured race or blood." Jews were excluded from universities and excoriated as "Hebes" or "kikes."
Today, Zionists or "Zios" are the preferred proxies for denigrating Jews, even calling for their exclusion ("No Zios on campus," as the protest slogan goes).
The old phrase "Zionism is racism," once discredited, has been reincarnated as "Zionism is colonialism" - which inevitably diminishes and denigrates most Jews. Why?
While Zionism takes many expressions, most Jews believe in its most common definition - self-determination for Jews (who have their own claims to indigenity in Israel). If you insult or isolate Zionists, treating them as adversaries, you are inevitably singling out most Jews for opprobrium and discrimination - your friends, neighbours, co-workers.
If you call yourself anti-Zionist (as opposed to merely non-Zionist) you are effectively opposed to Israel's raison d'être, which raises the question: What is to become of the Jewish state and its nine million citizens (of whom about one in five within its borders are Palestinian citizens who can vote - not to be confused with five million Palestinians proper living under Israeli occupation or control in the West Bank or Gaza).
To be sure, critics of Israel - of which I am one - are not necessarily anti-Israeli (or anti-Jewish). But a good many are so adamantly opposed to the existence of the state of Israel, for reasons of history or bigotry, that you have to ask:
Where would those millions of Jews go? Back to Poland, as some like to taunt? Here to Canada, where they feel increasingly besieged? Stay where they are in a single state where "Palestine shall be free, from the river to the sea," subsuming and consuming the Jewish state?
Israel is guilty of many sins during its long decades of occupation, although neither side is blameless about missed opportunities. After the Oct. 7 Hamas massacre of more than 1,200 Jews and the taking of hostages, Israel's overreaction and overreach transformed a just war of defence into a war without justifiable limits.
More than 36,000 Palestinians are now dead, according to the Hamas-run health authority (which does not tally armed fighters; Israel claims roughly one-third of Gaza's dead are combatants). It is undeniably a human disaster that could and should have been avoided.
Yet today, Israel alone is accused of genocide. One doesn't see other, proven genocides protested with similar ferocity - not even the most recent ones alleged to have taken place in Ethiopia (as many as 300,000 Tigrayans dead as of 2022) and Sudan (another 2.5 million Darfuris are imperilled right now).
If Israel's sins are so bad that they don't pass the smell test, are its odours any worse than those of other offending countries? Or do Israel's excesses rise above all others on earth, for all time?
That's not whataboutism, it's an attempt to understand what it's all about in a time of tunnel vision. It's also a reminder for us to place the world's troubles in the Canadian context.
While protesters accuse Israel of committing genocide in Gaza, one still hears remarkably little about reported hate crimes here at home that target mostly Jews but also many others. More on that in my next column.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford suggested immigrants were to blame for the recent shooting at a Jewish school in Toronto despite police saying they have no information on the suspects. Ford made the remarks while flanked by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and Mayor Olivia Chow, right, during a news conference at the facilities of vaccine producer Sanofi in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Document 148 of 500

Bank cuts key rate to 4.75%: Bank of Canada signals more cuts are on the way after quarter-point drop, the first in four years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bank-cuts-key-rate-4-75/docview/3064874647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bank+cuts+key+rate+to+4.75%25%3A+Bank+of+Canada+signals+more+cuts+are+on+the+way+after+quarter-point+drop%2C+the+first+in+four+years&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Let the cutting commence.
The Bank of Canada announced Wednesday morning that it's reducing its key overnight lending rate to 4.75 per cent, the first time it has dropped below five per cent since last July, and the first time in over four years it has made any cut at all.
"With continued evidence that underlying inflation is easing, Governing Council agreed that monetary policy no longer needs to be as restrictive and reduced the policy interest rate by 25 basis points," the bank said in a press release announcing its widely anticipated decision.
Since last July, the bank's key overnight lending rate had sat at five per cent. The bank raised rates 10 times between March 2022 and last summer in a bid to wrestle inflation down to its two per cent target.
The theory is that by making it more expensive to borrow money, consumers and businesses will spend less, driving down prices and slowing the economy.
The last time the bank cut rates was in March, 2020, when it dropped the overnight rate from 0.75 per cent to 0.25 per cent as the COVID-19 pandemic hammered the Canadian economy.
In a speech, bank governor Tiff Macklem said further cuts could be coming, but warned that it will be taking baby steps.
"If inflation continues to ease, and our confidence that inflation is headed sustainably to the two per cent target continues to increase, it is reasonable to expect further cuts to our policy interest rate," Macklem said. "But we are taking our interest-rate decisions one meeting at a time." Cutting too much too soon would be a mistake, he added.
"We don't want monetary policy to be more restrictive than it needs to be to get inflation back to target," Macklem said. "But if we lower our policy interest rate too quickly, we could jeopardize the progress we've made."
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford, who has repeatedly written to the Bank of Canada urging rate cuts, praised the move.
"I say to the Bank of Canada, it's about time," Ford told reporters.
"Now we look forward to more cuts and put money back into people's pockets so they can go out and afford an affordable home," he said.
"So yes, I'll be all over the Bank of Canada and I'll never stop until they continue lowering the interest rates."
While many private sector forecasters have the bank dropping rates all the way to four per cent by the end of the year, Macklem said anyone expecting a rush downwards will likely be disappointed.
When the Bank started raising rates in 2022, inflation was spiralling rapidly out of control, hitting a peak of 8.1 per cent in June of that year, Macklem argued. Now, the picture is different.
"Inflation rose rapidly. We needed to raise rates rapidly," said Macklem. "Our forecasts show inflation easing gradually. ... The path for interest rates is likely to be gradual."
Douglas Porter, BMO's chief economist, said the 25 basis point cut (a quarter of a percentage point) will have an important symbolic effect.
"The first cut may not necessarily be the deepest, but it is the most significant, as it marks the official turning point after more than two years of restrictive policy," said Porter, adding that the bank could cut again at its next meeting July 24.
"There are two CPI (and jobs) reports prior to the July 24 decision; if the inflation reports mimic the very mild results seen so far this year, a cut is very much on the table for that decision as well," Porter said in a research note.
TD economist James Orlando argued that the Bank will be on a "cut-pause-cut" path, meaning that it will leave rates alone in July, but drop them again in September. The last thing the Bank wants to do, said Orlando, is set real estate prices soaring again the way it did when announcing a conditional pause in its rate-raising campaign in January 2023.
Moving too quickly could also increase the divergence between the central bank's key interest rate, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's Fed Funds rate, which is currently at a range between 5.25 and 5.50 per cent.
If Canada's interest rate is substantially lower, the value of the loonie typically falls.
Macklem admitted that there's a limit to how much the bank can practically diverge from the Fed, but insisted it's not handcuffed to its American counterpart.
"We don't need to move in lockstep with the Federal Reserve," said Macklem. "There are limits to how far we can diverge, but we're not close to those limits."
In April, the annual inflation rate fell to 2.7 per cent, considerably lower that its peak of 8.1 per cent in June 2022.
Last week, Statistics Canada released Gross Domestic Product data showing that the Canadian economy grew at an annualized rate of just 1.7 per cent in the first quarter of 2024, slower than the 2.2 per cent expected by a consensus of economists, and substantially slower than the Bank of Canada's projections of 2.8 per cent.
That less-than-stellar GDP figure had an almost immediate impact in trading on the overnight interest swap market. Before the GDP release, trading on the OIS market was pricing in a slightly less than 50 per cent chance of an interest-rate cut this week. By midday Monday, that figure had jumped to 83 per cent.
With files from Robert Benzie
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star With files from Robert Benzie

Document 149 of 500

Doug Ford's beer in corner store move makes me wish for the good old fashioned boondoggles of the past

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-beer-corner-store-move-makes-me-wish/docview/3064874608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+beer+in+corner+store+move+makes+me+wish+for+the+good+old+fashioned+boondoggles+of+the+past&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Looking at government spending boondoggles-in-the-making this week is enough to make you long for the days when the public could expect to get a little boon alongside the doggle.
Time was, when the province built a "billion-dollar boondoggle" vanity subway line on Sheppard as a favour to a friendly mayor, at least at the end people wound up with a perfectly nice "stubway" line that served local passengers and provided express service to Ikea, and eventually could grow into a functional part of the network. When the federal government rained dollars onto Muskoka as part of Toronto hosting the G20, cottage country came away with a perfectly serviceable gazebo, among other amenities. When the government spent $570 million to build SkyDome and then eventually gave it away, at least we wound up with a downtown, skyline-defining stadium.
There was, I'm saying, a little bang provided for those too-many bucks. Some razzle dazzle to distract us from the misspending.
That feels like it's not the case anymore. And not just because our transit construction projects are proving to deliver more construction than transit.
Take the case of the bill already being rung up for hosting a few World Cup games in 2026. I don't want to whine too much about police spending $1.5 million on new cars and overtime and $8,000 worth of AirPods in preparation for the games.
I mean, sure, I personally might choose to make do with off-brand wireless pod headphones that cost $30 rather than buying the $230 Apple ones, and I may barely notice the difference. But I have a high school aged daughter who lets me know that the social pressures of teenagerhood dictate that traumatic humiliation would come with having anything but the real branded product in your ears.
And cops are kind of like the teenage status-seekers of the civil service. Their peers are riding around in helicopters and tanks; there's a lot of pressure to keep up appearances. You see them picking up work on evenings and weekends babysitting construction sites for extra pocket money. They put effort into their outfits. They love the participant ribbon of a "challenge coin" to provide recognition.
You expect them to attend a virtual meeting wearing generic ear buds? OK boomer.
But seriously, all told (including cars and such that will stay in service for years), we're talking about $1.5 million, out of a projected total event budget of $380 million. Big events like this, by their nature, come with an orgy of spending you kind of have to brace yourself for when you agree to host them. In return, you get to be the centre of attention for one of the world's biggest parties.
The thing with these World Cup games, as I've written before, is that it's questionable whether we're getting that trade. We're one of 16 host cities across three countries. We'll be home to six games, none in the final rounds. No doubt it will be fun, but it's hard to imagine this is going to be our moment.
The case of Premier Doug Ford's "boozedoggle" takes the same issue to a whole different scale. Ontario taxpayers will pay $225 million - minimum, the opposition suggests maybe as much as a billion - to rush beer and wine sales into convenience stores 14 months earlier than expected. To repeat: this is something that we were going to get anyway, at no cost. And while I (like many people) have long thought opening up sales to local shops was the right move, it isn't exactly urgent: there's no shortage of outlets where you can buy booze under the status quo.
And so it is $235 million for which we get approximately nothing. The premier appears to think he gets something - a "promise kept" he can point to if he calls a snap election in the fall or spring.
But man, oh man: $235 million (or more). How much could construction on the Gardiner, or on the Eglinton Crosstown LRT, be sped up with an injection of that kind of cash? My colleagues recently wrote about the nightmarish conditions (bullet holes, drug dealers, mould) at the Dan Harrison public housing complex - how much better could life be there if $235 million was put to fast-tracking upgrades? How many homeless shelter beds could those dollars buy?
It's not just the cash. It's what we're not getting for it. Obviously, it would be better if we didn't have any ridiculous overspending scandals to consider at all. But when we do, it would be nice if we could do the old-fashioned thing of pointing to the results and say, "at least we got that." According to Ford's plan, I guess what we'd get is more of him.
A dubious return on our investment. Ever more so all the time.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's move to rush beer and wine in corner stores 14 months earlier than expected gives us approximately nothing for hundreds of millions of dollars.

Document 150 of 500

If TTC workers go on strike, history suggests they're unlikely to be out for long

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-ttc-workers-go-on-strike-history-suggests/docview/3065073667/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+TTC+workers+go+on+strike%2C+history+suggests+they%27re+unlikely+to+be+out+for+long&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It remained a mystery Wednesday if Toronto's transit system is heading for a shutdown but the most recent strikes suggest the buses, subways and streetcars would not be parked for long.
Canada's biggest city hasn't seen a TTC strike since 2008 - thanks to a legislated ban that was overturned by the courts last year - but the most recent walkouts have seen provincial authorities quickly get transit workers back on the job.
The 2008 walkout caught commuters by surprise because union leadership and TTC management had agreed on a tentative contract later rejected by a majority of the union members. A union promise to give city hall 48 hours notice of a strike didn't happen, amid cited fears of riders abusing drivers during the two-day leadup to a walkout.
At 11:23 p.m. on Friday April 26, 2008, transit drivers got a private intercom message to shut down at midnight, and then announced it to shocked passengers, many of whom had no way to make a return trip, the Star reported at the time.
The next day, Dalton McGuinty's Liberal government called a rare Sunday sitting to pass legislation that got the TTC moving well before the Monday morning rush hour.
In 2006, an illegal "wildcat" walkout on May 29 ended the same day it started when workers complied with an afternoon order from the Ontario Labour Relations Board after ignoring an order earlier in the day.
A 1999 strike lasted two days. You have to look back to 1991 to find a TTC strike that lasted longer than a week - eight days - and back to 1974 to find one that went multiple weeks, a total of 23 days.
Adam Giambrone, who was TTC chair during the 2008 walkout, told the Star on Wednesday: "I expect that if a strike were to occur as early as Friday it would be fairly short-lived," depending on the Ontario government's actions. "One would expect provincial legislation and I don't think it would take long."
However, Giambrone added, "You can't replace the TTC. There's not enough Ubers, taxis, (bike shares) - any shutdown would have serious impacts on the people of Toronto and on the economy. Any strike shouldn't just be dismissed."
At Queen's Park on Wednesday, Premier Doug Ford would not reveal his plans in case of a strike as early as 12:01 a.m. Friday but said he is unhappy with the court decision that overturned essential service designation for TTC workers and he won't sit by and watch commuters stranded.
"I respect bargaining ... they should bargain in good faith ... and let's just avoid it at all costs," he added, referring to a possible work stoppage. "I love our TTC drivers and everyone who works at TTC but it's an essential service, it can shut down the economy in a big way."
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, whose party is the Official Opposition and has power to slow the passage of any back-to-work legislation, said Ford could help avoid a strike by increasing provincial funding for public transit.
"My focus right now is on getting a deal. There doesn't need to be a strike," added Stiles, who on Tuesday replied "absolutely not" when asked if her party would support back-to-work legislation.
A CBC story about the lengthy 1974 strike revealed how vital the TTC had become to many Torontonians over the past half-century of increased ridership, and how much less tolerance current users have for any disruption.
Almost two weeks into the transit shutdown, a CBC reporter said "the bloom had worn off the strike" and those who viewed it "as a charming midsummer adventure began to get a little testy."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: TTC streetcars sit idle in 2008 after transit workers began a surprise strike on Saturday, April 26. The next day, Ontario's Liberal government called a rare Sunday sitting to pass legislation that got the transit system moving before the Monday morning rush hour.

Subject: Legislation; Shutdowns; Labor disputes; Court decisions; Strikes

Title: If TTC workers go on strike, history suggests they're unlikely to be out for long

Document 151 of 500

'Plan ahead': TTC, union still on different tracks in negotiations as transit strike looms Friday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/plan-ahead-ttc-union-still-on-different-tracks/docview/3065073655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Plan+ahead%27%3A+TTC%2C+union+still+on+different+tracks+in+negotiations+as+transit+strike+looms+Friday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the clock ticks down to Toronto's first potential transit strike in more than a decade, the chair of the TTC and the head of its largest union are painting starkly different pictures of how close the two sides are to a deal.
Speaking to reporters at city hall on Wednesday with less than 36 hours until the strike deadline, TTC chair Coun. Jamaal Myers said contract talks between management and Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113 were "headed in the right direction."
"We are in a good position. Both parties remain bargaining. They both continue to bargain in good faith. And we are confident that there is a path to a deal," said Myers (Scarborough North).
As evidence, Myers cited the agreement between the union and TTC, announced Monday, to keep the Wheel-Trans service operational in the event of job action.
But hours before Myers' remarks, Local 113 president Marvin Alfred described a strike as all but certain.
"I'm not optimistic. We're going on strike on Friday. What it takes is a deal, and we don't have a deal. And we're not close enough to have any sort of optimism right now," he told Global News.
Local 113's collective agreement expired at the end of March, and its roughly 12,000 members will be in a legal strike position at 12:01 a.m. on Friday. TTC spokesperson Stuart Green confirmed that if they walk off the job, there's nothing the agency can do to prevent a shutdown of the system, which normally provides 2.5 million trips each weekday.
"Our contingency is only around having staff available at stations to advise customers if there's no service," he said.
Green said the agency's advice to transit users is "to plan ahead."
"If people absolutely rely on service and can't work from home, we advise looking at alternatives like carpooling, cycling or other shared services. GO transit and regional services will be operating too," he said.
Sources who spoke to the Star Wednesday, and were granted anonymity to discuss sensitive negotiations, gave differing accounts of how the negotiations are proceeding. Some said the two sides were still far apart, while others were confident a work stoppage could be averted.
One source said progress was made Wednesday on less costly items like benefits and working conditions, but agreements on bigger ticket issues like wage increases would likely go down to the wire.
"We're getting closer," the source said, predicting talks would proceed into Wednesday night.
Sources previously told the Star Local 113 was seeking a three-year deal with annual wage increases of between five and seven per cent, while the TTC had offered a four-year contract with yearly raises between three and 3.75 per cent.
Also at issue are job security and contracting out of maintenance jobs.
One of the major sticking points is the union's demand for guarantees around municipal transit agencies from outside Toronto providing local service within the city's borders, which Local 113 argues would amount to contracting out TTC service.
"We're fighting off having other agencies in the 905 region delivering Toronto transit," Alfred said Wednesday.
At Queen's Park, Premier Doug Ford would not tip his hand on any plans to pass back-to-work legislation in the event of a TTC strike but suggested he is not pleased that the courts overruled the previous provincial government's move to declare the TTC an essential service, as urged by his late brother Rob Ford, the former mayor.
The 2011 legislation barred the TTC from striking. Last year, the Ontario Superior Court ruled it unconstitutional, opening the door to the first walkout at the transit agency since 2008.
"I respect bargaining ... they should bargain in good faith ... and let's just avoid it at all costs," Ford said, referring to a possible work stoppage. "I love our TTC drivers and everyone who works at TTC but it's an essential service, it can shut down the economy in a big way."
Any attempt to legislate TTC employees back to work could be hampered by the provincial government's announcement Wednesday that the legislature will rise for the summer on Thursday. It would have to be recalled for an emergency session to pass a back-to-work law.
New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, whose party is the Official Opposition and has power to slow the passage of any strike-busting law, on Tuesday replied "absolutely not" when asked if her party would support back-to-work legislation.
She says Ford could help avoid a strike by increasing provincial funding for public transit.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION: As Toronto faces a potential transit strike in less than 36 hours,the chair of the TTC and the head of its largest union have indicated very different positions on how close the two sides are to a deal."Both parties remain bargaining," said TTC chair Jamaal Myers on Wednesday. "And we are confident that there is a path to a deal."TTC Local 113 president Marvin Alfred described a strike as all but certain. "What it takes is a deal, and we don't have a deal. And we're not close enough to have any sort of optimism right now."

Subject: Legislation; Strikes; Bargaining

Business indexing term: Subject: Strikes Bargaining

Title: 'Plan ahead': TTC, union still on different tracks in negotiations as transit strike looms Friday

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 152 of 500

Governments sign $4.7B deal to boost housing construction in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/governments-sign-4-7b-deal-boost-housing/docview/3064675341/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Governments+sign+%244.7B+deal+to+boost+housing+construction+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After months of squabbling and jockeying, Canada's three levels of government have come together to finalize $4.7 billion in funding from Ottawa to boost housing construction in Ontario.
Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser, Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra and Mayor Olivia Chow said Wednesday the "Canada Community-Building Fund," formerly known as the gas tax fund, would be renewed for another five years.
In a joint statement that was also signed by Association of Municipalities of Ontario president Colin Best, the federal, provincial and municipal governments said the money "will ensure that critical infrastructure that supports housing continues to be built, maintained and expanded."
While the funding had been announced in federal Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland's April budget, it was dependent upon Queen's Park and the municipalities signing on.
"The renewal of this agreement means that our communities' roads, bridges, and transit systems will be positioned to accommodate growth," said the joint statement.
That $4.7 billion infusion of cash over five years will bankroll projects across Ontario.
"We recognize the importance of infrastructure in increasing Canada's housing supply. By working together to strategically invest in projects aligned with regional housing goals, we will help increase the number of affordable homes in Ontario," the three levels of government said.
"Through this agreement, we have aligned on three priority goals, including reporting on affordable units created to meet the needs of communities and increase capacity of the non-profit sector; working across all orders of government to leverage public lands to meet housing needs; and supporting innovation in construction techniques by committing to a provincial innovation strategy for modular and prefabricated housing."
That includes working with Ottawa "to develop and adopt a design catalogue" to build new houses more quickly and cheaply.
"We will work together to build healthy and vibrant neighbourhoods across the province," the politicians said.
"We are taking a Team Canada approach to build more inclusive and connected communities, which will help support more housing by creating the public infrastructure that Ontarians need."
Wednesday's announcement comes eight days after Fraser and Calandra set aside months of rancour and inked a deal ensuring $357 million in federal funds for the province to build affordable housing.
Under that agreement between Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, Ontario revised its plan for affordable housing projects, meaning at least 8,644 more units will be built by the end of next March.
Queen's Park, which insists it could actually meet its original federally mandated target of 19,660 new units by that deadline, will also submit its affordable housing target for 2025-28 by year's end.
That's in order to secure additional federal funding for the final years of the decade-long $70-billion National Housing Strategy agreement.
Ford's Progressive Conservatives, who have promised to build 1.5 million new homes by 2031, have been working closely with Trudeau's Liberals toward that goal.
But so far the Tories are nowhere near the average annual housing starts of 150,000 units that are needed to achieve that, according to the government's online tracker.
Last year, 109,011 new homes were built, including 9,835 long-term-care beds that the province now classifies as homes.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra speaks to reporters at Queen's Park on March 7, 2024.

Subject: Agreements; Infrastructure; Affordable housing; Funding; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Affordable housing Housing starts
