Document 181 of 500

TTC strike looms this week - its first in 16 years. Here's what the issues are

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ttc-strike-looms-this-week-first-16-years-heres/docview/3063326610/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TTC+strike+looms+this+week+-+its+first+in+16+years.+Here%27s+what+the+issues+are&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Torontonians could face a total shutdown of the TTC as early as Friday, unless transit and union officials can agree on a new contract and avoid a strike.
Since February, leaders of the Amalgamated Transit Union Local 113 and TTC officials have tried and failed to sign a deal to replace the previous agreement, which expired on March 31. Major sticking points have included wages, job security and benefits as the clock ticks down to the possible start of the first transit strike in 16 years.
Sources told the Star late last week that the union had rebuffed a four-year TTC offer that would have given workers annual raises of 3.75 per cent, 3.5 per cent, 3.5 per cent, and three per cent. The sources, who were not authorized to disclose the figures, said the union - which had for years been legally prohibited from striking - was seeking a three-year deal with hikes of seven, six and five per cent.
Neither the TTC nor the union would confirm those numbers.
The union's previous contract saw a wage increase of about two per cent each year for three years.
A walkout by the TTC's roughly 12,000 front-line employees could mean a full shutdown of Toronto's bus, streetcar and subway network that normally carries passengers on almost 2.5 million trips each weekday. While many more people have work-from-home options now than during the previous strike in April 2008, the sheer volume of commuters in 2024 would worsen already terrible gridlock and fill the city's bike lanes and sidewalks.
TTC chair Coun. Jamaal Myers told reporters Friday both sides were at the negotiating table "multiple times" last week and he remains "very confident" that his agency and the union will avoid a shutdown.
"I understand that progress is being made and I'm confident that we can get there. Both sides want a deal," he said, after announcing that Rogers Communications is starting work to bring 5G cellular service to the whole subway system, beginning with tunnels between Kennedy and Warden stations.
Local 113 president Marvin Alfred wouldn't comment on details of the talks, but told the Star that offers the TTC has tabled so far have included "extreme conditions" that diminished their value.
Though Alfred declined to say what those conditions were, he said that union members, who kept the city moving through the worst of the pandemic, are frustrated, a feeling compounded by being deprived of the right to strike for the past 13 years. He described the 2011 Ontario law that banned TTC job action as a way for the city and province to "stifle our opportunities for dignity when it comes to our income."
The union regained the right to strike last year when the Ontario Superior Court ruled the legislation - introduced by the provincial Liberals after a request from then-mayor Rob Ford and city council - violated workers' charter rights.
Bruce Macgregor, the TTC deputy chief executive, told reporters Friday managers have contingency plans in case of a strike, but acknowledged a full walkout would "be devastating for this city. That is always the potential and we're prepared for that potential," he said, while hopeful for a deal before the deadline.
Mayor Olivia Chow said she has been briefed on contingency plans, but declined to reveal them. "I still remain optimistic," she said. "When you continue to talk it's always good to seek some kind of solution."
One source with knowledge of the negotiations late last week put the chances of a strike at 50/50, saying a significant gap remained on wages.
Another source familiar with the talks said it's difficult to predict whether the union will walk out. They said negotiations often go down to the wire and Local 113 and TTC management are still having "productive and constructive conversations."
The source predicted a work stoppage would be damaging for the union, management and the public, and both sides wanted to avoid it.
"No one really wants a strike," they said.
But the source described the union's requested wage hikes of between five and seven per cent over three years as "unrealistic." By comparison, last year Local 113's sister union, ATU Local 107 in Hamilton, won a four-year deal with annual increases of between three and 3.75 per cent.
On the first day of the 2008 strike, a college student told the Star about missing a job interview, a tired nurse almost cried at the prospect of having to walk home after a 12-hour hospital shift and a disabled Mississauga woman was trying to figure out how to visit her mother who had suffered a stroke in Toronto. That strike lasted only a weekend before the Ontario government passed legislation ordering TTC staff back to work.
This time TTC vehicle operators, fare collectors, maintenance workers and more would be on picket lines. Some ATU members put on signs and marched outside TTC headquarters last week in an information picket.
CAPTION: During the previous TTC strike in 2008, transit union members were of the job for a weekend before the provincial government legislated them back to work.

Subject: Labor disputes; Strikes; Wages & salaries; Contingency planning

Business indexing term: Subject: Labor disputes Strikes Wages & salaries Contingency planning

Title: TTC strike looms this week - its first in 16 years. Here's what the issues are

Document 182 of 500

Clock ticks down to possible TTC strike: City hopeful of deal before Friday, but wages are a sticking point, sources say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clock-ticks-down-possible-ttc-strike/docview/3063326535/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clock+ticks+down+to+possible+TTC+strike%3A+City+hopeful+of+deal+before+Friday%2C+but+wages+are+a+sticking+point%2C+sources+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

CREDIT: David Rider, Ben Spurr and Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Subject: Labor disputes; Strikes; Contingency planning; Mass transit

Business indexing term: Subject: Labor disputes Strikes Contingency planning

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Transit Commission; NAICS: 485113, 485119

Document 183 of 500

'A lot of people are very angry:' How a Doug Ford promise set off a health-care fight in cottage country

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/lot-people-are-very-angry-how-doug-ford-promise/docview/3063326505/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+lot+of+people+are+very+angry%3A%27+How+a+Doug+Ford+promise+set+off+a+health-care+fight+in+cottage+country&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Call it Muskoka's Battle of the Beds.
As cottage season arrives, a feud is raging over new hospitals for the Bracebridge and Huntsville areas, where the population swells dramatically every summer.
A basic question still festers two years after the Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare system got a $14-million planning grant from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Which community in the picturesque Muskoka lakes region 200 km north of Toronto should have the most in-patient beds?
Sharp disagreement between south Muskoka (Bracebridge) and the north (Huntsville), a 30-minute drive up Highway 11, is overshadowing the premier's assurances from April 2022, just weeks before the last provincial election.
"It's time you get the modern hospital capacity you desperately need," pledged Ford - who has a cottage between the two towns - as he made the announcement in Huntsville to increase the bed count at both.
But with planning for the $1 billion in replacement hospitals now getting down to the nitty-gritty - both facilities will see their emergency departments doubled to 27 treatment bays, for example- complications are popping up.
That's because Bracebridge is losing in-patient beds and Huntsville is gaining them under the latest plan from Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare, the organization formed two decades ago to operate the previously independent hospitals within a joint health-care system.
"There's definitely been some drama," says Dr. Luke Wu, an internal medicine specialist in Bracebridge, referring to tense moments at community meetings held over the winter. More consultations are slated this month.
"A lot of people are very angry," adds Wu, one of dozens of Bracebridge doctors concerned about the long-term future of hospital care there and for nearby Gravenhurst.
While patients remain plentiful - a May 22 memo to staff enacted the hospital system's "gridlock policy'' when "high occupancy" forced the Bracebridge campus to temporarily put admitted patients in the day surgery unit - patience is wearing thin and the clock is ticking.
Officials at Muskoka Algonquin are trying to nail down a final plan to submit for provincial government approval so that construction can begin in the next few years.
"We are dedicated to meeting Muskoka's evolving health-care needs now and into the future," says chief executive officer Cheryl Harrison.
"Enhancing specialized services like the regional stroke program, Level 3 ICU (intensive care unit) and rehabilitation unit ensures timely, expert care. Doubling our emergency departments improves capacity during peak seasonal periods."
The latest version of the plan puts 36 in-patient beds in the new Bracebridge hospital - down from 67 now at the town's South Muskoka Memorial Hospital.
Huntsville is slated for 121 beds, up from 56 currently at Huntsville District Memorial Hospital.
Proponents of the plan maintain it is not practical to have hospitals in the two towns duplicating services.
That blueprint was described as a "strategic transition from operating two independent small hospitals to adopting a cohesive regional health-care approach" in a recent memo from chief of staff Dr. Khaled Abdel-Razek and more than a dozen department heads on the Muskoka Algonquin medical advisory committee.
With battle lines drawn, provincial officials say they're waiting for a community consensus to emerge.
"We know that there are supporters of the plan, depending on what part of the riding you're in, and there are detractors," says local MPP Graydon Smith (Parry Sound-Muskoka), Ford's minister of natural resources and a former mayor of Bracebridge.
"I hope there's a continued amount of conversation and engagement and everyone can reach a point where it's a win-win."
It doesn't look like it will be easy.
"There is no way for the current administration to pull this out of the fire," insists Tony Vanderstelt, a vice chair of the Save South Muskoka Hospital Committee.
"We're calling on the government to put in an independent investigator to come in and look," says Vanderstelt, who was one of thousands at an Ontario Health Coalition rally outside the legislature Thursday protesting emergency room closures, losses of hospital services and plans for more private clinics.
Huntsville gets more beds because it is designated by the Ministry of Health as a regional stroke treatment centre owing to its location mid-way between Barrie and North Bay, ensuring "timely access to critical care," says Bobbie Clark, spokesperson for Muskoka Algonquin. There will be a 10-bed intensive care unit and a 14-bed stroke unit at Huntsville.
"In contrast, the (new) Bracebridge site focuses on high-frequency outpatient services, such as day surgeries and diagnostic imaging ... including four ICU beds and an obstetrical labour and delivery room," Clark said.
The latest allotment of in-patient beds is a shift from January when the new Bracebridge hospital was to be given 14 compared with 139 for Huntsville.
That announcement set off a firestorm and sowed seeds of mistrust that linger despite the subsequent increase to the Bracebridge bed count.
"The well is definitely poisoned," says Vanderstelt, a local businessman.
The boosted bed count is not enough for many doctors in south Muskoka, where 47 physicians recently put their names on an open letter to the community.
The letter warned the reduction in in-patient beds means many local patients "will therefore need to be transferred to Huntsville," further away from their families and one step closer to getting treatment in hospitals in Barrie and Toronto, which are better equipped to deal with complicated procedures.
"Our hospital currently operates over capacity on a regular basis. The population in south Muskoka is growing and aging, and it is only rational to expect that the health-care needs of our community will be drastically increasing," the letter continues.
The letter also predicts the reduction in beds will make it tougher to attract doctors and raises concerns about "the long-term viability of the Bracebridge site."
Bracebridge doctors are keeping up the fight because they fear the 36 in-patient beds won't be nearly enough to meet the community's needs. The Muskoka area has more than 60,000 people, but during the summer months seasonal residents double the population.
"We're trying to advocate for our community," says Dr. Tina Kappos, who has been associated with the hospital for 26 years and calls the initial decision to slash the bed count "irrational."
There has been a flurry of communications from both sides as plans have been adjusted in recent months.
In the face of stiff opposition from the Bracebridge area to the original plans, Muskoka Algonquin recently issued a statement boasting of "extensive community consultation" that led to "significant modifications ... including maintaining obstetric services at both sites and adjusting bed numbers to better meet local needs."
In their open letter, the Bracebridge-area doctors urged locals to "reject this proposed model for hospital redevelopment," noting patients transferred to a new hospital in Huntsville would likely not be seen by their own doctors for acute care, as is the case now.
Bracebridge Mayor Rick Maloney is urging more discussion.
"For the hospital redevelopment plan to succeed, it must be endorsed by the doctors and key stakeholders in both north and south Muskoka. I encourage Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare to continue to work toward a solution," he wrote in a statement last month.
In Toronto, Health Minister Sylvia Jones seems to be keeping it a local issue.
"We understand that Muskoka Algonquin Healthcare is in their detailed planning phase and consulting with their community and local partners to ensure they are creating stable, consistent and connected care in the region," she said in a statement to the Star.
"We will continue to work together to ensure access to care in the region for years to come."
CAPTION: Protesters concerned about their Bracebridge hospital losing beds in a redevelopment plan were among thousands protesting the Ontario government's health-care changes on May 30, 2024 at Queen's Park in Toronto.

Subject: Health care access; Hospitals; Patients; Emergency medical care; Stroke; Health care policy; Intensive care; Planning; Physicians

5 things we learned from a new book about Justin Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/5-things-we-learned-new-book-about-justin-trudeau/docview/3063113138/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=5+things+we+learned+from+a+new+book+about+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new book on Justin Trudeau, "The Prince" by Stephen Maher, documents a turbulent time at the top of this country. Here are five takeaways from the story of his reign, amassed through stories that hit the headlines and drama behind the scenes.
1. Even loyal MPs and close friends of Trudeau believe it's time for him to go and told Maher as much in the book, though they did so anonymously. "Every MP will tell you that their closest, most long-standing Liberal supporters all think it's time," one Liberal MP was quoted as saying in late 2023.
"I think of him more as the leader for a time and I think the times require a different kind of leader now," says one close friend.
2. When Trudeau first met Gerald Butts at the McGill University debating club, he went by the name of "Jason Tremblay" so as not to draw attention to his famous lineage. Granted, some news outlets had that little tidbit early after Trudeau became prime minister, but Maher's book reminds us of a time when Trudeau didn't even want to be called Justin or the son of a prime minister.
3. Mark Carney, the former governor of the central banks of Canada and the United Kingdom, was scared off running for the Liberals in the 2021 election because of the way he felt Trudeau's PMO has stuck a "shiv" in the career of former finance minister Bill Morneau. "Seeing Morneau being treated so shabbily seems to have given Carney pause, and he did not allow himself to be seduced. They spooked him by putting the shiv in."
4. Hanbury Strategy, a British data consultancy firm hired by the Conservatives for help in the 2021 election, flatly told leader Erin O'Toole and his entourage that their party's brand was "toxic" and that Canada was fundamentally a progressive country.
"They had no idea coming in," the 2021 national campaign manager, Fred DeLorey, told Maher. "They worked for conservative parties in Australia, U.K., mainland Europe. And we had such a toxic brand in our country. And they also identified, surprisingly to me, how progressive Canada is versus most European countries."
5. The ejection of Jody Wilson-Raybould and Jane Philpott from the Liberal caucus in 2019 was a drawn-out, messy affair and is often cast as Trudeau's imperious decision from on high. "The Prince" documents how the two dissenting ministers had lost support from much of the Liberal caucus too. In recounting a "lively" Liberal caucus gathering, Maher reports that meeting included some name-calling of Wilson-Raybould and appeals for the two women to be "banished to the worst seats in the backbench."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau greets supporters at a campaign volunteer event, in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Subject: State elections; Books; Prime ministers

Location: Canada; United Kingdom--UK

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 185 of 500

Swap at the top? Some Liberals like the look of Carney: Some in the party think the former top banker could fare well replacing Trudeau. Others roll their eyes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/swap-at-top-some-liberals-like-look-carney/docview/3063005482/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Swap+at+the+top%3F+Some+Liberals+like+the+look+of+Carney%3A+Some+in+the+party+think+the+former+top+banker+could+fare+well+replacing+Trudeau.+Others+roll+their+eyes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The way former Liberal cabinet minister Catherine McKenna sees it, change is coming. Either her party supplies it, or they lose power.
She's quick to say that doesn't necessarily mean Justin Trudeau needs to go, though she observes that what's on offer "clearly" isn't resonating with voters.
But there is someone else - someone whose name keeps swirling around the never-ending carousel of speculation about the prime minister's future. Someone of "real substance" who, according to McKenna, understands the big challenges of our era and would stack up well against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the Liberals' archrival of the moment.
That someone is Mark Carney.
"I think he'd be a good candidate," McKenna said, when asked about the entrenched impression in political circles that the former central banker has eyes on the Liberal leadership.
"Someone like Carney is a very attractive option. And - as a contrast to Pierre Poilievre - he is experienced; he has an even temperament; he has had a life outside of politics; he has a plan. I think those are really important things."
It's hard to pin down what is going on with Carney. A former civil servant and head of central banks in two G7 countries, Canada and the United Kingdom, the 59-year-old Carney has tied his wagon to the Liberal horse. With his financier's mien, hockey references, and economics-oriented advocacy for climate action, he is not shying away from the public eye. Through Canada 2020, a Liberal-linked think tank situated just off Parliament Hill, he has a platform to expound on domestic Canadian policy from the sidelines of the political arena.
Just last week, he entered the political money game when he appeared as a "special guest" at an evening fundraiser for Anna Gainey, a Liberal MP in Montreal and former party president who was part of the core group that helped Trudeau rise to the leadership and win power in 2015. He'll reprise the role on June 11, when he's slated to headline another fundraiser in Toronto for Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie.
All this has been grist for the rumour mill inside the Ottawa bubble. There was a flurry of political commentary about his perceived ambitions in recent days. Ever eager to poke at Trudeau, the Conservatives have taken to calling Carney "the next Liberal leader." But there are signs the Tories are taking him seriously, just in case. In April, Poilievre debuted a new nickname in Parliament, when he lampooned him as "Carbon Tax Carney." Other Conservatives have tried to paint Carney as an elitist, a would-be politician with "Bay Street buddies" who plans to descend from his "ivory tower" to take over the Liberal party.
Carney did not agree to an interview for this story. But some who count themselves as close to him said they're not aware of any serious organization that is getting staged for Carney to win the leadership. Whatever is happening appears less tangible than that, pre-positioning for a race that is still only hypothetical. Trudeau, after all, insists at every turn that he's not going anywhere. Carney has said he supports the prime minister and expects him to lead the Liberals in the next election.
Still. Everyone knows Trudeau can't stick around forever.
"I know for sure people constantly approach (Carney) ... They want him to do it," said Evan Solomon, the former CTV host and longtime friend of Carney's who now works with the Eurasia Group, a geopolitical consulting firm in New York City.
As Solomon explained, Carney is agreeing to help the Liberals and taking opportunities to deliver speeches, but is not "strategically weaponizing" the interest for an imminent leadership run.
"I think it's a much softer path. It's not hard organizing yet," Solomon said of Liberal leadership posturing in general. But, he added, if the leadership opened up and the interest kept growing, Carney would likely go for it.
"If an opportunity came up," Solomon said, "of course he would probably take that opportunity."
Along with McKenna, there are some Liberals willing to publicly express support for Carney, even if - with national polls consistently suggesting their party is en route to a harsh defeat at the hands of Poilievre and the Conservatives - they stop short of declaring it's time for Trudeau to go.
Responding to a query about his potential support for a Carney leadership bid, former Liberal justice minister David Lametti, who was turfed from Trudeau's cabinet before he resigned his Montreal seat early this year, replied by email: "Mark is an old friend of mine, and I have always thought highly of his intellect and his leadership skills. That is really all I am comfortable saying right now."
Others who remain in Trudeau's caucus are more critical of Carney. Some question his commitment to the party, given that he's been publicly Liberal for at least three years but has never put his name on a ballot.
"I think he should run for a seat in Parliament," one MP told the Star. "Come get in the trenches."
Another MP described Carney as smart and capable, but concluded - based on his manner of public speaking and his lack of experience on the front-lines of partisan politics - that he is not ready to step in and lead the Liberals. This MP said there is a perception in caucus that Carney only wants to jump into politics if he can do so as party leader.
"You can't just 'Ignatieff' this thing," the MP said, referring to the ex-Liberal leader Michael Ignatieff who entered politics after an academic career abroad and spearheaded the worst campaign defeat in the party's history in the 2011 election.
A third MP, who like the others agreed to speak about Carney on condition of anonymity, described him as the "love child of Bill Morneau and Michael Ignatieff" - another reference to the failed leader in 2011, combined with a nod to Trudeau's finance minister from 2015 to 2020, a corporate scion in Toronto who has since criticized the Liberal government for its high levels of spending.
"Honestly, (Carney)'s not much of a topic of conversation, other than to roll our eyes," the MP said. "If you're interested in being a future leader of a political party, it's best to put your name on a ballot and do the work."
McKenna, the former Liberal cabinet minister, rejected the comparison between Carney and Ignatieff as unfair. She cited Carney's years of experience as a public servant in Canada, and said his time abroad - including as governor of the Bank of England - is an asset, not a disadvantage, in an era of climate change and tensions between great powers. "He understands the world," she said of Carney.
For Tim Powers, a former Conservative adviser and chairman of Summa Strategies, Carney is a "wonderful human being" with an "impressive pedigree." He said: "Canadians, regardless of party, would be lucky if he entered the political arena."
Powers also argued Carney could craft a "compelling" narrative for more centrist voters, given his past in the upper ranks of the federal government when Conservative Jim Flaherty was finance minister, and how he was appointed governor of the Bank of Canada when Stephen Harper was prime minister. Born in the Northwest Territories and raised in Edmonton, Carney also brings a Western Canadian background to his international standing as a graduate from Harvard and Oxford who went on to work at the massive investment firm Goldman Sachs before he entered the public service.
Powers noted, however, that the Conservatives are already playing the "out-of-touch elite card" against Carney, which he predicted would be the challenge if the former Bank of Canada governor entered the political fray.
"If we're living in a moment where populism needs to express itself, and Pierre Poilievre is the vehicle for that expression, even someone from the outside with Mark Carney's very appealing resumé and background may find it hard to win," Powers said.
Michelle Rempel Garner, a Conservative MP from Calgary, recently penned a column that argued Carney - if he came to replace Trudeau before the next election - would likely be fated to a short-lived prime ministership akin to the reign of Progressive Conservative Kim Campbell before the party's near-wipeout defeat in the 1993 election.
Solomon countered such analysis by pointing to examples of how long-ruling parties managed to stay in power after changing leaders before an election. That includes Christy Clark in British Columbia, and Kathleen Wynne in Ontario. "The idea that a change of leader can revivify a brand, there's lots of precedent on that," he said.
Of course, unless Trudeau changes his mind about running again, the leadership won't be available until after the next general election, at the earliest. But Carney is "not deaf" to the speculation about his ambitions, Solomon said. Like many people in politics, Carney has the personality of someone who wants a key responsibility when they jump in the game, he said.
If the captain's job comes up for grabs, why wouldn't he want to wear the "C"?
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Document 186 of 500

Is carbon pricing a politically feasible climate policy?: Experts suggest Liberals may be fighting a losing battle, advocate for new ways to cut emissions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-carbon-pricing-politically-feasible-climate/docview/3063005468/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+carbon+pricing+a+politically+feasible+climate+policy%3F%3A+Experts+suggest+Liberals+may+be+fighting+a+losing+battle%2C+advocate+for+new+ways+to+cut+emissions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA It was supposed to do the heavy lifting for Canada's greenhouse gas emissions targets.
And it was supposed to remain a major part of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's legacy, both at home and abroad - part of an urgent global push to fight climate change.
But instead of fulfilling those Liberal hopes, carbon pricing has become a significant political liability.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's crusade against the consumer carbon price and his promise to "axe the tax" should he win the next election has resonated with many Canadians amidst an affordability crisis.
The Tory leader has blamed the climate policy for driving up the cost of food and fuel, while dismissing or ignoring its purported benefits, including consumer rebates.
The government has struggled to respond to the Conservatives' attacks, despite the carbon price enjoying widespread support among economists.
Did the Liberals drop the ball?
Or was the policy always destined for failure?
Research suggests the Liberals may be fighting a losing battle, and some experts are urging policymakers to look for alternative policies to lower emissions, warning the threat of climate change is too dire to delay action.
"It's very hard to find places with high, economy-wide carbon prices that have not generated significant political backlash," said Matto Mildenberger, an assistant professor of political science at the University of California Santa Barbara.
"That leads political scientists like myself to have real reservations about how viable carbon pricing is as a short-term strategy to confront the climate crisis."
Consumers pay the cost of carbon pricing upfront in a very visible way, Mildenberger said. Its benefits are only enjoyed in the long run.
The federal government's Canada Carbon Rebate is designed to compensate voters for the financial burden. According to the parliamentary budget officer, eight out of 10 families receive more in rebates than they pay in carbon taxes.
But Mildenberger's research suggests the rebate is not as effective in shoring up public support as Liberals would hope.
One study analyzing public support for carbon pricing in Canada and Switzerland found people don't know about the rebates they're getting and tend to underestimate their value.
Another looked at the effect of rebates on public support for a carbon tax in the U.S. and Switzerland and found there was ultimately little impact.
"Our results indicate that, absent political messaging, rebates increase public support for carbon taxes in both countries by building support among lower income groups," the 2022 paper said.
"However, policy is always politicized, and when respondents are exposed to political messages about carbon pricing the effects associated with rebates are dampened or eliminated."
Mildenberger said it's safe to conclude rebates have not been shifting people's perceptions.
"People's partisan, ideological preferences dominate their perceptions of carbon pricing, much more so than the objective costs or benefits that come from the policy."
Proponents often blame the Liberal government for failing to effectively communicate the policy and the rebates to Canadians.
Mildenberger agreed the Liberals did not do a good sales job.
For example, they did not heed advocates' advice to send rebates out in cheques, he said - something that would have connected the money with the policy in a "tangible" way.
But Katya Rhodes, an assistant professor of public administration at the University of Victoria, said blaming communication on its own is an oversimplification of the challenge.
Rhodes said some of her studies show that the more information people are provided about complex climate policies, the more confused they get.
"It's really hard to be a politician when you introduce a carbon tax. Is it the ideal approach? I wouldn't do it if I were a politician."
Rhodes added that trust in government plays a significant role in the success or failure of the carbon tax, as seen in countries like Finland and Norway.
Economists say carbon pricing is the cheapest and most effective way to address climate change.
By putting a price on pollution, the government is not dictating how emissions should be lowered. Instead, it offers an incentive for polluters to invest in emissions-reducing technologies, they say.
It also incentivizes consumers to opt for goods and services that emit less greenhouse gases.
More than 300 economists signed an open letter in March supporting the consumer carbon price and trying to dispel misconceptions about the policy.
"I think there are many Canadians who say they care about climate change ... but they somehow think that we can reduce emissions without changing behaviour," said Christopher Ragan, director of McGill University's Max Bell School of Public Policy and one of the organizers behind the letter.
Mildenberger and Rhodes both said they recognize that the carbon tax is, theoretically speaking, the best choice for fighting climate change.
But both are advocating for governments to find other ways to reduce emissions because of how politically challenging it is.
Experts say carbon pricing that uses a cap-and-trade system like Quebec does might be more palatable because people don't see its direct cost.
Such systems set an upper limit on the amount of greenhouse gases an organization can emit, but allow them to purchase unused credits from other groups or businesses that have not used their full allowance.
However, that form of carbon pricing isn't politically foolproof, either.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford killed plans for a cap-and-trade system in 2018, arguing the policy would hurt businesses and raise costs.
Mildenberger is a proponent of U.S. President Joe Biden's approach, which relies heavily on government investments and subsidies in the green economy.
He said that puts a focus on the economic benefits of fighting climate change "while sidestepping the politics of taxes."
But while Canada has tried to keep up with the U.S. by rolling out a suite of investment tax credits, Rhodes said Canada cannot compete with the U.S.'s deep pockets.
Instead, she said Canada could lower emissions via flexible regulations, such as clean fuel standards.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Anti-carbon tax protesters attend a rally in Cochrane, Alta., in April. Experts suggest the Canada Carbon Rebate is not as effective in shoring up public support as Liberals would hope.Jeff McIntosh The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Nojoud Al Mallees The Canadian Press

Subject: Greenhouse gases; Climate policy; Politics; Climate change; Emissions testing

Document 187 of 500

Five things we learned from 'The Prince': Maher documents a turbulent time for Canada and the Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/five-things-we-learned-prince/docview/3063004996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+things+we+learned+from+%27The+Prince%27%3A+Maher+documents+a+turbulent+time+for+Canada+and+the+Liberals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new book on Justin Trudeau, "The Prince" by Stephen Maher, documents a turbulent time at the top of this country. Here are five takeaways from the story of his reign, amassed through stories that hit the headlines and drama behind the scenes.
1. Even loyal MPs and close friends of Trudeau's believe it's time for him to go, and told Maher as much in the book, although they did so anonymously. "Every MP will tell you that their closest, most long-standing Liberal supporters all think it's time," one Liberal MP was quoted as saying in late 2023.
"I think of him more as the leader for a time and I think the times require a different kind of leader now," says one close friend.
2. When Trudeau first met Gerald Butts at the McGill University debating club, he went by the name of "Jason Tremblay" so as not to draw attention to his famous lineage. Some news outlets had that little tidbit early after Trudeau became prime minister, but Maher's book reminds us of a time when Trudeau didn't even want to be called Justin or the son of a prime minister.
3. Mark Carney, the former governor of the central banks of Canada and the United Kingdom, was scared off running for the Liberals in the 2021 election because of the way he felt Trudeau's PMO has stuck a "shiv" in the career of former finance minister Bill Morneau. "Seeing Morneau being treated so shabbily seems to have given Carney pause, and he did not allow himself to be seduced. They spooked him by putting the shiv in."
4. Hanbury Strategy, a British data consultancy firm hired by the Conservatives for help in the 2021 election, flatly told leader Erin O'Toole and his entourage that their party's brand was "toxic" and that Canada was fundamentally a progressive country.
"They had no idea coming in," the 2021 national campaign manager, Fred DeLorey, told Maher. "They worked for conservative parties in Australia, U.K., mainland Europe. And we had such a toxic brand in our country. And they also identified, surprisingly to me, how progressive Canada is versus most European countries."
5. The ejection of Jody Wilson-Raybould and Jane Philpott from the Liberal caucus in 2019 was a drawn-out, messy affair and is often cast as Trudeau's imperious decision from on high. "The Prince" documents how the two dissenting ministers had lost support from much of the Liberal caucus, too. In recounting a "lively" Liberal caucus gathering, Maher reports that meeting included some name-calling of Wilson-Raybould and appeals for the two women to be "banished to the worst seats in the backbench."
CREDIT: Susan Delacourt National Columnist

Subject: Books; Prime ministers

Location: Canada; United Kingdom--UK

Document 188 of 500

How my snaps of a fumble changed Canadian history: Robert Stanfield caught a few passes, but one drop 50 years ago became immortal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-my-snaps-fumble-changed-canadian-history/docview/3063004866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+my+snaps+of+a+fumble+changed+Canadian+history%3A+Robert+Stanfield+caught+a+few+passes%2C+but+one+drop+50+years+ago+became+immortal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Hey, Doug - did you take that shot of Stanfield on the front of the Globe and Mail this morning?"
We were boarding the Progressive Conservative campaign bus outside the Hotel Vancouver early on May 31 during the 1974 federal election.
I was a photographer working for The Canadian Press out of Montreal, where I had been stationed the previous year. Early the day before, we had flown out of Halifax heading for a rally in Saskatoon and then overnight in Vancouver.
We had a refueling stop in North Bay and were allowed to deplane and stretch our legs on the tarmac.
Someone with the PC party brought out a football and started throwing it around.
I asked Robert Stanfield, the then-Opposition leader, if he was going to join in - and he suggested he might.
I put my Nikon F2 camera aside and joined the game.
A few minutes in, I saw Stanfield take off his suit coat and prepare to enter the fray.
I quickly grabbed my camera and fired off the full roll of 36 frames of him catching the football, throwing it around - and missing it, too.
When I finished the roll, I ran into the terminal and sent the film back to my office in Toronto, where it would be developed, made into prints and transmitted to news clients.
After the evening rally in Saskatoon, I made a quick call to see how the photos had turned out.
All they said was: "It's on the front of the Globe and Mail!"
After waking up in Vancouver, I went to the door of my hotel room and saw two pictures on the front of the Vancouver Province newspaper - one of Stanfield dropping the football, another of him catching it.
Then I saw the Globe and Mail headline: "A Political Fumble?"
On the bus, while I waited to catch hell from Stanfield's political aides, Charles Lynch of Southam News sat down in front of me and asked the question - if it had been my photo. I said yes.
"Trudeau just won the election," he said.
Then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau had won a large majority in 1968, but Stanfield had given him a run for his money in 1972, cutting the Liberals down to minority control in Parliament.
Lynch wasn't wrong. Stanfield lost, and Trudeau formed another majority government.
Shortly after the election, Stanfield announced his intention to retire. He would go on to serve as the Opposition leader until 1976, when Joe Clark took the party helm.
When Stanfield spoke at the press gallery dinner in 1975, I wasn't in the room - but a friend told me that he gave the funniest speech, in which he called me out by name.
That year, I won the National Newspaper Award for best political photo.
More than a decade later, I got ahold of the ex-politician's phone number. I dialed and expected a secretary to answer. But Stanfield picked up the phone.
I got nervous. I blurted out my name. I asked the question I'd been wondering about for years: would he sign a copy of the football photo?
It was the most pregnant pause I had ever heard.
Then: "Ah yes," he said. "You won an award for that, and I got nothing. Unbelievable!"
We met for lunch a few weeks later, and he signed a copy: "Doug - I should have taken off my tie."
After the meal, we headed out into a grey, drizzling day and took a few pictures together. Stanfield asked the photographer if we could take one more, on the count of three.
On three, Stanfield put the hook of his umbrella around my neck and gave it a playful tug, and we laughed.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Then-Progressive Conservative leader Robert Stanfield grimaces as he drops a football while campaigning in North Bay, Ont., on May 30, 1974.DOUG BALL THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Doug Ball The Canadian Press;

Subject: Football

Title: How my snaps of a fumble changed Canadian history:   Robert Stanfield caught a few passes, but one drop 50 years ago became immortal

Document 189 of 500

We have only ourselves to blame for the chaos of delivery e-bikes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-have-only-ourselves-blame-chaos-delivery-e/docview/3063004854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+have+only+ourselves+to+blame+for+the+chaos+of+delivery+e-bikes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Everyone's talking about delivery people zooming around on e-bikes, riding and parking on sidewalks, blocking paths and overloading GO trains. If you listen to all the complaints, they're a dangerous scourge. Maybe the worst.
Yet they are there because we want and demand they exist. Their annoying presence in the city has profoundly changed the civic landscape, but they've also shifted the moral topography, too.
Tech people like to talk of "disruptions" - of the status quo and old economics. Think how ride-hailing apps shocked the taxi cab industry. Much disruption happens just out of view, or is easy to ignore in our peripheral vision, like the armies of precarious workers fulfilling our online orders that miraculously arrive days, hours or even minutes after being placed.
A line from "Steer Your Way," a poem and song by Leonard Cohen released just before his 2016 death comes to mind: "As he died to make men holy, let us die to make things cheap." Computers and apps have made things appear with a few clicks, as if humans aren't the ones fulfilling all these orders, as if there isn't a bigger cost.
The consequences are here, though. Delivery people are precarious, poorly paid workers impossible to ignore now, like during end-of-day scenes at Union Station. Delivery riders with their e-bikes line up for trains back to homes in relatively cheaper locations like Brampton. They overload passenger cars that already poorly accommodate just two permitted bikes. There have been cases of e-bike batteries catching fire, but they're an expensive purchase for a poorly paid job. Is it any surprise low-cost, low-quality bikes proliferate?
Do we ban them, punishing delivery people, or do we create train carriages just for bikes like they do in Denmark and other places?
The sidewalk situation is worse, where the hierarchy of road users starts with pedestrians, then cyclists and ends with motorists. The latter pose the greatest risk to life and limb, yet have the most protection. Each has to look out for the more vulnerable. That's the deal, in theory. Pedestrians, the most vulnerable users of city streets, should travel without fear and have a clear path ahead.
I get annoyed at delivery people who ride on the sidewalks, even pointing to the road at times, feeling guilty because the road is dangerous. I hope over time they'll become better riders. Some are new to it and don't know the rules. However, I've heard stories of delivery people being assaulted for their sidewalk transgressions, indicating the situation is escalating and must be fixed.
Empathy is needed all around. They may be annoying but the job is hard, working in the rain, snow and whenever we'd rather stay at home. There are abusive customers, and now a public who seems to dislike their very sight.
Another way of looking at this is to hate the game, not the player. The game here is partly the companies who created this situation, paying so little that delivery folk need to rush and take shortcuts to maximize profit.
Should we punish the players, or require the companies properly train them, just as old school taxi, courier or trucking companies do, and also be responsible when rules are transgressed?
I say companies are only part of the game because everyone who has ever received a delivery by bike, which is an awful lot of people, are also "the game." Without them - us - the companies would be out of business.
All of this makes arguments for and against cycling infrastructure different now. Putting aside the fact all members of society deserve to be safe, now everyone who has received a delivery in this manner is also morally implicated in how it got to their door. Even if they don't ride a bike themselves, there's now a personal connection to safe infrastructure.
As bike lanes extend into parts of Toronto that didn't have them, familiar and tired old protests have started. In Etobicoke a group absurdly named "Balance On Bloor" is opposing, as if new safe infrastructure isn't starting to finally add some balance. Amid other specious claims, they worry about the "deterioration of pedestrian experience." One way to get cyclists off the sidewalk - a bad pedestrian experience - is to provide safe bike lanes.
Even local Liberal MP Yvan Baker devoted an entire newsletter against the lanes, demonstrating it isn't just Doug Ford Conservatives who meddle in municipal affairs where they don't belong.
Before Baker lectures residents of Toronto about bike lanes, he should ask himself why his federal Liberal caucus isn't championing Toronto transit funding. The only way to ease congestion is with fewer cars and getting people on bikes or transit.
Another musical note comes to mind in all this: the 1987 album "Give me convenience OR give me death" by the punk band Dead Kennedys. Safe infrastructure for all helps prevent death and injury. Are those opposing it willing to give up the convenience, detaching themselves from personal moral culpability? Or are they OK with the risk others take for their convenience?
CAPTION: Delivery riders are precarious, poorly paid workers who have to work on our city's dangerous roads, Shawn Micallef writes. Everyone who has ever received a delivery by bike, which is an awful lot of people, share responsibility for the havoc they create on our roads and sidewalks.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Infrastructure; Walkways; Electric bicycles; Pedestrians; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

An 11-hour wait in emergency and Ford opts for beer in corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/11-hour-wait-emergency-ford-opts-beer-corner/docview/3062889260/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=An+11-hour+wait+in+emergency+and+Ford+opts+for+beer+in+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
I took an elderly friend to the ER two weeks ago. She'd called her doctor, who was too busy to see her, and my friend was worried about her numb arm. Eleven hours she waited to be seen, have blood taken and X-rays. But wow, beer in corner stores! We are so happy that our provincial government is spending in excess of $225 million to make THAT happen.
Mary Sullivan, Lindsay
Another misguided example of our 'race to the bottom' premier
Premier Doug Ford is about to pay the international beer conglomerates that own The Beer Store $225 million of our tax dollars so he can get beer and wine into corner stores faster so he can win an election because, by wasting this money, his approval rating will undoubtedly go up. All this while he nefariously starves our health care and education systems. I don't need help understanding the misguided priorities of our "race to the bottom" premier. After so many years of this foolishness, I have grown to understand him. But, in what bizarro world does he get away with and get rewarded for the constant wasting and foolish spending of our tax dollars? Oh, Ontario, for thee I shed a tear.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Ford is making it easier for Ontarians to become alcoholics
This is another example of Premier Doug Ford trying to get the populist vote and ignoring reality and being blind to the tragedy unfolding. Alcoholism is a real problem in our society and easy access at any time of day will only exasperate the societal problems. This legislation is a huge mistake. Rather than spending time and money on real problems such as our nurses and doctors and housing and teachers with excess class sizes, our premier is mistakenly focusing on easy wins. I am disgusted with a lazy premier suffering with myopic vision.
Richard Bino, Singhampton
We could use that beer money for health care or housing
In his typically impatient style, Premier Doug Ford will pay The Beer Store $225 million to achieve this goal sooner than previously announced. That $225 million could provide enormous support for our beleaguered health-care system and our housing affordability crisis, just to name two issues. I just wish that Ontarians would realize the harm being done by this populist premier. I read every day about suffering Ontarians who cannot afford the necessities of life and, yet this enormous amount of money will be used for frivolous purposes. People have much more serious concerns than easier access to booze, increasing speed limits and the return of paper bags to the LCBO. Ford is harmful to our collective health and to our pocketbooks.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga
Use the $600,000 for addiction and mental-health services rather than alcohol
I find it ironic that Premier Doug Ford during his announcement on easier access to alcohol, the most abused drug in the western world, doubled down on his opposition to the decriminalization of other drugs. Perhaps it would make more sense to leave things as they are and use this taxpayer money to provide people with better access to addiction and mental health services rather than alcohol.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
We are losing everything that makes this province livable
Many Ontarians are struggling to afford food and safe and secure housing and can't find a family doctor. Our hospitals are struggling with nursing recruitment and retention and there are not enough long-term-care beds in safe homes that optimize well-being. Meanwhile, the Doug Ford government is spending $600 million taxpayer dollars to make booze available in corner stores earlier than originally promised. This shows where this government's priorities lie. Wake up Ontario before we lose everything that makes this province livable.
Mary Lou Walker, Toronto

Ford has the feds financing his re-election bidTory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call, May 27
Premier Doug Ford is going to pour $225 million taxpayer dollars into The Beer Store (maybe more, but let's believe his fantasy number) to fulfil a previous election promise. And he is gearing up to call an election that will cost $155 million. Meanwhile, after months of dispute, Ontario and the federal government have reached a $357-million housing agreement, unlocking that amount of federal funding for Ontario housing.
Let's see now, 225 plus 155 equals 380. 380 minus 357 equals 23.
Hmmm. Looks like the feds are financing an Ontario election for Ford, though he might have to take another $23 million out of long-term care or education or any of those other less important files to make up the difference.
Graeme Elliott, Toronto

Document 191 of 500

Doug Ford's thirst for power will have the rest of us crying in our beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-thirst-power-will-have-rest-us-crying/docview/3062889244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+thirst+for+power+will+have+the+rest+of+us+crying+in+our+beer&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with Doug Ford's latest boondoggle over beer is that it amounts to double jeopardy.
First, taxpayers and teetotallers alike will be soaked by as much as $1 billion right off the top, according to opposition critics and industry analysts.
Second, drinkers are destined to be hosed by higher prices as Ontario deregulates - and disaggregates - the distribution of beer in this province.
Which means the premier is giving the brewing giants taxpayers' money so they can then sell less beer to corner stores at a higher price point. Increased costs for brewers, means more expensive beer for consumers.
How much more is hard to know. To understand the uncertainty, you first have to comprehend Ford's core agenda - and how he could have avoided taking on so much risk.
His objective isn't a better form of beer distribution. It's disruption for disruption's sake.
The premier opposes whatever his predecessors do and obsesses about what a populist should supposedly do to please the people.
A decade ago, I wrote a series of columns investigating the iron grip of The Beer Store on beer distribution, together with the government-owned LCBO. In fact, The Beer Store has always been controlled by Ontario's biggest brewers, who gave priority of placement to their own products at the expense of smaller craft brewers.
In 2017, the last Liberal government came up with an intelligent compromise solution - phasing out The Beer Store's stranglehold over the next decade while allowing supermarkets to get a piece of the action if they paid a licensing fee for the privilege. It turned out to be a practical, transitional plan that would take us to the end of 2026.
But in 2018, Ford won power by promising "buck-a-beer" and "beer in corner stores."
The first slogan was based on an unsustainable and uneconomical model that promptly went flat. The second promise was equally unaffordable, because it required ripping up a binding contract with The Beer Store - and related contracts with big box stores and supermarkets - merely to appease the convenience store sector.
Over time, Ford came to realize the infeasibility of that implausible promise, which would have triggered costly litigation amounting to as much as $1 billion in legal penalties and fees. He waited, and waited, until he could wait no longer.
Last week, Ford announced that the 10-year deal with the big brewers would be wound down ahead of schedule. But to get his way, we all have to pay.
There's $225 million in transition compensation to The Beer Store, plus another $375 million to rebate service fees that it pays the LCBO. The opposition Liberals argued this week that a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains could total more than $150 million in lost revenues.
If you add in the forgone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (typically charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table. Which adds up to a "billion-dollar beer boondoggle," according to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Ford insists the Liberal calculation is unfounded (the government added later that it will in fact get $9 million in licensing fees), and counters that the existing beer framework is costing the economy untold millions in unrealized savings, notably through increased competition - though he provided no data to back his claim. For what it's worth, industry analysts told the Star's Robert Benzie this week that the Liberal analysis appears to be well grounded.
And as the Star's Josh Rubin reported, there is now reason to believe that supermarkets may be rethinking their dedication to the distribution of beer. I've long argued for giving priority to supermarkets over so-called corner stores (no longer on every corner) that tend to charge inconveniently high prices as a premium for proximity.
Now, we may lose supermarket distribution while gaining more chain convenience stores selling a smaller selection of beer at higher prices. Which means higher costs for taxpayers and drinkers both.
We can't be certain how this will play out over the long term, but there is one absolute certainty over the short term: Ford could have hedged all that risk, avoided all that cost, eliminated all that exposure, by merely waiting out the original contract with the Beer Store until it lapsed in late 2026.
Net cost for waiting? Zero.
Why not wait? The premier has persuaded himself that a promise is a promise, that beer in corner stores is popular in the polls (as he boasted to reporters), and that delivering on distribution somehow clears the decks for an early election, possibly next spring, while he is riding high in public opinion surveys.
A premier who doesn't drink seems so hungry for power that he will spare no expense to make beer more expensive at the cash register and more costly for the treasury. Unfortunately this isn't about affordability, it's about electability.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Subject: Licenses; Prices; Beer; Convenience stores; Cost control; Public opinion surveys; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Cost control Public opinion surveys Supermarkets

Title: Doug Ford's thirst for power will have the rest of us crying in our beer

Document 192 of 500

Delivery riders on e-bikes are swarming the city's sidewalks. Guess who's to blame?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/delivery-riders-on-e-bikes-are-swarming-citys/docview/3062889200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Delivery+riders+on+e-bikes+are+swarming+the+city%27s+sidewalks.+Guess+who%27s+to+blame%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Everyone's talking about delivery people zooming around on e-bikes, riding and parking on sidewalks, blocking paths and overloading GO trains. If you listen to all the complaints, they're a dangerous scourge. Maybe the worst.
Yet they are there because we want and demand they exist. Their annoying presence in the city has profoundly changed the civic landscape, but they've also shifted the moral topography too.
Tech people like to talk of "disruptions" - of the status quo and old economics. Think how ride-hailing apps shocked the taxi cab industry. Much disruption happens just out of view, or is easy to ignore in our peripheral vision, like the armies of precarious workers fulfilling our online orders that miraculously arrive days, hours or even minutes after being placed.
A line from "Steer Your Way," a poem and song by Leonard Cohen released just before his 2016 death comes to mind: "As he died to make men holy, let us die to make things cheap." Computers and apps have made things appear with a few clicks, as if humans aren't the ones fulfilling all these orders, as if there isn't a bigger cost.
The consequences are here, though. Delivery people are precarious, poorly paid workers impossible to ignore now, like during end-of-day scenes at Union Station. Delivery riders with their e-bikes line up for trains back to homes in relatively cheaper locations like Brampton. They overload passenger cars that already poorly accommodate just two permitted bikes. There have been cases of e-bike batteries catching fire, but they're an expensive purchase for a poorly paid job. Is it any surprise low-cost, low-quality bikes proliferate?
Do we ban them, punishing delivery people, or do we create train carriages just for bikes like they do in Denmark and other places?
The sidewalk situation is worse, where the hierarchy of road users starts with pedestrians, then cyclists and ends with motorists. The latter pose the greatest risk to life and limb, yet have the most protection. Each has to look out for the more vulnerable. That's the deal, in theory. Pedestrians, the most vulnerable users of city streets, should travel without fear and have a clear path ahead.
I get annoyed at delivery people who ride on the sidewalks, even pointing to the road at times, feeling guilty because the road is dangerous. I hope over time they'll become better riders. Some are new to it and don't know the rules. However, I've heard stories of delivery people being assaulted for their sidewalk transgressions, indicating the situation is escalating and must be fixed.
Empathy is needed all around. They may be annoying but the job is hard, working in the rain, snow and whenever we'd rather stay at home. There are abusive customers, and now a public who seems to dislike their very sight.
Another way of looking at this is to hate the game, not the player. The game here is partly the companies who created this situation, paying so little that delivery folk need to rush and take shortcuts to maximize profit.
Should we punish the players, or require the companies properly train them, just as old school taxi, courier or trucking companies do, and also be responsible when rules are transgressed?
I say companies are only part of the game because everyone who has ever received a delivery by bike, which is an awful lot of people, are also "the game." Without them - us - the companies would be out of business.
All of this makes arguments for and against cycling infrastructure different now. Putting aside the fact all members of society deserve to be safe, now everyone who has received a delivery in this manner is also morally implicated in how it got to their door. Even if they don't ride a bike themselves, there's now a personal connection to safe infrastructure.
As bike lanes extend into parts of Toronto that didn't have them, familiar and tired old protests have started. In Etobicoke a group absurdly named "Balance On Bloor" is opposing, as if new safe infrastructure isn't starting to finally add some balance. Amid other specious claims, they worry about the "deterioration of pedestrian experience." One way to get cyclists off the sidewalk - a bad pedestrian experience - is to provide safe bike lanes.
Even local Liberal MP Yvan Baker devoted an entire newsletter against the lanes, demonstrating it isn't just Doug Ford Conservatives who meddle in municipal affairs where they don't belong.
Before Baker lectures residents of Toronto about bike lanes, he should ask himself why his federal Liberal caucus isn't championing Toronto transit funding. The only way to ease congestion is with fewer cars and getting people on bikes or transit.
Another musical note comes to mind in all this: the 1987 album "Give me convenience OR give me death" by the punk band Dead Kennedys. Safe infrastructure for all helps prevent death and injury. Are those opposing it willing to give up the convenience, detaching themselves from personal moral culpability? Or are they OK with the risk others take for their convenience?
CAPTION: Delivery people are precarious, poorly paid workers who have to work on our city's dangerous roads.

Subject: Infrastructure; Walkways; Electric bicycles; Pedestrians; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure
