Document 193 of 500

Some Liberals think Mark Carney could succeed Justin Trudeau. We talked to those who think he'd be a great leader - and others who roll their eyes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/some-liberals-think-mark-carney-could-succeed/docview/3062889086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Liberals+think+Mark+Carney+could+succeed+Justin+Trudeau.+We+talked+to+those+who+think+he%27d+be+a+great+leader+-+and+others+who+roll+their+eyes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The way former Liberal cabinet minister Catherine McKenna sees it, change is coming. Either her party supplies it, or they lose power.
She's quick to say that doesn't necessarily mean Justin Trudeau needs to go, though she observes that what's on offer "clearly" isn't resonating with voters.
But there is someone else - someone whose name keeps swirling around the never-ending carousel of speculation about the prime minister's future. Someone of "real substance" who, according to McKenna, understands the big challenges of our era and would stack up well against Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, the Liberals' archrival of the moment.
That someone is Mark Carney.
"I think he'd be a good candidate," McKenna said, when asked about the entrenched impression in political circles that the former central banker has eyes on the Liberal leadership.
"Someone like Carney is a very attractive option. And - as a contrast to Pierre Poilievre - he is experienced; he has an even temperament; he has had a life outside of politics; he has a plan. I think those are really important things."
It's hard to pin down what is going on with Carney. A former civil servant and head of central banks in two G7 countries, Canada and the United Kingdom, the 59-year-old Carney has tied his wagon to the Liberal horse. With his financier's mien, hockey references, and economics-oriented advocacy for climate action, he is not shying away from the public eye. Through Canada 2020, a Liberal-linked think tank situated just off Parliament Hill, he has a platform to expound on domestic Canadian policy from the sidelines of the political arena. Just this week, he entered the political money game when he appeared as a "special guest" at an evening fundraiser for Anna Gainey, a Liberal MP in Montreal and former party president who was part of the core group that helped Trudeau rise to the leadership and win power in 2015. He'll reprise the role on June 11, when he's slated to headline another fundraiser in Toronto for Ontario Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie.
All this has been grist for the rumour mill inside the Ottawa bubble. There was a flurry of political commentary about his perceived ambitions in recent days. Ever eager to poke at Trudeau, the Conservatives have taken to calling Carney "the next Liberal leader." But there are signs the Tories are taking him seriously, just in case. In April, Poilievre debuted a new nickname in Parliament, when he lampooned him as "Carbon Tax Carney." Other Conservatives have tried to paint Carney as an elitist, a would-be politician with "Bay Street buddies" who plans to descend from his "ivory tower" to take over the Liberal party.
Carney did not agree to an interview for this story. But some who count themselves as close to him said they're not aware of any serious organization that is getting staged for Carney to win the leadership. Whatever is happening appears less tangible than that, pre-positioning for a race that is still only hypothetical. Trudeau, after all, insists at every turn that he's not going anywhere. Carney has said he supports the prime minister and expects him to lead the Liberals in the next election.
Still. Everyone knows Trudeau can't stick around forever.
"I know for sure people constantly approach (Carney) ... They want him to do it," said Evan Solomon, the former CTV host and longtime friend of Carney's who now works with the Eurasia Group, a geopolitical consulting firm in New York City.
As Solomon explained, Carney is agreeing to help the Liberals and taking opportunities to deliver speeches, but is not "strategically weaponizing" the interest for an imminent leadership run.
"I think it's a much softer path. It's not hard organizing yet," Solomon said of Liberal leadership posturing in general. But, he added, if the leadership opened up and the interest kept growing, Carney would likely go for it.
"If an opportunity came up," Solomon said, "of course he would probably take that opportunity."
Along with McKenna, there are some Liberals willing to publicly express support for Carney, even if - with national polls consistently suggesting their party is en route to a harsh defeat at the hands of Poilievre and the Conservatives - they stop short of declaring it's time for Trudeau to go.
Responding to a query about his potential support for a Carney leadership bid, former Liberal justice minister David Lametti, who was turfed from Trudeau's cabinet before he resigned his Montreal seat early this year, replied by email: "Mark is an old friend of mine, and I have always thought highly of his intellect and his leadership skills. That is really all I am comfortable saying right now."
Others who remain in Trudeau's caucus are more critical of Carney. Some question his commitment to the party, given that he's been publicly Liberal for at least three years but has never put his name on a ballot.
"I think he should run for a seat in Parliament," one MP told the Star. "Come get in the trenches."
Another MP described Carney as smart and capable, but concluded - based on his manner of public speaking and his lack of experience on the front-lines of partisan politics - that he is not ready to step in and lead the Liberals. This MP said there is a perception in caucus that Carney only wants to jump into politics if he can do so as party leader.
"You can't just 'Ignatieff' this thing," the MP said, referring to the ex-Liberal leader Michael Ignatieff who entered politics after an academic career abroad and spearheaded the worst campaign defeat in the party's history in the 2011 election.
A third MP, who like the others agreed to speak about Carney on condition of anonymity, described him as the "love child of Bill Morneau and Michael Ignatieff" - another reference to the failed leader in 2011, combined with a nod to Trudeau's finance minister from 2015 to 2020, a corporate scion in Toronto who has since criticized the Liberal government for its high levels of spending.
"Honestly, (Carney)'s not much of a topic of conversation, other than to roll our eyes," the MP said. "If you're interested in being a future leader of a political party, it's best to put your name on a ballot and do the work."
McKenna, the former Liberal cabinet minister, rejected the comparison between Carney and Ignatieff as unfair. She cited Carney's years of experience as a public servant in Canada, and said his time abroad - including as governor of the Bank of England - is an asset, not a disadvantage, in an era of climate change and tensions between great powers. "He understands the world," she said of Carney.
For Tim Powers, a former Conservative adviser and chairman of Summa Strategies, Carney is a "wonderful human being" with an "impressive pedigree." He said: "Canadians, regardless of party, would be lucky if he entered the political arena."
Powers also argued Carney could craft a "compelling" narrative for more centrist voters, given his past in the upper ranks of the federal government when Conservative Jim Flaherty was finance minister, and how he was appointed governor of the Bank of Canada when Stephen Harper was prime minister. Born in the Northwest Territories and raised in Edmonton, Carney also brings a Western Canadian background to his international standing as a graduate from Harvard and Oxford who went on to work at the massive investment firm Goldman Sachs before he entered the public service.
Powers noted, however, that the Conservatives are already playing the "out-of-touch elite card" against Carney, which he predicted would be the challenge if the former Bank of Canada governor entered the political fray.
"If we're living in a moment where populism needs to express itself, and Pierre Poilievre is the vehicle for that expression, even someone from the outside with Mark Carney's very appealing resume and background may find it hard to win," Powers said.
Michelle Rempel Garner, a Conservative MP from Calgary, recently penned a column that argued Carney - if he came to replace Trudeau before the next election - would likely be fated to a short-lived prime ministership akin to the reign of Progressive Conservative Kim Campbell before the party's near wipeout defeat in the 1993 election.
Solomon, the former CTV host and Carney's friend, countered such analysis by pointing to examples of how long-ruling parties managed to stay in power after changing leaders before an election. That includes Christy Clark in British Columbia, and Kathleen Wynne in Ontario. "The idea that a change of leader can revivify a brand, there's lots of precedent on that," he said.
Of course, unless Trudeau changes his mind about running again, the leadership won't be available until after the next general election, at the earliest. But Carney is "not deaf" to the speculation about his ambitions, Solomon said. Like many people in politics, Carney has the personality of someone who wants a key responsibility when they jump in the game, he said.
If the captain's job comes up for grabs, why wouldn't he want to wear the "C"?
CAPTION: Mark Carney during the the Third "1+6" Roundtable Dialogue held by Chinese Premier Li Keqiang at Diaoyutai State Guesthouse on Nov. 6, 2018 in Beijing, China.Then-Governor of the Bank of England Mark Carney poses for photographs with a hockey stick as he represents the Canadian All-Stars team before the start of an exhibition hockey game against the TD Maple Leafs during a "Canada 150" celebration in Trafalgar Square, London, on July 1, 2017.

Subject: Conservatism; Political leadership; State elections; Central banks; Governors; Voters; Prime ministers

Why is beer Ford's focus?: Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24 Tory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call, May 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-beer-fords-focus/docview/3062846033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+beer+Ford%27s+focus%3F%3A+Doug+Ford+says+beer+and+wine+are+coming+to+Ontario+corner+stores%2C+May+24+Tory+insiders+say+fear+of+a+Pierre+Poilievre+victory+has+Doug+Ford+considering+an+early+election+call%2C+May+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
I took an elderly friend to the ER two weeks ago. She'd called her doctor, who was too busy to see her, and my friend was worried about her numb arm. Eleven hours she waited to be seen, have blood taken and X-rays. But wow, beer in corner stores! We are so happy that our provincial government is spending in excess of $225 million to make THAT happen.
Mary Sullivan, Lindsay, Ont.
Another example
of our 'race to the bottom' premier
Premier Doug Ford is about to pay the international beer conglomerates that own the Beer Store $225 million of our tax dollars so he can get beer and wine into corner stores faster so he can win an election because, by wasting this money, his approval rating will undoubtedly go up. All this while he nefariously starves our health care and education systems. I don't need help understanding the misguided priorities of our "race to the bottom" premier. After so many years of this foolishness, I have grown to understand him. But, in what bizarro world does he get away with and get rewarded for the constant wasting and foolish spending of our tax dollars? Oh, Ontario, for thee I shed a tear.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, Ont.
Ford is making it easier for us to become alcoholics
This is another example of Premier Doug Ford trying to get the populist vote and ignoring reality and being blind to the tragedy unfolding. Alcoholism is a real problem in our society and easy access at any time of day will only exasperate the societal problems. This legislation is a huge mistake. Rather than spending time and money on real problems such as our nurses and doctors and housing and teachers with excess class sizes, our premier is mistakenly focusing on easy wins. I am disgusted with a lazy premier suffering with myopic vision.
Richard Bino, Singhampton, Ont.
We could use that beer money for health or housing
In his typically impatient style, Premier Doug Ford will pay the Beer Store $225 million to achieve this goal sooner than previously announced. That $225 million could provide enormous support for our beleaguered health-care system and our housing affordability crisis, just to name two issues. I just wish that Ontarians would realize the harm being done by this populist premier.
I read every day about suffering Ontarians who cannot afford the necessities of life and, yet this enormous amount of money will be used for frivolous purposes. People have much more serious concerns than easier access to booze, increasing speed limits and the return of paper bags to the LCBO. Ford is harmful to our collective health and to our pocketbooks.
Pam Tomasevic, Mississauga
I find it ironic that Premier Doug Ford during his announcement on easier access to alcohol, the most abused drug in the western world, doubled down on his opposition to the decriminalization of other drugs.
Perhaps it would make more sense to leave things as they are and use this taxpayer money to provide people with better access to addiction and mental health services rather than alcohol.
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
We are losing
what makes this province livable
Many Ontarians are struggling to afford food and safe and secure housing and can't find a family doctor. Our hospitals are struggling with nursing recruitment and retention and there are not enough long-term-care beds in safe homes that optimize well-being. Meanwhile, the Doug Ford government is spending $600 million of taxpayer money to make booze available in corner stores earlier than originally promised. This shows where this government's priorities lie. Wake up Ontario before we lose everything that makes this province livable.
Mary Lou Walker, Toronto
Ford has Ottawa financing his re-election bid
Tory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call, May 27
Premier Doug Ford is going to pour $225 million taxpayer dollars into the Beer Store (maybe more, but let's believe his fantasy number) to fulfil a previous election promise. And he is gearing up to call an election that will cost $155 million. Meanwhile, after months of dispute, Ontario and the federal government have reached a $357-million housing agreement, unlocking that amount of federal funding for Ontario housing.
Let's see now, 225 plus 155 equals 380. 380 minus 357 equals 23.
Hmmm. Looks like the feds are financing an Ontario election for Ford, though he might have to take another $23 million out of long-term care or education or any of those other less important files to make up the difference.
Graeme Elliott, Toronto
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy and Attorney General Doug Downey announce that convenience stores will be able to sell beer and wine in September at a Toronto convenience store last week.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star

Document 195 of 500

Ford's thirst for power hurts taxpayers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-thirst-power-hurts-taxpayers/docview/3062846009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+thirst+for+power+hurts+taxpayers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The trouble with Doug Ford's latest boondoggle over beer is that it amounts to double jeopardy.
First, taxpayers and teetotallers alike will be soaked by as much as $1 billion right off the top, according to opposition critics and industry analysts.
Second, drinkers are destined to be hosed by higher prices as Ontario deregulates - and disaggregates - the distribution of beer in this province.
Which means the premier is giving the brewing giants taxpayers' money so they can then sell less beer to corner stores at a higher price point. Increased costs for brewers, means more expensive beer for consumers.
How much more is hard to know. To understand the uncertainty, you first have to comprehend Ford's core agenda - and how he could have avoided taking on so much risk.
His objective isn't a better form of beer distribution. It's disruption for disruption's sake.
The premier opposes whatever his predecessors do and obsesses about what a populist should supposedly do to please the people.
A decade ago, I wrote a series of columns investigating the iron grip of the Beer Store on beer distribution, together with the government-owned LCBO. In fact, the Beer Store has always been controlled by Ontario's biggest brewers, who gave priority of placement to their own products at the expense of smaller craft brewers.
In 2017, the last Liberal government came up with an intelligent compromise solution - phasing out the Beer Store's stranglehold over the next decade while allowing supermarkets to get a piece of the action if they paid a licensing fee for the privilege. It turned out to be a practical, transitional plan that would take us to the end of 2026.
But in 2018, Ford won power by promising "buck-a-beer" and "beer in corner stores."
The first slogan was based on an unsustainable and uneconomical model that promptly went flat. The second promise was equally unaffordable, because it required ripping up a binding contract with the Beer Store - and related contracts with big box stores and supermarkets - merely to appease the convenience store sector.
Over time, Ford came to realize the infeasibility of that implausible promise, which would have triggered costly litigation amounting to as much as $1 billion in legal penalties and fees. He waited, and waited, until he could wait no longer.
Last week, Ford announced that the 10-year deal with the big brewers would be wound down ahead of schedule. But to get his way, we all have to pay.
There's $225 million in transition compensation to the Beer Store, plus another $375 million to rebate service fees that it pays the LCBO. The opposition Liberals argued this week that a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains could total more than $150 million in lost revenues.
If you add in the forgone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (typically charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table. Which adds up to a "billion-dollar beer boondoggle," according to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.
Ford insists the Liberal calculation is unfounded (the government added later that it will in fact get $9 million in licensing fees), and counters that the existing beer framework is costing the economy untold millions in unrealized savings, notably through increased competition - though he provided no data to back his claim.
For what it's worth, industry analysts told the Star's Robert Benzie this week that the Liberal analysis appears to be well grounded.
And as the Star's Josh Rubin reported, there is now reason to believe that supermarkets may be rethinking their dedication to the distribution of beer. I've long argued for giving priority to supermarkets over so-called corner stores (no longer on every corner) that tend to charge inconveniently high prices as a premium for proximity.
Now, we may lose supermarket distribution while gaining more chain convenience stores selling a smaller selection of beer at higher prices.
Which means higher costs for taxpayers and drinkers both.
We can't be certain how this will play out over the long term, but there is one absolute certainty over the short term: Ford could have hedged all that risk, avoided all that cost, eliminated all that exposure, by merely waiting out the original contract with the Beer Store until it lapsed in late 2026.
Net cost for waiting? Zero.
Why not wait? The premier has persuaded himself that a promise is a promise, that beer in corner stores is popular in the polls (as he boasted to reporters) and that delivering on distribution somehow clears the decks for an early election, possibly next spring, while he is riding high in public opinion surveys.
A premier who doesn't drink seems so hungry for power that he will spare no expense to make beer more expensive at the cash register and more costly for the treasury.
Unfortunately this isn't about affordability, it's about electability.
CAPTION: Last week, Premier Doug Ford announced that the 10-year deal with the big brewers would be wound down ahead of schedule. But to get his way, Martin Regg Cohn writes, we all have to pay.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Document 196 of 500

Serial killer dies after prison assault: After 11 days on life-support, Pickton succumbs to injuries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/serial-killer-dies-after-prison-assault/docview/3062845272/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Serial+killer+dies+after+prison+assault%3A+After+11+days+on+life-support%2C+Pickton+succumbs+to+injuries&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of Canada's most notorious serial killers, whose arrest made international headlines and brought the issue of missing and murdered Indigenous women in this country into the collective consciousness, has died.
After 11 days on life-support and being placed in a medically induced coma by doctors at a Quebec hospital, Robert Pickton died on Friday, according to a release from Correctional Service Canada.
The 74-year-old suffered fatal wounds during an assault by another prisoner at Port-Cartier Institution in Quebec on May 19. He was reportedly speared by another prisoner in an unprovoked attack with a broom, although authorities have not confirmed details of the assault.
His alleged attacker, a 51-year-old prisoner who has not been publicly identified, remains in custody. As of Friday, he had not been charged.
"It probably won't happen for several weeks because there's no hurry to do that because he's already in jail," said Sgt. Hugues Beaulieu, a spokesperson for the Sûreté du Québec.
"We will finish our investigation, and then we will give it to the Crown prosecutor and then they will charge him."
Beaulieu said the inmate was in isolation after the attack, but confirmed he is now out.
Authorities had also been waiting to see if Pickton would recover from the wounds he sustained. Prison staff were not involved in the assault and Federal Minister of Public Safety Dominic LeBlanc said following the attack that Correctional Service Canada would be conducting its own investigation. Inquiries are usually held when an inmate dies in custody, but the findings are often not made public.
The release said that Pickton's next of kin has been notified, and that the investigation into how he died would continue.
"The safety and security of our institutions remains paramount for us. We are launching a Board of Investigation (BOI) into the assault that took place. The investigation will examine all of the facts and circumstances surrounding the assault, including whether policies and protocols were followed. It will also identify any recommendations and corrective measures, as needed," said the release.
It also noted the effect of the news, and what it might have on victim's families.
"We are mindful that this offender's case has had a devastating impact on communities in British Columbia and across the country, including Indigenous Peoples, victims and their families. Our thoughts are with them."
Robert William (Willie) Pickton was born Oct. 24, 1949, to a family of pig farmers in Port Coquitlam, B.C., just east of Vancouver. He, along with his brother, David, inherited the family business, and Robert eventually used the family property to dispose of his victims.
He is considered to be one of the most prolific serial killers in Canadian history, and the farm became one of its largest crime scenes. Heavy equipment was brought in to search 380,000 cubic yards of land looking for human remains, with over 200,000 DNA samples collected. The investigation was estimated to have cost nearly $70 million by the end of 2003.
Pickton was initially arrested in 2002, after a worker informed police of illegal guns on the property. The subsequent search found weapons and evidence which led to a second warrant as part of B.C.'s missing women investigation.
Pickton was convicted of six counts of second-degree murder and sentenced to life in prison in 2007, with the maximum parole ineligibility period of 25 years, after being charged with the murders of 26 women.
The remains or DNA of 33 women, many who were taken from Vancouver's Downtown Eastside, were found on Pickton's pig farm in Port Coquitlam, B.C. He once bragged to an undercover police officer that he killed a total of 49.
Pickton's confirmed victims were Sereena Abotsway, Mona Wilson, Andrea Joesbury, Brenda Ann Wolfe, Georgina Papin and Marnie Frey.
Some family members of victims said they were pleased to hear news of the attack on Pickton.
"I don't think anybody that evil should be walking on earth, as far as I'm concerned," Cynthia Cardinal, 63, said on Tuesday to The Canadian Press. "I have happy tears. Very happy tears."
Pickton murdered her sister, Georgina Papin.
Speaking after hearing the news of his death Cardinal said it means she can finally move on from her sister's murder.
"This is gonna bring healing for, I won't say all families, I'll just say most of the families," she said.
"Because they didn't get their day in court, (that's) what I'm really sad about. But I'm also feeling really happy right now," said Cardinal.
"I'm like - wow, finally. I can actually move on and heal and I can put this behind me."
The families of Pickton's victims have pointed to botched police work during the investigation, and say that it was possible to have caught him earlier. In 1997, Pickton was arrested and charged with attempted murder, assault with a weapon and forcible confinement, but the charges were dropped, as the authorities didn't believe the victim was a reliable witness because she was a sex worker who used drugs. Vancouver police also maintained for years that there was not a serial killer preying on women in the city's Downtown Eastside.
Pickton's case spawned both provincial and federal government inquiries detailing failures by police. It also highlighted systemic inadequacies in dealing with cases of missing and murdered women. There are several ongoing lawsuits against Robert and David Pickton.
"In my own view, the Pickton case represents the first glimmer of an awareness of the plight of murdered and missing women, and especially Indigenous women, sex workers and drug users, and how society's regulatory apparatus and policing apparatus can work together to marginalize those women and create conditions for exploitation by predatory members of public," said Jason Gratl, a Vancouver-based lawyer who represents 16 family members of victims in ongoing civil litigation against the Picktons.
Gratl said that Pickton's death changes nothing in his civil case which is expected to go to trial in the next few years. Currently, he and many other advocates are embroiled in a dispute with the RCMP, which is looking to destroy over 14,000 pieces of evidence that they gathered and have been stored at a warehouse. Gratl said there is a theory that Pickton may have had accomplices and want that evidence available to possibly hold others accountable. The next date for a hearing on that matter takes places on June 26th.
Angela Marie MacDougall, executive director of Vancouver-based Battered Women's Support Services, reflected on the weekend assault that left Pickton with what police called life-threatening injuries, telling The Canadian Press, "There's something to be said about jailhouse justice."
While that is the overwhelming sentiment of how many feel about Pickton dying from his assault injuries, it's also a warning about the rising numbers of inmate-on-inmate violence, something that a prison law advocate feels is being spurred along by poisonous rhetoric from politicians about prisoners.
"It's the legal obligation of Correctional Service Canada to keep all prisoners safe, and there must have been a serious failure on their part here," said Tom Engel, president of the Canadian Prison Law Association. "I am also concerned with the political climate that has been created by certain politicians, like (Pierre) Poilievre, Doug Ford and (B.C.) MP (Frank) Caputo. They are dehumanizing people in jail, saying things like, they should stay in jail and rot and only come out in a box," said Engel. "You've got politicians who are dehumanizing people like Pickton and (Paul) Bernardo, and openly advocating, hoping that they should die in jail and even that they should suffer violence. So, they're creating this atmosphere. Does that contribute to the attack on Pickton? Who knows? But it sure doesn't help."
With files from
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Raju Mudhar Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Document 197 of 500

Nostalgia is a narcotic and Seinfeld is an addict

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nostalgia-is-narcotic-seinfeld-addict/docview/3062845266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nostalgia+is+a+narcotic+and+Seinfeld+is+an+addict&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At the height of his sitcom fame, Jerry Seinfeld was apolitical.
But now everything is political, including Jerry. In recent months, the comedian has made headlines for his opinions more than his act. He is anti-woke, pro-Israel, anti-PC, pro-free speech, anti-overthinking, pro-meditation, anti-futurism, pro-nostalgia ...
His latest "controversy" arrived this week when Seinfeld longed for the days of "dominant masculinity." As he told the great Bari Weiss on her "Honestly" podcast: "I like a real man."
Newman!
The outrage was predictable. How could Jerry, he of the puffy shirt and non-fat yogurt, pine for the "agreed-upon hierarchy" of a halcyon yesteryear? Why is a man-boy who is obsessed with Pop-Tarts, Superman and baseball fouling out in the gender wars? Does he want to time travel back to when a husband could berate his wife if she failed to pour his beer in a frosted mug while barefoot in her third trimester?
Of course not. Nostalgia is a narcotic and Seinfeld is an addict. There is no 12-step program to tint the rose-coloured rearview mirror in his 1959 Porsche 718 RSK Spyder.
His coming-of-age observations animate his world view today.
The "real men" archetype when he was growing up in the '60s now seems extinct. That's all he was saying. Little Jerry wanted to be just like John F. Kennedy, Muhammad Ali, Sean Connery or Howard Cosell when he grew up.
But then he never grew up.
Seinfeld's mistake was not in saying, "I like a real man."
It was in overlooking how great it is to not be a real man.
What even is a real man? All I know for sure is I'm not one. What a relief. It's liberating. When something breaks around here, my wife looks at me in dead silence. I can see the hamster wheels spinning in her head. But she never asks me to fix the problem. Why? She knows I can't.
So she calls the Real Man Hotline, some burly dude in overalls shows up with power tools and I go outside to refill my bird feeders. Everyone is happy.
If someone breaks into my house, I'm not crouching into a karate pose to confront the intruder with an unspooled crane kick to the face. I'm hiding under the bed and calling 911. I will get pistol-whipped faster than Jerry can say, "What's the deal with losing consciousness?" Now if someone breaks into my house and wants to chat about Polynesian petroglyphs or the Roswell incident, I'm happy to be the unreal man in that social dynamic.
Seinfeld admits he is not a real man. He just doesn't appreciate how wonderful it is to not be a real man. No sensible army would put this guy in a foxhole. As the real men are firing grenades, he'd be twiddling with a Pez dispenser.
If Jerry was a firefighter, a lot of people would die in fires.
Real men don't have time to neurotically fret about the size of their Levi's or grouse for hours because another motorist didn't offer a thank-you wave. Real men are too busy changing flat tires and opening jars of pickles.
You know why Seinfeld has no bits on pickles? He's never opened a jar.
I wonder if he is also confused by what it means to be a "real man."
Seinfeld says Hugh Grant, one of the stars in his new Netflix movie "Unfrosted," is a real man because "he knows how to dress, he knows how to talk, he's charming, he has stories, he's comfortable at dinner parties, he knows how to get a drink ..."
That's not a real man. That's a gentleman. That's a raconteur. That's a metrosexual. That's suave. That's urbane. Mr. Grant may know how to get a drink, but is there a little umbrella in it? Do his "stories" involve thread counts or backup generators? You know who's not comfortable at a dinner party? A real man. Not unless that dinner party involves him shooting an elk and searing it over a wood and charcoal grill he made out of an old dome-top trunk.
It must be horrible to be a real man. They are expected to fight the wars and build the skyscrapers and clean the eaves. Yes, women also do that stuff. My point is simply that I don't want to be a man or woman who does any of it.
When I hear one of my daughters shriek, I know a spider is in the house. Thanks to gender stereotypes, I am expected to kill it. A real man would squash that bug into the arachnid afterlife with a closed fist to the wall. I fetch my Dyson and pretend I am not terrified.
To be a real man is to be burdened with unfair expectations in the modern world.
Jerry Seinfeld is lucky he never grew up.
CAPTION: Jerry Seinfeld, seen at Netflix's "Unfrosted" premiere in April, calls its star Hugh Grant a real man, but Vinay Menon says Grant is more of a gentleman, a raconteur, a metrosexual.Charley Gallay Getty Images for Netflix
CREDIT: Vinay Menon Toronto Star OPINION

Document 198 of 500

We need to define affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-need-define-affordable-housing/docview/3062844756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+need+to+define+affordable+housing&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After months of failing to provide mandated data, the Ontario government finally received its latest tranche of funding from the federal government as part of the 10-year National Housing Strategy.
At the same time, the yawning gap between competing visions of "good housing" is widening. Doug Ford's Conservatives stress that building more "single-dwelling homes" is the primary goal for its housing programs. Meanwhile, 2024 federal government budget links infrastructure funding to allowing more "missing middle homes," including fourplexes and multi-family apartments, with higher densities near mass transit.
It's becoming increasingly clear that to address the housing crisis, all three levels of government need to get on the same page about what "affordable housing" means and how they're going to make it happen. As part of federal Housing Accelerator Fund, municipalities must undertake a standardized housing need assessment, with income categories and data derived from the Housing Assessment Resource Tools project (which I helped develop). However, when it comes to federal programs and research, the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) uses at least six different definitions of "housing affordability." Meanwhile, the Ontario government consistently substitutes the term "attainable housing" without defining what it means.
Although it is far from ideal, the standard that "affordable housing" costs no more than 30 per cent of pre-tax household income is the most common measure both internationally and in Canada.
In the 1980s, first time home ownership was affordable to moderate- and median-income households across Canada, with the average house price costing three times the average household income. Now that multiple has tripled, meaning that buying a first home is only affordable to the top 10 per cent of incomes for those aged 25 to 34. So, the rental market is crowded by those who would otherwise own homes. Meanwhile, the average asking rent for a one-bedroom apartment in a major Canadian city is $2,188 a month, about twice what a median-income individual can afford, let alone a family.
This affordable housing crisis, equivalent to the crisis facing Canada just after the Second World War, requires co-ordinated action from all three levels of government.
The federal government should be setting housing targets, with nonmarket housing sub-targets, for all provinces, territories, and municipalities. A 1944 federal report recommended one third nonmarket housing for low-income households, one third rent-regulated (either co-operative or market rental) for moderate and median income households, and one third market ownership. Those sub-targets still make sense. Scotiabank recommends doubling nonmarket housing stock over the next decade. RBC recommends quadrupling current nonmarket housing construction rates, from an average of 14,000 to 57,000 per year. Across Canada, a target of 300,000 home completions a year, with a third each nonmarket, purpose-built rental, and market ownership, would be a great start to fixing the housing crisis.
There is a federal sub-target of a mere 19,660 nonmarket homes being produced in Ontario over the decade between 2018 and 2028 in return for federal funding support. To give a sense of how minimal this target is, more than 42,000 nonmarket homes were produced in Canada in the single year 1971, over half in Ontario. The provincial government was unable to say how it would meet even those exceedingly low targets. But the federal government shouldn't be throwing bricks from its glass house: a 2022 report from its own National Housing Council found that only three per cent of the homes created by its most costly program, now renamed the Apartment Construction Loan Program, were affordable to the low income households most in need.
All levels of government must start by being honest and transparent about what they mean by affordable housing, which income categories and types of households they are helping with their programs, and then track housing completions according to targets and sub-targets set at the federal level, monitored through publicly accessible dashboards.
Merely encouraging single-family homes to stretch out into infinity isn't enough.
Carolyn Whitzman is a housing policy researcher and adjunct professor at the University of Ottawa. She is the author of the forthcoming Home Truths: Fixing Canada's Housing Crisis (UBC/On Point Press, 2024).
CAPTION: This affordable housing crisis, equivalent to the crisis facing Canada just after the Second World War, requires co-ordinated action from all three levels of government, writes Carolyn Whitzman.Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Carolyn Whitzman Contributor;

Subject: World War II; Federal government; Family income; Affordable housing; Funding; Households

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Location: Canada

Title: We need to define affordable housing

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: IN.7

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jun 1, 2024

Section: Opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 199 of 500

'I stick with what I said': Ford unrepentant after furor over remarks linking shooting at Jewish school to immigrants

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/i-stick-with-what-said/docview/3062844644/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I+stick+with+what+I+said%27%3A+Ford+unrepentant+after+furor+over+remarks+linking+shooting+at+Jewish+school+to+immigrants&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unrepentant Premier Doug Ford is doubling down despite criticism over his suggestion that immigrants were to blame for the shooting by unknown assailants at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
"You know something, I stick with what I said," he told reporters Friday at Toronto Metropolitan University's new medical school in Brampton.
"We're tired of lawlessness from ... anyone, it doesn't matter if you're here, you're new, whatever," the premier said.
"It's very, very clear. My phone is blowing up from every community saying you're bang on," said Ford, who publicly shares his cellphone number and says he answers scores of calls and texts daily.
"Ninety-nine point nine per cent of everyone gets along and that's what I believe in."
But he bristled at the notion his comment Thursday - that "you're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians" - was xenophobic.
"There's no premier ever that's (gone) on the news 10 million times (to say), 'I want more people coming to Canada.' We need the people, I love every single community equally," insisted Ford.
Critics have accused him of fanning the flames of division at a time when hate crimes reported to Toronto police have risen sharply in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war.
Police said Friday that antisemitic incidents jumped by 37 per cent last year, with anti-Muslim incidents up seven per cent.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have each called upon Ford to apologize for maligning immigrants.
Amira Elghawaby, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's special representative for combating Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating, or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety."
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fuelling further hate and division," said Elghawaby.
The controversy erupted after Ford and Trudeau had denounced the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. last Saturday.
At a news conference just five kilometres from the school, the premier said, "Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," he said Thursday.
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from," said Ford, emphasizing "diversity is our No. 1 selling tool around the world."
After the initial outcry Thursday, the premier tried to clarify things later in the day.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said in a statement.
Police have said it is still too early to determine if the Saturday shooting, which left no one injured but caused property damage, was hate-motivated.
CAPTION: Speaking alongside Prime Minister Justin Trudeau on Thursday, Premier Doug Ford condemned the shooting at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, saying, "Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."Arlyn McAdorey The Canadian Press

Document 200 of 500

Robert Pickton, one of Canada's most notorious serial killers, has died

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/robert-pickton-one-canadas-most-notorious-serial/docview/3062923636/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Robert+Pickton%2C+one+of+Canada%27s+most+notorious+serial+killers%2C+has+died&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of Canada's most notorious serial killers, whose arrest made international headlines and brought the issue of missing and murdered Indigenous women in this country into the collective consciousness, has died.
After 11 days on life-support and being placed in a medically induced coma by doctors at a Quebec hospital, Robert Pickton died on Friday, according to a release from Correctional Services Canada.
The 74-year-old suffered fatal wounds during an assault by another prisoner at Port-Cartier Institution in Quebec on May 19. He was reportedly speared by another prisoner in an unprovoked attack with a broom, although authorities have not confirmed details of the assault.
His alleged attacker, a 51-year-old prisoner who has not been publicly identified, remains in custody. As of Friday, he had not been charged.
"It probably won't happen for several weeks because there's no hurry to do that because he's already in jail," said Sgt. Hugues Beaulieu, a spokesperson for the Sûreté du Québec.
"We will finish our investigation, and then we will give it to the Crown prosecutor and then they will charge him."
Beaulieu said the inmate was in isolation after the attack, but confirmed he is now out.
Authorities had also been waiting to see if Pickton would recover from the wounds he sustained. Prison staff were not involved in the assault and Federal Minister of Public Safety Dominic LeBlanc said following the attack that Correctional Services Canada would be conducting its own investigation. Inquiries are usually held when an inmate dies in custody, but the findings are often not made public.
The release said that Pickton's next of kind has been notified, and that the investigation into how he died would continue.
"The safety and security of our institutions remains paramount for us. We are launching a Board of Investigation (BOI) into the assault that took place. The investigation will examine all of the facts and circumstances surrounding the assault, including whether policies and protocols were followed. It will also identify any recommendations and corrective measures, as needed," said the release.
It also noted the effect of the news, and what it might have on this victim's families.
"We are mindful that this offender's case has had a devastating impact on communities in British Columbia and across the country, including Indigenous peoples, victims and their families. Our thoughts are with them."
Robert William "Willie" Pickton was born Oct. 24, 1949, to a family of pig farmers in Port Coquitlam, B.C., just east of Vancouver. He, along with his brother, David, inherited the family business, and Robert eventually used the family property to dispose of his victims.
He is considered to be one of the the most prolific serial killers in Canadian history, and the farm became one of its largest crime scenes. Heavy equipment was brought in to search 380,000 cubic yards of land looking for human remains, with over 200,000 DNA samples collected. The investigation was estimated to have cost nearly $70 million by the end of 2003.
Pickton was initially arrested in 2002, after a worker informed police of illegal guns on the property. The subsequent search found weapons and evidence which led to a second warrant as part of B.C.'s missing women investigation.
Pickton was convicted of six counts of second-degree murder and sentenced to life in prison in 2007, with the maximum parole ineligibility period of 25 years, after being charged with the murders of 26 women.
The remains or DNA of 33 women, many who were taken from Vancouver's Downtown Eastside, were found on Pickton's pig farm in Port Coquitlam, B.C. He once bragged to an undercover police officer that he killed a total of 49.
Pickton's confirmed victims were Sereena Abotsway, Mona Wilson, Andrea Joesbury, Brenda Ann Wolfe, Georgina Papin and Marnie Frey.
Some family members of victims said they were pleased to hear news of the attack on Pickton.
"I don't think anybody that evil should be walking on earth, as far as I'm concerned," Cynthia Cardinal, 63, said on Tuesday to The Canadian Press. "I have happy tears. Very happy tears."
Pickton murdered her sister, Georgina Papin.
The families of Pickton's victims have pointed to botched police work during the investigation, and say that it was possible to have caught him earlier. In 1997, Pickton was arrested and charged with attempted murder, assault with a weapon and forcible confinement, but the charges were dropped, as the authorities didn't believe the victim was a reliable witness because she was a sex worker who used drugs. Vancouver police also maintained for years that there was not a serial killer preying on women in the city's Downtown Eastside.
Pickton's case spawned both provincial and federal government inquiries detailing failures by police. It also highlighted systemic inadequacies in dealing with cases of missing and murdered women. There are also several ongoing lawsuits against Robert and David Pickton.
"In my own view, the Pickton case represents the first glimmer of an awareness of the plight of murdered and missing women, and especially Indigenous women, sex workers and drug users, and how society's regulatory apparatus and policing apparatus can work together to marginalize those women and create conditions for exploitation by predatory members of public," said Jason Gratl, a Vancouver-based lawyer who represents 16 family members of victims in ongoing civil litigation against the Picktons.
Gratl said that Pickton's death changes nothing in his civil case which is expected to go to trial in the next few years. Currently, he and many other advocates are embroiled in a dispute with the RCMP, which is looking to destroy over 14,000 pieces of evidence that they gathered and have been stored at a warehouse. Gratl said there is a theory that Pickton may have had accomplices and want that evidence available to possibly hold others accountable. The next date for a hearing on that matter takes places on June 26th.
Angela Marie MacDougall, executive director of Vancouver-based Battered Women's Support Services, reflected on the weekend assault that left Pickton with what police called life-threatening injuries, telling The Canadian Press, "There's something to be said about jailhouse justice."
While that is the overwhelming sentiment of how many feel about Pickton dying from his assault injuries, it's also a warning about the rising numbers of inmate-on-inmate violence, something that a prison law advocate feels is being spurred along by poisonous rhetoric from politicians about prisoners.
"It's the legal obligation of Correctional Services Canada to keep all prisoners safe, and there must have been a serious failure on their part here," said Tom Engal, president of the Canadian Prison Law Association. "I am also concerned with the political climate that has been created by certain politicians, like (Pierre) Poilievre, Doug Ford and (BC) MP (Frank) Caputo. They are dehumanizing people in jail, saying things like, they should stay in jail and rot and only come out in a box," said Engal. "You've got politicians who are dehumanizing people like Pickton and (Paul) Bernardo, and openly advocating, hoping that they should die in jail and even that they should suffer violence. So, they're creating this atmosphere. Does that contribute to the attack on Pickton? Who knows? But it sure doesn't help."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: An undated aerial photograph taken by Royal Canadian Mounted Police shows a portion of the land of accused serial killer Robert Pickton. The photo was used as evidence during the trial in New Westminster, British Columbia and was released by the court in February 2007.Elder Eugene Harry of the Squamish Nation looks at a poster of missing women prior to performing a ceremonial blessing at the start of the missing women inquiry in downtown Vancouver in Oct. 2011.Robert Pickton, a notorious serial killer, was the victim of a serious assault at Port Cartier Institution on May 19 (2024).An artist's sketch Robert Pickton taking notes during the second day of his trial in B.C. Supreme Court in New Westminster, B.C., in January 2006.Archeologists continue their work on a Port Coquitlam, B.C. pig farm, looking clues in the case of the missing women from Vancouver's Downtown Eastside in September 2002.An aerial view of the Pickton farm in Port Coquitlam, B.C. taken by police during their investigation in 2002.

Document 201 of 500

Doug Ford unrepentant after furor over comments suggesting immigrants were behind school shooting: 'I stick with what I said'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-unrepentant-after-furor-over-comments/docview/3062923634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+unrepentant+after+furor+over+comments+suggesting+immigrants+were+behind+school+shooting%3A+%27I+stick+with+what+I+said%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unrepentant Premier Doug Ford is doubling down despite criticism over his suggestion that immigrants were to blame for the shooting by unknown assailants at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
"You know something, I stick with what I said," he told reporters Friday at Toronto Metropolitan University's new medical school in Brampton.
"We're tired of lawlessness from ... anyone, it doesn't matter if you're here, you're new, whatever," the premier said.
"It's very, very clear. My phone is blowing up from every community saying you're bang on," said Ford, who publicly shares his cellphone number and says he answers scores of calls and texts daily.
"Ninety-nine point nine per cent of everyone gets along and that's what I believe in."
But he bristled at the notion his comment Thursday - that "you're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians" - was xenophobic.
"There's no premier ever that's (gone) on the news 10 million times (to say), 'I want more people coming to Canada.' We need the people, I love every single community equally," insisted Ford.
Critics have accused him of fanning the flames of division at a time when hate crimes reported to Toronto police have risen sharply in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war.
Police said Friday that antisemitic incidents jumped by 37 per cent last year, with anti-Muslim incidents up seven per cent.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and Green Leader Mike Schreiner have each called upon Ford to apologize for maligning immigrants.
Amira Elghawaby, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's special representative for combating Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating, or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety."
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fuelling further hate and division," said Elghawaby.
The controversy erupted after Ford and Trudeau had denounced the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. last Saturday.
At a news conference just five kilometres from the school, the premier said, "Don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," he said Thursday.
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from," said Ford, emphasizing "diversity is our number one selling tool around the world."
After the initial outcry Thursday, the premier tried to clarify things later in the day.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said in a statement.
Police have said it is still too early to determine if the Saturday shooting, which left no one injured but caused property damage, was hate-motivated.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has suggested immigrants were to blame for shooting at a Jewish school in Toronto last weekend despite police saying they have no information on the suspects. Ford, centre, delivers remarks while being flanked by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, left, and Mayor Olivia Chow, right, during a news conference in the facilities of vaccine producer Sanofi in Toronto on May 30, 2024.

Document 202 of 500

We've waited 40 years for beer in convenience stores. At this price, we can wait one more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/weve-waited-40-years-beer-convenience-stores-at/docview/3062689792/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We%27ve+waited+40+years+for+beer+in+convenience+stores.+At+this+price%2C+we+can+wait+one+more&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford appears seized by a sudden and injudicious haste to get beer, wine and cocktails into convenience stores ahead of schedule.
For a leader with a well-documented track record of rash acts and sheepish reversals, this new fervour can hardly help but raise alarms about potential costs and misplaced priorities.
Late last year, Ford announced plans to allow convenience stores to sell alcohol as of Jan. 1, 2026. His Progressive Conservatives had promised such a move in the election campaigns of 2018 and 2022, but were constrained by a master agreement signed with The Beer Store by the former Liberal government that ran until the end of 2025.
The impatient premier initially muttered about reneging on that agreement in order to get alcohol more widely available more quickly.
Until, that is, he was persuaded by cooler minds of the considerable legal costs and lamentable reputation for bad-faith dealing that such a move would cause.
Then last week came word that the premier's craving to get on with things had got the better of him.
The Ford government announced that it will pay The Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants Labatt, Molson and Sleeman - $225 million to allow "early implementation" of the expanded alcohol sales and make beer, wine and premixed cocktails available at more types of retail outlets by Oct. 31 this year.
The retailing of alcohol is no one's idea of the most pressing challenge facing Ontario. But from his earliest "buck-a-beer" days as Tory leader, Ford has regularly turned to it to burnish his populist image or distract from misjudgments and flip-flops.
Once again, there is a political sub-text to his latest enthusiasm for booze sales.
The move would give Ford flexibility to call a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote by allowing him to claim he had delivered on another popular campaign pledge.
The motivation for an early election call is the belief in Ford circles that he would fare better running for a third term before the possible installation of a Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre in Ottawa.
All Ford would say about such a scenario was a portentous "stay tuned."
The premier insists an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets stocking alcohol will boost sales and bring in more in taxes to provincial revenues.
"We're going to see revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion," he told the legislature. "That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care."
But as could reasonably be said before many of the premier's giddy assertions, critics were soon yelling not so fast.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has said the complexities of the deal with brewing giants mean Ford's rush to reform could cost Ontarians hundreds of millions of dollars more, ending up closer to $1 billion than the province's estimated $225 million.
Her Liberals have asked Ontario's auditor general and the legislature's Financial Accountability Office to investigate the true cost of the deal, which seems reasonable.
Opposition Leader Marit Stiles asked Ford this week why he was "pouring money into the pockets of these big alcohol corporations while our emergency rooms are closing?"
Stiles makes a fair point. There is little justification for Ford's urgency.
Ontario was promised sale of beer and wine in corner stores as long ago as 1985. The Star long supported such a measure on the grounds that Ontarians would benefit from the convenience and competition. But while we welcome the end of The Beer Store monopoly and applaud the direction, a change like this, with the risks and costs it entails, should be done with care rather than politically motivated haste.
It makes more sense to stick to the province's previous timeline and to spend the (at least) $225 million on truly urgent issues such as the province's crisis of primary health care or housing.
We have waited nearly 40 years for beer to reach convenience stores. Given the price tag, we can wait one more.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, left, and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy after announcing that beer and wine will be available at corner stores in the fall.

Ontarians struggling to pay for basics don't want an expensive election and overpriced booze

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-struggling-pay-basics-dont-want/docview/3062689666/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+struggling+to+pay+for+basics+don%27t+want+an+expensive+election+and+overpriced+booze&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No election needed in an affordability crisis
Ford was asked about calling on early election. 'Stay tuned,' he says, May 28
The affordability crisis doesn't seem to affect Queen's Park. In spite of having a majority government, Premier Doug Ford has teased the idea of calling an election two years early, at a cost of at least $155 million. Preempting the possible early election is the premier's campaign promise to allow the sale of beer and wine at corner stores. Current agreements do not allow for corner store sales and do not expire until December 31, 2025. How to get around this? Pay The Beer Store $225 million (which could rise to $600 million or more) using taxpayer money. Ontarians must ask ourselves: can we afford Ford?
Ruth Allen, Toronto
Alcohol drinkers also need health care, housing and education
Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
I am absolutely not a teetotaller. What I am, however, is both baffled and outraged by the apparent willingness of many Ontario voters to support a government that ignores the needs of every citizen in favour of the desires of a few. Do alcohol drinkers not need health care, housing, education and food security first, before finding convenient "comfort" in a glass? There may be some merit in having people take an IQ test before stepping into the voter's booth.
Michael Bines, Toronto
I cannot help but view the incredible gift of public funds being given to The Beer Store by Premier Doug Ford without thinking of better uses for such a huge amount of money. Several schools in Prince Edward Island, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia are currently under construction for the cost we are spending on this. Why are they the lucky ones? Or is it just good governance?
Malcolm Lowe, Unionville
Premier Doug Ford apparently doesn't drink, but it's obvious there is some sort of impaired judgment at Queen's Park with the decision to pay or forfeit at least $225 million taxpayer dollars (up to $1 billion) to prematurely end an agreement with The Beer Store that would have ended at no cost in a little over a year and a half. With the desperate need for funding in so many important areas, Ford chooses instead to effectively pay for the severance cheques for Beer Store staff.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
Encampments aren't a safety risk
U of T's fight for court injunction against encampment won't finish before convocation starts, May 29
I recently had an opportunity to walk around the fenced-in pro-Palestinian encampment at King's College Circle. I was even allowed inside the encampment after a protocol briefing at the only informal entry and exit point. Let me tell all those naysayers that I found the folks in the encampment very quiet and peaceful as they went about whatever they were doing. At no point was I approached and/or threatened. I felt quite safe walking around and in the encampment. The few posters affixed on the fence that I read were not abusive or offensive either. To the president of University of Toronto I say simply: chill. The folks in the encampment are no threat and it is their democratic right to protest. As to whether King's College Circle is private land or not, it's a moot point. Educational institutions are basically funded by taxpayers. I sincerely hope the judge allows the encampment to stay as-is.
Aquil Ali, Toronto
Have we forgotten what it means to be Canadian?
Canada is about celebrating pluralism and multiculturalism. It is about taking pride in the rights and freedoms afforded to all regardless of race, colour, sexual orientation, gender, religion, or any other superficial attribute. We may disagree with the foreign policy of Israel but there is no justification for attacks on Canadian Jews who, regardless of their views about the war, are not responsible for the actions of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's government. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford, and Mayor Olivia Chow have, at times, condemned the surge in antisemitism in Canada. None have stated clearly and often enough that antisemitism is anti-Canadian and is a threat to all of us.
Rickey Miller, North York
June Callwood's centenary
I recently headed up an initiative trying to create a centenary celebration of the profound life of activist June Callwood (June 2, 1924-April 14, 2007). Even though the City of Toronto has nothing planned to celebrate the occasion, I pushed forward and learned a lot about the fascinating life of a remarkable woman who brought ingenuity, hope and inspiration to all who were graced by her presence. Callwood's legacy will endure and live on, and we should all be extremely proud of what she has done and the legacy she left us. In honour of her birthday, we plan a meet and greet at June Callwood Park on Sunday at noon. In the spirit of leading by action, we welcome help with a light clean up of the park.
Christopher Simmons, Toronto
Bill Walton was a kind, heroic, unassuming man
How I will remember basketball legend Bill Walton, a giant in every sense of the word, May 27
I was in Houston for a business meeting and required to stay overnight before coming home. The Rockets were playing that night, so I thought to take in the game. As it happens, my hotel was connected by tunnel to the arena so I walked there. I had never seen a professional game but I knew of Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and was interested in seeing him play. I had barely started my walk over when I sensed a very tall man behind me. I looked up and recognized him immediately. It was Bill Walton and we fell into conversation.
He told me he would be broadcasting the game and asked me what I was doing in Houston. Where was I coming from? What was my business? He was very focused on getting to know me and making me comfortable in his presence. We chatted as we went along the path. When we got to the other end, he showed me how to get to the box office, warmly shook hands and said "very nice to meet you" and set off.
He was casual and relaxed, yet I had to speed up my pace to keep up with him. I remember his smile and how he turned and waved at me as he disappeared. I remember him as a kind, heroic, unassuming man who I was fortunate to meet. RIP Bill Walton.
Arnold Birnie, Oakville
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at a media event in Toronto on May 24, where he announced that beer and wine sales will soon be allowed in Ontario's corner stores

Document 204 of 500

If Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford actually want affordable housing in Ontario, they'll have to agree on this one basic thing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-justin-trudeau-doug-ford-actually-want/docview/3062394364/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+Justin+Trudeau+and+Doug+Ford+actually+want+affordable+housing+in+Ontario%2C+they%27ll+have+to+agree+on+this+one+basic+thing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After months of failing to provide mandated data, the Ontario government finally received its latest tranche of funding from the federal government as part of the 10-year National Housing Strategy.
At the same time, the yawning gap between competing visions of "good housing" is widening. Doug Ford's Conservatives stress that building more "single-dwelling homes" is the primary goal for its housing programs. Meanwhile, 2024 federal government budget links infrastructure funding to allowing more "missing middle homes," including fourplexes and multi-family apartments, with higher densities near mass transit.
It's becoming increasingly clear that to address the housing crisis, all three levels of government need to get on the same page about what "affordable housing" means and how they're going to make it happen. As part of federal Housing Accelerator Fund, municipalities must undertake a standardized housing need assessment, with income categories and data derived from the Housing Assessment Resource Tools project (which I helped develop). However, when it comes to federal programs and research, the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) uses at least six different definitions of "housing affordability." Meanwhile, the Ontario government consistently substitutes the term "attainable housing" without defining what it means.
Although it is far from ideal, the standard that "affordable housing" costs no more than 30 per cent of pre-tax household income is the most common measure both internationally and in Canada.
In the 1980s, first time home ownership was affordable to moderate- and median-income households across Canada, with the average house price costing three times the average household income. Now that multiple has tripled, meaning that buying a first home is only affordable to the top 10 per cent of incomes for those aged 25-34. So, the rental market is crowded by those who would otherwise own homes. Meanwhile, the average asking rent for a one-bedroom apartment in a major Canadian city is $2,188 a month, about twice what a median-income individual can afford, let alone a family.
This affordable housing crisis, equivalent to the crisis facing Canada just after the Second World War, requires co-ordinated action from all three levels of government.
The federal government should be setting housing targets, with nonmarket housing sub-targets, for all provinces, territories, and municipalities. A 1944 federal report recommended one third nonmarket housing for low-income households, one third rent-regulated (either co-operative or market rental) for moderate and median income households, and one third market ownership. Those sub-targets still make sense. Scotiabank recommends doubling nonmarket housing stock over the next decade. RBC recommends quadrupling current nonmarket housing construction rates, from an average of 14,000 to 57,000 per year. Across Canada, a target of 300,000 home completions a year, with a third each nonmarket, purpose-built rental, and market ownership, would be a great start to fixing the housing crisis.
There is a federal sub-target of a mere 19,660 nonmarket homes being produced in Ontario over the decade between 2018 and 2028 in return for federal funding support. To give a sense of how minimal this target is, more than 42,000 nonmarket homes were produced in Canada in the single year 1971, over half in Ontario. The provincial government was unable to say how it would meet even those exceedingly low targets. But the federal government shouldn't be throwing bricks from its glass house: a 2022 report from its own National Housing Council found that only three per cent of the homes created by its most costly program, now renamed the Apartment Construction Loan Program, were affordable to the low income households most in need.
All levels of government must start by being honest and transparent about what they mean by affordable housing, which income categories and types of households they are helping with their programs, and then track housing completions according to targets and sub-targets set at the federal level, monitored through publicly accessible dashboards.
Merely encouraging single-family homes to stretch out into infinity isn't enough.
CAPTION: This affordable housing crisis, equivalent to the crisis facing Canada just after the Second World War, requires co-ordinated action from all three levels of government, writes Carolyn Whitzman.
CREDIT: Carolyn Whitzman Contributor

Subject: World War II; Federal government; Family income; Affordable housing; Funding; Households

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing
