Full text: Amid affordability crisis, Ford wants an election?
Ford was asked about calling on early election. 'Stay tuned,' he says, May 28
The affordability crisis doesn't seem to affect Queen's Park. In spite of having a majority government, Premier Doug Ford has teased the idea of calling an election two years early, at a cost of at least $155 million. Pre-empting the possible early election is the premier's campaign promise to allow the sale of beer and wine at corner stores. Current agreements do not allow for corner store sales and do not expire until Dec. 31, 2025. How to get around this? Pay the Beer Store $225 million (which could rise to $600 million or more) using taxpayer money. Ontarians must ask ourselves: can we afford Ford?
Ruth Allen, Toronto
More important issues than booze on every corner
Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
I am absolutely not a teetotaller. What I am, however, is both baffled and outraged by the apparent willingness of many Ontario voters to support a government that ignores the needs of every citizen in favour of the desires of a few. Do alcohol drinkers not need health care, housing, education and food security first, before finding convenient "comfort" in a glass? There may be some merit in having people take an IQ test before stepping into the voter's booth.
Michael Bines, Toronto
I cannot help but view the incredible gift of public funds being given to the Beer Store by Premier Doug Ford without thinking of better uses for such a huge amount of money. Several schools in Prince Edward Island, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia are currently under construction for the cost we are spending on this. Why are they the lucky ones? Or is it just good governance?
Malcolm Lowe, Unionville
Premier Doug Ford apparently doesn't drink, but it's obvious there is some sort of impaired judgment at Queen's Park with the decision to pay or forfeit at least $225 million taxpayer dollars (up to $1 billion) to prematurely end an agreement with the Beer Store that would have ended at no cost in a little over a year and a half. With the desperate need for funding in so many important areas, Ford chooses instead to effectively pay for the severance cheques for Beer Store staff.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
Encampments aren't safety risk
U of T's fight for court injunction against encampment won't finish before convocation starts, May 29
I recently had an opportunity to walk around the fenced-in pro-Palestinian encampment at King's College Circle. I was even allowed inside the encampment after a protocol briefing at the only informal entry and exit point. Let me tell all those naysayers that I found the folks in the encampment very quiet and peaceful as they went about whatever they were doing. At no point was I approached and/or threatened. I felt quite safe walking around and in the encampment. The few posters affixed on the fence that I read were not abusive or offensive either.
To the president of University of Toronto I say simply: chill. The folks in the encampment are no threat and it is their democratic right to protest. As to whether King's College Circle is private land or not, it's a moot point. Educational institutions are basically funded by taxpayers. I sincerely hope the judge allows the encampment to stay as is.
Aquil Ali, Toronto
There's nothing Canadian about antisemitism
Canada is about celebrating pluralism and multiculturalism. It is about taking pride in the rights and freedoms afforded to all regardless of race, colour, sexual orientation, gender, religion, or any other superficial attribute. We may disagree with the foreign policy of Israel but there is no justification for attacks on Canadian Jews who, regardless of their views about the war, are not responsible for the actions of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's government. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow have, at times, condemned the surge in antisemitism in Canada. None have stated clearly and often enough that antisemitism is anti-Canadian and is a threat to all of us.
Rickey Miller, North York
June Callwood's centenary
I recently headed up an initiative trying to create a centenary celebration of the profound life of activist June Callwood (June 2, 1924-April 14, 2007). Even though the City of Toronto has nothing planned to celebrate the occasion, I pushed forward and learned a lot about the fascinating life of a remarkable woman who brought ingenuity, hope and inspiration to all who were graced by her presence. Callwood's legacy will endure and live on, and we should all be extremely proud of what she has done and the legacy she left us. In honour of her birthday, we have planned a meet and greet at June Callwood Park on Sunday at noon. In the spirit of leading by action, we welcome help with a light clean up of the park.
Christopher Simmons, Toronto
Bill Walton was
a kind, heroic, unassuming man
How I will remember basketball legend Bill Walton, a giant in every sense of the word, May 27
I was in Houston for a business meeting and required to stay overnight before coming home. The Rockets were playing that night, so I thought to take in the game. As it happens, my hotel was connected by tunnel to the arena so I walked there. I had never seen a professional game but I knew of Kareem Abdul-Jabbar and was interested in seeing him play. I had barely started my walk over when I sensed a very tall man behind me. I looked up and recognized him immediately. It was Bill Walton and we fell into conversation.
He told me he would be broadcasting the game and asked me what I was doing in Houston. Where was I coming from? What was my business? He was very focused on getting to know me and making me comfortable in his presence. We chatted as we went along the path. When we got to the other end, he showed me how to get to the box office, warmly shook hands and said "very nice to meet you" and set off. He was casual and relaxed, yet I had to speed up my pace to keep up with him. I remember his smile and how he turned and waved at me as he disappeared. I remember him as a kind, heroic, unassuming man who I was fortunate to meet. RIP Bill Walton.
Arnold Birnie, Oakville

Document 206 of 500

Ford slammed for 'racist' remarks: Premier asked to apologize after suggesting immigrants to blame for shooting at Jewish school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-slammed-racist-remarks/docview/3062393040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+slammed+for+%27racist%27+remarks%3A+Premier+asked+to+apologize+after+suggesting+immigrants+to+blame+for+shooting+at+Jewish+school&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford was forced to do damage control after appearing to blame immigrants for the weekend shooting at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had condemned the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. Saturday.
Speaking to reporters just five kilometres from the school, the premier seemed to point the finger at newcomers to Canada - despite the fact police have not identified the shooters.
"You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians," he said, as Trudeau and Mayor Olivia Chow stood nearby, their eyebrows raised.
Under fire from many quarters - including Trudeau's special representative for combating Islamophobia - Ford later scrambled to clarify his statement.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said.
Prior to Ford's comments, Trudeau said he was "disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism."
"Just a short drive away, parents dropped off their daughters this morning at a Jewish girls' elementary school that was shot at over the weekend. And in Montreal, another Jewish school was targeted by an overnight shooting last night," he said.
"It needs to stop right now," the prime minister said at the opening of Sanofi's new $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility on Steeles Avenue West he attended with Ford and Chow.
"The Jewish parents and students across this country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada," he said.
Ford expressed outrage at the increase in antisemitic hate crimes in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war that began last October.
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," said the premier.
But he sparked furor when he said "don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from."
Stressing that "diversity is our number one selling tool around the world," Ford said there is no place for racial discrimination in Ontario.
Amira Elghawaby, the federal government's point person on combating Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating, or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety.
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fuelling further hate and division."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she was "appalled by the premier's racist remarks" by suggesting new Canadians were to blame for the attack.
"Fighting hate with hate has never worked. Fighting antisemitism with xenophobia won't keep communities safe. He should apologize," Stiles said in a statement.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "dividing the people of Ontario isn't leadership - it further promotes hate and xenophobia."
"Our province deserves better from its premier. Doug Ford must apologize now," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "dangerous and unacceptable rhetoric from Premier Ford."
"What happened at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School was an alarming and unacceptable act of antisemitism," said Schreiner.
"But using it as an opportunity to stoke anti-immigrant sentiment only fans the flames of division and increases tension at a time when we as leaders should be focused on helping our community members come together and heal," he said.
Meanwhile, Chow noted she had visited the school on Monday and said city council has been working with the local community on safety measures. She said that can include "shatterproof glass, fencing, lighting or trimming of bushes or cameras" to make vulnerable schools or places of worship feel more secure.
Prior to the controversy over Ford's comments, federal Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks, who was also in attendance at Sanofi, praised the premier and prime minister for expressing support for the Jewish community.
"Allies matter. As a Jewish mother in this community we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," said Saks.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Antisemitism; Leadership; Community; Islamophobia; Xenophobia; Prime ministers; Elementary schools

Document 207 of 500

Former OPSEU lawyer arrested for contempt after refusing to turn over documents explaining $3.7 million in missing funds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-opseu-lawyer-arrested-contempt-after/docview/3062392122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+OPSEU+lawyer+arrested+for+contempt+after+refusing+to+turn+over+documents+explaining+%243.7+million+in+missing+funds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A former lawyer for the Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU) has been arrested after being found in contempt of court, for refusing a court order to turn over records showing what happened to $3.7 million in union funds he is alleged to have misappropriated, according to court documents obtained by the Star.
It's the latest twist in a saga that began in 2022. Mark Mendl, who was the OPSEU's external legal counsel from 2017 to 2022, allegedly received $3.7 million in payments from the union leading up to a union election, according to the statement of claim filed in a civil action in April. When these payments were discovered by the new OPSEU leadership in an audit following the election, Mendl "evaded the union's requests" for records explaining where the funds had gone, the documents say.
After the union investigated further, according to its claim it discovered the funds transferred to Mendl were then given to former OPSEU executives and employees, who are not named, and that some had been deposited in an account Mendl owns jointly with his spouse.
"Most of the transfers were made without proper documentation or justification," court documents read.
The allegations have not been proven in court. No statement of defence has been filed and Mendl did not respond to multiple requests to comment for this story. His lawyer, Bryan Badali, declined to comment on behalf of Mendl as the matter is before the courts.
A court order obtained by the Star details that Mendl was "apprehended" after a warrant was issued for his arrest, and he appeared in the Superior Court of Justice on Wednesday. The order permits Mendl to be released from custody, on the condition he not leave Ontario during the contempt proceedings, surrender his passport and post a $30,000 bond - along with mandating he attend all future hearings in person.
The union, which represents more than 180,000 public sector employees across the province, is suing Mendl to recover the funds, as well as $3.7 million in damages for negligence and breach of contract, and $500,000 for aggravated and punitive damages.
As part of an application filed by the union in February 2023, Mendl was ordered by the courts to produce records explaining what happened to the funds in his trust account - which he declined to do, according to the statement of claim. OPSEU then filed a civil action against him in April 2024 seeking damages.
Mendl was found in contempt, and a warrant was issued for his arrest. The union said Toronto Police arrested Mendl on Monday.
Toronto Police declined to comment on his arrest, stating they "cannot confirm any information that is not criminal in nature."
The union is alleging that Mendl "breached his duties" to the OPSEU and abused his position of power, as he was "intentionally involved in, or was negligent in facilitating," the misappropriation of $3.7 million in union funds.
The claim alleges that Mendl received "significant payments" to his trust account between December 2021 and April 2022 adding up to $3.7 million, days before the union held its 2022 elections. After the new union leadership took over, they conducted an audit that revealed "serious concerns" about how previous administrations had used union funds, resulting in legal action and further investigation.
The unexplained funds transferred to Mendl, as well as his refusal to account for them, "raised concerns that Mendl may have been involved in alleged financial impropriety." The union terminated his retainer in July 2022, and tried to get information from him directly explaining where the money had gone.
"Mendl either ignored the union's requests or provided false assurances that the documents would be delivered, even going as far as stating that the documents had been sent in the mail, which he later admitted was untrue," reads the factum of the applicant in the civil action.
In February 2023, the union began legal proceedings to compel him to produce the records.
"Because these are ongoing legal proceedings, we are limited in what we can say at this time," wrote Kim Johnston, communications supervisor for the OPSEU. "However, the union does see these proceedings as an opportunity to seek justice for OPSEU/SEFPO members and we look forward to moving to the next steps, including with respect to Mr. Mendl."
In the wake of the audit in 2022, the OPSEU also undertook separate legal action against three former union leaders, including former president Warren "Smokey" Thomas, former first vice-president/treasurer Eduardo Almeida and former administrator of the union's financial services division Maurice Gabay, seeking more than $6 million in damages. The union alleges the three former leaders withdrew $670,000 in cash from a strike fund without explanation, received "significant compensation" atop their salaries and had union-purchased vehicles transferred to themselves and their families. None of the allegations have been proven in court.
Thomas, in his statement of defence, claimed the suit is politically motivated due to his appearances alongside Premier Doug Ford, adding that the legal action "has no basis in reality, and is entirely manufactured."
"We will not waver in our commitment to seeking justice in this matter, and we have the full support of the board to pursue all available legal avenues ... I want to assure you that despite today's news our union remains strong and our finances stable," wrote current OPSEU president JP Hornick in an email to members obtained by the Star in 2023.
CAPTION: Ontario Public Service Employees Union offices on Wellesley Street East in Toronto.Ontario Public Service Employees Union offices on Wellesley Street East in Toronto.

Subject: Arrests; Contempt of court; Breach of contract; Employees; Union leadership

Document 208 of 500

How did Justin Trudeau screw up so badly? Maybe the answer was hiding in plain sight

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-did-justin-trudeau-screw-up-so-badly-maybe/docview/3062392047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+did+Justin+Trudeau+screw+up+so+badly%3F+Maybe+the+answer+was+hiding+in+plain+sight&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The question I have these days about Justin Trudeau is not "will he run again?" (which is unknowable at the moment) but "how did he and the Liberals screw things up so badly?" How did they manage to fall from the heights of "sunny ways" back in 2015 to the electoral depths they're stuck in now?
I read Stephen Maher's new book, "The Prince: The Turbulent Reign of Justin Trudeau," with that in mind, looking for clues as to when and why the wheels came off the Liberal bus.
The arc of the story is well known. Trudeau swept into power as a "handsome, smiling prince" (Maher's words) riding a wave of public optimism. The SNC-Lavalin affair of 2019, with the resignation of two top female ministers, cracked that image, perhaps definitively. But Canadians mostly rallied around the government during the pandemic, allowing Trudeau to squeeze out a third victory, albeit a minority one.
By the fall of 2022, though, the Liberals really started losing the thread. They didn't see the cost-of-living crisis coming on. They failed to come to grips with housing and immigration, and they didn't grasp the connection between those issues. They didn't go at Pierre Poilievre hard when he took over as Conservative leader, leaving him a clear field to define himself as the champion of middle-class Canada.
Most obviously, the Liberals completely bungled the foreign interference file. If they'd taken it seriously and done what pretty much everyone suggested - set up a foreign agents register and promise an inquiry - they could have contained the damage.
Instead, inexplicably, they dodged and weaved and turned a problem into a crisis. By the summer of 2023 they'd fallen to 20 points behind the Conservatives and nothing they've done since has turned that around.
It was quite predictable that the post-pandemic era would bring new challenges, so why were Trudeau and the Liberals so unable to get their heads around them? How did they miss the moment so badly?
Perhaps it's just that people get tired of a strong personality like Trudeau and the "pious scolding" (Maher's words again) he too often aims at those who don't share his progressive vision. But the Liberals didn't really fight back for months on end.
In part, Liberal insiders told Maher, it's because the government is so highly centralized. "Everything gets jammed in Trudeau's office," he writes, "so nothing gets done except for the top items handled effectively by the small team of talented, hard-working staffers trusted by the boss." The result looked a lot like paralysis throughout 2023, which Maher calls the Liberals' "annus horribilis."
I wonder if part of the answer is hiding in plain sight. Canadian journalists don't like to pry into politicians' private lives. That, we tell ourselves, is for crass Americans and online trolls. But you can take that to extremes, and in Trudeau's case it's possible we're missing something obvious.
Right in the middle of the Liberals' year from hell, Maher notes, Trudeau and his wife Sophie announced they were splitting up. "The announcement," he writes, "was not a shock," though I have to say it was a bit of a shock to me and I daresay to most Canadians who aren't privy to Ottawa gossip. Political journalists may have known the marriage was rocky but as far as I remember they didn't write about it.
It became clear from divorce papers that by April of 2023 Sophie Grégoire Trudeau was already "re-partnered" with an Ottawa surgeon. The breakup "was a long time coming," writes Maher. I can't get inside Trudeau's head, but it's hardly a stretch to imagine that the collapse of his marriage had weighed on him for a long time - throughout that crucial stretch in 2022 and 2023 when the government seemed frozen while new problems came at it thick and fast.
Maybe that had no effect on how Trudeau operated in the office. Maybe he's that rare person able to put aside personal issues and stay on top of his game. Or maybe, just maybe, "the boss" lost focus at exactly the wrong moment for the future of his government and party.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and wife Sophie Gregoire-Trudeau wait to greet U.S. President Joe Biden and First Lady Jill Biden to Rideau Cottage in Ottawa on March 23, 2023.

Subject: Pandemics

Document 209 of 500

Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford won't allow Toronto to decriminalize drugs. So now what?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-doug-ford-wont-allow-toronto/docview/3062391057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+and+Doug+Ford+won%27t+allow+Toronto+to+decriminalize+drugs.+So+now+what%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If not decriminalization, then what?
That's a question that demands an answer. Because opioid deaths in Toronto are an epidemic. According to a Toronto Public Health announcement on Wednesday, 523 people died from opioid toxicity in 2023 - a sharp rise from before the pandemic, but something that after more than 500 deaths in each of 2020, 2021 and 2022, is coming to seem like business as usual. There's no way we can accept it as usual.
By way of comparison, 523 deaths is more than seven times the number of homicides recorded in Toronto in 2023. It is more than 11 times the number of traffic fatalities in that year. It is an unacceptable level of carnage from addiction to an unregulated black-market drug supply.
So what are we going to do about it?
Perhaps, as the Star's editorial board wrote this week, it's not a surprise that the federal government, siding with Premier Doug Ford, vetoed the idea of decriminalizing small amounts of hard drugs in an attempt at harm reduction. Though the city had been asking for that measure and some public health authorities believe it would help, the examples of jurisdictions in British Columbia and Oregon, where decriminalization has been implemented and then repealed (partially in B.C.) might make the skepticism of some in upper governments understandable. Neither place saw the hoped-for reductions in use or fatalities, and both saw spreading street use and associated problems. Whatever its merits, clearly decriminalization alone is not a cure-all.
Perhaps it's good to remind ourselves, as Alexander Caudarella, CEO of the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction, told the Star Wednesday, that the search for a "silver bullet" will never be successful, because addiction, the illegal drug market and its frequently contaminated supplies, and the quality of life issues that contribute to addiction difficulties are complex and have a lot of layers. It's not about one solution.
But it can't be about giving up on finding a solution either. We can't see more than 500 of our fellow citizens dying each year of a preventable cause - and all the destruction of lives and bodies that occurs even besides those fatalities - and just think we're doing all we can.
In the cases of both B.C. and Oregon, experts studying the results of the decriminalization experiments have said that part of the failure to achieve the hoped-for results can be chalked up to inadequate resources devoted to addiction treatment and street outreach to funnel people into it, alongside things like housing and mental health supports and other general services that can make it easier - or even possible - for many of the most desperately addicted to think about getting clean.
If the provincial and federal governments are saying no to decriminalization, are they saying yes to more resources to help addicts in other ways?
Wait times for addiction treatment in Toronto, Mayor Olivia Chow recently lamented, stretch up to a year. The provincial government, meanwhile, is aiming to impose new "accountability measures" on supervised consumption sites aimed at reducing the threat of overdose (or deaths from them).
Chow, answering questions during a press conference Thursday, said she's asking the federal and provincial governments to partner with the city to fund a 24/7 treatment facility where people could go, that could prevent overdoses, provide counselling and help people find treatment. Discussions with the feds, she said, have been "very, very encouraging."
Ford, at the same press conference, suggested he wants to champion more detox and rehabilitation beds, and suggested he, Chow and the prime minister "were on the same page."
That page, clearly, needs to include a whole heck of a lot more funding to make treatment programs accessible, and perhaps for the kind of outreach and counselling - including, in the criminalization regime currently in place, from police and the Crown - to guide people into treatment.
None of this is easy, or cheap, except talking about it and claiming to want to do something. Things are easy to say, when faced with numbers showing the staggering death toll. They're harder to accomplish.
Rejecting decriminalization is easy too, if the concept is unpopular and you can point to examples that show it hasn't solved the problem. But what we're doing now has created the problem. It's time to start doing something to alleviate it.
With files from Ben Spurr.
CAPTION: Justin Trudeau's government sided with Premier Doug Ford, vetoing the idea of decriminalizing small amounts of hard drugs in an attempt at harm reduction. Deaths from opioids in Toronto remain at record levels.

Subject: Press conferences; Decriminalization; Public health; Drug overdose; Fatalities; Addictions; Substance abuse treatment; Narcotics; Harm reduction

Location: United States--US; Oregon

People: Trudeau, Justin

Easy to reject decriminalization, challenging to solve opioid crisis

Document 211 of 500

No need to rush booze to corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-need-rush-booze-corner-stores/docview/3062390507/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+need+to+rush+booze+to+corner+stores&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford appears seized by a sudden and injudicious haste to get beer, wine and cocktails into convenience stores ahead of schedule.
For a leader with a well-documented track record of rash acts and sheepish reversals, this new fervour can hardly help but raise alarms about potential costs and misplaced priorities.
Late last year, Ford announced plans to allow convenience stores to sell alcohol as of Jan. 1, 2026. His Progressive Conservatives had promised such a move in the election campaigns of 2018 and 2022, but were constrained by a master agreement signed with the Beer Store by the former Liberal government that ran until the end of 2025.
The impatient premier initially muttered about reneging on that agreement in order to get alcohol more widely available more quickly. Until, that is, he was persuaded by cooler minds of the considerable legal costs and lamentable reputation for bad-faith dealing that such a move would cause.
Then last week came word that the premier's craving to get on with things had got the better of him. The Ford government announced that it will pay the Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants Labatt, Molson and Sleeman - $225 million to allow "early implementation" of the expanded alcohol sales and make beer, wine and premixed cocktails available at more types of retail outlets by Oct. 31 this year.
The retailing of alcohol is no one's idea of the most pressing challenge facing Ontario. But from his earliest "buck-a-beer" days as Tory leader, Ford has regularly turned to it to burnish his populist image or distract from misjudgments and flip-flops.
Once again, there is a political subtext to his latest enthusiasm for booze sales.
The move would give Ford flexibility to call a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote by allowing him to claim he had delivered on another popular campaign pledge.
The motivation for an early election call is the belief in Ford circles that he would fare better running for a third term before the possible installation of a Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre in Ottawa.
All Ford would say about such a scenario was a portentous "stay tuned."
The premier insists an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big-box outlets stocking alcohol will boost sales and bring in more in taxes to provincial revenues.
"We're going to see revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion," he told the legislature. "That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care."
But as could reasonably be said before many of the premier's giddy assertions, critics were soon yelling not so fast.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has said the complexities of the deal with brewing giants mean Ford's rush to reform could cost Ontarians hundreds of millions of dollars more, ending up closer to $1 billion than the province's estimated $225 million.
Her Liberals have asked Ontario's auditor general and the legislature's Financial Accountability Office to investigate the true cost of the deal, which seems reasonable.
Opposition Leader Marit Stiles asked Ford this week why he was "pouring money into the pockets of these big alcohol corporations while our emergency rooms are closing?"
Stiles makes a fair point. There is little justification for Ford's urgency.
Ontario was promised sale of beer and wine in corner stores as long ago as 1985. The Star long supported such a measure on the grounds that Ontarians would benefit from the convenience and competition. But while we welcome the end of the Beer Store monopoly and applaud the direction, a change like this, with the risks and costs it entails, should be done with care rather than politically motivated haste.
It makes more sense to stick to the province's previous timeline and to spend the (at least) $225 million on truly urgent issues such as the province's crisis of primary health care or housing.
We have waited nearly 40 years for beer to reach convenience stores. Given the price tag, we can wait one more.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, left, and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy after last week's announcement that beer and wine will be available at corner stores in the fall.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star

Why did Trudeau screw up so badly?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-did-trudeau-screw-up-so-badly/docview/3062390352/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+did+Trudeau+screw+up+so+badly%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The question I have these days about Justin Trudeau is not "will he run again?" (which is unknowable at the moment), but "how did he and the Liberals screw things up so badly?" How did they manage to fall from the heights of "sunny ways" back in 2015 to the electoral depths they're stuck in now?
I read Stephen Maher's new book, "The Prince: The Turbulent Reign of Justin Trudeau," with that in mind, looking for clues as to when and why the wheels came off the Liberal bus.
The arc of the story is well known. Trudeau swept into power as a "handsome, smiling prince" (Maher's words) riding a wave of public optimism. The SNC-Lavalin affair of 2019, with the resignation of two top female ministers, cracked that image, perhaps definitively. But Canadians mostly rallied around the government during the pandemic, allowing Trudeau to squeeze out a third victory, albeit a minority one.
By the fall of 2022, though, the Liberals really started losing the thread. They didn't see the cost-of-living crisis coming on. They failed to come to grips with housing and immigration, and they didn't grasp the connection between those issues. They didn't go at Pierre Poilievre hard when he took over as Conservative leader, leaving him a clear field to define himself as the champion of middle-class Canada.
Most obviously, the Liberals completely bungled the foreign interference file. If they'd taken it seriously and done what pretty much everyone suggested - set up a foreign agents register and promise an inquiry - they could have contained the damage.
Instead, inexplicably, they dodged and weaved and turned a problem into a crisis. By the summer of 2023, they'd fallen to 20 points behind the Conservatives and nothing they've done since has turned that around.
It was quite predictable that the post-pandemic era would bring new challenges, so why were Trudeau and the Liberals so unable to get their heads around them? How did they miss the moment so badly?
Perhaps it's just that people get tired of a strong personality like Trudeau and the "pious scolding" (Maher's words again) he too often aims at those who don't share his progressive vision. But the Liberals didn't really fight back for months on end.
In part, Liberal insiders told Maher, it's because the government is so highly centralized. "Everything gets jammed in Trudeau's office," he writes, "so nothing gets done except for the top items handled effectively by the small team of talented, hard-working staffers trusted by the boss." The result looked a lot like paralysis throughout 2023, which Maher calls the Liberals' "annus horribilis."
I wonder if part of the answer is hiding in plain sight. Canadian journalists don't like to pry into politicians' private lives. That, we tell ourselves, is for crass Americans and online trolls. But you can take that to extremes, and in Trudeau's case it's possible we're missing something obvious.
Right in the middle of the Liberals' year from hell, Maher notes, Trudeau and his wife Sophie announced they were splitting up. "The announcement," he writes, "was not a shock," though I have to say it was a bit of a shock to me and I daresay to most Canadians who aren't privy to Ottawa gossip. Political journalists may have known the marriage was rocky but as far as I remember they didn't write about it.
It became clear from divorce papers that by April of 2023 Sophie Grégoire Trudeau was already "re-partnered" with an Ottawa surgeon. The breakup "was a long time coming," writes Maher. I can't get inside Trudeau's head, but it's hardly a stretch to imagine that the collapse of his marriage had weighed on him for a long time - throughout that crucial stretch in 2022 and 2023 when the government seemed frozen while new problems came at it thick and fast.
Maybe that had no effect on how Trudeau operated in the office. Maybe he's that rare person able to put aside personal issues and stay on top of his game. Or maybe, just maybe, "the boss" lost focus at exactly the wrong moment for the future of his government and party.
Andrew Phillips is a Toronto-based staff columnist for the Star's Opinion page. Reach him via email: aphillips@thestar.ca
CAPTION: It's hardly a stretch to imagine that the collapseof Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's marriage had weighed on him for a long time - throughout that crucial stretch in 2022 and 2023 when the government seemed frozen while new problems came at it thick and fast, writes Andrew Phillips.The Canadian Press file photo

Air travellers are feeling the turbulent effects of global warming

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/air-travellers-are-feeling-turbulent-effects/docview/3061993339/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Air+travellers+are+feeling+the+turbulent+effects+of+global+warming&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 12 people injured after Qatar Airways plane hits turbulence on flight to Dublin, May 26
Several injured passengers on turbulence-hit Singapore flight need spinal surgery, hospital says, May 23
Twelve people were injured by severe turbulence on a Qatar Airways flight from Qatar to Dublin. Singapore Airlines experienced a similar event where one passenger died and many were injured. A sharp increase has been observed in this phenomenon, once considered normal in the aviation world. While previously it could be ignored, scientists are now attributing it to global warming and have warned that this is going to increase.
Scientists have been warning about this for decades. The legendary Carl Sagan detailed these concerns during a Senate hearing back in 1985. However, irresponsibility on the part of world leaders has exacerbated the issue over the last few decades. Naked greed and the exponential rise in industrialization have paid little attention to this most serious subject of our time. What is the point of amassing gold and money when the only habitable planet is increasingly becoming uninhabitable at a rapid pace?
Some global leaders of the past publicly made jokes about the Paris Agreement and other environmental protocols. Here in Canada, people loathe the carbon tax, seeing it as just another burden without understanding its value. The increasing population is eating up farmlands for housing, and the delicate balance of the life-sustaining ecosystem is destabilizing at a rate that was hardly thought possible in the past. We are destroying our planet.
Bahadar Ali Khan, Richmond Hill
A nationally funded mandatory service program could be a unity builder
Don't laugh at the UK: Mandatory service for young Canadians might be just what we need, May 29
Expanding the Canada Service Corps to include a mandatory service period for Canadian youth could be just the nation-building exercise we need . I'd go one step further, make it a government-supported exchange program. Canada is a big country with several regional and cultural identities. For a few weeks during their service, youth would be sent to volunteer in another part of the country.
Imagine the insights, compassion and understanding that our youth could develop living and working in a community in a different region or cultural setting?
With the planned arrival of 500,000 newcomers per year until 2030, the demographic landscape of Canada will change. It's in our collective best interest to make immigrants feel welcome and hasten their integration. Through a nationally funded mandatory service program we could pass along our Canadian values of multiculturalism, fairness and social justice to immigrant youth while also exposing our native-born to diversity.
Ryan Frigault, Toronto
Pot calling the kettle black Prosecutor says Trump tried 'to hoodwink voters' while defence attacks key witness in last arguments, May 28
Donald Trump's defence lawyers have branded his erstwhile lawyer and fixer, Michael Cohen, the "Greatest Liar of all Time." By way of comparison, the Washington Post's fact-checker team tallied up no less than 30,573 false or misleading statements made by Trump during the four years of his presidency - and that was only counting the lies he spouted publicly. Truly, there can be no doubt about who is really the GLOAT.
Mick Welch, Toronto
What if every artist spoke out on any issue they are passionate about at every award ceremony
Kagiso Molope's challenge at Ottawa gala spotlights artists fighting institutional silence on Gaza, May 21
Any artist who feels he or she has the right to express their views while disrupting an awards ceremony is working on the principle that their ideas are more important than anyone else's and that they have the right, perhaps the obligation to spoil happy occasions for others in order to do so. Shree Parkadar's column reported on Kagiso Molope's speech and the writer's claim that it is difficult for artists to "speak out."
Other artists may have had other issues that were important to them or opposing views to Molope's. They too, according to her, have the right and obligation to "speak out."
If all artists followed Molope's example we could have a debate every time the Junos or Oscars came around as every artist spoke out on any issue she or he felt passionate about.
Molope has shown her lack of respect for the opinions and rights of others. An awards ceremony is not the time to spout one's one-sided views.
Jacqueline Mills, Kingston
Ford makes no economic sense
In Premier Doug Ford, we have a teetotaller of a leader who thinks he knows what beer and wine drinkers want. A man who has no economic sense. He's closing beer stores and turning them into recycling centres. Now I can buy my favourite suds down at the corner but have to return the empties to The Beer Store. My local convenience store will want nothing to do with returns. Ford has created thousands more places to distribute to adding to the carbon footprint. Please keep him away from the cash register. He doesn't know how to add or subtract. He doesn't know that he doesn't know.
John Adamthwaite, Thornhill
Closing down tobacco sales will require enforcement, political willDoug Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business, May 27
Provinces should take all steps to close down the legal sale of tobacco, while federally we should follow the lead of European countries phasing in an age-related ban, to ensure the next generation is not hooked. This will require significant enforcement, and the political will not to look the other way when Indigenous retailers swoop in to fill the gap. None of it will be easy, but it will be worthwhile.
Doug Lewis, Clarington
Spend time in a LTC home before denying the option of MAID
Let's care for our elderly rather than offering an easy way out, May 4
Recently, I lost a family member to the ravages of Alzheimer's disease. I'm currently helping to care for my wife who was diagnosed with the same horrific illness 12 years ago. Our ordeal might still have several years to run.
Daily trips to her nursing home - and as such institutions go, it is an excellent one - repeatedly expose me to the tragic situations of most of the residents there. None of them had or would have today, the option of choosing a humane and dignified death through MAID.
Some of them receive periodic visitors. Others none at all. The majority have lost all personal independence and pass most of the remaining time in beds or wheelchairs. The sights and sounds can be disturbing.
Before rushing to deny them "an easy way out," folks should spend some time in our LTC homes keeping in mind the theme of the Broadway play/Hollywood movie "Whose Life Is It Anyway?"
Allan Evans, Burlington

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, May 30 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on dentists skeptical of Canada's national dental plan, potential flaws in Doug Ford's Beer Store deal and Canada's family reunification program.
DON'T MISS
More Canadians will qualify for the federal dental care plan in June - but thousands of dentists haven't signed on
More than a million Canadians are set to gain access to the Liberal government's dental care plan next month - but the leaders of all but one of Canada's dental associations have yet to sign up. They join the roughly two-thirds of the nation's oral health providers that haven't signed on to the program, citing unanswered questions they say Ottawa must resolve as the plan continues to expand, Raisa Patel reports. Privacy issues are near the top of the list - wording in the current agreements means dental staff would have to release their patients' personal information to the government, for example. Take a closer look at their concerns.
The details: While an exact date to apply for the program has not been set, those under 18 and people with disabilities will become eligible for dental coverage starting in June.Word from the health minister: Mark Holland's office said nearly 10,000 providers have signed up to offer the program, and that 120,000 people have received services. He was having "conversations" to ensure more providers would climb on board.Doug Ford claims his beer store deal will be a money-maker - here's why critics say he's wrong
Depending on who you ask, Premier Doug Ford's alcohol liberalization gambit is either a $225 million investment that will reap dividends for the treasury - or a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle," Robert Benzie reports. Ford told his legislature that bringing alcohol to supermarkets and convenience stores will lead to "new revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion." But while liberalization in jurisdictions like Alberta made it more convenient for consumers, it didn't necessarily net greater government revenues - partly because beer prices spiked so high, the province was forced to lower the tax it charged. Here's why Ford's Beer Store deal has some critics concerned.
Wait, what? Convenience stores across Ontario will be allowed to sell beer, wine, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails starting Sept. 5 - but prices could be up to 50 per cent higher than in the LCBO, industry insiders say.Context: By buying out the Beer Store's control over certain alcohol sales, Ford can be seen as fulfilling one of his key campaign promises, giving him more flexibility to hold a snap election. When asked whether one was coming, he said: "Stay tuned."These immigrants can't even get into the queue to sponsor parents and grandparents
Ayon De chose to immigrate to Canada instead of the U.S. because he thought his parents could join him faster under Ottawa's family reunification program. But after being here for almost six years, the India native hasn't even been able to get into the queue to sponsor his parents to join him. It's already too late for his father, who died of cancer in September, Nicholas Keung reports. Now, to the outrage of potential sponsors like De, Canada's Immigration Department has closed the program to new candidates for the fourth year in a row, only drawing from the pool of applications submitted back in 2020. What's going on?
What we know: Canadian citizens and permanent residents who submitted an expression of interest to the program may be randomly chosen to apply for a limited number of sponsorship spots. Officials will then screen them for eligibility based on criteria like their income level.More: Potential sponsors are upset by Ottawa's goal to only accept up to 20,500 complete applications out of all the invitees. With another 1.3 million new permanent residents admitted in Canada over the last three years, they say demand for the program will only keep climbing.WHAT ELSE

Document 215 of 500

Doug Ford slammed for 'racist' remarks after suggesting immigrants to blame for shooting at Toronto Jewish school

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-slammed-racist-remarks-after-suggesting/docview/3061992903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+slammed+for+%27racist%27+remarks+after+suggesting+immigrants+to+blame+for+shooting+at+Toronto+Jewish+school&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford was forced to do damage control after appearing to blame immigrants for the weekend shooting at a Jewish girls' school in North York.
Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau had condemned the attack at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, which was shot up by two unidentified suspects around 5 a.m. Saturday.
Speaking to reporters just five kilometres from the school, the premier seemed to point the finger at newcomers to Canada - despite the fact police have not identified the shooters.
"You're bringing your problems from everywhere else in the world, you're bringing them to Ontario and you're going after other Canadians," he said, as Trudeau and Mayor Olivia Chow stood nearby, their eyebrows raised.
Under fire from many quarters -- including Trudeau's special representative for combatting Islamophobia -- Ford later scrambled to clarify his statement.
"My comments today meant to stress that there is more that unites us than divides us. While there will always be room for disagreement, violent acts that target specific religions or ethnicities do not reflect who we are or the values that represent our province," he said.
Prior to Ford's comments, Trudeau said he was "disgusted by these vile and despicable acts of antisemitism."
"Just a short drive away, parents dropped off their daughters this morning at a Jewish girls' elementary school that was shot at over the weekend. And in Montreal, another Jewish school was targeted by an overnight shooting last night," he said.
"It needs to stop right now," the prime minister said at the opening of Sanofi's new $800-million vaccine manufacturing facility on Steeles Avenue West he attended with Ford and Chow.
"The Jewish parents and students across this country, we stand with you. We will always work to safeguard your right to live proudly Jewish lives in Canada," he said.
Ford expressed outrage at the increase in antisemitic hate crimes in the wake of the Hamas-Israel war that began last October.
"What lunatic goes around shooting up schools? Imagine a little kid in a school and because they're from the Jewish faith someone goes by and starts shooting through the school windows? These guys need to be caught, they need to be punished, they need to be thrown in jail," said the premier.
But he sparked furor when he said "don't come to Canada if you're going to start terrorizing neighbourhoods like this, simple as that."
"If you're a resident of Ontario, you get along with everyone, I don't care what background, what religion, what race you come from," he said.
Stressing that "diversity is our number one selling tool around the world," Ford said there is no place for racial discrimination in Ontario.
Amira Elghawaby, the federal government's point person on combatting Islamophobia, said "legitimizing irrational fears, scapegoating, or spreading unfounded conspiracies about immigrants, or any group, undermines social cohesion and safety."
"We can and should condemn antisemitism and the heinous acts against Jewish schools without fueling further hate and division," said Elghawaby.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she was "appalled by the premier's racist remarks" by suggesting new Canadians were to blame for the attack.
"Fighting hate with hate has never worked. Fighting antisemitism with xenophobia won't keep communities safe. He should apologize," Stiles said in a statement.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "dividing the people of Ontario isn't leadership - it further promotes hate and xenophobia."
"Our province deserves better from its premier. Doug Ford must apologize now," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said it was "dangerous and unacceptable rhetoric from Premier Ford."
"What happened at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School was an alarming and unacceptable act of antisemitism," said Schreiner.
"But using it as an opportunity to stoke anti-immigrant sentiment only fans the flames of division and increases tension at a time when we as leaders should be focused on helping our community members come together and heal," he said.
Meanwhile, Chow noted she had visited the school on Monday and said city council has been working with the local community on safety measures.
She said that can include "shatterproof glass, fencing, lighting or trimming of bushes or cameras" to make vulnerable schools or places of worship feel more safe and secure.
Prior to the controversy over Ford's comments, federal Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks, who was also in attendance at Sanofi, praised the premier and prime minister for expressing support for the Jewish community.
"Allies matter. As a Jewish mother in this community we have the right to be safe and feel safe each and every day," said Saks.
Toronto police have said it is still too early to determine if the Saturday shooting was hate-motivated.
"It was pretty shocking," Rabbi Yaacov Vidal, the school principal, said Sunday.
"I think it's very sad that people target schools where children are innocent and just create this extra fear and chaos for no reason."
At a rally in support of the community on Monday, Daniel Held, chief program officer at the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, said the shooting "was a deliberate attempt to spread fear across our entire Jewish community, to make us cower and hide who we are, but as you can see here today, the attackers completely failed."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford attend an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston, Ont. on April 25, 2024.

Document 216 of 500

Will Beer Store plan pay off?: Critics say taxpayers could be on the hook for far more than $225M

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-beer-store-plan-pay-off/docview/3061629736/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Beer+Store+plan+pay+off%3F%3A+Critics+say+taxpayers+could+be+on+the+hook+for+far+more+than+%24225M&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is Premier Doug Ford's alcohol liberalization gambit a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" or a $225-million investment that will reap dividends for the treasury?
It depends who you ask.
The premier jolted Ontario's political landscape last Friday when he announced beer, wine and premixed cocktails would be more widely available at many different types of retail outlets by Oct. 31.
Ford said that's because the province is paying the Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants Labatt, Molson and Sleeman - $225 million to end their restrictive 10-year "master framework agreement" earlier than its slated Dec. 31, 2025 completion.
That move gives the premier the flexibility to hold a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote because he can be seen to have delivered on a key campaign pledge to voters.
While Ford is coy about a snap vote - "stay tuned" was all he would say after the Star revealed he's actively thinking about it - he insists his reforms will bring in more taxes.
"We're going to see new revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion. That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care," he told the legislature Tuesday, insisting an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets will translate into more sales.
But recent history has shown that while liberalization in other jurisdictions, such as Alberta, led to greater convenience for consumers, it did not necessarily mean increased government revenues.
In part, that's because beer prices spiked so high in Alberta that the government was forced to lower the tax it charged in order to offset price hikes that had drinkers fuming.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the bar tab for Ford's changes could be three or four times higher than he claims.
"He's spending more than $1 billion to cater to wealthy insiders at the expense of our public services," Crombie said Monday.
"This billion-dollar booze boondoggle is the latest example of Doug Ford lining the pockets of the wealthy, well-connected few at the expense of the people of Ontario," she said, charging the only beneficiaries will be "Ford's friends at the major grocery chains, who are already making billions in record profits, and big-box American stores."
Crombie said the $225-million payout to the Beer Store - which the premier said is designed to "protect jobs," because scores of outlets will inevitably close since the company will no longer enjoy a monopoly on sales of 24-packs - is only the beginning.
As first reported by the Star, the rebate of service fees the Beer Store pays to the government-owned LCBO is worth about $250 million a year, so over the 18 months of the wind-down agreement, that could be $375 million.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office disputes that figure, but industry sources do not.
Additionally, there is a 10 per cent discount for the private companies that must buy their products from the LCBO, which could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
As well, the government is not charging any licensing fees for the 8,500 new retail outlets, which could mean foregone revenue of at least $300 million.
Finally, an amendment to the Ontario Deposit Return Program - the bottle recycling system run by the breweries and bankrolled by the government since 2007 - will see the subsidy paid for each recycled container jump from about 11 cents to more than 16 cents.
Industry sources say that could mean another $25 million for the brewing giants, who annually divert 445 million wine and spirits bottles from landfill. (The company also recycles 1.2 billion beer bottles and cans each year.)
In total, the tab could be as much as $1.075 billion - even though Ford and Bethlenfalvy insist taxpayers will only be on the hook for $225 million.
If the master framework agreement had been allowed to expire on schedule at the end of next year, none of these costs would have been incurred by the provincial treasury.
"It's very complicated, but at the end of the day, we were losing hundreds of millions of dollars," the premier assured Newstalk 1010 host Jerry Agar on Tuesday.
"What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people, treating them like adults and creating hundreds of millions of dollars of revenue."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says that by expanding alcohol sales to an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets, the province will "see new revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion," money that he says can go toward areas such as health care.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

NDP calls for end to evictions in retirement homes: Ontario senior's life 'turned upside down,' family says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-calls-end-evictions-retirement-homes/docview/3061626424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+calls+for+end+to+evictions+in+retirement+homes%3A+Ontario+senior%27s+life+%27turned+upside+down%2C%27+family+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario NDP is calling on Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives to amend the Residential Tenancies Act to prevent the eviction of Ontario retirement home residents.
The demand comes in the wake of the eviction of more than 200 residents of Heritage Glen Retirement Residence in Mississauga earlier this year. Owner Chartwell told the residents, many of whom are in their 80s and 90s, in March that they had until the end of July to vacate their units for "extensive" renovations.
The property is being sold to real estate giant Minto, which plans to redevelop the property into residential rental units for all ages.
"Seniors need safe, affordable places to live, but when the bulk of seniors' housing exists to make profits for investors, a real estate trust, like Chartwell, can sell their homes out from under them," said the NDP's seniors critic, MPP Lise Vaugeois, at a news conference Wednesday morning at Queen's Park, where she was joined by several relatives of Heritage Glen's evicted residents.
"It is unconscionable for a senior on a fixed income to be suddenly uprooted and told to find another place to live. For many, it will be impossible to find comparable housing or any housing at all that they can afford."
Unlike seniors in long-term care, retirement home residents are treated as tenants under the province's Residential Tenancies Act, which means they can be evicted - as long as the landlord can demonstrate to the Landlord and Tenant Board that they intend to do the repair and renovation work described on the eviction notice and that such work requires the tenant to leave.
However, under the act, tenants, including retirement home residents, who receive an eviction notice for these reasons do not have to move out right away.
The Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing did not answer the Star's question about whether it would consider amending the Residential Tenancies Act to prevent the eviction of seniors from retirement homes.
Responding to the NDP during question period Wednesday, Minister for Seniors and Accessibility Raymond Cho said Heritage Glen has been working with the Retirement Homes Regulatory Authority to transition residents to a new home.
Chartwell says it has helped more than 170 Heritage Glen residents find alternative accommodations as part of an assistance package, which includes moving costs and compensation in the amount of three months' rent.
"The decision to close Heritage Glen was not made lightly," said Chartwell spokesperson Mary Perrone Lisi.
"We have been operating at less than 60 per cent capacity for several years. That, combined with the aging infrastructure of the residence, made it unsustainable to continue operating as a retirement residence. Its future use as a multi-residential rental unit for all age groups will add much needed capacity to Mississauga's rental inventory."
Such comments are cold comfort to Karen Santaguida, whose mother, Joyce Dodge, 92, who has lived at Heritage Glen for nearly 20 years and was shocked to learn on March 19 that she was being evicted.
"Our lives have been turned upside down," said Santaguida at Wednesday's press conference, adding that Chartwell has "decimated" Heritage Glen's community of residents by closing the building.
"Everybody is working really hard at trying to get Chartwell held accountable and to change the laws going forward at the provincial level to protect seniors. Because one day, it's going to be all of us.
"We need this to stop and we plead for this to end."
CREDIT: Kenyon Wallace Toronto Star

Subject: Retirement homes; Evictions

Full text: Air travellers feeling effects of global warming
12 people injured after Qatar Airways plane hits turbulence on flight to Dublin, May 26 Several injured passengers on turbulence-hit Singapore flight need spinal surgery, hospital says, May 23
Twelve people were injured by severe turbulence on a Qatar Airways flight from Qatar to Dublin. Singapore Airlines experienced a similar event where one passenger died and many were injured. A sharp increase has been observed in this phenomenon. While previously it could be ignored, scientists are now attributing it to global warming and have warned that this is going to increase.
Scientists have been warning about this for decades. The legendary Carl Sagan detailed these concerns during a Senate hearing back in 1985. However, irresponsibility on the part of world leaders has exacerbated the issue over the last few decades. Naked greed and the rise in industrialization have paid little attention to this most serious subject of our time.
Some global leaders of the past publicly made jokes about the Paris Agreement and other environmental protocols. Here in Canada, people loathe the carbon tax, seeing it as just another burden without understanding its value. The increasing population is eating up farmlands for housing, and the delicate balance of the life-sustaining ecosystem is destabilizing at a rate that was hardly thought possible in the past. We are destroying our planet.
Bahadar Ali Khan,
Richmond Hill
National service program could
be a unity builder
Don't laugh at the U.K.:
Mandatory service for young Canadians might be just what we need, May 29
Expanding the Canada Service Corps to include a mandatory service period for Canadian youth could be just the nation-building exercise we need. I'd go one step further, make it a government-supported exchange program. Canada is a big country with several regional and cultural identities. For a few weeks during their service, youth would be sent to volunteer in another part of the country.
With the planned arrival of 500,000 newcomers per year until 2030, the demographic landscape of Canada will change. It's in our collective best interest to make immigrants feel welcome and hasten their integration. Through a nationally funded mandatory service program we could pass along our Canadian values of multiculturalism, fairness and social justice to immigrant youth while also exposing our native-born to diversity.
Ryan Frigault, Toronto
Pot calling
kettle black
Prosecutor says Trump tried 'to hoodwink voters' while defence attacks key witness in last arguments, May 28
Donald Trump's defence lawyers have branded his erstwhile lawyer and fixer, Michael Cohen, the "Greatest Liar of all Time." By way of comparison, the Washington Post's fact-checker team tallied up no less than 30,573 false or misleading statements made by Trump during the four years of his presidency - and that was only counting the lies he spouted publicly. Truly, there can be no doubt about who is really the GLOAT.
Mick Welch, Toronto
Premier's plan makes no sense
In Premier Doug Ford, we have a teetotaller of a leader who thinks he knows what beer and wine drinkers want. A man who has no economic sense. He's closing beer stores and turning them into recycling centres. Now I can buy my favourite suds down at the corner but have to return the empties to the Beer Store. My local convenience store will want nothing to do with returns. Ford has created thousands more places to distribute to adding to the carbon footprint. Please keep him away from the cash register.
John Adamthwaite, Thornhill
Awards ceremony not the time to speak on issues
Kagiso Molope's challenge at Ottawa gala spotlights artists fighting institutional silence on Gaza, May 21
Any artist who feels he or she has the right to express their views while disrupting an awards ceremony is working on the principle that their ideas are more important than anyone else's and that they have the right, perhaps the obligation to spoil happy occasions for others in order to do so. Shree Parkadar's column reported on Kagiso Molope's speech and the writer's claim that it is difficult for artists to "speak out."
Other artists may have had other issues that were important to them or opposing views to Molope's. They too, according to her, have the right and obligation to "speak out."
If all artists followed Molope's example we could have a debate every time the Junos or Oscars came around as every artist spoke out on any issue she or he felt passionate about. An awards ceremony is not the time to spout one's views.
Jacqueline Mills, Kingston, Ont.
Closing down tobacco sales
Doug Ford should put
Big Tobacco out of business, May 27
Provinces should take all steps to close down the legal sale of tobacco, while federally we should follow the lead of European countries phasing in an age-related ban, to ensure the next generation is not hooked. This will require significant enforcement, and the political will not to look the other way when Indigenous retailers swoop in to fill the gap. None of it will be easy, but it will be worthwhile.
Doug Lewis, Clarington
Spend time in LTC home before criticizing MAID
Let's care for our elderly rather than offering an easy way out, May 4
Recently, I lost a family member to the ravages of Alzheimer's disease. I'm currently helping to care for my wife who was diagnosed with the same horrific illness 12 years ago. Our ordeal might still have several years to run. Daily trips to her nursing home repeatedly expose me to the tragic situations of most of the residents there. None of them had or would have today, the option of choosing a humane and dignified death through MAID.
Some of them receive periodic visitors. Others none at all. The majority have lost all personal independence and pass most of the remaining time in beds or wheelchairs. The sights and sounds can be disturbing.
Before rushing to deny them "an easy way out," folks should spend some time in our LTC homes, keeping in mind the theme of the Broadway play/Hollywood movie "Whose Life Is It Anyway?"
Allan Evans, Burlington

Document 219 of 500

NDP calls for amendment to end 'unconscionable' seniors' homes evictions in Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-calls-amendment-end-unconscionable-seniors/docview/3061626274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+calls+for+amendment+to+end+%27unconscionable%27+seniors%27+homes+evictions+in+Ontario&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
