Document 220 of 500

Doug Ford claims his $225M beer store deal will be a money-maker. Here's why critics say he's wrong - and how taxpayers could be on the hook for far more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-claims-his-225m-beer-store-deal-will-be/docview/3062239185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+claims+his+%24225M+beer+store+deal+will+be+a+money-maker.+Here%27s+why+critics+say+he%27s+wrong+-+and+how+taxpayers+could+be+on+the+hook+for+far+more&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is Premier Doug Ford's alcohol liberalization gambit a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" or a $225-million investment that will reap dividends for the treasury?
It depends who you ask.
The premier jolted Ontario's political landscape last Friday when he announced beer, wine and premixed cocktails would be more widely available at many different types of retail outlets by Oct. 31.
Ford said that's because the province is paying The Beer Store - which is controlled by foreign-owned brewing giants Labatt, Molson and Sleeman - $225 million to end their restrictive 10-year "master framework agreement" earlier than its slated Dec. 31, 2025 completion.
That move gives the premier the flexibility to hold a provincial election sooner than the scheduled June 2026 vote because he can be seen to have delivered on a key campaign pledge to voters.
While Ford is coy about a snap vote - "stay tuned" was all he would say after the Star revealed he's actively thinking about it - he insists his reforms will bring in more taxes.
"We're going to see new revenues of $895 million to $1.16 billion. That's hundreds of millions of dollars that we can put towards health care," he told the legislature Tuesday, insisting an additional 8,500 supermarkets, convenience stores and big box outlets will translate into more sales.
But recent history has shown that while liberalization in other jurisdictions, such as Alberta, led to greater convenience for consumers, it did not necessarily mean increased government revenues.
In part, that's because beer prices spiked so high in Alberta that the government was forced to lower the tax it charged in order to offset price hikes that had drinkers fuming.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie says the bar tab for Ford's changes could be three or four times higher than he claims.
"He's spending more than $1 billion to cater to wealthy insiders at the expense of our public services," Crombie said Monday.
"This billion-dollar booze boondoggle is the latest example of Doug Ford lining the pockets of the wealthy, well-connected few at the expense of the people of Ontario," she said, charging the only beneficiaries will be "Ford's friends at the major grocery chains, who are already making billions in record profits, and big-box American stores."
Crombie said the $225-million payout to The Beer Store - which the premier said is designed to "protect jobs," because scores of outlets will inevitably close since the company will no longer enjoy a monopoly on sales of 24-packs - is only the beginning.
As first reported by the Star, the rebate of service fees The Beer Store pays to the government-owned LCBO is worth about $250 million a year, so over the 18 months of the wind-down agreement, that could be $375 million.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's office disputes that figure, but industry sources do not.
Additionally, there is a 10 per cent discount for the private companies that must buy their products from the LCBO, which could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
As well, the government is not charging any licensing fees for the 8,500 new retail outlets, which could mean foregone revenue of at least $300 million.
Finally, an amendment to the Ontario Deposit Return Program - the bottle recycling system run by the breweries and bankrolled by the government since 2007 - will see the subsidy paid for each recycled container jump from about 11 cents to more than 16 cents.
Industry sources say that could mean another $25 million for the brewing giants, who annually divert 445 million wine and spirits bottles from landfill. (The company also recycles 1.2 billion beer bottles and cans each year.)
In total, the tab could be as much as $1.075 billion - even though Ford and Bethlenfalvy insist taxpayers will only be on the hook for $225 million. If the master framework agreement had been allowed to expire on schedule at the end of next year, none of these costs would have been incurred by the provincial treasury.
"It's very complicated, but at the end of the day, we were losing hundreds of millions of dollars," the premier assured Newstalk 1010 host Jerry Agar on Tuesday.
"What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people, treating them like adults and creating hundreds of millions of dollars of revenue."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at a media event in Toronto on May 24, 2024, where he announced that beer and wine sales will soon be allowed in Ontario's corner stores.

Subject: Recycling; Beer; Liquor; Liberalization; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Liberalization Breweries

Title: Doug Ford claims his $225M beer store deal will be a money-maker. Here's why critics say he's wrong - and how taxpayers could be on the hook for far more

Document 221 of 500

Five more Ontario school boards and two private schools file lawsuits against Snapchat, TikTok and Meta, alleging they're harming students

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/five-more-ontario-school-boards-two-private/docview/3061275386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+more+Ontario+school+boards+and+two+private+schools+file+lawsuits+against+Snapchat%2C+TikTok+and+Meta%2C+alleging+they%27re+harming+students&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Five more boards and two private religious schools in Ontario have joined multi-billion lawsuits targeting TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram, alleging the social media giants have hurt not only students' health and well-being but also their academic achievement, the Star has learned.
The most recent lawsuits, filed Tuesday, total almost $2.6 billion, on top of the $4.5 billion sought by the original four boards - Toronto public, Toronto Catholic, Peel public and Ottawa-Carleton public - that launched similar court action in March.
The new plaintiffs are the Catholic boards in Ottawa, Dufferin-Peel and York, the District School Board Niagara, Trillium Lakelands public, as well as Eitz Chaim Jewish day school in Toronto and Holy Name of Mary College School in Mississauga.
Although use of social media is an issue across his school board, Trillium Lakelands Director of Education Wes Hahn said it is complicated because he has schools in urban and also in rural areas of Muskoka, Kawartha and Haliburton, where kids are more isolated.
"We've been noticing the increased phone use, the bullying from that phone, the addictions to some of the gaming, the Instagram bullying, the Snapchat bullying - all of that certainly was happening prior, but post-COVID we started to see it regularly in our suspensions and expulsions," he said. There has also been a rise in student anxiety, which "we attribute to the use of social media and the addictive nature of being on their phones," he said.
Rabbi Mordechai Loiterman of Eitz Chaim said social media has had the same impact, but at his schools has also "interfered with the religious education" of students.
While phones are banned, "we did a survey with our own students and we asked them what's the first thing they do (when they get home) now that they are unplugged for the entire school day? ... And of course, the first thing is, 'We go online and check out YouTube videos or some other social media content to see what we missed.'
"And they get on it, and the obvious problems are - when are they doing their homework? When are they socializing? When are they exercising? And it goes from there, because the nature of the product is to keep them focused, and addicted."
The boards and two schools have filed their own separate, but similar cases in Ontario's Superior Court of Justice, alleging that the popular social media platforms "have rewired the way children think, behave, and learn" and are calling on the companies to make improvements, say their statements of claim.
Schools have had to bring in staff, resources and programming to address behavioural and learning issues, say the statements of claim.
None of the allegations has been tested in court, though the social media companies do plan to defend the claims.
A spokesperson for Snapchat said the platform "was intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends. Snapchat opens directly to a camera - rather than a feed of content - and has no traditional public likes or comments. While we will always have more work to do, we feel good about the role Snapchat plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson said "we want to reassure every parent that we have their interests at heart in the work we're doing to provide teens with safe, supportive experiences" on Facebook and Instagram.
The spokesperson noted it has "more than 30 tools to support teens and their families, including tools that allow parents to decide when, and for how long, their teens use Instagram, age verification technology, automatically setting accounts belonging to those under 16 to private when they join Instagram, and sending notifications encouraging teens to take regular breaks."
Meta says it has also "invested in technology that finds and removes content related to suicide, self-injury or eating disorders before anyone reports it to us. These are complex issues, but we will continue working with experts and listening to parents to develop new tools, features and policies that are effective and meet the needs of teens and their families."
As well, the default setting for both Instagram and Facebook content is the most restrictive.
The boards and two schools are represented by Neinstein LLP, and will not be out of pocket for legal costs as the firm will take a contingency fee.
About 500 school districts in the U.S. have launched similar cases, but these are believed to be the only ones in Canada.
Both Premier Doug Ford and Education Minister Stephen Lecce have chided the boards for taking the court action and wasting staff resources and time. In March, Ford said, "Let's focus on the core values of education - let's focus on math, reading and writing. That's what we need to do, put all the resources into the kids."
In new legislation to help curb kids' access to harmful online content, Lecce noted that he reached out to social media companies to work with the government to help find solutions, rather than turning to the courts.
CAPTION: The Catholic boards in Ottawa, Dufferin-Peel and York, the District School Board Niagara, Trillium Lakelands public, as well as Eitz Chaim Jewish day school in Toronto andHoly Name of Mary College School in Mississauga have joinedmulti-billion lawsuits targeting TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook and Instagram.

Subject: Families & family life; Students; Bullying; Education; Social networks; School boards; School districts

Title: Five more Ontario school boards and two private schools file lawsuits against Snapchat, TikTok and Meta, alleging they're harming students

Document 222 of 500

Human rights tribunal's abysmal showing is part of social justice failures under Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/human-rights-tribunals-abysmal-showing-is-part/docview/3061272420/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Human+rights+tribunal%27s+abysmal+showing+is+part+of+social+justice+failures+under+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Here is a fun stat. There is about as much chance of getting a summary hearing at Ontario's human rights court as there is of the Leafs winning the Stanley Cup each year. Which is to say, minuscule. A 1/32 chance, to be precise.
Not so fun is the alarm bells being sounded by Tribunal Watch in its newest report on the state of the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario, which says an "unprecedented 96 per cent" of all final HRTO decisions in 2023-24 were dismissals of applications for "jurisdictional or procedural" reasons, in which members of the public looking for justice did not have an opportunity to make oral submissions or attempt mediation.
This abysmal grading for the quasi-judicial body, taken together with the dilution of anti-racism directorate duties and a new bill impacting mental health and anti-racism policies in universities, exposes how the province is quashing progress on the intrinsic human right to live without discrimination in Ontario.
In 2018, two years after the creation of the anti-racism directorate - which was itself 10 years in the making - Doug Ford came into power and swiftly made cuts to its programs and disbanded its advisory subcommittees on anti-Black racism, anti-Indigenous racism, antisemitism and Islamophobia.
At the same time, his government issued an ultimatum to universities asking them to come up with policies to protect free speech or else face funding cuts. The faux panic over "campus free speech" was imported as a wedge issue from the U.S. where students at the time were shutting down speeches on campuses by far-right provocateurs such as Milo Yiannopoulos.
In 2023, at least one advocacy group raised concerns about anti-racism strategies disappearing from the Ministry of Education website. Earlier this year the ministry forced the Toronto school board to take down an anti-racism teaching resource that called white supremacy a "structural reality" and called for the decolonization of education.
Last month, a new bill sought to again bring university practices under the Ford government's thumb. Bill 166, called the Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, is touted as a safety and support mechanism for post-secondary students. Critics say it undermines the independence of Ontario's universities by allowing the government to dictate policies on anti-racism and mental health, and that it creates more red tape rather than offering additional funding.
On Monday, the Colleges and Universities Minister of the same free-speech championing Ford government told media she wanted to see an encampment of students at the University of Toronto "cleaned up and dealt with."
The students are exercising their right to free expression in support of Palestinian lives. The issue of free speech, it seems, is rarely about genuine threats to free expression but about political expediency.
Within this context, it may not be surprising that backlogs at the tribunal began growing after this government came into power in 2018, but it is no less distressing. It's a backlog that has, the tribunal watchdog found, doubled in the past six years even though fewer people are now filing complaints.
Why would they file any, when they know their application is overwhelmingly likely to be dismissed?
Applicants whose cases were dismissed for "jurisdictional or procedural" reasons abandoned their applications in almost 80 per cent of cases. When the watchdog reviewed those who had abandoned complaints in 2024, it found an "overwhelming majority" were filed three or more years prior by applicants who were not represented by lawyers. Tribunal Watch is a non-partisan organization of law professors, former tribunal members, judges and civil servants.
"What this record proves," the report says, is that if the human rights tribunal keeps failing to move applications along, "it can count on a significant number of applicants giving up."
The blow will be felt most keenly among disabled people; a majority - 55 per cent - of the complaints are on grounds of disability, Tribunals Ontario data shows. Some 60 per cent of all complaints are related to employment.
The HRTO took a final decision based on merit in 40 cases in 2023-2024, up from 33 decisions the year before, but down from the 110 decisions it used to take prior to 2019 annually. The tribunal finally has a full complement of adjudicators; however, the watchdog has said that "most of them were appointed without the background necessary to effectively adjudicate or mediate human rights cases."
Where in the past, a seemingly weak application would be scheduled for a summary hearing or sent to mediation, the tribunal now requires applicants who may not have lawyers to file additional written submissions addressing the legal test for discrimination, the report says. It imposed this new filing deadline after years of inactivity and many applicants didn't, or couldn't, meet it. "The HRTO has created new barriers to an accessible process."
Legislation that led to the creation of this tribunal once gave Ontarians who were discriminated against the chance to be heard and the hope they would find justice. They're now being obstructed by the tribunal itself.
One step forward on social justice, it seems, is invariably followed by two steps back.
CAPTION: Last month, a new bill sought to again bring university practices under the Ford government's thumb. Bill 166, called the Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, is touted as a safety and support mechanism for post-secondary students.

Document 223 of 500

E-scooters create more dangers than benefits - especially for people with disabilities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/e-scooters-create-more-dangers-than-benefits/docview/3061272168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=E-scooters+create+more+dangers+than+benefits+-+especially+for+people+with+disabilities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Why Toronto needs to embrace e-scooters: a path to a more connected and a sustainable Toronto, May 26
Guest columnist Walied Khogali got it crushingly wrong when he blasted Toronto City Council for saying no to e-scooters. E-scooters especially endanger vulnerable seniors and people with disabilities. Blind people like me can't tell when e-scooters, a silent menace, rocket at them at more than 20 kph, driven by unlicensed, untrained, uninsured, often unhelmeted fun-seeking joyriders. Left strewn on sidewalks, e-scooters are tripping hazards for blind people and accessibility barriers for wheelchair users. Toronto has become less accessible to people with disabilities. Allowing e-scooters would make that worse.
Allowing e-scooters would impose significant costs on taxpayers for new law enforcement, OHIP for treating those injured by e-scooters, and lawsuits by the injured. Toronto has more pressing budget priorities.
Khogali utterly disregards the huge danger e-scooters pose for vulnerable people with disabilities, seniors and others. Toronto's official Accessibility Advisory Committee has three times pressed city council to say no to them. More than 20 major disability organizations representing a diversity of disabilities pleaded with city hall to say no to e-scooters. The dangers e-scooters pose to the public have been repeatedly proven in cities that allow them. We thank an overwhelming majority of Toronto City Council members for listening.
We support aggressively battling climate change and strongly applaud micromobility to help with this. But we don't need e-scooters for this, with all the dangers they create. Other forms of micromobility, like cycling, amply meet our needs. Where they are allowed, e-scooters don't add substantial new benefits. They just add new dangers.
David Lepofsky, Chair Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act Alliance, Toronto
E-scooters in their current form are a menace
While I appreciate e-scooters are a zero-emission travel alternative, among other things, I believe that in their current form they are a menace. Bicycles, unicycles and other forms of non-car transport - and yes that includes e-scooters - need to be licensed or have some kind of traceable ownership paperwork. Helmets need to be mandatory for starters. All these transportation options need to be treated like a Vespa or similar. Usually, they don't use the bike lanes, they are often in the traffic, always at maximum speed and now you can even buy an aftermarket "seat," so you don't even have to stand up. Once the city has addressed just some of these issues, maybe I'll come over to the e-scooter side. But not until then.
Carol McKenzie, Toronto
Plan safely for e-scooters
Why Toronto needs to embrace e-scooters: a path to a more connected and a sustainable Toronto, May 26
Many regular cyclists either don't know or disobey traffic laws. When there are no rules to follow, it's a free-for-all for e-scooter riders. Dangerous left-hand turns into oncoming traffic, lane-splitting during congestion, and whizzing between pedestrians are common occurrences whenever I walk downtown. As the author points out, the city has the opportunity to set up the successful integration of e-scooters. Let's start with a clear set of rules and a massive education campaign, including a manual similar to the MTO Driver's Handbook provided whenever an e-scooter (or a bike, for that matter) is purchased. Additionally, let's anticipate their popularity by setting aside dedicated train and streetcar areas for e-scooters and their users, avoiding the mistakes made with insufficient space for e-bikes on GO Trains.
By planning smartly, we can pave the way for a safer commute for all road users.
James Ip, Toronto
The youth will make Toronto vibrant againToronto has made its streets boring. Here's how we can bring them back to life, May 25
A big thank you to Shawn Micallef for this inspiring and timely column. Micallef's vision will happen because our younger generation demands it. Thankfully, some longtime establishments never left the neighbourhoods. Check out Clinton Street south of College Street, where you can get a pizza slice to go at Bitondo's, a sandwich to go at San Francesco's and down them with a pint at the accommodating Monarch Tavern, the perfect trifecta.
Bernhardt's on Dovercourt Road is one of the best restaurants anywhere. Sorauren Avenue has retail interspersed between homes. A shout out is merited for the bold owners of the new Strawbella Restaurant at 95 Greenlaw Ave., smack in the middle of a quiet neighbourhood. Granted, we need more than restaurants in neighbourhoods, and our unboring youth will ensure it. Bring it on.
Gino Salvo, Mississauga
Climate change one reason to support lab-grown meatWhy cultivate, or lab-grown meat, is not only safe, and ethical, but also a smart way to create protein, May 25
Many thanks for Jessica Scott-Reid's article on the reasons to support the innovation of cultivated meat. As our government struggles to reach climate change goals, surely aiding in the development of this industry should be top of mind. As always, people will be wary of something new, but once it is explained, and the benefits known, it seems like a win-win solution to me.
Cheryl Simpson, Stratford
No need for lab-meat when we have healthful and delicious plant foods
Why cultivate, or lab-grown meat, is not only safe, and ethical, but also a smart way to create protein, May 25
Many vegans oppose the production of lab-grown "meat" for philosophical and practical reasons. Every year, tens of billions of land animals, and innumerable sea animals, are slaughtered for human consumption worldwide. Tens of millions more are forcibly bred, held captive, and killed in laboratories, rodeos, circuses, zoos, and the pet trade. Lab meat does nothing to challenge the pernicious idea that our fellow animals are resources for human use and consumption. Lab meat will probably never be produced in quantities large enough to replace the animal agribusiness. Plant foods are available now - beans, fruits, vegetables, grains, nuts, and seeds are healthful and delicious - with a much smaller environmental footprint. It would be wonderful if we would collectively turn our backs on animal use and support the veganic (vegan + organic) farmers growing a peaceful future.
Carolyn Harris, Nepean
Ontario's misguided response to the housing crisis
Doug Ford hasn't learned from the Greenbelt scandal: Ontario's housing strategy still promotes sprawl and paving over farmland, May 25
I strongly support David Crombie and Anne Golden's critique of the housing crisis and the misguided Ontario response, which, if legislated, will gut the environment and shatter local planning authority - a trip to the Dirty Thirties! Wake up Canada/Ontario. Crombie's and Golden's comments are a call to a reasoned response to a crisis of national concern.
Paul Cosgrove, former Scarborough Mayor and Federal Housing Minister, Brockville
CAPTION: An e-scooter goes along Danforth at Carlaw avenues. E-scooters are illegal in Toronto, still hundreds of people are zipping around on them.

Subject: Scooters; Veganism; Councils; Handicapped accessibility; Animals; People with disabilities; Climate change; Cities; Ontarians with Disabilities Act-Ontario; Bicycling

Document 224 of 500

Court dismisses Jama's censure challenge

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/court-dismisses-jamas-censure-challenge/docview/3060988873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Court+dismisses+Jama%27s+censure+challenge&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A court has dismissed a push by Independent MPP Sarah Jama for a judicial review of her censure by a majority vote in the Ontario legislature last fall.
The censure - which prevents Jama (Hamilton Centre) from asking questions of Premier Doug Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the legislature - was passed Oct. 23 by the Progressive Conservatives on the same day New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles ejected her from the party caucus for insubordination.
"The law is well-settled," says the nine-page decision by Ontario Superior Court of Justice Divisional Court. "Courts have no jurisdiction to review matters that fall within parliamentary privilege, including review under the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," adds the May 23 decision.
Stiles booted Jama from the NDP over a statement about the Israel-Hamas war that prompted the censure motion by the PC government after two weeks of growing controversy surrounding the first-term MPP. Jama's statement on her MPP letterhead did not condemn the Oct. 7 surprise attack on Israel by Hamas or the many lives lost, and focused on the plight of Palestinians under Israeli occupation. That statement remained online even after she posted an apology following a 24-hour standoff with Stiles, who had demanded the statement be retracted.
Under the censure, Speaker Ted Arnott said he would not call on Jama to speak unless she retracts and deletes her statement and apologizes in the house.
"The privilege is key to maintaining the separation of powers between the legislature and the courts," the divisional court decision continued. "The applicant must pursue remedies before the Legislative Assembly, not the courts."
Jama, who was not in the legislature Tuesday, said in a statement the court's decision is "disappointing."
"I will not apologize," she added. "As I consider whether I have the energy and resources to appeal this decision, I continue to ask my fellow MPPs to do the right thing and vote to end my censure."
Government House Leader Paul Calandra said he was "very, very happy that the courts ruled in our favour."
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama was booted from the NDP over a statement about the Israel-Hamas war.The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 225 of 500

'Strength in speaking our language': Indigenous MPP makes history becoming first to speak Anishininiimowin on legislature floor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strength-speaking-our-language/docview/3060988851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Strength+in+speaking+our+language%27%3A+Indigenous+MPP+makes+history+becoming+first+to+speak+Anishininiimowin+on+legislature+floor&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Sol Mamakwa made history speaking in - and speaking of - his first language in the Ontario legislature.
The New Democrat - whose family and supporters packed the gallery, including his mother Kezia who was celebrating her 79th birthday - spoke for 10 minutes in Anishininiimowin at the start of daily question period Tuesday morning, the first time that has been allowed in the province's history.
He also then asked the first question of the day in his language, which in English is known as Oji-Cree.
Mamakwa, who represents Kiwetinoong, noted the significance of the moment, saying it was his "great privilege and honour to speak in my First Nations, Indigenous language."
He said that in residential schools, young people - including himself - were punished for speaking in their language, and their mouths were washed out with soap when they did.
There is a need for youth to learn the language, "to teach it to kids so they speak it, and be proud of it," Mamakwa said as his words were simultaneously translated into both English and French.
He then recalled how his mother "used to take me out into the wilderness ... teaching me the language. This is why I am able to speak my language."
"We receive strength in speaking our language," he also said, and by doing so in the legislature, "it's a healing moment that we are in."
MPPs, family and visitors to the legislature stood and applauded Mamakwa - and his mother - several times in the house. Following his speech - and in an unprecedented show of support - Premier Doug Ford crossed the aisle to congratulate and embrace the MPP, as did Long-Term Care Minister Stan Cho and Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Ford later promised the much-needed long-term-care beds in Sioux Lookout that Mamakwa has been advocating for - for which Ford received a standing ovation from his own MPPs as well as all opposition members.
"Sol - and sorry for using your first name, but this is a different situation - I had you in my office (Monday), told you how proud I am of you, how you're blazing a new trail," Ford told the legislature.
"No one's ever done this, what you're doing today. I just want to tell you how proud I am of you, how proud everyone here in the legislature is and how proud everyone in the First Nations are."
Speaking to reporters later, Mamakwa said "today was monumental for me."
The change came after Mamakwa commented during an event last March that he was not allowed to speak in his first language in the chamber, and caught the attention of Government House Leader Paul Calandra. The two later worked to change the rules to allow MPPs to speak in their own Indigenous languages, instead of just in English and French.
On Tuesday, Calandra said this sets a precedent for other provinces and Ottawa, "and I certainly hope now that Ontario has done it, there are lessons to be learned in other legislatures and we're prepared to help out any way that we can."
CAPTION: MPP Sol Mamakwa said his mother Kezia used to take him into the wilderness to teach him Anishininiimowin, which he was punished for speaking while in residential school.Steve RussellToronto Star
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Document 226 of 500

Former mayor Ford lives on at Centennial Park stadium

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-mayor-ford-lives-on-at-centennial-park/docview/3060988537/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+mayor+Ford+lives+on+at+Centennial+Park+stadium&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto now has a permanent tribute to Rob Ford - a namesake stadium at Centennial Park where the controversial former mayor often coached high school football.
Premier Doug Ford, speaking to relatives, city council members and hundreds of fans on his late brother's 55th birthday, became emotional at the "Rob Ford Stadium" naming ceremony.
"This would not happen if it were not for the mayor and city council," Ford said, adding of Mayor Olivia Chow, who was there to help unveil new signs for the city-owned stadium, "I absolutely love her and I think the world of her."
The premier paid tribute to his brother's dedication to the Don Bosco Eagles football team and the players, dozens of whom came to the ceremony in their old uniforms. The boys came only behind his family and serving the city, Ford said, telling stories of his brother's famed customer service ethos.
The only hint at the scandal that engulfed much of Rob Ford's mayoralty was a quote from a former player, read by the premier, that said the media didn't show the true man.
Chow's husband, Jack Layton, was a friend of Rob Ford at council. After Layton's death, Rob Ford approved renaming Toronto's ferry terminal after the former city councillor and former federal NDP leader. Chow sang Rob Ford's praises, to the delight of the audience.
"He was so dedicated to public service, let me tell you," Chow said, triggering a cheer of "Best mayor ever" from the crowd sitting in the bleachers. "Ya, best mayor ever," Chow replied.
Stephanie Ford, Rob Ford's teenage daughter, told the crowd: "If anything were to be named after my father, this stadium would be the most fitting. It represents his love for Etobicoke and how much he did for people as mayor but also what he did for his second love - football."
City council voted 17 to 6 in December 2023 to rename the stadium after Ford, who died of cancer in 2016 at the age of 46.
Opponents at the time echoed objections that saw a previous renaming attempt at council withdrawn, arguing it was improper to honour a man who became world famous for being filmed smoking crack cocaine with gang members before getting treatment and starting to seek re-election only to be struck by a rare, aggressive cancer.
None of that seemed to matter, however, to the large crowd that waited in heavy rain for the gates to open and the delayed ceremony to begin.
With files from Ben Spurr
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow converse at the ceremony to rename Centennial Park's stadium Rob Ford Stadium. "He was so dedicated to public service, let me tell you," Chow told a cheering crowd, speaking of the ex-mayor of Toronto.David RiderToronto Star
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter With files from Ben Spurr

Document 227 of 500

Ottawa, Ontario reach deal ensuring $357M for affordable housing: Province submits revised 'action plan' detailing projects

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-ontario-reach-deal-ensuring-357m/docview/3060987416/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa%2C+Ontario+reach+deal+ensuring+%24357M+for+affordable+housing%3A+Province+submits+revised+%27action+plan%27+detailing+projects&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa and Queen's Park have reached a deal ensuring $357 million in federal funds will flow to the province to build affordable housing.
After months of negotiations - and an acrimonious letter made public in the Star - federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser and provincial Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra on Tuesday announced the impasse had been cleared.
"Solving the housing crisis requires a Team Canada approach. We will continue to work together, along with our municipal partners, to make sure the people of Ontario have the homes they need," Fraser and Calandra said in a joint statement.
"Canada and Ontario recognize that our collaboration is imperative to solving the housing crisis. That is why we are pleased to share that an agreement has been reached on a revised action plan from Ontario that will unlock $357 million of federal funding under the National Housing Strategy," they said.
Under the bilateral agreement between the governments of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, Queen's Park has submitted a revised "action plan" detailing the affordable housing projects that are receiving provincial funding.
The province's revamped 2022-25 plan forecasts at least 8,644 more affordable housing units will be built by March 31, 2025.
But Ford's government could actually meet its original federally mandated target of 19,660 new units by that deadline.
"Ontario has also included new measures in its action plan which better reflect Ontario's funding delivery model, as the only jurisdiction which flows the funds through municipal service managers," the joint statement says.
"These measures include: establishing provincial supply targets with service managers, directing funding toward new projects, setting annual goals, and implementing robust data collection and reporting mechanisms."
The province will also submit a 2025-28 "action plan" to Ottawa by the end of this year in order to secure additional federal funding for the final years of the decade-long $70-billion National Housing Strategy agreement.
In Ottawa, Fraser said "Ontario has charted a path that will allow it to meet its obligations under the original agreement."
At Queen's Park, Calandra told reporters his federal counterpart had "rightfully pointed out that it is a challenge to count Ontario's numbers given the fact that we fund our housing differently."
"Once we were able to fully explain how we fund affordable housing with our service partners and their municipal partners, then we were able to make progress on that," he said.
It's a marked shift in tone from two months ago when Fraser had warned Ford's failure to meet federal affordable housing targets was jeopardizing the $357 million in funding.
The province had argued Ontario wasn't being treated fairly because its affordable housing stock is the oldest in Canada.
That means necessary repairs to existing units have come at the expense of building new affordable housing.
"Should Ontario fail to provide a revised plan demonstrating how it intends to meet its housing targets under the agreement, the province will not receive $357 million intended for affordable housing from the federal government," Fraser chided Calandra in March.
"Speaking frankly, the proposed action plan is a disappointment. It shows almost no progress toward reaching the affordable housing expansion target, and proposes to achieve only 1,184 units of the 19,660 required by the end of 2024-25," he wrote.
"This leaves 94 per cent of the target to be achieved during the last three years of the agreement, which is not realistic."
While Fraser had insisted "no further extensions are possible" beyond March 31 - because Ottawa had already given Queen's Park an additional year to revise its plan in 2023 - he relented after Calandra pointed out that was punitive and would hurt the most vulnerable.
With files from Tonda MacCharles and Kristin Rushowy
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Tonda MacCharles and Kristin Rushowy

Full text: Ford's misguided response to the housing crisis
Doug Ford hasn't learned from the Greenbelt scandal: Ontario's housing strategy still promotes sprawl and paving over farmland, May 25
I strongly support David Crombie and Anne Golden's critique of the housing crisis and the misguided Ontario response, which, if legislated, will gut the environment and shatter local planning authority - a trip to the Dirty Thirties! Wake up Canada/Ontario.
Crombie's and Golden's comments are a call to a reasoned response to a crisis of national concern.
Paul Cosgrove, former Scarborough mayor and federal housing minister, Brockville, Ont.
E-scooters create dangers for those with disabilities
Why Toronto needs to embrace e-scooters: a path to a more connected and a sustainable Toronto, May 26
Guest columnist Walied Khogali got it crushingly wrong when he blasted city council for saying no to e-scooters. E-scooters especially endanger vulnerable seniors and those with disabilities. Blind people like me can't tell when e-scooters, a silent menace, rocket at them at more than 20 km/h, driven by unlicensed, untrained, uninsured, often unhelmeted fun-seeking joyriders. Left strewn on sidewalks, e-scooters are tripping hazards for blind people and accessibility barriers for wheelchair users. Toronto has become less accessible to people with disabilities. Allowing e-scooters would make that worse.
Allowing e-scooters would impose significant costs on taxpayers for new law enforcement, OHIP for treating those injured by e-scooters, and lawsuits by the injured. Toronto has more pressing budget priorities.
Toronto's official Accessibility Advisory Committee has three times pressed city council to say no to e-scooters. More than 20 major disability organizations representing a diversity of disabilities pleaded with city hall to say no to e-scooters. The dangers e-scooters pose to the public have been repeatedly proven in cities that allow them. We thank an overwhelming majority of city councillors for listening.
We support battling climate change and strongly applaud micromobility to help with this. But we don't need e-scooters for this, with all the dangers they create. Other forms of micromobility, like cycling, amply meet our needs. Where they are allowed, e-scooters don't add substantial new benefits. They just add new dangers.
David Lepofsky, Chair Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act Alliance, Toronto
Proper planning needed to avoid
a free-for-all
Why Toronto needs to embrace e-scooters: a path to a more connected and a sustainable Toronto, May 26
Many regular cyclists either don't know or disobey traffic laws. When there are no rules to follow, it's a free-for-all for e-scooter riders. Dangerous left-hand turns into oncoming traffic, lane-splitting during congestion, and whizzing between pedestrians are common occurrences whenever I walk downtown. As the author points out, the city has the opportunity to set up the successful integration of e-scooters. Let's start with a clear set of rules and a massive education campaign, including a manual similar to the MTO Driver's Handbook provided whenever an e-scooter (or a bike, for that matter) is purchased. Additionally, let's anticipate their popularity by setting aside dedicated train and streetcar areas for e-scooters and their users, avoiding the mistakes made with insufficient space for e-bikes on GO Trains.
By planning smartly, we can pave the way for a safer commute for all road users.
James Ip, Toronto
Climate change a reason to support lab-grown meat
Why cultivate, or lab-grown meat, is not only safe, and ethical, but also a smart way to create protein, May 25
Many thanks for Jessica Scott-Reid's article on the reasons to support the innovation of cultivated meat. As our government struggles to reach climate change goals, surely aiding in the development of this industry should be top of mind. As always, people will be wary of something new, but once it is explained, and the benefits known, it seems like a win-win solution to me.
Cheryl Simpson, Stratford
We already have healthy, delicious plant foods
Why cultivate, or lab-grown meat, is not only safe, and ethical, but also a smart way to create protein, May 25
Many vegans oppose the production of lab-grown "meat" for philosophical and practical reasons. Every year, tens of billions of land animals, and innumerable sea animals, are slaughtered for human consumption worldwide. Tens of millions more are forcibly bred, held captive, and killed in laboratories, rodeos, circuses, zoos, and the pet trade.
Lab meat does nothing to challenge the pernicious idea that our fellow animals are resources for human use and consumption. Lab meat will probably never be produced in quantities large enough to replace the animal agribusiness. Plant foods are available now - beans, fruits, vegetables, grains, nuts, and seeds are healthful and delicious - with a much smaller environmental footprint. It would be wonderful if we would collectively turn our backs on animal use and support the veganic (vegan and organic) farmers growing a peaceful future.
Carolyn Harris, Nepean, Ont.
The youth will make Toronto vibrant again
Toronto has made its streets boring. Here's how we can bring them back to life, May 25
A big thank you to Shawn Micallef for this inspiring and timely column. Micallef's vision will happen because our younger generation demands it. Thankfully, some longtime establishments never left the neighbourhoods. Check out Clinton Street south of College Street, where you can get a pizza slice to go at Bitondo's, a sandwich to go at San Francesco and down them with a pint at the accommodating Monarch Tavern, the perfect trifecta.
Bernhardt's on Dovercourt Road is one of the best restaurants anywhere. Sorauren Avenue has retail interspersed between homes. A shout out is merited for the bold owners of the new Strawbella Restaurant at 95 Greenlaw Ave., smack in the middle of a quiet neighbourhood. Granted, we need more than restaurants in neighbourhoods, and our unboring youth will ensure it. Bring it on.
Gino Salvo, Mississauga

Document 229 of 500

'Stay tuned,' Ford says of possible early vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stay-tuned-ford-says-possible-early-vote/docview/3060987173/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Stay+tuned%2C%27+Ford+says+of+possible+early+vote&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Stay tuned."
That's Premier Doug Ford's message when pressed on whether he will call an early election in the wake of his expedited liberalization of booze sales, which could cost taxpayers $1 billion.
After the Star reported Monday that Ford is considering holding a vote a year or more before the scheduled June 2026 election, the premier spoke with Newstalk 1010 Tuesday morning.
"Are we going to have an early election?" asked host Jerry Agar.
"Again, I can't answer that. I just can't right now," said a defensive Ford.
"Well, who else can?" replied Agar.
"Jerry, Jerry, as far as I'm concerned we're going to focus on our agenda, getting things done and the people are going to decide - that's what's beautiful about a democracy," said Ford.
"Stay tuned," the premier added.
Under fire over criticism of an agreement that will pay the Beer Store $225 million - though opposition rivals claim the tab could actually balloon to between $600 million and $1 billion - Ford scrambled to defend his move.
"That's going to bring in revenues of anywhere from $895 million to $1.16 billion that we were leaving on the table year after year after year," he said, citing the 8,500 new points of sale that Ontarians should be able to shop at after Oct. 31.
"We're going to create more (tax) revenues. What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people - treating them like adults."
Source say Ford rushed the agreement in a bid to clear the decks by fulfilling a key election promise that dates back to 2018, giving him the flexibility to call a snap election this fall or next spring.
Progressive Conservative insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the premier is concerned about budget cuts a future federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might foist upon the province.
Ford, who works closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals on many shared priorities, is worried there could be reduced transfer payments to Ontario and a scrapping of the federal-provincial electric-vehicle subsidies strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy.
At his announcement Friday to tout the sped-up beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the June 2026 election date, leaving open the possibility of an early vote, which could cost more than $155 million.
"I just want to get our agenda through," he coyly told reporters.
Tory insiders say it is shrewd of Ford to keep his options open after the PCs won two byelections earlier this month and boast a double-digit poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," confided one loyalist.
As well, an early election might get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under RCMP investigation.
But other Tories close to Ford oppose an early call, warning it would be seen as "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after the premier received a re-election mandate in June 2022.
They point to the 1990 election when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called an election a year earlier than required and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's NDP in one of the biggest upsets in Canadian political history.
While Ford is aware of that perilous precedent, he is also mindful of what's known as the "Underhill balance theory" that posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
The phenomenon has held true for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford fears a Poilievre victory in an expected October 2025 federal election would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026.
Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, whose Liberals made history in 1985 by ending 42 years of PC rule.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 230 of 500

Rent in my building can vary by as much as $800. More federal funds won't fix Ontario's rent control problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rent-my-building-can-vary-as-much-800-more/docview/3060797515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rent+in+my+building+can+vary+by+as+much+as+%24800.+More+federal+funds+won%27t+fix+Ontario%27s+rent+control+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I'm one of the lucky Torontonians who has relatively "good rent." But despite our shared landlord and similar units, some of my neighbours do not.
Because we're close to a university, our subdivided house in Annex has a high turnover of renters, it's easy to barely know your neighbours beyond a passing nod on the stairs.
But connections do occur. I met my first neighbour in 2022, when I asked them to water our plants while we were away. During the conversation, we exchanged our rental rates. At that time, I was paying just under $1,900 a month for my one-bedroom apartment, while theirs was $2,100.
Earlier this year however, when I knocked on our new neighbour's door to ask if I could use their Wi-Fi while mine was down, I learned their rent had soared to $2,700 - a nearly 30 per cent spike.
Our building was built well before Nov. 15, 2018, so it is rent controlled - though our landlord can still request above guideline increases for existing tenants. But rates skyrocket due to vacancy decontrol, which allows landlords to raise rents without limits between leases.
Over the last decade, rent-controlled units in Ontario should have only increased in price by 16.5 per cent. In reality, they surged more than threefold by 54.5 per cent, according to the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.
On Tuesday, the federal and provincial governments came to a deal that would ensure $357 million in federal funds will be given to Ontario for affordable housing.
But with the loopholes in our current rent control system, market costs have already ballooned; the promise of more money will not solve our housing issues.
Thus far no level of government has put forth an effective plan to help renters.
The Ford government's budget failed to offer any relief, which was no surprise given it's the party that established the 2018 date-based exemption.
I was hopeful that the federal budget would rise to the occasion, especially given the Liberals' declining popularity in the polls. Instead, renters were offered a Bill of Rights filled with half-measures, rather than the immediate actions necessary in a "crisis." You know, the dire sort of situation we all - regardless of our political stripes - acknowledge we're in.
From 2011 to 2021, the average cost of a home in Ontario climbed by 135 per cent - the largest increase in the country. To bridge the disparity between prices and earnings, average home values need to decrease by $530,000 - over 60 per cent - or average earnings need to increase by 150 per cent to reach $137,000 per year, according to a report by Generation Squeeze.
The federal budget proposed increasing the amount we can borrow from our RRSPs and extending first-time mortgage amortization limits. None this actually lowers the cost of a home and instead favours lenders and developers.
I don't understand how paying more interest on a longer mortgage and having access to more debt won't drive prices higher. Isn't dependence on debt one of the factors that's made homes so unaffordable to begin with?
It also still leaves a crucial question unanswered: How can we realistically save for a downpayment when rents are skyrocketing out of control?
There is more room for the federal government to intervene and they have in the past. In the 1940s the Canadian government froze rents, as did many countries between the world wars. Then in the mid-1970s, high inflation and social pressure made the Liberals persuade provinces to introduce rent control. Or, we can look beyond our borders for current examples; France and Spain both impose rent caps nationally.
Yet with so many provincial cabinet members and federal MPs invested in real estate, will elected leaders be deterred from enacting robust rent control?
The solutions offered now by the Liberals are bleak, and yet the opposition may offer even less, with Pierre Poilievre's suggestion for the government to "get out of home-building" and rely on the market.
To afford housing, tenants are often consumed with work to afford rent. But advocacy movements are coming together. Some residents have gone on rent strikes. Platforms like @coolpeoplehouses on Instagram and apps like Housemate Space facilitate sublets and lease takeovers, allowing tenants to pass along stable rent. Groups like No Demovictions are advocating for a moratorium on all profit-driven demoviction applications; they recently scored a victory at city council, which passed a motion to more fairly distribute the costs of displacement from tenants to developers.
In the meantime, my partner and I know we are in good company with our housing anger and anxieties. We are millennials, in our mid-thirties, postponing starting a family, putting our lives on hold, struggling to save for a downpayment - let alone retirement - and unable to build equity, or move anywhere else because we can't afford to lose our "good rent"- which isn't "good" at all.
CAPTION: With the loopholes in our current rent control system, market costs have already ballooned; the promise of more money will not solve our housing issues.
CREDIT: Rachel Cairns, Contributor

Subject: Federal budget; Down payments; Federal funding; Costs; Tenants; Rent control

Document 231 of 500

Doug Ford was asked about calling on early election. 'Stay tuned,' he says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-was-asked-about-calling-on-early/docview/3060797176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+was+asked+about+calling+on+early+election.+%27Stay+tuned%2C%27+he+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Stay tuned."
That's Premier Doug Ford's message when pressed on whether he will call an early election in the wake of his expedited liberalization of booze sales, which could cost taxpayers $1 billion.
After the Star reported Monday that Ford is considering holding a vote a year or more before the scheduled June 2026 election, the premier spoke with Newstalk 1010 Tuesday morning.
"Are we going to have an early election?" asked host Jerry Agar.
"Again, I can't answer that. I just can't right now," said a defensive Ford.
"Well, who else can?" replied Agar.
"Jerry, Jerry, as far as I'm concerned we're going to focus on our agenda, getting things done and the people are going to decide - that's what's beautiful about a democracy," said Ford.
"Stay tuned," the premier added.
Under fire over criticism of an agreement that will pay The Beer Store $225 million - though opposition rivals claim the tab could actually balloon to between $600 million and $1 billion - Ford scrambled to defend his move.
"That's going to bring in revenues of anywhere from $895 million to $1.16 billion that we were leaving on the table year after year after year," he said citing the 8,500 new points of sale that Ontarians should be able to shop at after Oct. 31.
"We're going to create more (tax) revenues. What it comes down to is making sure that we have convenience for people - treating them like adults."
Source say Ford rushed the agreement in a bid to clear the decks by fulfilling a key election promise that dates back to 2018, giving him the flexibility to call a snap election this fall or next spring.
Progressive Conservative insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the premier is concerned about budget cuts a future federal Conservative government led by Pierre Poilievre might foist upon the province.
Ford, who works closely with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals on many shared priorities, is worried there could be reduced transfer payments to Ontario and a scrapping of the federal-provincial electric-vehicle subsidies strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario industrial policy.
At his announcement Friday to tout the sped-up beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the June 2026 election date, leaving open the possibility of an early vote, which could cost more than $155 million.
"I just want to get our agenda through," he coyly told reporters that day.
Tory insiders say it is shrewd of Ford to keep his options open after the PCs won two byelections earlier this month and boast a double-digit poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," confided one loyalist.
As well, an early election might get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal that is under RCMP investigation.
But other Tories close to Ford oppose an early call, warning it would be seen as "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after the premier received a re-election mandate in June 2022.
They point to the 1990 election when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called an election a year earlier than required and was punished by voters, losing to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the biggest upsets in Canadian political history.
"Peterson was higher in the polls (50 per cent in summer of 1990) than Ford (at 39 per cent in the latest Abacus Data survey) is now and had won a larger majority (in 1987 than Ford did in 2022)," noted another Tory.
While Ford is aware of that perilous precedent, he is also mindful of what's known as the "Underhill balance theory" that posits that when Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they tend to choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially at Queen's Park.
The phenomenon has held true for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford fears a Poilievre victory in an expected October 2025 federal election would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026.
Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, whose Liberals made history in 1985 by ending 42 years of PC rule in Ontario.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto on May 24, 2024.

Document 232 of 500

Trudeau and Ford governments reach $357M deal in federal funds for affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-governments-reach-357m-deal-federal/docview/3060797143/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+and+Ford+governments+reach+%24357M+deal+in+federal+funds+for+affordable+housing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa and Queen's Park have reached a deal ensuring $357 million in federal funds will flow to the province to build affordable housing.
After months of negotiations - and an acrimonious letter made public in the Star - federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser and provincial Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra on Tuesday announced the impasse had been cleared.
"Solving the housing crisis requires a Team Canada approach. We will continue to work together, along with our municipal partners, to make sure the people of Ontario have the homes they need," Fraser and Calandra said in a joint statement.
"Canada and Ontario recognize that our collaboration is imperative to solving the housing crisis. That is why we are pleased to share that an agreement has been reached on a revised action plan from Ontario that will unlock $357 million of federal funding under the National Housing Strategy," they said.
Under the bilateral agreement between the governments of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford, Queen's Park has submitted a revised "action plan" detailing the affordable housing projects that are receiving provincial funding.
The province's revamped 2022-25 plan forecasts at least 8,644 more affordable housing units will be built by March 31, 2025.
But Ford's government could actually meet its original federally mandated target of 19,660 new units by that deadline.
"Ontario has also included new measures in its action plan which better reflect Ontario's funding delivery model, as the only jurisdiction which flows the funds through municipal service managers," the joint statement says.
"These measures include: establishing provincial supply targets with service managers, directing funding toward new projects, setting annual goals, and implementing robust data collection and reporting mechanisms."
The province will also submit a 2025-28 "action plan" to Ottawa by the end of this year in order to secure additional federal funding for the final years of the decade-long $70-billion National Housing Strategy agreement.
In Ottawa, Fraser said "Ontario has charted a path that will allow it to meet its obligations under the original agreement.
At Queen's Park, Calandra told reporters his federal counterpart had "rightfully pointed out that it is a challenge to count Ontario's numbers given the fact that we fund our housing differently."
"Once we were able to fully explain how we fund affordable housing with our service partners and their municipal partners, then we were able to make progress on that," he said.
It's a marked shift in tone from two months ago when Fraser had warned Ford's failure to meet federal affordable housing targets was jeopardizing the $357 million in funding.
The province had argued Ontario wasn't being treated fairly because its affordable housing stock is the oldest in Canada.
That means necessary repairs to existing units have come at the expense of building new affordable housing.
"Should Ontario fail to provide a revised plan demonstrating how it intends to meet its housing targets under the agreement, the province will not receive $357 million intended for affordable housing from the federal government," Fraser chided Calandra in March.
"Speaking frankly, the proposed action plan is a disappointment. It shows almost no progress toward reaching the affordable housing expansion target, and proposes to achieve only 1,184 units of the 19,660 required by the end of 2024-25," he wrote.
"This leaves 94 per cent of the target to be achieved during the last three years of the agreement, which is not realistic."
While Fraser had insisted "no further extensions are possible" beyond March 31 -because Ottawa had already given Queen's Park an additional year to revise its plan in 2023 -he relented after Calandra pointed out that was punitive and would hurt the most vulnerable.
With files from Tonda MacCharles and Kristin Rushowy
CAPTION: Under the agreement between the governments, Queen's Park has submitted a revised "action plan" detailing the affordable housing projects that are receiving provincial funding.

MPP Sarah Jama's court challenge of her censure at Ontario legislature is dismissed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mpp-sarah-jamas-court-challenge-her-censure-at/docview/3060797141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+Sarah+Jama%27s+court+challenge+of+her+censure+at+Ontario+legislature+is+dismissed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A court has dismissed a push by Independent MPP Sarah Jama for a judicial review of her censure by a majority vote in the Ontario legislature last fall.
The censure - which prevents Jama (Hamilton Centre) from asking questions of Premier Doug Ford and his cabinet ministers or otherwise speaking in the legislature - was passed Oct. 23 by the Progressive Conservatives on the same day New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles ejected her from the party caucus for insubordination.
"The law is well-settled," says the nine-page decision by Ontario Superior Court of Justice Divisional Court.
"Courts have no jurisdiction to review matters that fall within parliamentary privilege, including review under the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," adds the May 23 decision.
Stiles booted Jama from the NDP over a statement about the Israel-Hamas war that prompted the censure motion by the PC government after two weeks of growing controversy surrounding the first-term MPP.
Jama's statement on her MPP letterhead did not condemn the Oct. 7 surprise attack on Israel by Hamas or the many lives lost, and focused on the plight of Palestinians under Israeli occupation.
That statement remained online even after she posted an apology following a 24-hour standoff with Stiles, who had demanded the statement be retracted.
Under the censure, Speaker Ted Arnott of the legislature said he would not call on Jama to speak unless she retracts and deletes her statement and apologizes in the house.
"The privilege is key to maintaining the separation of powers between the legislature and the courts," the divisional court decision continued.
"The applicant must pursue remedies before the Legislative Assembly, not the courts."
Jama was not in the legislature Tuesday. Her office did not immediately reply to a request for comment.
"I'm very, very happy that the courts ruled in our favour," said Government House Leader Paul Calandra, who noted a court also upheld parliamentary privilege in a 2022 ruling that upheld a rare speaker's warrant approved by MPPs seeking financial records from Laurentian University in Sudbury.

CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama speaks to media outside thelegislature in Toronto on May 6, 2024.

Document 234 of 500

'We receive strength in speaking our language': Indigenous MPP makes history at Queen's Park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-receive-strength-speaking-our-language/docview/3060797127/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+receive+strength+in+speaking+our+language%27%3A+Indigenous+MPP+makes+history+at+Queen%27s+Park&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MPP Sol Mamakwa made history speaking in - and speaking of - his first language in the Ontario legislature on Tuesday.
The New Democrat - whose family and supporters packed the gallery, including his mother Kezia who was celebrating her 79th birthday - spoke for 10 minutes in Anishininiimowin at the start of daily question period, the first time that has been allowed in the province's history.
He also then asked the first question of the day in his language, which in English is known as Oji-Cree.
Mamakwa, who represents Kiwetinoong, noted the significance of the moment, saying it was his "great privilege and honour to speak in my First Nations, Indigenous language."
He said that in residential schools, young people - including himself - were punished for speaking in their language, and their mouths were washed out when they did.
There is a need for youth to learn the language, "to teach it to kids so they speak it, and be proud of it," Mamakwa said as his words were simultaneously translated into both English and French.
He then recalled how his mother "used to take me out into the wilderness ... teaching me the language. This is why I am able to speak my language."
"We receive strength in speaking our language," he said, and by doing so in the legislature, "it's a healing moment that we are in."
MPPs, family and visitors to the legislature stood and applauded Mamakwa - and his mother - several times in the house. Following his speech - and in an unprecedented show of support - Premier Doug Ford crossed the aisle to congratulate and embrace the MPP, as did Long-Term Care Minister Stan Cho and Energy Minister Todd Smith.
Ford noted he'd previously met with Mamakwa in his office, and said he was proud of him for "blazing a new trail."
The premier also promised the much-needed long-term-care beds in Sioux Lookout for which Mamakwa has been advocating - for which Ford received a standing ovation from his own MPPs as well as all opposition members.
Tuesday's historic moment came after Mamakwa commented during an event last March in the legislative building that he was not allowed to speak in his first language in the chamber. That caught the attention of Government House Leader Paul Calandra.
The two later worked to change the rules to allow MPPs to speak in their own Indigenous languages, instead of just in English and French.
CAPTION: New Democrat MPP Sol Mamakwa made history in the Ontario legislature on Tuesday when he asked aquestion in his first language, Anishininiimowin.
