Document 235 of 500

Is The Beer Store finished? Experts say retail operations will likely close as corner stores start selling beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-beer-store-finished-experts-say-retail/docview/3060719748/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+The+Beer+Store+finished%3F+Experts+say+retail+operations+will+likely+close+as+corner+stores+start+selling+beer&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Beer Store might not make it to its 100th birthday.
With the announcement last week by Premier Doug Ford that beer, wine, cider and seltzers will be coming to corner stores across Ontario by September and big box stores by Halloween, the once-mighty beer juggernaut is on the verge of disappearing from the retail scene, experts say.
With 8,500 more places for Ontarians to buy their beer, there's just not much need for The Beer Store, said beer author and consultant Stephen Beaumont.
"I don't think there's any question that their days as a retail entity are numbered. And that number's pretty small," said Beaumont."It's quite conceivable we could see the end of The Beer Store as a retail entity by 2026."
The Beer Store began life as a co-operative venture between local breweries in 1927, the same year Prohibition ended in Ontario. Today, it's owned by Molson Coors, Labatt, who each control roughly 49 per cent, and Sleeman, which controls the remainder. At its peak, it sold 90 per cent of the beer consumed in Ontario.
As part of an agreement with The Beer Store and its owners, the provincial government is paying $225 million to get an early jump on wide-open alcohol retailing that would otherwise have had to wait until the beginning of 2026, after the official end of the Master Framework Agreement.
The MFA - introduced by then-premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 - is set to expire at the end of 2025.
As part of the "early implementation agreement" announced Friday, The Beer Store - which currently has 440 stores across the province - has to keep at least 386 stores open until July 1, 2025. From July 1, 2025 through the end of that year, they have to keep at least 300 open. The following year? There's no minimum store count mandated.
"From and after Jan. 1, 2026, TBS shall be entitled to close any retail location as determined by TBS in its sole and absolute discretion," says the agreement.
The province has the right to terminate the agreement if The Beer Store goes into insolvency, or one of its owners pulls out of the company.
Beer educator and author Jordan St. John says it's clear The Beer Store - which is owned by Molson Coors, Labatt and Sleeman - is getting out of the retail business, even if it keeps its distribution arm alive.
"There's no reason for it to exist in a world where there are 8,500 other locations to buy beer," said St. John.
Even if The Beer Store suddenly started offering different ranges of products - including food - they'd be playing catch-up with thousands of other competitors, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson.
"What are they going to add? Gourmet chips? Another food element? But then they'd have to be more unique than the convenience store, and it goes back to 'they're no different, and no more convenient,'" said Hutcheson, managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group.
In a written statement, The Beer Store didn't specifically address whether it would be closing some - or all - of its stores, but said it plans to stay in business, and pointed to $100 million worth of "future-focused" investments, including a new distribution centre in Bolton, Ont.
"The Beer Store is planning to adapt and thrive. We will expand our distribution fleet and network to service the new retail outlets," the statement said.
The head of the union local which represents The Beer Store's 7,000 full and part-time employees said the province's changes could be "disastrous" for his members.
"We are concerned by these changes, and it could be disastrous: many of our members could lose their jobs as retail stores close," said John Nock, president of Local 12R24 of United Food and Commercial Workers.
The $225 million pledged to The Beer Store's owners should go toward supporting workers, Nock said, including for severance payments, retraining, and other supports.
"These are workers who have families and contribute to their communities: They were guaranteed stability until 2026, and this announcement revokes it," said Nock.
The new agreement also gives The Beer Store the right to operate the Ontario Deposit Return Program until the end of 2030.
That, says Beaumont, could keep the company alive, if it transitions some remaining stores to simply becoming bottle return depots.
"That's what The Beer Store is now, much more than it is anything else. They're certainly not making money selling beer any more. That much has been obvious for a couple of years," said Beaumont.
Hutcheson agreed, and suggested that The Beer Store's distribution arm could have some long-term viability, given that its fleet of trucks already distributes beer to thousands of restaurants and bars across the province.
"Maybe they become more of a distributor and then rationalize stores, and use themselves as just that distribution point for more bars and restaurants. That might make more sense for them," said Hutcheson.
Still, argued Beaumont, even as a distribution-only entity, The Beer Store's survival wouldn't be guaranteed.
"It will be a viable service so long as Molson and Labatt decide it's in their best interests for it to be a viable service," said Beaumont. "The moment they decide it doesn't make sense any more, it's gone."
CAPTION: In a statement the Beer Store said it is "planning to adapt and thrive. We will expand our distribution fleet and network to service the new retail outlets."

Subject: Agreements; Retail stores; Beer; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Breweries

Company / organization: Name: Molson Coors Beverage Co; NAICS: 312120

Document 236 of 500

New polls show Mississauga's mayoral byelection is a tight race

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-polls-show-mississaugas-mayoral-byelection-is/docview/3060719655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+polls+show+Mississauga%27s+mayoral+byelection+is+a+tight+race&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With just two weeks until Mississauga byelection, numbers from a new poll out today shows that for the first time in decades, there could be a real race for the mayoral seat.
It also suggests that the decision of front-runner Carolyn Parrish, who has dropped eight points since the last poll by Liaison Strategies was conducted in April, to pull out of all mayoral debates this month may have harmed the double-digit lead she has held over her competitors until now.
Most of the gain was seen by Councillor Dipika Damerla, who has narrowed the lead between her and Parrish, only trailing by five per cent. Alvin Tedjo's campaign also saw a two per cent boost - as the number of undecided voters decreased.
The numbers come as the city says it saw a "significant increase in voter turnout" during the advanced voting last weekend. There was a "42 per cent increase compared to the first three days of advance polling in the 2022 municipal election" with over 2,250 voters voting early. And one pollster says the numbers show that the race is "wide-open."
David Valentin, the Principal at Liaison Strategies said the numbers indicate a "major shift before election day."
Valentin says that "as we reach the final days of the campaign there is a large pool of reliable voters that still hasn't made up their minds" and this "really could be anyone's race."
This is Mississauga's first mayoral race in five decades in which there is no incumbent or clear front-runner. The election was triggered when former mayor Bonnie Crombie stepped down in January to become the leader of the Ontario Liberal Party.
The latest poll numbers show that Parrish still leads among decided voters, with 29 per cent saying they will still vote for Parrish, a long-time politician who was a federal MP in the early 2000s and has been a municipal politician for several terms. Damerla has 24 per cent of the decided vote, while Tedjo has 19 per cent, per poll numbers.
Valentin says that while Parrish is still leading but with a lead "smaller and more tenuous than before."
In an email, Damerla says she was encouraged by the recent numbers and "also feeling very positive about what we are hearing at the doors" and "overwhelming request for signs."
Damerla says she has an "ambition and practical plan to keep Mississauga affordable and livable" and has been showing up at debates to talk to as many residents as possible.
Valentin says that the undecided voter number is also one to watch. "The undecided rate has dropped to 16 per cent among all voters - but it has jumped to 37 per cent among seniors." He says that could likely be attributed to uncertainty amongst seniors given Parrish's decision to pull out of the debates.
Earlier this month, Parrish said she would not be participating in any future debates in the city, citing concerns that she was being "personally threatened." However, she did not share specifics about the nature of the threats.
The decision also came days after a debate where Parrish was accused of making controversial statements that critics called transphobic and discriminatory towards refugee claimants - and that has led to criticism on social media.
In an email, Parrish said she "remains convinced" that citizens don't respond to "negative campaigning."
"They value experience, an excellent track record of accomplishments and a solid platform for the future of the city."
"If Parrish is able to win back seniors, she has a very good chance of being mayor but the opportunity is there for the Damerla and Tedjo campaigns to seize the momentum and votes," he said.
The poll was conducted on May 24-25, 2024, the poll surveyed 907 Mississauga residents with a margin of error for the poll is +/- 3.22 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
Voters have another chance to vote early on June 1 and June 2.
Election day is on June 10.
CAPTION: Mississauga will vote for its next mayor on June 10.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Local elections; Mayors; Negative campaigning; Voters; Voter behavior

Ontarians want better access to health care not beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarians-want-better-access-health-care-not-beer/docview/3060718811/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+want+better+access+to+health+care+not+beer&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
Apparently, Premier Doug Ford and his government are using valuable time and funds to put beer and wine in convenience stores. As a resident of a rural community in the province of Ontario, I would far rather see time and money spent on providing adequate health-care facilities, doctors and nurse practitioners to those of us who have no primary care provider and very little hope of attaining one in the near future. To me this push to supply more access to alcohol is misguided when so many Ontarians are struggling to get help for a wide range of health issues. Wake up, Ford. Realign your priorities to meet the real needs of the people of Ontario, the people who voted you into power.
Sharon Phelps, Lindsay
LCBO thieves will now target corner stores
Why would any small store want to sell alcohol when the liquor stores can't prevent wholesale theft as it is? We are about to see an uptick in violent crime and theft. Money given to the Beer Store to accommodate this should be going to the housing crisis or the crisis in health care or to water and resources for Indigenous people or to any of the other crises facing Ontario right now! Why would I want to divert profits from the LCBO which helps fund services in Ontario to private concerns? Why would you ruin something that has worked well for decades and is a revenue source for Ontario? No, no, no. This is yet another example of a very bad decision on behalf of this government.
Kathryn Grimbly, Toronto
U.S. and Canada are not trying to help Palestinians
For more than 225 days, Palestinians have experienced non-stop atrocities in Gaza and the West Bank . The U.S. and Canada, amongst many other governments and institutions, have made it seem as though they are trying to prevent further loss of life but, in fact, they are standing by and watching. They express concern about Palestinians and convey "warnings" to the Israel government without any follow-through. Their actions such as abstaining from non-binding United Nations votes, pausing small amounts of military spending to Israel, sanctioning specific West Bank settlers and one-off aid programs, have no impact. They don't stop these atrocities from happening. They don't create accountability. They don't do anything meaningful for Palestinian lives or human rights.The strategy is political - do what they can to try to show they are not complicit, make a case that they care, and wait for attention to subside with the ongoing news cycles. It doesn't involve moral courage or principles or human rights. If it did, these countries would do whatever it takes, including yielding their significant influence in the region towards justice for Palestine.
Shamir Tanna, Mississauga
Abusing the Charter of Rights and Freedoms has become common place
The occupations of university campuses during the last few weeks by pro-Palestinian demonstrators are striking examples of the abuses of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms which have become far too commonplace. It has become the norm to accept claims of rights by dissident individuals or groups with little or no consideration of the responsibilities that come with rights. The Charter provides for rights of freedom of conscience, thought, expression, PEACEFUL assembly and association.
The Charter also provides for responsibility to faithfully observe the laws of Canada, respect all Canadians and allow them to enjoy their rights without discrimination, become educated on the country's history, laws and heritage. It is beyond time that those provisions of the Charter and other laws be swiftly invoked in dealing with such situations as the encampments on campus.
Remember what happened in Ottawa when the Emergencies Act was finally invoked? Canada prides itself as a country living under the "rule of law."
Donald M. Macpherson, Oakville
Right on protesters!
Pro-Palestinian encampment protesters get trespass notices at U of T - but what will Monday bring? May 25
We two geriatric alums raise our canes to salute the protesters encamped at U of T. As we used to say in a certain decade: Right On!
Jo-Anne St. James and Richard Shapcott, Gore's Landing, ON
Sweet solitude
A robot with Scarlett Johansson's voice won't solve loneliness. But this might, May 24
Rick Salutin's observations on the subject of loneliness are comprehensive and insightful. It's good that someone has written on this subject, as it seems to be an epidemic in our times. I was once advised to not find my own "company onerous," so I learned to live alone, doing what I want when I want, without having to answer to anyone else. This was some good advice which I advocate to all and sundry. Not that loneliness ever entirely goes away, but at least it's better to be "alone" by "choice" (and it can be a choice.) After living mostly alone for 21 years between marriages, and having developed some good friendships through my favourite activities, I've found self-reliance and contentedness. I am now in a fulfilling relationship for all of the above reasons. Invest in yourself.
Liz Fisher, Toronto
Teenage soldiers died for the freedom young voters now take for grantedPolls for the EU elections indicate a hard right turn is coming. And these aren't your parents' conservatives, May 26
The right wing's ascent in Europe is benefitting from young voters. That suggests that parents, grandparents and educators are failing in teaching young people the important lessons learned from the Second World War and the heavy price paid by soldiers to defeat the Nazis. Before they vote take these young people to the soldiers' cemeteries all over Europe. They will cry as I did when they see the endless rows of little white crosses over the graves of 18, 19 and 20 year olds who died so that today's generation could enjoy democracy and freedom.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
A love letter to women's sportsI never got a chance to make my sporting dreams come true. But things are changing for girls in Toronto, May 26
Thank you Gail J. Cohen for expressing the emotions and joy of watching women's professional sports. I am simply enthralled while watching the Toronto PWHL team. They are fast and furious, all while excelling at the sport we love. I missed the opportunity of acquiring those valuable PWHL tickets. As a result, I am now on the waiting list for the WNBA and will do the same for Project 8 Women's Pro Soccer. In the interim, I will support our Canadian Women's Soccer team June 4 at the BMO field for the Summer Send Off series. Such an exciting time to appreciate these incredible female athletes, I am having so much fun!
Susan Mayrand, Toronto

Document 238 of 500

Doug Ford promised to pay Toronto for any shortfall caused by his housing rules. Here's how that's going

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-promised-pay-toronto-any-shortfall/docview/3060718653/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+promised+to+pay+Toronto+for+any+shortfall+caused+by+his+housing+rules.+Here%27s+how+that%27s+going&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Our two-year-old has gotten really into imaginative play lately. It's cute and fun, but also baffling, because he's got no sense of consistency.
So when we play together, the roles and rules are constantly changing, with no logic or narrative. One minute he's pretending he's a baby crocodile. Then he's declared himself an evil triceratops. We go from racing to jumping to dancing for no clear reason.
I was thinking about this while watching last week's meeting of Toronto council where Mayor Olivia Chow and councillors were trying to grapple with the latest piece of housing legislation to emerge from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Because it turns out Ford's approach to housing has a lot in common with my toddler: things are happening at a very rapid pace, the rules are ever-changing and no one knows what's next.
But at least playing with a toddler is fun. Watching Ford's government shamble around with the housing file? Definitely less fun.
Toronto's interim chief planner Kerri Voumvakis certainly didn't seem like she was enjoying herself when she gave a presentation at city hall last week detailing the ins and outs of the province's Bill 185, the "Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act."
"I find - and I think all of you will find - the pace of legislative change rather challenging," she said in the council chamber. "Certainly, we as staff are finding it as such. If I look back since 2018, there have been 12 reducing red-tape bills that have been enacted, six of which have been focused on planning act changes."
That's a hell of a lot of change packed into a relatively short period of time, and for Ontario's largest city the major outcome of all this provincial legislation has been to cast the planning department in a state of uncertainty as they're constantly required to rewrite the rule book for development applications. City planners have not generally been consulted in advance of this legislation, or even been told what's coming.
But the confusion is not the worst part. This piecemeal tinkering with the planning act has left Toronto city hall facing a major financial gap that is already imperiling its ability to deliver on badly needed affordable housing and shelter projects.
Bill 23, one of the earlier provincial housing bills, changed the rules for development charges - the fee municipalities charge for each new unit built - and the community benefits developers have traditionally been required to fund. When Toronto crunched the numbers, they found that the changes would amount to a whopping $2.3 billion in forgone revenue over 10 years - money that was earmarked for affordable housing, shelters and community infrastructure.
But the Ford government told city hall not to worry about it. It committed to "make the city whole" by replacing any lost revenue. But it never adequately explained how exactly it would do that, or when the money would come. It's been nearly 18 months since Bill 23 received royal assent, and Toronto is still waiting for answers.
Bill 185 does walk back some of the changes in Bill 23, but the walk-back only amounts to a "baby step" according to Voumvakis. The city calculates that the new piece of housing legislation reduces the impact from Bill 23 by about $144 million.
That amounts to about six per cent of what Toronto needs and was promised. For every dollar the province owes the city, Doug Ford's offering six cents.
So far, anyway. Chow told councillors to "stay hopeful" and that the Ford government is listening, but damage has already been done. City CFO Stephen Conforti said the province's continued refusal to allow Toronto to use development charges to fund affordable housing initiatives has already seen some housing projects "paused and removed from the current capital plan."
I'm not advocating for an outright return to the way things were before Bill 23. There's a good argument that Toronto and other municipalities were relying too heavily on development charges - which are generally passed on to homebuyers - to fund infrastructure.
But the Ford government removing revenue Toronto was counting on to fund important infrastructure with no immediate recourse was never a reasonable way to address that.
There's another world where instead of jamming through a series of rapid-fire housing bills that sometimes contradicted one another, Ford took time to craft a singular piece of comprehensive housing reform legislation written in collaboration with municipalities, affordable housing providers and the development industry.
As it is, while I have some confidence Chow may be able to cajole the rest of the missing funds out of Ford, I have no confidence that the premier's continued tinkering with the planning act will ever make progress toward what Toronto needs: a sustainable path to building lots of affordable housing. Because at this point, Ford declaring that he's building affordable housing has all the seriousness of my kid declaring himself to be a dinosaur.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government said it would "make the city whole" for any revenue lost when it changed development charges, which the city estimated would cost is $2.1 billion in forgone revenue over 10 years.

Subject: Legislation; Infrastructure; Affordable housing; Planning

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Affordable housing

Title: Doug Ford promised to pay Toronto for any shortfall caused by his housing rules. Here's how that's going

Document 239 of 500

Beer Store's days are numbered, experts say: Retailer says it has plan 'to adapt and thrive' as province opens up market

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beer-stores-days-are-numbered-experts-say/docview/3060710888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Beer+Store%27s+days+are+numbered%2C+experts+say%3A+Retailer+says+it+has+plan+%27to+adapt+and+thrive%27+as+province+opens+up+market&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Beer Store might not make it to its 100th birthday.
With the announcement last week by Premier Doug Ford that beer, wine, cider and seltzers will be coming to corner stores across Ontario by September and big box stores by Halloween, the once-mighty beer juggernaut is on the verge of disappearing from the retail scene, experts say.
With 8,500 more places for Ontarians to buy their beer, there's just not much need for the Beer Store, said beer author and consultant Stephen Beaumont.
"I don't think there's any question that their days as a retail entity are numbered. And that number's pretty small," said Beaumont. "It's quite conceivable we could see the end of the Beer Store as a retail entity by 2026."
The Beer Store began life as a co-operative venture between local breweries in 1927, the same year Prohibition ended in Ontario. Today, it's owned by Molson Coors, Labatt, who each control roughly 49 per cent, and Sleeman, which controls the remainder. At its peak, it sold 90 per cent of the beer consumed in Ontario.
As part of an agreement with the Beer Store and its owners, the provincial government is paying $225 million to get an early jump on wide-open alcohol retailing that would otherwise have had to wait until the beginning of 2026, after the official end of the master framework agreement.
The MFA - introduced by then-premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 - is set to expire at the end of 2025.
As part of the "early implementation agreement" announced Friday, the Beer Store - which currently has 440 stores across the province - has to keep at least 386 stores open until July 1, 2025. From July 1, 2025, through the end of that year, they have to keep at least 300 open. The following year? There's no minimum store count mandated.
"From and after Jan. 1, 2026, TBS shall be entitled to close any retail location as determined by TBS in its sole and absolute discretion," says the agreement.
The province has the right to terminate the agreement if the Beer Store goes into insolvency, or one of its owners pulls out of the company.
Beer educator and author Jordan St. John says it's clear the Beer Store is getting out of the retail business, even if it keeps its distribution arm alive.
"There's no reason for it to exist in a world where there are 8,500 other locations to buy beer," said St. John.
Even if the Beer Store suddenly started offering different ranges of products - including food - they'd be playing catch-up with thousands of other competitors, said retail consultant Lisa Hutcheson.
"What are they going to add? Gourmet chips? Another food element? But then they'd have to be more unique than the convenience store, and it goes back to 'they're no different, and no more convenient,'" said Hutcheson, managing partner at retail consultancy J.C. Williams Group.
In a written statement, the Beer Store didn't specifically address whether it would be closing some - or all - of its stores, but said it plans to stay in business, and pointed to $100 million worth of "future-focused" investments, including a new distribution centre in Bolton, Ont.
"The Beer Store is planning to adapt and thrive. We will expand our distribution fleet and network to service the new retail outlets," the statement said.
The head of the union local which represents the Beer Store's 7,000 full and part-time employees said the province's changes could be "disastrous" for his members.
"We are concerned by these changes, and it could be disastrous: many of our members could lose their jobs as retail stores close," said John Nock, president of Local 12R24 of United Food and Commercial Workers.
The $225 million pledged to the Beer Store's owners should go toward supporting workers, Nock said, including for severance payments, retraining, and other supports.
"These are workers who have families and contribute to their communities: They were guaranteed stability until 2026, and this announcement revokes it," said Nock.
The new agreement also gives the Beer Store the right to operate the Ontario Deposit Return Program until the end of 2030.
That, says Beaumont, could keep the company alive, if it transitions some remaining stores to simply becoming bottle return depots.
"That's what the Beer Store is now, much more than it is anything else. They're certainly not making money selling beer any more. That much has been obvious for a couple of years," said Beaumont.
Hutcheson agreed, and suggested that the Beer Store's distribution arm could have some long-term viability, given that its fleet of trucks already distributes beer to thousands of restaurants and bars across the province.
"Maybe they become more of a distributor and then rationalize stores, and use themselves as just that distribution point for more bars and restaurants. That might make more sense for them," said Hutcheson.
Still, argued Beaumont, even as a distribution-only entity, the Beer Store's survival wouldn't be guaranteed.
"It will be a viable service so long as Molson and Labatt decide it's in their best interests for it to be a viable service," said Beaumont. "The moment they decide it doesn't make sense any more, it's gone."
CREDIT: Josh Rubin Toronto Star

Subject: Agreements; Beer; Breweries

Document 240 of 500

Polls show mayoral byelection a tight race: Front-runner down eight points since skipping debates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/polls-show-mayoral-byelection-tight-race/docview/3060710442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Polls+show+mayoral+byelection+a+tight+race%3A+Front-runner+down+eight+points+since+skipping+debates&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With just two weeks until Mississauga's byelection, numbers from a new poll out today shows that for the first time in decades, there could be a real race for the mayoral seat.
It also suggests that the decision of front-runner Carolyn Parrish, who has dropped eight points since the last poll by Liaison Strategies was conducted in April, to pull out of all mayoral debates this month may have harmed the double-digit lead she has held over her competitors until now. Most gains were seen by Coun. Dipika Damerla, who has narrowed Parrish's lead, only trailing by five per cent. Alvin Tedjo's campaign also saw a two per cent boost - as the number of undecided voters decreased.
The numbers come as the city says it saw a "significant increase in voter turnout" during the advanced voting last weekend. There was a "42 per cent increase compared to the first three days of advance polling in the 2022 municipal election" with over 2,250 voters voting early. And one pollster says the numbers show that the race is "wide-open."
David Valentin, the principal at Liaison Strategies said the numbers indicate a "major shift before election day." He said "as we reach the final days of the campaign there is a large pool of reliable voters that still hasn't made up their minds."
This is Mississauga's first mayoral race in five decades without an incumbent or clear front-runner. The election was triggered when former mayor Bonnie Crombie stepped down in January to become leader of the Ontario Liberal Party.
The latest poll numbers show that Parrish still leads among decided voters, with 29 per cent saying they will still vote for Parrish, a long-time politician who was a federal MP in the early 2000s and has been a municipal politician for several terms. Damerla has 24 per cent of the decided vote, while Tedjo has 19 per cent, per poll numbers.
Valentin said that while Parrish is still in front, her lead is "smaller and more tenuous than before."
Damerla said via email that she was encouraged by the recent numbers and "feeling very positive about what we are hearing at the doors" and "overwhelming request for signs." She said has an "ambition and practical plan to keep Mississauga affordable and livable" and has been showing up at debates to talk to as many residents as possible.
Valentin says that the undecided voter number is also one to watch. "The undecided rate has dropped to 16 per cent among all voters - but it has jumped to 37 per cent among seniors." He says that could likely be attributed to uncertainty amongst seniors given Parrish's decision to pull out of the debates.
Earlier this month, Parrish said she would not be participating in any future debates in the city, citing concerns that she was being "personally threatened," without sharing specifics about the threats.
The decision also came days after a debate where Parrish was accused of making controversial statements that critics called transphobic and discriminatory towards refugee claimants - and that has led to criticism on social media.
"If Parrish is able to win back seniors, she has a very good chance of being mayor but the opportunity is there for the Damerla and Tedjo campaigns to seize the momentum and votes," Valentin said.
The poll was conducted on May 24-25, 2024, the poll surveyed 907 Mississauga residents with a margin of error for the poll is +/- 3.22 per cent, 19 times out of 20.
Voters have another chance to vote early on Saturday and Sunday.
Election day is on June 10.
CREDIT: Noor Javed Toronto Star

Subject: Polls & surveys; Local elections; Mayors; Voters; Voter behavior

Document 241 of 500

Ford's pledge on housing shortfalls falls short

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-pledge-on-housing-shortfalls-falls-short/docview/3060709755/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+pledge+on+housing+shortfalls+falls+short&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Our two-year-old has gotten really into imaginative play lately. It's cute and fun, but also baffling, because he's got no sense of consistency.
So when we play together, the roles and rules are constantly changing, with no logic or narrative. One minute he's pretending he's a baby crocodile. Then he's declared himself an evil triceratops. We go from racing to jumping to dancing for no clear reason.
I was thinking about this while watching last week's meeting of Toronto council where Mayor Olivia Chow and councillors were trying to grapple with the latest piece of housing legislation to emerge from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Because it turns out Ford's approach to housing has a lot in common with my toddler: things are happening at a very rapid pace, the rules are ever-changing and no one knows what's next.
But at least playing with a toddler is fun. Watching Ford's government shamble around with the housing file? Definitely less fun.
Toronto's interim chief planner Kerri Voumvakis certainly didn't seem like she was enjoying herself when she gave a presentation at city hall last week detailing the ins and outs of the province's Bill 185, the "Cutting Red Tape to Build More Homes Act."
"I find - and I think all of you will find - the pace of legislative change rather challenging," she said in the council chamber. "Certainly, we as staff are finding it as such. If I look back since 2018, there have been 12 reducing red-tape bills that have been enacted, six of which have been focused on planning act changes."
That's a hell of a lot of change packed into a relatively short period of time, and for Ontario's largest city the major outcome of all this provincial legislation has been to cast the planning department in a state of uncertainty as they're constantly required to rewrite the rule book for development applications. City planners have not generally been consulted in advance of this legislation, or even been told what's coming.
But the confusion is not the worst part.
This piecemeal tinkering with the planning act has left Toronto city hall facing a major financial gap that is already imperiling its ability to deliver on badly needed affordable housing and shelter projects.
Bill 23, one of the earlier provincial housing bills, changed the rules for development charges - the fee municipalities charge for each new unit built - and the community benefits developers have traditionally been required to fund.
When Toronto crunched the numbers, they found that the changes would amount to a whopping $2.3 billion in forgone revenue over 10 years - money that was earmarked for affordable housing, shelters and community infrastructure.
But the Ford government told city hall not to worry about it. It committed to "make the city whole" by replacing any lost revenue. But it never adequately explained how exactly it would do that, or when the money would come. It's been nearly 18 months since Bill 23 received royal assent, and Toronto is still waiting for answers.
Bill 185 does walk back some of the changes in Bill 23, but the walk-back only amounts to a "baby step" according to Voumvakis. The city calculates that the new piece of housing legislation reduces the impact from Bill 23 by about $144 million.
That amounts to about six per cent of what Toronto needs and was promised. For every dollar the province owes the city, Doug Ford's offering six cents.
So far, anyway. Chow told councillors to "stay hopeful" and that the Ford government is listening, but damage has already been done. City CFO Stephen Conforti said the province's continued refusal to allow Toronto to use development charges to fund affordable housing initiatives has already seen some housing projects "paused and removed from the current capital plan."
I'm not advocating for an outright return to the way things were before Bill 23. There's a good argument that Toronto and other municipalities were relying too heavily on development charges - which are generally passed on to homebuyers - to fund infrastructure.
But the Ford government removing revenue Toronto was counting on to fund important infrastructure with no immediate recourse was never a reasonable way to address that.
There's another world where instead of jamming through a series of rapid-fire housing bills that sometimes contradicted one another, Ford took time to craft a singular piece of comprehensive housing reform legislation written in collaboration with municipalities, affordable housing providers and the development industry.
As it is, while I have some confidence Chow may be able to cajole the rest of the missing funds out of Ford, I have no confidence that the premier's continued tinkering with the planning act will ever make progress toward what Toronto needs: a sustainable path to building lots of affordable housing. Because at this point, Ford declaring that he's building affordable housing has all the seriousness of my kid declaring himself to be a dinosaur.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Legislation; Infrastructure; Affordable housing; Planning

Business indexing term: Subject: Infrastructure Affordable housing

Document 242 of 500

Report on staff emails delayed: Watchdog has 19 active freedom-of-information appeal files on subject

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/report-on-staff-emails-delayed/docview/3060709739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Report+on+staff+emails+delayed%3A+Watchdog+has+19+active+freedom-of-information+appeal+files+on+subject&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's privacy watchdog says a report on the use of personal email accounts by staff conducting government business related to the Greenbelt is "not imminent," and will only be issued after a number of freedom-of-information appeals are dealt with.
In a statement to the Star, the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario said it currently has "19 active appeal files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt. Upon conclusion of these appeals, we plan to publish a special report on our website encapsulating our findings and providing a coherent and thoughtful summary of our conclusions and insights resulting from all 19 appeals."
This "will not impact our normal appeal process," the statement said, adding that "while we cannot provide a specific release date for the special report at this time, we are committed to fully completing the appeals process for the 19 current appeals. The release of the special report, as such, is not imminent."
Privacy commissioner Patricia Kosseim confirmed that work is underway on the report in a May 21 letter to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who has formally raised concerns about the use of private emails or the use of code words to evade freedom-of-information requests.
"Transparency in government actions and decisions is a fundamental principle that underpins the public's trust in government," Kosseim said. "Ensuring such actions are open to external scrutiny is essential to maintaining this trust and upholding the values that guide our work at the IPC ... My office takes these matters seriously and is committed to a thorough, fair, and impartial examination of the issues arising from these various appeals, your letter and the auditor general's report."
The RCMP is already investigating the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal, although no updates have been provided. Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in the selection of sites to be removed from the Greenbelt for housing - decisions later reversed by the Ford government as it restored the properties to their protected status - but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
In the legislature Monday morning, Stiles said Premier Doug Ford should ask his chief of staff to resign in light of a Global News report that "revealed new evidence that the premier's chief of staff used his personal email account to conduct government business on dozens of occasions. That directly contradicts his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner when he claimed, 'I do not conduct government business on my personal email.' But guess what? He does.
"The premier's chief of staff appears to have repeatedly and directly contradicted his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner under oath."
In response, Government House Leader Paul Calandra said the government "will continue to work with both the integrity commissioner and the information and privacy commissioner. Nothing has changed on that score
"If the leader of the Opposition has any additional information that she would like to provide the integrity commissioner, I encourage her to do so."
In April, the Star reported that the province's integrity commissioner cleared Ford's chief of staff Patrick Sackville of wrongdoing after an email from his personal account came to light. After looking into a complaint launched by Stiles, the integrity commissioner said he "accepted" Sackville's explanation that the messages sent in October 2022 were missed.
The NDP had also provided the information and privacy commissioner documents indicating that "G*" was being used instead of "Greenbelt" in emails, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said he welcomes the privacy commissioner's decision to report on "the Ford government's Greenbelt secrecy."
All along, he added, "we've known that the government's story doesn't add up. From deleted emails to misremembered dates, this government has gone out of its way to conceal the truth from the public. I hope this investigation sheds some much-needed light on the government's actions and delivers the honesty and accountability Ontarians deserve."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 243 of 500

Tories consider calling early election: Ford reportedly worried by potential funding cuts under a Conservative PM

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-consider-calling-early-election/docview/3060709476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+consider+calling+early+election%3A+Ford+reportedly+worried+by+potential+funding+cuts+under+a+Conservative+PM&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is considering an early election call before the scheduled 2026 vote over concerns about cuts a future federal Conservative government might impose, the Star has learned.
Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives.
That's a major reason why the premier is paying the Beer Store $225 million to liberalize booze sales as of this fall - more than one year earlier than planned - in a deal critics warn could actually cost taxpayers between $600 million and $1 billion.
At his announcement Friday to tout the expedited beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the planned June 2026 election and left open the possibility of a snap vote, which could cost more than $155 million, this fall or next spring.
"I just want to get our agenda through," the premier told reporters, noting the expansion of beer sales was a long-standing PC campaign promise.
But Tory insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss ongoing internal deliberations, say Ford was giving himself options at a time when his party has just won two byelections and enjoys a big poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," said one insider, who is bullish on the plan.
Another said it would force the Tory caucus to focus on priorities like delivering on the 2022 campaign's "Get It Done" pledges, such as improved highways, public transit and hospital construction.
"We have to show results and (booze liberalization) is something we promised to deliver and the premier has done that," said the second official.
A third PC source conceded an earlier-than-anticipated election could get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the ongoing RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
But some Tories fear a reprise of 1990 - when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called a snap vote, and then lost to Bob Rae's NDP in one of the most stunning upsets in Canadian political history.
"Peterson was higher in the polls (50 per cent in summer of 1990) than Ford (at 39 per cent in the latest Abacus Data survey) is now and had won a larger majority (in 1987 than Ford did in 2022)," said a fourth Tory. "But voters were ornery and punished him for the early election."
A fifth Tory expressed alarm that voters would feel it was "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after Ford received such a resounding re-election mandate in June 2022.
"What would the rationale be for going early?" asked the Ford loyalist.
The premier's inner circle is mindful of the potential perils - but also aware of provincial political history. According to what's known as the "Underhill balance theory," when Ontario voters elect Liberals federally, they tend to elect Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially.
That has been the case for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford's team believes a Poilievre victory in October 2025 would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026, which is why they may act pre-emptively.
For his part, Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of Ford going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, who governed Ontario from 1985 to 1990.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
Still, some on Ford's team are poring over "Not Without Cause," the searing 1991 post-mortem book on Peterson's gamble by former Liberal aides Georgette Gagnon and Dan Rath. The book - billed as "a cautionary tale for voters and politicians everywhere" - chronicled the mounting anger voters felt over an early election that cost $40 million at the time.
"Confident they knew what Ontarians were thinking in the summer of 1990, the Liberal strategists gave no thought to the possibility Peterson might actually be defeated in the election he was about to call," wrote Gagnon and Rath.
Elections Ontario said the 2022 election cost $145.3 million, which according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator, is the equivalent of $155.8 million this year.
Rivals charged Ford risks the wrath of voters if he holds an early vote for his own benefit.
"The people of this province would see it for what it is, which is an attempt to divert and hide what's coming or the RCMP criminal investigation, but I say also - bring it on," said Stiles.
Crombie said governments calling early elections are "typically punished" for wasting tens of millions of dollars.
"We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer. They have a majority government," she said, calling Ford's liberalization push "a billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
Under Ontario's election law, a premier does not have to abide by the fixed date every four years.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+want+better+access+to+health+care+not+beer+Doug+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Doug+Ford+says+beer+and+wine+are+coming+to+Ontario+corner+stores%2C+May+24+Pro-Palestinian+encampment+protesters+get+trespass+notices+at+U+of+T+-+but+what+will+Monday+bring%3F+May+25+A+robot+with+Scarlett+Johansson%27s+voice+won%27t+solve+loneliness.+But+this+might%2C+May+24+Polls+for+the+EU+elections+indicate+a+hard+right+turn+is+coming.+And+these+aren%27t+your+parents%27+conservatives%2C+May+26+I+never+got+a+chance+to+make+my+sporting+dreams+come+true.+But+things+are+changing+for+girls+in+Toronto%2C+May+26&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We want better access to health care, not beer
Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
Premier Doug Ford's government is using valuable time and funds to put beer and wine in convenience stores. As a resident of a rural community in Ontario, I would far rather see time and money spent on providing adequate health-care facilities, doctors and nurse practitioners to those of us who have no primary care provider and very little hope of attaining one in the near future. To me this push to supply more access to alcohol is misguided when so many Ontarians are struggling to get help for a wide range of health issues. Wake up, Ford. Realign your priorities to meet the real needs of the people of Ontario, the people who voted you into power.
Sharon Phelps, Lindsay
LCBO thieves will now target corner stores
Why would any small store want to sell alcohol when the liquor stores can't prevent wholesale theft as it is? We are about to see an uptick in violent crime and theft. Money given to the Beer Store to accommodate this should be going to the housing crisis or the crisis in health care or to water and resources for Indigenous people or to any of the other crises facing Ontario right now. Why would I want to divert profits from the LCBO, which helps fund services in Ontario to private concerns? Why would you ruin something that has worked well for decades and is a revenue source for Ontario? No, no, no. This is yet another example of a very bad decision on behalf of this government.
Kathryn Grimbly, Toronto
U.S. and Canada are not trying to help Palestinians
For more than 225 days, Palestinians have experienced non-stop atrocities in Gaza and the West Bank. The U.S. and Canada, among many other governments and institutions, have made it seem as though they are trying to prevent further loss of life but, in fact, they are standing by and watching. They express concern about Palestinians and convey "warnings" to the Israeli government without any follow-through. Their actions such as abstaining from non-binding United Nations votes, pausing small amounts of military spending to Israel, sanctioning specific West Bank settlers and one-off aid programs have no impact. They don't stop these atrocities from happening. They don't create accountability. They don't do anything meaningful for Palestinian lives or human rights. The strategy is political - do what they can to try to show they are not complicit, make a case that they care, and wait for attention to subside with the ongoing news cycles. It doesn't involve moral courage or principles or human rights. If it did, these countries would do whatever it takes, including yielding their significant influence in the region toward justice for Palestine.
Shamir Tanna, Mississauga
Too many care about rights over responsibilities
The occupations of university campuses during the past few weeks by pro-Palestinian demonstrators are striking examples of the abuses of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms that have become far too commonplace. It has become the norm to accept claims of rights by dissident individuals or groups with little or no consideration of the responsibilities that come with rights.
Donald M. Macpherson, Oakville
Right on, protesters!
Pro-Palestinian encampment protesters get trespass notices at U of T - but what will Monday bring? May 25
We two geriatric alums raise our canes to salute the protesters encamped at U of T. As we used to say in a certain decade: Right on!
Jo-Anne St. James and Richard Shapcott, Gore's Landing, Ont.
Sweet solitude and self-reliance
A robot with Scarlett Johansson's voice won't solve loneliness. But this might, May 24
Rick Salutin's observations on loneliness are comprehensive and insightful. It's good people are writing on this subject, as it seems to be an epidemic in our times. I was once advised to not find my own "company onerous," so I learned to live alone, doing what I want when I want, without having to answer to anyone else. This was some good advice which I advocate to all and sundry. Not that loneliness ever entirely goes away, but it's better to be "alone" by "choice" (and it can be a choice.) After living mostly alone for 21 years between marriages, and having developed some good friendships through my favourite activities, I've found self-reliance and contentedness. I'm now in a fulfilling relationship for all of the above reasons. Invest in yourself.
Liz Fisher, Toronto
Teens died for freedom many take for granted
Polls for the EU elections indicate a hard right turn is coming. And these aren't your parents' conservatives, May 26
The right wing's ascent in Europe is benefitting from young voters. That suggests that parents, grandparents and educators are failing in teaching young people the important lessons learned from the Second World War and the heavy price paid by soldiers to defeat the Nazis. Before they vote, take these young people to the soldiers' cemeteries all over Europe. They will cry as I did when they see the endless rows of little white crosses over the graves of 18-, 19- and 20-year-olds who died so that today's generation could enjoy democracy and freedom.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
A love letter to women's sports
I never got a chance to make my sporting dreams come true. But things are changing for girls in Toronto, May 26
Thank you Gail J. Cohen for expressing the emotions and joy of watching women's professional sports. I am simply enthralled while watching the Toronto PWHL team. They are fast and furious, all while excelling at the sport we love. I missed the opportunity of acquiring those valuable PWHL tickets. As a result, I am now on the waiting list for the WNBA and will do the same for Project 8 women's pro soccer. In the interim, I'll support our Canadian women's soccer team June 4 at the BMO field for the Summer Send Off series. Such an exciting time to appreciate these incredible female athletes; I am having so much fun!
Susan Mayrand, Toronto

Subject: Health care access; Atrocities; Womens sports; Beer; Human rights; Palestinian people; Loneliness

Document 245 of 500

Settlement offers have been made and rejected Deadlines have come ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/settlement-offers-have-been-made-rejected/docview/3060709431/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Settlement+offers+have+been+made+and+rejected+Deadlines+have+come+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Settlement offers have been made and rejected. Deadlines have come and gone. Convocation is around the corner. As the Palestinian solidarity camp at the University of Toronto stretches into its fourth week, all outcomes other than a police intervention appear increasingly unlikely.
With tensions stretching to their limit, representatives of workers have thrown their hat into the ring, pledging to support the student protesters till the end.
On Monday, union leaders from across Ontario descended on the University of Toronto campus, vowing to physically defend the students.
"Our job is to put our bodies in between you and whatever the administration brings to you," JP Hornick, president, Ontario Public Service Employees' Union (OPSEU), told a rally by the protesters and their allies.
"If the police come, we will be your human shields. We will be your line of defence. And I promise you that we will be here for as long as it takes to make sure that you are safe."
Laura Walton, president of the Ontario Federation of Labour (OFL), pledged not only her union membership in defence of the protesters, but her own maternal instinct.
"I'm the proud mother of one of these young women," she said. "So I stand here as your mom. And momma bears are hard to get through, just so you know."
The labour groups made their position crystal clear on a day that saw U of T file an application with the courts for a judicial injunction to have the encampment removed, and the province reiterate its wish to seen an end to the protest.
Meanwhile, the university and protesters were continuing negotiations. The protesters have been calling for the school to divest from Israel. The university has said it would set up a committee to study divestment, but the protesters say the urgency of the war demands immediate commitments.
"Gazans need us to act, to get on the street, to demand from our institutions to disclose, divest and cut ties with Israeli institutions complicit in genocide," said Mariam Khatib, a U of T student with family in Gaza, including a second cousin who she said was recently killed. "I will never undergo the strife my family in Gaza is experiencing. The very least I can do is set up a tent."
The encampment has been polarizing. It's been a demonstration of solidarity amid a tragic war for supporters, but it has also been a magnet for criticism, with some calling it an intimidating display with shows of antisemitism.
On Saturday, in response to the university's trespassing notice, the OFL's Walton issued a call to all unions to support the encampment, and on Monday she was joined by four past and present union leaders, including Sid Ryan, former head of CUPE; Fred Hahn, current president of CUPE; and Carolyn Egan, president, United Steelworkers (USW) Toronto Area Council.
While some welcome the unions' stand as bringing institutional heft to the pro-Palestinian movement, others worry about the implications of big-tent organizations taking sides.
Walton said that, in her mind, support for the protesters is undeniably linked to labour issues.
"If the university administrators can get away with trampling on your rights to protest and dismissing your legitimate demands, then employers everywhere will feel emboldened to do the same," she said.
More broadly, Walton said that, in her view, Palestinian issues clearly affect workers in Canada. She said political instability and violence in the region disrupts global supply chains and can drive up costs here at home.
"If you really wanted to fight inflation and the cost of living crisis, then quit cheerleading the war in Gaza and demand a ceasefire."
Labour groups aren't the only ones making their position clear.
Ontario's post-secondary minister said Monday universities should have acted sooner and she wants to ensure encampments are dealt with in advance of upcoming graduation ceremonies.
"We want to ensure that this is cleaned up and dealt with right away - convocation is coming up in the next couple of weeks," Colleges and Universities Minister Jill Dunlop told reporters at Queen's Park.
"This is a safety issue. We want to ensure that families feel safe going on campus, that they are not intimidated, to attend those services. But we want to see this cleaned up right away."
Dunlop said she is also worried about the safety of those in the encampments, as well as faculty and staff.
When asked if police should be involved, she said that universities are autonomous and "they need to make those decisions. They were clear from the beginning that this was considered trespassing and action would be taken. Now, here we are a couple of weeks later and nothing has happened. But the premier and I have been very supportive of ending the encampments."
Opposition NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she disagrees with the university appealing to the courts for an injunction, saying, "This is a peaceful protest. The students have the right to protest and the University of Toronto would be better off actually negotiating with the students."
Elsewhere, hours after being the focus of frustrations at the U of T rally, university president Meric Gertler found himself appearing before a House of Commons committee, along with other school administrators, to answer questions about concerns of antisemitism on campus.
Thornhill MP Melissa Lantsman asked particularly about the protest encampment.
Gertler said there had been some incidents of offensive signs and language during the encampment, and that the university had asked student organizers to address them.
Labour organizations, despite the strong show of support Monday, have faced questions over their support for the pro-Palestinian movement throughout the current war.
In an effort to put a stop to OPSEU involvement in pro-Palestinian protests, about 160 union members signed an open letter in January, urging union leadership to condemn the members who bring OPSEU flags to protests supporting this cause or "any cause that is not labour-related."
"We do not pay union dues to have death chants against us seemingly glorified by the SAME union that represents us," the letter read.
In November, Jewish members of CUPE made a complaint to the Human Rights Tribunal alleging antisemitic discrimination from union leadership, including comments from CUPE Ontario's president Fred Hahn that supported Hamas's Oct. 7 attack. Hahn has since apologized.
Unions do have a history of international advocacy. But widespread criticism of Israel from the Canadian labour movement is a relatively new phenomenon.
In 2017, the Canadian Labour Congress passed a resolution to push the federal government to support Palestinian prisoners, who were on hunger strike seeking better treatment and an end to Israel's practice of detention without trial.
The Canadian Union of Postal Workers, which authored the resolution, noted this marked a turning point after years of Israel being a "taboo subject" for unions.
Canadian trade unionists had not shied away from wading into controversy. They joined the anti-war cause in the 1960s, demanding the Canadian government stop the flow of arms to Vietnam. And in the 1970s and '80s, thousands took part in anti-Apartheid movements to persuade the government to take action against South Africa.
CREDIT: Ben Cohen, Marco Chown Oved and Ana Pereira Toronto Star

Document 246 of 500

Ford's $225M beer bet buys time: Premier hopes boosting availability will help win early election - as taxpayers cover the tab

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-225m-beer-bet-buys-time/docview/3060709424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+%24225M+beer+bet+buys+time%3A+Premier+hopes+boosting+availability+will+help+win+early+election+-+as+taxpayers+cover+the+tab&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has figured out the price of power. Too bad we're all paying for it.
To the premier, it's small beer - a mere $225 million in subsidies for suds. That's how much he's admitted to paying out in taxpayer funds to the big brewers as financial compensation for allowing beer into convenience stores roughly a year ahead of schedule.
The political payoff? Ford wins bragging rights, with our money, for belatedly delivering on his 2018 campaign promise to make beer available just about anywhere, anytime.
It also buys him the flexibility to hold an early election - not in 2026, as scheduled under Ontario's fixed election law, but a year sooner. At what price, precisely?
By some counts, it is destined to be far higher than the purported low ball figure of $225 million - as much as $1 billion, according to opposition critics.
Remember "buck-a-beer," the other cynical slogan Ford dined out on in 2018? It fizzled out fast because that unsustainable price for a can of beer defied common sense and business sense.
During his winning campaign, buck-a-beer was twinned with another slogan that was similarly oversold - "beer in corner stores." Ford knew perfectly well that the Beer Store's owners could sue the province for defaulting on a binding 10-year contract under which they'd first agreed to relax their grip on the market by phasing in supermarket beer sales.
That pioneering framework, negotiated by the previous Liberal government, broke the stranglehold of the big brewers. But it wasn't good enough for Ford - who always boasts of bettering what came before.
Mercifully, Ford's most influential cabinet ministers managed to talk him out of breaking the beer pact, persuading him that the province faced costly litigation and possible penalties exceeding $1 billion. Yet it always rankled our teetotalling premier that he'd failed to deliver on a forgotten promise.
Now, all these years later, he has contented himself with an anticlimactic announcement about an antiquated issue. Belatedly, beer will become more convenient - a mere one year ahead of when it otherwise would have, and four decades after it first became a political issue.
Back in the 1980s, the Liberal leader of the day, David Peterson, first promised beer in corner stores but found it inconveniently complicated to deliver on it. Now, it's a non-issue that no one notices.
They're not even called corner stores anymore. They're no longer on every corner, not all that convenient, and mostly owned by big chains, not Mom and Pop operators.
Which is why Ford's news fell flat on Friday - flatter than a stale beer washing down a nothing burger. Still, there is good reason to pay attention, for we will potentially be paying far more than Ford pretends.
Beyond the stated subsidy of $225 million going to the Beer Store's owners, another $375 million in fees now paid by them to the LCBO will be rebated.
As Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie points out, a wholesale discount of 10 per cent for the big grocery chains - now enjoying record profits - could easily total more than $150 million in lost revenues. If you add in the foregone licensing fees from 8,500 new stores (charged by other jurisdictions), that's at least $300 million more money left on the table.
By Crombie's count, it adds up to a "billion-dollar booze boondoggle" that is "lining the pockets" of the wealthy and well-connected - an echo of the campaign rhetoric Ford used against the Liberals in 2018. Buy beyond the opposition critiques, there's also the opportunity cost of spending so much time and money so utterly opportunistically on a faded, defanged oligopoly.
Quite apart from financial resources, consider the governmental bandwidth being wasted by a premier who doesn't touch a drink deploying the best minds in the public service to bringing beer into convenience stores.
Imagine if Ford spent more time and money solving real problems rather than creating new ones - dealing with overflowing surgical wait-lists, say, or a housing and homelessness crisis.
The speculation is that Ford prefers to gear up for an early provincial campaign to get out ahead of a federal election later that same year likely to crown his Tory cousin Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as prime minister. The premier is understandably wary of how shabbily the federal Conservatives might treat Ontario as they slash spending.
An early election could also allow Ford to dodge the results of an RCMP probe into his other boondoggle (that'd be the Greenbelt imbroglio).
He might also catch Crombie's Liberals off guard while deflating the NDP's Marit Stiles before either gains traction.
But it's a quid pro quo that's hard to quaff - or swallow: Ford's fantasy is that by keeping his stale promise on beer in corner stores, he is now free to break the promise of fixed election dates every four years codified in law.
Ford's billion-dollar bet may be one boondoggle too many.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's announcement fell flatter than a stale beer washing down a nothing burger, Martin Regg Cohn writes. Still, there is good reason to pay attention, as taxpayers will potentially be paying far more than Ford pretends.Andrew Francis WallaceToronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Beer; Convenience stores; Taxpayers

People: Ford, Doug

Doug Ford is betting that beer will help him win an early election - and taxpayers are stuck with the tab

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-betting-that-beer-will-help-him-win/docview/3060857370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+betting+that+beer+will+help+him+win+an+early+election+-+and+taxpayers+are+stuck+with+the+tab&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/
