Document 248 of 500

Tory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tory-insiders-say-fear-pierre-poilievre-victory/docview/3060857205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+insiders+say+fear+of+a+Pierre+Poilievre+victory+has+Doug+Ford+considering+an+early+election+call&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is considering an early election call before the scheduled 2026 vote over concerns about cuts a future federal Conservative government might impose, the Star has learned.
Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives.
That's a major reason why the premier is paying The Beer Store $225 million to liberalize booze sales as of this fall - more than one year earlier than planned - in a deal critics warn could actually cost taxpayers between $600 million and $1 billion.
At his announcement Friday to tout the expedited beer-and-wine-in-corner-stores scheme, Ford repeatedly refused to commit to the planned June 2026 election and left open the possibility of a snap vote, which could cost more than $155 million, this fall or next spring.
"I just want to get our agenda through," the premier told reporters, noting the expansion of beer sales was long-standing PC campaign promise.
But Tory insiders, speaking confidentially in order to discuss ongoing internal deliberations, say Ford was giving himself options at a time when his party has just won two byelections and enjoys a big poll lead over Bonnie Crombie's Liberals and Marit Stiles's New Democrats.
"It's smart. It gets us in front of a tough budget from Pierre that could hurt the Conservative brand and help Bonnie," said one insider, who is bullish on the plan.
Another said it would force the Tory caucus to focus on priorities like delivering on the 2022 campaign's "Get It Done" pledges, such as improved highways, public transit and hospital construction.
"We have to show results and (booze liberalization) is something we promised to deliver and the premier has done that," said the second official.
A third PC source conceded that an earlier than anticipated election could get the Tories ahead of any fallout from the ongoing RCMP criminal probe of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
But some Tories fear a reprise of 1990 - when popular Liberal premier David Peterson called a snap vote, and then lost to Bob Rae's New Democrats in one of the most stunning upsets in Canadian political history.
"Peterson was higher in the polls (50 per cent in summer of 1990) than Ford (at 39 per cent in the latest Abacus Data survey) is now and had won a larger majority (in 1987 than Ford did in 2022)," said a fourth Tory.
"But voters were ornery and punished him for the early election."
A fifth Tory expressed alarm that voters would feel it was "cynical and self-serving" to have a vote so soon after Ford received such a resounding re-election mandate in June 2022.
"What would the rationale be for going early?" asked the Ford loyalist.
The premier's inner circle is mindful of the potential perils - but also aware of provincial political history.
According to what's known as the "Underhill balance theory," when Ontario voters elect Liberals federally, they tend to elect Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the NDP - usually win provincially.
That has been the case for 70 of the past 81 years in Ontario, so Ford's team believes a Poilievre victory in October 2025 would most likely help Crombie's Liberals in June 2026, which is why they may act pre-emptively.
For his part, Peterson, now the chair of Torstar, parent company of the Toronto Star, was circumspect when asked Monday about the wisdom of Ford going to the polls early.
"Every single situation is different - different players, different times and I'm in no position to judge," said the former premier, who governed Ontario from 1985 to 1990.
"If I was so smart, I'd still be there," he joked.
Still, some on Ford's team are poring over "Not Without Cause," the searing 1991 post-mortem book on Peterson's gamble by former Liberal aides Georgette Gagnon and Dan Rath.
The book - billed as "a cautionary take for voters and politicians everywhere" - chronicled the mounting anger voters felt over an early election that cost $40 million at the time.
"Confident they knew what Ontarians were thinking in the summer of 1990, the Liberal strategists gave no thought to the possibility Peterson might actually be defeated in the election he was about to call," wrote Gagnon and Rath.
Elections Ontario said the 2022 election cost $145.3 million, which according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator, is the equivalent of $155.8 million this year.
Rivals charged Ford risks the wrath of voters if he holds an early vote for his own benefit.
"The people of this province would see it for what it is, which is an attempt to divert and hide what's coming or the RCMP criminal investigation, but I say also - bring it on," said Stiles.
Crombie said governments calling early elections are "typically punished" for wasting tens of millions of dollars.
"We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer. They have a majority government," she said, calling Ford's liberalization push "a billion-dollar booze boondoggle."
Under Ontario's election law, a premier does not have to abide by the fixed date every four years.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and his wife Karla celebrate after Ford led his Progressive Conservatives to a second election victory on June 2, 2022.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Election law; Scandals; Voters; Political history; Liberalization; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Liberalization

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Tory insiders say fear of a Pierre Poilievre victory has Doug Ford considering an early election call

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 249 of 500

Report on personal emails in Greenbelt scandal will follow FOI appeals, privacy commissioner says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/report-on-personal-emails-greenbelt-scandal-will/docview/3060857040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Report+on+personal+emails+in+Greenbelt+scandal+will+follow+FOI+appeals%2C+privacy+commissioner+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's privacy watchdog says a report on the use of personal email accounts by staff conducting government business related to the Greenbelt is "not imminent," and will only be issued after a number of freedom-of-information appeals are dealt with.
In a statement to the Star, the Office of the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario said it currently has "19 active appeal files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt. Upon conclusion of these appeals, we plan to publish a special report on our website encapsulating our findings and providing a coherent and thoughtful summary of our conclusions and insights resulting from all 19 appeals."
This "will not impact our normal appeal process," the statement said, adding that "while we cannot provide a specific release date for the special report at this time, we are committed to fully completing the appeals process for the 19 current appeals. The release of the special report, as such, is not imminent."
Privacy commissioner Patricia Kosseim confirmed that work is underway on the report in a May 21 letter to NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who has formally raised concerns about the use of private emails or the use of code words to evade freedom-of-information requests.
"Transparency in government actions and decisions is a fundamental principle that underpins the public's trust in government," Kosseim said.
"Ensuring such actions are open to external scrutiny is essential to maintaining this trust and upholding the values that guide our work at the IPC ... My office takes these matters seriously and is committed to a thorough, fair, and impartial examination of the issues arising from these various appeals, your letter and the auditor general's report."
The RCMP is already investigating the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal, although no updates have been provided. Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in the selection of sites to be removed from the Greenbelt for housing - decisions later reversed by the Ford government as it restored the properties to their protected status - but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
In the legislature Monday morning, Stiles said Premier Doug Ford should ask his chief of staff to resign in light of a Global News report that "revealed new evidence that the premier's chief of staff used his personal email account to conduct government business on dozens of occasions. That directly contradicts his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner when he claimed, 'I do not conduct government business on my personal email." But guess what? He does.
"The premier's chief of staff appears to have repeatedly and directly contradicted his sworn testimony to the integrity commissioner under oath."
In response, Government House Leader Paul Calandra said the government" will continue to work with both the integrity commissioner and the information and privacy commissioner. Nothing has changed on that score.
"If the leader of the Opposition has any additional information that she would like to provide the integrity commissioner, I encourage her to do so."
In April, the Star reported that the province's integrity commissioner cleared Ford's chief of staff Patrick Sackville of wrongdoing after an email from his personal account came to light.
After looking into a complaint launched by Stiles, the integrity commissioner said he "accepted" Sackville's explanation that the messages sent in October 2022 were missed.
The NDP had also provided the information and privacy commissioner documents indicating that "G*" was being used instead of "Greenbelt" in emails, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
"We have been deeply concerned by revelations of deleted emails, the use of personal accounts, and other apparent attempts to cover up the government's actions as it schemed to give preferential treatment to well-connected Greenbelt speculators," Stiles said Monday.
Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said he welcomes the privacy commissioner's decision to report on "the Ford government's Greenbelt secrecy."
All along, he added, "we've known that the government's story doesn't add up. From deleted emails to misremembered dates, this government has gone out of its way to conceal the truth from the public. I hope this investigation sheds some much-needed light on the government's actions and delivers the honesty and accountability Ontarians deserve."
CAPTION: Patricia Kosseim is Ontario's information and privacy commissioner.

Subject: Electronic mail systems; Appeals; Privacy; Scandals

Remembering children at G7, ticket pricing and the Ontario Line transit project

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/remembering-children-at-g7-ticket-pricing-ontario/docview/3060398112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Remembering+children+at+G7%2C+ticket+pricing+and+the+Ontario+Line+transit+project&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada must champion children at G7 summit
As we approach the G7 summit in Italy in June, Canada has an important opportunity to stand up for children caught up in our global conflicts. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must use this platform to unequivocally condemn violence against civilians, reinforce the need for humanitarian access and protection, and recognize the unique needs and rights of children in emergencies around the world. The situation is dire. Conflicts are escalating in Gaza, Haiti, and Sudan, and children are suffering the most. They face unimaginable challenges without access to health care, nutritious food, education, clean water, and sanitation. According to the UN, slightly more children have died in just over four months of conflict in Gaza than in four years of war worldwide combined. This is not only tragic, it is unacceptable. Our action at the G7 meeting in Italy lays the groundwork for the leadership we must show at the 2025 summit in Canada. It is time to protect the rights and well-being of every child around the world.
Shunyu Yu, Oakville
This decade has brought an array of challenges and surprises to our global community, unlike anything we have seen in recent memory. As a sixteen-year-old, I constantly hear from adults that my generation has witnessed more atrocities and global issues than those before us. While wars, genocides, and conflicts are not new, we often overlook the most vulnerable victims: children.
Children are the most marginalized in these conflicts. Since the Hamas attack on Oct. 7, over 14,500 children have been killed in Gaza. Additionally, 17,000 Palestinian children are believed to have been orphaned since the war began. According to UNICEF, the Taliban's recapture has left an estimated 3.7 million children out of school in Afghanistan, with 60 per cent of them being girls. The ongoing war in Ukraine has meant thousands of children are reported to have been illegally deported or forcibly transferred, not to mention the trauma on children who remain. These statistics only begin to highlight the devastating impact of global conflicts on children.
Many of these war-torn countries face repeated cycles of violence, and the cries for help from children are often ignored by global leaders. As a concerned and heartbroken Canadian, I urge Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to bring attention to these affected children. At the upcoming G7 summit in Italy, I hope he demonstrates leadership by advocating for the protection of children's lives, upholding humanitarian law and ensuring humanitarian access.
Maud Arkaah-Boafo, Brampton
Beer and circuses
Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores, May 24
I am intoxicated with giddiness. Thanks to the man once known as "buck-a-beer" Doug Ford, I now have rapid access to beer in my neighbourhood convenience store. Too bad there isn't another $225 million to help our glacially slow and inefficient Ontario health care system, but then again feeding the masses alcohol diverts their attention from the failure of government to address the real problems of our society.
Jim Smith, Port Stanley, Ont.
TPS response is inappropriate on many levels
How a Toronto police complaints officer belittled a criticism about bike lanes - and why advocates say it's a problem, May 17
I find the TPS's characterization of the community along Sherbourne very disturbing. They said there are "the good people" and then the "criminal class whose primary social activities seem to (be) fentanyl consumption." This isn't just inappropriate for a TPS spokesperson, it demonstrates a profound lack of understanding about a serious public health issue we are facing in this city and country. And then there's the "good people" vs. "criminal class" dichotomy, alongside the "brave men and women of 51 Division" as written. This self-serving depiction of what life can be like for people experiencing extreme poverty and marginalization (a failure of our system, not the individuals) reveals a systemic ignorance of, and hostility towards, the communities the TPS is mandated to serve and protect. And the police wonder why so many are calling for its radical restructuring.
Katryn Pereira, Toronto
Legislation needed to combat ticket pricing
The Justice Department is suing Ticketmaster and Live Nation. What does that mean for concertgoers? May 23
The U.S. Department of Justice wants to break up Live Nation and Ticketmaster, even though a number of years ago they supported their merger. As usual, this only affects the symptoms and ignores the cure for high event prices. Many European countries have the solution. They have passed laws to regulate pricing and assure event tickets cannot be resold for more than face value. This eliminates bots buying up tickets as soon as they go on sale only to resell them moments later for several thousand dollars more. With these laws there is no incentive to buy tickets. Of course, the U.S. and Canada should pass similar laws, but that would mean that the legislators would have to have intestinal fortitude, which the current office-holders lack.
Jacob Psutka, Toronto
Transit to a vacated science centre?
If they are moving the science centre to Ontario Place, why is the province and Metrolinx building the Ontario Line to the existing Science Centre at Eglinton Avenue and Don Mills? The province should butt out and return Toronto transit to the TTC.
Scott Fairweather, Toronto
Munro made ordinary extraordinary
As Alice Munro's longtime publisher, I had two important jobs, May 23
I read publisher Douglas Gibson's column about the late writer Alice Munro with great interest. Much has been written around the writer and her literary career. A writer of short-stories encapsulated the ordinariness of mainly rural folk, as myself, yet reveals the other side - us as world citizens. She wrote what we could not and I in particular wish to thank her so very much.
Murray McCullough, Colborne, Ont.
CAPTION: Displaced Palestinian children line up to receive food in Rafah, on the southern Gaza Strip, on May 19 amid the ongoing conflict between Israel and the militant Hamas group.

U of T deadline for pro-Palestinian encampment looms + the sad fate of the Dan Harrison public housing complex

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, May 27 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on encampment protests at U of T, the sad fate of a public housing complex and a special report on the Greenbelt scandal.
DON'T MISSNegotiations continue as U of T threatens to evict protesters this morning
A rally is planned at the University of Toronto student encampment this morning - action organizers hope will insulate them from police efforts to dismantle the site as the university's eviction deadline approaches, Ben Cohen and Emily Fagan report. They also plan to send lawyers Monday morning to fight the urgent injunction U of T is seeking against them to end the weeks-long standoff, as tense negotiations continue between university administrators and pro-Palestinian protesters. University officials warned that protesters must tear down the encampment by 8 a.m. this morning or face legal action. Catch up on what's happening.
More: U of T sent a trespass notice to protesters on Friday, demanding they dismantle their encampment or face legal repercussions - including suspension for students or termination for participating faculty and staff.Background: Last week, university officials said they will consider disclosure and divestment from Israel if the encampment was dismantled. Protesters rejected the offer, demanding "upfront commitments" from the university.Context: Protesters say the encampment, first set up on May 2, will not be torn down until the university divests from Israel. Here's what that actually means.Dan Harrison was supposed to be a shining example of public housing done right. How did it go so wrong?
The row of Victorian houses and midrise apartment blocks at Sherbourne and Dundas was once hailed as an example of what public housing could be. But half a century later, the Dan Harrison Community Complex - meant to be a refuge for Toronto's low-income residents - has become a picture of some of the city's greatest challenges, with tenants struggling with mental health, addiction, isolation and poverty, Victoria Gibson and Calvi Leon report. But despite mounting social challenges and disrepair being flagged to city hall for more than 30 years, the complex has been left to fester. Take a look inside.
Wait, what? Strangers often venture into the complex in search of potent street drugs, while vulnerable tenants have their units taken over by dealers.Go deeper: Reports have long urged the city to act - but efforts to rebuild or rehabilitate the buildings have so far proved ineffective, stalled by technical challenges, limited budgets and a tight real estate market.Ontario's privacy commissioner is expected to release a special report on the Greenbelt
Ontario's information and privacy commissioner will release a "rare and very serious" special report on Greenbelt-related emails, documents and the Ford government's record-keeping on the matter, according to NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "One way or another, the truth about Ford's Greenbelt dealings is coming out of the backrooms and into the light," she said. The special report comes in response to nearly two dozen active requests to the commissioner for records related to the Greenbelt and amid an ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28 billion land swap scandal. Here's what you need to know.
More: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie sent a letter to the RCMP on Thursday requesting an update on its Greenbelt investigation, in light of recent revelations regarding a senior staffer in premier Doug Ford's office.ICYMI: That staffer, Carlo Oliviero, met privately with at least one developer regarding their land that was later removed from the Greenbelt, newly released records detail - reigniting questions on how involved Ford's office was in selecting the parcels to be removed from the protected zone.WHAT ELSE

Document 252 of 500

Ontario's privacy commissioner to release special report on Greenbelt, NDP says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-privacy-commissioner-release-special/docview/3060279220/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+privacy+commissioner+to+release+special+report+on+Greenbelt%2C+NDP+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province's information and privacy commissioner will release a special report on Greenbelt-related emails, documents and the Ford government's record keeping on the matter, says the opposition NDP.
"We have been deeply concerned by revelations of deleted emails, the use of personal accounts, and other apparent attempts to coverup the government's actions as it schemed to give preferential treatment to well-connected Greenbelt speculators," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "This report will provide a clearer understanding of the government's conduct."
Stiles said "reports like these are rare and very serious," and added "the walls are closing in for this government. One way or another, the truth about Ford's Greenbelt dealings is coming out of the backrooms and into the light."
In April, the NDP gave documents to the Information and Privacy Commissioner indicating that in emails, "G*" was being used instead of Greenbelt, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
Stiles also says she provided examples of inconsistencies in testimony from government staffers.
The commissioner responded May 21, "revealing that they have 19 active files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt - so many that they have decided to dedicate an entire special report on access-to-information and record-keeping relating to changes to the Greenbelt," Stiles also said.
The RCMP is conducting an investigation into the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal and on Thursday Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released a letter to the commissioner and assistant commissioner
"I am asking that you provide an update on the progress of your investigation, in light of these most recent revelations," including those in a Star story about a senior staffer in Premier Doug Ford's office who met with a developer involved in the scandal.
Premier Doug Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in site selection, but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks to reporters outside the Ontario Legislature on Feb. 20, 2024.

Subject: Privacy

Title: Ontario's privacy commissioner to release special report on Greenbelt, NDP says

Document 253 of 500

Doug Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-should-put-big-tobacco-out-business/docview/3060279176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+should+put+Big+Tobacco+out+of+business&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew did something that no other provincial leader has done.
He made public how the provincial lawsuits against tobacco companies will likely be settled. Manitoba would receive an initial payment of up to $500 million, he announced, and future payments are expected.
With Ontario's population more than 10 times that of Manitoba, Premier Doug Ford may similarly be anticipating an initial payment from tobacco companies of up to $5 billion and a commitment to future installments.
Some might see this as good news, as tobacco companies would finally pay for some of the health-care costs resulting from their wrongful behaviour.
However, there's a dark side to a settlement with Big Tobacco requiring payments that go beyond the $12 billion the companies currently have available: it would need to rely on future sales of addictive and harmful products.
Combined, Ontario and the other provinces have claimed more than $500 billion from tobacco companies to compensate for the health-care costs caused by decades of wrongful marketing and public deception. This amount is more than enough to drive the Canadian branches of multinational tobacco companies into bankruptcy, forcing them to shut down.
Any future compensation payments can only be generated by keeping alive these same companies whose products currently kill 17,000 Ontarians each year and who have hooked generations of young people through vaping.
In addition, using future sales revenues to fund ongoing compensation to the provinces and other creditors means that consumers - not companies - will finance a global settlement between the provinces and the companies. It means that the purchases, health and well-being of smokers, vapers and other nicotine users will underwrite compensation for past wrongdoing.
The vicious cycle of addiction, disease and health-care costs will continue. Meanwhile, corporate directors and others whose wrongful behaviours are detailed in the provinces' claim will go unpunished.
No premier should accept a settlement proposal that legitimizes and sustains the harmful tobacco industry behaviour that is at the root of these lawsuits. No government should seek compensation for past wrongdoing that results in a continuation of harm.
Instead of extending the viability of the tobacco business and prolonging the smoking and addiction epidemic, Premier Doug Ford and his colleagues should work together to impose an orderly wind-down of the commercial tobacco market. They should seize this historic opportunity to make the companies stop recruiting new customers and start phasing out this harmful business.
If these leaders prefer a monetary settlement to one focused on protecting health, they are out of step with public opinion. A poll conducted for our organizations by Leger in the fall of 2023 found that 70 per cent of Canadians (and 80 per cent of those with an opinion) supported their "province using these lawsuits to require tobacco manufacturers to phase out the commercial sale of cigarettes in Canada." Only 17 per cent of respondents opposed this outcome.
Kinew deserves credit for breaking the silence that Ford and others have imposed over the past five years onto these negotiations as a way to deny public input or insight about the future of the tobacco industry in Canada.
Secret settlement discussions are fundamentally undemocratic. The decisions on whether this industry is allowed to escape bankruptcy and how it will be held accountable for decades of wrongdoing should not be made through a backroom deal. They are important public policy issues which deserve transparency, open discussion and public engagement.
Now is the time for Ford and his legislative colleagues to consult with Ontarians about the future they want for the tobacco industry. Such a listening exercise would not contravene any court-ordered confidentiality on insolvency discussions. It would instead help those talks by informing those at the negotiating table about the priorities of those they represent.
Without such transparency and engagement, the upcoming settlement looks very much like a sugar-coated money grab.
CAPTION: Manitoba Premier Wab Kinewhas said that his province would receive an initial payment of up to $500 million, withfuture payments expected.
CREDIT: Cynthia Callard, Flory Doucas and Les Hagen Contributors

Document 254 of 500

Why won't leaders offer real rent control?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-wont-leaders-offer-real-rent-control/docview/3060279047/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+won%27t+leaders+offer+real+rent+control%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I'm one of the lucky Torontonians who has relatively "good rent." But despite our shared landlord and similar units, some of my neighbours do not.
Because we're close to a university, our subdivided house in Annex has a high turnover of renters, it's easy to barely know your neighbours beyond a passing nod on the stairs.
But connections do occur. I met my first neighbour in 2022, when I asked them to water our plants while we were away. During the conversation, we exchanged our rental rates. At that time, I was paying just under $1,900 a month for my one-bedroom apartment, while theirs was $2,100.
Earlier this year however, when I knocked on our new neighbour's door, I learned their rent had soared to $2,700 - a nearly 30 per cent spike.
Our building was built well before Nov. 15, 2018, so it is rent controlled but rates skyrocket due to vacancy decontrol, which allows landlords to raise rents without limits between leases.
Over the last decade, rent-controlled units in Ontario should have only increased in price by 16.5 per cent. In reality, they surged more than threefold by 54.5 per cent, according to the Canadian Centre for Policy Alternatives.
And thus far no level of government has put forth an effective plan to help renters.
The Ford government's budget failed to offer any relief, which was no surprise given it's the party that established the 2018 date-based exemption.
I was hopeful that the federal budget would rise to the occasion, especially given the Liberals' declining popularity in the polls. Instead, renters were offered a Bill of Rights filled with half-measures, rather than the immediate actions necessary in a "crisis."
From 2011 to 2021, the average cost of a home in Ontario climbed by 135 per cent - the largest increase in the country. To bridge the disparity between prices and earnings, average home values need to decrease by $530,000 - over 60 per cent - or average earnings need to increase by 150 per cent to reach $137,000 per year, according to a report by Generation Squeeze.
The federal budget proposed increasing the amount we can borrow from our RRSPs and extending first-time mortgage amortization limits. None this actually lowers the cost of a home and instead favours lenders and developers.
I don't understand how paying more interest on a longer mortgage and having access to more debt won't drive prices higher. Isn't dependence on debt one of the factors that's made homes so unaffordable to begin with?
It also still leaves a crucial question unanswered: How can we realistically save for a down payment when rents are skyrocketing out of control?
Earlier this month, the Trudeau government decided to bypass Ontario and allocate affordable housing funding directly to municipalities due to the Progressive Conservatives' failure to meet building targets.
But there is more room for the federal government to intervene. And they have in the past. In the 1940s the Canadian government froze rents, as did many countries between the world wars. Then in the mid-1970s, high inflation and social pressure made the Liberals persuade provinces to introduce rent control.
Yet with so many provincial cabinet members and federal MPs invested in real estate, will elected leaders be deterred from enacting robust rent control?
The solutions offered now by the Liberals are bleak and yet the opposition may offer even less, with Pierre Poilievre's suggestion for the government to "get out of home-building" and rely on the market.
To afford housing, tenants are often consumed with work to afford rent. But advocacy movements are coming together. Some residents have gone on rent strikes. Platforms like @coolpeoplehouses on Instagram and apps like Housemate Space facilitate sublets and lease takeovers, allowing tenants to pass along stable rent.
In the meantime, my partner and I know we are in good company with our housing anger and anxieties. We are millennials, in our mid-30s, postponing starting a family, putting our lives on hold, struggling to save for a down payment - let alone retirement - and unable to build equity, or move anywhere else because we can't afford to lose our "good rent"- which isn't "good" at all.
Rachel Cairns is a writer, actor and creator. Check out her work at rachelcairns.ca
CREDIT: Rachel Cairns Contributor;

Subject: Federal budget; Down payments; Rent control

Business indexing term: Subject: Federal budget Rent control

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Privacy commissioner will release report on Greenbelt, NDP says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/privacy-commissioner-will-release-report-on/docview/3060279028/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Privacy+commissioner+will+release+report+on+Greenbelt%2C+NDP+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province's information and privacy commissioner will release a special report on Greenbelt-related emails, documents and the Ford government's record keeping on the matter, says the opposition NDP.
"We have been deeply concerned by revelations of deleted emails, the use of personal accounts, and other apparent attempts to cover up the government's actions as it schemed to give preferential treatment to well-connected Greenbelt speculators," said NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "This report will provide a clearer understanding of the government's conduct."
Stiles said "reports like these are rare and very serious," and added "the walls are closing in for this government. One way or another, the truth about (Doug) Ford's Greenbelt dealings is coming out of the backrooms and into the light."
In April, the NDP gave documents to the Information and Privacy Commissioner indicating that in emails, "G*" was being used instead of Greenbelt, and that personal phones and email accounts were being used to conduct government business.
Stiles also says she provided examples of inconsistencies in testimony from government staffers.
The commissioner responded May 21, "revealing that they have 19 active files concerning requests for records related to the Greenbelt - so many that they have decided to dedicate an entire special report on access-to-information and record-keeping relating to changes to the Greenbelt," Stiles also said.
The RCMP is conducting an investigation into the $8.28 billion Greenbelt scandal and on Thursday Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie released a letter to the commissioner and the assistant commissioner.
"I am asking that you provide an update on the progress of your investigation, in light of these most recent revelations," including those in a Star story about a senior staffer in Premier Doug Ford's office who met with a developer involved in the scandal.
Premier Doug Ford's office has repeatedly said it had no direct knowledge and played no role in selecting the lands to be removed from the Greenbelt.
Reports by the province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both determined that staff were not involved in site selection, but did say well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles says a report from the province's privacy commissioner "will provide a clearer understanding of the government's conduct."Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3060279026/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Beer%2C+wine+coming+to+corner+stores%2C+May+25+Police+slam+door+on+cyclist%27s+complaint%2C+May+21+U.S.+seeks+to+break+up+ticketing+monopoly%2C+May+24+Munro+made+the+old+seem+new%2C+Opinion%2C+May+23&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada must stand up for kids
As we approach the G7 summit in Italy in June, Canada has an important opportunity to stand up for children caught up in our global conflicts.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau must use this platform to unequivocally condemn violence against civilians, reinforce the need for humanitarian access and protection, and recognize the unique needs and rights of children in emergencies around the world. The situation is dire.
Conflicts are escalating in Gaza, Haiti, and Sudan, and children are suffering the most. They face unimaginable challenges without access to health care, nutritious food, education, clean water, and sanitation. According to the UN, slightly more children have died in just over four months of conflict in Gaza than in four years of war worldwide combined. This is not only tragic, it is unacceptable. Our action at the G7 meeting in Italy lays the groundwork for the leadership we must show at the 2025 summit in Canada. It is time to protect the rights and well-being of every child around the world.
Shunyu Yu, Oakville
This decade has brought an array of challenges and surprises to our global community, unlike anything we have seen in recent memory.
As a 16-year-old, I constantly hear from adults that my generation has witnessed more atrocities and global issues than those before us. While wars, genocides, and conflicts are not new, we often overlook the most vulnerable victims: children.
Children are the most marginalized in these conflicts. Since the Hamas attack on Oct. 7, over 14,500 children have been killed in Gaza.
Additionally, 17,000 Palestinian children are believed to have been orphaned since the war began. According to UNICEF, the Taliban's recapture has left an estimated 3.7 million children out of school in Afghanistan, with 60 per cent of them being girls. The ongoing war in Ukraine has meant thousands of children are reported to have been illegally deported or forcibly transferred, not to mention the trauma on children who remain. These statistics only begin to highlight the devastating impact of global conflicts on children.
Many of these war-torn countries face repeated cycles of violence, and the cries for help from children are often ignored by global leaders. As a concerned and heartbroken Canadian, I urge Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to bring attention to these affected children. At the upcoming G7 summit in Italy, I hope he demonstrates leadership by advocating for the protection of children's lives, upholding humanitarian law and ensuring humanitarian access.
Maud Arkaah-Boafo, Brampton
Beer and circuses
Beer, wine coming to corner stores, May 25
I am intoxicated with giddiness. Thanks to the man once known as "buck-a-beer" Doug Ford, I now have rapid access to beer in my neighbourhood convenience store.
Too bad there isn't another $225 million to help our glacially slow and inefficient Ontario health-care system, but then again feeding the masses alcohol diverts their attention from the failure of government to address the real problems of our society.
Jim Smith, Port Stanley, Ont.
TPS response
is inappropriate on many levels
Police slam door on cyclist's complaint, May 21
I find the Toronto Police Service's characterization of the community along Sherbourne very disturbing. They said there are "the good people" and then the "criminal class whose primary social activities seem to (be) fentanyl consumption." This isn't just inappropriate for a TPS spokesperson, it demonstrates a profound lack of understanding about a serious public health issue we are facing in this city and country.
And then there's the "good people" vs. "criminal class" dichotomy, alongside the "brave men and women of 51 Division" as written. This self-serving depiction of what life can be like for people experiencing extreme poverty and marginalization (a failure of our system, not the individuals) reveals a systemic ignorance of, and hostility toward, the communities the TPS is mandated to serve and protect.
And the police wonder why so many are calling for its radical restructuring.
Katryn Pereira, Toronto
Legislation needed to combat ticket pricing
U.S. seeks to break up ticketing monopoly, May 24
The U.S. Department of Justice wants to break up Live Nation and Ticketmaster, even though a number of years ago they supported their merger. As usual, this only affects the symptoms and ignores the cure for high event prices.
Many European countries have the solution. They have passed laws to regulate pricing and assure event tickets cannot be resold for more than face value. This eliminates bots buying up tickets as soon as they go on sale only to resell them moments later for several thousand dollars more. With these laws there is no incentive to buy tickets. Of course, the U.S. and Canada should pass similar laws, but that would mean that the legislators would have to have intestinal fortitude, which the current office-holders lack.
Jacob Psutka, Toronto
Transit
to a vacated science centre?
If they are moving the science centre to Ontario Place, why is the province and Metrolinx building the Ontario Line to the existing Science Centre at Eglinton Avenue and Don Mills? The province should butt out and return Toronto transit to the TTC.
Scott Fairweather, Toronto
Munro made
the ordinary extraordinary
Munro made the old seem new, Opinion, May 23
I read publisher Douglas Gibson's column about the late writer Alice Munro with great interest. Much has been written around the writer and her literary career. A writer of short-stories encapsulated the ordinariness of mainly rural folk, as myself, yet reveals the other side - us as world citizens. She wrote what we could not and I in particular wish to thank her so very much.
Murray McCullough,
Colborne, Ont.

Document 257 of 500

Doug Ford should put Big Tobacco out of business

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-ford-should-put-big-tobacco-out-business/docview/3060278985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+should+put+Big+Tobacco+out+of+business&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew did something that no other provincial leader has done.
He made public how the provincial lawsuits against tobacco companies will likely be settled. Manitoba would receive an initial payment of up to $500 million, he announced, and future payments are expected.
With Ontario's population more than 10 times that of Manitoba, Premier Doug Ford may similarly be anticipating an initial payment from tobacco companies of up to $5 billion and a commitment to future instalments.
Some might see this as good news, as tobacco companies would finally pay for some of the health-care costs resulting from their wrongful behaviour.
However, there's a dark side to a settlement with Big Tobacco requiring payments that go beyond the $12 billion the companies currently have available: It would need to rely on future sales of addictive and harmful products.
Combined, Ontario and the other provinces have claimed more than $500 billion from tobacco companies to compensate for the health-care costs caused by decades of wrongful marketing and public deception. This amount is more than enough to drive the Canadian branches of multinational tobacco companies into bankruptcy, forcing them to shut down.
Any future compensation payments can only be generated by keeping alive these same companies whose products currently kill 17,000 Ontarians each year and who have hooked generations of young people through vaping.
In addition, using future sales revenues to fund ongoing compensation to the provinces and other creditors means that consumers - not companies - will finance a global settlement between the provinces and the companies. It means that the purchases, health and well-being of smokers, vapers and other nicotine users will underwrite compensation for past wrongdoing.
The vicious cycle of addiction, disease and health-care costs will continue. Meanwhile, corporate directors and others whose wrongful behaviours are detailed in the provinces' claim will go unpunished.
No premier should accept a settlement proposal that legitimizes and sustains the harmful tobacco industry behaviour that is at the root of these lawsuits. No government should seek compensation for past wrongdoing that results in a continuation of harm.
Instead of extending the viability of the tobacco business and prolonging the smoking and addiction epidemic, Premier Doug Ford and his colleagues should work together to impose an orderly wind-down of the commercial tobacco market. They should seize this historic opportunity to make the companies stop recruiting new customers and start phasing out this harmful business.
If these leaders prefer a monetary settlement to one focused on protecting health, they are out of step with public opinion. A poll conducted for our organizations by Leger in the fall of 2023 found that 70 per cent of Canadians (and 80 per cent of those with an opinion) supported their "province using these lawsuits to require tobacco manufacturers to phase out the commercial sale of cigarettes in Canada." Only 17 per cent of respondents opposed this outcome.
Kinew deserves credit for breaking the silence that Ford and others have imposed over the past five years onto these negotiations as a way to deny public input or insight about the future of the tobacco industry in Canada.
Secret settlement discussions are fundamentally undemocratic. The decisions on whether this industry is allowed to escape bankruptcy and how it will be held accountable for decades of wrongdoing should not be made through a backroom deal. They are important public policy issues that deserve transparency, open discussion and public engagement.
Now is the time for Ford and his legislative colleagues to consult with Ontarians about the future they want for the tobacco industry.
Such a listening exercise would not contravene any court-ordered confidentiality on insolvency discussions. It would instead help those talks by informing those at the negotiating table about the priorities of those they represent.
Without such transparency and engagement, the upcoming settlement looks very much like a sugar-coated money grab.
Cynthia Callard represents Physicians for a Smoke-Free Canada, Flory Doucas, the Quebec Coalition for Tobacco Control and Les Hagen, Action on Smoking & Health.
CAPTION: Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew has done something that no other provincial leader has done in making public how the province's lawsuits against Big Tobacco will likely be settled.
CREDIT: Cynthia Callard, Flory Doucas and Les Hagen Contributors;

Document 258 of 500

Europe is set for hard right turn: Polls suggest coalition of far-right neo-fascists could hold balance of power after EU elections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/europe-is-set-hard-right-turn/docview/3060278969/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Europe+is+set+for+hard+right+turn%3A+Polls+suggest+coalition+of+far-right+neo-fascists+could+hold+balance+of+power+after+EU+elections&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: By all polling indications, Europe is about to be turned inside-out. The radically regressive are charging in from deep right field.
It's been dubbed the Great Leap Forward - for racist and reactionary forces. Not to be confused with the Great Replacement Theory, a racist fiction promulgated on this side of the Atlantic by conspiracists who claim that non-whites are being brought into the United States to replace white voters. But the narratives share a vehement anti-immigrant ideology.
Transmogrification of the continent is building steam leading up to next week's European Parliament elections, 720 seats decided from an electorate of some 400 million stretching across the EU's 27 member states. Those countries are allied in half-a-dozen competing blocs that have traditionally been dominated by the centre-right European People's Party (EPP) and the centre-left Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats (S&D).
But there's a new kid on the bloc block and it's quite hideous - Identity and Democracy (ID), a coalition of far-right neo-fascists that includes Marine Le Pen's National Rally in France, Germany's Alternative fur Deutschland (AfD), Italy's Lega and a clutch of like-minded nativists from Austria, Belgium, the Netherlands, Hungary, Poland and Estonia. Meanwhile, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni - she of the Brothers of Italy, with its post-fascist roots - sees herself as a kingmaker by uniting the outlier right.
A deal-making collaboration would prop up centre-right incumbent Ursula Von der Leyen, who needs a majority of 361 votes to retain the EU presidency and may have lost the critical support of French President Emmanuel Macron, according to a Bloomberg report. That kind of concordat could establish Meloni as a parallel power leveraging influence in Brussels to drag the EU farther right. (In a recent speech Meloni described Europe as "tired, submissive and lazy," which she blamed on the "crazy policies of the left.")
Meloni runs Italy in coalition with the far-right Lega and centre-right Forza. Her EU grouping, the European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR), is expected to boost its seats in the European Parliament from six to 22. "We want to create a majority that brings together the centre-right forces and sends the left into the opposition even in the EU," she said last month.
The European Council on Foreign Relations, a pan-European think tank, has projected that the ID and the ECR could win 183 seats, rendering it a powerful force.
All the polls indicate this will be the most right-tilting and populist-driven EU Parliament in history, featuring a host of Euro-skeptics. And they are not your parents' conservatives. Many are beyond the pale of even Donald Trump's idolizing Republicans.
What do they stand for? Fortress Europe. A hard-right dogma on crime and the environment, targeting a rollback of the EU's efforts to mitigate climate change, but mostly a doctrine that coalesces around the migrant crisis and religious minorities, sealing off the continent to asylum seekers, building border walls (real and figurative).
The right in Europe has made significant gains in recent years, most especially in 2024 national elections, playing on public fears about escalating violent crime and the diluting of cultural identity - always a profound factor on the continent - hanging the blame on immigrants and refugees.
That hard right has turned its devotional eyes particularly towards the Netherlands, where Muslim-bashing Geert Wilder's PVV party was the clear winner in November's national election. Wilder has said he won't claim the top job as prime minister. It's not yet decided who will be named PM. However, Wilder has made it clear that this individual must cleave to PVV policies that clash with the EU, as currently constructed. Specifically, Wilders is seeking exceptions to EU migration and environmental rules.
What's interesting, and alarming, is that it's not just an older and more conservative demographic that is leaning into right wing appeal. According to research by the University of Amsterdam, 32 per cent of voters opted for anti-establishment parties in Europe in 2021, up from 12 per cent in the early 1990s, and radical-right parties made up about half of that share. Their support has risen faster than any other group.
The right wing's ascent in Europe is benefitting hugely from young voters. They're not necessarily xenophobic but they are deeply angered by the cost of living, housing and health care crises that traditional parties haven't been able to solve. And yes, they're vulnerable to a hardcore alternative, particularly in countries that have extended the vote to 16-year-olds who are getting all of their information from TikTok.
A German study published this year found that 22 per cent of young people (14-29) would vote for the execrable AfD. This past week, an AfD politician said members of the SS - the elite black boot guard of the Nazi regime - weren't automatically "criminals." That even got up Le Pen's nose, as she announced that her National Rally would no longer sit with the AfD in the European Parliament.
So now we know where the fascist right draws the line - at the storm trooper Schutzstaffel.
CAPTION: Alice Weidel is the co-leader of the far-right Alternative for Germany, which has seen its support rise among young Germans.INA FASSBENDER AFP via Getty Images
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Young adults; Fascism; Voters; Parliaments; Prime ministers; International relations; Political parties

Document 259 of 500

Principal at Jewish girls school in North York says students, parents feeling scared after 'shocking' weekend shooting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/principal-at-jewish-girls-school-north-york-says/docview/3060427166/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Principal+at+Jewish+girls+school+in+North+York+says+students%2C+parents+feeling+scared+after+%27shocking%27+weekend+shooting&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rabbi Yaacov Vidal was observing Shabbat, the Jewish Day of Rest, when a knock came on his door in the early Saturday afternoon.
There had been an incident at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School where he's the principal, Vidal said he was told. Multiple shots were fired at the school in the early morning. No one was hurt but there was damage to the school property.
"It was pretty shocking," Vidal told the Star. "I think it's very sad that people target schools where children are innocent and just create this extra fear and chaos for no reason."
When the principal got to the private Jewish girls' school in North York, he said he saw a hole in one of the front windows and another on top of the window frame. Both have since been boarded up.
Police said it's too early in the investigation to determine if this was a hate crime, but that the Hate Crime Unit will be supporting the Gun and Gang Task Force in the investigation.
But for Vidal, it was quite clear.
"I definitely think that it is antisemitic. I think I would consider this a hate crime. I don't see any other reason why this would occur."
As the school prepares to have students back in class on Monday, Vidal said the school has reassured the parents and the students they'll do their best to increase their security.
They have also sent statements to parents to keep them updated and help them cope with the incident. Vidal said the incident "concerns us very much" and that students, parents and staff were feeling "a bit scared."
Police said multiple suspects arrived at the school just after 4:50 a.m. Saturday in a dark-coloured vehicle. Once there, they got out, fired multiple shots and then got back in the vehicle and fled.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk, with the Integrated Gun and Gang Task Force, told reporters on scene Saturday that the neighbourhood will see an increased police presence and that officers will be going door-to-door and business-to-business to see if anyone has information about the incident, or if they have any videos of the area.
Bais Chaya Mushka is an Orthodox Jewish girls' school located near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West which offers programs from junior kindergarten to Grade 8.
With a total of 225 students enrolled, the school caters to the Jewish Chabad community. Chabad is a major movement within mainstream Jewish tradition with its roots in the Chassidic movement of the 18th century, according to Chabad.org.
Just about two years ago, the school received funding from the federal government to increase "a tremendous amount of security," Vidal said.
The funding through the federal Security Infrastructure Program (SIP) made it possible for the Jewish school to put up a fence, replace their windows with armoured glass and add lighting outside the school, he said.
"That really served as a deterrent. When they (the suspects) came they couldn't get past the fence. So they had to stand on the other side of the fence. Never mind entering the school which we're very grateful for," the principal said.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau posted a statement on X Saturday night, calling the incident "despicable" and "a brazen act of antisemitism."
"Its perpetrators need to be brought to justice," Trudeau wrote.
Premier Doug Ford also posted a statement to X on Saturday evening, calling the shooting a "gross display of antisemitism."
"We'd never felt unsafe. Children are happy to come to school, and we have great neighbours. It's sad that in a country like Canada, we have to feel afraid to practice our religion and to open up schools. Just because we're Jewish," Vidal said.
With files from Abhiraj Lamba
CAPTION: Police marked bullet holes at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West on Saturday.Toronto police investigate at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West on Saturday.

Document 260 of 500

Police investigating after shots fired outside Jewish girls school: Too early to tell if incident was a hate crime, but officers will be patrolling neighbourhood

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/police-investigating-after-shots-fired-outside/docview/3060128573/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+investigating+after+shots+fired+outside+Jewish+girls+school%3A+Too+early+to+tell+if+incident+was+a+hate+crime%2C+but+officers+will+be+patrolling+neighbourhood&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police are investigating after shots were fired outside an elementary school for Jewish girls in North York early Saturday morning, police said.
According to police, multiple suspects arrived at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West just after 4:50 a.m. in a dark-coloured vehicle. Once there, they got out, fired multiple shots and then got back in the vehicle and fled.
While there are no reports of injuries, evidence of gunfire has been located and the front of the school was damaged.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk, with the Integrated Gun and Gang Task Force, told reporters on scene that the neighbourhood will see an increased police presence and that officers will be going door-to-door and business-to-business to see if anyone has information about the incident, or if they have any videos of the area.
Krawczyk said it's too early in the investigation to determine if this was a hate crime, but that the Hate Crime Unit will be supporting the Gun and Gang Task Force in the investigation.
Krawczyk added that there will also be officers at other schools and synagogues in the area to ensure "the public understands that community safety is No. 1 for us."
Krawczyk also added that while police will release a video of the incident "in due course," he urged members of the public who may have the video to not release it at this time. He said that police do want people to judge what information may or may not be relevant based on the video and to share everything they might know with the police.
Premier Doug Ford posted a statement to X on Saturday evening calling the shooting a "gross display of antisemitism."
"It's beyond belief that anyone could be this hateful," he wrote. "Every student deserves to feel safe at school. If you have any information, please contact Toronto police."
In a statement Saturday afternoon, Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Centre For Holocaust Studies (FSWC) said they were "horrified" by the incident.
"FSWC is horrified to learn that last night there were shots fired at a Jewish school for girls, Bais Chaya Mushka in North York," it said. "Police have informed FSWC that they are increasing patrols throughout Jewish neighbourhoods, in particular near schools and synagogues, and that they will be on-site at Bais Chaya Mushka this week to support staff and students."
The National Council of Canadian Muslims also posted a statement on X Saturday evening, saying they are "disgusted" by the shooting and that the "pathetic cowards" responsible "should be brought to justice and face the strongest of punishments."
Anyone with information has been asked to contact police at 416-808-2510.
With files from Emily Fagan
CAPTION: Toronto police investigate at Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West on Saturday.According to police, multiple suspects arrived at the school just after 4:50 a.m. on Saturday, got out and fired multiple shots before fleeing.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Abhiraj Lamba Toronto Star With files from Emily Fagan

Document 261 of 500

How an Indigenous MPP won the right to speak in his own voice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-indigenous-mpp-won-right-speak-his-own-voice/docview/3059926660/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+an+Indigenous+MPP+won+the+right+to+speak+in+his+own+voice&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Sol Mamakwa speaks, politicians listen.
A sudden hush falls over the legislative chamber that is eerily respectful. The heckling stops, the chatter ceases, the clacking of keyboards is suspended.
A silence so strong, in a place riven by rancour and resentment, that it has a special power. How can it be so, in a place so often boisterous and poisonous?
Mamakwa is a charismatic First Nations MPP representing a majority-Indigenous riding. When he rises, recognized by the Speaker, no one else speaks - for in that moment, all partisan rivalry is suddenly suspended.
And yet, for all his commanding rhetoric and political gravitas in the legislature, Mamakwa's voice has always been subdued, his countenance constrained. He could speak, yes, but the crown's legacy and the legislature's rules would permit him to speak only in English (or French, Canada's other official language).
Mamakwa could not say what he was thinking - in his head, in his heart, in his mother tongue, Anishinaabemowin. He might be empowered by his Indigenous voters, yet he could not give them voice - in their own voice.
Now, six years after winning his far-north riding of Kiiwetinoong, and winning over so much of the legislature, Mamakwa has finally prevailed. He will soon have his say, in his own way.
On Tuesday, his mother Kezia Mamakwa and Indigenous dignitaries will descend on the legislature from Northern Ontario to hear him speak to them - and all of us - in his first language for the first time.
"All languages matter, especially Indigenous languages that are on the brink of extinction," Mamakwa tells me.
"Language connects us to our history, language connects us to who we are, it connects us to the land where we came from. It is the language of our people."
His victory is a testament to the power of persuasion, political negotiation, and artful performance. How did it happen, after all these years?
The seeds were planted when Mamakwa was asked to speak at a public event inside the legislature earlier this year, alongside the powerful PC cabinet minister, Paul Calandra, who serves as government house leader. Calandra had the microphone first, bringing greetings from the government.
But when it was Mamakwa's turn to speak on behalf of the NDP, he made his move - speaking first in Anishinaabemowin. He then translated for himself, explaining that his mother had always taught him to speak his own language lest he lose it - as so many other Indigenous people already have.
Lingering and listening in the audience was Calandra, who couldn't believe that his language was forbidden. Bumping into the New Democrat in the legislative cafeteria the next morning, Calandra suggested it was surely a misunderstanding of the rules.
But it wasn't. In fact, Mamakwa been ruled out of order by the Speaker within a few seconds of trying to speak his language.
Calandra's staff quickly confirmed that Mamakwa was correct. Incredulous about the archaic rules, the cabinet minister told Mamakwa that together, they would right this wrong.
"I was dumbfounded," Calandra recalls. "Over breakfast, we figured it out."
He credits Mamakwa for forcing the rule change - the rare opposition MPP who wins respect and gets results.
"Honestly, had Sol not been there to say that, nobody else would have brought it up, frankly," Calandra told me. "It struck me that it should just be done."
Which is not often said by Tories to New Democrats (or vice-versa). But Mamakwa knew what he was doing by speaking out while Calandra was within earshot.
"That was me putting a bug in his ear when I spoke my language," he confesses. "I did that on purpose. Sometimes you says things that will make people think later."
Within a week - warp speed by legislative standards - changes to the rules had been circulated by the government and approved in a vote. Looking back, Calandra says Mamakwa has a gift for connecting with people and deploying humour to make a serious point.
I've written before about his fondness for social media - "As a First Nations person, humour is part of who I am - and his penchant for sly protests in the legislature. Mamakwa once poked fun at the solemn procession that marks the start of legislative sittings by smiling and hissing, "Colonizers!"
Mamakwa muses that he is rarely on the same page with Calandra, but "I think he understood that they cannot continue the old ways of the colonial institutions that did not allow First Nations people to speak."
He gives the sometimes cantankerous Calandra full marks for listening - and acting: "Even though it seems like a small change, it's a monumental change to me."
It's a change Mamakwa could not have fathomed when, as a boy, he attended a residential school in the far north. The Mennonite teachers prohibited his mother tongue and the punishment included hard manual labour, with students sent out in the bitter cold to cut wood without any gloves.
All these years later, Mamakwa is about to make his first official remarks in Anishinaabemowin and then pose a question to the government on behalf of the NDP. The topic will likely be long-term care for Indigenous people in his riding, because he wants to mark the event as both substantive and symbolic.
Language matters, Mamakwa says, turning sombre.
"It's going to be a serious question - it's not just for show."
CAPTION: MPP Sol Mamakwa speaks with media inside the Ontario legislature at Queens Park in Toronto on April 22, 2024.

Subject: Language; Legislatures; Native North Americans

Title: How an Indigenous MPP won the right to speak in his own voice

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 25, 2024

Section: politics

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-indigenous-mpp-won-right-speak-his-own-voice/docview/3059926660/se-2?accountid=15090

Copyright: Copyright 2024 Toronto Star Newspapers Limited. All Rights Reserved.
