Document 262 of 500

Doug Ford's hasn't learned from the Greenbelt scandal: Ontario's housing strategy still promotes sprawl and paving over farmland

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-hasnt-learned-greenbelt-scandal/docview/3059926647/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+hasn%27t+learned+from+the+Greenbelt+scandal%3A+Ontario%27s+housing+strategy+still+promotes+sprawl+and+paving+over+farmland&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is about to undo the protections we have for the province's watersheds, wilderness and farmland. They're also taking away the public's ability to speak out - claiming it's the only way to get affordable housing.
The government is wrong. The consequences of two new housing laws introduced by Premier Doug Ford will remove remaining guardrails to unbridled, costly, and unaffordable low-density sprawl with no guarantee that affordable homes will ever be built.
Ford's proposed laws run contrary to the advice from his own Housing Task Force as well as the recent Blueprint for More and Better Housing prepared by public and private sector experts.
Both of these reports call for new housing to be built within cities and communities - not in fields and forests - to save money, combat climate change, and facilitate rental housing in particular.
The proposals also put the province directly at odds with the federal government's recently announced plans to promote new housing within existing cities and communities. For the first time in decades, Ottawa has signaled clearly to Canadians that Ottawa is back in the housing business, committing $8.5 billion in new spending on dozens of programs.
These programs focus on building badly needed rental housing and a recommitment to affordable co-op and non-profit housing, including on publicly owned lands, which typically happens in existing towns and cities.
The province is headed in the opposite direction. Shortly before the federal housing plan was released, Ford's government introduced Bill 185, which it calls the Cutting Red Tape to Build more Homes Act, along with proposed changes to the Provincial Planning Statement, Ontario's land use master plan.
If passed, the proposed provincial laws and policies will give greenfield developers a free hand to open up farmland, natural areas and waterways to build expensive single family homes, in new subdivisions. This won't help the housing crisis. The vast majority of Ontarians cannot afford to buy single family homes in new subdivisions where you need a car to get anywhere.
The premier's stated preference to focus on single family units is at odds with the fact that Ontario's median household income is about $75,000 and that one- and two person households now comprise over half of all households. No surprise that eight out of 10 housing starts in 2023 were multi-residential units - that's what's needed.
The proposed provincial laws remove legal requirements that direct growth to existing communities rather than greenspace. They also remove any requirements for even minimum density on farmland approved for housing - so there's no guarantee that affordable units would be built. And they take away any municipal powers to stop urban expansion onto farmland.
At the same time, the public will lose its voice in the planning process. The proposed legislation removes the ability of any person, landowner, non-government organizations, or upper tier municipality to challenge an urban expansion at the Ontario Land tribunal (OLT).
It's true that pedantic legal challenges can sometimes slow up planning and housing, but rather than trying to streamline the process, Ford's proposals simply take away peoples' rights. And that's wrong.
Even after being stung by the Greenbelt scandals and while still tainted with the whiff of corruption, the Ford government is moving forward on the removal of Ontario's farmland and environmental protections with this proposed legislation. It's also needlessly stripping the rights of elected municipal councillors and ratepayers to participate in the future planning of their communities.
We call upon Queen's Park to join with Ottawa to ensure that we build the housing we need in the right places within our existing towns and cities. Shelve Bill 185 and the proposed changes to the Provincial Planning Statement. We need both levels of government rowing in the same direction to solve our housing crisis.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's housing strategy ignores calls for new housing "to be built within cities and communities - not in fields and forests -to save money, combat climate change, and facilitate rental housing in particular," write David Crombie and Anne Golden.
CREDIT: David Crombie and Anne Golden Contributors

Subject: Rentals; Affordable housing; Planning; Housing starts

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing Housing starts

Title: Doug Ford's hasn't learned from the Greenbelt scandal: Ontario's housing strategy still promotes sprawl and paving over farmland

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 25, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Housing strategy won't solve crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/housing-strategy-wont-solve-crisis/docview/3059815974/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Housing+strategy+won%27t+solve+crisis&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Shelve Bill 185 and the proposed changes to the Provincial Planning Statement. We need both levels of government rowing in the same direction to solve our housing crisis.
David Crombie is the former mayor of Toronto and
federal cabinet minister. Anne Golden is the former president of the Conference Board of Canada. Both are founders of the Alliance for a Liveable Ontario.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's housing strategy ignores calls for new housing to be built within cities and communities - not in fields and forests - to save money, combat climate change and facilitate rental housing in particular, write David Crombie and Anne Golden.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo

Document 264 of 500

MPP finally gets his say, in his own way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-finally-gets-his-say-own-way/docview/3059815864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+finally+gets+his+say%2C+in+his+own+way&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Sol Mamakwa speaks, politicians listen.
A sudden hush falls over the legislative chamber that is eerily respectful. The heckling stops, the chatter ceases, the clacking of keyboards is suspended.
A silence so strong, in a place riven by rancour and resentment, that it has a special power. How can it be so, in a place so often boisterous and poisonous?
Mamakwa is a charismatic First Nations MPP representing a majority-Indigenous riding. When he rises, recognized by the Speaker, no one else speaks - for in that moment, all partisan rivalry is suddenly suspended.
And yet, for all his commanding rhetoric and political gravitas in the legislature, Mamakwa's voice has always been subdued, his countenance constrained. He could speak, yes, but the Crown's legacy and the legislature's rules would permit him to speak only in English (or French, Canada's other official language).
Mamakwa could not say what he was thinking - in his head, in his heart, in his mother tongue, Anishinaabemowin. He might be empowered by his Indigenous voters, yet he could not give them voice - in their own voice.
Now, six years after winning his far-north riding of Kiiwetinoong, and winning over so much of the legislature, Mamakwa has finally prevailed. He will soon have his say, in his own way.
On Tuesday, his mother, Kezia Mamakwa, and Indigenous dignitaries will descend on the legislature from Northern Ontario to hear him speak to them - and all of us - in his first language for the first time.
"All languages matter, especially Indigenous languages that are on the brink of extinction," Mamakwa tells me.
"Language connects us to our history, language connects us to who we are, it connects us to the land where we came from. It is the language of our people."
His victory is a testament to the power of persuasion, political negotiation and artful performance. How did it happen, after all these years?
The seeds were planted when Mamakwa was asked to speak at a public event inside the legislature earlier this year, alongside the powerful PC cabinet minister Paul Calandra, who serves as government house leader. Calandra had the microphone first, bringing greetings from the government.
But when it was Mamakwa's turn to speak on behalf of the NDP, he made his move - speaking first in Anishinaabemowin. He then translated for himself, explaining that his mother had always taught him to speak his own language lest he lose it - as so many other Indigenous people already have.
Lingering and listening in the audience was Calandra, who couldn't believe that his language was forbidden. Bumping into the New Democrat in the legislative cafeteria the next morning, Calandra suggested it was surely a misunderstanding of the rules.
But it wasn't. In fact, Mamakwa been ruled out of order by the Speaker within a few seconds of trying to speak his language.
Calandra's staff quickly confirmed that Mamakwa was correct. Incredulous about the archaic rules, the cabinet minister told Mamakwa that together, they would right this wrong.
"I was dumbfounded," Calandra recalls. "Over breakfast, we figured it out."
He credits Mamakwa for forcing the rule change - the rare opposition MPP who wins respect and gets results.
"Honestly, had Sol not been there to say that, nobody else would have brought it up, frankly," Calandra told me. "It struck me that it should just be done."
Which is not often said by Tories to New Democrats (or vice-versa). But Mamakwa knew what he was doing by speaking out while Calandra was within earshot.
"That was me putting a bug in his ear when I spoke my language," he confesses. "I did that on purpose. Sometimes you says things that will make people think later."
Within a week - warp speed by legislative standards - changes to the rules had been circulated by the government and approved in a vote. Looking back, Calandra says Mamakwa has a gift for connecting with people and deploying humour to make a serious point.
I've written before about his fondness for social media - "As a First Nations person, humour is part of who I am" - and his penchant for sly protests in the legislature. Mamakwa once poked fun at the solemn procession that marks the start of legislative sittings by smiling and hissing, "Colonizers!"
Mamakwa muses that he is rarely on the same page with Calandra, but "I think he understood that they cannot continue the old ways of the colonial institutions that did not allow First Nations people to speak."
He gives the sometimes cantankerous Calandra full marks for listening - and acting: "Even though it seems like a small change, it's a monumental change to me."
It's a change Mamakwa could not have fathomed when, as a boy, he attended a residential school in the far north. The Mennonite teachers prohibited his mother tongue and the punishment included hard manual labour, with students sent out in the bitter cold to cut wood without any gloves.
All these years later, Mamakwa is about to make his first official remarks in Anishinaabemowin and then pose a question to the government on behalf of the NDP. The topic will likely be long-term care for Indigenous people in his riding, because he wants to mark the event as both substantive and symbolic.
Language matters, Mamakwa says, turning sombre.
"It's going to be a serious question - it's not just for show."
CAPTION: Indigenous NDP MPP Sol Mamakwa will officially speak in Anishinaa- bemowin in the legislature Monday after Queen's Park changed its language rules. His victory is a testament to the power of persuasion, political negotiation and artful performance, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Cole Burston The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Document 265 of 500

Pierre the politician, poet and prankster: New book sheds light on Conservative leader's personal journey and the ideas, ideals that drive him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pierre-politician-poet-prankster/docview/3059815158/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+the+politician%2C+poet+and+prankster%3A+New+book+sheds+light+on+Conservative+leader%27s+personal+journey+and+the+ideas%2C+ideals+that+drive+him&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "It's just Pierre being Pierre."
That's the answer many in Conservative circles give these days when they're quizzed about why party leader Pierre Poilievre does or says something that observers struggle to understand.
But over eight months and through more than 60 interviews, journalist Andrew Lawton sought to probe more into what that actually means: Who is Poilievre? What drives him?
"By explaining who Poilievre is, where he came from, and how he fits into the changing landscape of Canadian conservative politics, we get a sense of what the future may hold as his political life unfolds," Lawton writes in the introduction to "Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life," his newly released book obtained ahead of its publication by the Star.
Here are some key takeaways from the first book-length look at a man polls suggest could be well on the road to becoming Canada's next prime minister.
Why is he sometimes called Jeff?
That Poilievre is the adopted son of two schoolteachers is well known; he references it in his regular rally speeches and elsewhere all the time.
Lawton reveals that one of the reasons Poilievre is sometimes referred to as "Jeff" is because that's the name his birth mother would have given him, if she'd raised him on her own. His adoptive parents also adopted his biological half-brother Patrick. The brothers forged a relationship with their biological mother when they were in their 20s.
His adoptive parents separated when he was 12, and he remains close to his mother Marlene, as well as his father Don and Don's long-time partner Ross McWhirter. Lawton also shoots down a rumour that has circulated for years that Poilievre's father was sitting in the House of Commons when Poilievre voted against legalizing same-sex marriage and also for a broad reopening of the debate itself.
He eloped to Portugal
Poilievre grew up in Calgary and got involved in politics early - he was so involved that he ultimately dropped out of his bachelor's degree program at the University of Calgary in 2002 to take a job with then-foreign affairs critic Stockwell Day. He was three courses shy at the time, Lawton writes, and eventually took online courses through Athabasca University to finish off his degree in 2008.
His affection for his wife Anaida Poilievre - and the political force she is in her own right - also comes across at campaign rallies, but Lawton's book reveals the early days of their courtship.
She dodged him initially and was miffed he unilaterally set the terms for their very first date in 2012 - at a downtown Ottawa coffee shop Poilievre was able to have access to before it opened to the public. Their first date went so long Poilievre ended up with a parking ticket.
He told her he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017 to get married.
The duo have two children, and Lawton notes that their older daughter, Valentina, has been diagnosed with autism and is non-verbal. She receives therapy, though what kind and from who is not specified, but Lawton reports it has benefited her.
Known as a prankster
Lawton reports Poilievre likes playing pranks on his own staff and other colleagues, often using foreign accents or mimicking other politicians. He's posed as a foreign diplomat to call the office of then-Conservative Foreign Affairs Minister John Baird claiming Baird had promised something during a trip abroad and demanding he deliver.
Poilievre, Lawton writes, also once had a woman call his own office to pretend she was pregnant after a one-night stand with him and wanted to track him down to tell him about it.
Poilievre was in a relationship with longtime Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne at the time.
Byrne did speak to Lawton about their relationship for the book - the pair dated for 12 years - but declined to delve into it in detail. The relationship ended, she told Lawton, because they had simply grown apart.
Lawton also attempted to interview people who worked with Poilievre during his time as a junior cabinet minister, but many did not return his calls.
One unnamed source said Poilievre was "insufferable" as a minister, though has grown out of it, while others told Lawton that if people chafe under Poilievre's leadership, it means they simply aren't cut out to work on the Hill. He once told his staff "this is not a self-esteem factory," a phrase that was then turned into a poster and put on the wall of his office.
As party leader, Lawton reports "Poilievre and his team have run a tight ship, with high expectations for attendance and performance in question period. Non-essential absenteeism is forbidden," but Poilievre also ensures caucus has a chance to speak their mind behind closed doors so they have their say, Lawton writes.
He is often his own harshest critic; colleagues recount how he was "sullen and withdrawn" for some time after stepping on his government's 2008 apology for residential schools.
Where did he cut
his political teeth?
Lawton traces a long arc for Poilievre's political ideas and ideals all the way back to his childhood - his first political outing, Lawton says was a provincial Progressive Conservative riding association meeting he attended with his mother.
"He enjoyed debate already and sometimes his teachers didn't like that very much," his mother Marlene told Lawton. "Some teachers told me he sat on the edge of his desk and couldn't wait to debate."
As a teen, among his go-to books? The dictionary, Lawton writes. He also began reading about politics and economics.
He tried to join the board of a Calgary riding association, but was rebuffed because it was already full - so they signed him on as a fundraiser, and he would go on to impress with his ability to rake in cash.
He also, Lawton writes, showcased an interest in mastering procedural rules for debates at the Model United Nations, and teaching his team lessons about where to put their hands or how to use body language to make a point.
"Pierre hasn't changed," Lawton quotes one of his schoolmates - someone who works today on Parliament Hill though not for the Tories. "I think some people think this is a persona, but he is identical to the Pierre I remember from the speech and debate club, model Parliament, and Speakers' Corner."
He's written a book
As an adult, he also once worked on a book - ahead of the 2021 campaign, Poilievre had also been writing something tentatively called "Debtonation," which Lawton describes as a "a meticulously researched, chart-heavy manuscript" meant to explore Canada's current fiscal situation and what needed to happen to - in Poilievre's view - fix it.
Lawton's book includes an excerpt from the never-published manuscript that references the same themes Poilievre hammers on now - removing the gatekeepers and giving people back their freedom.
Lawton also writes about the tensions between Poilievre and the media, quoting Byrne as saying whatever people may think it's not strategy but just "Pierre being Pierre." But Lawton also quotes current House Leader and former Conservative leader Andrew Scheer as reflecting on why it should be a strategy: Scheer believes the media "concoct and contrive" narratives to hurt Conservatives and help Liberals.
"So at the very least, if we can show that bias to Canadians, then come campaign time, we'll have a fighting chance to get our message through."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre told his wife, Anaida, he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017.Jacques Boissinot The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star

Document 266 of 500

Booze coming to corner stores: Beer, wine will be available ahead of schedule thanks to $225M payout to privately run Beer Store

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/booze-coming-corner-stores/docview/3059814905/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Booze+coming+to+corner+stores%3A+Beer%2C+wine+will+be+available+ahead+of+schedule+thanks+to+%24225M+payout+to+privately+run+Beer+Store&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians could be drowning their sorrows during a potential snap provincial election in the wake of Premier Doug Ford's liberalized booze sales.
Ford says there will finally be beer and wine in corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween - after a $225-million payout to the privately run Beer Store.
"We are delivering on our commitment to give consumers in Ontario the choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys, and we're doing so even sooner than we had originally promised," he said Friday at an Etobicoke gas bar variety store.
The surprise move gives the premier the flexibility to call a snap election in 2025 - a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 vote - because he will have delivered a key campaign pledge.
Ford repeatedly refused to commit to a 2026 election and pointedly left open the possibility of an early call while his Progressive Conservatives still enjoy a double-digit poll lead over the opposition Liberals and New Democrats.
"I just want to get our agenda through," he coyly told reporters at the campaign-style announcement.
An election in the first half of next year would get the Tories in front of an expected October 2025 federal vote and give rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie even less time to gear up.
When Ford unveiled the plan to give additional choice for buyers of beer, wine, cider, hard seltzer and premixed cocktails on Dec. 14, he had conceded it might take until 2026 to implement due to long-standing agreements with the beer and wine industry.
But taxpayer-funded subsidies of $225 million to The Beer Store, which is majority-owned by breweries Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, over the next 19 months expedited the process.
As well, the beer companies could receive an additional $375 million because some fees they currently pay the LCBO will be rebated, potentially meaning a total cost to taxpayers of $600 million or more.
A defensive Ford insisted the cash is designed to "protect jobs" of Beer Store workers.
"It's not going into the big breweries' pockets or anything like that - it's going to the front-line people," he said.
But the move was denounced by a "disappointed" Retail Council of Canada president and CEO Diane Brisebois, who represents major grocers, as a "sweetheart deal for the big multinational beer companies" that won't help supermarkets.
Crombie said the announcement was "a blatant attempt by Doug Ford to buy the election at a massive cost to the people of Ontario" and said the Tories are scrambling to change the channel from the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being investigated by the RCMP.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles questioned Ford's priorities, noting that paying off the brewers comes as "emergency rooms are closing, millions of Ontarians are without a family doctor and our shelters are at capacity."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "people need the government to fast-track funding for housing affordability and health care instead of giving hundreds of millions of taxpayer dollars to The Beer Store to speed up a timeline they announced just five months ago."
The Beer Store will also continue to be paid to oversee recycling and bottle returns for all alcohol beverage containers until 2031, and the publicly owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) will maintain its monopoly on spirits sales such as whisky, vodka, gin and rum.
Private retailers, who can begin applying for licensing online on June 17, will buy their booze from the government monopoly. They will get an interim wholesale discount of 10 per cent off LCBO retail prices until 2026.
Under the new timetable, up to 450 supermarkets that were licenced to sell beer, wine and cider by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 will be allowed to sell premixed cocktails on Aug. 1.
As well, those grocery outlets, which are currently restricted to selling beer in six-packs, will be able to offer cases of 24 that only The Beer Store has been permitted to sell.
After Sept. 5, all eligible convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages. And after Oct. 31, eligible grocery and big-box stores - such as Walmart and Costco - will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and premixed booze.
Queen's Park will continue to impose minimum price restrictions to thwart any attempts at cut-price sales, although "private-label" products could be available in 2026.
When liberalization is completed, there will be an additional 8,500 booze retailers in Ontario.
There are about 2,935 outlets - including the LCBO's 685 locations plus 389 agency stores, 422 Beer Stores, 449 supermarkets, 526 wine retailers, 373 craft brewery shops and 75 distillery stores - licensed to sell booze in the province.
As well, about 19,000 bars and restaurants have been permitted sales of alcohol to go since 2020 - thanks to pandemic-era changes implemented by Ford's former treasurer Rod Phillips.
Last Christmas, Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced Queen's Park would not renew the 10-year "master framework agreement" with the beer and wine industry when it expires on Dec. 31, 2025.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, left, and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy raise a drink Friday after announcing the expansion of alcohol sales in convenience stores.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Document 267 of 500

A 'monumental' victory for 'lost Canadians' + Israeli official calls on Canada to be a better ally

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/monumental-victory-lost-canadians-israeli/docview/3059407620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+%27monumental%27+victory+for+%27lost+Canadians%27+%2B+Israeli+official+calls+on+Canada+to+be+a+better+ally&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, May 24 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a major victory for "lost Canadians," Israel calling on Canada to be a better ally and an "extraordinary" hurricane season on the horizon.
DON'T MISSCanada is moving to restore the citizenship rights of "lost Canadians"
Ottawa has introduced legislation restoring the citizenship rights of "lost Canadians" born abroad and ensuring what happened to them doesn't happen to others in the future, Nicholas Keung reports. "It will be the first time that the Citizenship Act is actually charter compliant," advocate Don Chapman said after the bill was tabled Thursday. "It's monumental. And it has huge ramifications." The legislation would automatically grant Canadian citizenship to people born abroad to a Canadian parent who was also born abroad - reversing the 2009 rule that spawned a generation of lost Canadians. The proposed changes come after lost Canadians and their families launched a successful constitutional challenge last year. Here's what you need to know.
What we know: As per Bill C-71, Canadians born abroad who have children outside Canada would need to spend at least 1,095 cumulative days in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child to pass on citizenship.Context: At the end of last year, a court ruled it unconstitutional for Canada to deny automatic citizenship to children born abroad just because their Canadian parents were also born abroad - what was known as the "second-generation cut-off" rule."It's not enough." Israeli official is calling on Canada to be a more steadfast ally
Israel's special adviser on Canada is calling for the nation to be a more steadfast ally. In an exclusive interview with the Star's Tonda MacCharles, Ronen Gilor pointed to several spots of tension between the two countries - including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's private pledge that there would be "no more deals" to export Canadian-made light armoured vehicles to Israel. Gilor also noted concerns that Trudeau had not yet visited Israel to show "solidarity," and that the nation had voted "against Israel" in the UN General Assembly. "Canada cannot say that they are seeking peace more than we do," he said. "We seek peace even more than Canada. Why? Because we have war." Take a closer look at what's happening.
Go deeper: Gilor said Canada should more regularly oppose and block pro-Palestinian motions condemning Israel at the UN, allow the export of military equipment and speak more strongly, for example, against the ICC seeking arrest warrants for Israel's prime minister and defence minister.On the front lines: The bodies of three more people taken hostage on Oct. 7 were recovered from Gaza, Israel's army said this morning, as the UN prepares to rule on whether Israel must halt its military operations and withdraw from the enclave.Meanwhile: In response to pro-Palestine protests, the University of Toronto's president said he will consider disclosure and divestment from Israel if students tear down the three-week-old encampment on the school's campus.An "extraordinary" hurricane season is being forecasted
Forecasters with the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration are warning of an "extraordinary" Atlantic hurricane season this year, featuring the greatest number of storms the agency has ever predicted in its spring outlook amid record-shattering ocean temperatures. Experts forecast between 17 and 25 named storms in the Atlantic this season, which starts on June 1, Kate Allen reports. About 35 per cent of these typically make it to Canadian waters, experts say, warning that Canadians should prepare early - especially as evidence suggests climate change is shifting hurricanes further north, on top of intensifying severe storms. Here's how Canada could be impacted.
Wait, what? A major factor contributing to the forecast is the record-setting ocean heat that has persisted for over a year now, with the Atlantic running a full one to two degrees Celsius warmer than historic norms. Everything from shipping routes to oceanic wildlife may be impacted.On the home front: A tornado warning was briefly issued for parts of Ontario this week, leaving many confused when no one actually experienced a tornado. Experts explain the alert only signals when a nearby storm is capable of producing the phenomenon, but that it's good to be alert.WHAT ELSE
Pierre Poilievre says he'll "axe the carbon tax" for consumers - but what about carbon pricing for industries?Liberals were hammered over their auto theft action plan during a meeting on public safety and national security.Pickering's mayor has asked the Ford government to revoke a MZO to build housing near airport lands, but some are on the Greenbelt.A "nice gesture but inappropriate." Critics are dismissing the RCMP's addition of Indigenous ribbon skirts to their uniform.Ottawa will let gun shop owners use couriers for the firearm buyback program.Bonnie Crombie has asked the RCMP for an update on their Greenbelt investigation.Outdated rules and mounting losses: Can anything be done to fix Canada Post?Work on the Ontario Line may force Pape Avenue Junior Public School to relocate.Patricia Jaggernauth's case against Bell will move forward after the Human Rights Commission found grounds to support her claim of discrimination.Toronto is approving more housing on "major streets" - including these three roads - despite a local councillor wanting them exempt.The birth of Toronto's WNBA team is a Vince Carter moment for the next basketball generation.One NBA club voted against Toronto getting a WNBA expansion team. Guess who?POV
Liberals like to portray Pierre Poilievre as scary, but a lot of Canadians simply aren't frightened.
CLOSE-UP

Document 268 of 500

Concert venues present challenges for seniors, plus the need for community health centres

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/concert-venues-present-challenges-seniors-plus/docview/3059407294/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Concert+venues+present+challenges+for+seniors%2C+plus+the+need+for+community+health+centres&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Venues make it hard for seniors
Neil Young and Crazy Horse deliver the rowdy goods at three-star Budweiser Stage concert, May 21
Yes, Neil Young and Crazy Horse sure did deliver the goods at their recent concert, but Live Nation Entertainment did not. While making the hour-long trip with our $200+ lawn tickets in hand, my buddy and I reached the Exhibition parking lot only to find out to pay for parking you had to download a parking app with a credit card attached, no debit or cash allowed. Nonpayment results in a $75 ticket. Next, getting into the venue, tickets can only be on your phone, no more printed copies allowed. Now, just remember for a moment who we are going to see here - a senior - with a bunch of seniors in the audience, not all of whom are so tech savvy. In our case, we got in no problem but you can't take a bag in and have to check in portable seats. Theirs are available, at a cost. No thanks. After all of this, I needed a drink. It was $17 a shot or $15 a beer. My buddy who doesn't drink found water was $6 a bottle. No thanks. They do sell plastic cups for the free water, very generous. On our way home after a really good concert, we weren't talking about the music. We talked about the gouging. Lesson learned.
Roy Wilkie, Richmond Hill
We need community health centres
Canada has a primary health-care crisis. Here's how to fix it: Philpott, May 23
I was excited to read the opinion piece written by Jane Philpott. I have worked in community health and recognize the harms that can happen when individuals are unable to access primary care, but as I read the piece I was disappointed to see that Philpott seemed unaware that the answer to the problem exists already. We just need to expand it. That answer is community health centres. I have had the privilege to work at one, and it has existed for more than 50 years. The centre responds to the specific need of this community. Staff include family doctors, nurse practitioners, nurses, chiropodists, physiotherapists, social workers and community workers. We provide programs focused on new mothers and their children, teenagers getting through high school and for newcomers. We also serve clients who face many barriers to service, including language barriers, substance use, homelessness and mental health issues. The clients we serve benefit from this model. They are able to receive a wrap around model of care that benefits both themselves, the community and society as a whole. We have the model and solution to the primary health-care crisis. Let's expand it to all communities.
Fiona Husband, Toronto
Look beyond 'snow-washing'
Why a hearing into Canada's 'snow-washing' problems must look beyond TD: Olive, May 23
Once again columnist David Olive nails a key financial issue in his column on the Canadian banking sector's inadequate anti-money laundering practices - $130 billion annually is more than three times the current deficit. Surely it is worth the investment in money and resources to attack this problem with full force. As I mentioned in a previous letter, outside auditors, internal auditors and the boards must be told to focus their efforts directly on this problem and produce regular reports for shareholders and the financial community on the efficacy of banking controls. When controls are not sufficient heavy fines must be levied. Million dollar fines are nothing to these financial behemoths. Laws with no teeth and paltry penalties are worthless.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Another example of harm to Indigenous people
The mercury poisoning Grassy Narrows First Nation was supposed to go away over time. A new study reveals why it's worse than it should be, May 23
A new report tells us that sulphate-infused industrial wastewater discharged by the Dryden mill into the Wabigoon River is causing a new form of mercury contamination in the waterway. On top of this, Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government is proceeding with mining and logging plans in the Grassy Narrows area without the permission of the Grassy Narrows First Nations and the Wabaseemoong Independent Nations. These Indigenous people have still not been properly compensated for the mercury poisoning of their river in the 1960s. Meanwhile, the W'etsuw'eten First Nation on Canada's West coast are struggling day and night, facing abusive behaviour by the RCMP, as they try to stop the Coastal GasLink pipeline from impacting their land and water. The Star recently published an article on the horrible state of housing in "deliberately underfunded" First Nations. There are many horrific examples of the way our governments treat our First Nations people.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
Rethinking the wildfire fight
As wildfires rage, some wonder if Justin Trudeau's government is doing enough, May 20
I fully agree that Canada must rethink its effort in terms of the growing wildfire threat. In the recent budget, the government set billions of dollars aside for the armed forces, yet climate change is the war Canada is fighting right now, with worsening wildfires across our land. It is time to invest in a national wildfire services. I strongly recommend the federal government purchase aircraft and convert them to carry water to wildfire zones. Unless we make significant changes to fighting fires, more of Canada's rich forest will be destroyed. It is the right time for the federal government to step up and fight the wildfire threat to our nation.
George Caron, Glenburnie, Ont.
Vacant tax is a hardship on all homeowners
Toronto and Hamilton have ex-NDPers as mayors and unsurprisingly support taxation for vacant homes. Vacant home tax is backwards in a free enterprise society with a civic government. This tax is like placing blame or punishment on citizens who can travel for long periods or who may have a home on probate, waiting for a sale. And it hasn't been easy. The rollout in Toronto was complete turmoil. This is a mistake made by politicians with agendas against the so-called rich. I'm glad we moved to the west for a house and acreage with better increasing equity, not to mention less crime.
Wayne Robertson, Chatham, Ont.
CAPTION: Neil Young, far right, and Crazy Horse - Micah Nelson, Billy Talbot and Ralph Molina - shown here in New Orleans on May 4, brought their loose, ragged glory to Budweiser Stage on May 20.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Community; Health facilities; Poisoning; Native North Americans; Contamination; Internal auditors

Business indexing term: Subject: Internal auditors

Document 269 of 500

Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-beer-wine-are-coming-ontario/docview/3059407213/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+beer+and+wine+are+coming+to+Ontario+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontarians will drink to that.
There will finally be beer and wine in corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween - thanks to a $225-million payout to the privately run Beer Store.
"We are delivering on our commitment to give consumers in Ontario the choice and convenience every other Canadian enjoys, and we're doing so even sooner than we had originally promised," Premier Doug Ford said Friday at an Etobicoke convenience store.
The surprise move would give the premier the flexibility to call a snap provincial election in 2025 - a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 vote - because he will have kept a key campaign pledge.
When Ford unveiled the plan to give additional choice for buyers of beer, wine, cider, hard seltzer and premixed cocktails on Dec. 14, he conceded it might take until 2026 to implement due to long-standing agreements with the beer and wine industry.
But taxpayer-funded subsidies of up to $225 million to The Beer Store, which is majority-owned by breweries Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, over the next 19 months expedited the process.
That cash is designed to "protect jobs" and maintain the foreign-owned company's retail "footprint" as liberalization unfolds.
The move was denounced by a "disappointed" Retail Council of Canada president and CEO Diane Brisebois, who represents major grocers, as a "sweetheart deal for the big multinational beer companies" that won't help supermarkets.
The Beer Store will continue to be paid to oversee recycling and bottle returns for all alcohol beverage containers until 2031, and the publicly owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) will maintain its monopoly on spirits sales such as whisky, vodka, gin and rum.
Private retailers, who can begin applying for licensing online on June 17, will buy their booze from the government monopoly. They will get an interim wholesale discount of 10 per cent off LCBO retail prices until 2026.
Under the new timetable, up to 450 supermarkets that were licenced to sell beer, wine and cider by former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne in 2015 will be allowed to sell premixed cocktails - like NÜTRL, Aperol Spritz, White Claw and Dillon's Small Batch - on Aug. 1.
As well, those grocery outlets, which are currently restricted to selling beer in six-packs, will be able to offer cases of 24 that only The Beer Store has been permitted to sell.
After Sept. 5, all eligible convenience stores will be allowed to sell beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink alcoholic beverages.
And after Oct. 31, eligible grocery and big-box stores - such as Walmart and Costco - will be able to sell beer, cider, wine and premixed booze.
The government will continue to impose minimum price restrictions to thwart any attempts at cut-price sales, although "private-label" products could be available in 2026.
The province's Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO) will police the liberalized system.
"In the coming weeks and months, people in Ontario, like many Canadians across the country, will have the option to responsibly and conveniently purchase a case of beer or a bottle of wine on their way up to the cottage or to a summer barbecue, all while having even more opportunity to support local Ontario breweries and wineries," the premier said.
Ford - a teetotaller whose government last week announced stiffer penalties for those who drink and drive and has previously pledged $10 million over five years to promote social responsibility - emphasized it was time Ontarians were treated like grown-ups.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy, who counts on the LCBO to generate around $2 billion a year in profits to provincial coffers, echoed that.
"Our responsible and balanced approach treats Ontario consumers like adults by giving them more choice and convenience, while also supporting Ontario retailers, domestic producers and workers in the alcohol industry," said Bethlenfalvy.
"As we launch this new marketplace, we will continue to meet, consult and work closely with industry partners, local beverage alcohol producers and other stakeholders."
When liberalization is finally completed, there will be an additional 8,500 booze retailers in Ontario.
Currently, there are about 2,935 outlets - including the LCBO's 685 locations plus 389 agency stores, 422 Beer Stores, 449 supermarkets, 526 wine retailers, 373 craft brewery shops and 75 distillery stores - licensed to sell booze in the province.
As well, about 19,000 bars and restaurants have been permitted sales of alcohol to go since 2020 - thanks to pandemic-era changes implemented by Ford's former treasurer Rod Phillips.
Phillips's move, which was intended to help small businesses cope with COVID-19 restrictions that prevented them from operating normally, has led to the opening of hundreds of new boutique bottle shops.
Last Christmas, Ford and Bethlenfalvy announced Queen's Park would not renew the 10-year "master framework agreement" with the beer and wine industry when it expires on Dec. 31, 2025.
The winding down that accord, signed by Wynne, means the Progressive Conservatives can finally keep their 2018 campaign pledge of "beer in corner stores" in time for the next election.
After a series of stories by the Star's Martin Regg Cohn on The Beer Store's private monopoly, Wynne inked the 2015 agreement with the brewing giants and vintners in order to allow sales of beer and wine in up to 450 supermarkets across Ontario.
In a miscalculation that generated international headlines, the newly elected Ford unilaterally tried to scuttle her deal with legislation in 2019.
But the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, strongly warned of the grave consequences of scrapping such a contract and outlawing compensation payments for the multinational companies involved.
With Ontario at risk of being accused of being anti-business, Ford's Tories retreated and agreed to let the master framework agreement run its course.
It is unclear how much demand exists for additional points of sale - some supermarkets that are already licensed and equipped with trained staff to sell booze, are abandoning the practice due to shoplifting concerns and thin profit margins.
CAPTION: Beer and wine will be sold in corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween, Premier Doug Ford announced Friday.

Subject: Agreements; Retail stores; Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores; Liberalization; Supermarkets; Grocery stores; Breweries

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores Sales Convenience stores Liberalization Supermarkets Grocery stores Breweries

Title: Doug Ford says beer and wine are coming to Ontario corner stores

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 270 of 500

Liberals accused of being soft on crime

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-accused-being-soft-on-crime/docview/3059209480/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+accused+of+being+soft+on+crime&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Auto theft remains a front burner issue in Ottawa, days after the federal government unveiled a new action plan to tackle the crisis.
Conservatives attempted to portray the Liberals as soft-on-crime on Thursday before the standing committee on Public Safety and National Security.
"Are they concerned about the victims or are they concerned about the criminals?" Arnold Viersen (Cons. Peace River-Westlock) asked the committee. Viersen highlighted what he called soft requirements for bail, saying that criminals benefit from a "revolving door," in the court system.
"Many of the crimes are committed by criminals who are out on bail," Viersen said.
Viersen said that current laws make it tough to get theft convictions, even when criminals are seemingly caught red-handed.
"You have to prove that the individual stole the vehicle," Viersen said. "Being in possession of the car that was stolen does not prove the fact that you stole the car."
Vehicle theft has shot up 34.1 per cent nationally between 2015 and 2022, Conservative Frank Caputo (Kamloops-Thompson-Cariboo) told the standing committee.
Statistics are far worse in some centres, including a 122.5 per cent rise in vehicle theft in Ontario between 2015 and 2022, Caputo said.
Things have gotten so bad that drivers are sometimes advised to keep their keys in plain view so that there won't be violence when auto thieves break into their homes, Caputo said.
"It's almost like a harm reduction," Caputo said.
This is apparently in reference to comments made by a Toronto police officer at a community town hall, who told Etobicoke residents they should try leaving their car FOBs at the front door to make it easier to find by thieves who are breaking into homes looking for car keys.
There was a 120 per cent increase in vehicle theft in New Brunswick, with a 190 per cent rise in Moncton, Caputo said.
In Montreal, auto theft has shot up 105.9 per cent between 2015 and 2022. The next set of statistics may even be worse when they're compiled, Caputo said.
His comments come days after the federal government unveiled a national plan to combat auto theft, including changes to the criminal code.
On Monday, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland unveiled the new strategy in Brampton, Ont., calling auto theft a complex and growing international concern.
The newly announced federal measures include more intelligence sharing among police forces and a working group involving all levels of government, Freeland said, flanked by senior cabinet ministers, including Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc.
The comments come as authorities estimate a vehicle is stolen roughly every six minutes in Canada, with 12,000 stolen annually in the GTA.
Experts estimate auto theft funnels more than $1-billion yearly towards various established and emerging organized crime groups.
The new federal strategy includes providing $28 million meant to help prevent cars from being shipped abroad through expanded searches of shipping containers at seaports. Many vehicles stolen in Canada end up resold abroad, including in West Africa, experts say.
Proposed amendments to the Criminal Code also include the addition of new offences which target the possession and distribution of tools used to steal vehicles.
The new federal strategy also includes integrating the RCMP's stolen vehicle database with one kept by Interpol.
With files from
the Canadian Press
CREDIT: Peter Edwards Toronto Star With files from the Canadian Press

Subject: Committees; Theft; Crime; Criminal statistics; Public safety; Vehicles

Location: Canada

Title: Liberals accused of being soft on crime

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication date: May 24, 2024

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

As wildfires rage, some wonder if Justin Trudeau's government is doing enough, May 20
I fully agree that Canada must rethink its effort in terms of the growing wildfire threat. In the recent budget, the government set billions of dollars aside for the Armed Forces, yet climate change is the war Canada is fighting right now, with worsening wildfires across our land. It is time to invest in a national wildfire services. I strongly recommend the federal government purchase aircraft and convert them to carry water to wildfire zones. Unless we make significant changes to fighting fires, more of Canada's rich forest will be destroyed. It is the right time for the federal government to step up and fight the wildfire threat to our nation.
George Caron, Glenburnie, Ont.
Vacant home tax is a hardship on homeowners
Toronto and Hamilton have ex-NDPers as mayors and unsurprisingly support taxation for vacant homes. Vacant home tax is backwards in a free enterprise society with a civic government. This tax is like placing blame or punishment on citizens who can travel for long periods or who may have a home on probate, waiting for a sale. And it hasn't been easy. The rollout in Toronto was complete turmoil. This is a mistake made by politicians with agendas against the so-called rich. I'm glad we moved to the West for a house and acreage with better increasing equity, not to mention less crime.
Wayne Robertson,
Chatham, Ont.

Document 272 of 500

Law to restore rights to citizens born abroad: Feds move to help 'lost Canadians' after court ruling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/law-restore-rights-citizens-born-abroad/docview/3059208580/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Law+to+restore+rights+to+citizens+born+abroad%3A+Feds+move+to+help+%27lost+Canadians%27+after+court+ruling&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following a court order, the federal government has introduced legislation to restore the citizenship rights of "lost Canadians" born outside Canada and ensure that what happened to them doesn't happen to others in the future.
This legislation would automatically confer Canadian citizenship on people born abroad to a Canadian parent who was also born abroad before the changes are enacted. Anyone born outside the country subsequently would need to prove their foreign-born Canadian parent had a "substantial connection" with Canada by meeting a residency requirement.
"It will be the first time that the Citizenship Act is actually Charter-compliant," said Don Chapman, a staunch advocate for lost Canadians, after Bill C-71 was tabled in Parliament on Thursday. "It's monumental. And it has huge ramifications."
In 2009, the Conservative government abolished the "substantial connection" regime and adopted a blanket rule that denies the first generation born abroad the right to pass on citizenship by descent outside Canada to the second generation born abroad. The result created a generation of "lost Canadians."
"We want our citizenship to be fair, accessible, with clear and transparent rules," Immigration Minister Marc Miller told reporters. "Not everyone is entitled to it, but for those who are, it needs to be fair.
"We wanted to take this opportunity to continue to minimize differential outcomes as much as possible for children born abroad ... compared to children born to Canadians" in Canada.
According to the proposed amendment, children born abroad to Canadians since 2009 would automatically be granted citizenship, and a new substantial connection test would be created for those born after the new law comes into effect.
Once it passes, Canadians born abroad who have or adopt children also born outside Canada would need to have spent at least 1,095 cumulative days of physical presence in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child in order to pass on citizenship.
Last year, lost Canadians and their families launched a constitutional challenge in court of the two-generation citizenship cut-off.
In December, the Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled that it's unconstitutional for Canada to deny automatic citizenship to children born abroad because their parents also happened to be born abroad. It gave the federal government six months, until June 19, to repeal the second-generation cut-off rule and amend the Citizenship Act.
With the looming court-stipulated deadline to roll out the new scheme, Miller said the government is unlikely to receive royal assent in time and will have to go before the judge to ask for an extension, which could cause further delays for affected children and grandchildren of Canadians to acquire citizenship and join families in Canada.
"We are still looking at a number of options," Miller said. "We don't want an extension ad nauseam because there are people that are being prejudiced by this."
The Harper government changed the citizenship law and imposed the second-generation cut-off on Canadians born abroad in response to its massive effort to evacuate 15,000 Lebanese Canadians stranded in Lebanon during Israel's month-long war in that country in 2006.
The $85-million price tag of the evacuation effort sparked a debate over "Canadians of convenience" - referring to individuals with Canadian citizenship who live permanently outside of Canada without "substantive ties" to Canada, but who were nonetheless part of the government's liability.
"People travel. People study abroad. People work abroad. People fall in love abroad. People have families abroad," said NDP immigration critic Jenny Kwan, who joined Miller at the media scrum.
"But guess what? As a result of this law, it meant that for some of those families, they have had to be separated from their children. Some children have been rendered stateless."
Kwan also sniped at the Opposition Conservatives for filibustering a recent bill by the Senate that called for similar changes to extend citizenship by descent to children born abroad to Canadians.
"We have to fix this once and for all," she said. "I'm standing here not for partisan reasons. I'm standing here because this is the right thing to do - not only a moral imperative to do this, but also a legal imperative."
Canadians who adopt overseas have two ways to acquire citizenship for their internationally born adoptees: apply for a direct citizenship grant from abroad so the child arrives here as a citizen; or sponsor the child to Canada as a permanent resident and apply for naturalization after meeting the 1,095-day residency requirement in the country.
Under the current law, adoptees who obtain citizenship through direct grant cannot pass on citizenship to a child born or adopted abroad except through a sponsorship.
The proposed change would allow this group's foreign-born descendants a citizenship pathway if the connection requirement were met. (They would still be treated differently from the other adoptees, who would be considered first-generation Canadians.)
"So the government says to us, 'It's OK because all you have to do is to qualify for the 1,095 days. And if they can pass the test, then it doesn't matter,'" said Kat Lanteigne of Toronto, who with her husband adopted their son, Nathanael, from Zambia in 2017 under direct citizenship grant.
"We say, 'You have to stop testing adopted children ... They're treating children who receive a direct grant as second-generation and second-class citizens, and we will absolutely not accept it."
In a statement, Conservative immigration critic Tom Kmiec blamed the Liberals for the "broken" immigration system and allowing "fraud, chaos and delays to run rampant."
"Contrary to his incompetent immigration minister's disingenuous claims, it is Justin Trudeau's government that has been found guilty of infringing on the rights of Canadians and invoking laws unconstitutionally," said the statement.
"Justin Trudeau and his NDP coalition partners control the majority of seats in Parliament and have the power to block or pass what they want."
CREDIT: Nicholas Keung Toronto Star

Document 273 of 500

Poilievre silent on 'axing the tax' for industries: Tory leader's stance a question mark in his campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-silent-on-axing-tax-industries/docview/3059208344/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+silent+on+%27axing+the+tax%27+for+industries%3A+Tory+leader%27s+stance+a+question+mark+in+his+campaign&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He would repeal incoming federal electricity regulations. He would scrap Liberal clean fuel standards. And, yes, he wants to "axe the tax." But it's less clear where Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stands on another major piece of federal climate policy: the national requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
It's a significant question mark in his campaign against the federal levy on consumer fuel, which Poilievre denounces as a tax that unfairly imposes costs on Canadians. That levy, formally called a "fuel charge" on things like gasoline, is just one piece of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon pricing regime. The other part is the legislated need for all provinces and territories to have an industrial pricing system for sectors like oil and gas, cement and steel production, or adopt one that was made in Ottawa.
The murkiness has some industry players, lobbyists and climate policy advocates trying to decipher what the Conservative leader thinks, with some urging him to come clean so businesses don't delay spending that will be crucial to meeting Canada's emissions reduction commitments.
Numerous political and business insiders say Poilievre's office is devoting time and resources to thinking through their environmental policy, and despite his ongoing criticisms of the lobbying sector, remain open to hearing from industry leaders about how existing programs and policies help or hurt their sector's own environmental ambitions.
But few expect clarity on industrial pricing program anytime soon, arguing that if industry wants certainty their questions should be directed not to Ottawa, but to provinces like Alberta and Ontario that have their own industrial pricing systems meeting federal requirements.
Poilievre has not held a press conference on Parliament Hill in more than a month. On Thursday, his office provided a statement to the Star that lambasted Trudeau's climate policies but didn't say whether a future Conservative government would keep, change or ditch the current rules for industrial pricing.
The head-scratching only got more vigorous after the new head of the Pathways Alliance, an oil industry lobby group devoted to reducing the sector's Canada-leading greenhouse gas pollution, told the CBC that "it would be very helpful for him to provide greater clarity" on industrial pricing.
The group declined an interview request from the Star this week.
The Liberal government quickly took note of the demand. Last weekend, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault posted on social media: "When will Pierre Poilievre come clean to Canadians about his plan to help his big polluter friends off the hook?"
Guilbeault also told a parliamentary committee on Wednesday that industrial pricing helped spur projects like Dow Chemical's $11.5-billion upgrade to make an Edmonton-area facility produce no emissions, and suggested such investments could be in jeopardy if these pricing systems were dismantled.
But some say uncertainty around future climate policies - including what the Conservatives would do with industrial pricing - is also dragging potential investments to reduce emissions from heavy industry. Catherine Cobden, the president and chief executive officer of the Canadian Steel Producers Association, said "big questions" are hanging about the future of the industrial pricing system is "putting a chill" on private spending to slash emissions that cause climate change.
She also cited questions around Liberal government policies, including over supports for heavy industries as the minimum carbon price is scheduled to keep increasing every year, from the current $85 per tonne to $170 per tonne in 2030.
"From the government, we are still seeking clarification regarding the future carbon pricing regime and associated supports for our industry," Cobden said in a statement to the Star. "From the opposition parties, we are looking for more detail on their climate policies should they form a future government."
Other industry groups, like the Chemical Industry Association of Canada (CIAC), say they want industrial pricing to stay in place, but also want to see additional support like the promised tax credits for green investments that the government says will be worth more than $90 billion over the next 11 years.
"Our message is to continue with carbon pricing," said CIAC president Bob Masterson. Like Guilbeault, he pointed to investments like the Dow Chemical project near Edmonton as evidence the industrial systems can help spur green spending.
But he said more support will be needed if the price keeps going up as the Liberals have planned, since a heavier price could risk them losing business to competitors in places without carbon pricing. "You have to be concerned about the outlook of companies," he said.
For Michael Bernstein, executive director of the pro-carbon pricing think tank Clean Prosperity, significant emissions reductions from heavy industries are critical for Canada to hit its 2030 target. The country has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by that year, while the most recent government data shows Canada was 7.1 per cent below 2005 levels in 2022.
With an unprecedented amount of reductions needed in a span of less than a decade, Bernstein said clarity from Poilievre on industrial pricing would be welcome, since there are questions about whether the system will remain in place, whether the minimum price under federal law would be changed, and what provinces would do if the federal requirements were removed or weakened.
One recent report, from the Canadian Climate Institute, estimated that industrial pricing could be responsible for a big chunk - between 20 to 48 per cent - of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030.
Poilievre has previously expressed support for Alberta's version of industrial carbon pricing, and that the Conservative leader told reporters last year that he is not proposing to withdraw Canada from the international Paris Agreement.
But if Poilievre kept Canada's current emissions targets and scrapped industrial pricing, that would raise questions about how provinces would respond, and how the country would turn to alternative policies - like government regulations or taxpayer subsidies - to reduce industrial emissions.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall and Stephanie Levitz Toronto Star

Document 274 of 500

The birth of Toronto's WNBA team is a Vince Carter moment for the next basketball generation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/birth-torontos-wnba-team-is-vince-carter-moment/docview/3059207724/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+birth+of+Toronto%27s+WNBA+team+is+a+Vince+Carter+moment+for+the+next+basketball+generation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What once could be only imagined is now tangible: The WNBA has arrived in Toronto, and in Canada.
And those upon which the foundation of Canadian women's basketball was built know how significant the country's first WNBA franchise truly is.
"It's so important," 12-year WNBA veteran Tammy Sutton-Brown said after Toronto's expansion team was officially announced Thursday. "The value of 'you can see it, you can be it' and the value of meeting your favourite players up close and personal, there's something really special about that.
"I just got chills. Who would have thought? Dreams really do come true."
It was - as Denise Dignard, Canada Basketball's executive vice-president for women's high performance, called it - the "Carter effect" moment for young Canadian women just like Vince Carter's arrival inspired a generation of Canadian stars in the NBA.
"This is just a long time coming," said Stacey Dales, a 2000 Canadian Olympian who hosted Thursday's event.
"The hyperbole is real. It is a monumental moment for somebody who grew up in Brockville, Ont., population about 27,000 when I was a kid, to never seeing basketball on TV, to seeing 27 years later now a league that's evolved into (the) WNBA (in) Toronto. It has been incredible."
If there was one overriding sentiment at what was a celebration of the game Thursday, it was that the moment for women's sports here has truly arrived. "Many say women's sports is having a moment, I disagree," franchise owner Larry Tanenbaum said. "Women's sports has arrived. It's a movement.
"The world is finally taking notice of something that's been there all along: immense talent, passion and competition. And that's why we're here today at the right time ... for our first WNBA team in Canada."
The Canadian aspect is not just talk. While the team will play primarily at the Coca-Cola Coliseum when it debuts in May 2026, there are plans to play regular-season games in Vancouver and Montreal.
"It was Larry's idea, let's maybe make this more like 'Canada's team, let's play games in Montreal and Vancouver,' " WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert said. "We haven't even set the number of games we'll be playing in 2026, because that will be the first year of the new collective bargaining agreement, so as we get into those negotiations, we'll see how many games we do play outside of the city of Toronto."
The job of building a franchise from scratch now lies with team president Teresa Resch, who joined Tanenbaum's Kilmer Sports Ventures organization after more than a decade with Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment. She was the driving force behind the construction of the Raptors' OVO training centre, oversaw the team's COVID year in Tampa and was involved in establishing the G League's Raptors 905. But an entire entity is a whole bigger task.
"Honestly, this is unprecedented," Resch said. "The WNBA has never had a team internationally and that comes along with a lot of very difficult details to work through that there is no precedent for. So it's not easy, but it's worth it."
Toronto will become the 14th WNBA franchise after a team owned by the Golden State Warriors opens in 2025.
There were several other bidders for the franchise - the league expects to add two right after Toronto joins - but the strength of this bid was too good for the WNBA to pass up.
"(I) talked to Larry about why would he want to do this. Women's sports is hard, being an owner is hard, it takes long-term commitment," Engelbert said. "He was just so gracious about the growth of the league, wanting to contribute to that, getting a new fan base in. He had all the right narrative on why he would want to do this. Just because a city calls you, you don't say, 'OK, the data looks good, let's grant a franchise.' "
Resch said the team expects to solicit suggestions for a name from fans and has opened a ticket wait list through the WNBA website.
Tanenbaum is committed to funding a $40-million training facility - the team will initially work out of the University of Toronto - and there will be some necessary renovations to the 8,500-capacity Coca-Cola Coliseum.
"It's all privately (funded)," Resch said. "This is Larry Tanenbaum investing in women's sport."
It is an investment - the cost could reach $115 million (U.S.) - he makes willingly.
"Our team will complete the pathway for women in this country," Tanenbaum said. "They will see heroes that look like them in person and on the air also.
"They'll gain inspiration from A'ja Wilson, Diana Taurasi, Breanna Stewart and Kia Nurse. They can see that the sport they play as girls ... is just as important and worth investing in because the more we all recognize the value and potential of women's sports, the closer we get to a more equitable future in the world of sports."
That was the overwhelming feeling among a diverse group offering support and praise.
Scottie Barnes, a near permanent fixture working out in Toronto these days, was at the announcement and ex-Raptor Kyle Lowry flew in for the event. Raptors global ambassador Drake was there and the leaders from all three levels of government - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario premier Doug Ford and Toronto mayor Olivia Chow - spoke at the event.
They paid tribute to the moment, for the arrival of the franchise, and to the past players who didn't grow up in an era when the WNBA existed, or if it did, it didn't resonate in Canada.
"I looked up to Michael Jordan growing up, the New York Knicks, the Chicago Bulls ... now these young girls are going to get to see women in their country playing the sport they love," Canadian Olympian Tamara Tatham said.
"You get to look up to people, you get to look up to women, I think that's an amazing opportunity for our country and athletes."
CAPTION: The souvenir ball from the news conference to announce the expansion of the WNBA to Canada.
