Document 275 of 500

Mayor wants MZO revoked: Move would allow housing builds on properties now included in Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mayor-wants-mzo-revoked/docview/3059207329/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+wants+MZO+revoked%3A+Move+would+allow+housing+builds+on+properties+now+included+in+Greenbelt&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The mayor of Pickering wants Premier Doug Ford's help to have land set aside in the 1970s for a never-built international airport eventually opened up for housing.
Kevin Ashe has written to provincial Housing Minister Paul Calandra formally requesting he revoke a 1972 minister's zoning order (MZO) that prevents construction of any houses or apartment towers on properties adjacent to the federal-owned Pickering Airport Lands.
But some of the lands in question are now part of the protected Greenbelt.
Former premier Bill Davis's government issued the MZO as part of an agreement with then-prime minister Pierre Trudeau to ensure the flight paths of jetliners using the planned Pickering international airport wouldn't be hindered by tall buildings.
Ashe's request was quietly posted to the Environmental Registry of Ontario (ERO) late Wednesday and would affect 3,563 hectares, or 8,804 acres, of land.
In the wake of Ford's Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP, Queen's Park will only consider an amendment to the MZO rather than revoking it. That's because the provincial government, still stinging from the $8.28-billion land debacle, does not want anything built on the Greenbelt.
"This posting is being undertaken in response to a request from the City of Pickering to revoke Ontario Regulation 102/72 to remove restrictions on future urban development, on a portion of the lands subject to the zoning order," the ERO post said.
"This future urban development is proposed in the Region of Durham Official Plan, which is currently before the minister for a decision," it continued.
"The city's request sought full revocation of the zoning order; however, the ministry is consulting on a proposal toamend the zoning order to only remove a portion of the lands that are outside of the Greenbelt Plan Area, which would ensure continued application of the MZO in the Greenbelt area."
In a letter to Calandra sent last Friday, Ashe wrote that he was "respectfully requesting that the Government of Ontario revoke O. Reg. 102/72, a provincial MZO" in northeast Pickering, which would enable the construction of homes for 40,000 people, creating 10,000 jobs.
"Restrictions regarding a potential airport are outlined in the Regional Official Plan, which makes it clear that constructing an airport and developing northeast Pickering are separate and independent matters," the mayor wrote.
"The Regional Official Plan does not limit an airport nor depend on one. In fact, planning and developing northeast Pickering will help inform the federal government's decision making on whether or not to site an airport in Pickering," he continued, noting he is using his "strong mayor powers" to achieve provincially mandated housing construction targets.
More than a half century ago, Ottawa expropriated about 7,500 hectares (18,600 acres) of farm land for a new airport 56 kilometres from Toronto because what is now known as Pearson International Airport was at the time considered to be at full capacity. Pearson has since been expanded several times and is now the largest and busiest airport in Canada.
While the Pickering airport scheme has been scaled back over the years, the project remains in a holding pattern and has never been scrapped.
In 2013, Ottawa created the Rouge National Urban Park from a significant chunk of the land, reserving 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) for a "potential future aviation development."
Thirteen months ago, the federal government announced a new study "analyze current and future airport supply and demand" in southern Ontario that could determine what, if anything, is built in Pickering.
A spokesperson for federal Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez would only say the work is ongoing.
"Our government will consider the results of this study among many other factors to determine next steps on the Pickering Lands," Laurent de Casanove said.
Opponents, such as community group Land Over Landings that worry about the impact on agricultural land, hope the airport plan will crash and burn once and for all.
After Ford's election in 2018, MZOs have become a far more commonly used tool to expedite development.
But the orders have sparked concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the Progressive Conservatives were invoking too many of them.
Since taking over the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing last September in the wake of the Greenbelt fiasco, Calandra has made sweeping reforms to curb the use of MZOs.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded certain well-connected developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when Ford opened up 3,000 hectares (7,400 acres) of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
After a public outcry, plunging poll numbers and resignations of key ministers and aides, the premier cancelled the land swap last Sept. 21. "It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt," he said at the time.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CAPTION: Although the Pickering airport scheme has been scaled back over the years, the project remains in a holding pattern and has never been scrapped. Community groups such as Land Over Landings worry about the project's impact on agricultural land.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from Mark Ramzy

Document 276 of 500

A moment, and a movement: Birth of franchise a huge step forward for Canadian sport

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/moment-movement/docview/3059207229/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+moment%2C+and+a+movement%3A+Birth+of+franchise+a+huge+step+forward+for+Canadian+sport&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=S.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What once could be only imagined is now tangible: The WNBA has arrived in Toronto, and in Canada.
And those upon which the foundation of Canadian women's basketball was built know how significant the country's first WNBA franchise truly is.
"It's so important," 12-year WNBA veteran Tammy Sutton-Brown said after Toronto's expansion team was officially announced Thursday. "The value of 'you can see it, you can be it' and the value of meeting your favourite players up close and personal, there's something really special about that.
"I just got chills. Who would have thought? Dreams really do come true."
It was - as Denise Dignard, Canada Basketball's executive vice-president for women's high performance, called it - the "Carter effect" moment for young Canadian women just like Vince Carter's arrival inspired a generation of Canadian stars in the NBA.
"This is just a long time coming," said Stacey Dales, a 2000 Canadian Olympian who hosted Thursday's event.
"The hyperbole is real. It is a monumental moment for somebody who grew up in Brockville, Ont., population about 27,000 when I was a kid, to never seeing basketball on TV, to seeing 27 years later now a league that's evolved into (the) WNBA (in) Toronto. It has been incredible."
If there was one overriding sentiment at what was a celebration of the game Thursday, it was that the moment for women's sports here has truly arrived.
"Many say women's sports is having a moment, I disagree," franchise owner Larry Tanenbaum said. "Women's sports has arrived. It's a movement.
"The world is finally taking notice of something that's been there all along: immense talent, passion and competition. And that's why we're here today at the right time ... for our first WNBA team in Canada."
The Canadian aspect is not just talk. While the team will play primarily at the Coca-Cola Coliseum when it debuts in May 2026, there are plans to play regular-season games in Vancouver and Montreal.
"It was Larry's idea, let's maybe make this more like 'Canada's team, let's play games in Montreal and Vancouver,' " WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert said. "We haven't even set the number of games we'll be playing in 2026, because that will be the first year of the new collective bargaining agreement, so as we get into those negotiations, we'll see how many games we do play outside of the city of Toronto."
The job of building a franchise from scratch now lies with team president Teresa Resch, who joined Tanenbaum's Kilmer Sports Ventures organization after more than a decade with Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment. She was the driving force behind the construction of the Raptors' OVO training centre, oversaw the team's COVID year in Tampa and was involved in establishing the G League's Raptors 905. But an entire entity is a whole bigger task.
"Honestly, this is unprecedented," Resch said. "The WNBA has never had a team internationally and that comes along with a lot of very difficult details to work through that there is no precedent for. So it's not easy, but it's worth it."
Toronto will become the 14th WNBA franchise after a team owned by the Golden State Warriors opens in 2025.
There were several other bidders for the franchise - the league expects to add two right after Toronto joins - but the strength of this bid was too good for the WNBA to pass up.
"(I) talked to Larry about why would he want to do this. Women's sports is hard, being an owner is hard, it takes long-term commitment," Engelbert said. "He was just so gracious about the growth of the league, wanting to contribute to that, getting a new fan base in. He had all the right narrative on why he would want to do this. Just because a city calls you, you don't say, 'OK, the data looks good, let's grant a franchise.' "
Resch said the team expects to solicit suggestions for a name from fans and has opened a ticket wait-list through the WNBA website.
Tanenbaum is committed to funding a $40-million training facility - the team will initially work out of the University of Toronto - and there will be some necessary renovations to the 8,500-capacity Coca-Cola Coliseum.
"It's all privately (funded)," Resch said. "This is Larry Tanenbaum investing in women's sport."
It is an investment - the cost could reach $115 million (U.S.) - he makes willingly.
"Our team will complete the pathway for women in this country," Tanenbaum said. "They will see heroes that look like them in person and on the air also.
"They'll gain inspiration from A'ja Wilson, Diana Taurasi, Breanna Stewart and Kia Nurse. They can see that the sport they play as girls ... is just as important and worth investing in because the more we all recognize the value and potential of women's sports, the closer we get to a more equitable future in the world of sports."
That was the overwhelming feeling among a diverse group offering support and praise.
Scottie Barnes, a near permanent fixture working out in Toronto these days, was at the announcement and ex-Raptor Kyle Lowry flew in for the event. Raptors global ambassador Drake was there and the leaders from all three levels of government - Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Ontario premier Doug Ford and Toronto mayor Olivia Chow - spoke at the event.
They paid tribute to the moment, for the arrival of the franchise, and to the past players who didn't grow up in an era when the WNBA existed, or if it did, it didn't resonate in Canada.
"I looked up to Michael Jordan growing up, the New York Knicks, the Chicago Bulls ... now these young girls are going to get to see women in their country playing the sport they love," Canadian Olympian Tamara Tatham said.
"You get to look up to people, you get to look up to women, I think that's an amazing opportunity for our country and athletes."
CREDIT: Doug Smith Toronto Star

Subject: Womens sports; Labor contracts; Professional basketball; Collective bargaining

Document 277 of 500

Historical echoes from across the pond: British PM's call for election evokes memories of Campbell's 1993 defeat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/historical-echoes-across-pond/docview/3059207208/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Historical+echoes+from+across+the+pond%3A+British+PM%27s+call+for+election+evokes+memories+of+Campbell%27s+1993+defeat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tired Conservative party with an unelected leader who also happens to be a barrier-smashing political pioneer.
A right-wing Reform party threatening to siphon votes from the Tories in key constituencies around the country that could turn the election into a landslide for the main opposition party.
A grey and not particularly inspiring lawyer poised to become prime minister, returning his party to power after years on the opposition benches.
Sound familiar?
No, not Canada in 1993, but the United Kingdom in 2024.
British Prime Minister Rishi Sunak on Wednesday called a July 4 election, after months of speculation that has included a surprising amount of interest in the 1993 Canadian election that ushered out prime minister Kim Campbell's Tories and elected Jean Chrétien's Liberals.
That's because Sunak's Conservatives - in power for 14 years and five prime ministers, having presided over the economically disastrous Brexit that saw Britons vote to leave the European Union in a 2016 referendum - trail Sir Keir Starmer's Labour by more than 20 percentage points in every major public-opinion poll.
Andrew MacDougall, former director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper and now a director of London-based Trafalgar Strategy, said the Canadian comparison is an imperfect one.
Even though it has been cited in Fleet Street newspapers and hit podcasts like The News Agents, which featured dramatic clips from the CBC's Oct. 25, 1993 election special, MacDougall emphasized Wednesday that "it's oversold" in the UK.
"Everybody got a little excited about that parallel," he said in an interview from London.
Like Campbell, the first (and still only) woman to be Canadian PM, Sunak, the first person of colour to govern Britain, made domestic political history after taking over from an unpopular and controversial predecessor.
But MacDougall stressed that Campbell inheriting Brian Mulroney's legacy of constitutional wrangling over the Meech Lake and Charlottetown Accords and imposing the Goods and Services Tax is not quite the same as Sunak taking the reins from Liz Truss, who served just seven weeks as British PM in 2022 after tanking the British economy with unfunded tax cut promises the markets strongly rejected.
Truss had briefly succeeded the scandal-plagued Boris Johnson, who had been ousted after Sunak and other cabinet members resigned en masse earlier that summer.
"It's different in the causes (of an anticipated Tory defeat), but the outcome could be similar (to 1993)," he said.
"Though it would be nothing like the two seats (Campbell's party won.) A terrible outcome here would be around 80 seats for the Tories of the 650 in the Commons."
Unlike the Canadian PCs - who lost votes nationwide to the breakaway Alberta-based Reform party, which helped Chrétien's Liberals win a stunning 98 of the 99 seats in Ontario and 31 of 32 in Atlantic Canada in 1993 - MacDougall notes Reform UK has no geographical stronghold.
"Canadian Reform had split away from the Progressive Conservatives," he said, pointing out Reform UK is a protest movement that probably won't even win a seat.
CAPTION: U.K. Prime Minister Rishi Sunak's Conservatives trail Sir Keir Starmer's Labour by more than 20 percentage points in every major public opinion poll.BENJAMIN CREMEL AFP via Getty Images
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 278 of 500

Pierre Poilievre says he'll 'axe the carbon tax' for consumers - but when it comes to the pricing system for Canadian industries, he won't say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-says-hell-axe-carbon-tax/docview/3059525218/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+says+he%27ll+%27axe+the+carbon+tax%27+for+consumers+-+but+when+it+comes+to+the+pricing+system+for+Canadian+industries%2C+he+won%27t+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-He would repeal incoming federal electricity regulations. He would scrap Liberal clean fuel standards. And, yes, he wants to "axe the tax." But it's less clear where Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre stands on another major piece of federal climate policy: the national requirement for industrial carbon pricing.
It's a significant question mark in his campaign against the federal levy on consumer fuel, which Poilievre denounces as a tax that unfairly imposes costs on Canadians. That levy, formally called a "fuel charge" on things like gasoline, is just one piece of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon pricing regime. The other part is the legislated need for all provinces and territories to have an industrial pricing system for sectors like oil and gas, cement and steel production, or adopt one that was made in Ottawa.
The murkiness has some industry players, lobbyists, and climate policy advocates trying to decipher what the Conservative leader thinks, with some urging him to come clean so businesses don't delay spending that will be crucial to meeting Canada's emissions reduction commitments.
Numerous political and business insiders say Poilievre's office is devoting time and resources to thinking through their environmental policy, and despite his ongoing criticisms of the lobbying sector, remain open to hearing from industry leaders about how existing programs and policies help or hurt their sector's own environmental ambitions.
But few expect clarity on industrial pricing program anytime soon, arguing that if industry wants certainty their questions should be directed not to Ottawa, but to provinces like Alberta and Ontario that have their own industrial pricing systems meeting federal requirements.
Poilievre has not held a press conference on Parliament Hill in more than a month. On Thursday, his office provided a statement to the Star that lambasted Trudeau's climate policies but didn't say whether a future Conservative government would keep, change or ditch the current rules for industrial pricing.
The head-scratching only got more vigorous after the new head of the Pathways Alliance, an oil industry lobby group devoted to reducing the sector's Canada-leading greenhouse gas pollution, told the CBC that "it would be very helpful for him to provide greater clarity" on industrial pricing.
The group declined an interview request from the Star this week.
The Liberal government quickly took note of the demand. Last weekend, Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault posted on social media: "When will Pierre Poilievre come clean to Canadians about his plan to help his big polluter friends off the hook?"
Guilbeault also told a parliamentary committee on Wednesday that industrial pricing helped spur projects like Dow Chemical's $11.5 billion upgrade to make an Edmonton-area facility produce no emissions, and suggested such investments could be in jeopardy if these pricing systems were dismantled.
But some say uncertainty around future climate policies - including what the Conservatives would do with industrial pricing - is also dragging potential investments to reduce emissions from heavy industry. Catherine Cobden, the president and chief executive officer of the Canadian Steel Producers Association, said "big questions" are hanging about the future of the industrial pricing system is "putting a chill" on private spending to slash emissions that cause climate change.
She also cited questions around Liberal government policies, including over supports for heavy industries as the minimum carbon price is scheduled to keep increasing every year, from the current $85 per tonne to $170 per tonne in 2030.
"From the government, we are still seeking clarification regarding the future carbon pricing regime and associated supports for our industry," Cobden said in a statement to the Star.
"From the opposition parties, we are looking for more detail on their climate policies should they form a future government."
Other industry groups, like the Chemical Industry Association of Canada (CIAC), say they want industrial pricing to stay in place, but also want to see additional support like the promised tax credits for green investments that the government says will be worth more than $90 billion over the next 11 years.
"Our message is to continue with carbon pricing," said CIAC president Bob Masterson. Like Guilbeault, he pointed to investments like the Dow Chemical project near Edmonton as evidence the industrial systems can help spur green spending.
But he said more support will be needed if the price keeps going up as the Liberals have planned, since a heavier price could risk them losing business to competitors in places without carbon pricing. "You have to be concerned about the outlook of companies," he said.
For Michael Bernstein, executive director of the pro-carbon pricing think tank, Clean Prosperity, significant emissions reductions from heavy industries are critical for Canada to hit its 2030 target. The country has pledged to slash emissions to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by that year, while the most recent government data shows Canada was 7.1 per cent below 2005 levels in 2022.
With an unprecedented amount of reductions needed in a span of less than a decade, Bernstein said clarity from Poilievre on industrial pricing would be welcome, since there are questions about whether the system will remain in place, whether the minimum price under federal law would be changed, and what provinces would do if the federal requirements were removed or weakened.
One recent report, from the Canadian Climate Institute, estimated that industrial pricing could be responsible for a big chunk - between 20 to 48 per cent - of Canada's emissions reductions by 2030.
Poilievre has previously expressed support for Alberta's version of industrial carbon pricing, and that the Conservative leader told reporters last year that he is not proposing to withdraw Canada from the international Paris Agreement.
But if Poilievre kept Canada's current emissions targets and scrapped industrial pricing, that would raise questions about how provinces would respond, and how the country would turn to alternative policies - like government regulations or taxpayer subsidies - to reduce industrial emissions.
"Industry is not going to decarbonize, at least in a systematic way, without some policy push," he said. "You currently need the industrial pricing system, or you have to rely on one - or both - of regulations or subsidies."
There is also a political question of whether, in keeping a form of industrial pricing at the federal level, Poilievre would take hits for supporting a form of carbon pricing - something that hurt his predecessor as Conservative leader, Erin O'Toole, within his own party.
Ken Boessenkool, the executive director of Conservatives for Clean Growth, said politically, Poilievre has no need to fear attacks for keeping the industrial system even if he goes ahead and scraps the consumer carbon levy, because voters simply don't see them as the same. Former Alberta premier Jason Kenney, he noted, removed the provincial consumer price on carbon yet embraced an industrial program, named TIER, for Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction.
"If you go up and down Alberta, 95 per cent of people would agree that Kenney got rid of the carbon tax," Boessenkool said. "They would be wrong. But they would also be right."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre during question period, in Ottawa on May 23, 2024.

Document 279 of 500

Liberals hammered over auto theft action plan during meeting on public safety and national security

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberals-hammered-over-auto-theft-action-plan/docview/3058772672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+hammered+over+auto+theft+action+plan+during+meeting+on+public+safety+and+national+security&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Auto theft remains a front burner issue in Ottawa, days after the federal government unveiled a new action plan to tackle the crisis.
Conservatives attempted to portray the Liberals as soft-on-crime on Thursday before the standing committee on Public Safety and National Security.
"Are they concerned about the victims or are they concerned about the criminals?," Arnold Viersen (Cons. Peace River-Westlock) asked the committee.
Viersen hit out against what he called soft requirements for bail, saying that criminals benefit from a "revolving door," in the court system.
"Many of the crimes are committed by criminals who are out on bail," Viersen said.
Viersen said that current laws make it tough to get theft convictions, even when criminals are seemingly caught red-handed.
"You have to prove that the individual stole the vehicle," Viersen said. "Being in possession of the car that was stolen does not prove the fact that you stole the car."
Vehicle theft has shot up 34.1 per cent nationally between 2015 and 2022, Conservative Frank Caputo (Kamloops-Thompson-Cariboo) told the standing committee.
Statistics are far worse in some centres, including a 122.5 per cent rise in vehicle theft in Ontario between 2015 and 2022, Caputo said.
Things have gotten so bad that drivers are sometimes advised to keep their keys in plain view so that there won't be violence when auto thieves break into their homes, Caputo said.
"It's almost like a harm reduction," Caputo said.
This is apparently in reference to comments made by a Toronto police officer at a community town hall, who told Etobicoke residents they should try leaving their car FOBs at the front door to make it easier to find by thieves who are breaking into homes looking for carkeys.
There was a 120 per cent increase in vehicle theft in New Brunswick, with a 190 per cent rise in Moncton, Caputo said.
In Montreal, auto theft has shot up 105.9 per cent between 2015 and 2022.
The next set of statistics may even be worse when they're compiled, Caputo said.
His comments come days after the federal government unveiled a national plan to combat auto theft, including changes to the criminal code.
On Monday, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland unveiled the new strategy in Brampton, Ont., calling auto theft a complex and growing international concern.
The newly announced federal measures include more intelligence sharing among police forces and a working group involving all levels of government, Freeland said, flanked by senior cabinet ministers, including Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc.
The comments come as authorities estimate a vehicle is stolen roughly every six minutes in Canada, with 12,000 stolen annually in the GTA.
Experts estimate auto theft funnels more than $1-billion yearly towards various established and emerging organized crime groups.
The new federal strategy includes providing $28 million meant to help prevent cars from being shipped abroad through expanded searches of shipping containers at seaports.
Many vehicles stolen in Canada end up resold abroad, including in West Africa, experts say.
Proposed amendments to the Criminal Code also include the addition of new offences which target the possession and distribution of tools used to steal vehicles.
The new federal strategy also includes integrating the RCMP's stolen vehicle database with one kept by Interpol.
LeBlanc told the meeting earlier this week that Ontario residents - especially those residing in the GTA have become "all too acquainted" with auto theft.
The government said on Monday that Canada Border Services Agency has intercepted 1,205 stolen vehicles in rail yards and ports so far in 2024.
With files by Canadian Press
CAPTION: On Monday,Finance MinisterChrystiaFreelandunveiled the new strategy in Brampton, Ont., calling auto theft a complexandgrowing international concern.

Document 280 of 500

Pickering mayor asks Ford government to revoke MZO to build housing near airport lands, but some are on the Greenbelt

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pickering-mayor-asks-ford-government-revoke-mzo/docview/3058772616/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pickering+mayor+asks+Ford+government+to+revoke+MZO+to+build+housing+near+airport+lands%2C+but+some+are+on+the+Greenbelt&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The mayor of Pickering wants Premier Doug Ford's help to have land set aside in the 1970s for a never-built international airport eventually opened up for housing.
Kevin Ashe has written to provincial Housing Minister Paul Calandra formally requesting he revoke a 1972 minister's zoning order (MZO) that prevents construction of any houses or apartment towers on properties adjacent to the federal-owned Pickering Airport Lands.
But some of the lands in question are now part of the protected Greenbelt.
Former premier Bill Davis's government issued the MZO as part of an agreement with then prime minister Pierre Trudeau to ensure the flight paths of jetliners using the planned Pickering international airport wouldn't be hindered by tall buildings.
Ashe's request was quietly posted to the Environmental Registry of Ontario (ERO) late Wednesday and would affect 3,563 hectares, or 8,804 acres, of land.
In the wake of Ford's Greenbelt land swap scandal now under investigation by the RCMP, Queen's Park will only consider an amendment to the MZO rather than revoking it.
That's because the provincial government, still stinging from the $8.28-billion land debacle, does not want anything built on the Greenbelt.
"This posting is being undertaken in response to a request from the City of Pickering to revoke Ontario Regulation 102/72 to remove restrictions on future urban development, on a portion of the lands subject to the zoning order," the ERO post said.
"This future urban development is proposed in the Region of Durham Official Plan, which is currently before the minister for a decision," it continued.
"The city's request sought full revocation of the zoning order; however, the ministry is consulting on a proposal to amend the zoning order to only remove a portion of the lands that are outside of the Greenbelt Plan Area, which would ensure continued application of the MZO in the Greenbelt area."
In a letter to Calandra sent last Friday, Ashe wrote that he was "respectfully requesting that the Government of Ontario revoke O. Reg. 102/72, a provincial MZO" in northeast Pickering, which would enable the construction of homes for 40,000 people, creating 10,000 jobs.
"Restrictions regarding a potential airport are outlined in the Regional Official Plan, which makes it clear that constructing an airport and developing northeast Pickering are separate and independent matters," the mayor wrote.
"The Regional Official Plan does not limit an airport nor depend on one. In fact, planning and developing northeast Pickering will help inform the federal government's decision making on whether or not to site an airport in Pickering," he continued, noting he is using his "strong mayor powers" to achieve provincially mandated housing construction targets.
More than a half century ago, Ottawa expropriated about 7,500 hectares (18,600 acres) of farm land for a new airport 56 kilometres from Toronto because what is now known as Pearson International Airport was at the time considered to be at full capacity. Pearson has since been expanded several times and is now the largest and busiest airport in Canada.
While the Pickering airport scheme has been scaled back over the years, the project remains in a holding pattern and has never been scrapped.
In 2013, Ottawa created the Rouge National Urban Park from a significant chunk of the land, reserving 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) for a "potential future aviation development."
Thirteen months ago, the federal government announced a new study "analyze current and future airport supply and demand" in southern Ontario that could determine what, if anything, is built in Pickering.
A spokesperson for federal Transport Minister Pablo Rodriguez would only say the work is ongoing.
"Our government will consider the results of this study among many other factors to determine next steps on the Pickering Lands," Laurent de Casanove said.
Opponents, such as community group Land Over Landings that worry about the impact on agricultural land, hope the airport plan will crash and burn once and for all.
After Ford's election in 2018, MZOs have become a far morecommonly used tool to expedite development.
But the orders have sparked concerns from municipalities, opposition parties, community groups and environmentalists that the Progressive Conservatives were invoking too many of them.
Since taking over the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing last September in the wake of the Greenbelt fiasco, Calandra has made sweeping reforms to curb the use of MZOs.
Last August, separate reports by the auditor general and integrity commissioner concluded certain well-connected developers with Tory connections were "favoured" when Ford opened up 3,000 hectares (7,400 acres) of protected Greenbelt land to build housing.
After a public outcry, plunging poll numbers and resignations of key ministers and aides, the premier cancelled the land swap last Sept. 21.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt," he said at the time.
"I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise. As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes."
With files from Mark Ramzy

Bonnie Crombie asks RCMP for update on Greenbelt investigation of Ford government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bonnie-crombie-asks-rcmp-update-on-greenbelt/docview/3058772596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bonnie+Crombie+asks+RCMP+for+update+on+Greenbelt+investigation+of+Ford+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie is asking the RCMP for an update on its investigation into the Greenbelt scandal, saying one is needed given new information that has been uncovered.
In a letter sent to RCMP Commissioner Mike Duheme and Assistant Commissioner Matt Peggs on Thursday, Crombie said, "I am asking that you provide an update on the progress of your investigation, in light of these most recent revelations," including those in a Star story about a senior staffer in Premier Doug Ford's office.
"Mr. Carlo Oliviero attended at least one meeting with a developer involved in the scandal," according to the Star, Crombie wrote.
"Further, records show that correspondence on this meeting was sent to Mr. Oliviero's personal email. This meeting calls into question the premier's assertion that his office was 'not involved in specific-site selection.'
"I urge you to closely review this new information, to assess whether further investigations are warranted into the conduct of Premier Ford and his staff and provide the public with a progress update on the current investigation."
Crombie also raises concerns about what she calls the "unacceptable revolving door between the premier's office and corporate lobbyists connected to the Greenbelt scandal. Mr. Oliviero, who we now know was directly involved, joins a growing list of registered lobbyists who appear to be using their close ties to the premier's office to advance private interests."
As reported by the Star's Sheila Wang and Noor Javed, Oliviero - who at the time worked for Ford - was invited to a meeting to discuss Greenbelt lands in Hamilton that were later released from their protected status. Oliviero has said that after the initial meeting, he "referred the issue to the appropriate ministry."
The meeting was uncovered in documents released by the province after a recent freedom of information request by the Ontario NDP about the $8.28 billion scandal, documents they say have raised even more questions about the involvement of the premier's office in removing land from the Greenbelt.
Ford's office has said it had no direct knowledge or role in the changes. The province's auditor general and integrity commissioner have both said staff were not involved in site selection, but have said well-connected developers were favoured in the process.
Last October, the RCMP began a criminal investigation into the Greenbelt plan, but there have been no updates on it.
A spokesperson for Ford directed all inquiries to the RCMP.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie speaks to the 2024 Ontario budget at Queens Park in Toronto on March 26, 2024.

Subject: Site selection; Lobbyists; Criminal investigations; Scandals

Document 282 of 500

There's a new book about Pierre Poilievre. Here are five things we learned about the Conservative leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-new-book-about-pierre-poilievre-here-are/docview/3058772560/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+a+new+book+about+Pierre+Poilievre.+Here+are+five+things+we+learned+about+the+Conservative+leader&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-"It's just Pierre being Pierre."
That's the answer many in Conservative circles give these days when they're quizzed about why party leader Pierre Poilievre does or says something that observers struggle to understand.
But over eight months and through more than 60 interviews, journalist Andrew Lawton sought to probe more into what that actually means: Who is Poilievre? What drives him?
"By explaining who Poilievre is, where he came from, and how he fits into the changing landscape of Canadian conservative politics, we get a sense of what the future may hold as his political life unfolds," Lawton writes in the introduction to "Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life", his newly-released book obtained ahead of its publication by the Star.
Here are some key takeaways from the first book-length look at a man polls suggest could be well on the road to becoming Canada's next prime minister.
Why is he sometimes called Jeff?
That Poilievre is the adopted son of two schoolteachers is well known; he references it in his regular rally speeches and elsewhere all the time.
Lawton reveals that one of the reasons Poilievre is sometimes referred to as "Jeff" is because that's the name his birth mother would have given him, if she'd raised him on her own. His adoptive parents also adopted his biological half-brother Patrick. The brothers forged a relationship with their biological mother when they were in their 20s.
His adoptive parents separated when he was 12, and he remains close to his mother Marlene, as well as his father Don and Don's long-time partner Ross McWhirter.
Lawton also shoots down a rumour that has circulated for years that Poilievre's father was sitting in the House of Commons when Poilievre voted against legalizing same-sex marriage and also for a broad reopening of the debate itself.
He eloped to Portugal
Poilievre grew up in Calgary and got involved in politics early - he was so involved that he ultimately dropped out of his bachelor's degree program at the University of Calgary in 2002 to take a job with then-foreign affairs critic Stockwell Day. He was three courses shy at the time, Lawton writes, and eventually took online courses through Athabasca University to finish off his degree in 2008.
His affection for his wife Anaida Poilievre - and the political force she is in her own right - also comes across at campaign rallies, but Lawton's book reveals the early days of their courtship.
She dodged him initially and was miffed he unilaterally set the terms for their very first date in 2012 - at a downtown Ottawa coffee shop Poilievre was able to have access to before it opened to the public. Their first date went so long Poilievre ended up with a parking ticket.
He told her he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017 to get married.
The duo have two children, and Lawton notes that their older daughter, Valentina, has been diagnosed with autism and is non-verbal. She receives therapy, though what kind and from who is not specified, but Lawton reports it has benefitted her.
Known as a prankster
Lawton reports Poilievre likes playing pranks on his own staff and other colleagues, often using foreign accents or mimicking other politicians.
He's posed as a foreign diplomat to call the office of then-Conservative Foreign Affairs Minister John Baird claiming Baird had promised something during a trip abroad and demanding he deliver.
Poilievre, Lawton writes, also once had a woman call his own office to pretend she was pregnant after a one-night stand with him and wanted to track him down to tell him about it.
Poilievre was in a relationship with longtime Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne at the time.
Byrne did speak to Lawton about their relationship for the book - the pair dated for 12 years - but declined to delve into it in detail. The relationship ended, she told Lawton, because they had simply grown apart.
Lawton also attempted to interview people who worked with Poilievre during his time as a junior cabinet minister, but many did not return his calls.
One unnamed source said Poilievre was "insufferable" as a minister, though has grown out of it, while others told Lawton that if people chafe under Poilievre's leadership, it means they simply aren't cut out to work on the Hill. He once told his staff "this is not a self-esteem factory," a phrase that was then turned into a poster and put on the wall of his office.
As party leader, Lawton reports "Poilievre and his team have run a tight ship, with high expectations for attendance and performance in question period. Non-essential absenteeism is forbidden," but Poilievre also ensures caucus has a chance to speak their mind behind closed doors so they have their say, Lawton writes.
He is often his own harshest critic; colleagues recount how he was "sullen and withdrawn" for some time after stepping on his government's 2008 apology for residential schools.
Where did he cut his political teeth?
Lawton traces a long arc for Poilievre's political ideas and ideals all the way back to his childhood - his first political outing, Lawton says was a provincial Progressive Conservative riding association meeting he attended with his mother.
"He enjoyed debate already and sometimes his teachers didn't like that very much," his mother Marlene told Lawton. "Some teachers told me he sat on the edge of his desk and couldn't wait to debate."
As a teen, among his go-to books? The dictionary, Lawton writes. He also began reading about politics and economics.
He tried to join the board of a Calgary riding association, but was rebuffed because it was already full - so they signed him on as a fundraiser, and he would go on to impress with his ability to rake in cash.
He also, Lawton writes, showcased an interest in mastering procedural rules for debates at the Model United Nations, and teaching his team lessons about where to put their hands or how to use body language to make a point.
"Pierre hasn't changed," Lawton quotes one of his schoolmates - someone who works today on Parliament Hill though not for the Tories.
"I think some people think this is a persona, but he is identical to the Pierre I remember from the speech and debate club, model Parliament, and Speakers' Corner."
He's written a book
As an adult, he also once worked on a book - ahead of the 2021 campaign, Poilievre had also been writing something tentatively called "Debtonation," which Lawton describes as a "a meticulously researched, chart-heavy manuscript" meant to explore Canada's current fiscal situation and what needed to happen to - in Poilievre's view - fix it.
Lawton's book includes an excerpt from the never-published manuscript that references the same themes Poilievre hammers on now - removing the gatekeepers and giving people back their freedom.
Lawton also writes about the tensions between Poilievre and the media, quoting Byrne as saying whatever people may think it's not strategy but just "Pierre being Pierre."
But Lawton also quotes current House Leader and former Conservative leader Andrew Scheer as reflecting on why it should be a strategy: Scheer believes the media "concoct and contrive" narratives to hurt Conservatives and help Liberals.
"So at the very least, if we can show that bias to Canadians, then come campaign time, we'll have a fighting chance to get our message through."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his wife Anaida walk from a news conference in Quebec City on Sept. 6, 2023.

Subject: Political leadership

Location: Portugal; Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: There's a new book about Pierre Poilievre. Here are five things we learned about the Conservative leader

Document 283 of 500

'Monumental': Canada to extend citizenship to children born abroad, restoring rights of 'lost Canadians'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/monumental-canada-extend-citizenship-children/docview/3058772501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Monumental%27%3A+Canada+to+extend+citizenship+to+children+born+abroad%2C+restoring+rights+of+%27lost+Canadians%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Following a court order, the federal government has introduced legislation to restore the citizenship rights of "lost Canadians" born outside Canada and ensure that what happened to them doesn't happen to others in the future.
This legislation would automatically confer Canadian citizenship on people born abroad to a Canadian parent who is also born abroad before the changes are enacted. Anyone born outside the country subsequently would need to prove their foreign-born Canadian parent had a "substantial connection" with Canada by meeting a residency requirement.
"It will be the first time that the Citizenship Act is actually charter compliant," said Don Chapman, a staunch advocate for lost Canadians, after Bill C-71 was tabled in Parliament on Thursday. "It's monumental. And it has huge ramifications."
In 2009, the Conservative government abolished the "substantial connection" regime and adopted a blanket rule that denies the first generation born abroad the right to pass on citizenship by descent outside Canada to the second generation born abroad. The result created a generation of "lost Canadians."
"We want our citizenship to be fair, accessible, with clear and transparent rules," Immigration Minister Marc Miller told reporters. "Not everyone is entitled to it, but for those who are, it needs to be fair.
"We wanted to take this opportunity to continue to minimize differential outcomes as much as possible for children born abroad ... compared to children born to Canadians" in Canada.
According to the proposed amendment, children born abroad to Canadians since 2009 would automatically be granted citizenship, and a new substantial connection test would be created for those born after the new law comes into effect.
Once it passes, Canadians born abroad who have or adopt children also born outside Canada would need to have spent at least 1,095 cumulative days of physical presence in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child to pass on citizenship.
Last year, lost Canadians and their families launched a constitutional challenge in court of the two-generation citizenship cut-off.
In December, the Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled that it's unconstitutional for Canada to deny automatic citizenship to children born abroad because their parents also happened to be born abroad. It gave the federal government six months, until June 19, to repeal the second-generation cut-off rule and amend the Citizenship Act.
With the looming court-stipulated deadline to roll out the new scheme, Miller said the government is unlikely to receive royal assent in time and will have to go before the judge to ask for an extension, which could cause further delays for affected children and grandchildren of Canadians to acquire citizenship and join families in Canada.
"We are still looking at a number of options," Miller said. "We don't want an extension ad nauseam because there are people that are being prejudiced by this."
The Harper government changed the citizenship law and imposed the second-generation cut-off on Canadians born abroad in response to its massive effort to evacuate 15,000 Lebanese Canadians stranded in Lebanon during Israel's month-long war in that country in 2006.
The $85-million price tag of the evacuation effort sparked a debate over "Canadians of convenience" - referring to individuals with Canadian citizenship who live permanently outside of Canada without "substantive ties" to Canada, but who were nonetheless part of the government's liability.
"People travel. People study abroad. People work abroad. People fall in love abroad. People have families abroad," said NDP immigration critic Jenny Kwan, who joined Miller at the media scrum.
"But guess what? As a result of this law, it meant that for some of those families, they have had to be separated from their children. Some children have been rendered stateless."
Kwan also sniped at the opposition Conservatives for filibustering a recent bill by the Senate that called on similar changes to extend citizenship by descent to children born abroad to Canadians.
"We have to fix this once and for all," she said. "I'm standing here not for partisan reasons. I'm standing here because this is the right thing to do, not only a moral imperative to do this, but also a legal imperative."
Canadians who adopt overseas have two ways to acquire citizenship for their internationally born adoptees: apply for direct citizenship grant from abroad so the child arrives here as a citizen; or sponsor the child to Canada as a permanent resident and apply for naturalization after meeting the 1,095-day residency requirement in the country.
Under the current law, adoptees who obtain citizenship through direct grant cannot pass on citizenship to a child born or adopted abroad except through a sponsorship. The proposed change would allow this group's foreign-born descendants a citizenship pathway if the connection requirement were met. (They would still be treated differently from the other adoptees, who would be considered first-generation Canadians.)
"So the government says to us, 'It's OK because all you have to do is to qualify for the 1,095 days. And if they can pass the test, then it doesn't matter,'" said Kat Lanteigne of Toronto, who with her husband adopted their son, Nathanael, from Zambia in 2017 under direct citizenship grant.
"We say, 'You have to stop testing adopted children ... They're treating children who receive a direct grant as second-generation and second-class citizens, and we will absolutely not accept it."
In a statement, Conservative immigration critic Tom Kmiec blamed the Liberals for the "broken" immigration system and allowing "fraud, chaos and delays to run rampant."
"Contrary to his incompetent immigration minister's disingenuous claims, it is Justin Trudeau's government that has been found guilty of infringing on the rights of Canadians and invoking laws unconstitutionally," said the statement.
"Justin Trudeau and his NDP coalition partners control the majority of seats in Parliament and have the power to block or pass what they want."
CAPTION: "The current rules generally restrict citizenship by descent to the first generation, excluding some people who have a genuine connection to Canada," said Immigration Minister Marc Miller.

Subject: Families & family life; Citizenship; Canadians; Children & youth; Court decisions; Immigration

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: 'Monumental': Canada to extend citizenship to children born abroad, restoring rights of 'lost Canadians'

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 23, 2024

Document 284 of 500

Unpredictability has been key for Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unpredictability-has-been-key-ford/docview/3058532155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unpredictability+has+been+key+for+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's enduring popularity is a peculiarity.
Despite the disdain of so many, Ontario's premier dominates the public opinion polls and defies expectations in byelections.
Why is Ford so strangely immune to the midterm decline so typical and predictable for any politician - let alone a leader savouring his second term in power? How does a premier who keeps losing face and executing about-faces still stay in the good graces of his supporters?
A survey published in the Star this week shows his Progressive Conservative government still well out in front. In fact, the numbers from Abacus Data show higher positive ratings for Ford than for his opposition rivals (albeit higher negatives, too).
Much of Ford's re-election victory in 2022 can surely be attributed to the ebbing popularity of his political rivals. Yet both opposition parties now have new, able leaders in place - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - with little apparent traction to date.
Beyond the numbers, consider the premier's latest byelection triumphs, in which the Tories won two out of two. By way of disclaimer, I'd normally ignore the polls or byelections in between Ontario's general elections, because they typically attract protest votes from people who want to punish the party in power without necessarily forcing a change in government.
What's more, both of this month's byelections could be seen as signs of decline in the ruling party, triggered by two cabinet ministers who bailed out early. Voters often punish parties for these early departures, but happily for the Tories, one of the two seats - vacated by Monte McNaughton, the former minister of labour - was in a relatively safe, largely rural stronghold beyond the grasp of the opposition.
But the race in Milton was too close for comfort. Parm Gill, the incumbent cabinet minister, bizarrely and unexpectedly defected to the federal Tories - word is that he feared losing his provincial seat in the next general election for 2026.
So how did Ford's Tories hold the riding against all odds?
Turns out that Milton's newest MPP is not just from the governing party. He is also new to the party - having defected from the Liberals before the byelection.
Zee Hamid ran federally for the Liberals a decade ago, has donated to the party ever since, and actively supported the former provincial leader, Steven Del Duca when he campaigned for the post in 2020. The Karachi-born tech entrepreneur, with degrees in math and computer sciences and a CV that includes Microsoft and Amazon, embodies the kind of business-savvy, multicultural candidate most parties covet in 905 ridings.
Why would he flip?
When I caught up with him at a Queen's Park event, mingling easily with MPPs from other parties, I asked him why he'd rather switch to Ford's Tories than fight for the Liberals he once loved. He chuckled and talked about feeling comfortable running for a Tory party with broad appeal, after serving as a councillor in Milton over the years.
That's an interesting admission and evolution (regardless of whether one believes Ford's claim that he too has "evolved"). As a recent graduate of municipal politics, Hamid understands how partisan affiliation remains in the background - a mindset that also animates much of Ford's politics at the provincial level, where the premier plays up his personal brand more than party loyalty.
The long-standing recipe for Ford's popularity is populism, not partisanship. That's why his loose rhetoric - like his undisciplined spending - is so often aimed at pleasing people, rather than appeasing policy experts; at short-term tactics over long-term strategy; at political positioning rather than dispassionate deliberating.
The impulse for quick fixes - and fast flips - can be seen in Ford's fondness for seductive slogans over enduring solutions. Hence his resort to privately run medical clinics as an antidote to hospital wait-lists, as if these pilot programs can somehow be scaled up quickly to save the system; similarly with Ford's Greenbelt fiasco, he pretended that lifting the protections on a few parcels of land would make a dent in a much bigger housing shortage that defies Band-Aids and bulldozers.
That's the problem with Ford's short-term outlook and midterm run of luck - long term, his luck is bound to run out when the bad news catches up to him. Hospital wait-lists, housing shortages, LRT delays, deficit growth - sometimes the stars are in alignment, but over time the bad news cumulatively comes together in a perfect storm.
That day may come after next year's federal election, if (as the polls suggest) Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister - putting Ford under the gun should a new government in Ottawa misfire badly. Or Ford may yet dodge that bullet by burying the Ontario Tory brand, pivoting even more to the centre, and pushing back against Poilievre's anticipated antics.
In politics there is no predictability, just uncertainty. Which is why Ford keeps defying expectations.
CAPTION: Zee Hamid, Progressive Conservative MPP for Milton, middle, celebrates with Premier Doug Ford after winning this month's byelection. Hamid's defection from the Liberals prior to the byelection paid off for Ford, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Roland Cilliers Metroland file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 285 of 500

Unpredictability has been the key to Doug Ford's popularity, but it won't carry him forever

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/unpredictability-has-been-key-doug-fords/docview/3058532126/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unpredictability+has+been+the+key+to+Doug+Ford%27s+popularity%2C+but+it+won%27t+carry+him+forever&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's enduring popularity is a peculiarity.
Despite the disdain of so many, Ontario's premier dominates the public opinion polls and defies expectations in byelections.
Why is Ford so strangely immune to the midterm decline so typical and predictable for any politician - let alone a leader savouring his second term in power? How does a premier who keeps losing face and executing about-faces still stay in the good graces of his supporters?
A survey published in the Star this week shows his Progressive Conservative government still well out in front. In fact, the numbers from Abacus Data show higher positive ratings for Ford than for his opposition rivals (albeit higher negatives, too).
Much of Ford's re-election victory in 2022 can surely be attributed to the ebbing popularity of his political rivals. Yet both opposition parties now have new, able leaders in place - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - with little apparent traction to date.
Beyond the numbers, consider the premier's latest byelection triumphs, in which the Tories won two out of two. By way of disclaimer, I'd normally ignore the polls or byelections in between Ontario's general elections, because they typically attract protest votes from people who want to punish the party in power without necessarily forcing a change in government.
What's more, both of this month's byelections could be seen as signs of decline in the ruling party, triggered by two cabinet ministers who bailed out early. Voters often punish parties for these early departures, but happily for the Tories, one of the two seats - vacated by Monte McNaughton, the former minister of labour - was in a relatively safe, largely rural stronghold beyond the grasp of the opposition.
But the race in Milton was too close for comfort. Parm Gill, the incumbent cabinet minister, bizarrely and unexpectedly defected to the federal Tories - word is that he feared losing his provincial seat in the next general election for 2026.
So how did Ford's Tories hold the riding against all odds?
Turns out that Milton's newest MPP is not just from the governing party. He is also new to the party - having defected from the Liberals before the byelection.
Zee Hamid ran federally for the Liberals a decade ago, has donated to the party ever since, and actively supported the former provincial leader, Steven Del Duca when he campaigned for the post in 2020. The Karachi-born tech entrepreneur, with degrees in math and computer sciences and a CV than includes Microsoft and Amazon, embodies the kind of business-savvy, multicultural candidate most parties covet in 905 ridings.
Why would he flip?
When I caught up with him at a Queen's Park event, mingling easily with MPPs from other parties, I asked him why he'd rather switch to Ford's Tories than fight for the Liberals he once loved. He chuckled and talked about feeling comfortable running for a Tory party with broad appeal, after serving as a councillor in Milton over the years.
That's an interesting admission and evolution (regardless of whether one believes Ford's claim that he too has "evolved"). As a recent graduate of municipal politics, Hamid understands how partisan affiliation remains in the background - a mindset that also animates much of Ford's politics at the provincial level, where the premier plays up his personal brand more than party loyalty.
The long-standing recipe for Ford's popularity is populism, not partisanship. That's why his loose rhetoric - like his undisciplined spending - is so often aimed at pleasing people, rather than appeasing policy experts; at short term tactics over long term strategy; at political positioning rather than dispassionate deliberating.
The impulse for quick fixes - and fast flips - can be seen in Ford's fondness for seductive slogans over enduring solutions. Hence his resort to privately run medical clinics as an antidote to hospital wait-lists, as if these pilot programs can somehow be scaled up quickly to save the system; similarly with Ford's Greenbelt fiasco, he pretended that lifting the protections on a few parcels of land would make a dent in a much bigger housing shortage that defies Band-Aids and bulldozers.
That's the problem with Ford's short-term outlook and midterm run of luck - long term, his luck is bound to run out when the bad news catches up to him. Hospital wait-lists, housing shortages, LRT delays, deficit growth - sometimes the stars are in alignment, but over time the bad news cumulatively comes together in a perfect storm.
That day may come after next year's federal election, if (as the polls suggest) Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre becomes prime minister - putting Ford under the gun should a new government in Ottawa misfire badly. Or Ford may yet dodge that bullet by burying the Ontario Tory brand, pivoting even more to the centre, and pushing back against Poilievre's anticipated antics.
In politics there is no predictability, just uncertainty. Which is why Ford keeps defying expectations.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative candidate Zee Hamid celebrates with Premier Doug Ford after winning a provincial byelection in Milton on May 2, 2024.

Subject: Partisanship; Politics; Elections

Document 286 of 500

Province urged to provide better 'heat stress' protection for workers: Hotter summers making for 'unsafe' working conditions, MPP says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-urged-provide-better-heat-stress/docview/3058532098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+urged+to+provide+better+%27heat+stress%27+protection+for+workers%3A+Hotter+summers+making+for+%27unsafe%27+working+conditions%2C+MPP+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The early arrival of hot, sticky weather has the NDP and Ontario Federation of Labour pressing Premier Doug Ford's government for stronger "heat stress" protections for workers.
"Ontario summers are getting hotter and hotter leading to unsafe working conditions for many workers," New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) said Wednesday, noting the trend will worsen as climate change accelerates.
Tabuns and OFL president Laura Walton said improved protections are needed for both outdoor and indoor workers in jobs where they are subject to high temperatures, such as in schools without air conditioning or factories where air conditioning is not feasible.
Measures could include access to cooling rooms, cold water and shade, more breaks, and regulated or legislated standards for when workers are entitled to get out of the heat to avoid medical problems that can lead to serious health consequences, such as heat stroke and possibly death.
"This isn't something that can be put off," said Walton, whose federation last year called for heat stress to be recognized as a workplace hazard under the Occupational Health and Safety Act.
She and Tabuns said the Progressive Conservative government conducted a 30-day consultation on heat stress regulation with employers, unions and other stakeholders last August.
"Nothing's happened since," Tabuns said.
In a statement to the Star, the office of Labour Minister David Piccini did not explain why no major new policies on the issue have been developed in the nine months since the consultation.
The minister's spokeswoman pointed to voluntary guidelines issued to employers three years ago.
"Under the Occupational Health and Safety Act (OSHA), employers and supervisors have a duty to take every precaution reasonable in the circumstances for the protection of a worker, including protection in hot environments," said Zoe Knowles.
"The employer should have established a heat stress control plan for the summer to manage job tasks in high temperatures and humidity," she added.
"Any worker in Ontario who feels unsafe should report a violation for the ministry to investigate their complaint, and issue orders if necessary to ensure worker health and safety."
Walton said the government has missed "a really good opportunity over the winter to deal with a situation like this" and develop "real legislation that will protect workers."
In the consultation, the province proposed regulations to protect workers from heat-related illnesses that would require employers to comply with heat stress exposure limits for various types of jobs, from light workloads to heavy ones.
The plan was also to require employers to implement measures to control heat exposures and provide information to workers on how to recognize signs of heat-related health problems and illnesses.
Some unions have been able to negotiate heat stress protections with employers, such as cooling stations and more air-conditioned lunch rooms, said Walton.
Heat stress has garnered attention in recent weeks after Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signed a law preventing cities and counties in a southern state known for its hot, humid weather from enacting heat protections for outdoor workers.
California, Oregon and Washington have adopted requirements that employers provide shade and breaks for workers exposed to high temperatures.
CAPTION: New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns said improved protections are needed for both outdoor and indoor workers in jobs where they are subject to high temperatures.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 287 of 500

Canadian history gets a tip of the hat as a struggling British prime minister calls an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-history-gets-tip-hat-as-struggling/docview/3058963185/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+history+gets+a+tip+of+the+hat+as+a+struggling+British+prime+minister+calls+an+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tired Conservative party with an unelected leader who also happens to be a barrier-smashing political pioneer.
A right-wing Reform party threatening to siphon votes from the Tories in key constituencies around the country that could turn the election into a landslide for the main opposition party.
A grey and not particularly inspiring lawyer poised to become prime minister, returning his party to power after years on the opposition benches.
Sound familiar?
No, not Canada in 1993, but the United Kingdom in 2024.
British Prime Minister Rishi Sunak on Wednesday called a July 4 election, after months of speculation that has included a surprising amount of interest in the 1993 Canadian election that ushered out prime minister Kim Campbell's Tories and elected Jean Chrétien's Liberals.
That's because Sunak's Conservatives - in power for 14 years and five prime ministers, having presided over the economically disastrous Brexit that saw Britons vote to leave the European Union in a 2016 referendum - trail Sir Keir Starmer's Labour by more than 20 percentage points in every major public-opinion poll.
Andrew MacDougall, former director of communications to prime minister Stephen Harper and now a director of London-based Trafalgar Strategy, said the Canadian comparison is an imperfect one.
Even though it has been cited in Fleet Street newspapers and hit podcasts like The News Agents, which featured dramatic clips from the CBC's Oct. 25 1993 election special, MacDougall emphasized Wednesday that "it's oversold" in the UK.
"Everybody got a little excited about that parallel," he said in an interview from London, which was abuzz with election fever.
Like Campbell, the first (and still only) woman to be Canadian PM, Sunak, the first person of colour to govern Britain, made domestic political history after taking over from an unpopular and controversial predecessor.
But MacDougall stressed that Campbell inheriting Brian Mulroney's legacy of constitutional wrangling over the Meech Lake and Charlottetown Accords and imposing the Goods and Services Tax is not quite the same as Sunak taking the reins from Liz Truss, who served just seven weeks as British PM in 2022 after tanking the British economy with unfunded tax cut promises the markets strongly rejected.
Truss had briefly succeeded the scandal-plagued Boris Johnson, who had been ousted after Sunak and other cabinet members resigned en masse earlier that summer.
"It's different in the causes (of an anticipated Tory defeat), but the outcome could be similar (to 1993)," he said.
"Though it would be nothing like the two seats (Campbell's party won.) A terrible outcome here would be around 80 seats for the Tories of the 650 in the Commons."
Unlike the Canadian PCs - who lost votes nationwide to the breakaway Alberta-based Reform party, which helped Chrétien's Liberals win a stunning 98 of the 99 seats in Ontario and 31 of 32 in Atlantic Canada in 1993 - MacDougall notes Reform UK has no geographical stronghold.
"Canadian Reform had split away from the Progressive Conservatives," he said, pointing out Reform UK is a protest movement that probably won't even win a seat.
However one similarity is that Starmer, like Chrétien 31 years ago, does not get many voters excited.
A famed lawyer knighted by Queen Elizabeth II for his work as head of the Crown Prosecution Services and director of public prosecutions, the Labour leader is seen more as a safe set of hands than a dynamic agent of change.
"He's beige, he's dull, people just aren't that convinced about him," said MacDougall, joking the party leaders, including the Liberal Democrats' Ed Davey, will be like a group of "accountants debating on stage."
Indeed, the wealthy Sunak - his wife is the Infosys heiress Akshata Murty - also has the public image of a dreary technocrat.
But Mother Nature appears to be voting for Starmer's party - a miserable-looking Sunak was drenched by pelting rain as he stood outside 10 Downing Street to announce the election, his voice at times drowned out by a protester cranking out former Labour PM Tony Blair's campaign song, "Things Can Only Get Better."
"Things can only get wetter," quipped more than one British pundit.
MacDougall, a veteran of many elections and an expert in political communications, said it was a cringe-inducing launch.
"We're an hour into this campaign and he's already a meme. It's not a great look," he said.
"I mean, if the (Tory) premise is 'it's a dangerous world out there and only he can protect us,' mate, you can't even get an umbrella."
CAPTION: Britain's Prime Minister Rishi Sunak, soaked by rain, pauses as he delivers a speech to announce July 4 as the date of the UK's next general election, at 10 Downing Street in London, on May 22, 2024.
