Document 288 of 500

Five things you didn't know about Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/five-things-you-didnt-know-about-pierre-poilievre/docview/3058962948/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+things+you+didn%27t+know+about+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-"It's just Pierre being Pierre."
That's the answer many in Conservative circles give these days when they're quizzed about why party leader Pierre Poilievre does or says something that observers struggle to understand.
But over eight months and through more than 60 interviews, journalist Andrew Lawton sought to probe more into what that actually means: Who is Poilievre? What drives him?
"By explaining who Poilievre is, where he came from, and how he fits into the changing landscape of Canadian conservative politics, we get a sense of what the future may hold as his political life unfolds," Lawton writes in the introduction to "Pierre Poilievre: A Political Life", his newly-released book obtained ahead of its publication by the Star.
Here are some key takeaways from the first book-length look at a man polls suggest could be well on the road to becoming Canada's next prime minister.
Why is he sometimes called Jeff?
That Poilievre is the adopted son of two schoolteachers is well known; he references it in his regular rally speeches and elsewhere all the time.
Lawton reveals that one of the reasons Poilievre is sometimes referred to as "Jeff" is because that's the name his birth mother would have given him, if she'd raised him on her own. His adoptive parents also adopted his biological half-brother Patrick. The brothers forged a relationship with their biological mother when they were in their 20s.
His adoptive parents separated when he was 12, and he remains close to his mother Marlene, as well as his father Don and Don's long-time partner Ross McWhirter.
Lawton also shoots down a rumour that has circulated for years that Poilievre's father was sitting in the House of Commons when Poilievre voted against legalizing same-sex marriage and also for a broad reopening of the debate itself.
He eloped to Portugal
Poilievre grew up in Calgary and got involved in politics early - he was so involved that he ultimately dropped out of his bachelor's degree program at the University of Calgary in 2002 to take a job with then-foreign affairs critic Stockwell Day. He was three courses shy at the time, Lawton writes, and eventually took online courses through Athabasca University to finish off his degree in 2008.
His affection for his wife Anaida Poilievre - and the political force she is in her own right - also comes across at campaign rallies, but Lawton's book reveals the early days of their courtship.
She dodged him initially and was miffed he unilaterally set the terms for their very first date in 2012 - at a downtown Ottawa coffee shop Poilievre was able to have access to before it opened to the public. Their first date went so long Poilievre ended up with a parking ticket.
He told her he loved her after only two weeks of dating, and two weeks later wrote her a poem about their first month together. They moved in together shortly after, and would later elope to Portugal in 2017 to get married.
The duo have two children, and Lawton notes that their older daughter, Valentina, has been diagnosed with autism and is non-verbal. She receives therapy, though what kind and from who is not specified, but Lawton reports it has benefitted her.
Known as a prankster
Lawton reports Poilievre likes playing pranks on his own staff and other colleagues, often using foreign accents or mimicking other politicians.
He's posed as a foreign diplomat to call the office of then-Conservative Foreign Affairs Minister John Baird claiming Baird had promised something during a trip abroad and demanding he deliver.
Poilievre, Lawton writes, also once had a woman call his own office to pretend she was pregnant after a one-night stand with him and wanted to track him down to tell him about it.
Poilievre was in a relationship with longtime Conservative strategist Jenni Byrne at the time.
Byrne did speak to Lawton about their relationship for the book - the pair dated for 12 years - but declined to delve into it in detail. The relationship ended, she told Lawton, because they had simply grown apart.
Lawton also attempted to interview people who worked with Poilievre during his time as a junior cabinet minister, but many did not return his calls.
One unnamed source said Poilievre was "insufferable" as a minister, though has grown out of it, while others told Lawton that if people chafe under Poilievre's leadership, it means they simply aren't cut out to work on the Hill. He once told his staff "this is not a self-esteem factory," a phrase that was then turned into a poster and put on the wall of his office.
As party leader, Lawton reports "Poilievre and his team have run a tight ship, with high expectations for attendance and performance in question period. Non-essential absenteeism is forbidden," but Poilievre also ensures caucus has a chance to speak their mind behind closed doors so they have their say, Lawton writes.
He is often his own harshest critic; colleagues recount how he was "sullen and withdrawn" for some time after stepping on his government's 2008 apology for residential schools.
Where did he cut his political teeth?
Lawton traces a long arc for Poilievre's political ideas and ideals all the way back to his childhood - his first political outing, Lawton says was a provincial Progressive Conservative riding association meeting he attended with his mother.
"He enjoyed debate already and sometimes his teachers didn't like that very much," his mother Marlene told Lawton. "Some teachers told me he sat on the edge of his desk and couldn't wait to debate."
As a teen, among his go-to books? The dictionary, Lawton writes. He also began reading about politics and economics.
He tried to join the board of a Calgary riding association, but was rebuffed because it was already full - so they signed him on as a fundraiser, and he would go on to impress with his ability to rake in cash.
He also, Lawton writes, showcased an interest in mastering procedural rules for debates at the Model United Nations, and teaching his team lessons about where to put their hands or how to use body language to make a point.
"Pierre hasn't changed," Lawton quotes one of his schoolmates - someone who works today on Parliament Hill though not for the Tories.
"I think some people think this is a persona, but he is identical to the Pierre I remember from the speech and debate club, model Parliament, and Speakers' Corner."
He's written a book
As an adult, he also once worked on a book - ahead of the 2021 campaign, Poilievre had also been writing something tentatively called "Debtonation," which Lawton describes as a "a meticulously researched, chart-heavy manuscript" meant to explore Canada's current fiscal situation and what needed to happen to - in Poilievre's view - fix it.
Lawton's book includes an excerpt from the never-published manuscript that references the same themes Poilievre hammers on now - removing the gatekeepers and giving people back their freedom.
Lawton also writes about the tensions between Poilievre and the media, quoting Byrne as saying whatever people may think it's not strategy but just "Pierre being Pierre."
But Lawton also quotes current House Leader and former Conservative leader Andrew Scheer as reflecting on why it should be a strategy: Scheer believes the media "concoct and contrive" narratives to hurt Conservatives and help Liberals.
"So at the very least, if we can show that bias to Canadians, then come campaign time, we'll have a fighting chance to get our message through."
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and his wife Anaida walk from a news conference in Quebec City on Sept. 6, 2023.

Subject: Political leadership

Location: Portugal; Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Five things you didn't know about Pierre Poilievre

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 22, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 289 of 500

Ford government must provide better heat protections for workers, critics say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-must-provide-better-heat/docview/3057805179/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+must+provide+better+heat+protections+for+workers%2C+critics+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The early arrival of hot, sticky weather has the NDP and Ontario Federation of Labour pressing Premier Doug Ford's government for stronger "heat stress" protections for workers.
"Ontario summers are getting hotter and hotter leading to unsafe working conditions for many workers," New Democrat MPP Peter Tabuns (Toronto-Danforth) said Wednesday, noting the trend will worsen as climate change accelerates.
Tabuns and OFL president Laura Walton said improved protections are needed for both outdoor and indoor workers in jobs where they are subject to high temperatures, such as in schools without air conditioning or factories where air conditioning is not feasible.
Measures could include access to cooling rooms, cold water and shade, more breaks, and regulated or legislated standards for when workers are entitled to get out of the heat to avoid medical problems that can lead to serious health consequences, such as heat stroke and possibly death.
"This isn't something that can be put off," said Walton, whose federation last year called for heat stress to be recognized as a workplace hazard under the Occupational Health and Safety Act.
She and Tabuns said the Progressive Conservative government conducted a 30-day consultation on heat stress regulation with employers, unions and other stakeholders last August.
"Nothing's happened since," Tabuns said.
In a statement to the Star, the office of Labour Minister David Piccini did not explain why no major new policies on the issue have been developed in the nine months since the consultation.
The minister's spokeswoman pointed to voluntary guidelines issued to employers three years ago.
"Under the Occupational Health and Safety Act (OSHA), employers and supervisors have a duty to take every precaution reasonable in the circumstances for the protection of a worker, including protection in hot environments," said Zoe Knowles.
"The employer should have established a heat stress control plan for the summer to manage job tasks in high temperatures and humidity," she added.
"Any worker in Ontario who feels unsafe should report a violation for the ministry to investigate their complaint, and issue orders if necessary to ensure worker health and safety."
Walton said the government has missed "a really good opportunity over the winter to deal with a situation like this" and develop "real legislation that will protect workers."
In the consultation, the province proposed regulations to protect workers from heat-related illnesses that would require employers to comply with heat stress exposure limits for various types of jobs, from light workloads to heavy ones.
The plan was also to require employers to implement measures to control heat exposures and provide information to workers on how to recognize signs of heat-related health problems and illnesses.
Some unions have been able to negotiate heat stress protections with employers, such as cooling stations and more air-conditioned lunch rooms, said Walton.
Heat stress has garnered attention in recent weeks after Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signed a law preventing cities and counties in a southern state known for its hot, humid weather from enacting heat protections for outdoor workers.
California, Oregon and Washington have adopted requirements that employers provide shade and breaks for workers exposed to high temperatures.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Peter Tabuns is shown in the Ontario legislature on July 23, 2018.

Subject: Heat; Employers; Air conditioning; Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

Business indexing term: Subject: Employers Occupational Health & Safety Act-Ontario

The missing link in Ontario's EV supply chain

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/missing-link-ontarios-ev-supply-chain/docview/3057803882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+missing+link+in+Ontario%27s+EV+supply+chain&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Listen here or subscribe at Apple Podcasts, Spotify or wherever you listen to your favourite podcasts.
Road Trip is back for a surprise bonus episode where I visit the new factory announcement in Port Colborne, Ont. that's filling in the missing link in Ontario's EV supply chain. Plus, I go for a drive in a Tesla with Canada's Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne.
Toronto Star photographer Steve Russell and climate change reporter Marco Chown Oved went on a road trip through northern Ontario in the dead of winter. It was cold, blizzarding and icy. They travelled 2,300 km during the coldest week of the year. And they did it in an electric vehicle (EV).

Document 291 of 500

We'll all lose if Doug Ford wages a war on drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/well-all-lose-if-doug-ford-wages-war-on-drugs/docview/3057803106/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We%27ll+all+lose+if+Doug+Ford+wages+a+war+on+drugs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's no great surprise that Ottawa has quashed Toronto's bid to decriminalize drugs. Yes, there is strong evidence that criminalization is a significant barrier to fighting addiction and reducing its many downstream costs; that people addicted to drugs won't seek the help they need if they feel they will be punished for doing so; that as long as they avoid such help, the cost will be not just to them, but also to the health care system, the justice system and public safety. But with a provincial government loudly opposed to decriminalization, and the federal Liberals' appetite for political risk at an all-time low, Toronto's timing could hardly have been worse.
For those with the power to decide, the example of British Columbia's experiment with decriminalization was surely impossible to ignore. While the precise effects of B.C.'s short-lived policy will take time to fully emerge, the costs - both human and political - were clear. As drug use became increasingly evident in the streets, and public overdoses spiked, many British Columbians complained of a sense of unease and disorder in their communities, pushing the province's NDP government to modify its approach.
It may well be true, as many advocates have argued, that British Columbia did not give the policy its best chance to succeed by, for instance, making the necessary investments in housing and anti-poverty measures that are widely seen as prerequisites for progress. But in any case, if Toronto's push to decriminalize ever had a chance, B.C.'s experience no doubt killed it.
Ottawa and Queen's Park decided the risks were too big to ignore. The question now is whether the province will take as seriously the well-documented risks of over-criminalization or whether it will once again wage a misguided war on drugs.
It can be hard to tell where Doug Ford stands on this. Over the years he has intermixed punitive bluster with notes of compassion. While running for office in 2018, he said he was "dead against" supervised consumption sites, but added that he believed "in supporting people, getting them help."
Stressing the need for treatment is common among governments that oppose decriminalization and other harm reduction measures. Less common, however, is actually providing that help.
Ontario is a case in point. Six years after Ford first pledged to help those struggling with addictions, that help remains elusive for many. Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow noted that wait times for residential treatment "can be as long as a year."
With decriminalization off the table, how will the province ensure adequate treatment services? How will it eliminate barriers that impede access to those services? In a pre-decriminalization survey in British Columbia, illicit drug users cited fear of criminal sanctions as the number one reason they were hesitant to seek help. Obviously one goal of decriminalization is to allay that fear. In the absence of such a policy, other harm-reduction measures like supervised consumption sites and safer supply become even more important.
Several studies, in both B.C. and Ontario, have shown that safe-injection sites and safe supply of drugs are the most effective ways of addressing drug addiction and its diverse harms. Despite this evidence, Ford has been less than enthusiastic about harm reduction. In keeping with his 2018 comments about supervised consumption facilities, Ford paused approval of new sites pending review, and at least one existing site has closed as a result. On the heels of Ottawa's rejection of decriminalization, Ford wrote to the feds asking "to immediately stop approving new [safer supply] sites, and conduct a formal review of existing ones in the province."
We have seen that some of the concerns around decriminalization also apply to safe-injection sites. These facilities have in some cases created a sense of unease in communities that must be managed, and certainly not glibly dismissed. But nor should fear alone dictate the government's direction. Nor should the province dismiss the ample evidence that, despite the trade-offs, harm reduction is an important tool in fighting addiction, reducing the burden it puts on society and public coffers and enhancing public safety.
Ford got his way on decriminalization. But he and his province will lose if he lurches in the other direction. If Ford is truly interested in getting people help, if he truly wants to take on the epidemic of drug addiction and the resulting unease in the streets, and if he truly wants a drug policy that promotes public safety, he should remember that drug users won't seek the treatment they need if they think doing so will get them in trouble.
CAPTION: Six years after Doug Ford first pledged to help those struggling with addictions, that help remains elusive for many.

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug addiction; Criminalization; Public safety; Harm reduction

Location: Canada; British Columbia Canada

Title: We'll all lose if Doug Ford wages a war on drugs

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 22, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 292 of 500

Mixed reactions to Oakville fourplexes and police parked in bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mixed-reactions-oakville-fourplexes-police-parked/docview/3057803105/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mixed+reactions+to+Oakville+fourplexes+and+police+parked+in+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mixed reaction to Oakville fourplexes...
Federal minister threatens to pull housing funding after Oakville reneges on promises, May 18
There is no "livability" or "affordability" in many Oakville neighbourhoods. In areas with small bungalows or split levels, usually dating several decades back but still perfectly serviceable, many of the houses put up for sale are grabbed by a developer who immediately bulldozers it and sets to building a mansion. It's a manic process which turns formerly quiet neighbourhoods into construction zones. To speak of "livability," as the mayor does, with that much noise and air pollution, is a sick joke. It's an environmental travesty too, considering the carbon burden of cement. Those older houses exacted their carbon costs long ago, but in livability-ville the environmental burden of house construction has to be paid all over again, usually with double the square footage. It is better to build fourplexes. They would take up no more space and serve the public good much more than faux castles.
John Goyder, Oakville
Oh me, oh my! I feel for the Oakvillians having to consider risking their precious neighbourhoods if they accept the measly $25 million from the federal government to encourage building some affordable housing for the struggling people of Ontario. Heaven forbid they allow fourplexes to land on their doorsteps, which would so spoil their pristine existence. And little wonder they are worried because they have the highest house sale average across the GTA, not to mention their average income compared to the rest of Ontario. When Mayor Rob Burton says such builds are a "risk to our neighbourhood's livability," he is expressing an extremely snobbish sentiment, speaking for those in the community more concerned about the riff-raff that would supposedly come with these ghastly fourplexes than the fact that Ontario badly needs such buildings.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
While the Liberal politicians sit in Ottawa contemplating how to fix Canada's housing problems, residents of the GTA deal daily with the stark reality that the Greater Toronto Area is full. Be it the traffic, the condition of our roads, our overburdened health care system, our schools struggling to teach so many children without extra resources, our court system which cannot keep up, or our housing where students crowd into one basement apartment, we are full. We are a very large country but almost half the swelling number of new Canadians seek a home in our area. Thank you to the mayor of Oakville for saying no to fourplexes. Oakville is just too dense. We need more of our local leaders to do just that.
Stephen Handler, Mississauga
Finally Ottawa is bypassing Premier Doug Ford and placing affordable housing funds where they must go, directly to municipalities. It's pathetic to see Ford calling the move "unacceptable" and "jurisdictional creep." Maybe it's time to review previous program funding sent to the Ford government to ensure it was spent as directed by Ottawa.
Chris Andrews, Vaughan
... and to police in bike lanes
How a Toronto police complaints officer belittled a criticism about bike lanes, May 17
I enjoyed the article about the cyclist complaining about cops parking in bike lanes. I enjoyed even more the response the person got from the police. It's about time that people got honest responses instead of feel-good PR hype. People expect the police to respond to their calls and expect delivery of their must-have packages from wherever but feel these vehicles should not park to fulfil their duties. Where do these people think these vehicles should park downtown? Just another case of the pervasive "me first" attitude in our society today.
Doug Fisher, Toronto
The Toronto police response to the complaint of their cruisers parked in bike lanes misses the mark by at least a car width. It's not that cyclists can't see their giant police emblazoned cruisers and SUVs. It's that other drivers aren't expecting cyclists to suddenly swerve out of the bike lane to get around their monster trucks. Being safe on a bicycle in traffic is dependent on being expected and consistent. Swerving around illegal parkers, police or otherwise, is not expected and puts cyclists at great risk.
Brian Huntley, Toronto
Supt. Stacy Clarke should resign
Role model? Insider who led Black cops to 'career suicide'? The many narratives of the Toronto police Supt. Stacy Clarke cheating scandal, May 21
Supt. Stacy Clarke is a disgrace to the Toronto Police Service. Attempting to deflect her abhorrent behaviour and point blame elsewhere is shameful and unacceptable. The most important attribute any police officer has is their credibility. Without that you are of little use to any policing organization. While the Toronto Police Service is far from perfect, it does not deserve the kind of criticism that Clarke has directed at it.
Rod Perrin, Mississauga
While Supt. Stacy Clarke has pleaded guilty to seven counts of police misconduct, what about the six officers given the questions? They were complicit in accepting the exam questions. Apparently, they have been given unit-level discipline. Has justice really been served here?
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
As we await the discipline for Supt. Stacy Clarke, certain facts remain without dispute. She broke the rules. As a last act of honour, Supt. Clarke should now resign.
Jeff Stephan
Thankful for my carbon rebate
We just received our latest rebate of $210 from Trudeau's government. I drive a plug-in hybrid SUV which I fill up about once a month. This rebate is worth almost four months of driving for me. My wife previously had a Prius C, which our daughter now drives. It used about $60 per month so this would pay for over three months of gas for that vehicle. It pays, it seems, to give the planet a break for our children and grandchildren!
James Richards, Georgetown
CAPTION: "We don't need to risk our neighbourhoods livability for the small amount in the housing accelerator fund grant," said Mayor Rob Burton at the council meeting where he brought in the motion to reject the gentle density policies.

Subject: Neighborhoods; Affordable housing; Carbon

Business indexing term: Subject: Affordable housing

Title: Mixed reactions to Oakville fourplexes and police parked in bike lanes

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 22, 2024

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 294 of 500

Liberals, NDP defend Speaker: Conservatives, Bloc Québécois called on Fergus to step down over alleged partisan behaviour

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-ndp-defend-speaker/docview/3057618659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%2C+NDP+defend+Speaker%3A+Conservatives%2C+Bloc+Qu%26eacute%3Bb%26eacute%3Bcois+called+on+Fergus+to+step+down+over+alleged+partisan+behaviour&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal government and its NDP parliamentary allies are standing by Liberal MP Greg Fergus, who faced renewed calls to resign as Speaker of the House of Commons on Tuesday over allegations of inappropriate partisan behaviour.
The Conservatives raised what they described as just the latest issue with Fergus, arguing a June 4 political event organized by his Liberal riding association was advertised in an overly partisan tone that attacked their leader, Pierre Poilievre, for his "reckless policies."
The Bloc Québécois also objected to how the event was publicized, with the party's House leader declaring Fergus should resign because he "doesn't understand" how to keep out of the partisan fray.
But Fergus's office said Tuesday that the federal Liberal party posted about the event using "generic text" that the Speaker's team did not approve in advance, while Fergus's riding association used non-partisan language in its own notice to invite his constituents to the "community BBQ." Azam Ishmael, the Liberal party's national director, also said the language was posted automatically due to a misunderstanding and apologized for what he called a "mistake" on Tuesday.
Meanwhile, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters the Liberal government still has confidence in Fergus as Speaker, and the opposition NDP - which is propping up Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority administration in a parliamentary alliance - said Fergus did nothing wrong.
Peter Julian, the NDP's House leader, said it's the Liberal party - and not Fergus - that should apologize for the language advertising the event, and noted that Fergus's office also said Tuesday that the Clerk of the House of Commons raised "no concerns" when asked whether the event was within parliamentary rules.
Asked how long the House can function if two opposition parties - the Conservatives and the Bloc - want the Speaker to resign, Julian said the NDP is "not going to play games every day with the Conservatives trying to raise this constantly." He also said the NDP wants to focus on more important priorities during the final weeks before Parliament goes on summer break.
"The Speaker has a role to play and we'd like him to continue to play," he said.
Asked for other examples of when the Liberal party used the "generic text" that was in the original notice for Fergus's event, party spokesperson Parker Lund provided a link to one event on May 30 described with the same language.
Earlier in the House, Conservative MP Chris Warkentin asked the Speaker's office to make an official declaration on the party's allegations that Fergus is too partisan to hold the job as arbiter of debate in Canada's seat of democracy.
He also penned a letter to Fergus directly, demanding that he step down because of his "partisan behaviour," that included tribute video in which he appeared in his Speaker's robes and praised the outgoing interim leader of the Ontario Liberals.
That incident sparked calls for his resignation last year, with the Conservatives and Bloc Québécois declaring they no longer had confidence in his ability to serve as Speaker. Fergus apologized for the video and repaid the parliamentary resources used to make it.
When Fergus later presided over debate in the chamber, he was heckled a couple times with calls to resign from opposition benches, but was otherwise not challenged.
In his letter, Warkentin argued that the latest event advertisement provides another reason why Fergus should step down, and he called on the NDP to reverse its support for the current Speaker. He cited House of Commons rules that say the Speaker should abstain from "all partisan political activity" and be careful, when running for re-election under a party banner, to avoid statements that "might prejudice their perceived impartiality.
"A Speaker cannot credibly claim to be objectively policing debate in the House of Commons by day while holding partisan attack rallies against the Leader of the Opposition by night," Warkentin wrote in his letter.
"Every ruling you give now - and, with hindsight, every ruling you have given - will now all be assumed to have been delivered with a red tint."
Therrien, the Bloc House Leader, told the chamber Tuesday that debates are becoming more "acrimonious," meaning the House needs a Speaker in whom all parties can have confidence.
He called it "bad casting" to put Fergus - with his years of devoted Liberal partisanship - in the Speaker's chair.
"We must understand that he doesn't have his place on that seat," Therrien said in French.
With files from Stephanie Levitz and Mark Ramzy
CAPTION: The federal Conservatives are arguing a June 4 political event organized by House Speaker Greg Fergus's Liberal riding association was advertised in an overly partisan tone that attacked their leader, Pierre Poilievre, for his "reckless policies."Adrian WyldTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star With files from Stephanie Levitz and Mark Ramzy

Document 295 of 500

We all lose if Ford wages a war on drugs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/we-all-lose-if-ford-wages-war-on-drugs/docview/3057618499/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+all+lose+if+Ford+wages+a+war+on+drugs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's no great surprise that Ottawa has quashed Toronto's bid to decriminalize drugs. Yes, there is strong evidence that criminalization is a significant barrier to fighting addiction and reducing its many downstream costs; that people addicted to drugs won't seek the help they need if they feel they will be punished for doing so; that as long as they avoid such help, the cost will be not just to them, but also to the health care system, the justice system and public safety. But with a provincial government loudly opposed to decriminalization, and the federal Liberals' appetite for political risk at an all-time low, Toronto's timing could hardly have been worse.
For those with the power to decide, the example of British Columbia's experiment with decriminalization was surely impossible to ignore. While the precise effects of B.C.'s short-lived policy will take time to fully emerge, the costs - both human and political - were clear. As drug use became increasingly evident in the streets, and public overdoses spiked, many British Columbians complained of a sense of unease and disorder in their communities, pushing the province's NDP government to modify its approach.
It may well be true, as many advocates have argued, that British Columbia did not give the policy its best chance to succeed by, for instance, making the necessary investments in housing and anti-poverty measures that are widely seen as prerequisites for progress. But in any case, if Toronto's push to decriminalize ever had a chance, B.C.'s experience no doubt killed it.
Ottawa and Queen's Park decided the risks were too big to ignore. The question now is whether the province will take as seriously the well-documented risks of over-criminalization or whether it will once again wage a misguided war on drugs.
It can be hard to tell where Doug Ford stands on this. Over the years he has intermixed punitive bluster with notes of compassion. While running for office in 2018, he said he was "dead against" supervised consumption sites, but added that he believed "in supporting people, getting them help."
Stressing the need for treatment is common among governments that oppose decriminalization and other harm reduction measures. Less common, however, is actually providing that help.
Ontario is a case in point. Six years after Ford first pledged to help those struggling with addictions, that help remains elusive for many. Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow noted that wait times for residential treatment "can be as long as a year."
With decriminalization off the table, how will the province ensure adequate treatment services? How will it eliminate barriers that impede access to those services? In a pre-decriminalization survey in British Columbia, illicit drug users cited fear of criminal sanctions as the number one reason they were hesitant to seek help. Obviously one goal of decriminalization is to allay that fear. In the absence of such a policy, other harm-reduction measures like supervised consumption sites and safer supply become even more important.
Several studies, in both B.C. and Ontario, have shown that safe-injection sites and safe supply of drugs are the most effective ways of addressing drug addiction and its diverse harms. Despite this evidence, Ford has been less than enthusiastic about harm reduction. In keeping with his 2018 comments about supervised consumption facilities, Ford paused approval of new sites pending review, and at least one existing site has closed as a result. On the heels of Ottawa's rejection of decriminalization, Ford wrote to the feds asking "to immediately stop approving new (safer supply) sites, and conduct a formal review of existing ones in the province."
We have seen that some of the concerns around decriminalization also apply to safe-injection sites. These facilities have in some cases created a sense of unease in communities that must be managed, and certainly not glibly dismissed. But nor should fear alone dictate the government's direction. Nor should the province dismiss the ample evidence that, despite the trade-offs, harm reduction is an important tool in fighting addiction, reducing the burden it puts on society and public coffers and enhancing public safety.
Ford got his way on decriminalization. But he and his province will lose if he lurches in the other direction. If Ford is truly interested in getting people help, if he truly wants to take on the epidemic of drug addiction and the resulting unease in the streets, and if he truly wants a drug policy that promotes public safety, he should remember that drug users won't seek the treatment they need if they think doing so will get them in trouble.
CAPTION: Six years after Ontario Premier Doug Ford first pledged to help those struggling with addiction, help remains elusive for many.Christopher KatsarovThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Decriminalization; Criminalization; Drug addiction; Public safety; Harm reduction

Document 296 of 500

PCs dip but still ahead of Liberals, NDP: Survey shows rise in number of undecided voters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-dip-still-ahead-liberals-ndp/docview/3057618455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+dip+but+still+ahead+of+Liberals%2C+NDP%3A+Survey+shows+rise+in+number+of+undecided+voters&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have dipped slightly in the latest Abacus Data poll, but still enjoy a healthy lead over the Liberals and New Democrats.
In the Abacus monthly tracking survey for the Star, Ford's Tories were at 39 per cent to 26 per cent for Bonnie Crombie's Liberals, 22 per cent for Marit Stiles's New Democrats and nine per cent for Mike Schreiner's Greens.
"The new normal is they're hovering around the high 30s and low 40s," Abacus president David Coletto said Tuesday.
Last month, the Tories were at 41 per cent, the Liberals 25 per cent, the NDP 21 per cent and Greens seven per cent.
"Ford's ability to deflect and to dodge and to change positions so easily is allowing him to continue to do well despite what other incumbent governments are facing," said Coletto, referring to recent polling results for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals who trail Pierre Poilievre's federal Conservatives.
Unlike some other politicians who are pilloried for policy U-turns, voters appear to give Ford a pass when he alters course - as was the case with the aborted Greenbelt land swap fiasco now being investigated by the RCMP.
"That tag doesn't stick to him. It's not seen as flip-flopping, it's seen as being responsive."
Abacus surveyed 1,000 Ontarians from May 10 until last Wednesday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll found a jump in the undecided vote.
When asked for their "current provincial vote intention," 28 per cent of poll respondents said Tory, 19 per cent Liberal, 16 per cent NDP, seven per cent Green, three per cent another party while 28 per cent were undecided.
That compares with an undecided vote of 21 per cent last month.
"It shows a lot of people in the province aren't thinking that much about who their preference is," the pollster said.
Coletto pointed out that the governing party's byelection wins in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex on May 2 support the poll's findings.
"The byelection results took all of the air out of any momentum Bonnie Crombie may have had after the (Dec. 2 Liberal) leadership," he said.
An onslaught of PC attack ads criticizing Crombie and burnishing Ford also appears to be helping the Tories and hurting the Liberals.
Asked for their impressions of the four party leaders, 31 per cent of respondents said they had a positive impression of Ford while 39 per cent had a negative impression with 24 per cent neutral and five per cent didn't know enough to say.
That's a net impression rating for the premier of minus eight per cent.
Crombie was 27 per cent positive, 27 per cent negative, 27 per cent neutral and 10 per cent unsure for a zero net rating.
Stiles had a 27 per cent positive, 21 per cent negative, 31 per cent neutral and 22 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus six per cent.
Schreiner had a 23 per cent positive, 18 per cent negative, 35 per cent neutral and 24 per cent unsure for a net rating of plus five per cent.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Polls & surveys; Negative campaigning

Ford's Tories dip slightly but still lead Liberals and NDP, survey shows

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-tories-dip-slightly-still-lead-liberals-ndp/docview/3058298572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Tories+dip+slightly+but+still+lead+Liberals+and+NDP%2C+survey+shows&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 298 of 500

Opponents of Willowdale supportive housing project again fail to stop construction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opponents-willowdale-supportive-housing-project/docview/3058298435/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opponents+of+Willowdale+supportive+housing+project+again+fail+to+stop+construction&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An Ontario court has rejected the latest attempt to stop the construction of supportive housing in Willowdale for people escaping homelessness.
The Superior Court of Justice has refused to hear an appeal of a January decision by the Ontario Land Tribunal that seemed to finally pave the way for the city's long-delayed three-storey building at 175 Cummer Avenue. The project will provide 59 units of deeply affordable rental homes, with on-site supports such as mental and physical health care, as a stepping stone toward fully independent living.
The court gave no reasons and did not award legal costs to the City of Toronto which successfully argued that the tribunal decision should stand.
In a statement, the city said: "Almost 11,000 people are experiencing homelessness in Toronto and new safe, affordable homes with supports are urgently needed across the city to ensure everyone has a place to call home. The City is currently reviewing plans to proceed with on-site construction at 175 Cummer Avenue in the near future."
First endorsed by council in March 2021, the proposed development was among a series of supportive housing projects city hall aimed to expedite - in part, by using modular construction where pieces are built elsewhere and assembled on site, as well as by requesting a ministerial zoning order from the province to fast-track the necessary rezoning.
The Cummer site, on green space adjacent to a Toronto Community Housing Corp. seniors' building, was announced the same day as a similar supportive project on Cedarvale Boulevard in East York that initially met fierce community opposition but has been operating successfully since 2022.
Some Willowdale residents, politicians and developers have fiercely fought the Cummer project, arguing the site is unsuitable and that its residents could pose a threat to the neighbouring seniors.
The city undertook the normal rezoning process but then faced a challenge at the Ontario Land Tribunal, which sided with the city, calling the project "good planning" that gives options to people facing homelessness. It was an attempt to challenge the tribunal's ruling in court, by the Bayview Cummer Neighbourhood Association, LiVante Holdings (Cummer) Inc. and a local group called Voices of Willowdale, that the Superior Court has now rejected.
Eric Gillespie, the lawyer representing the project's opponents, had faulted the tribunal's interpretation of what constitutes a "walk-up apartment" as well as a finding that the tribunal could not ensure that a portion of the land will house only seniors.
Gillespie, in an email to the Star on Tuesday, said it appears that the court may not have seen an issue of law to argue in the tribunal's decision.
"However, a recent case has said that if a question of law is not raised cases are still judicially reviewable," he wrote. "As a result, we filed the request for judicial review within the required 30 days and will now speak with our clients about whether they want to proceed with that application."
When seeking judicial review, a party asks a three-judge panel of the Divisional Court to change or set aside a decision of an administrative body where the party can show an error was made that warrants action by the court.
Mark Richardson, a member of the volunteer group HousingNowTO, said enough is enough, it's time to build the Cummer project.
"It's been over a thousand days since this site was originally approved by Toronto city council," he said.
"This is a perfect example of how neighbourhood cranks with time and money and access to legal services can block a small-scale affordable housing project,'" Richardson said. "We're about six or seven months from the first snowfall of 2024 - the city and its agencies should focus like a laser on ensuring this project is completed so people can move in before Christmas."
The Star could not reach John Melinte, president of the Bayview Cummer Neighbourhood Association, by publication time.
With files from Victoria Gibson
CAPTION: Council approved the construction of 59 supportive homes on this Cummer Avenue site in March 2021, but they still haven't been built.

Subject: Councils; Neighborhoods; Affordable housing; Judicial reviews

Document 299 of 500

Conservatives, BQ demand resignation of House Speaker over 'partisan behaviour'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/conservatives-bq-demand-resignation-house-speaker/docview/3057220920/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Conservatives%2C+BQ+demand+resignation+of+House+Speaker+over+%27partisan+behaviour%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Opposition Conservatives and the Bloc Québécois are renewing calls for Liberal MP Greg Fergus to resign as Speaker of the House of Commons, alleging he has engaged in a pattern of partisan behaviour that taints his decisions as the arbiter of debate in Canada's seat of democracy.
Chris Warkentin, a Conservative MP from Alberta, rose in the House on Tuesday morning to raise concerns over the online description of a partisan Liberal event Fergus is slated to host on June 4 near Parliament Hill.
Alain Therrien, the Bloc's House Leader, echoed Warkentin in arguing Fergus "doesn't understand" how to keep out of the partisan fray, and that he should resign as Speaker.
The original notice advertising the event included allegations about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's "reckless policies," which Warkentin argued were inappropriate in light of how Fergus recently ejected Poilievre from the House for called Prime Minister Justin Trudeau a "wacko" and refusing to unequivocally withdraw the comment.
As of late Tuesday morning, the Liberal party's website was still advertising the event, but without the partisan language that Warkentin had quoted.
In an email to the Star, Fergus's office said it didn't know the Liberal party had advertised the event using "generic text" that had not been approved by the Speaker's team. His office also consulted the Clerk of the House of Commons about the event, and the clerk raised "no concerns" since it's within the rules for the Hull-Aylmer MP riding to organize events to help him get re-elected, wrote Mathieu Gravel, a spokesperson in Fergus's office.
The Speaker's team asked the Liberal party to remove the post, Gravel said, while Fergus's riding association used non-partisan language in its own notice to invite his constituents to the "community BBQ," Gravel said.
Asked about the issue on Tuesday, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said the Liberal government still has confidence in Fergus as Speaker.
But for the Conservatives, it was another troubling example of what they allege is Fergus's inappropriate partisanship.
In a letter to Fergus dated Tuesday, Warkentin said it was just the latest example of Fergus's questionable "partisan behaviour," which also included a tribute video in which he appeared in his Speaker's robes and praised the outgoing interim leader of the Ontario Liberals. That incident sparked calls for his resignation last year, with the Conservatives and Bloc Québécois declaring they no longer had confidence in his ability to serve as Speaker.
Fergus apologized for the video and repaid the parliamentary resources used to make it.
Warkentin argued Tuesday that the latest event advertisement provides another reason why Fergus should step down, and he called on the NDP to reverse its support for Fergus and join the Tories and BQ in declaring non-confidence in him if he does not resign. He cited House of Commons rules that say the Speaker should abstain from "all partisan political activity" and be careful, when running for re-election under a party banner, to avoid statements that "might prejudice their perceived impartiality.
"A Speaker cannot credibly claim to be objectively policing debate in the House of Commons by day while holding partisan attack rallies against the Leader of the Opposition by night," said the letter, which was obtained by the Star.
"Every ruling you give now - and, with hindsight, every ruling you have given - will now all be assumed to have been delivered with a red tint."
Heather McPherson, an NDP MP from Edmonton, said her party is indeed "very concerned" with the situation, but that it would "reserve" judgment "until some point in the future.
"It's important for us to take the time to look at this very clearly," she said.
Therrien, the Bloc House Leader, told the chamber Tuesday that debates are becoming more "acrimonious," meaning the House needs a Speaker in whom all parties can have confidence.
He called it "bad casting" to put Fergus - with his years of devoted Liberal partisanship - in the Speaker's chair.
"We must understand that he doesn't have his place on that seat," Therrien said in French.
With files from Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Speaker Greg Fergus is shown in the House of Commons on April 17, 2024.

Subject: Partisanship; Prime ministers

Ottawa unveils national strategy: Plan includes more police intelligence sharing, Criminal Code changes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-unveils-national-strategy/docview/3057038253/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+unveils+national+strategy%3A+Plan+includes+more+police+intelligence+sharing%2C+Criminal+Code+changes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ramped-up intelligence sharing among police forces and a working group involving all levels of government are among the measures Ottawa unveiled Monday as part of a broader national strategy to combat auto theft.
A slew of senior federal cabinet ministers, including Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland and Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc, released details of the National Action Plan at a rare holiday Monday news conference in Brampton. The joint announcement came months after Ottawa convened a summit to address what government and law enforcement officials both describe as a complex and growing international concern.
"We need everybody to come to the table and work together with practical solutions," LeBlanc said at the news conference. "And that's exactly what's happening."
The federal government estimates 90,000 cars are stolen each year in Canada, and says many of the thefts involve connections to organized crime.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada paid out around $1.5 billion in claims in 2023, a record high. That year, vehicle thefts rose by 50 per cent in Quebec and almost as much in Ontario.
Insurers have said the majority of vehicles are being stolen from Ontario and Quebec and exported through the busy Port of Montreal. Around 1.7 million containers moved through the port last year, including 70 per cent of Canada's legal vehicle exports, according to port authorities.
Several of the measures touted in the new strategy were previously announced either around the time of the February summit or in this year's federal budget. That included $28 million meant to help prevent cars from being shipped abroad by giving the Canada Border Services Agency more capacity to detect and search containers.
The budget included details on amendments the Liberals propose to make to the country's criminal code, which stemmed from feedback received at the summit.
The government has proposed new charges for the use of violence while stealing a vehicle and for links to organized crime, as well as laundering money for the benefit of a criminal organization.
They're also planning to make it illegal to own or sell the electronic and digital devices police say are being commonly used to steal cars. A new aggravating factor will be created for thieves who involve a youth in their operation.
"This is about taking the tools away from people who are committing these crimes, making us unsafe," said Freeland.
The new strategy calls for shipment container inspections to expand even further, including deploying scanning and detection technology beyond ports in places like rail yards.
It suggests the RCMP will be asked to teach courses on auto theft techniques at the Canadian Police College, while the Mounties, CBSA and local police forces will be required to launch a "GPS tracking protocol" that victims and law enforcement can use to help find missing vehicles.
It also says Public Safety Canada will lead a working group involving all provincial and territorial governments, a move LeBlanc said will ensure everyone remains at the table focused on finding practical solutions to tackle the issue.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland led several federal ministers during a Monday news conference announcing new measures Ottawa is taking to tackle auto theft.Cole BurstonTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Alessia Passafiume The Canadian Press

Subject: Press conferences; Organized crime; Theft; Law enforcement; Working groups; Public safety; Robbery; Vehicles

Document 301 of 500

Here's what I learned from my conversation with Justin Trudeau: There's only one way to fix Canada and everyone is going to hate it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-what-i-learned-my-conversation-with-justin/docview/3057038191/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what+I+learned+from+my+conversation+with+Justin+Trudeau%3A+There%27s+only+one+way+to+fix+Canada+and+everyone+is+going+to+hate+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I was not a big believer in re-opening the constitution, until Prime Minister Justin Trudeau convinced me otherwise.
It's not that Trudeau thinks this is a good idea. He doesn't. He believes his own steering of the machinery of government is going very well, thank you, even if few in the country seem to agree with him.
But over an hour-long conversation in March, it became obvious to me that many of our most endemic problems - rock-bottom trust, jurisdictional knife-fights, arbitrary suspensions of basic liberties - flow back to the documents which lay out how we govern ourselves.
And the worst solution, unfortunately, is the best one. We have to reopen the constitution. We have to finally solve the problem Trudeau's father left us in 1982.
Trench wars
Like most Canadians, I don't relish the prospect of returning to the political trench wars that defined the last two attempts to fix our highest law. In fact, the idea makes my scalp itch. At a time when the problems facing this country are so big, so numerous, I know it feels like trying to rewrite the Charter of Rights and Freedoms could be seen as a time-wasting side-quest.
But it is entirely because of those problems that I believe that some kind of structural renovation must be done. We are living under a constitution designed for a different country in a different world, one that doesn't do nearly enough to address the needs of cities and First Nations, that shunts the hard jobs to the courts, and that has left us stuck in a bottomless pit of federal/provincial feuding.
So it isn't a question anymore of fixing our problems or fixing the constitution. We have to fix our problems by fixing the constitution, at least in part, no matter how difficult and frustrating that process will be.
When I talked to Trudeau, for a feature in the Walrus magazine, I was trying to figure out why his oft-repeated promises to build new housing kept coming up short. Waving away the failure, he spun an old axiom: "If you want to run fast, run alone. If you want to run far, run together." Unfortunately, he said, some of his running partners have been "more challenging" than others.
Blaming others for his shortcomings was a common thread through the interview, and here he was laying blame at the feet of the provinces who, apparently, weren't playing along. I asked him whether that didn't belie a deeper problem with our federation. After all, he had had eight years and the federal-provincial conflict had only gotten worse.
"You forget the most important part about democracy," he replied in the breathy tone he reserves for points he considers especially weighty. "People can vote the way they want to."
A broken machine
That is true. But people don't vote for dysfunction, at least not on purpose. People vote for things to get better. They vote for things to work. And right now, they don't, and more voting won't solve that.
We like to say our system is cooperative federation: A big machine that can ingest a climate crusading prime minister and a climate skeptic premier, for example, and spit out some kind of functioning policy on climate change. This is a system designed to work despite, maybe even because of, discordant politics at different levels of government. We drew up divisions of power to settle who would have the final say on these conflicts.
But that system doesn't work anymore. Canadians have consistently voted for federal parties that support a national carbon price. The Supreme Court ruled that imposing such a price is within the federal mandate. And yet, Saskatchewan has opted to defy that law. Canadians have also consistently voted for housing action. And yet Ottawa's urbanist plans have found an enemy in suburb-loving Doug Ford, among other premiers.
The provinces have in fact grown so hostile to federal programming that every significant federal plan now goes through years of expensive legal wrangling, as the Supreme Court tries to interpret how our aging constitution interacts with everything from climate change to the internet, reconciliation, and globalization. That squabbling is why Canada can't have a national securities regulator, an embarrassing rarity in the rich world, nor a national environmental assessment program.
Say what you want, but nobody is voting for this kind of balkanization. And the more things get jammed up, the more people lose faith in the system itself.
So Trudeau can say voters choose this all he wants. There is no vote that can fix this rusty engine. The problems are too structural. And if they plague Trudeau, they will torment his successor, whoever that might be.
I'm not the only one who believes constitutional overhaul is overdue in Canada. "If you really want to change the rules of the game," Kent Roach, a University of Toronto constitutional law professor, told me recently. "You have to change the constitution."
Our aging 19th century constitution, he says, "contemplates two levels of government, where we really have four" - federal, provincial, municipal and Indigenous.
"It defies belief," Roach points out. "That most people experience Indigenous and municipal governments as real things, but under the 1867 constitutions they are simply creations of the federal and provincial governments."
And those problems aren't just academic. I spoke to one former big city mayor last year who warned me Canada's cities are in much worse shape than anyone realizes. They were ravaged by the pandemic, then savaged by post-pandemic weirdness. Without a mid-pandemic federal bailout, the mayor said, things would have fallen apart.
Our constitution doesn't give cities, like Toronto, the powers to tax citizens or incur debt. They are instead creatures of the provinces that contain them. As long as that remains the case, they will be forever on the brink of going broke, even as they try to solve some of the country's most intractable problems.
The situation for Indigenous governments is, if anything, worse. David Lametti, the former justice minister, told me recently that revising the constitution to enshrine Indigenous self-governance isn't just advisable, it's inevitable. Despite Ottawa's professed love for a nation-to-nation relationship, First Nations have consistently had to go to court to address obvious and systemic under-resourcing. People in government now talk about the cost of this litigation in terms of billions of dollars.
There has been some good progress on issues like child welfare, but Indigenous peoples are tired of knocking on Parliament's door to rectify longstanding injustices.
A constitution that recognizes municipal and Indigenous governments as equal partners in the federation is one real, tangible way of solving some of our country's jurisdictional nightmares. But it's not the only thing a constitutional revision could fix.
Hitting eject just for kicks
When we built our current legal vehicle, back in the 1980s, a last-minute deal installed the Notwithstanding Clause, the constitutional equivalent of an ejector seat.
As Trudeau himself noted, the consensus on the clause - an override for other parts of the Charter - was at the time of its creation that "the cost of that, politically, would be prohibitive for anyone" to pay. And yet, here we are and people are paying it.
Today, some provinces are hitting eject just for kicks. Saskatchewan is using the clause to bully transgender kids at school, Ontario wielded it to pass a political advertising law, and Quebec has used it to tell religious minorities how to dress on the job. Pierre Poilievre even wants to use it to pass cruel, inhumane, ineffective, unconstitutional criminal sentencing laws.
Trudeau's strategy has been to leave it to the courts, hoping against hope that they would find some ingenious way to reinstall the glass box marked "DO NOT PRESS." This is a legal fantasy. The only way to uninstall this constitutional vestige - or, at least, to make it harder to use - is to amend the constitution.
Nothing about this country is easy, as we all know by living in it. But if things were always easy, we wouldn't need government. And if governments always worked, we wouldn't need constitutions. And if constitutions were timeless and infallible, we wouldn't need to update them.
As Roach puts it: "It is an exercise in self-government to think about these things every 20 or so years."
I did, of course, raise the prospect of tuning up our constitutional plumbing to the prime minister. He didn't like it.
"I'm not going to start a constitutional nonstarter," Trudeau told me.
Maybe it's time for somebody else to do it instead.
CAPTION: Queen Elizabeth II signs Canada's constitutional proclamation as Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau looks on, in Ottawa, April 17, 1982."We are living under a constitution designed for a different country in a different world, one that doesn't do nearly enough to address the needs of cities and First Nations, that shunts the hard jobs to the courts, and that has left us stuck in a bottomless pit of federal/provincial feuding," writes Justin Ling.
CREDIT: Justin Ling

Subject: Constitutions; Constitutional law; Climate change; Prime ministers; Employment; Native North Americans; Pandemics; Government

Business indexing term: Subject: Employment

Location: Saskatchewan Canada; Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

A constitutional overhaul is overdue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/constitutional-overhaul-is-overdue/docview/3057037963/se-2?accountid=15090

investigative journalist
based in Montreal.

Document 303 of 500

Ottawa's refusal renews debate on drugs: Some experts cast doubt on city's bid for decriminalization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawas-refusal-renews-debate-on-drugs/docview/3057037563/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa%27s+refusal+renews+debate+on+drugs%3A+Some+experts+cast+doubt+on+city%27s+bid+for+decriminalization&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Toronto's request to the federal government officially rejected, proponents of decriminalization for illegal drugs say they are "dismayed" the city has lost what they say is a key measure to fight the ongoing toxic drug crisis.
Colin Johnson, co-chair of the Toronto Harm Reduction Alliance - a grassroots organization composed of front-line workers, researchers and people who use drugs - said the group was "despondent" to see Ottawa's refusal late Friday to grant Toronto's request to decriminalize drug possession for personal use.
Removing drug use from the justice system would have a positive knock-on effect, Johnson said, including reducing the overrepresentation of Black and Indigenous people in the court system and avoiding the escalating social problems associated with having a criminal record.
"We are somewhat quite dismayed," Johnson said. "Decriminalization was one part to resolving the drug situation that we have here in Canada."
But amid public sparring in recent weeks over Toronto's request to Health Canada - including vocal opposition from the province and federal Conservatives after B.C. re-criminalized drug possession in public places - some experts had already been expressing skepticism over the city's request, saying the focus on decriminalization is an unhelpful distraction from the lack of progress on other solutions to the drug toxicity problem.
It's "an easy scapegoat," Daniel Werb, the director of the Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation at St. Michael's Hospital, said in an interview earlier this month.
"The failure of the government to address the housing crisis, the failure of government to address drug toxicity - these are what is driving not only drug poisonings but all the spillover social problems that we're seeing about public drug use," he said.
Werb added that decriminalization "is not what is causing social problems related to drug use," and it's not what is going to fix it.
Citing safety concerns, Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks announced Friday that Ottawa would not grant the request from Toronto Public Health. The city's request to decriminalize drug possession, which had been sitting with Health Canada since last year, did not do enough to "protect public health and maintain public safety," Saks said. Among her concerns was the protection of youth "and lack of support from key players including the province of Ontario," she said.
Asked over the weekend how she responded to Ottawa's rejection, Mayor Olivia Chow called the debate over whether someone goes to jail for drug possession "artificial," stressing the focus must be on treating addiction as a health issue, one that takes 500 lives a year in Toronto.
"If there's no treatment, putting the person jail makes no sense. So the most important piece is focusing on the person, treat it like a disease and focus on how to save lives," she said.
On Friday, before Ottawa rejected the city's request, Chow had written a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appealing for help, highlighting the need for a crisis centre that's open 24 hours a day "to relieve pressure on emergency departments."
The provincial government has ramped up opposition to decriminalization in recent weeks. Michael Tibollo, associate minister of mental health and addictions, recently called the request a "made-in-Toronto disaster waiting to happen." Upon hearing word of Ottawa's final decision, Premier Doug Ford posted a succinct response on the social media platform X: "Good."
In a statement Friday, Toronto's medical officer of health, Dr. Eileen de Villa, defended decriminalization as an "evidence-informed policy tool to help remove barriers to care."
"As Minister Saks has refused Toronto Public Health's request to decriminalize personal possession of controlled drugs and substances, the need to invest in other available evidence-based interventions is all the more critical," de Villa said.
Toronto first applied in 2022 for an exemption from Health Canada that would mean people in Toronto would no longer be arrested or charged for personal possession of drugs under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act. Other criminal charges including drug trafficking would still be in effect.
The number of charges laid for simple drug possession is very low in Toronto, and prosecutors rarely take these cases, making it seem as though decriminalization of illegal drugs for personal use already effectively exists.
Both the Ontario and national medical officers of health, prominent law enforcement groups and Toronto police have endorsed decriminalization of drug possession for personal use.
But not all law enforcement agencies are on board. Six months ago, Peterborough police Chief Stuart Betts announced a change in policing approach for public drug use: zero tolerance. The idea was to strike a balance between treating drug use as a health issue rather than a criminal one, and also responding to community concerns about open drug use in playgrounds and outside downtown businesses.
Views on decriminalization have shifted for some amid British Columbia's provincewide pilot program early last year. In response to safety concerns over public drug use, the province recently recriminalized drug possession in public places, a move some advocates say has essentially gutted the approach.
Toronto's request had differed from B.C. in some ways. There was no set limit on the amount of drugs that constituted personal use, and it would have applied to youth as well as adults, with the argument being that criminalizing youth has the same negative consequences as it does for adults. Drug possession would still be illegal in airports and a few other locations, including child-care spaces.
One disappointing aspect of Ottawa's decision is that Toronto won't get to compare its proposed approach with that of B.C., said Matthew Wood, a public safety reform consultant and former Toronto police officer.
"It's just unfortunate that we didn't take this opportunity to improve upon what British Columbia was doing," he said.
Wood stressed that alternative solutions to the drug crisis must be pursued, such as increasing access to rehabilitative treatment centres. Wait times at free residential treatment programs can be more than a year, while at paid programs it can be up to 270 days.
"When the wait time for those programs exceeds a year, you're just producing the environment for recidivism to occur," Wood said.
The new information emerging from B.C. provides an opportunity to figure out what approach works best, said Dr. Leslie Buckley, head of the addictions division at the Centre for Addiction and Mental Health, in a recent interview. She remains in favour of the policy overall but said public buy-in is important.
"We have to be nimble and pivot and listen to everybody, from people with lived experience, to nurses to the public," she said.
At the same time, "we need to do more at every level when it comes to substances. We need more prevention and treatment. It is very hard to access the help to get better in our current strained system," she said.
While there has been progress on immediate access to medical treatment for opioid and alcohol use through rapid-access addiction programs, it is still difficult for many to access residential treatment or in-patient medical care.
Buckley said CAMH has been piloting "virtual intensive programs" that are similar to traditional rehabilitation, which could be scaled up at a lower cost. The hospital has also been working on programs to address the rise in crystal meth use in the city - a reminder that the drug crisis exists in many forms.
"We need to do more," she said.
CREDIT: Wendy Gillis and Alyshah Hasham Toronto Star
