Document 305 of 500

Byelection vote set for rural riding: Observers expect Pictou West to stay under Tory control

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/byelection-vote-set-rural-riding/docview/3056592165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Byelection+vote+set+for+rural+riding%3A+Observers+expect+Pictou+West+to+stay+under+Tory+control&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voters in the rural Nova Scotia provincial electoral district of Pictou West are ready to go to the polls Tuesday in a riding that voted decisively Tory in the 2021 provincial election.
The riding borders another held by Premier Tim Houston, who quickly called the byelection for May 21 following last month's surprise retirement of the legislature's Speaker, Karla MacFarlane.
MacFarlane had held the riding, which includes the town of Pictou, N.S., since 2013 and easily retained the seat for the Progressive Conservatives in the 2021 general election by taking nearly 64 per cent of the vote.
Houston, who had six months to call the byelection, did so on April 21, a little over two weeks after MacFarlane announced her decision.
Lori Turnbull, a political scientist at Dalhousie University, believes the speed of the premier's call is an indication the governing Tories are confident about their standing in a riding where they are running a newcomer to politics, Marco MacLeod. "They went with a neophyte and they were comfortable doing that," said Turnbull.
MacLeod, who runs a small custom lumber mill on his family farm, said he was conscious of the need to establish his own presence despite his party's perceived strength.
"I'm a fresh face here, so I'm not taking anything for granted and we've been putting a lot of energy into reaching all corners of the riding," he said in an interview last week. "I'm not a huge social media guy, I prefer going on doorsteps and having conversations with people."
Also vying for the seat are Liberal candidate Mary Wooldridge-Elliott, who finished a distant second to MacFarlane in 2021, the NDP's Melinda MacKenzie and Clare Brett, who also ran in 2021 for the Green Party of Nova Scotia.
Wooldridge-Elliott, who is a school bus driver and member of Pictou County council, and MacKenzie, who is a school teacher and a member of Pictou town council, believe they have a fighting chance because of their political experience at the local level.
"I couldn't imagine what (campaigning) would be like if I hadn't had the experience," said MacKenzie. She added that she's a good option for voters who want closer links between municipal and provincial issues.
Wooldridge-Elliott makes a similar pitch. "I can bring to the table what I've learned as a municipal councillor," she said.
The candidates all said the state of the province's health-care system and the cost of living are the main issues voters have been raising during the campaign.
"I think food and shelter are the two biggest things that everybody is dealing with right now," said Brett, a singer-songwriter and caregiver who moved to Nova Scotia from B.C., six years ago and felt compelled to run for the Greens in order to provide voters with an alternative to the three major parties.
Meanwhile, Turnbull said observers would be surprised if the riding doesn't stay in the hands of the Progressive Conservatives. As a result, she said it won't serve as an indicator of the government's strength heading into a provincial election that must be called no later than July 15 of next year.
"I don't think Houston is going to an election early," Turnbull said. "So whoever wins this (byelection) is going to be in the seat for close to a year."
Heading into Tuesday's vote the Tories hold 32 seats in the 55 seat legislature, while the Liberals hold 15, the NDP has six seats and there is one Independent.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Keith Doucette The Canadian Press

Subject: Provincial elections; Voters; Elections

Location: Nova Scotia Canada; Canada; Pictou Nova Scotia Canada

Document 306 of 500

Poilievre takes page from Ford playbook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-takes-page-ford-playbook/docview/3056360240/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+takes+page+from+Ford+playbook&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tories have traditionally been the party of big business with a simple subtext: labour be damned.
Today, it's not so simple. Right-wing politicians who once bashed union leaders as "bosses" are rebranding themselves as friends of the working man.
Doug Ford famously reinvented himself as the premier of the proletariat after taking power in 2018, winning the loyalty of major construction unions that had long opposed Ontario's Progressive Conservatives.
Now, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is taking a page from Ford Nation's gains in Ontario, quietly repositioning himself as a supposedly pro-labour politician ahead of the next election due in 2025.
Yes, you heard right. If you think right-wing politicians don't know the playbook for pivoting - ideologically, politically, economically - you're wrong.
Ford did it by cosying up to key labour leaders with a major charm offensive and a modest boost in the minimum wage - after first freezing the hourly rate for years. Swallowing his visceral disdain, Ford has been steadily eating the lunch of rival New Democrats and Liberals in lunch-bucket territory.
How can Poilievre repeat that feat? No, not a charm offensive - not in his nature.
Just watch him.
"Conservatives are 100 per cent onside with workers - union and non-union - who are fighting for pay hikes," he said earnestly late last year.
Poilievre startled his critics last February by supporting an "anti-scab" bill that bans the use of replacement workers during labour disputes in federally regulated workplaces, put forward by the Liberal government at the behest of the NDP in a minority Parliament.
No one saw that one coming. Not from a Tory party leader who made his name bashing unions while promoting a controversial U.S.-style "right-to-work" law long before he became party leader.
Labour leaders have seen this movie before - a political conversion on the road to the campaign trail. As Poilievre tries to rewrite the script, his union adversaries want people to hear the full story.
The Canadian Labour Congress has launched a campaign to hold politicians to account for their latest words and past actions. Top of the list for CLC president Bea Bruske are the federal and Ontario Tory leaders.
"I look at some of the pretty words ... that politicians like Ford and his party have used - saying they stand up for workers - but then when you peel back the layers of that onion and you look at the policies, they don't align," Bruske told me. "We see that the pretty words ... don't match the history in the Doug Ford situation."
She points to Ford's controversial plan to override collective bargaining rights by invoking the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" against striking CUPE workers in late 2022, triggering threats of a major labour confrontation until he quickly backed down.
Yet as Bruske readily conceded in our interview, Ford's contradictions have not yet cut into his strong voter support after nearly six years in power, according to the latest polling. Poilievre's federal Conservatives are enjoying stratospheric levels of support.
How to explain? Bruske says the CLC wants to better explain Poilievre's past record of union-bashing - and why shouldn't be airbrushed. "Workers are busy ... they're not reading the newspaper," she argues. "So it's going to be up to labour to remind folks of what that voting record means."
Poilievre's track record is a matter of public record. As he told the Star more than a decade ago, he supported "workers freedom" that makes membership optional even when a union local has negotiated contract increases for everyone's benefit (so-called free riders).
"I believe in free choice for workers and I am going to do my part to see that happens at the federal level," he vowed. "Over time, I believe I can convince people of its merits. And hope springs eternal that one day we will have free choice for workers in Canada."
Fighting words, way back then. All these years later, never mind.
That said, Ontario's Tories embraced a similar "right-to-work" ideology in opposition, led by MPP Monte McNaughton. But when he became Ford's labour minister, McNaughton pivoted adroitly, shedding the anti-bosses baggage and dropping the minimum wage freeze.
Alongside McNaughton, Ford showed up at pre-election photo-ops with leaders of the construction trades, and even coaxed the then-leaders of Ontario's biggest unions - Unifor and OPSEU - to appear alongside the premier. It was a remarkable reinvention, obscuring the fact that Ford had axed many of the labour reforms enacted by the previous Liberal government (notably on legislated sick days).
Bruske says labour leaders and voters must now be vigilant about Poilievre's makeover. She points to a new poll for the CLC by GQR Canada showing nearly two-thirds of Canadians are pro-union, and 70 per cent want governments to lower barriers to joining unions.
Perhaps Poilievre can read those polls. Or perhaps he can just pivot.
CAPTION: Federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is drawing inspiration from Ford Nation's gains in Ontario, quietly repositioning himself as a supposedly pro-labour politician ahead of the next election due in 2025, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Conservatism; Politics; Right to work; Union leadership; Collective bargaining; Politicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Right to work Union leadership Collective bargaining

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Poilievre takes page from Ford playbook

Document 307 of 500

At a breaking point and forced to provide "inhumane" care for their son, Jonathan's parents considered the unthinkable: leaving him in hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/at-breaking-point-forced-provide-inhumane-care/docview/3056207439/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=At+a+breaking+point+and+forced+to+provide+%26quot%3Binhumane%26quot%3B+care+for+their+son%2C+Jonathan%27s+parents+considered+the+unthinkable%3A+leaving+him+in+hospital&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One morning in January, 14-year-old Jonathan Mitkovski refused to get out of bed. His parents wondered, at first, if he was coming down with a virus. It had always been a challenge to figure out what was bothering Jonathan. Born with Down syndrome and diagnosed with autism at age three, he had never spoken.
Before his sudden decline, Jonathan had been a gentle child who greeted everyone with a smile. He loved roller coasters, the Maple Leafs and McDonald's french fries. He played drums in a school concert. Whenever his father asked, "Who wants to go outside?" Jonathan would be the first at the door, waiting for someone to tie his shoes.
His parents, Jole Mitkovski, a schoolteacher, and Katherine Mitkovski, a public servant, struggled at times to manage Jonathan's needs while caring for their two younger children, nine and 11, and working full time, but they were getting by until his health took a turn.
Within days, Jonathan stopped walking, eating and using the toilet. He wouldn't go to school. He stayed in bed for more than a week. When his parents tried to move him, he kicked, punched and threw things, putting holes in the walls. "He was like a different child," his father said. Over time, Jonathan lost weight and became dehydrated.
At a crisis point, his parents called 911. The dispatcher sent an ambulance and a police escort.
Doctors at the Hospital for Sick Children diagnosed Jonathan with Down syndrome regression disorder, a rare and debilitating condition that causes a rapid loss in functioning and behavioural changes.
Jonathan spent a total of 14 days at SickKids, where his parents say he received excellent care, but did not improve. Doctors tried to transfer him to Holland Bloorview Hospital, a rehabilitation centre for children and youth, but he did not meet the criteria for inpatient admission, his parents said.
Jole and Katherine assumed there would be another in-patient or long-term rehabilitation option, a place with specialized health-care workers who could manage their son's immense physical needs and help him relearn the skills he'd lost. Jonathan's pediatrician and his medical team at SickKids wrote letters supporting the family's urgent request for a rehab or group home placement.
But there was no such place available for Jonathan, the parents were told. They would have to manage his care at home.
Released from hospital on Feb. 14, Jonathan was still not walking, struggling to eat and was initially unable to attend school.
The parents say the care they have been forced to provide at home over the past four months is "inhumane" and has made their son sicker, compromised the family's safety and brought them to the brink of mental collapse. Jole is on a stress leave from work.
"They've made us be 24-hour nurses and we don't know what we're doing," Katherine said.
Jole and Katherine say their caseworkers from the provincial agency tasked with helping families through crises have been supportive, but told them what they need does not exist: there are no emergency beds for children like Jonathan whose parents cannot safely manage their care at home. Such spaces exist but only for adults, the workers said.
The Mitkovski family's ordeal comes amid a health crisis affecting some of Ontario's most vulnerable children and their families. The pediatric health system is under enormous pressure, after decades of underfunding and strain from the COVID-19 pandemic, and is struggling to meet the needs of a growing population and a staggering increase in medically complex cases.
Though Premier Doug Ford's government has invested hundreds of millions of dollars in pediatric health since the pandemic and expanded supports for children and youth with complex needs, advocates say not much has changed for families in crisis who must navigate a system with numerous agencies and often wait months or years to receive specialized care.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services did not respond to questions for this story, but a spokesperson sent a statement listing available supports and programs, some of which the Mitkovski family used and found did not meet their needs, others which their son is not eligible for.
Nearly everyone Jole and Katherine have spoken to - dozens of people from at least six provincial agencies and government offices - has tried to be helpful. The parents say Jonathan received excellent care at SickKids's ABILITY clinic, a program tailored to children with autism and Down syndrome that neither the Mitkovski family nor their pediatrician knew existed before Jonathan's hospital admission. But the services available since his discharge from hospital, they say, have been inadequate.
Moving Jonathan from one room to another is a two-person job that requires the use of lifting straps, which the parents ordered from Amazon. The straps frequently break and are unsafe, but they have no other option. Once, while Jole was carrying a thrashing Jonathan upstairs with a personal support worker, Jonathan fell and the three of them nearly tumbled down the stairs.
Jole and Katherine have spent hundreds of hours advocating for appropriate medical care for their son. They have sought assistance from provincial agencies, health networks and government ministries. They have appealed directly to their MPP and the premier. They keep hearing the same thing: Jonathan's needs are too high, or there is a months-long wait list, or he doesn't meet the criteria, or the family hasn't exhausted all treatment options in the community.
In March, they contacted the Children's Aid Society, hoping it could offer respite care or a foster home placement.
Two people who work in the health-care system had told Jole and Katherine confidentially that if they wanted an urgent placement for Jonathan, they should bring him to a hospital and refuse to take him home.
The parents floated the idea in a meeting with Children's Aid, sharing with caseworkers that they felt they had no choice but to leave their son at the hospital.
"We can't do this anymore," Jole said. "We've been crying out for help and nobody is listening to us."
They say a CAS supervisor told them they would be charged with child abandonment.
"They said, 'You can't just give away your child,' " Katherine recalled.
"We don't want to abandon our child," Jole said. "We want our child to get the help that he needs."
They say CAS case workers told them Jonathan's needs were complex and they couldn't find a placement for him.
"If his needs are so complex, how are we supposed to take care of him?" the parents asked.
They say an agency supervisor told them: "With love."
But love didn't give them superpowers, or a nursing degree. Love didn't protect them from mental and physical collapse, and it didn't help their son learn to walk again.
"We're not neglectful parents. We want the best for him," Jole said at home last month. "But we also are hanging on by a thread."
York Region CAS said it cannot comment on the specific case due to privacy, but CEO Ginelle Skerritt said in a statement that the story "highlights systemic challenges that families face in accessing health care and community resources across the province."
Janet McLaughlin, an associate professor of health studies at Wilfrid Laurier University and co-director of the Laurier Autism Research Consortium, said parents should not be expected to fill in the gaps for social systems that are not equipped or funded to care for children with high needs, but in Ontario that is the reality, and it comes with profound consequences for a family's mental health, physical health and economic stability.
"If we had better supports to offer them, I think far fewer families would reach that point of absolute crisis," she said. "If we had specially trained respite workers and crisis teams who could be there to provide the parents the support they need, that they could continue working, and that they could stay safe in a state of crisis, that would be great. But right now, what parents are told in these situations, is you can call the police, or you can take your child to the ER, and maybe a psych ward. And none of these are humane options."
"It's the minority of children on the autism spectrum whose needs are this high ... so when these extreme cases occur, we should have wrap-around supports ready to go."
Jonathan's health crisis was years in the making and started with the pandemic, when schools shut down and he lost his social connections.
His pediatrician recommended anti-depressants, which seemed to help. But over the holidays in December 2023, his mood dipped again.
In January, when he was diagnosed with Down syndrome regression, his parents were told that little was known about the condition and the prognosis was uncertain. Jonathan might never return to his baseline.
Dr. Eileen Quinn, a developmental pediatrician in Ohio and the mother to a child with Down syndrome, said the behavioural changes that come with regressions are so severe that "it's like your child is gone."
"The child that I knew, who was the light of my life and made me laugh out loud every day, was just totally different," Quinn said in an interview with 3.21: Canada's Down Syndrome Magazine. "It can really be a very catastrophic thing to happen to families."
The Mitkovskis were sent home and offered occasional respite care and 10 hours of personal support a week. It wasn't enough.
An urgent support agency sent a respite worker to assist them for a few hours, but the worker was unable to feed Jonathan or change his diaper. Katherine, who was working from home at the time, had to take over. It didn't feel like respite.
The first personal support worker sent to their home was unable to manage Jonathan's physical care. They have since found a new worker who comes for an hour each morning and afternoon to get Jonathan ready for school and help Jole carry him downstairs with the lifting straps.
Since February, Katherine and Jole have been sending increasingly desperate and panicked emails to anyone who might be able to help them.
"His needs are immense and we cannot do it on our own," they wrote to a crisis support agency.
"This child is literally deteriorating in front of us," they wrote to SickKids, Children's Aid and their case workers at Your Support Services Network.
"My son needs urgent help. My family needs urgent help," they wrote to the premier.
The father offered Ford and his MPP an invitation: "Come for a visit," he wrote, "to see the struggles we face on a daily basis to help our son."
"Jonathan's behavioural and care needs are proving to be extremely challenging for his family," Jonathan's medical team at SickKids wrote in a letter to support the family's application for residential placement. "Despite their work with all of their community-based resources, the family continues to struggle and are exhausted and reporting caregiver burnout."
Jonathan spent six years on the waitlist for Ontario Autism Program funding. In July 2023, he received the maximum amount - about $43,000. That money, which would have been helpful when he was younger, now sits in a bank account because he is not well enough for the core services, such as speech therapy, the family is allowed to use it for.
"If that funding had been available five years ago, would we have gone through this?" Jole said. "I don't know."
Last week, the family received word that their case had been considered by a provincial "resolution council," and Jonathan has been endorsed to receive ministry funding for a group home placement. A regional council will consider the case for final approval in June. The parents met on Wednesday with a home that may be able to take him. It's two hours away, in Belleville, which is not ideal, but the home comes highly recommended. It will be several weeks before they find out if Jonathan is accepted and likely months before the home can take him in.
Jole and Katherine believe that their persistence - "making noise" by repeatedly emailing the ministry, the premier's office and their MPP - likely made a difference. They have been thinking about how their ability to advocate for their son, even as doing so nearly broke them, isn't something all parents can manage.
There are so many more kids like Jonathan, Katherine said. "What happens to them?"
Do you have a child with complex developmental or mental health needs who you are struggling to safely care for at home, and for whom you are unable to access in-patient or residential care? Email adempsey@thestar.ca or moglivie@thestar.ca

Jole, a teacher, is on a mental health leave from work due to the strain of caring for Jonathan, but the family needs more care than they can provide.Jonathan shares a laugh with his mother, Katherine Mitkovski.This year, doctors diagnosed Jonathan with Down syndrome regression disorder, a rare and debilitating condition that causes a rapid loss in functioning and behavioural changes.Jonathan Mitkovski, 14, at his family's home in Stouffville last month. Can you be conservative and 'pro-labour'? Pierre Poilievre takes a page from Doug Ford's playbook

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/can-you-be-conservative-pro-labour-pierre/docview/3056207382/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+you+be+conservative+and+%27pro-labour%27%3F+Pierre+Poilievre+takes+a+page+from+Doug+Ford%27s+playbook&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tories have traditionally been the party of big business with a simple subtext: labour be damned.
Today, it's not so simple. Right-wing politicians who once bashed union leaders as "bosses" are rebranding themselves as friends of the working man.
Doug Ford famously reinvented himself as the premier of the proletariat after taking power in 2018, winning the loyalty of major construction unions that had long opposed Ontario's Progressive Conservatives.
Now, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is taking a page from Ford Nation's gains in Ontario, quietly repositioning himself as a supposedly pro-labour politician ahead of the next election due in 2025.
Yes, you heard right. If you think right-wing politicians don't know the playbook for pivoting - ideologically, politically, economically - you're wrong.
Ford did it by cosying up to key labour leaders with a major charm offensive and a modest boost in the minimum wage - after first freezing the hourly rate for years. Swallowing his visceral disdain, Ford has been steadily eating the lunch of rival New Democrats and Liberals in lunch-bucket territory.
How can Poilievre repeat that feat? No, not a charm offensive - not in his nature.
Just watch him.
"Conservatives are 100 per cent onside with workers - union and non-union - who are fighting for pay hikes," he said earnestly late last year.
Poilievre startled his critics last February by supporting an "anti-scab" bill that bans the use of replacement workers during labour disputes in federally regulated workplaces, put forward by the Liberal government at the behest of the NDP in a minority Parliament.
No one saw that one coming. Not from a Tory party leader who made his name bashing unions while promoting a controversial U.S.-style "right-to-work" law long before he became party leader.
Labour leaders have seen this movie before - a political conversion on the road to the campaign trail. As Poilievre tries to rewrite the script, his union adversaries want people to hear the full story.
The Canadian Labour Congress has launched a campaign to hold politicians to account for their latest words and past actions. Top of the list for CLC president Bea Bruske are the federal and Ontario Tory leaders.
"I look at some of the pretty words ... that politicians like Ford and his party have used - saying they stand up for workers - but then when you peel back the layers of that onion and you look at the policies, they don't align," Bruske told me. "We see that the pretty words ... don't match the history in the Doug Ford situation."
She points to Ford's controversial plan to override collective bargaining rights by invoking the Constitution's "notwithstanding clause" against striking CUPE workers in late 2022, triggering threats of a major labour confrontation until he quickly backed down.
Yet as Bruske readily conceded in our interview, Ford's contradictions have not yet cut into his strong voter support after nearly six years in power, according to the latest polling. Poilievre's federal Conservatives are enjoying stratospheric levels of support.
How to explain? Bruske says the CLC wants to better explain Poilievre's past record of union-bashing - and why shouldn't be airbrushed.
"Workers are busy ... they're not reading the newspaper," she argues. "So it's going to be up to labour to remind folks of what that voting record means."
Poilievre's track record is a matter of public record. As he told the Star more than a decade ago, he supported "workers freedom" that makes membership optional even when a union local has negotiated contract increases for everyone's benefit (so-called free riders).
"I believe in free choice for workers and I am going to do my part to see that happens at the federal level," he vowed. "Over time, I believe I can convince people of its merits. And hope springs eternal that one day we will have free choice for workers in Canada."
Fighting words, way back then. All these years later, never mind.
That said, Ontario's Tories embraced a similar "right-to-work" ideology in opposition, led by MPP Monte McNaughton. But when he became Ford's labour minister, McNaughton pivoted adroitly, shedding the anti-bosses baggage and dropping the minimum wage freeze.
Alongside McNaughton, Ford showed up at pre-election photo-ops with leaders of the construction trades, and even coaxed the then-leaders of Ontario's biggest unions - Unifor and OPSEU - to appear alongside the premier. It was a remarkable reinvention, obscuring the fact that Ford had axed many of the labour reforms enacted by the previous Liberal government (notably on legislated sick days).
Bruske says labour leaders and voters must now be vigilant about Poilievre's makeover. She points to a new poll for the CLC by GQR Canada showing nearly two-thirds of Canadians are pro-union, and 70 per cent want governments to lower barriers to joining unions.
Perhaps Poilievre can read those polls. Or perhaps he can just pivot.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre delivers a speech at a Canada's Building Trades Unions conference in Gatineau, Que. onApril 30, 2024.

Quebec separation is back on the boil

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/quebec-separation-is-back-on-boil/docview/3056157910/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Quebec+separation+is+back+on+the+boil&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not a topic of conversation in English Canada. Mention "separation" in a political context and, just like Google search results, the discussion is likely to be about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's split from his spouse Sophie Grégoire or, as the Edmonton Journal recently put it, Alberta's "slow-motion" separation from Canada. Quebec separation? Well, that matter was placed on the far back burner - you know, the one you forget about until it starts to burn?
Quebec may not be ignoring the federal carbon pricing law like Saskatchewan (it doesn't need to since it has its own cap-and-trade system) or threatening to pull out of the Canada Pension Plan like Alberta (Quebec already has its own pension plan), but unrest is brewing in the province and it could play a crucial role in the next federal election.
Last month, the Parti Québécois's youthful and charismatic leader, Paul St-Pierre Plamondon, promised a third referendum on Quebec sovereignty before the end of the decade.
It's a real threat. With Premier François Legault's Coalition Avenir Québec (CAQ) cratering in public opinion surveys, the Parti Québécois is poised to win a majority mandate - a feat last accomplished in 1998 - when Quebecers next head to the polls in 2026.
In a fiery speech to party supporters in April, St-Pierre Plamondon - or "PSPP," as he is known in the province - laid out the case for Quebec sovereignty. He claimed Trudeau has encroached on provincial jurisdiction more than any other prime minister, and that he has refused to hand over immigration powers to the province in order to "destabilize Quebec," to "weaken us."
"It is a regime that will not change," PSPP told the crowd. "Either we remain a minority that keeps declining in Canada until we disappear, or we become the majority at home with a guarantee that our language and culture survives."
Outside of Quebec, this may seem like hyperbole. The Trudeau government has given child-care funding to Quebec with opt-out provisions and no strings attached. It has revamped language laws to protect French in the province that are so deferential to Quebec's own language laws that Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather voted against them.
But there are real concerns, shared in newspapers, on the airwaves and at kitchen tables, that Canada's current trajectory of large population increases through immigration will inevitably lead to the decline of French and Quebec's influence in Canada.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, for example, could easily win (not that he wants to) a majority mandate without any seats in Quebec - despite the province being home to 22 per cent of Canada's population. Even with a grandfather clause that provides Quebec with seven extra seats in the House of Commons, its weight in the chamber will decline over the next decade after more seats were awarded to British Columbia, Alberta and Ontario to reflect their growing populations - populations whose second language isn't French.
The primary duty of Canada's prime minister is to keep its citizens safe and the country united. If the PQ continues to lead in the polls, voters will need to ask themselves which federal leader can best keep the country together. Who can win the hearts and minds of Quebecers, making the case that their interests and aspirations are better served within Canada than outside of it?
Poilievre was part of a government that recognized the Québécois as a nation within a united Canada. His Conservatives are less likely to trample on provincial jurisdiction with new social programs than the Liberals or the NDP.
But will Quebecers see themselves reflected in a Conservative leader whose list of spending priorities ("roads, bridges, police, military, border security and a safety net for those who can't provide for themselves") makes no mention of culture?
Will environmentally conscious Quebecers see themselves in a leader whose priorities include building more pipelines and green-lighting big oil and gas projects? Is that the Canada they want to be a part of?
Unfortunately for the NDP, Quebecers have not warmed to Jagmeet Singh. Since 2019, the party has held just one seat in the province, making it hard to see Singh take on the role of Captain Canada.
Quebec remains the only province where the federal Liberals aren't currently faring too badly in public support - they closely trail the Bloc Québécois in second place. Quebec, according to a recent Leger survey, is the only province where voters believe Trudeau would be the best prime minister.
Despite this, PSPP's criticisms stand, and a recent incident involving a Liberal Franco-Ontarian MP telling a Quebec witness at a Commons committee that he was "full of s--t" did the Liberals no favours.
As a francophone Montreal MP, does Trudeau maintain home-field advantage? Do Liberal values best reflect Quebecers' values? Is Trudeau too toxic outside of Quebec to make the case for Canada? And if Trudeau goes, will the next Liberal leader be chosen based at least in part on their appeal to Quebecers?
These are questions that all Canadian voters - not just Quebecers - would do well to contemplate.
CAPTION: Paul St-Pierre Plamondon, the Parti Québécois's youthful and charismatic leader, is promising a third referendum on Quebec sovereignty before the end of the decade, Althia Raj writes.Jacques Boissinot The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 310 of 500

Banning encampment protests is not a solution, addressing the issues is

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/banning-encampment-protests-is-not-solution/docview/3056157853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Banning+encampment+protests+is+not+a+solution%2C+addressing+the+issues+is&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford is right. I too am 'fed up' with the anti-Israel protests, May 15
Mark McQueen needs to explain why he believes that Canada should stand by while yet another genocide is taking place. Instead, he complains about things that inconvenience him and misrepresents what the protesters are doing.
For example, none of the protesters are harassing Jewish Canadians. In fact, many of the protesters are Jewish Canadians. They are critical of those who support the genocide but I would hope we can all agree that genocide is bad. The ones who feel "harassed" are those who equate the Zionist movement with Judaism - a mistake akin to equating Nazism with being German.
I don't care if you believe God gave you a territory. You still need to negotiate to take possession.
Perhaps the boiling frog analogy McQueen tries to shoehorn into his narrative would be better used to describe the 75 years of Israel's failure to negotiate a just peace with the people it displaced. We are so used to seeing Israel grab more and more territory while continuing to kill Palestinians with impunity that it rarely makes the news. Yet this is daily life for Palestinians .
Sorry if McQueen feels inconvenienced by protesters, but the solution isn't to ban protests but rather to address the issues they are calling attention to. We declared "none is too many" to Jewish refugees fleeing the Nazis. We stood by for decades while South Africa segregated its people by race and did little while Hutus slaughtered Tutsis in Rwanda.
It's long past time that we called for Israel to negotiate a just peace with the Palestinians.
Gary Dale, West Hill, ON
Time for university leaders to do their jobs
Mark McQueen clearly points out the obvious. Too many protesters claiming they are chanting for the freedom of Palestinians are behaving like anarchists. They have been disrupting the lives of Torontonians for far too long.
Premier Doug Ford is voicing what many believe - it is time for university administrators to do their jobs regarding the racist elements on their campuses. It is time to uphold and enforce our laws.
The mission statement of the University of Toronto states: The university "is dedicated to fostering an academic community in which the learning and scholarship of every member may flourish, with vigilant protection for individual human rights, and a resolute commitment to the principles of equal opportunity, equity and justice."
This statement is similar to the mission statements of most universities. It is time for university leaders to step up to the plate and start behaving in ways that show a commitment to what, they claim, is the backbone of their institutions. If they cannot or will not do this, they should be replaced.
Phyllis Levin, Toronto
CAPTION: A pro-Palestinian protester walks through the fenced off encampment at the University of Toronto, May 13, 2024.

Document 311 of 500

'Can't imagine a worse situation': Turmoil, anger within New Brunswick Tories before election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cant-imagine-worse-situation/docview/3056157848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Can%27t+imagine+a+worse+situation%27%3A+Turmoil%2C+anger+within+New+Brunswick+Tories+before+election&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: FREDERICTONNew Brunswick's Progressive Conservatives are in turmoil, with some members feeling disappointed and angry ahead of the next provincial election, which must be held by the fall.
Last week, the party's general meeting, scheduled for next weekend, was abruptly cancelled. A party newsletter to members suggested candidates, riding associations and volunteers thought the time and financial resources needed for the meeting were becoming "distracting" from election preparation. New dates have not been decided.
Longtime member Roger Leger, once a party executive, said the Progressive Conservatives are beset by a clash of ideas and values. Progressives who want a smaller government and lower taxes are in conflict, he said, with a wing of the party that is socially conservative and increasingly right-wing.
In 2016, the 77-year-old was among the Progressive Conservatives who supported Blaine Higgs's bid to lead the party, but since then the premier has "drifted way too far to the right on many fronts."
"He is also an autocrat; he listens to no one," Leger said. "So that's anti-democratic, and it concerns me and many others."
Higgs did not respond to an interview request about the divisions in his party and the reasons behind the cancellation of the PC general meeting.
A major fracture in the party occurred over Higgs's decision to change the province's policy on gender identity in schools, with the new rules requiring students to get parental consent before teachers can use their preferred names and pronouns. Several members - including cabinet ministers - publicly dissented against Higgs's leadership over the file, leading the premier to the precipice of calling an early election.
The early vote didn't happen, but a wave of resignations did, leaving Higgs scrambling to fill cabinet positions and defending against accusations his party was a sinking ship.
As well, the party has never been able to consolidate support among francophones, who remain wary about a premier who doesn't speak French and who made a politician - Kris Austin - known for his disdain with official bilingualism, minister of public safety.
The government's decision to reform French-language education in anglophone schools sparked outrage, leading Higgs to back down.
Jason Sully, who was the executive assistant for Dorothy Shephard before she resigned in June as health minister, said the mood of the party is one of disappointment. "It's disenfranchising," he said. "I've never seen such a disengaged party."
Sully, 43, said he got his Progressive Conservative party membership when he was in high school but now feels like a "political orphan."
"I still have hope that maybe once we have a new leader, that maybe we can go back to having meaningful party conversations about policy and ideas and moving this province forward. But right now, I don't see that happening."
It's a "bit of a stretch," he said, to say the current iteration of the party is progressive and conservative.
"We've morphed into an ultraconservative party here in New Brunswick."
Nowhere is the party's swerve to the right more apparent, some members say, than with the acclamation in December of Faytene Grasseschi, a Christian conservative, in the riding adjacent to the premier's. Grasseschi, who will run in Hampton-Fundy-St. Martins, has said she was drawn to the party because of its decision to reform the gender policy for trans and non-binary students.
In response, the Liberals have chosen John Herron, a two-time Progressive Conservative member of Parliament, to challenge Grasseschi in the riding. In his nomination speech May 11, Herron said the PC party "has lost its way. This premier's choice for the nominee in this riding is perhaps the starkest illustration."
Sully said annual general meetings of the party used to attract hundreds of people but now have just dozens, adding that the lack of enthusiasm will hurt the Tories in the upcoming elections.
Polling released by Narrative Research in late February indicated 66 per cent of residents were dissatisfied with the performance of the government, with the Liberals under leader Susan Holt maintaining a six-point lead in voting intentions - 40 per cent to 34 per cent.
J.P. Lewis, political science professor at the University of New Brunswick, Saint John, agreed with Sully that low morale within the party risks spilling over into the grassroots membership.
"What kind of impact does it have on recruiting volunteers, getting strong candidates, enthusiasm of the base?" he said.
However, Higgs's PCs remain competitive in the election, despite the "level of turmoil." What stands out, Lewis said, is that Higgs has been unable to "fully contain disgruntled members."
"It's a bit unusual because it normally doesn't happen to the party that's in power and competitive."
John Williston, who stepped down last month as regional vice-president of Westmorland Albert, which is part of the greater Moncton area, called the mood of the party "rather subdued."
"I think that there's a deep undercurrent of frustration, you might even say anger."
Herron's nomination in Hampton-Fundy-St. Martins, Williston said, was heavily attended by recent Progressive Conservative board members and other prominent Tories.
"That's just one example of how deeply troubled the party is at this moment ... There's a great number of us in the party who don't even know if we'll be able to actively support the party going into a general election. I mean, I can't imagine a worse situation, or worse scenario."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A major fracture in the party occurred over New Brunswick Premier Blaine Higgs's decision to change the province's policy on gender identity in schools.Then-prime minister Paul Martin shakes hands with former Tory MP John Herron in 2004 after Herron switched to the Liberals. Twenty years later, Herron now leads the latter party provincially.Stephen MacGillivrayTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photoThe Canadian Press file photo

Document 312 of 500

Councillor passionate about community: Four-term North York rep played key roles on transit, road safety and cultural events

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillor-passionate-about-community/docview/3056157826/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillor+passionate+about+community%3A+Four-term+North+York+rep+played+key+roles+on+transit%2C+road+safety+and+cultural+events&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto city councillor Jaye Robinson, the four-term North York representative who played a leading role on transit and road safety, and helped deliver a popular art festival, has died.
Her office confirmed Friday that Robinson, who represented Ward 15, Don Valley West, died Thursday night in the presence of her family. She was 61.
"She was deeply dedicated to the residents of Ward 15, a strong voice on city council, and a beloved mentor to her team. We are honoured to have had the opportunity to work with her," the statement said. "She will be deeply missed by her many friends and colleagues at city hall."
Mayor Olivia Chow told reporters that Robinson represented her constituents "tremendously well" and her death was a loss for Toronto.
"We miss her work. We miss her passion in serving her communities, and her dedication to supporting the arts and culture of the city. She's done phenomenal work, and she's just really loved by the residents she represented," Chow said.
A mother of three, Robinson announced in 2019 that she was temporarily stepping away from her council duties to be treated for breast cancer. The North York councillor returned to work a year later after undergoing surgery, chemotherapy and radiation.
At the time, she told the Star that doctors had discovered she was suffering from two different types of cancer, necessitating additional treatment. She said couldn't be sure she was cancer-free, but she was optimistic about her prognosis.
"I'm going to keep living and I'm very hopeful for a good outcome," she said.
For the past four years, Robinson continued working but attended council meetings virtually, and was rarely seen in person at city hall.
Robinson was first elected in 2010, and won re-election three times. Before entering politics, she was a manager in the city's economic development division for more than two decades.
As director of special events for Toronto, she introduced popular city programs, including the annual Summerlicious and Winterlicious restaurant festivals and Nuit Blanche.
Considered a council centrist, as a rookie representative Robinson was appointed to then-mayor Rob Ford's executive committee. Initially a reliable ally of the polarizing Ford, she broke ranks with him early in the term over his attempted budget cuts and plans to build a megamall and monorail on the waterfront.
Robinson also worked closely with centre-right mayor John Tory, who after winning the 2014 election picked her to lead city hall's public works committee. In that role, she helped launch Toronto's first Vision Zero strategy aimed at reducing traffic injuries and deaths.
In 2018, Tory selected her as TTC chair. She presided over one of the most difficult periods in the transit agency's history when the pandemic devastated ridership and revenue, although her work at the commission was limited by her poor health.
In recent years, Robinson fought proposals to add density to residential neighbourhoods. The stance drew criticism from housing advocates, but was popular with constituents who saw her as sticking up for them.
"She was very much a representative of the residents and somebody who was very responsive," said Geoff Kettel, co-president of the Leaside Residents' Association and co-chair of the Federation of North Toronto Residents' Associations.
He credited Robinson with pushing to regulate "iceberg" basements larger than the homes above them, and limiting the impact of Bayview Avenue development on surrounding neighbourhoods.
"You always had the sense that she was listening, that she was paying attention. It was sincere. She wasn't just a politician," Kettel said.
After news of her death broke on Friday, Robinson's current and former colleagues were quick to share tributes. They praised her efforts to protect the city's green spaces and promote the arts, and commended her strength for working through her illness until the final days of her life.
"I'm devastated to hear the news of Jaye's passing," Premier Doug Ford said in a post on X. "She was a dedicated public servant who I was fortunate to work with at city hall and who was devoted to the people she served in Don Valley West. My thoughts and prayers are with her family and loved ones as they grieve this terrible loss."
Tory said in a statement that Robinson was "a principled representative who fought for the things she believed in and more importantly fought hard for the people she represented at city hall."
She gave decades of public service "to make life better for Torontonians," and "has been taken from us way too soon," he added.
TTC CEO Rick Leary said Robinson oversaw the agency's initial response to COVID-19 and helped a chart a course to recovery. "I personally have benefitted from her advice throughout the years and I am proud to have had the opportunity to work with directly with her," he said.
With files from Mahdis Habibinia
CAPTION: Toronto Coun. Jaye Robinson, who represented Ward 15, Don Valley West, died Thursday night at age 61.Cole Burston Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Ben Spurr and Alyshah Hasham Toronto Star With files from Mahdis Habibinia

Document 313 of 500

Considering the unthinkable: Parents forced to provide 'inhumane' home care say leaving son at hospital may be only option

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/considering-unthinkable/docview/3056157815/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Considering+the+unthinkable%3A+Parents+forced+to+provide+%27inhumane%27+home+care+say+leaving+son+at+hospital+may+be+only+option&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.22&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One morning in January, 14-year-old Jonathan Mitkovski refused to get out of bed. His parents wondered, at first, if he was coming down with a virus. It had always been a challenge to figure out what was bothering Jonathan. Born with Down syndrome and diagnosed with autism at age three, he had never spoken.
Before his sudden decline, Jonathan had been a gentle child who greeted everyone with a smile. He loved roller coasters, the Maple Leafs and McDonald's french fries. He played drums in a school concert. Whenever his father asked, "Who wants to go outside?" Jonathan would be the first at the door, waiting for someone to tie his shoes.
His parents, Jole Mitkovski, a schoolteacher, and Katherine Mitkovski, a public servant, struggled at times to manage Jonathan's needs while caring for their two younger children, 9 and 11, and working full time, but they were getting by until his health took a turn.
Within days, Jonathan stopped walking, eating and using the toilet. He wouldn't go to school. He stayed in bed for more than a week. When his parents tried to move him, he kicked, punched and threw things, putting holes in the walls. "He was like a different child," his father said. Over time, Jonathan lost weight and became dehydrated.
At a crisis point, his parents called 911. The dispatcher sent an ambulance and a police escort.
Doctors at the Hospital for Sick Children diagnosed Jonathan with Down syndrome regression disorder, a rare and debilitating condition that causes a rapid loss in functioning and behavioural changes.
Jonathan spent a total of 14 days at SickKids, where his parents say he received excellent care, but did not improve. Doctors tried to transfer him to Holland Bloorview Hospital, a rehabilitation centre for children and youth, but he did not meet the criteria for in-patient admission, his parents said.
Jole and Katherine assumed there would be another in-patient or long-term rehabilitation option, a place with specialized health-care workers who could manage their son's immense physical needs and help him relearn the skills he'd lost. Jonathan's pediatrician and his medical team at SickKids wrote letters supporting the family's urgent request for a rehab or group home placement.
But there was no such place available for Jonathan, the parents were told. They would have to manage his care at home.
Released from hospital on Feb. 14, Jonathan was still not walking, struggling to eat and was initially unable to attend school.
The parents say the care they have been forced to provide at home over the past four months is "inhumane" and has made their son sicker, compromised the family's safety and brought them to the brink of mental collapse. Jole is on a stress leave from work.
"They've made us be 24-hour nurses and we don't know what we're doing," Katherine said.
Jole and Katherine say their caseworkers from the provincial agency tasked with helping families through crises have been supportive, but told them what they need does not exist: there are no emergency beds for children like Jonathan whose parents cannot safely manage their care at home. Such spaces exist, but only for adults, the workers said.
The Mitkovski family's ordeal comes amid a health crisis affecting some of Ontario's most vulnerable children and their families. The pediatric health systemis under enormous pressure, after decades of underfunding and strain from the COVID-19 pandemic, and is struggling to meet the needs of a growing population and a staggering increase in medically complex cases.
Although Premier Doug Ford's government has invested hundreds of millions of dollars in pediatric health since the pandemic and expanded supports for children and youth with complex needs, advocates say not much has changed for families in crisis who must navigate a system with numerous agencies and often wait months or years to receive specialized care.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services did not respond to questions for this story, but a spokesperson sent a statement listing available supports and programs, some of which the Mitkovski family used and found did not meet their needs, others which their son is not eligible for.
Nearly everyone Jole and Katherine have spoken to - dozens of people from at least six provincial agencies and government offices - has tried to be helpful. The parents say Jonathan received excellent care at SickKids's ABILITY clinic, a program tailored to children with autism and Down syndrome that neither the Mitkovski family nor their pediatrician knew existed before Jonathan's hospital admission. But they say the services available since his discharge from hospital have been inadequate.
Moving Jonathan from one room to another is a two-person job that requires the use of lifting straps, which the parents ordered from Amazon. The straps frequently break and are unsafe, but they have no other option. Once, while Jole was carrying a thrashing Jonathan upstairs with a personal support worker, Jonathan fell and the three of them nearly tumbled down the stairs.
Jole and Katherine have spent hundreds of hours advocating for appropriate medical care for their son. They have sought assistance from provincial agencies, health networks and government ministries. They have appealed directly to their MPP and the premier.
They keep hearing the same thing: Jonathan's needs are too high, or there is a months-long wait-list, or he doesn't meet the criteria, or the family hasn't exhausted all treatment options in the community.
In March, they contacted the Children's Aid Society, hoping it could offer respite care or a foster home placement.
Two people who work in the health-care system had told Jole and Katherine confidentially that if they wanted an urgent placement for Jonathan, they should bring him to a hospital and refuse to take him home.
The parents floated the idea in a meeting with Children's Aid, sharing with caseworkers that they felt they had no choice but to leave their son at the hospital.
"We can't do this anymore," Jole said. "We've been crying out for help and nobody is listening to us."
They say a CAS supervisor told them they would be charged with child abandonment.
"They said, 'You can't just give away your child,'" Katherine recalled.
"We don't want to abandon our child," Jole said. "We want our child to get the help that he needs."
They say CAS case workers told them Jonathan's needs were complex and they couldn't find a placement for him.
"If his needs are so complex, how are we supposed to take care of him?" the parents asked.
They say an agency supervisor told them: "With love."
But love didn't give them superpowers, or a nursing degree. Love didn't protect them from mental and physical collapse, and it didn't help their son learn to walk again.
"We're not neglectful parents. We want the best for him," Jole said at home last month.
"But we also are hanging on by a thread."
York Region CAS said it cannot comment on the specific case due to privacy, but CEO Ginelle Skerritt said in a statement that the story "highlights systemic challenges that families face in accessing health care and community resources across the province."
Janet McLaughlin, an associate professor of health studies at Wilfrid Laurier University and co-director of the Laurier Autism Research Consortium, said parents should not be expected to fill in the gaps for social systems that are not equipped or funded to care for children with high needs, but in Ontario that is the reality, and it comes with profound consequences for a family's mental health, physical health and economic stability.
"If we had better supports to offer them, I think far fewer families would reach that point of absolute crisis," she said. "If we had specially trained respite workers and crisis teams who could be there to provide the parents the support they need, that they could continue working, and that they could stay safe in a state of crisis, that would be great. But right now, what parents are told in these situations, is you can call the police, or you can take your child to the ER, and maybe a psych ward. And none of these are humane options.
"It's the minority of children on the autism spectrum whose needs are this high ... so when these extreme cases occur, we should have wraparound supports ready to go."
Jonathan's health crisis was years in the making and started with the pandemic, when schools shut down and he lost his social connections.
His pediatrician recommended antidepressants, which seemed to help. But over the holidays in December 2023, his mood dipped again.
In January, when he was diagnosed with Down syndrome regression, his parents were told that little was known about the condition and the prognosis was uncertain.
Jonathan might never return to his baseline.
Dr. Eileen Quinn, a developmental pediatrician in Ohio and the mother to a child with Down syndrome, said the behavioural changes that come with regressions are so severe that "it's like your child is gone."
"The child that I knew, who was the light of my life and made me laugh out loud every day, was just totally different," Quinn said in an interview with 3.21: Canada's Down Syndrome Magazine. "It can really be a very catastrophic thing to happen to families."
The Mitkovskis were sent home and offered occasional respite care and 10 hours of personal support a week. It wasn't enough.
An urgent support agency sent a respite worker to assist them for a few hours, but the worker was unable to feed Jonathan or change his diaper. Katherine, who was working from home at the time, had to take over. It didn't feel like respite.
The first personal support worker sent to their home was unable to manage Jonathan's physical care. They have since found a new worker who comes for an hour each morning and afternoon to get Jonathan ready for school and help Jole carry him downstairs with the lifting straps.
Since February, Katherine and Jole have been sending increasingly desperate and panicked emails to anyone who might be able to help them.
"His needs are immense and we cannot do it on our own," they wrote to a crisis support agency.
"This child is literally deteriorating in front of us," they wrote to SickKids, Children's Aid and their case workers at Your Support Services Network.
"My son needs urgent help. My family needs urgent help," they wrote to the premier.
The father offered Ford and his MPP an invitation: "Come for a visit," he wrote, "to see the struggles we face on a daily basis to help our son."
"Jonathan's behavioural and care needs are proving to be extremely challenging for his family," Jonathan's medical team at SickKids wrote in a letter to support the family's application for residential placement. "Despite their work with all of their community-based resources, the family continues to struggle and are exhausted and reporting caregiver burnout."
Jonathan spent six years on the wait-list for Ontario Autism Program funding. In July 2023, he received the maximum amount - about $43,000. That money, which would have been helpful when he was younger, now sits in a bank account because he is not well enough for the core services, such as speech therapy, the family is allowed to use it for.
"If that funding had been available five years ago, would we have gone through this?" Jole said. "I don't know."
Lastweek, the family received word that their case had been considered by a provincial "resolution council," and Jonathan has been endorsed to receive ministry funding for a group home placement. A regional council will consider the case for final approval in June.
The parents met on Wednesday with a home that may be able to take him. It's two hours away, in Belleville, which is not ideal, but the home comes highly recommended. It will be several weeks before they find out if Jonathan is accepted and likely months before the home can take him in.
Jole and Katherine believe that their persistence - "making noise" by repeatedly emailing the ministry, the premier's office and their MPP - likely made a difference. They have been thinking about how their ability to advocate for their son, even as doing so nearly broke them, isn't something all parents can manage.
There are so many more kids like Jonathan, Katherine said. "What happens to them?"
Do you have a child with
complex developmental or
mental health needs who you are struggling to safely care for at home, and for whom you are unable to access in-patient or residential care? Email adempsey@thestar.ca or
moglivie@thestar.ca

Document 314 of 500

Harris' 'Common Sense Revolution' didn't benefit the common people

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/harris-common-sense-revolution-didnt-benefit/docview/3056157810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Harris%27+%27Common+Sense+Revolution%27+didn%27t+benefit+the+common+people&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've seen claims of 'Common Sense' before and it wasn't pretty, May 11
John Cartwright's opinion piece resonated with my whole family, who at the time of Premier Mike Harris's regime were a pretty easygoing gathering of professionals in Sudbury. We might be justifiably categorized as slightly self-satisfied, successful, albeit hard-working middle-class people, joiners and helpers, summer cottage owners, and not too cynical or critical of governments.
My wife was a social worker, later the director of her department, at the main hospital in town. The cuts to health care by Harris didn't go unnoticed by our family. She had to work harder and longer than before, but she handled the challenges well. She was eulogized for her hard work, compassion, being a friend to her patients and staff; while at the same time a loving wife and mother for her family.
I was a partner in a small architectural office whose portfolio of work in progress consisted of three or four multi-unit seniors and social housing projects. We were suddenly, unexpectedly exposed to being unemployed in an instant. We did survive to live for other jobs and projects, but it was difficult for a couple of years; luckily our bank manager was good to us. Only a few friends and colleagues knew how close we were to insolvency. We had to let go a few of our staff. Many others were good to us too, including the client groups and associations whose funding sources were suddenly cut off and so they stopped paying our fees for months. Our cash flow dried up. The client groups expressed their sorrow and embarrassment to us.
Our two sons were in high school and university. We had educational saving funds for them, not enough for everything, so they worked part time to support their living expenses in other cities. The older graduated with a master's in education but school boards were not hiring. He came to be associated with the international school system and has taught with his wife/classmate in many countries. They never looked back and are now principals of their expat schools in their current chosen country. In their success, they were a big loss to education in Ontario. Our younger was studying philosophy and art, but after second year decided to go for a more practical profession. He eventually became a talented, creative chef and worked in France at upscale resorts, and at high-end fine dining establishments in Toronto and Oakville.
Who had the "common sense?" I conclude Harris had plenty of it for benefitting himself. For the regular, "common" people, not so much.
Art Peach, Sudbury
I too shudder at hearing Pierre Poilievre use the term 'Common Sense'
Thanks for the reminder as to what the "Common Sense Revolution" was all about under Conservative Premier Mike Harris in the mid-1990s. John Cartwright is right on in describing the cuts made to affordable housing, social programs, health care, schools and downloading to the municipalities at that time. Twenty-five years later, we continue to live with the results of those short-sighted changes. Let's not forget the selling off of the toll highway 407 to the private sector - a highway which could have brought in much needed revenue to the provincial coffers if ownership had been retained by the province. I too shudder at hearing "common sense" used by Pierre Poilievre for I know what he really means and it won't be different from what we saw in Ontario years ago - support for private business and corporate interests, cuts to public services.
Vicki Smith, Scarborough
Mike Harris left a strong 'Common Sense' legacy
Sorry to see your editorial page gave space to John Cartwright's painfully misinformed and shamelessly partisan revisiting of the Mike Harris legacy. As we have seen with the recent passing of former Prime Minister Brian Mulroney, it often takes time for the long-term impacts of substantive policy decisions by brave leaders to be acknowledged. In the Harris case, he inherited a broken province after the lost decade of Liberal leader David Petersen and NDP leader Bob Rae. Despite the controversies of the time, very few of his highly consequential policy changes were in fact reversed. Why? Because they were the right thing to do and Harris' Liberal successors understood that completely. Your own Robert Benzie reviewed a recent collection of essays published as "The Harris Legacy" which revealed this reality in policy area after policy area. Among the documented results of Harris reforms were improved reading scores in elementary and secondary schools.
Gary Collins, Acton
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Mike Harris talks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto in 1999.

Document 315 of 500

If you thought Canada was over its separation anxiety, there's an unpleasant surprise brewing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/if-you-thought-canada-was-over-separation-anxiety/docview/3056157605/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=If+you+thought+Canada+was+over+its+separation+anxiety%2C+there%27s+an+unpleasant+surprise+brewing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not a topic of conversation in English Canada. Mention "separation" in a political context and, just like Google search results, the discussion is likely to be about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's split from his spouse Sophie Grégoire or, as the Edmonton Journal recently put it, Alberta's "slow-motion" separation from Canada. Quebec separation? Well, that matter was placed on the far back burner - you know, the one you forget about until it starts to burn?
Quebec may not be ignoring the federal carbon pricing law like Saskatchewan (it doesn't need to since it has its own cap-and-trade system) or threatening to pull out of the Canada Pension Plan like Alberta (Quebec already has its own pension plan), but unrest is brewing in the province and it could play a crucial role in the next federal election.
Last month, the Parti Québécois's youthful and charismatic leader, Paul St-Pierre Plamondon, promised a third referendum on Quebec sovereignty before the end of the decade.
It's a real threat. With Premier François Legault's Coalition Avenir Québec (CAQ) cratering in public opinion surveys, the Parti Québécois is poised to win a majority mandate - a feat last accomplished in 1998 - when Quebecers next head to the polls in 2026.
In a fiery speech to party supporters in April, St-Pierre Plamondon - or "PSPP," as he is known in the province - laid out the case for Quebec sovereignty. He claimed Trudeau had encroached on provincial jurisdiction more than any other prime minister, and that he has refused to hand over immigration powers to the province in order to "destabilize Quebec," to "weaken us."
"It is a regime that will not change," PSPP told the crowd. "Either we remain a minority that keeps declining in Canada until we disappear, or we become the majority at home with a guarantee that our language and culture survives."
Outside of Quebec, this may seem like hyperbole. The Trudeau government has given child-care funding to Quebec with opt-out provisions and no strings attached. It has revamped language laws to protect French in the province that are so deferential to Quebec's own language laws that Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather voted against them.
But there are real concerns, shared in newspapers, on the airwaves and at kitchen tables, that Canada's current trajectory of large population increases through immigration will inevitably lead to the decline of French and Quebec's influence in Canada.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, for example, could easily win (not that he wants to) a majority mandate without any seats in Quebec - despite the province being home to 22 per cent of Canada's population. Even with a grandfather clause that provides Quebec with seven extra seats in the House of Commons, its weight in the chamber will decline over the next decade after more seats were awarded to British Columbia, Alberta and Ontario to reflect their growing populations - populations whose second language isn't French.
The primary duty of Canada's prime minister is to keep its citizens safe and the country united. If the PQ continues to lead in the polls, voters will need to ask themselves which federal leader can best keep the country together. Who can win the hearts and minds of Quebecers, making the case that their interests and aspirations are better served within Canada than outside of it?
Poilievre was part of a government that recognized the Québécois as a nation within a united Canada. His Conservatives are less likely to trample on provincial jurisdiction with new social programs than the Liberals or the NDP. But will Quebecers see themselves reflected in a Conservative leader whose list of spending priorities ("roads, bridges, police, military, border security and a safety net for those who can't provide for themselves") makes no mention of culture? Will environmentally conscious Quebecers see themselves in a leader whose priorities include building more pipelines and green-lighting big oil and gas projects? Is that the Canada they want to be a part of?
Unfortunately for the NDP, Quebecers have not warmed to Jagmeet Singh. Since 2019, the party has held just one seat in the province, making it hard to see Singh take on the role of Captain Canada.
Quebec remains the only province where the federal Liberals aren't currently faring too badly in public support - they closely trail the Bloc Québécois in second place. Quebec, according to a recent Leger survey, is the only province where voters believe Trudeau would be the best prime minister. Despite this, PSPP's criticisms stand, and a recent incident involving a Liberal Franco-Ontarian MP telling a Quebec witness at a Commons committee that he was "full of s - t" did the Liberals no favours.
As a francophone Montreal MP, does Trudeau maintain home-field advantage? Do Liberal values best reflect Quebecers' values? Is Trudeau too toxic outside of Quebec to make the case for Canada? And if Trudeau goes, will the next Liberal leader be chosen based at least in part on their appeal to Quebecers?
These are questions that all Canadian voters - not just Quebecers - would do well to contemplate.
CAPTION: Parti Quebecois Leader Paul St-Pierre Plamondon speaks at the opening of a Parti Quebecois national council meeting in Drummondville, Que. on April 13, 2024.

Subject: Jurisdiction; Voters; Prime ministers; Public opinion surveys; Immigration; Sovereignty; Pension plans

Business indexing term: Subject: Public opinion surveys Pension plans

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada; Quebec Canada

Company / organization: Name: Parti Quebecois; NAICS: 813940
