Document 316 of 500

Idea of drug decriminalization had to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/idea-drug-decriminalization-had-go/docview/3056157576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Idea+of+drug+decriminalization+had+to+go&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At this moment, there is a poor soul, drug-stupefied, sprawled on the pavement outside my home. It's 11 o'clock in the morning.
Some time back, two young people were found dead - overdosed, likely from fentanyl-laced drugs - beneath the back porch.
Half a block away, outside a drug-injection site, a cluster of users panhandle aggressively while joggers, moms pushing strollers, kids on their way to school, steer a wide berth.
A few weeks ago, property managers of my building sealed shut and barred a street-level entrance - removed the handle, disabled the security access controls - because intruders had repeatedly jimmied open the door, then broke into units.
Desperate needs make for desperate criminality.
I don't use "recreational" drugs - such a benign descriptor for a social scourge - soft or hard. But the rampant drug use all around me has made drugs my problem, my neighbourhood's problem. I can't fix it, social agencies can't fix it, cops can't fix it, except sporadically. The Toronto Board of Health sure as hell can't fix it.
And the board's push to decriminalize drugs - following in the footsteps of a disastrous pilot project in British Columbia, which had the goal of inducing addicts towards treatment and preventing them from dying alone in back alleys, a holistic approach - would have only made the problem exponentially worse.
It can't always or primarily be about the torment and misfortune of drug addicts. There's neighbourhood and community health, too - what makes a city livable. The board of health, which should be alert to the well-being of everyone, has given most of the city's residents short shrift. Certainly pockets of Toronto where high levels of open drug use, poverty, homelessness, dangerous street sex work and crime have coalesced can already give Vancouver's Downtown Eastside a run for its money. Homelessness, for one, doesn't equate to criminality, though encampments contribute to a sense of fear and do-not-go-near in neighbourhoods. But many of these ills are interconnected.
And it's not as if Toronto police are hounding drug users: Last year they made a mere 15 arrests for single counts of possession, mostly related to other enforcement actions, such as drugs found on an individual or in a car in an unrelated criminal offence. Many people may be under the misconception that hard drugs have already been decriminalized, as was cannabis, precisely because law enforcement rarely intervenes.
The board's 2022 application to Health Canada - to decriminalize small amounts of illegal drugs for personal use - was on Friday rebuffed by Ottawa, on the grounds that the proposal didn't adequately protect public health and maintain public safety. The federal government had previously stated that approval wouldn't be granted unless Queen's Park was onside, although the messaging was mixed and confusing. In recent days, there has been a flurry of letters from the province to the federal government - and the health board and city hall - serving notice that legalizing hard drugs isn't happening, characterizing the board's intention as "dangerous."
Premier Doug Ford took the objection a step further in a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, asking Health Canada to cease approving safe supply sites in the province and calling for a formal review of existing ones.
Last fall, Ontario halted approvals of any new supervised consumption and treatment sites while a review of their effectiveness was underway.
I'm on board for just about anything that will improve the lives of the ruinously drug-addicted, including safe-injection sites. But I also have first-hand experience of how traumatizing and blighting addiction can be - for the addict, the addict's family and friends, the many who are dragged into the orbit of the user. It is soul-crushing, it tears families apart, watching helplessly. It destroys people.
Addiction is a disease. But it is also, however tortuous, a choice. And it is a damnably hard choice to get clean. Enabling a user - someone already far beyond the bounds of reason and self-preservation - to stay in a dark, toxic place does them no good. Destigmatizing isn't a panacea, luring them towards treatment; it's a further seduction.
There is ample evidence of the counterproductive fallout from decriminalization in the B.C. experiment. It has severely confounded communities while doing precious little to ease tragedy for addicts.
Since the radical landmark project's launch 15 months ago, in a province where toxic drug deaths had soared - decriminalizing small amounts of drugs including meth, cocaine and heroin, and throwing open the gates to nearly all public areas where those drugs can be openly consumed unfettered without harassment from police - all the metrics have gone the wrong way.
A policy ostensibly aimed at slowing the addiction crisis and encouraging users to seek treatment instead resulted, in its first year, in overdoses rising by 25 per cent and fatalities reaching a record high. In 2023, a grim record was set of more than 2,500 deaths from suspected illicit drugs.
Faced with a furious backlash from the public and local politicians in cities and towns that have been beset by brazen drug use - with attendant crime and other social complications - B.C. earlier this month requested, and immediately received, permission to recriminalize hard drugs in public spaces, including in hospitals, on transit and in parks - effective immediately. Exemptions for possession of small amounts of illicit drugs for personal use will still apply in private residences, certain health-care clinics, places where people are "lawfully sheltering," and overdose and drug-checking sites.
It had become a grievous matter of safety and security.
How this outcome wasn't foreseen is beyond my understanding, except that the decriminalization movement has been driven by pro-decriminalization zealots, particularly in the health-care field. Blinded-by-enlightenment revolutionaries such as Eileen de Villa, Toronto's outgoing medical officer of health. De Villa continues to cling to her ideology, as does Mayor Olivia Chow.
As if toxic addiction is a disorder that can be alleviated by removing the stigma and providing extended treatment.
But B.C., since 2017, when the crisis first reached alarming heights, has opened 600 publicly funded substance-use treatment beds and 50 overdose prevention sites.
You know what? There are never enough beds and there will never be enough money to blunt the addiction crisis.
Holding Toronto hostage to the fantasies of fanatics helps neither the wretchedly addicted nor the stricken city. But the health cabal will keep bucking the evidence.
CREDIT: Rosie DiManno Toronto Star OPINION

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug overdose; Fatalities; Drug addiction; Substance abuse treatment; Drug use

Document 317 of 500

Ottawa rejects decriminalization plan: Federal government says proposal fails to protect public health, lacks provincial support

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-rejects-decriminalization-plan/docview/3056156025/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+rejects+decriminalization+plan%3A+Federal+government+says+proposal+fails+to+protect+public+health%2C+lacks+provincial+support&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government has rejected Toronto's request to decriminalize drug possession for personal use, ending weeks of sparring between three levels of government.
Toronto's proposal "does not adequately protect public health and maintain public safety. This includes concerns with feasibility and ability for law enforcement to implement the proposed model, protection of youth, and lack of support from key players including the Province of Ontario," said a statement released late Friday by Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks.
Premier Doug Ford posted a one-word response on X: "Good."
The federal minister's statement comes one day after Ontario's health minister Sylvia Jones sent a scathing letter to Toronto's medical officer of health calling the decriminalization plan "dangerous" and "misguided."
In a second letter, this time to the federal government on Friday, Ford added: "We do not, and never will, support their application to decriminalize illegal drugs. Please consider this our formal confirmation to the federal government that we are 100 per cent opposed."
Despite the province's position, Toronto's medical officer of health Dr. Eileen de Villa and Board of Health chair Chris Moise continued to back the "made-in-Toronto" plan, which differs from the B.C. decriminalization pilot by not setting limits on the amount of drugs that constitute personal use and extending decriminalization to youth. The plan included a range of responses to the toxic drug crisis, in addition to decriminalization.
"Decriminalization is one evidence-informed policy tool to help remove barriers to care," de Villa said in a statement Friday. "As Minister Saks has refused Toronto Public Health's request to decriminalize personal possession of controlled drugs and substances, the need to invest in other available evidence-based interventions is all the more critical."
That includes funding and access to treatment options, which are provincial responsibilities, she said. Wait times at free residential treatment programs can be more than a year, while at paid programs it can be up to 270 days.
Toronto's decriminalization plan, first submitted in 2022 and updated in 2023, was supported by Toronto police, who already lay few drug possession charges.
The top medical officers of health for Ontario and Canada have also endorsed decriminalization of drugs for personal use, with the aim of encouraging drug users to access health care and supports, and preventing the harm that can come from arrests and criminal records. At the federal government's direction in August 2020, prosecutors also only pursue extremely serious cases of drug possession.
However, B.C., which began a province-wide pilot early last year, recently recriminalized drug possession in public places in response to safety concerns over public drug use, a move that some advocates say has effectively gutted the approach and led Ford to call it "a failure."
The B.C. changes brought fresh scrutiny to Toronto's stalled proposal, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau effectively killing the plan by saying it would not be approved without provincial support. That didn't stop frequent criticism from opposition leader Pierre Poilievre.
"After pushing to decriminalize hard drug use in public places, (Trudeau) is backtracking in the hopes he can impose it after the election, when voters can't stop him," Poilievre responded on X to Friday's rejection of Toronto's application.
Earlier this month, Health Canada said the delay in deciding on Toronto's application was because Toronto had not responded to questions pertaining to public safety. De Villa, meanwhile, told the Star in a recent interview the city has tried to answer questions "to the best of our ability."
De Villa did not rule out changes to Toronto's proposal in light of new information emerging from B.C., but said she believes concerns over public drug use can be addressed without resorting to criminal charges. Decriminalization does not mean a total lack of enforcement, she said, adding that public health has been working with Toronto police.
De Villa stressed the city's approach is focused on saving lives, and noted the majority of the 500 yearly preventable drug toxicity deaths in Toronto happen in private residences.
Since 2016, there have been more than 42,000 deaths in Canada from the drug toxicity crisis and the number continues to climb. The majority are under 50.
On Friday, Ford shifted from decriminalization to asking the federal government to stop approving "safe supply" sites in the province.
In a letter to Trudeau released Friday, the premier said Health Canada's approval process leaves Ontario "completely in the dark" on where the sites are operating and the quantities of controlled and illegal drugs, such as opioids, that are handed out to users.
"This is frankly unacceptable, given its adverse effects on our communities," Ford wrote.
"For that reason, I'm calling on the federal government to immediately stop approving new sites and conduct a formal review of existing ones in the province."
In Winnipeg, Trudeau was asked about Ford's request, but did not say how he would respond.
"We have been very, very clear that the toxic drug crisis and the opioid epidemic is something that we need to respond to with the best science possible, with the best support for people struggling with addictions possible and with an emphasis on community safety," he told reporters.
"No order of government can do this alone. That's why we're focused on working with people on the front lines, whether it's at the provincial health systems or at the cities to keep people safe."
Ontario warned Toronto the province will soon announce "enhanced accountability measures" for existing drug consumption and treatment services sites to ensure public safety.
Approvals by the province for new consumption and treatment sites were paused last fall while their effectiveness was reviewed.
Experts including de Villa have pointed out that safe consumption sites not only prevent fatal drug overdoses, but they can also reduce public drug use.
Mayor Olivia Chow said Thursday that arresting people with drug addictions is not going to provide them with the help they need, and asked for more help with treatment services and the housing shortage that has made public drug use more common.
"All of this conversation about whether it should be criminalized or not criminalized, where does it leave us?" she said Thursday. "Nowhere. Absolutely nowhere."
Chow wrote her own letter to Trudeau on Friday appealing for help.
"I believe we share common ground on this critical issue," the mayor wrote.
"The increased risks of the toxic unregulated drug supply, coupled with the housing and affordability crisis, pose a serious threat to community health and well-being."
With files from Mark Ramzy
and Stephanie Levitz
CAPTION: Before Ottawa's rejection, Premier Doug Ford called on the federal government to conduct a formal review of existing safe consumption sites in the province.Richard LautensToronto Star
CREDIT: Alyshah Hasham Toronto Star Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau With files from Mark Ramzy and Stephanie Levitz

Document 318 of 500

There is worth in being true to our values: The student encampments make one clear demand. The problem is it almost certainly won't help Gaza, May 14

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/there-is-worth-being-true-our-values/docview/3056155727/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+is+worth+in+being+true+to+our+values%3A+The+student+encampments+make+one+clear+demand.+The+problem+is+it+almost+certainly+won%27t+help+Gaza%2C+May+14&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The student encampments make one clear demand. The problem is it almost certainly won't help Gaza, May 14
The U of T students' anti-genocide student encampment won't help Gaza.
Nor the ICJ decision that provisional measures are needed to uphold Palestinians' "plausible right" to be protected from genocide.
Nor the UN special rapporteur's report on the anatomy of the genocide.
Nor Canada's oft-stated commitment to a two-state solution.
Nor our illogical abstention in the UN vote on enhancing Palestine's UN presence.
Nor Australia's nor New Zealand's "yes" vote on the same UN vote.
Nor our halting new arms shipments while letting existing arms agreements flow.
Nor the voices of the Indigenous People of Turtle Island recognizing settler colonialism.
Nor all of Noam Chomsky's half century of explanations of the origins of the conflict.
Nor Ilan Pappé's history of the ethnic cleansing of Palestine.
Nor Rashid Khalidi's history of the "Hundred Years War on Palestine."
Nor Avi Shlaim's explanation of brutal forms of Zionism especially against Arab Jews.
Nor Norman Finkelstein's life spent documenting the plight of Gazans.
Nor Refaat Alareer's poem "If I must die."
Nor Gabor Matté's account of how he, a Holocaust child, left Zionism.
Nor Owen Jones' interviews trying to probe the hearts of those who support Israel's inhumane actions in Gaza.
Nor John Mearscheimer's realist analysis of the stupidity of U.S. foreign policy.
Nor Jewish Voices for Peace supporting non- and anti-Zionist Jews' stand against the war.
Nor Mehdi Hasan's call to Biden to stop supporting the catastrophe.
Nor university faculties calling out Israel's destruction of all of Gaza's universities and libraries.
Nor UNRWA calling for humanitarian help against the famine and epidemic in Gaza.
Nor Naomi Klein's call at Columbia University for a new Judaism without Zionism.
Nor me writing this letter, probably unpublished, to the Toronto Star.
None of us on our own can help Gaza, but maybe together ...
Ian Martin, Toronto
CAPTION: Protesters gather at the University of Toronto. Premier Doug Ford is voicing what many believe: It is time for university administrators to do their jobs regarding the racist elements on their campuses, writes Phyllis Levin from Toronto.

Document 319 of 500

No benefit for common people: We've seen claims of 'Common Sense' before and it wasn't pretty, Opinion, May 11

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-benefit-common-people/docview/3056155663/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+benefit+for+common+people%3A+We%27ve+seen+claims+of+%27Common+Sense%27+before+and+it+wasn%27t+pretty%2C+Opinion%2C+May+11&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We've seen claims of 'Common Sense' before and it wasn't pretty, Opinion, May 11
John Cartwright's opinion piece resonated with my whole family, who at the time of premier Mike Harris's regime were a pretty easygoing gathering of professionals in Sudbury. We might be justifiably categorized as slightly self-satisfied, successful, albeit hard-working middle-class people, joiners and helpers, summer cottage owners, and not too cynical or critical of governments.
My wife was a social worker, later the director of her department, at the main hospital in town. The cuts to health care by Harris didn't go unnoticed by our family. She had to work harder and longer than before, but she handled the challenges well. She was eulogized for her hard work, compassion, being a friend to her patients and staff; while at the same time a loving wife and mother for her family.
I was a partner in a small architectural office whose portfolio of work in progress consisted of three or four multi-unit seniors and social housing projects. We were suddenly, unexpectedly exposed to being unemployed in an instant. We did survive to live for other jobs and projects, but it was difficult for a couple of years; luckily our bank manager was good to us. Only a few friends and colleagues knew how close we were to insolvency. We had to let go a few of our staff. Many others were good to us too, including the client groups and associations whose funding sources were suddenly cut off and so they stopped paying our fees for months. Our cash flow dried up. The client groups expressed their sorrow and embarrassment to us.
Our two sons were in high school and university. We had educational saving funds for them, not enough for everything, so they worked part time to support their living expenses in other cities.
The older graduated with a master's in education but school boards were not hiring. He came to be associated with the international school system and has taught with his wife/classmate in many countries. They never looked back and are now principals of their expat schools in their current chosen country.
In their success, they were a big loss to education in Ontario. Our younger was studying philosophy and art, but after second year decided to go for a more practical profession. He eventually became a talented, creative chef and worked in France at upscale resorts, and at high-end fine dining establishments in Toronto and Oakville.
Who had the "common sense?" I conclude Harris had plenty of it for benefitting himself. For the regular, "common" people, not so much.
Art Peach, Sudbury, Ont.
I too shudder at hearing Poilievre use the term
Thanks for the reminder as to what the "Common Sense Revolution" was all about under Conservative premier Mike Harris in the mid-1990s.
John Cartwright is right on in describing the cuts made to affordable housing, social programs, health care, schools and downloading to the municipalities at that time. Twenty-five years later, we continue to live with the results of those short-sighted changes. Let's not forget the selling off of the toll Highway 407 to the private sector - a highway which could have brought in much needed revenue to the provincial coffers if ownership had been retained by the province. I too shudder at hearing "common sense" used by Pierre Poilievre for I know what he really means and it won't be different from what we saw in Ontario years ago - support for private business and corporate interests, cuts to public services.
Vicki Smith, Scarborough
Harris left a
strong legacy of 'common sense'
Sorry to see your editorial page gave space to John Cartwright's painfully misinformed and shamelessly partisan revisiting of the Mike Harris legacy. As we have seen with the recent passing of former prime minister Brian Mulroney, it often takes time for the long-term impacts of substantive policy decisions by brave leaders to be acknowledged. In the Harris case, he inherited a broken province after the lost decade of Liberal leader David Petersen and NDP leader Bob Rae.
Despite the controversies of the time, very few of his highly consequential policy changes were in fact reversed. Why? Because they were the right thing to do and Harris's Liberal successors understood that completely. Your own Robert Benzie reviewed a recent collection of essays published as "The Harris Legacy" which revealed this reality in policy area after policy area. Among the documented results of Harris reforms were improved reading scores in elementary and secondary schools.
Gary Collins, Acton
CAPTION: Among the documented results of former Ontario premier Mike Harris's reforms were improved reading scores in elementary and secondary schools, writes reader Gary Collins from Acton.Paul Hourigan The Hamilton Spectator file photo

Document 320 of 500

Province moves to fast-track Ontario Place redevelopment: Zoning order exempts project from city controls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-moves-fast-track-ontario-place/docview/3056155617/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+moves+to+fast-track+Ontario+Place+redevelopment%3A+Zoning+order+exempts+project+from+city+controls&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government is using an "enhanced" minister's zoning order to speed up construction as it moves ahead with changes to Ontario Place.
The zoning order, called an eMZO, will "provide development permissions for new and existing land uses at Ontario Place," said a statement from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma.
"The eMZO is a land use planning tool that will allow the province to maintain site plan control for portions of the site and make much-needed improvements to the publicly accessible open space on the East Island as part of the rebuild of Ontario Place. The eMZO will also allow for expanded public spaces and parks on the West Island."
Ash Milton, Surma's press secretary, said an "enhanced ministers zoning order gives the minister additional powers as it relates to site plan. In this case, the eMZO will exempt certain parts of the Ontario Place site from the municipal site plan process."
Infrastructure Ontario has already put out a "request for qualifications" as it looks to hire a team to design, build and finance the new Ontario Science Centre, which is to be located at Ontario Place's waterfront site.
Surma's announcement also noted that the government is "working with Live Nation to create an updated, year-round concert venue at Ontario Place" for a crowd of 20,000.
Ontario Place will also have 50 acres of green space and beach, as well as a privately run spa.
Late last year, Premier Doug Ford announced a $9-billion deal with the city, with the province uploading responsibility for the Gardiner Expressway and the Don Valley Parkway and providing funding for housing and new subway trains. At the same time, Mayor Olivia Chow agreed to the controversial development at Ontario Place.
Surma has previously said "we just have to make a decision and move on" and noted that dozens of environmental assessments and other related studies have already been done on the site.
The NDP has said it opposes giving a 95-year lease to Therme for the spa, and also moving the Ontario Science Centre to the site.
Enhanced MZOs, introduced in 2020, are a relatively new tool for the minister. This one allows the government to "establish land use permissions for the entire site; establish detailed development permissions (i.e. height, gross floor area, and setback requirements); exempt some parts of the site from municipal site plan control; and direct that the City of Toronto's Official Plan cannot prevent the issuance of any licence, permits, or approvals for Ontario Place."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Zoning

Full text: Addressing the issues will help calm campuses
Doug Ford is right. I too am 'fed up' with the anti-Israel protests,
May 15
Mark McQueen needs to explain why he believes that Canada should stand by while yet another genocide is taking place. Instead, he complains about things that inconvenience him and misrepresents what the protesters are doing.
For example, none of the protesters are harassing Jewish Canadians. In fact, many of the protesters are Jewish Canadians. The ones who feel "harassed" are those who equate the Zionist movement with Judaism - a mistake akin to equating Nazism with being German.
Perhaps the boiling frog analogy McQueen tries to shoehorn into his narrative would be better used to describe the 75 years of Israel's failure to negotiate a just peace with the people it displaced.
We are so used to seeing Israel grab more and more territory while continuing to kill Palestinians with impunity that it rarely makes the news.
Yet this is daily life for Palestinians.
Sorry if McQueen feels inconvenienced by protesters, but the solution isn't to ban protests, but rather to address the issues they are calling attention to. We declared "none is too many" to Jewish refugees fleeing the Nazis. We stood by for decades while South Africa segregated its people by race and did little while Hutus slaughtered Tutsis in Rwanda.
It's long past time that we called for Israel to negotiate a just peace with the Palestinians.
Gary Dale, Scarborough
It's time for university leaders to do their jobs
Mark McQueen clearly points out the obvious. Too many protesters claiming they are chanting for the freedom of Palestinians are behaving like anarchists. They have been disrupting the lives of Torontonians for far too long.
Premier Doug Ford is voicing what many believe - it is time for university administrators to do their jobs regarding the racist elements on their campuses. It is time to uphold and enforce our laws.
The mission statement of the University of Toronto states: The university "is dedicated to fostering an academic community in which the learning and scholarship of every member may flourish, with vigilant protection for individual human rights, and a resolute commitment to the principles of equal opportunity, equity and justice."
This statement is similar to the mission statements of most universities.
It is time for university leaders to step up to the plate and start behaving in ways that show a commitment to what, they claim, is the backbone of their institutions. If they cannot or will not do this, they should be replaced.
Phyllis Levin, Toronto

Ford government issues 'enhanced' minister's zoning order to fast-track Ontario Place development

Document 323 of 500

Why it was time to say goodbye to the idea of drug decriminalization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-was-time-say-goodbye-idea-drug/docview/3056241614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+it+was+time+to+say+goodbye+to+the+idea+of+drug+decriminalization&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At this moment, there is a poor soul, drug-stupefied, sprawled on the pavement outside my home. It's 11 o'clock in the morning.
Some time back, two young people were found dead - overdosed, likely from fentanyl-laced drugs - beneath the back porch.
Half a block away, outside a drug-injection site, a cluster of users panhandle aggressively while joggers, moms pushing strollers, kids on their way to school, steer a wide berth.
A few weeks ago, property managers of my building sealed shut and barred a street-level entrance - removed the handle, disabled the security access controls - because intruders had repeatedly jimmied open the door, then broke into units.
Desperate needs make for desperate criminality.
I don't use "recreational'' drugs - such a benign descriptor for a social scourge - soft or hard. But the rampant drug use all around me has made drugs my problem, my neighbourhood's problem. I can't fix it, social agencies can't fix it, cops can't fix it, except sporadically. The Toronto Board of Health sure as hell can't fix it.
And the board's push to decriminalize drugs - following in the footsteps of a disastrous pilot project in British Columbia, which had the goal of inducing addicts towards treatment and preventing them from dying alone in back alleys, a holistic approach - would have only made the problem exponentially worse.
It can't always or primarily be about the torment and misfortune of drug addicts. There's neighbourhood and community health, too - what makes a city livable. The board of health, which should be alert to the well-being of everyone, has given most of the city's residents short shrift. Certainly pockets of Toronto where high levels of open drug use, poverty, homelessness, dangerous street sex work and crime have coalesced can already give Vancouver's Downtown Eastside a run for its money. Homelessness, for one, doesn't equate to criminality, though encampments contribute to a sense of fear and do-not-go-near in neighbourhoods. But many of these ills are interconnected.
And it's not as if Toronto police are hounding drug users: Last year they made a mere 15 arrests for single counts of possession, mostly related to other enforcement actions, such as drugs found on an individual or in a car in an unrelated criminal offence. Many people may be under the misconception that hard drugs have already been decriminalized, as was cannabis, precisely because law enforcement rarely intervenes.
The board's 2022 application to Health Canada - to decriminalize small amounts of illegal drugs for personal use - was on Friday rebuffed by Ottawa, on the grounds that the proposal didn't adequately protect public health and maintain public safety. The federal government had previously stated that approval wouldn't be granted unless Queen's Park was onside, although the messaging was mixed and confusing. In recent days, there has been a flurry of letters from the province to the federal government - and the health board and city hall - from the province, serving notice that legalizing hard drugs isn't happening, characterizing the board's intention as "dangerous.''
Premier Doug Ford took the objection a step further in a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, asking Health Canada to cease approving safe supply sites in the province and calling for a formal review of existing ones.
Last fall, Ontario halted approvals of any new supervised consumption and treatment sites while a review of their effectiveness was underway.
I'm on board for just about anything that will improve the lives of the ruinously drug-addicted, including safe-injection sites. But I also have first-hand experience of how traumatizing and blighting addiction can be - for the addict, the addict's family and friends, the many who are dragged into the orbit of the user. It is soul-crushing, it tears families apart, watching helplessly. It destroys people.
Addiction is a disease. But it is also, however tortuous, a choice. And it is a damnably hard choice to get clean. Enabling a user - someone already far beyond the bounds of reason and self-preservation - to stay in a dark, toxic place does them no good. Destigmatizing isn't a panacea, luring them towards treatment; it's a further seduction.
There is ample evidence of the counterproductive fallout from decriminalization in the B.C. experiment. It has severely confounded communities while doing precious little to ease tragedy for addicts. Since the radical landmark project's launch 15 months ago, in a province where toxic drug deaths had soared - decriminalizing small amounts of drugs including meth, cocaine and heroin, and throwing open the gates to nearly all public areas where those drugs can be openly consumed unfettered without harassment from police - all the metrics have gone the wrong way.
A policy ostensibly aimed at slowing the addiction crisis and encouraging users to seek treatment instead resulted, in its first year, in overdoses rising by 25 per cent and fatalities reaching a record high. In 2023, a grim record was set of more than 2,500 deaths from suspected illicit drugs.
Faced with a furious backlash from the public and local politicians in cities and towns that have been beset by brazen drug use - with attendant crime and other social complications - B.C. earlier this month requested, and immediately received, permission to recriminalize hard drugs in public spaces, including in hospitals, on transit and in parks - effective immediately. Exemptions for possession of small amounts of illicit drugs for personal use will still apply in private residences, certain health-care clinics, places where people are "lawfully sheltering,'' and overdose and drug-checking sites.
It had become a grievous matter of safety and security.
How this outcome wasn't foreseen is beyond my understanding, except that the decriminalization movement has been driven by pro-decriminalization zealots, particularly in the health-care field. Blinded-by-enlightenment revolutionaries such as Eileen de Villa, Toronto's outgoing medical officer of health. De Villa continues to cling to her ideology, as does Mayor Olivia Chow. As if toxic addiction is a disorder that can be alleviated by removing the stigma and providing extended treatment.
But B.C., since 2017, when the crisis first reached alarming heights, has opened 600 publicly funded substance-use treatment beds and 50 overdose prevention sites.
You know what? There are never enough beds and there will never be enough money to blunt the addiction crisis.
Holding Toronto hostage to the fantasies of fanatics helps neither the wretchedly addicted nor the stricken city. But the health cabal will keep bucking the evidence.
CAPTION: The focus can't always be about the torment and misfortune of drug addicts, writes Rosie DiManno. There's neighbourhood and community health, too - what makes a city livable.

Subject: Decriminalization; Drug overdose; Fatalities; Drug addiction; Neighborhoods; Substance abuse treatment; Drug use

Company / organization: Name: Health Canada; NAICS: 923120

Title: Why it was time to say goodbye to the idea of drug decriminalization

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 17, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndicatio n Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document 324 of 500

More ServiceOntario locations could move into private retail stores after $10M Staples expansion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-serviceontario-locations-could-move-into/docview/3055933544/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+ServiceOntario+locations+could+move+into+private+retail+stores+after+%2410M+Staples+expansion&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial government is looking for more private retail partners to host ServiceOntario locations, after its pilot program moved six ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores at the beginning of the year.
A request for qualifications was issued by the government on Wednesday, "to develop a roster of qualified retail partners willing to offer lease space across Ontario to host future ServieOntario centres," Matthew D'Amico, a spokesperson for the minister of public and business service delivery, said in a statement.
The Ford government earlier in the year announced a $10.2 million partnership with the stationary big box store, a move opposition parties said was inching public services toward privatization, while the government contended that the new partnership would lead to savings for taxpayers.
D'Amico emphasized that there are currently 193 privately run ServiceOntario locations (in addition to 80 that are publicly run) and said, "our partnerships with retailers have no impact on publicly run ServiceOntario centres."
The new call for retailers to pitch their case to host ServiceOntario locations requires the retailer to have convenient locations that meet the government's service coverage criteria and provide a "cost-effective lease space," according to the proposal.
Global News previously reported that other major retailers - like Ikea, Costco, Loblaws and Rexall - were all considered as potential landing spots for ServiceOntario, while Staples Canada is set to receive $8.29 million over three years to host its new ServiceOntario locations.
The Staples partnership - worth $10.2 million for operating, labour and retrofit costs - will lead to an estimated $1 million in savings for the government, according to D'Amico, but "the exact savings will be better understood in the future as we further analyze the impacts of this pilot."
Why are there ServiceOntario locations in Staples?
In January, the Ontario government made the move to shutter the doors of six ServiceOntario locations and replace them with new counters within Staples stores.
The decision to move ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores drew widespread attention after initial reporting from CityNews. The continued media attention on the move, at one point had Premier Doug Ford telling CityNews reporter Richard Southern at a news conference, "Buddy, you gotta get a new story."
ServiceOntario is already operating in private retail stores even before the Staples deal, with branches within Canadian Tire stores, including at its Yonge-Davenport and St. Clair-Old Stock Yards locations. The partnership began in 2010 under then-premier Dalton McGuinty.
NDP MPP Catherine Fife said, in January, that the ServiceOntario-Staples partnership was "another attempt for Ford to quietly hand over our public services to private corporations," and lamented the "uncertainty" that some public servants would face if ServiceOntario locations were to close.
In the same vein, Green Leader Mike Schreiner took aim the "back room deals" that he alleges the Ford government has prioritized over everyday Ontarians, and questioned why Staples was chosen as the government's partner.
Previous governments had also tried introducing automated ServiceOntario kiosks, but those were permanently shut down in 2012 due to security concerns.
But, the human element hasn't been without its issues.
In December 2023, several ServiceOntario employees were charged in Toronto police's Project Safari investigation and were alleged to have provided criminals with vehicle and driver information.
CAPTION: The government is exploring more partnerships with private retailers to host ServiceOntario locations, similar to the deal it inked with Staples Canada earlier this year.

Subject: Retail stores; Public services

Business indexing term: Subject: Retail stores

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Document 325 of 500

Doug Ford asks Justin Trudeau to stop approving safe drug supply sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-asks-justin-trudeau-stop-approving-safe/docview/3055933452/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+asks+Justin+Trudeau+to+stop+approving+safe+drug+supply+sites&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stepping up his fight against Toronto's bid to decriminalize drugs, Premier Doug Ford is now asking the federal government to stop approving "safe supply" sites in the province.
In a letter to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau released Friday, the premier said Health Canada's approval process leaves Ontario "completely in the dark" on where the sites are operating and the quantities of controlled and illegal drugs, such as opioids, that are handed out to users.
"This is frankly unacceptable, given its adverse effects on our communities," Ford wrote.
"For that reason, I'm calling on the federal government to immediately stop approving new sites and conduct a formal review of existing ones in the province."
The two-page letter follows what Ford's government says is a "dangerous" push by Toronto to have Health Canada decriminalize small amounts of illegal drugs for personal use.
Toronto board of health chair Chris Moise on Thursday rejected the province's demand that the city drop its application, saying, "I have no intention of doing that."
Moise said the application is a small part of the city's plan to ease a drug crisis killing about 500 people annually and called on the province to provide a 24/7 crisis treatment centre to help people with addictions.
In his letter to Trudeau, Ford, who has previously said he would fight decriminalization "tooth and nail," reiterates his opposition.
"As we've already indicated to Toronto Public Health, we do not, and never will, support their application to decriminalize illegal drugs. Please consider this our formal confirmation to the federal government that we are 100 per cent opposed."
Moise acknowledged Thursday that the city's application to Health Canada is effectively stalled because the federal government has said it would not be approved unless the Ontario government is on board.
Ford said he applauds the federal government for taking that position following the "failure and subsequent reversal of decriminalizing hard drugs in B.C."
"In B.C., a province one-third the population of Ontario, there are three times as many deaths involving dangerous opioids," Ford wrote.
He noted there are concerns that hard drugs from safe supply sites on the West coast have been diverted and traded "for more lethal and harmful drugs like fentanyl."
Earlier this month, British Columbia asked the federal government to recriminalize public drug possession, following a pilot project on decriminalization under which overdoses rose by 25 per cent in the first year.
Ontario warned Toronto the province will soon announce "enhanced accountability measures" for existing drug consumption and treatment services sites" to ensure public safety.
Approvals by the province for new consumption and treatment sites were paused last fall while their effectiveness was reviewed.
Mayor Olivia Chow said Thursday that arresting people with drug addictions is not going to provide them with the help they need, and asked for more help with treatment services and the housing shortage that has made public drug use more common.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford is shown at Queen's Park on May 6, 2024.

Subject: Drugs; Decriminalization; Federal government; Narcotics

People: Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Health Canada; NAICS: 923120

Title: Doug Ford asks Justin Trudeau to stop approving safe drug supply sites

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 17, 2024

Section: #inhousearticle_thestar

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 326 of 500

Party money: Is it time to change how we finance politics?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/party-money-is-time-change-how-we-finance/docview/3055933335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Party+money%3A+Is+it+time+to+change+how+we+finance+politics%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hours after Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was expelled from the House of Commons for calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "a wacko" and refusing to withdraw his comments, both leaders' parties had issued fundraising emails pointing to the incident on Parliament Hill and asking for donors for more cash.
Fundraising is a huge part of what political parties do, it keeps their operations going and builds their war chest for the next election campaign. It also helps to buy them votes. The more money they have, the more they can spend on advertising and organization, and the more votes they can get. Since the end of the per-vote subsidy, parties are now dependent on public donations and those who donate tend to be more partisan and more ideologically extreme. So what does that mean for our politics?
On this episode of "It's Political," we take a look at party financing. First, we'll take a look at the current rules, how they evolved and who they give voice to. Then, we'll head up to the Hill to get some MPs' views on the topic, before finally sitting down with three former party operatives to break down the perceived challenges of our current system and how it could be reformed to reduce the influence of those with deeper pockets.
Some of the clips this week were sourced from the House of Commons, CPAC, the CBC, the Liberal Party of Canada, the NDP, the Conservative Party of Canada, the National Citizens Coalition's YouTube channel, CNBC, WTOL, and PBS,
Listen here and follow or subscribe at Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, Google Podcasts or wherever you listen to your favourite podcasts.
This episode of "It's Political" was produced by Althia Raj and Michal Stein. Joy SpearChief-Morris assisted with production. Kevin Sexton mixed the show. Our theme music is by Isaac Joel.
In this episode: Former Conservative Party of Canada national campaign manager and former director of operations Fred DeLorey, former Liberal Party of Canada national director Sheila Gervais, former NDP director of operations David Hare, former Conservative Party of Canada director of fundraising Jaime Girard, co-founder of Democracy Watch Duff Conacher, Canada 2020 CEO and former senior director of communications for the Liberal Party of Canada Braeden Caley, McMaster University political science associate professor Andrea Lawlor, Royal Military College political science associate professor Holly Ann Garnett, Acadia University Politics Professor Alex Marland, Green Party Leader Elizabeth May, Calgary Conservative MP Greg McLean, Ajax Liberal MP and Health Minister Mark Holland, Langley-Aldergrove MP Tako Van Popta, and Montreal-area Lac-Saint-Louis Liberal MP Francis Scarpaleggia.
Selected Readings:
Funding Political Parties: Can a Fair System be Devised?Reforming Electoral Democracy, Volume 1Attorney General of Ontario v. Working Families Coalition (Canada) Inc., et al.Canada Elections Act
CAPTION: On this episode of "It's Political," we head up to the Hill to get some MPs' views on the topic of party financing.

Subject: College professors; Podcasts; Digital broadcasting; Political science; Canada Elections Act; Political advertising; Political parties

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Document 327 of 500

Cars just keep getting stolen in Ontario. Doug Ford's 'useless proposal' won't fix the problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cars-just-keep-getting-stolen-ontario-doug-fords/docview/3055933330/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cars+just+keep+getting+stolen+in+Ontario.+Doug+Ford%27s+%27useless+proposal%27+won%27t+fix+the+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Revoking licence is ineffective
Car thieves could lose driver's licence for life, Ford government says, May 14
Ontario's proposed driver's licence ban for convicted car thieves does not go far enough! Let's really take the bull by the horns! Sterilize convicted car thieves so that they cannot pass on their genes to subsequent generations! That should really put a dent in the problem! Seriously, folks, take a deep breath. This is the kind of ineffective measure to which fake populists often resort to make it seem as if they're doing something, rather than just pandering to the worst instincts of their supporters.
Gerry A. Corcoran, Toronto
Here Premier Doug Ford's government goes again grandstanding with hyped up announcements. Does anyone believe that a car thief who is willing to use violence or a weapon during the theft is going to worry about a licence suspension? Is a person who chooses to drive impaired by alcohol or drugs really going to change their behaviour because the driving suspension will go from three days to seven days for a first offence and seven to 14 for a second offence?
Greg Sheehan, Mississauga
Our governments should band together to force car manufacturers to improve fob security rather than spend taxpayers' money to police, charge and imprison. Focusing and solving the cause rather than outcome would save us a lot of time and money! It's a no-brainer.
Larry Peloso, Toronto
What a completely useless proposal after thinking that thieves, potentially armed, are going to be worried about getting a ticket for driving without a valid licence. Why do they get three chances? Most of these stolen cars end up being exported very quickly. Why not investigate these thieves as being members of organized crime, which many ultimately are? Such criminal punishments would be a much better deterrent.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge
Pardon my cynicism, but aside from allowing the Ontario government to go around flaunting that they are being tough on crime, the proposed new legislation where convicted cars thieves would lose they driver's licence for 10 or 15 years will have little, if any, impact on deterring crime. Let's face it, having a suspended driver's licence does not deter most offenders from driving.
Anthony Martins, Elora, Ont.
A lifetime driving ban after a third conviction is like fighting for peace. Perhaps the first conviction should include a jail sentence. Having driven for over 60 years, I can count on two fingers how many times I have been stopped by the police and asked to produce my driver's licence.
Wilf McQuinn, Huntsville
Listen to Izzeldin Abuelaish
I am in Canada. My family is constantly reliving the Nakba in Gaza. To me, it's clear what Palestinians need from the international community: Abuelaish, May 15
I concur with what Palestinian-Canadian refugee Izzeldin Abuelaish has said in his article. I would not call what is happening a war. While Hamas is the target, innocent Palestinians have been killed, with no means of defence and nowhere to escape. Gaza has been pounded and razed and would take many years of hard work to rebuild. It boggles my mind and breaks my heart how we humans can do to others what we would not have done to us!
Beatrice Cleary, North York
No one has more moral authority to declare what Palestinians need than Izzeldin Abuelaish. Yet, the Palestinians are caught up in a war between superpowers. Long ago, the Arab League proved its unwillingness to do much for the Palestinians, a tragic people victimized by their own leaders and the indifference and hostility of those around them.
Ron Charach, Toronto
I have been following Izzeldin Abuelaish's harrowing and tragic life since his home in the Gaza Strip was demolished in an Israeli airstrike despite being given assurances that it would not be targeted. He lost daughters and close family. After closely reading his column, it occurred to me that here is a man who will not hate. That could bring much clarity to the resolution of this horrific conflict. Perhaps he should be strongly considered to be a part of the mediation process. As he so poignantly stated: "Bring peace to a land that was created for peace and never saw a peaceful day."
Grace Taylor, Streetsville
Toronto's waterfront woes
Floating patios. Attractive retail. Bike valets. How a new vision could revive Toronto's 'underwhelming' waterfront, May 15
Reporter Francine Kopun's article on Toronto's waterfront explores ideas for making the waterfront a more interesting place for tourists and residents of the city. Unfortunately, it may be too late. For some unknown reason, Toronto has succeeded in creating one of the ugliest waterfronts in North America. The west side of Queen's Quay has many nice features, but the east side of Queen's Quay, from Yonge to Parliament, is like a trip up Bay Street with nothing but big, ugly buildings blocking the view of the waterfront.
From what I've learned, this total ugliness was not created by Waterfront Toronto. Waterfront Toronto did a great job on the west end of the harbourfront. As I understand it, responsibility of the eastern stretch of the waterfront fell elsewhere. Who knows why they did it, but it's a terrible result. And then, there's the money-losing island airport, which needs subsides to stay alive. We'd be better off with the whole island as the city's Central Park, but apparently it's not the city, but Bay Street, that seems to own the waterfront. So much for the city's waterfront playground. Maybe next century, the city might have a chance to do it better.
Max Moore, Toronto
Construction at Scotiabank Arena
Scotiabank Arena unveils second phase of renovations, May 14
It appears Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment has come up with the Toronto version of putting lipstick on a pig. If you continually put a losing team on the ice at playoff time and then use the extra days while the players are booking tee times to spruce up the place for a better fan experience, that is code meant to distract you from the fact that fans don't get their money's worth. They certainly have their priorities in order.
Terry Kushnier, Scarborough
CAPTION: The federal plan to combat thefts can't come soon enough. The crisis is worsening and the onus must be placed on car manufacturers to do more to make cars more difficult to steal. Law enforcement can only do so much.

Subject: Automobile theft; Palestinian people

Location: Gaza Strip

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Nova Scotia; NAICS: 522110

Title: Cars just keep getting stolen in Ontario. Doug Ford's 'useless proposal' won't fix the problem

Reporter Francine Kopun's article on Toronto's waterfront explores ideas for making the waterfront a more interesting place for tourists and residents of the city. Unfortunately, it may be too late. For some unknown reason, Toronto has succeeded in creating one of the ugliest waterfronts in North America. The west side of Queens Quay has many nice features, but the east side of Queens Quay, from Yonge to Parliament, is like a trip up Bay Street with nothing but big, ugly buildings blocking the view of the waterfront.
From what I've learned, this total ugliness was not created by Waterfront Toronto. Waterfront Toronto did a great job on the west end of the harbourfront. As I understand it, responsibility of the eastern stretch of the waterfront fell elsewhere. Who knows why they did it, but it's a terrible result. And then, there's the money-losing island airport, which needs subsides to stay alive. We'd be better off with the whole island as the city's Central Park, but apparently it's not the city, but Bay Street, that seems to own the waterfront. So much for the city's waterfront playground. Maybe next century, the city might have a chance to do it better.
Max Moore, Toronto

Subject: Automobile theft; Palestinian people

Location: Gaza Strip

Title: Letters:   Scotiabank Arena unveils second phase of renovations, Car thieves could lose driver's licence for life, Ford government says, I am in Canada. My family is constantly reliving the Nakba in Gaza. To me, it's clear what Palestinians need from the international community: Abuelaish, Floating patios. Attractive retail. Bike valets. How a new vision could revive Toronto's 'underwhelming' waterfront,

Document 329 of 500

Tories, city spar over drug exemption request: Province pans plan to allow narcotics, while health officials refuse to back down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-city-spar-over-drug-exemption-request/docview/3055842579/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories%2C+city+spar+over+drug+exemption+request%3A+Province+pans+plan+to+allow+narcotics%2C+while+health+officials+refuse+to+back+down&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto is refusing to back down from what Premier Doug Ford's government calls a "dangerous" push to have Health Canada decriminalize illegal drugs.
The battle lines hardened Thursday after Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner released a blunt two-page letter to Toronto medical officer of health Dr. Eileen de Villa.
It was a formal notification that Ontario is "100 per cent opposed" and warned of consequences if Toronto's proposal to decriminalize small amounts of illegal drugs for personal use is not scrapped.
"I have no intention of doing that," Toronto board of health chair Chris Moise declared hours after the letter was released publicly.
"If Toronto Public Health fails to rescind its misguided application, we will be forced to explore all options available to us," Jones and Kerzner warned in the letter.
"We've seen what happens in other jurisdictions. We don't want to have more accessibility of illegal drugs, of illicit drugs, of dangerous drugs in our streets," the solicitor general told reporters later.
Moise acknowledged the federal government has said the application will not be granted without provincial approval - effectively stalling it.
Both Moise and Mayor Olivia Chow defended the decriminalization approach as a small part of a plan to tackle a crisis that is killing more than 500 Torontonians a year.
They criticized the province's progress on providing affordable treatment options without waiting lists that can stretch up to a year or longer.
"We need a 24/7 crisis centre so people can actually get the help they need. Right now there isn't one. Our hospitals are inundated," Moise said. "The province needs to work with us."
The letter from Queen's Park said the city's approach flies in the face of evidence from British Columbia, which got federal approval this month to recriminalize public drug possession following a decriminalization pilot project that saw overdoses rise by 25 per cent in the first year.
"The recent disastrous examples of British Columbia and other jurisdictions that have attempted this experiment are just the latest examples that show decriminalization does not work," the letter continues.
"Instead, it encourages dangerous behaviour in public spaces, victimizes innocent people and undermines law enforcement's ability to protect our communities.
"Under no circumstances will our government ever support your request, which would only add to crime and public drug use while doing nothing to support people struggling with addiction."
The letter adds the province will make its opposition "clear" to the federal government.
In the meantime, Ontario will also "begin enacting enhanced accountability measures for existing consumption and treatment services site to ensure that the safety and the well-being of the public is protected," the cabinet ministers pledged.
A spokesperson for the Health Ministry said details will be worked out in the coming weeks.
Health Canada is reviewing Toronto's 2022 application for decriminalization. The city maintains that criminalizing drug possession makes it more difficult for drug users to get the aid and supports they need, such as improved mental health service and harm reduction strategies.
Last fall, Ontario halted approvals of any new supervised consumption and treatments sites while a review of their effectiveness was underway.
Chow said it is clear to everyone, including police, that arresting someone with a drug addiction isn't going to help them.
"People should not have to wait almost a year for treatment programs," she told reporters, saying far more needs to be done to address the housing crisis, which has made public drug use a more common problem and prevents people from accessing the care and support they need.
De Villa said in a statement Thursday that "decriminalization is one tool, supported by the best available evidence, to address the drug toxicity epidemic" that kills more than 500 Torontonians a year.
Toronto's exemption request was made following consultation with health-care experts, police, people with lived experience of drug use and service providers, at the request of Toronto's board of health.
"Given the current state of the epidemic, Toronto Public Health prioritizes saving lives ... I support increased access to treatment and harm reduction services, which are life-saving and serve as the front door to health care and other supports," de Villa said.
"I agree with the province on the need for increased investment in prevention, increased access to health-care interventions, and whole-of-society solutions including housing and supportive housing. All community members should feel safe and supported as we work to address this epidemic."
With files from Ben Spurr
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Alyshah Hasham Toronto Stars With files from Ben Spurr

Subject: Public health; Decriminalization; Harm reduction; Drug use; Federal government; Political parties

Why retirement homes should be publicly not privately owned

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-retirement-homes-should-be-publicly-not/docview/3055508326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+retirement+homes+should+be+publicly+not+privately+owned&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Mississauga retirement home is evicting its residents - but some are refusing to budge. Turns out they have the same rights as tenants, May 14
Retirement homes should be publicly not privately owned for the same reason we don't allow private interests to own hospitals. Care facilities of this kind cannot be at the whim of owners looking for quick profits and willing to throw those in need out the door. This is a disgrace and any self-respecting government would act to end this. Unfortunately, that is not what the Doug Ford government cares about. It will probably argue that the rental homes proposed for the property will ease the housing crisis. And after all these aging residents won't be voting for too much longer.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Are cops and lawyers watching too much crime TV?
Baby's death was a tragedy. Did it have to be a crime? May 12
A few weeks ago, we heard about the injustice experienced by Umar Zameer, where the heading could have also read "Officers death a tragedy. Did it have to be a crime?" or just last week we read about the death of visiting grandparents and their grandchild created mainly by an unnecessary chase by police. In these cases, as well as what Lindsay Hewson was subjected to, the culprits seem to be over exuberant law enforcement officers, and prosecutors, whom I fear have been taking their cues from the Hollywood cop and courtroom shows where everything is about the drama and the chase and little to do with reality or the well-being of people, allowing the truth to be muddied by innuendo and unfounded assumptions.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Apply social media research to preschool kids
Fighting corrosive effects of social media, May 10
I was fortunate to be in the audience of the Democracy Forum looking at the impact of social media and mobiles on local schools on May 8 at Toronto Metropolitan University. It seems to me the current efforts to control social media use in schools are a bit wrong-headed. The youth in schools now are addicted and deserve support. However, to keep this addiction problem from happening in the future, would it not make sense to take research and intervention etc. to the preschool level and maximize parental involvement?
Carol Rawson, Toronto
All talk, little action on the net-zero frontTransforming our concrete jungles into carbon sinks is essential to reach climate goals, May 12
Madison Savilow says, "in Canada, with its commitment to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050." There is no tangible evidence of such a commitment. If Canada were committed to the net-zero target, surely by now we would have seen some significant progress toward reaching that goal. We have not. Reaching net zero is a more difficult task than defeating Germany in the Second World War, yet Canada's willingness to make the necessary sacrifices, falls far short of the commitment it displayed from 1939 to 1945. Our commitment today is to talk without useful action.
Patrick Cowan, North York
The future of the federal carbon levy is just another tax
The federal carbon levy and rebate process is modelled on British Columbia's 2008 Carbon Tax. B.C.'s provincial government promised the tax would be revenue neutral for all residents and adhered to that promise. After a few years, that changed, and it became just another tax with revenues feeding provincial coffers. This predicts the future of the federal carbon levy.
R.A. Bennett, Victoria, B.C.
Mantas' questionable expenses should be deducted from his paycheque
Don't let one bad trip to Italy ruin our city council's need to travel. Toronto can learn from other cities, May 12
City councillors got it all wrong when they reimbursed Councillor Nick Mantas for his questionable expenses during a trip to Turin, Italy. If they wanted to teach Mantas a good lesson and show taxpayers that they respect the use of public funds, they should have calculated reasonable expenses for the trip and the excess amount spent by Mantas should be deducted from his paycheque.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Take complaints of discrimination from all groups seriously
Why student encampments and protests should be encouraged, May 11
Faisal Kutty included many interesting points, but one argument is remarkably outdated - surely, we've learned in recent years to take complaints of discrimination seriously. When women complain of misogyny, society has learned to take those concerns seriously. Society has learned to take Black Canadians' complaints of anti-Black racism seriously. We've learned not to tell minorities they're wrong to feel hurt by others' actions - even when the hurt is unintended. So why do Kutty and so many others insist on telling Jews when we are allowed to experience discrimination and when we are not? When we have the right to feel hurt and when we don't? The fact that some Jews sincerely separate their Jewish identity from Israel doesn't erase the intense connection that many other Jews feel with Israel and the thousand different types of agony that arise from that connection. Simply telling us that we shouldn't be hurt erases one of the most important lessons progressive societies have learned: to take our neighbours hurt as seriously as we take our own.
Robert Steiner, Toronto
Eschew violence, engage in peaceful negotiations
Faisal Kutty argues that civil disobedience includes vandalism and violence and is a feature of a mature democracy. Au contraire, in a mature democracy these criminal acts would be prosecuted not valued. It wasn't violence which got rid of slavery, apartheid or the Vietnam War. It was democratic legislation in Congress, Mandela's campaign of respect, kindness and reconciliation, and peaceful protest ("Make love, not war!"). The violence came from those wanting to keep things as they were. I am appalled that Kutty promotes change through actions which include violence. The Palestinians will make larger and faster strides toward independence and autonomy through eschewing violence and engaging in peaceful negotiations.
Naomi Nurgitz, Richmond Hill
If all farms are ethical, visits from animal rights activists should not be an issue Put video cameras in barns so we can all see how humane modern animal farming is, May15
I fully agree with letter writer Cheryl Simpson's suggestion that "If animal farming is so humane, put cameras in barns." This being so, the farm owners should have no objection to the visits of animal rights activists to their farms. They will only report what they see and if the farms are humane, the activists will only speak well of their business practices. Animals are sentient beings. They love their lives and feel pain as much as we do. Animal abuse is sinful.
Deepak Narain, Oakville

Ford government warns Toronto to drop request for drug decriminalization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-warns-toronto-drop-request-drug/docview/3055508096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+warns+Toronto+to+drop+request+for+drug+decriminalization&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government is warning Toronto to scrap its "dangerous" push to have Health Canada decriminalize illegal drugs - or face the consequences.
"If Toronto Public Health fails to rescind its misguided application, we will be forced to explore all options available to us," Health Minister Sylvia Jones and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner said in a letter to Toronto's medical officer of health, Dr. Eileen de Villa.
In the meantime, Ontario will "begin enacting enhanced accountability measures for existing consumption and treatment services site to ensure that the safety and the well-being of the public is protected," the cabinet ministers pledged.
Officials said details will be worked out in the coming weeks.
The blunt two-page letter, released Thursday, reiterates that the province is "100 per cent opposed" to the Toronto proposal to decriminalize drugs for personal use.
"We are frankly surprised that, in the aftermath of British Columbia's and other jurisdictions that have attempted this experiment are just the latest examples that show decriminalization does not work," the letter continues.
"Instead, it encourages dangerous behaviour in public spaces, victimizes innocent people and undermines law enforcement's ability to protect our communities."
"Under no circumstances will our government ever support your request, which would only add to crime and public drug use while doing nothing to support people struggling with addiction."
The letter adds the province will make its opposition "clear" to the federal government.
Health Canada is reviewing Toronto's application, which it made in 2022. The city maintains that criminalizing drug possession makes it more difficult for drug users to get the aid and supports they need, such as improved mental health service and harm reduction strategies.
Last fall, Ontario halted approvals of any new supervised consumption and treatments sites while a review of their effectiveness was underway.
Mayor Olivia Chow and de Villa, who announced her pending resignation earlier this week, have called on the province to provide free or affordable and accessible drug rehabilitation, which is beyond the city's scope.
The province's letter is expected to be discussed at a city hall news conference this afternoon.
De Villa said in a statement that "decriminalization is one tool, supported by the best available evidence, to address the drug toxicity epidemic" that kills more than 500 Torontonians a year.
Toronto's exemption request was made following consultation with health-care experts, police, people with lived experience of drug use and service providers, at the request of Toronto's Board of Health. De Villa said the Board of Health would have to order the request to be rescinded.
"Given the current state of the epidemic, Toronto Public Health prioritizes saving lives ... I support increased access to treatment and harm reduction services, which are life-saving and serve as the front door to health care and other supports," she said. "I agree with the province on the need for increased investment in prevention, increased access to health-care interventions, and whole-of-society solutions including housing and supportive housing. All community members should feel safe and supported as we work to address this epidemic."
With files from Alyshah Hasham
