Document 332 of 500

Critics blast impaired driving bill: Civil liberties advocates warn tougher penalties unlikely to have effect

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/critics-blast-impaired-driving-bill/docview/3055355251/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Critics+blast+impaired+driving+bill%3A+Civil+liberties+advocates+warn+tougher+penalties+unlikely+to+have+effect&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hoping to scare impaired drivers straight by proposing a tough new road safety law aimed at taking offending motorists off the roads longer or for good.
The legislation would require the installation of ignition interlock devices in the vehicles of first offenders, longer roadside suspensions for driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, and automatic lifetime driving bans for those convicted of impaired driving causing death.
"There is never an excuse for getting behind the wheel impaired," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Wednesday.
"It's about time we stopped making excuses for those who do," he added. "There's always a choice ... plan ahead on a friend, call a cab or take public transit."
But critics and civil libertarians said the approach is too black and white, and likely won't have the impact the Ford government hopes because logic doesn't work on people under the influence.
They also warned the new approach could result in police pulling over drivers indiscriminately, perhaps leading to racial profiling.
"There's a real risk here," warned Noa Mendelsohn Aviv, executive director of the Canadian Council of Civil Liberties. "We are deeply concerned about the tragedy, of the loss of life in relation to impaired driving, but that kind of sadness doesn't mean there are easy solutions."
Ontario Provincial Police laid more than 10,000 impaired driving charges last year. There were about 50 deaths caused by impaired driving.
Sarkaria, who will introduce the legislation Thursday, said the government also plans a public-relations campaign on the dangers and consequences of drug-impaired driving, as police report a rising number of cases of motorists using cannabis.
Police will be given more resources and drivers convicted of impaired driving would face mandatory remedial education and treatment.
"If you think there are no consequences for drinking and driving, my advice would be to start planning for life without your car," added Sarkaria, who on Tuesday proposed tougher penalties for car thieves as part of the same package of legislation.
Immediate roadside suspensions for a first impaired driving offence would increase to seven days from three, and to 14 days from seven for second offences, Sarkaria said.
The proposed changes follow public outrage at cases like the one involving convicted drunk driver Marco Muzzo.
He was sentenced to 10 years in prison in 2016 and saw his driver's licence suspended for 12 years after he blew through a stop sign on the way home from the airport after an alcohol-fuelled bachelor party in Miami in 2015, killing three children from the Neville-Lake family and their grandfather.
Muzzo was released on full parole after five years in prison.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said some of the changes are "a step in the right direction" but cautioned "a lot of victims, a lot of survivors, are not getting their day in court because our courts are so overwhelmed and underfunded."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Criminal sentences; Legislation; Automobile drivers; Racial profiling; Drunk driving; State laws; Automobile safety

Full text: Retirement homes should be publicly owned

Retirement homes should be publicly not privately owned for the same reason we don't allow private interests to own hospitals. Care facilities of this kind cannot be at the whim of owners looking for quick profits and willing to throw those in need out the door. This is a disgrace and any self-respecting government would act to end this. Unfortunately, that is not what the Doug Ford government cares about. It will probably argue that the rental homes proposed for the property will ease the housing crisis. And after all these aging residents won't be voting for too much longer.
Peter Bradley, Mississauga
Are cops, lawyers watching too much crime TV?
Baby's death was a tragedy. Did it have to be a crime? May 12
A few weeks ago, we heard about the injustice experienced by Umar Zameer, where the heading could have also read "Officer's death a tragedy. Did it have to be a crime?" or just last week we read about the death of visiting grandparents and their grandchild created mainly by an unnecessary chase by police. In these cases, as well as what Lindsay Hewson was subjected to, the culprits seem to be over-exuberant law enforcement officers, and prosecutors, who I fear have been taking their cues from the Hollywood cop and courtroom shows where everything is about the drama and the chase and little to do with reality or the well-being of people, allowing the truth to be muddied by innuendo and unfounded assumptions.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora
Get parents in
on children's use of social media
Fighting corrosive effects of social media, May 10
I was fortunate to be in the audience of the Democracy Forum looking at the impact of social media and mobiles on local schools this month at Toronto Metropolitan University. It seems to me current efforts to control social media use in schools are a bit wrong-headed. The youth in schools now are addicted and deserve support. However, to keep this addiction problem from happening in the future, would it not make sense to take research and intervention, etc., to the preschool level and maximize parental involvement?
Carol Rawson, Toronto
All talk, little action on the net-zero front
Transforming our concrete jungles into carbon sinks is essential to reach climate goals, May 12
Madison Savilow says, "in Canada, with its commitment to achieve net-zero emissions by 2050." There is no tangible evidence of such a commitment. If Canada were committed to the net-zero target, surely by now we would have seen some significant progress toward reaching that goal. We have not. Reaching net zero is a more difficult task than defeating Germany in the Second World War, yet Canada's willingness to make the necessary sacrifices, falls far short of the commitment it displayed from 1939 to 1945. Our commitment today is to talk without useful action.
Patrick Cowan, North York
Future of federal carbon levy is
just another tax
The federal carbon levy and rebate process is modelled on British Columbia's 2008 carbon tax. The provincial government promised the tax would be revenue neutral for all residents and adhered to that promise. After a few years, that changed, and it became just another tax with revenues feeding provincial coffers. This predicts the future of the federal levy.
R.A. Bennett, Victoria, B.C.
Deduct Mantas's expenses from his paycheque
Don't let one bad trip to Italy ruin our city council's need to travel. Toronto can learn from other cities, May 12
City councillors got it all wrong when they reimbursed Councillor Nick Mantas for his questionable expenses during a trip to Turin, Italy. If they wanted to teach Mantas a good lesson and show taxpayers that they respect the use of public funds, they should have calculated reasonable expenses for the trip and the excess amount spent by Mantas should be deducted from his paycheque.
Charles Campisi, Oakville
Take complaints of discrimination seriously
Why student encampments and protests should be encouraged, May 11
Faisal Kutty included many interesting points, but one argument is remarkably outdated - surely, we've learned in recent years to take complaints of discrimination seriously. We've learned not to tell minorities they're wrong to feel hurt by others' actions - even when the hurt is unintended. So why do Kutty and so many others insist on telling Jews when we are allowed to experience discrimination and when we are not? When we have the right to feel hurt and when we don't? The fact that some Jews sincerely separate their Jewish identity from Israel doesn't erase the intense connection that many other Jews feel with Israel and the thousand different types of agony that arise from that connection. Simply telling us that we shouldn't be hurt erases one of the most important lessons progressive societies have learned: to take our neighbours' hurt as seriously as our own.
Robert Steiner, Toronto
Eschew violence and engage in peaceful talks
Faisal Kutty argues that civil disobedience includes vandalism and violence and is a feature of a mature democracy. Au contraire, in a mature democracy these criminal acts would be prosecuted not valued. It wasn't violence which got rid of slavery, apartheid or the Vietnam War. It was democratic legislation in Congress, Mandela's campaign of respect, kindness and reconciliation, and peaceful protest ("Make love, not war!"). The violence came from those wanting to keep things as they were. I am appalled that Kutty promotes change through actions which include violence. The Palestinians will make larger and faster strides toward independence and autonomy through eschewing violence and engaging in peaceful negotiations.
Naomi Nurgitz, Richmond Hill
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Civil disobedience; Violence; Jewish people; Retirement homes; Democracy; Carbon; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Italy; Canada; Israel

Title: Letters:   A Mississauga retirement home is evicting its residents - but some are refusing to budge. Turns out they have the same rights as tenants, May 14 Baby's death was a tragedy. Did it have to be a crime? May 12 Fighting corrosive effects of social media, May 10 Transforming our concrete jungles into carbon sinks is essential to reach climate goals, May 12 Don't let one bad trip to Italy ruin our city council's need to travel. Toronto can learn from other cities, May 12 Why student encampments and protests should be encouraged, May 11

Document 334 of 500

The NDP says it will hold this government to account ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-says-will-hold-this-government-account/docview/3055355069/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+NDP+says+it+will+hold+this+government+to+account+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The NDP says it will "hold this government to account" when it comes to tracking and reporting the number of sex assault cases thrown out of court each year due to trial delays - something MPP Catherine Fife calls a "shameful record" in the province.
Fife, who represents Waterloo, said the Ford government used its majority to shuffle her private member's bill - "Lydia's Law" - to be studied by a committee, adding that New Democrats "have to keep the pressure on" to ensure it doesn't languish there.
"We're going to develop a strategy not to let this government run away from their own disastrous courtroom system," Fife told reporters at Queen's Park on Wednesday.
She said 1,326 sex assault cases were thrown out of Ontario courts in 2022 because of delays in coming to trial or a shortage of judges, and that another 1,119 cases were thrown out last year.
"This government has had opportunities to be transparent and be accountable and they've chosen not to ... so yes I am worried that this is just a way to avoid accountability and transparency," Fife said. "They've shown us who they really are."
Both the Ontario Court of Justice and the Superior Court of Justice can handle sex assault cases, typically depending on the severity.
The federal government has recently appointed eight new judges in Ontario, although a handful of vacancies remain, and staff shortages continue at both levels.
The Star has reported on several sexual assault, child abuse and human trafficking cases that were stayed due to a lack of judges, or because the constitutional rights of an accused were violated when they took too long to get to trial.
Government House Leader Paul Calandra said the bill was sent "directly to a committee of the legislature ... so that it could be part of the study that the justice committee is doing on intimate partner violence," which will lead to "comprehensive recommendations on how we can provide better services to victims of intimate partner violence."
Fife's bill is based on recommendations from the province's auditor general five years ago. It stipulates that the attorney general should keep tabs on criminal cases that have been in the system for eight months and not heard, and report on why.
The bill would also ensure that survivors of sexual assault are informed about the province's independent legal advice program.
Lydia's Law is named after a young sexual assault survivor who told Fife of a two-year experience with the courts that included many delays.
"She told me that she did not want any other survivor to go through what she went through," Fife said.
Cait Alexander, a sexual assault survivor who used to live in Toronto and is now an outspoken advocate for changes in the justice system, said "we don't need more studies, we don't need more committees, we need the governments who listen and pass the bills," The Canadian Press's Liam Casey reported.
Fife's bill had the support of the Liberals, with MPP Andrea Hazell (Scarborough-Guildwood) saying "delays in bringing these cases to trial are excruciating to victims, and often result in the charges being dropped, as these delays can mean the constitutional right to a speedy trial is violated."
Hazell added that it should "be a non-partisan issue. The government committed five years ago to addressing this issue, but it has only gotten worse."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Domestic violence; Sex crimes; Constitutional amendments; Trials; Court hearings & proceedings; Political parties

Document 336 of 500

Some tough questions for Tory leader

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/some-tough-questions-tory-leader/docview/3055354720/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+tough+questions+for+Tory+leader&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one knows what the ballot-box question will be in the next election, whenever it does come.
But at least one legal scholar in Canada believes voters should be asking some hard questions about the Constitution - specifically, whether a future prime minister would be willing to opt out of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints in the past couple of weeks that he could be ready to blaze a trail here as prime minister. No federal government has ever used the notwithstanding clause in the 40-plus years it has been part of the Constitution. But the Conservative leader told a police chiefs' gathering he might go down that road to get tougher on criminals.
"All of my proposals are constitutional, and we will make sure we will make them constitutional. We're using whatever tools the Constitution allows me to use to make them constitutional," Poilievre said in his April 29 speech.
"I think you know exactly what I mean."
Jeffrey Meyers, a B.C. lawyer and legal instructor, says even hinting about it should worry citizens. He stresses that his concerns have nothing to do with partisanship - he's not a fan of how the Liberals have been trying to politicize this debate in recent days, for instance.
But Meyers says that Poilievre's hints cannot be seen in isolation from what's happening in the United States or the increasing willingness of conservative provinces in Canada to talk about opting out of federal legislation they don't like.
He points also to Doug Ford's efforts to use the notwithstanding clause on political financing and the right to strike, as well as the Ontario premier's comments about wanting "like-minded" judges on the provincial bench.
"The bottom line is that it's getting normalized at the provincial level," Meyers said, and he fears it's only a matter of time before some prime minister, quite possibly Poilievre, will try to do the same at the federal level.
That's how the law and the courts got increasingly politicized in the United States, he argues - to the point where former president Donald Trump is openly badmouthing judges, jurors and the courts, and where a lineup of Republicans appeared this week outside a New York court to describe the legal process as a sham. Not so long ago, this would be seen as outrageous. Now it's just another day in the Trump bid for re-election.
Meyers says there's already a playbook for politicizing the justice system. It starts with taking an established political party, Republicans in the United States, for instance, and then "reshuffling the deck in terms of what the established norms are."
Trump now boasts about how he has made the U.S. Supreme Court more friendly to Republicans and the anti-abortion crowd in the States. Meyers says imagine what Trump would have done - or could do in future - if he had something like an opt-out clause from the American constitution.
"That's a shortcut alarmingly available here."
Meyers, who wrote a column in The Conversation last fall on this very issue, says this is why Poilievre's most recent remarks to the police chiefs deeply worried him; that the Conservative leader is tilling the ground to make opting-out of the Charter no big deal.
"It's a norm that hasn't yet been broken. He breaks that norm, though it undermines the Charter so profoundly. And I just think it really, really changes the direction of the country and in unbelievably significant ways and opens the door also to eventually increasing politicization of the courts ... I don't think that's alarmism. I think that's just real."
Meyers, no big fan of Justin Trudeau and the Liberals either, fears the current governing party will "demagogue" the whole debate in ways that citizens will dismiss as the usual political fare. It's not, he says.
What he would like to see is an election campaign in which citizens demand that politicians commit to never using the notwithstanding clause, or, better yet, to getting it out of the Constitution altogether (which wouldn't be easy.)
Former prime minister Paul Martin made that very promise in the dying days of the 2005-06 campaign, which knocked the Liberals out of power. Some Conservatives in the Commons last week were taunting Liberals that all this talk about the notwithstanding clause is a mark of the same desperation that made Martin issue the eleventh-hour promise not to use it.
Canadians should already be braced for an election campaign in which Liberals will accuse Poilievre of trying to import Trumpism to Canada, which Conservatives will frame similarly as desperation or an exaggeration.
Meyers, for his part, doesn't think Canada is immune to the Trump style of politicizing the courts and the basic laws of the land. One clear way to protect ourselves, he says, is to demand that any future government keep its hands off the notwithstanding clause.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints recently that he may use the notwith- standing clause to override the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Susan Delacourt writes.ETHAN CAIRNSTHE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

NDP blasts Ford government for 'shameful record' on sex assault prosecutions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-blasts-ford-government-shameful-record-on-sex/docview/3055570592/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+blasts+Ford+government+for+%27shameful+record%27+on+sex+assault+prosecutions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

CAPTION: Cait Alexander, an advocate for survivors of sexual assault, is shown at Queen's Park in Toronto on May 15, 2024.NDP MPP Catherine Fife speaks to reporters at Queen's Park in Toronto on May 15, 2024.

Title: NDP blasts Ford government for 'shameful record' on sex assault prosecutions

Document 338 of 500

Tougher penalties for impaired driving won't end the problem, critics warn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tougher-penalties-impaired-driving-wont-end/docview/3055570575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tougher+penalties+for+impaired+driving+won%27t+end+the+problem%2C+critics+warn&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hoping to scare impaired drivers straight by proposing a tough new road safety law aimed at taking offending motorists off the roads longer or for good.
The legislation would require the installation of ignition interlock devices in the vehicles of first offenders, longer roadside suspensions for driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs, and automatic lifetime driving bans for those convicted of impaired driving causing death.
"There is never an excuse for getting behind the wheel impaired," Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Wednesday.
"It's about time we stopped making excuses for those who do," he added. "There's always a choice ... plan ahead on a friend, call a cab or take public transit."
But critics and civil libertarians said the approach is too black and white, and likely won't have the impact the Ford government hopes because logic doesn't work on people under the influence.
They also warned the new approach could result in police pulling over drivers indiscriminately, perhaps leading to racial profiling.
"There's a real risk here," warned Noa Mendelsohn Aviv, executive director of the Canadian Council of Civil Liberties.
"We are deeply concerned about the tragedy, of the loss of life in relation to impaired driving, but that kind of sadness doesn't mean there are easy solutions."
Ontario Provincial Police laid more than 10,000 impaired driving charges last year. There were about 50 deaths caused by impaired driving.
Sarkaria, who will introduce the legislation Thursday, said the government also plans a public-relations campaign on the dangers and consequences of drug-impaired driving, as police report a rising number of cases of motorists using cannabis.
Police will be given more resources and drivers convicted of impaired driving would face mandatory remedial education and treatment.
"If you think there are no consequences for drinking and driving, my advice would be to start planning for life without your car," added Sarkaria, who on Tuesday proposed tougher penalties for car thieves as part of the same package of legislation.
Immediate roadside suspensions for a first impaired driving offence would increase to seven days from three, and to 14 days from seven for second offences, Sarkaria said.
The proposed changes follow public outrage at cases like the one involving convicted drunk driver Marco Muzzo.
He was sentenced to 10 years in prison in 2016 and saw his driver's licence suspended for 12 years after he blew through a stop sign on the way home from the airport after an alcohol-fuelled bachelor party in Miami in 2015, killing three children from the Neville-Lake family and their grandfather.
Muzzo was released on full parole after five years in prison and will be eligible to drive again in 2028. If passed, the new legislation would not retroactively apply to him.
The proposed changes from Premier Doug Ford's government were applauded by Mothers Against Drunk Driving.
"Despite progress, too many people are still making the choice to drive impaired," said chief executive Steve Sullivan.
Carolyn Swinson, a MADD volunteer counsellor whose father and son were killed by impaired drivers in separate accidents, said families whose lives have been tragically changed forever typically want the offending motorists to lose their driver's licences permanently.
"We need to keep people safe," Swinson said at a news conference with Sarkaria.
Prominent defence lawyer Calvin Barry, a former Crown attorney, said he considers automatic lifetime driver's licence suspensions "a little draconian" because they do not give judges any leeway for exceptional circumstances, or the prospect of rehabilitation or recovery from addiction.
There could be situations where a fatal accident was inevitable because of traffic, weather or other conditions, and automatic lifetime driving bans can make it impossible for some people, such as truckers or people who live in remote areas, to earn a living.
"There should be some discretion for the judge," said Barry. "But the best defence is take an Uber."
He cautioned that ignition interlock systems can be defeated if impaired motorists have someone who is sober blow into them.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said some of the changes are "a step in the right direction" but cautioned "a lot of victims, a lot of survivors, are not getting their day in court because our courts are so overwhelmed and underfunded."
CAPTION: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria is shownin St. Catharines on April 5, 2024.

Subject: Fines & penalties; Criminal sentences; Fatalities; Legislation; Automobile drivers; Racial profiling; Breath tests; Drunk driving

Document 339 of 500

Pierre Poilievre hints he'd like to strip Canadians of some rights. There's something to think about when it's time to vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-hints-hed-like-strip-canadians/docview/3054978844/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+hints+he%27d+like+to+strip+Canadians+of+some+rights.+There%27s+something+to+think+about+when+it%27s+time+to+vote&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No one knows what the ballot-box question will be in the next election, whenever it does come.
But at least one legal scholar in Canada believes voters should be asking some hard questions about the Constitution - specifically, whether a future prime minister would be willing to opt out of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints in the past couple of weeks that he could be ready to blaze a trail here as prime minister. No federal government has ever used the notwithstanding clause in the 40-plus years it has been part of the Constitution. But the Conservative leader told a police chiefs' gathering he might go down that road to get tougher on criminals.
"All of my proposals are constitutional, and we will make sure we will make them constitutional. We're using whatever tools the Constitution allows me to use to make them constitutional," Poilievre said in his Apr. 29 speech.
"I think you know exactly what I mean."
Jeffrey Meyers, a B.C. lawyer and legal instructor, says even hinting about it should worry citizens. He stresses that his concerns have nothing to do with partisanship - he's not a fan of how the Liberals have been trying to politicize this debate in recent days, for instance.
But Meyers says that Poilievre's hints cannot be seen in isolation from what's happening in the United States or the increasing willingness of conservative provinces in Canada to talk about opting out of federal legislation they don't like.
He points also to Doug Ford's efforts to use the notwithstanding clause on political financing and the right to strike, as well as the Ontario premier's comments about wanting "like-minded" judges on the provincial bench.
"The bottom line is that it's getting normalized at the provincial level," Meyers said, and he fears it's only a matter of time before some prime minister, quite possibly Poilievre, will try to do the same at the federal level.
That's how the law and the courts got increasingly politicized in the United States, he argues - to the point where former president Donald Trump is openly badmouthing judges, jurors and the courts, and where a lineup of Republicans appeared this week outside a New York court to describe the legal process as a sham. Not so long ago, this would be seen as outrageous. Now it's just another day in the Trump bid for re-election.
Meyers says there's already a playbook for politicizing the justice system. It starts with taking an established political party, Republicans in the United States, for instance, and then "reshuffling the deck in terms of what the established norms are."
Trump now boasts about how he has made the U.S. Supreme Court more friendly to Republicans and the anti-abortion crowd in the States. Meyers says imagine what Trump would have done - or could do in future - if he had something like an opt-out clause from the American constitution.
"That's a shortcut alarmingly available here."
Meyers, who wrote a column in The Conversation last fall on this very issue, says this is why Poilievre's most recent remarks to the police chiefs deeply worried him; that the Conservative leader is tilling the ground to make opting-out of the Charter no big deal.
"It's a norm that hasn't yet been broken. He breaks that norm, though it undermines the Charter so profoundly. And I just think it really, really changes the direction of the country and in unbelievably significant ways and opens the door also to eventually increasing politicization of the courts ... I don't think that's alarmism. I think that's just real."
Meyers, no big fan of Justin Trudeau and the Liberals either, fears the current governing party will "demagogue" the whole debate in ways that citizens will dismiss as the usual political fare. It's not, he says.
What he would like to see is an election campaign in which citizens demand that politicians commit to never using the notwithstanding clause, or, better yet, to getting it out of the Constitution altogether (which wouldn't be easy.)
Former prime minister Paul Martin made that very promise in the dying days of the 2005-06 campaign, which knocked the Liberals out of power. Some Conservatives in the Commons last week were taunting Liberals that all this talk about the notwithstanding clause is a mark of the same desperation that made Martin issue the eleventh-hour promise not to use it.
Canadians should already be braced for an election campaign in which Liberals will accuse Poilievre of trying to import Trumpism to Canada, which Conservatives will frame similarly as desperation or an exaggeration.
Meyers, for his part, doesn't think Canada is immune to the Trump style of politicizing the courts and the basic laws of the land. One clear way to protect ourselves, he says, is to demand that any future government keep its hands off the notwithstanding clause.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has dropped some broad hints in the past couple of weeks that he could be ready to use the notwithstanding clause to override the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Susan Delacourt writes.

Subject: Conservatism; Political campaigns; Charters; Constitutional law; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Pierre Poilievre hints he'd like to strip Canadians of some rights. There's something to think about when it's time to vote

Document 340 of 500

Doug Ford is right. I too am 'fed up' with the anti-Israel protests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-right-i-too-am-fed-up-with-anti/docview/3054758412/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+right.+I+too+am+%27fed+up%27+with+the+anti-Israel+protests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The "boiling frog" theory has it that if you put a frog into a pot of boiling water, it'll jump out to save itself; but if you turn the heat up slowly, it won't notice that it's being boiled alive.
As I watched anti-Israel protestors block rush hour traffic at Yonge and Bloor again last week and read about Jewish Canadians being harassed at the University of Toronto, it seemed that the time had come admit that we are that "boiling frog."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford appears to be thinking the same thing.
After months of disruptive anti-Israel protests across Canada, Ford reflected the mood of a growing silent constituency when he declared that "people are fed up." Ford called the instances of racism at various university encampments "disturbing," encouraging administrators to "remove those people. You don't come to Ontario, no matter what culture you are, and stir things up."
Some on the left went bananas. Author Dr. David Moscrop took issue with Ford's "dangerous" choice of words, claiming they "threaten to whip up xenophobia or worse." Even if many of the anti-Israel campus protestors "are from somewhere beyond the borders of Ontario or Canada," "so what if they are?" said Moscrop.
Moscrop exemplifies what many "progressives", including elected officials, think, I fear.
But while it's true that the last thing we should want is to whip up racism against any group, many on the left seem pretty forgiving of the racism too often on display at these protests.
Moscrop predicts that "history will once again side with the [university] dissenters," despite characterizing the 2022 Freedom Convoy "occupation" as "toxic" "right-wing extremism."
U of T protest organizer Heigo Parsa told the CBC that his compatriots "have dealt with threats and incidents of racism from 'outside agitators.'" Parsa's attempt to distance his caustic throng from documented antisemitic acts was quickly undercut when his protest "marshals" physically prevented a professor from accessing parts of the campus, leaving him little choice but to believe that this was because he was Jewish.
This shameful incongruence comes as no surprise.
For months, these so-called "peaceful protests" have consistently included calls for a new "intifada," which for many harkens back to the 2000-02 Al Aqsa intifada, when Hamas, among others, organized terror attacks that killed or wounded thousands of Israeli citizens.
I embrace free speech, but chants that are widely interpreted as calls for terror attacks against Jews do not reflect the Canadian values that I grew up with.
Are there no limits to civil disobedience?
What of the protesters who slowed ambulances from reaching Toronto's key downtown hospitals?
What about the agitators who wouldn't allow children to enjoy their moment with an Ottawa Mall Santa?
What of protests that apparently blocked access to a predominately Jewish neighbourhood in North York?
One protestor recently tore down a sign advertising food for Calgary's homeless because "you got food, music, good times, but there's no peace in the Middle East." Others defaced the Regina veterans' memorial.
These antics won't bring peace to Gaza.
Ford at least seems to recognize that we're the frog in the proverbial pot, and the water's about to boil.
CAPTION: "Palestinian Spider-Man," or spiderman4palestine on social media was widely condemned for climbing up historically Jewish hospital Mount Sinai during a protest in February.

Subject: Civil disobedience; Racism; Demonstrations & protests

Location: Canada; Israel

Title: Doug Ford is right. I too am 'fed up' with the anti-Israel protests

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 15, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Like the premier, I'm 'fed up' too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/like-premier-im-fed-up-too/docview/3054756218/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Like+the+premier%2C+I%27m+%27fed+up%27+too&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Mark McQueen is a Toronto-based entrepreneur who has worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an advisor
in the Brian Mulroney PMO.
He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.

Document 342 of 500

City's top doctor is stepping down: De Villa lauded for calm leadership during pandemic - despite the job's personal toll

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/citys-top-doctor-is-stepping-down/docview/3054756177/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City%27s+top+doctor+is+stepping+down%3A+De+Villa+lauded+for+calm+leadership+during+pandemic+-+despite+the+job%27s+personal+toll&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Dr. Eileen de Villa, the physician who calmly led Toronto through the COVID-19 pandemic and other public health emergencies, is leaving her post at the end of this year.
De Villa, 55, announced her departure in a video released Tuesday in which she said that, after leading Toronto Public Health since 2017, she's ready to move on to a new challenge.
"It has been a remarkable time these past eight years ... We have navigated significant challenges," including the pandemic, a dramatic cut in provincial funding that was reversed after a pressure campaign, the deadly drug toxicity crisis and the mental health crisis, de Villa said.
Leaving the high-profile job on Dec. 31 is "not a decision I took lightly," de Villa said, adding she reached it after several months of heartfelt discussion with family members.
"I'm ready to embark on the next chapter of my professional life and to spend more time with family," she said, adding that she will continue working hard until the end of the year with her "incredible and strong team." She expects the Toronto Public Health board to soon announce steps to replace her and a transition plan.
"To the people of Toronto, thank you for your support over these years and for everything we've done together," de Villa said. "I'm incredibly grateful."
Her departure announcement comes days after Toronto fire Chief Matthew Pegg - who, with de Villa, former mayor John Tory and former public health chair Joe Cressy, led the city's pandemic response - announced he is leaving his post on Oct. 4.
Pegg thanked de Villa, on social media platform X, for "your dedicated, caring, compassionate and unwavering leadership." He lauded her "calm and poise during the unprecedented COVID-19 pandemic (that) showed the world the depth of your character."
Cressy told the Star: "During the COVID pandemic - arguably the most challenging and uncertain period in Toronto's history - Dr. de Villa led with remarkable integrity, courage and compassion.
"At all times principled and evidence-driven, Dr. de Villa's work was public service at its very best."
While Torontonians saw her at frequent televised COVID-19 briefings, often wearing a trademark scarf, when she urged residents to take precautions, get vaccinated and seek testing and treatment, Cressy said she was the same rock behind the scenes despite massive pressure to save lives.
"She led 2,600 public health staff through a moment of real trauma where all of them, every single member of a team, was working punishing hours with the weight of the world on their shoulders," he said. "We all saw the public face of leadership but most never saw the day-to-day leadership of a team."
Cressy recalled de Villa briefing the COVID-19 command table, in March 2020, on computer modelling that showed the virus could kill tens of thousands of Torontonians. "In her compassionate but also professional way," Cressy said, "she walked us through the scenarios, and she didn't break. Any other human would break in that moment but she never did - not then, not during the next two-and-a-half years."
A rapid expansion of immunization clinics, and a relentless public awareness campaign by de Villa and others, made Toronto one of the most vaccinated big cities on the planet. COVID-19 has killed fewer than 4,000 Torontonians, although many continue to suffer the virus's effects.
Tory, in a text, said Torontonians benefited greatly from the efforts of the "smart, balanced, compassionate and collegial leader ... I can say without hesitation that lives were saved thanks to her expertise and her efforts." Tory said de Villa made Torontonians realize the importance of public health.
Mayor Olivia Chow, in a post, thanked de Villa for navigating Toronto Public Health "through an incredibly difficult and uncertain time during the pandemic with integrity and compassion. As mayor, I'm grateful for your work to keep our city healthy."
The Boston-born University of Toronto graduate, a daughter of two physicians who immigrated from the Philippines, was named Toronto's top doctor in March 2017 after working as an associate medical officer of health in Peel Region.
Shortly after her Toronto appointment, de Villa told the Star's Donovan Vincent she was driven by a parent-instilled need to pay back her privilege to society and by seeing first-hand - including a homeless man whose blood flow was so poor that maggots were feasting on his feet - "very complex medical situations that were clearly aggravated by social circumstances, income challenges, housing challenges, access to services challenges."
Her agency's ability to do its many tasks, including the DineSafe program, water testing and more, was threatened in 2019 when Premier Doug Ford's government announced cuts to public health that would have totalled $1 billion over a decade. Although the Ford government at first branded warnings from de Villa and others that the cuts would cost lives as fear-mongering, the province backed down and the pandemic hit less than a year later.
Under her watch, in 2018, Toronto Public Health called for drug decriminalization for personal use, and in 2022 submitted to the federal government a formal application. That request, part of what city officials called a "made-in-Toronto" model that would cover young people, remains in limbo.
But it will be Toronto's fight against COVID-19 for which de Villa's Toronto tenure will be most remembered.
It is also one for which she has paid a heavy personal toll. De Villa told the Star in 2022 that, after she endorsed mandatory mask rules and other COVID-19 restrictions, threats and other abuse she had experienced exploded.
Protesters came to the street where she lives with her physician husband and sons, and she received many death threats.
"When powdered substances are sent to you, that elicits a whole protocol - are you trying to poison me or the staff at Toronto Public Health because of your views on how we're managing the pandemic?" she told the Star. "That's a whole other level."
With files from Ben Spurr, Alyshah Hasham and Stephanie Levitz
CREDIT: David Rider Toronto Star Chief With files from Ben Spurr, Alyshah Hasham and Stephanie Levitz

Subject: Compassion; Public health; Leadership; COVID-19 vaccines; Pandemics; Resignations; Medical personnel

Document 343 of 500

Lifetime driving ban eyed for auto thieves: Critics of new bill say harsher penalties won't solve crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lifetime-driving-ban-eyed-auto-thieves/docview/3054755870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Lifetime+driving+ban+eyed+for+auto+thieves%3A+Critics+of+new+bill+say+harsher+penalties+won%27t+solve+crisis&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Car thieves could be banned from driving for life under new legislation meant to help deter the growing problem of auto theft in the province.
"Car theft is a cowardly and often violent crime that can traumatize victims and communities who experience it," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria in announcing the legislation Tuesday morning in Toronto.
"Our government is sending a clear message to those who commit these crimes and using every tool in our tool box to keep them off our streets."
Under the proposed new Ontario law, anyone convicted of motor vehicle theft would automatically lose their licence for 10 years. A second conviction would mean a 15-year suspension; three convictions and the suspension is for life.
Critics, however, said the new penalties will have little effect.
In a written release, the government said "the licence suspensions would apply to convictions where the court found that aggravating factors were involved in the commission of the offence, such as violence, use of a weapon, use of force, threat, or pursuit of financial gain."
The legislation would also set out mandatory suspensions for those convicted of stunt driving, including a lifetime driving ban for repeat offenders.
In Ontario, a car is stolen every 14 minutes - and the problem is particularly bad in Brampton and Mississauga, which have seen a 187 per cent increase in auto thefts since 2019.
Car thieves face a mandatory prison sentence of at least six months for a third offence.
Adam Weisberg, managing partner at Weisberg Law Criminal Lawyers LLP, called the proposed law "misguided" and "completely ineffective."
"The perpetrators of these crimes are already not deterred by the prison sentences they face," Weisberg said.
"There is little that the provincial government can do to combat auto theft, other than direct police resources at investigations into these auto-theft rings," he said, adding "it's just a frustrated attempt from our province to signal that frustration to the public."
Weisberg called on Ottawa to act. "The federal government should tighten up security at our ports. These stolen cars, for the most part, are being shipped overseas because a re-vinned stolen vehicle has little value domestically," he said. (Re-vinning refers to changing an automobile's vehicle identification number, or VIN, in order to strip it of its ownership history.)
"The federal government should also consider introducing regulations to force auto manufacturers to increase their key fob security. The auto manufacturers currently have no incentive to tighten security on their own as they are getting to sell cars twice - once to the customer and again to the insurer," he said.
But Premier Doug Ford, speaking at an unrelated announcement, shot back at skeptics.
"There's many solutions, but one is making sure that when we catch these criminals, we throw them behind bars and keep them there and discourage the rest of them," he said in Port Colborne, where a new feeder plant for Honda electric-vehicle batteries was announced.
Opposition parties, however, were less convinced.
"If you steal a car and get caught, I'm not sure whether you're going to be worried about getting caught again and not having a driver's licence," said Liberal house leader John Fraser. "So I'm not sure it's going to be as effective a tool as it's made out to be."
Fraser added the longer sentences could be subject to a constitutional challenge. "Everything that this government does is for show, and then they try to figure out if it's going to work after."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the government should be taking stronger steps to encourage automakers to render vehicles more theft-proof, given that many auto thefts appear to go unsolved.
"Certainly, it's not clear how many people are actually being apprehended. And that is going to be needed if the government changes are going to be effective."
Shakir Rahim, director of the criminal justice program at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, said "car theft is a serious problem. However, harsh mandatory lifetime licence suspensions are not the solution because they undermine rehabilitation. Without a car, people may not be able to see their family or hold down a job. No job and a lack of social support increases the prospect of future offences ... We need to invest in common-sense solutions to stop vehicles from being stolen, like improving anti-theft technology and port inspections."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Criminal sentences; Legislation; Automobile theft; Robbery; Vehicles; Bans; State laws

Document 344 of 500

New $1.6B parts plant coming to Niagara: Honda-backed factory hailed as link in supply chain

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-1-6b-parts-plant-coming-niagara/docview/3054755709/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+%241.6B+parts+plant+coming+to+Niagara%3A+Honda-backed+factory+hailed+as+link+in+supply+chain&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The announcement of a key EV component factory in Port Colborne Tuesday had the air of an international summit, with the Japanese ambassador standing shoulder-to-shoulder with the prime minister of Canada and the premier of Ontario.
This is far more than a $1.6-billion investment in Niagara, they said. It's the beginning of a green economic alliance, one that will fight climate change while ensuring the jobs and benefits accrue to Canada and its allies for generations.
"The world of today is not easy ... it's very difficult to see the future," said Kanji Yamanouchi, Japan's top emissary to Canada. "(We ask) who are our friends? ... And this is a victory of friendship."
The dignitaries were on hand to announce a "separator" plant, which produces a membrane that divides the cathodes within an EV battery, backed by Japan's Asahi Kasei corporation in partnership with Honda. It's the first link of the supply chain to come as part of Honda's $15-billion investment in Ontario, with EVs to be assembled at a new plant in Alliston.
Overshadowing the proceedings was the threat of getting sucked into a trade war between the United States and China over control of critical minerals and technologies for a clean economic future. U.S. President Joe Biden quadrupled tariffs on Chinese EVs this week, several months after China threatened to choke exporting of critical minerals that U.S. EV makers rely on.
Asked about this, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Canada's strategy is to build as much of the EV supply chain as possible domestically.
"Canada has, over the last number of years, strategically and deliberately built itself up as one of the number one places in the world to invest in an EV supply chain," he said.
In the past four years, more than $46 billion in international investments have been announced by EV makers in Canada, including a Stellantis-LG battery factory in Windsor, a Volkswagen assembly plant in St. Thomas and a GM plant in Ingersoll.
"When is a $1.6-billion investment a secondary announcement? Only in this crazy era we're in," said Flavio Volpe, president of the Automotive Manufacturers Association of Canada.
Bloomberg recently ranked Canada first in the world in its EV battery supply chain ranking.
Last year, Canada had the highest foreign direct investment per capita in the G7, Trudeau said, and was third in the world in terms of total foreign investment dollars.
"We're starting to see the EV supply chain fill out," Premier Doug Ford said, adding that this plant will help "ensure that all the components needed for the cars of the future are made in Ontario, by Ontario workers, from start to finish."
Ontario is now the only jurisdiction in North America where all six major car manufacturers have a presence.
Federal Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne and Ontario Minister of Economic Development Vic Fideli both hinted more EV and critical-mineral investments are expected before the end of the year.
Fideli added that he expects a lithium hydroxide plant as well as a new nickel mine and two new lithium mines to be announced soon.
At Queen's Park, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the Port Colborne investment is welcome but urged Ford to bring back subsidies for consumers to purchase EVs.
With files from Rob Ferguson
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter With files from Rob Ferguson

Document 345 of 500

Liberals re-evaluating after byelection losses

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-re-evaluating-after-byelection-losses/docview/3054754730/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+re-evaluating+after+byelection+losses&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Liberals are taking a closer look at their strategy after losing two byelections to Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives earlier this month, says Bonnie Crombie.
"I'm not going to deny that we have a lot of work to do and we're going to go back and see how we can improve our numbers," the party's leader said Tuesday.
In her first news conference at Queen's Park since the May 2 defeats in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, the former Mississauga mayor said a blitz of feel-good and attack ads from Ford and the Progressive Conservatives in addition to government advertising are proving difficult to overcome.
"It certainly is a challenge for us, but we're all up for the challenge," Crombie said as she unveiled the promise of a tax cut that would save small businesses about $450 million a year. "We'll be going back and taking a look at where we could have made changes, where we'll do better next time and where to go forward."
In Milton, the PCs won with candidate Zee Hamid, a former Liberal, who defeated Liberal hopeful Galen Naidoo-Harris, the son of the riding's former Liberal MPP and cabinet minister Indira Naidoo-Harris.
Despite an RCMP investigation into the Ford government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap, the Progressive Conservative margin of victory was 2,407 votes - higher than the 1,680 former PC cabinet minister Parm Gill won by in 2022 as Ford was elected to a second term.
Hamid's nomination had rankled some Tories given his Liberal past, but as the Star's Robert Benzie reported, Ford was convinced that would help the PCs in a close race. Liberal sources have also said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's slide in the polls is not helping the provincial party.
In the meantime, Crombie said she will continue travelling the province, meeting with Liberals and local media to become better known in advance of the next provincial election campaign two years from now.
The party will soon open nominations for candidates in ridings across Ontario for the 2026 election.
"I've spent the last four or five months building capacity with our team, putting organizational resources in place, building up a fundraising team that is going to help us raise the kind of money we need to be competitive in the next election," said Crombie, who became leader in December.
In the most recent monthly tracking poll of 995 Ontarians for the Star by Abacus Data on April 20, the PCs were at 41 per cent support, the Liberals at 25 per cent, the NDP 21 and the Greens at seven.
The Liberals were down two points from the previous month, while the other parties were unchanged.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Tuesday that her party has "a lot of work to do."Steve RussellToronto Star File Photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 346 of 500

Israeli flag raised at Toronto city hall amid noisy protest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/israeli-flag-raised-at-toronto-city-hall-amid/docview/3055175131/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Israeli+flag+raised+at+Toronto+city+hall+amid+noisy+protest&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Israeli flag was raised at Toronto city hall Tuesday as a small band of pro-Palestinian protesters chanted through loudspeakers below.
Some city councillors and other politicians attended the annual Israeli independence day event, along with Israeli consular officials and representatives of groups including UJA Federation of Greater Toronto and the Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center for Holocaust Studies.
Mayor Olivia Chow was at city hall but skipped the ceremony, telling reporters she considered raising Israel's flag on the podium overlooking Nathan Phillips Square to be provocative during the war in Gaza that has triggered pro-Palestinian protests in the city.
"I generally don't go to flag-raising events for countries but there are quite a lot of them," Chow said. "I think it's a bit divisive because there's a war going on in the Middle East and people are protesting every weekend."
The mayor's office sent out an email stating: "The Mayor will not attend the flag-raising. She believes raising it is divisive at this time, and understands the deep pain and anguish felt by many in the community."
Coun. James Pasternak, who helped organize the event, rejected the mayor's assertion that raising the Israeli flag was divisive.
"This was a gathering of praying for peace, praying for the return of the hostages," he said, a reference to the more than 100 people who were captured by Hamas and are believed to be still in captivity.
Although Pasternak said organizers wanted Chow to take part and had adjusted the schedule to enable her to attend, he stopped short of criticizing her for her absence.
"Obviously, she was welcome, and we would have liked to see her, but if she can't make it, we understand," he said.
Pasternak got applause from the crowd when he said the city has been raising the Israeli flag to mark the country's independence for about 12 years "and we're not going to stop."
But he said this year's ceremony was taking place at a "dark time for Israel" and Jewish communities around the world, amid a reported rise in antisemitism and the ongoing Israel-Hamas war.
"Toronto will never waver from its unshakable commitment to Israel. But there's work to be done. We will continue to vigorously fight antisemitism in all its forms," he said.
Pasternak called for an end to the "hate rallies" that have taken place on Toronto's streets, and the "hateful encampment" at the University of Toronto.
Organizers of protests and the university encampment say they're not antisemitic but oppose Israel's attacks on Gaza. Israel's bombings and ground operations have killed more than 35,000 people, according to local health officials, and forced the displacement of hundreds of thousands.
Hamas, which Canada has designated a terrorist organization, killed about 1,200 people and took 250 hostages in its surprise raid on Israel seven months ago.
At points, dignitaries at city hall raised their voices to drown out chants from about 25 protesters in the square below, many wearing T-shirts that read "Jews for Free Palestine," who were pushed by police away from the event to the far side of the square, much of which was fenced off.
Protester Gur Tsabar called the flag-raising "pretty atrocious. We're here to share our absolute disgust with the City of Toronto for raising the Israeli flag as Israel is committing a mass atrocity. As Jews, we're here to say, 'Not in our name.'"
Noah Shack, the vice president of countering antisemitism at the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, said it was "unfortunate" Chow didn't attend.
He said previous mayors have attended the event "and we look forward to her participating in the next community gathering.
"It's very important for us to be together with our political leaders at events like this," he said.
Michael Levitt, president of the Friends of Simon Wiesenthal Center for Holocaust Studies, said he was "disappointed" Chow skipped the ceremony.
He noted that Ottawa Mayor Mark Sutcliffe asked officials to find a way to go ahead with an Israeli flag raising in that city after a public ceremony was cancelled over security concerns.
"That is the type of leadership we need to see from mayors, from leaders at all levels of government," Levitt said.
Shouting erupted in the square below when two counterprotesters started yelling that people should be criticizing Hamas and not be allowed to disrupt the flag-raising, but they departed after a few minutes of heated confrontation with the protesters.
At Queen's Park, about two hundred metres east of the Pro-Palestinian encampment at the University of Toronto, another Israel flag-raising took place at noon without incident amid a heavy police presence.
The event attracted about 250 people with speakers from the Jewish community, the governing Progressive Conservatives and the Liberal Party. The New Democrats said none of their MPPs were available to attend.
With files from Rob Ferguson

While the Abacus opt-in poll cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.

Document 348 of 500

Car thieves could lose driver's licence for life, Ford government says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/car-thieves-could-lose-drivers-licence-life-ford/docview/3054467728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Car+thieves+could+lose+driver%27s+licence+for+life%2C+Ford+government+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Car thieves could be banned from driving for life under new legislation meant to help deter the growing problem of auto theft in the province.
"Car theft is a cowardly and often violent crime that can traumatize victims and communities who experience it," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria in announcing the legislation Tuesday morning in Toronto.
"Our government is sending a clear message to those who commit these crimes and using every tool in our tool box to keep them off our streets."
Under the proposed new law, anyone convicted of motor vehicle theft would automatically lose their licence for 10 years. A second conviction would mean 15 years' suspension; three convictions and the suspension is for life.
Critics, however, said the new penalties will have little effect.
In a written release, the government said "the licence suspensions would apply to convictions where the court found that aggravating factors were involved in the commission of the offence, such as violence, use of a weapon, use of force, threat, or pursuit of financial gain."
The legislation would also set out mandatory suspensions for those convicted of stunt driving, including a lifetime driving ban for repeat offenders.
In Ontario, a car is stolen every 14 minutes - and the problem is particularly bad in Brampton and Mississauga, which have seen a 187 per cent increase in auto thefts since 2019.
Car thieves face a mandatory prison sentence of at least six months for a third offence.
Adam Weisberg, managing partner at Weisberg Law Criminal Lawyers LLP, called the proposed law "misguided" and "completely ineffective."
"The perpetrators of these crimes are already not deterred by the prison sentences they face," he said.
"... There is little that the provincial government can do to combat auto-theft, other than direct police resources at investigations into these auto-theft rings," he said, adding "it's just a frustrated attempt from our province to signal that frustration to the public" and called on Ottawa to act.
"The federal government should tighten up security at our ports. These stolen cars, for the most part, are being shipped overseas because a re-vinned stolen vehicle has little value domestically. The federal government should also consider introducing regulations to force auto-manufacturers to increase their key fob security. The auto manufacturers currently have no incentive to tighten security on their own as they are getting to sell cars twice - once to the customer and again to the insurer."
But Premier Doug Ford, speaking at an unrelated announcement, shot back at skeptics.
"There's many solutions, but one is making sure that when we catch these criminals, we throw them behind bars and keep them there and discourage the rest of them," he said in Port Colborne where a new feeder plant for Honda electric vehicle batteries was announced.
Opposition parties, however, were less convinced.
"If you steal a car and get caught, I'm not sure whether you're going to be worried about getting caught again and not having a driver's licence," said Liberal House Leader John Fraser. "So I'm not sure it's going to be as effective a tool as it's made out to be."
Fraser added the longer sentences could be subject to a constitutional challenge.
"Everything that this government does is for show, and then they try to figure out if it's going to work after."
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the government should be taking stronger steps to encourage automakers to render vehicles more theftproof, given that many auto thefts appear to go unsolved.
"Certainly, it's not clear how many people are actually being apprehended. And that is going to be needed if the government changes are going to be effective."
Shakir Rahim, director of criminal Justice at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association, said "car theft is a serious problem. However, harsh mandatory lifetime license suspensions are not the solution because they undermine rehabilitation. Without a car, people may not be able to see their family or hold down a job. No job and a lack of social support increases the prospect of future offences ... We need to invest in common-sense solutions to stop vehicles from being stolen, like improving anti-theft technology and port inspections."
CAPTION: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria will make the announcement Tuesday morning in Toronto.

Subject: Theft; Robbery

Title: Car thieves could lose driver's licence for life, Ford government says

Tenants fight back against retirement home evictions + why his mentally ill wife was jailed for 10 months

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tenants-fight-back-against-retirement-home/docview/3054467488/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tenants+fight+back+against+retirement+home+evictions+%2B+why+his+mentally+ill+wife+was+jailed+for+10+months&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, May 14 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the eviction of retirement home residents, how a mental health crisis turned into a jail stint and the bizarre "circus" around Drake's house.
DON'T MISSA Mississauga retirement home is evicting its residents - but it turns out they have the same rights as tenants
When Margaret Omielczenko received an eviction notice from the owners of her Mississauga retirement home, panic set in. It didn't help that the relocation team suggested she consider moving into a women's shelter. "I was very upset," the 65-year-old told the Star's Kenyon Wallace. With an eviction date looming, Omielczenko and other residents have refused to budge - it turns out retirement home residents are considered tenants under the law and can only be terminated by the Landlord and Tenant Board. Check out what that actually means.
What we know: Experts say tenants who receive an eviction notice don't have to move out right away. If they still haven't moved by the termination date, the landlord can apply for a hearing from the Landlord and Tenant Board - but that could take weeks or months due to backlog.Go deeper: The 205 residents of Heritage Glen Retirement Residence were notified in March that the building was being sold to be redeveloped into residential rental units. Chartwell claims it has helped more than 165 residents find other accommodations and will cover the cost of moving plus compensation worth three months' rent.A Toronto man called police to take his mentally ill wife to the hospital - she was jailed for 10 months
Janice Saville believed her partner, who was being treated for cancer, was surrounded by dangerous radiation. She sprayed him with Febreze and approached his face with a pillow in hopes of protecting him. Her husband, Alex McLarty, asked police to take his partner to hospital for a psychiatric evaluation. Instead, Jacques Gallant reports, the 69-year-old was taken from her home in handcuffs and arrested for attempted murder and domestic assault - against McLarty's objections. After spending 10 months in pretrial custody, Saville was finally released on bail before being made to stand trial; she was acquitted in just half a day. Take a closer look at how and why this happened.
Wait, what? Saville's case was taken to trial while Ontario's justice system strains under a massive caseload - leading serious criminal matters from sexual assault to human trafficking to be thrown out due to delay.Another angle: Toronto is expanding its new crisis response teams, an alternative to police for handling wellness checks and aiding people in a mental health crisis. Here's how to reach them.With trespassers, aspiring artists and a "huge entourage," Drake's Toronto mansion is a circus
Superstar rap artist Drake's palatial Toronto mansion has become what some in the Bridal Path neighbourhood call a "tourist trap" and "a bit of a circus." Towering trees, hedges and fences twice the height of city limits help shield the residence from public eyes, and at least one security guard is always stationed outside. Even still, the location has become a magnet for local traffic and unusual behaviour - especially after a member of Drake's security team was seriously injured in an overnight shooting last week. Staff at the estate spoke with the Star's Calvi Leon about some of the more bizarre scenes they've witnessed. Take a peek behind the scenes at Drake's house.
ICYMI: A shooting at the mansion last Tuesday left one person in life-threatening condition. The suspects sped off in a vehicle caught on video, although police have yet to release a description.Context: Drake has dominated international headlines lately amid his high-profile feud with Pulitzer Prize-winning rapper Kendrick Lamar. Serious allegations have made by both parties but the beef appears to have ended for now.WHAT ELSE
Wildfires are already prompting evacuations across Canada - Ontario's air quality could soon feel the effects.Palestinians mark 76 years of dispossession as a potentially larger catastrophe unfolds in Gaza."I am personally threatened." Mississauga mayoral front-runner Carolyn Parrish has pulled out of future debates.TD fines show Canada becoming the "soft underbelly" of international money laundering - here's why."It's the TTC. There's always a problem." A portion of the TTC Bloor-Danforth subway line shut down yesterday amid afternoon rush hour.Border services workers at airports and crossings expected to vote for strike mandate Wednesday and could walk off the job by June.This refugee from Ethiopia fought red tape to be a neurosurgeon. Health Canada's decision came at the 11th hour.Ontario will need tens of thousands more nurses and personal support workers by 2032, document shows."Do you know that lady?" Crown psychiatrist assessed accused Toronto murderer from courtroom gallery, defence reveals.The criminal trial of "Freedom Convoy" organizer Pat King begins with a not guilty plea.Canada's 100 Best Restaurants list for 2024 is here - check out which Toronto-area spots made the cut."Fifteen Dogs" and the acclaimed musical "Life After" will return to Toronto in Off-Mirvish season.POV
Doug Ford has bungled affordable housing - now Ottawa is rubbing his nose in it.
CLOSE-UP
UNIVERSITY OF TORONTO: Pro-Palestinian protesters tore apart their university diplomas during a news conference at U of T yesterday. Students are demanding that the institution divest from Israel. Here's what that means and why they want it.
CAPTION: The McDougall Creek wildfire burns on the mountainside above a lakefront home, in West Kelowna, B.C. in 2023.Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Ontario legislature in May.Protesters at U of T are demanding the school publicize all its investments, end any investments that support the Israeli military or illegal West Bank settlements, and cut ties with some Israeli universities.Margaret Omielczenko is photographed in her apartment at Heritage Glen Retirement Home on May 6.
