Document 365 of 500

Kaffiyeh ban at Queen's Park has been a needless distraction. It should be reversed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kaffiyeh-ban-at-queens-park-has-been-needless/docview/3052716902/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kaffiyeh+ban+at+Queen%27s+Park+has+been+a+needless+distraction.+It+should+be+reversed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP MPP Jill Andrew was tossed from the Ontario legislature Wednesday for wearing a kaffiyeh, defying the ban on political attire.
Her ejection from the chamber is further fallout from the emotional -- and needless -- showdown instigated by long-time MPP Ted Arnott, speaker of the Ontario legislature. In mid-April, as the war between Israel and Hamas raged on, Arnott banned the wearing of the kaffiyeh in the Queen's Park precincts after complaints that the black-and-white scarves violated a longstanding rule against the wearing of "overtly political" symbols.
The decision promptly blew up in Arnott's face.
Independent MPP Sarah Jama, no stranger to controversy, having been turfed from the NDP caucus over her comments about the Israel-Hamas conflict, refused the speaker's order to remove her kaffiyeh.
And the standoff was on.
The images of Jama, a Black, Muslim politician from Hamilton Centre who uses a mobility scooter, sitting almost nose-to-nose with a sergeant-at-arms tasked with removing her from her seat were not the sort to make Ontario look to be the progressive place it claims.
Neither was the province done proud when individuals arriving at Queen's Park to meet with NDP Leader Marit Stiles were barred from entering because they were wearing kaffiyehs.
Critics said that, depending who is wearing it and why, the kaffiyeh may be cultural or political or both and it wasn't the business of the legislature speaker to define why any individual was wearing one.
The kaffiyeh has been a significant cultural symbol for Palestinians for centuries. It is widely understood in the Arab world and internationally as an expression of identity.
For her part, Stiles noted that MPPs have worn kilts, kirpans, kippahs and many other articles of clothing that express faith, identity, association and, in some cases at some times, politics.
Two motions to overturn Arnott's ruling were defeated. Government members defied Premier Doug Ford's urgings to undo the kaffiyeh ban. People watching from the public galleries who put on kaffiyehs were ejected by security.
The Canadian Civil Liberties Association said the suggestion inherent in Arnott's ruling that the legislature "is somehow not the place for politics is certainly questionable."
Indeed, the proposition that provocative articles of clothing offend the "standard practice of decorum" in the legislature grants to the chamber a standard of dignity seldom justified by the political players and often inane antics therein.
In calling for an end to the kaffiyeh ban, the association said Arnott should not issue orders that "undermine some of the most basic human rights" of legislature members.
The Ontario Federation of Labour also called for an end to what it called a "racist and discriminatory ban," saying it not only targets a particular community, but also those who speak out for them.
This week, Arnott clarified his ruling by saying kaffiyehs may be worn in the legislature building but not in the chamber. But the measure did not resolve matters.
Two NDP members - Kristyn Wong-Tam (Toronto Centre) and Joel Harding (Ottawa Centre) - were asked to leave the chamber for wearing kaffiyehs, despite Arnott's ruling, and Jama left, too, because of the solidarity shown by other members.
At root, Arnott has essentially produced a solution in search of a problem. The wearing of kaffiyehs was scarcely the incendiary political statement the speaker suggested. It was actually his ruling that thrust the garment into the spotlight.
For two weeks, the issue has consumed the legislature while more pressing matters and the urgent needs of Ontarians for better housing, health care and economic opportunity demand attention.
Arnott's half-hearted climbdown is essentially an admission that the original action was misguided. But it only went halfway to correcting the problem.
He should rescind his ban, allow kaffiyehs and let the focus shift to more pressing business.
CAPTION: Independent MPP Sarah Jama has been one of several politicians to wear a kaffiyeh in the Ontario legislature, defying a ban imposed by the speaker last month.

Subject: Bans; Legislatures; Politics

Location: Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: Kaffiyeh ban at Queen's Park has been a needless distraction. It should be reversed

'These documents tell a different story:' Newly released records reignite questions about Premier Ford's office's involvement in the Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-documents-tell-different-story-newly/docview/3052716675/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27These+documents+tell+a+different+story%3A%27+Newly+released+records+reignite+questions+about+Premier+Ford%27s+office%27s+involvement+in+the+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A member of Premier Doug Ford's office attended a private meeting with a developer regarding his land that was later removed from the Greenbelt, reigniting questions about how directly involved the premier's staff were in the ill-fated housing development plan.
Newly released records show that Carlo Oliviero, who was at the time the executive director of stakeholder relations for Ford, was invited to the meeting about Greenbelt lands in Hamilton. The invitation was forwarded by a well-known lobbyist and former executive assistant of the premier's.
Within days of the meeting, internal emails show, housing ministry staff were actively researching the land in Hamilton before ultimately selecting it for Greenbelt removal.
The meeting is mentioned in a roughly 4,000-page trove of private emails, text messages and other internal government documents obtained by the Ontario NDP through freedom of information legislation. The party says the documents add to the growing evidence that suggests the Premier's office was more deeply involved in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal than it claims.
As the scandal unfolded, there have been questions about how involved the Premier's office was in the Greenbelt development plan, and whether any of his staffers were involved in picking the parcels that developers wanted removed from the protected area.
Ford's office has maintained it had no direct knowledge or role in the selection of the Greenbelt lands for removal, which the Auditor General and the Integrity Commissioner found was rushed and favoured well-connected developers.
Premier's office says staff 'were not involved' in selecting Greenbelt lands
In the internal emails reviewed by the Star, Oliviero wasn't involved in subsequent discussions about removing the Hamilton land from the Greenbelt. In a statement, Oliviero said that after the initial meeting, "I referred the issue to the appropriate ministry."
Now a registered lobbyist in Ontario, Oliviero also said that in his former role in the premier's office, he was "not involved in housing policy or Greenbelt related issues."
In a response to questions for this story, Caitlin Clark, a spokesperson for the premier's office, said "As explicitly outlined in both the Integrity Commissioner and Auditor General's reports, premiers' office staff were not involved in specific site selection."
Ontario's NDP say the new documents challenge the messaging coming from the premier's office.
"Ford can pretend his government wasn't covering their tracks and hiding their intentions to give special treatment to land speculators. His government can pretend the Premier's office was barely involved. But pretending isn't reality. These documents tell a different story," Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles said in a statement.
Invitations sent to staffers' personal emails
Oliviero received the invitation on his personal email to the one-hour-long virtual meeting with the developer from lobbyist Nico Fidani-Diker, who previously worked as an executive assistant in Ford's office from 2018 to 2020.
Fidani-Diker also sent the invitation to the private email of then-housing minister's chief of staff, Ryan Amato, the former political staffer at the centre of Ford's government's Greenbelt debacle.
Fidani-Diker told the Star that he did not lobby for the landowner of the Greenbelt land in Hamilton and did not attend the meeting. He received the invitation from the landowner's company and forwarded the invitation to the two staffers who he knew dealt with housing matters.
Amato did not respond to the Star's questions by publication time.
Previous disclosures hinted a premier's office's involvement
Amato has previously hinted at the premier's office's involvement in selecting properties earmarked for development.
In October, Ecojustice and Environmental Defence released records they had obtained through freedom of information, which revealed how political staff directed bureaucrats on municipal boundary changes, often ignoring expert advice.
Included in those records were multiple instances of Amato suggesting that the "PO" - shorthand for the premier's office - was pushing for things behind the scenes.
The records show that Amato commented "PO wants this done" when discussing a request to include a developer's property in the expansion of the settlement area in York Region. In another email, Amato asked for a map of properties in Nobleton slated to be open for development, saying "po has asked me for a picture to make sure it's captured."
In his Aug. 2023 report, the Integrity Commissioner noted, "I find it more likely than not that Mr. Amato did say that the properties were given from the premier or the Premier's Office but ... his motive for dropping the name of the premier or the office was probably done to lend authority to his direction to the ministry public servants and not based in reality."
Ford announced that his government was reversing its Greenbelt plan in September 2023.
The following month, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police launched a criminal investigation into the government's plan to open up Greenbelt land for development. Ford has promised to co-operate with the ongoing probe including giving the RCMP access to his personal phone records.
The RCMP said there's no further update on the investigation at this time.
CAPTION: Ryan Amato, the former chief of staff to Ontario Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing.Newly released records show astaffer from Premier Doug Ford'soffice attended a privatemeeting with adeveloper regarding his Hamilton land that was later removed from the Greenbelt.

Document 367 of 500

Province warned of school cutbacks: Per-student funding not keeping up with inflation, boards say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-warned-school-cutbacks/docview/3052471092/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+warned+of+school+cutbacks%3A+Per-student+funding+not+keeping+up+with+inflation%2C+boards+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario school boards say provincial education funding is not keeping up with inflation and are warning of looming cutbacks.
"We know we're getting less money year over year," said Cathy Abraham, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association. "We know how much we're struggling to do all of the good things we want to do for kids, based on the financial reality of today."
The province will spend about $29 billion in operational funding for education in 2024-25, about $745 million more than in 2023-24.
In total, including capital funding, education spending is $36.6 billion this fiscal year, which is forecast to rise to $39.4 billion in 2026-27.
But Abraham said her association's analysis reports that while funding has increased, when adjusted for inflation, the province is spending $776 less per student in the next school year - and with two million students across the province, "that makes it a significant amount of money."
The association says that for each pupil, the province will spend $13,834, up from the current $13,584 - an increase of $250 a year or 1.8 per cent, which is below inflation, as it has been for several years.
Abraham said apart from sick leave, boards have also been left to cover the full costs of mandatory increases to Canada Pension Plan and employment insurance contributions, which alone have left a $100-million shortfall.
"These are costs we can't avoid," Abraham said. "It's not a commentary on whether or not these things should happen - these things should happen, but we have no way to make up any revenue for us to offset those costs. We've had to absorb these costs ... and it's going to have an impact on what we're able to do for students."
Education Minister Stephen Lecce has said the additional funds for 2024-25 will help pay for 300 math facilitators and 600 specialized teachers for reading help, and provide $3.5 billion in special education as well as $1.3 billion for student transportation - an increase of $80 million.
"I'm proud that our government has increased funding for special education by $659 million" compared to the previous Liberal government, Lecce said Wednesday during question period at Queen's Park. "That is the highest investment - $3.5 billion - ever recorded in Ontario history. We've also increased funding to $29 billion overall for education. We've increased the staffing by 9,000 more education workers, 3,000 more teachers."
On Monday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "all you have to do is talk to one parent in this province and you will know that the status quo is not working. It's not working for our kids in overcrowded classrooms; it's not working for our under-resourced teachers. The minister might want to try actually talking to parents, teachers and students."
Michael Bellmore, the new president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association, said there "were a few glimmers of hope (in funding), understanding that the province is doing what it can," but added that boards have been covering the additional CPP and EI costs for some time.
"As a result, it's got to come from somewhere, which affects the classroom," he said. "Those benchmarks that are associated with those kinds of costs, our recommendation is that the government increases them to align with what we have to pay."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Special education; Students; Funding

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Province warned of school cutbacks:   Per-student funding not keeping up with inflation, boards say

Document 368 of 500

Reverse needless kaffiyeh ban.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reverse-needless-kaffiyeh-ban/docview/3052470942/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reverse+needless+kaffiyeh+ban.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP MPP Jill Andrew was tossed from the Ontario legislature Wednesday for wearing a kaffiyeh, defying the ban on political attire.
Her ejection from the chamber is further fallout from the emotional - and needless - showdown instigated by long-time MPP Ted Arnott, Speaker of the Ontario legislature. In mid-April, as the war between Israel and Hamas raged on, Arnott banned the wearing of the kaffiyeh in the Queen's Park precincts after complaints that the black-and-white scarves violated a long-standing rule against the wearing of "overtly political" symbols.
The decision promptly blew up in Arnott's face.
Independent MPP Sarah Jama, no stranger to controversy, having been turfed from the NDP caucus over her comments about the Israel-Hamas conflict, refused the Speaker's order to remove her kaffiyeh.
And the standoff was on.
The images of Jama, a Black, Muslim politician from Hamilton Centre who uses a mobility scooter, sitting almost nose-to-nose with a sergeant-at-arms tasked with removing her from her seat were not the sort to make Ontario look to be the progressive place it claims. Neither was the province done proud when individuals arriving at Queen's Park to meet with NDP Leader Marit Stiles were barred from entering because they were wearing kaffiyehs.
Critics said that, depending who is wearing it and why, the kaffiyeh may be cultural or political or both and it wasn't the business of the legislature Speaker to define why any individual was wearing one.
The kaffiyeh has been a significant cultural symbol for Palestinians for centuries. It is widely understood in the Arab world and internationally as an expression of identity.
For her part, Stiles noted that MPPs have worn kilts, kirpans, kippahs and many other articles of clothing that express faith, identity, association and, in some cases at some times, politics.
Two motions to overturn Arnott's ruling were defeated. Government members defied Premier Doug Ford's urgings to undo the kaffiyeh ban. People watching from the public galleries who put on kaffiyehs were ejected by security.
The Canadian Civil Liberties Association said the suggestion inherent in Arnott's ruling that the legislature "is somehow not the place for politics is certainly questionable."
Indeed, the proposition that provocative articles of clothing offend the "standard practice of decorum" in the legislature grants to the chamber a standard of dignity seldom justified by the political players and often inane antics therein.
In calling for an end to the kaffiyeh ban, the association said Arnott should not issue orders that "undermine some of the most basic human rights" of legislature members.
The Ontario Federation of Labour also called for an end to what it called a "racist and discriminatory ban," saying it not only targets a particular community, but also those who speak out for them.
This week, Arnott clarified his ruling by saying kaffiyehs may be worn in the legislature building but not in the chamber. But the measure did not resolve matters.
Two NDP members - Kristyn Wong-Tam (Toronto Centre) and Joel Harding (Ottawa Centre) - were asked to leave the chamber for wearing kaffiyehs, despite Arnott's ruling, and Jama left, too, because of the solidarity shown by other members.
At root, Arnott has essentially produced a solution in search of a problem. The wearing of kaffiyehs was scarcely the incendiary political statement the Speaker suggested. It was actually his ruling that thrust the garment into the spotlight.
For two weeks, the issue has consumed the legislature while more pressing matters and the urgent needs of Ontarians for better housing, health care and economic opportunity demand attention.
Arnott's half-hearted climbdown is essentially an admission that the original action was misguided. But it only went halfway to correcting the problem. He should rescind his ban, allow kaffiyehs and let the focus shift to more pressing business.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: ;

Document 369 of 500

Here's what's really going on inside: With more than 200 tents at King's College Circle, there are no signs anyone is leaving soon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/heres-whats-really-going-on-inside/docview/3052470388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what%27s+really+going+on+inside%3A+With+more+than+200+tents+at+King%27s+College+Circle%2C+there+are+no+signs+anyone+is+leaving+soon&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A young man in ball cap, shorts and sneakers carries his phone aloft, livestreaming as he strides around the pro-Palestinian protest camp at U of T.
"These people support terrorists," he proclaims. "They don't believe what happened on Oct. 7 was terrorism."
"That's because it wasn't!" says a woman, head wrapped in a kaffiyeh, face covered in a surgical mask. "You're delusional."
The two stop to face each other, separated only by their extended arms and phones, bothrecording.
More angry words are exchanged. Others start circling and it appears things might escalate, when university security in fluorescent vests intervene to separate them.
It's yet another example of an ugly interaction between people on opposing sides of the conflict in the Middle East. Similar scenes have taken place at demonstrations across the city for months. But the quick defusing of the conflict shows hints of how the atmosphere at the protest camp is different.
The collection of tents has become a magnet for both those outraged by Israel's military campaign in Gaza and those seeking to debate, deride or sometimes delegitimize the pro-Palestinian movement.
What one sees in this protest varies wildly. For supporters, it's a moment of unity and solidarity in the face of a devastating war overseas and a history of injustice. There is an urgency to the cause, that outweighs all else, they say. But the protests have faced fierce criticism, too, ranging from those on the confrontational far right to those who say they're supportive of the cause but disturbed by some of the messages they've seen emanating from the encampment.
Protesters describe far-right media outlets sticking cameras in tents and filming people's underwear and cars showing up in the middle of the night to blast "O Canada" to wake them up.
But the camp inhabitants don't appear to have let such provocations rile them. With marshalls trained in de-escalation, they quash screaming matches, like the one that occurred Monday evening, before they go viral.
Unlike the occasionally aggressive and antagonistic protests that have taken over Toronto streets for the past seven months, the atmosphere at the encampment in the centre of the city is largely chill.
A giant Palestinian flag has been erected at the entrance to the camp, reminiscent of the one that flies atop a monument in central Ramallah. Dozens of smaller flags have been affixed to the southern fence line.
There's a tent for food, first aid and a stockpile of camping supplies - so much has been donated that the surplus has been sent to people living in Allan Gardens. There are First Nations elders gathered around a fire. Gluten-free meals are available upon request.
A call to prayer echoes over the PA in Arabic.
Classes have wrapped up at U of T, and while the summer semester officially began Monday, the few students around had little trouble getting to class. Both the university and Premier Doug Ford have said the camp must go, but with more than 200 tents now dotting the lawn at King's College Circle, there are no signs that anyone is going anywhere.
On Tuesday morning, shortly after a group discussion of Frantz Fanon's anti-colonial classic "The Wretched of the Earth" wraps up, the camp is quiet. The sun is high and shade is sparse. Free water and sunscreen are available to help the occupiers weather the heat, but several who spent the night slip away to go home and shower.
The camp is open to the public, and volunteers stand sentry at the openings to the fence, screening arrivals. Masks and head coverings are encouraged to protect protesters from being doxxed - identified online - by people they call "Zionist agitators" who have been coming to the fence line.
On-boarding of first-time arrivals consists of a short speech containing a few tips and pointers, including a recitation of the three principles of Palestinian liberation: 1) the Palestinians have a right to self-determination, 2) that Jerusalem is the capital of Palestine and 3) that Palestinians have the right to return.
Oh, and please don't engage with the press, the counter protesters or the police.
Despite the bucolic surroundings and commune-like atmosphere, the camp is still seen as violently promoting hate speech by some in the U of T community.
"They've created the illusion of a very peaceful encampment," says Robert Schwartz, a professor in the Dalla Lana School of Public Health. "But read the signs. That's verbal violence."
Professors at the University of Toronto have staked positions in support of and against the encampment. While some have lined up with student protesters and their goals, others have expressed outrage at the encampment taking root on their campus.
Adding to the concerns, there has been chalk graffiti with messages such as "Go back to Europe" - an antisemitic trope, mixed with more familiar refrains heard throughout war protests.
As a survivor of an attack during the first intefadeh in Israel, Schwartz says signs calling for intefadeh are calling for "armed terrorist resistance against innocent civilian Jews." Banners showing a map of the entire region in the colours of the Palestinian flag cannot be interpreted other than as a call for the removal of Jews from the region, he argues.
"The message is very clear. They are not interested in a two-state solution that has room both for Jewish Israelis and Palestinians. They, are interested in annihilating the state of Israel," he said.
"I'm pro-Palestinian. I am against the atrocities that Israel has committed in Gaza. I am against the atrocities that Hamas committed on Oct. 7," he said. "I agree with free speech and peaceful protest of these things would be fine. But what we are seeing and hearing at the encampment at King's College Circle is really hate speech."

Only a few weeks ago, King's College circle was a construction site and workers are still sawing sections of pipe on the south end of the lawn.
For more than three years, the historic heart of U of T's downtown St. George campus has been fenced off as a massive geothermal system was installed, involving the drilling of 370 boreholes 250 metres deep.
Shortly after the sod was re-laid, university officials decided to erect fences again, this time in a futile attempt to prevent students from copying protest camps that have been set up on campuses across the U.S.
Last week, under the cover of darkness, U of T students opened a gap in the fence and established their own encampment, which has since grown to a sprawling protest village.
While several of the encampments in the U.S. have been violently broken up by police, with hundreds of arrests, U of T says its goal is a "peaceful conclusion to the unauthorized encampment as soon as possible."
At a press conference Wednesday, camp organizers complained that they're being watched at night by police and private security hired by the university to monitor the situation. They also said the university has restricted their washroom use at night, though the administration said current students can enter buildings using fob-access, "as per normal practice."
Camp spokesperson Erin Mackey says they've met twice with administration, but their demands have not come up once. The protesters want U of T to disclose and divest from its investment in arms manufacturers and other companies supporting the war in Gaza. They are also calling for an academic boycott of Israeli scholars.
Mackey, a fourth-year political science and environmental science student, was active in the successful campaign to get U of T to divest from fossil fuel companies in 2021 and sees this new divestment target as an achievable goal.
The university has already backed off its first deadline for the campers to leave and appears content to wait them out for the time being. The real test may come in a month, when U of T starts a month of Convocation ceremonies centred on the lawn where the tents are. U of T declined to comment on its graduation ceremony plans.
"If they want to call the police on their students to end this encampment, it says a lot more about them than it does about us," Mackey said.
On Tuesday morning, in front of the Gerstein Science building, plastic patio furniture welcomed new students munching pizza and looking at U of T brochures only a few metres from the fence line. A half dozen grey and white SUVs that look a lot like police cars are parked at intervals around the camp's perimeter, while uniformed private security patrol.
Despite the very tangible presence of tents, much of the action is online. U of T's wifi is strong and campers co-ordinate daily activities on private chat groups. Online, protesters expressed their horror at Israel's latest military offensive, which began only hours after Hamas agreed to terms of a ceasefire that were promptly rejected.
"Children in Rafah sent us a message this morning. They are the reason why we do this," wrote the UofT Occupy for Palestine account on X, accompanied by a photo of kids with a sign thanking University of Toronto St. George campus.
"You honour and appreciate us despite the fact that we should have done this long ago."
One Palestinian student organizer, whom the Star agreed to keep anonymous out of concern for her safety, said she feels that people are having so much fun at the camp, sometimes they almost forget why they're protesting - until they look at their phones.
"Yes, have fun. Enjoy your time. But don't let your guard down and don't forget why you're here."
Online the message was the same: "A reminder that while UofT admin complain about language and fences, they are actively funding genocide and scholasticide," camp organizers posted on X.
Like other critics, Schwartz, who was planning to speak at a rally condemning the encampment Wednesday evening, said he feels threatened by the signs and banners every time he walks to and from his nearby office. However just the cause, he says, this sense of fear and intimidation should not be diminished or dismissed.
Many in the encampment, however, insist nothing should deflect focus from the horrors playing out in Gaza.
"We are here to fight for the liberation of all Palestinians and that is all we care about. Not anybody's comfort," said the camp account on X.
"All eyes on Rafah."
With files from Ben Cohen
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter With files from Ben Cohen

Subject: Jewish people; Atrocities; Students; Tents; Hate speech; Demonstrations & protests; Palestinian people; Truces & cease fires

Location: Gaza Strip; United States--US; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Document 370 of 500

MPP ejected from legislature over kaffiyeh

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-ejected-legislature-over-kaffiyeh/docview/3052470331/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+ejected+from+legislature+over+kaffiyeh&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: New Democrat MPP Jill Andrew was ejected from the Ontario legislature Wednesday for wearing a kaffiyeh in defiance of a ban on "political attire" in the chamber.
Fuelling a rift in the NDP caucus over the kaffiyeh issue, the incident upstaged party leader Marit Stiles as she grilled Premier Doug Ford's government on the doctor shortage that has left 2.3 million Ontarians without family physicians.
Andrew (Toronto-St. Paul's) was escorted from the chamber for the day after refusing a request from Speaker Ted Arnott to remove the black-and-white scarf worn in solidarity with Palestinians in Gaza.
A teary Andrew told reporters she wore the kaffiyeh, as well as a Jewish prayer cloth, "to stand for peace, for freedom, for the civil rights of people who are having their civil rights attacked by this legislature."
She is the third MPP to be ejected from the chamber this week for wearing a kaffiyeh.
Her decision to don the black-and-white scarf followed what sources told the Star was a heated debate in Tuesday's NDP caucus meeting over the merits of the party's recent focus on overturning the ban.
There are concerns it is distracting from the party's efforts to hold the government to account on provincial issues such as education and health care, insiders said privately in order to discuss internal deliberations.
Andrew stressed she strongly disagrees with Arnott's ruling that kaffiyeh may not be worn in the chamber.
"We cannot ask Ontarians to check their heritage, their culture, their religion, their identity at the door," she said.
The Speaker waded into the issue in March, deciding that kaffiyehs had become a political symbol since the Hamas-Israel War began Oct. 7.
He banned them from the building, but softened that position in a clarification Monday that allows MPPs, staff and visitors to wear them in the building but not in the legislative chamber or public viewing galleries.
"I am standing with Palestinians, Arabs, Muslims and Jews in my riding and across Ontario who are against the kaffiyeh ban," Andrew said.
Andrew said she pulled the kaffiyeh out of her bag and donned it before the legislature's daily question period began.
It appears Arnott did not notice the scarf until the one-hour session was in progress.
New Democrat MPPs Kristyn Wong-Tam (Toronto Centre) and Joel Harden (Ottawa Centre) followed Andrew out of the chamber in support but were not wearing kaffiyehs as they did on Monday. That's when Harden was ejected along with Independent MPP Sarah Jama (Hamilton Centre), a former New Democrat.
Stiles has tried twice to have the kaffiyeh ban lifted by a vote of the legislature, but attempts have been blocked by a handful of Progressive Conservative MPPs. All party leaders have said they do not agree with the ban.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Bans; Legislatures; Civil rights; Palestinian people; Religious clothing

Title: MPP ejected from legislature over kaffiyeh

Document 371 of 500

Here's what's going on inside the U of T encampment protest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-whats-going-on-inside-u-t-encampment/docview/3052470282/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+what%27s+going+on+inside+the+U+of+T+encampment+protest&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"I'm pro-Palestinian. I am against the atrocities that Israel has committed in Gaza. I am against the atrocities that Hamas committed on Oct. 7," he said. "I agree with free speech and peaceful protest of these things would be fine. But what we are seeing and hearing at the encampment at King's College Circle is really hate speech."
Only a few weeks ago, King's College circle was a construction site and workers are still sawing sections of pipe on the south end of the lawn.
For more than three years, the historic heart of U of T's downtown St. George campus has been fenced off as a massive geothermal system was installed, involving the drilling of 370 boreholes 250 metres deep.
Shortly after the sod was relaid, university officials decided to erect fences again, this time in a futile attempt to prevent students from copying protest camps that have been set up on campuses across the U.S.
Last week, under the cover of darkness, U of T students opened a gap in the fence and established their own encampment, which has since grown to a sprawling protest village.
While several of the encampments in the U.S. have been violently broken up by police, with hundreds of arrests, U of T says its goal is a "peaceful conclusion to the unauthorized encampment as soon as possible."
At a press conference Wednesday, camp organizers complained that they're being watched at night by police and private security hired by the university to monitor the situation. They also said the university has restricted their washroom use at night, though the administration said current students can enter buildings using fob-access, "as per normal practice."
Camp spokesperson Erin Mackey says they've met twice with administration, but their demands have not come up once. The protesters want U of T to disclose and divest from its investment in arms manufacturers and other companies supporting the war in Gaza. They are also calling for an academic boycott of Israeli scholars.
Mackey, a fourth-year political science and environmental science student, was active in the successful campaign to get U of T to divest from fossil fuel companies in 2021 and sees this new divestment target as an achievable goal.
The university has already backed off its first deadline for the campers to leave and appears content to wait them out for the time being. The real test may come in a month, when U of T starts a month of Convocation ceremonies centred on the lawn where the tents are. U of T declined to comment on its graduation ceremony plans.
"If they want to call the police on their students to end this encampment, it says a lot more about them than it does about us," Mackey said.
***
On Tuesday morning, in front of the Gerstein Science building, plastic patio furniture welcomed new students munching pizza and looking at U of T brochures only a few metres from the fence line. A half dozen grey and white SUVs that look a lot like police cars are parked at intervals around the camp's perimeter, while uniformed private security patrol.
Despite the very tangible presence of tents, much of the action is online. U of T's wifi is strong and campers co-ordinate daily activities on private chat groups.
Online, protesters expressed their horror to learn of Israel's latest military offensive, which began only hours after Hamas agreed to terms of a ceasefire that were promptly rejected.
"Children in Rafah sent us a message this morning. They are the reason why we do this," wrote the UofT Occupy for Palestine account on X, formerly Twitter, accompanied by a photo of kids with a sign thanking University of Toronto St. George campus.
"You honour and appreciate us despite the fact that we should have done this long ago."
One Palestinian student organizer, whom the Star agreed to keep anonymous out of concern for her safety, said she feels that people are having so much fun at the camp, sometimes they almost forget why they're protesting - until they look at their phones.
"Yes, have fun. Enjoy your time. But don't let your guard down and don't forget why you're here," she said.
Online the message was the same: "A reminder that while UofT admin complain about language and fences, they are actively funding genocide and scholasticide," camp organizers posted on X.
Like other critics, Professor Schwartz, who was planning to speak at a rally condemning the encampment Wednesday evening, said he feels threatened by the signs and banners every time he walks to and from his nearby office. However just the cause, he says, this sense of fear and intimidation should not be diminished or dismissed.
Many in the encampment, however, insist nothing should deflect focus from the horrors playing out in Gaza.
"We are here to fight for the liberation of all Palestinians and that is all we care about. Not anybody's comfort," said the camp account on X.
"All eyes on Rafah."
With files from Ben Cohen
CAPTION: Protestors have opened University of Toronto's 41st library amid their encampment. Currently it boasts upwards of 200 books, with a variety of topics. Daily discussions are also held in the tent where people with varying viewpoints come together in dialogue.Pro-Palestinian protester encampment at University of Toronto's King's College Circle.Pro-Palestinian protester encampment at University of Toronto's King's College Circle.Encampment residents have strengthened the fence surrounding them. Pro-Palestinian protester encampment at University of Toronto's King's College Circle.Pro-Palestinian protester encampment at University of Toronto's King's College Circle.Pro-Palestinian protester encampment at University of Toronto's King's College Circle.

Document 372 of 500

Triple-grade plan slammed: Kensington parents 'very frustrated' with proposal to combine three classes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/triple-grade-plan-slammed/docview/3052470262/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Triple-grade+plan+slammed%3A+Kensington+parents+%27very+frustrated%27+with+proposal+to+combine+three+classes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Students and parents protested outside a Toronto school Wednesday morning, voicing concerns about a plan to combine three grades in one class taught by a single teacher.
The move, slated to take effect next year, means one teacher at Kensington Community School will oversee a class with kids in grades 4, 5 and 6.
"Parents across the community are very frustrated," said Sepideh Shahi, co-chair of the school's parent council, noting that in addition to creating a triple-grade class, the school is losing 2.5 teaching positions for the 2024-25 academic year. "It's simple math: You'll have one teacher and three curriculums and six hours ... There is no way that you can go to the depth and breadth of those three curriculums in those six hours."
About 50 community members gathered before class outside the school, on College Street near Bathurst Street. Some held signs, such as "Funding Cuts Hurt Kids" and "No Teacher Cuts," and chanted "fund our schools," while passing motorists honked in support.
The province recently announced it will increase education funding to school boards for the coming year by $745 million, with total funds amounting to $29 billion, which is the highest investment in Ontario's history. Funding to the Toronto District School Board will increase by $68 million and it will receive a total of $3.3 billion.
However, an analysis by the Ontario Public School Boards' Association notes that when adjusted for inflation, per-pupil funding is at the "lowest level" in over a decade. Meanwhile, the TDSB is facing a projected $26.5-million deficit next year, noting it spends more than it receives. The board warns that a funding shortfall could jeopardize student and community programming.
At Kensington - it has 120 kids from JK to Grade 6 - students are currently in a grades 4 and 5 split class, and a Grade 6 class. Parents say reinstating one full-time teacher next year would enable the current composition to continue. In a school bulletin sent last month to parents, the principal said "a triple-grade class is not ideal and I have advocated to the planning department for more support." But he also noted that just 24 students are projected to be in Grades 4, 5 and 6 next year, so only one teacher for a junior class was allocated.
TDSB spokesperson Ryan Bird said combining three grades is not uncommon and "is present in a number of TDSB schools each year."
"Despite the multiple grades, students will be well-supported," he told the Star, adding that the class configuration will be finalized in September when the actual number of students is confirmed.
Given current budget pressures, he said the board cannot allot another teacher to the school, with the small number of students.
"While it may seem insignificant at one school, we also have to be cognizant that we have 581 other schools ... If we were to make exceptions at some of them, we're talking about hundreds of thousands and into the millions of dollars" in costs.
A key concern for parents is that learning will suffer, says mother Rebecca Osolen, adding that little is known about the impacts of mixing three grades in a class and integrating students with and without diagnosed special needs.
"This decision to have a three-grade split at our school is not based on learning outcomes," she said. "It doesn't account for all of the range of abilities in the classroom. And it's definitely not supportive of teachers."
She notes that experts disagree on the advantages and disadvantages of combining grades, and says research the TDSB gave parents did little to assuage concerns because it focused on classes with two-grade splits.
Osolen also says Kensington's low enrolment is because of the TDSB's boundaries of catchment areas, noting neighbouring schools are oversubscribed.
Ben Kunder, whose son will be in Grade 4 next year, is concerned about the range of ages in the class. He notes the difference between an eight-year-old and 11-year-old "is vast in terms of their maturity and what they can handle and how they learn."
Also at the protest was NDP MPP Jessica Bell, who represents the riding (University-Rosedale). She said education is underfunded, so schools across Ontario are making "very tough decisions around classroom allocations."
Bell said Kensington will see a three-grade split class and the loss of two teachers, while other schools are losing vice-principals and support staff such as lunchroom supervisors.
"It's all playing out in different ways, but what is very clear is there's one reason why this is happening: The Ontario government has consistently cut funding to schools year in and year out. And it's our kids who are feeling the impact."
The Ministry of Education says it provides the framework, funding and flexibility to support school boards in meeting class size requirements, but the boards make the staffing decisions and organize classes to meet regulations.
CAPTION: Parents at Kensington Community School protest plans to combine classes for students in Grades 4 to 6 next fall under one teacher. With just 120 pupils from JK to Grade 6, the school is losing 2.5 teacher positions.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star
CREDIT: Isabel Teotonio Toronto Star

Subject: Students; Community; Funding; Learning; Teachers; Education; School boards

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

A Toronto school is combining three grades in one class. Parents are 'very frustrated'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-school-is-combining-three-grades-one/docview/3052826685/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 374 of 500

Umar Zameer 'overwhelmed' by support as GoFundMe for legal fees surpasses $300,000

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/umar-zameer-overwhelmed-support-as-gofundme-legal/docview/3052826519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Umar+Zameer+%27overwhelmed%27+by+support+as+GoFundMe+for+legal+fees+surpasses+%24300%2C000&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Umar Zameer, who was cleared of charges of murder and manslaughter in the death of Toronto police Const. Jeffrey Northrup, says he's been "overwhelmed" by the outpouring of support he's received in the wake of his acquittal.
A crowdsourced fundraiser meant to offset Zameer's legal fees has now shattered its goal.
The GoFundMe has surpassed $300,000 - more than $100,000 above its initial target - and will help offset what experts say would have been a growing cost over the three-year lead-up to his trial.
"I could have never imagined that Canadians from across the country would come together to support me during the most challenging time of my life," Zameer told the Star in an email.
Zameer was arrested and charged with first-degree murder after he fatally struck Northrup with his car following a chaotic incident in the underground parking garage under Nathan Phillips Square just after midnight on July 2, 2021. Three years and a gripping trial lead to Zameer's acquittal.
The GoFundMe - organized by Zameer's wife, Aaida Shaikh, shortly after his arrest in 2021 - had just $30,000 in donations before his verdict in mid-April. By Sunday, it had raised more than $310,000, with donations still rolling in.
"My family and I are forever indebted to everyone for their unconditional support," Zameer said.
Attached to the thousands of donations to Zameer and his family, including a top donation of $5,000 from one individual, are messages of support and hope from donors.
"An injustice was avoided after an injustice was perpetrated. I hope your family can find some peace in moving forward," one donor writes.
"I believe I speak for many Canadians when I say that I am deeply ashamed at the way you have been treated," another added.
Zameer said he's read "every word of support" on the GoFundMe page, "Every time I read them, I feel very grateful that everyone has come to believe our truth and has extended their empathy toward my family and myself."
Those messages, he added, have helped him and his family decide to stay and live in Canada.
Born in Pakistan, Zameer came to Canada as a permanent resident. He worked his way through several part-time jobs before becoming an accountant. Both his children were born in Canada.
"I hope one day I will also be able to give back to Canada," Zameer said.
Zameer said his legal team, lawyers Nader Hasan and Alexandra Heine, have always kept in mind his "financial constraints" and been "fundamental pillars of support" for both Zameer and his family.
"I owe so much to them for their selfless hard work and expert advocacy to get my freedom and my honour back," he added.
Hasan said while his firm worked to reduce costs for Zameer, his client's family and friends pulled together to cover many of their costs over the past three years.
Zameer would have racked up considerable legal fees, experts have previously told the Star, for everything from the cost of hiring experts to testify, to going through five weeks of trial.
What happened to Umar Zameer?After Zameer was released on bail, politicians such as Premier Doug Ford and former Toronto mayor John Tory spoke out against his release, bringing national attention to the case.
At trial, Zameer testified he did not know he was being approached by plainclothes Toronto police officers after a night out celebrating Canada Day with his wife and two-year-old son. Saying he feared for his life and was trying to flee who he thought were armed criminals, he testified he had no idea he had run Northrup over until after the beloved 55-year-old veteran officer was already dead.
On April 21, after three days of deliberations, a jury found him not guilty of both murder and manslaughter, definitively rejecting the Crown's theory that he had intentionally killed Northrup, knowing he was an officer.
Behind the scenes at trial, Superior Court Justice Anne Molloy was critical of the prosecution's case, saying the evidence contradicted the police eyewitnesses who testified Zameer ran Northrup down intentionally.
"What motive would this person have?" she asked prosecutors.
After his acquittal, Molloy made the rare move of apologizing to Zameer.
"You have my ... deepest apologies for what you have been through," the judge said.
With files from Calvi Leon
CAPTION: The GoFundMe had just $30,000 in donations before Zameer's verdict was delivered in mid-April.

Document 375 of 500

Ontario students will suffer from Ford government's education funding shortfalls, school boards warn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-students-will-suffer-ford-governments/docview/3052826515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+students+will+suffer+from+Ford+government%27s+education+funding+shortfalls%2C+school+boards+warn&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario school boards say provincial education funding is not keeping up with inflation and are warning of looming cutbacks.
"We know we're getting less money year over year," said Cathy Abraham, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association. "We know how much we're struggling to do all of the good things we want to do for kids, based on the financial reality of today."
The province will spend about $29 billion in operational funding for education in 2024-25, some $745 million more than in 2023-24.
In total, including capital funding, education spending is $36.6 billion this fiscal year, which is forecast to rise to $39.4 billion in 2026-27.
But Abraham said her association's analysis reports that while funding has increased, when adjusted for inflation, the province is spending $776 less per student in the next school year - and with two million students across the province, "that makes it a significant amount of money."
The association says per pupil, the province will spend $13,834, up from the current $13,584 - an increase of some $250 a year or 1.8 per cent, which is below inflation as it has been for several years.
Abraham said apart from sick leave, boards have also been left to cover the full costs of mandatory increases to Canada Pension Plan and Employment Insurance contributions, which alone have left a $100-million shortfall.
"These are costs we can't avoid," Abraham said. "It's not a commentary on whether or not these things should happen - these things should happen, but we have no way to make up any revenue for us to offset those costs. We've had to absorb these costs ... and it's going to have an impact on what we're able to do for students."
Education Minister Stephen Lecce has said the additional funds for 2024-25 will help pay for 300 math facilitators and 600 specialized teachers especially for reading help, and provide $3.5 billion in special education as well as $1.3 billion for student transportation - or $80 million more.
"I'm proud that our government has increased funding for special education by $659 million" compared to the previous Liberal government, Lecce said Wednesday during question period at Queen's Park. "That is the highest investment - $3.5 billion - ever recorded in Ontario history. We've also increased funding to $29 billion overall for education. We've increased the staffing by 9,000 more education workers, 3,000 more teachers."
On Monday, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "all you have to do is talk to one parent in this province and you will know that the status quo is not working. It's not working for our kids in overcrowded classrooms; it's not working for our under-resourced teachers. The minister might want to try actually talking to parents, teachers and students."
Michael Bellmore, the new president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees' Association, said there "were a few glimmers of hope (in funding), understanding that the province is doing what it can," but added that boards have been covering the additional CPP and EI costs for some time.
"As a result, it's got to come from somewhere, which affects the classroom," he said. "Those benchmarks that are associated with those kinds of costs, our recommendation is that the government increases them to align with what we have to pay ."
The cost of sick leave for teachers continues to strain board budgets, he said, but added that they have "engaged the services of third parties to help manage and mitigate that concern. I think we're going to start seeing some results from that - but it's definitely a problem that's province-wide."
The Toronto District School Board, the country's largest, is looking at a $26.5-million deficit for the 2024-25 school year.
CAPTION: Cathy Abraham is president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association.

Subject: Special education; Students; Costs; Sick leave; Funding

Business indexing term: Subject: Costs Sick leave

Title: Ontario students will suffer from Ford government's education funding shortfalls, school boards warn

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 376 of 500

Plenty of reasons women don't want children - including men who generally fail to do their part

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/plenty-reasons-women-dont-want-children-including/docview/3052139840/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Plenty+of+reasons+women+don%27t+want+children+-+including+men+who+generally+fail+to+do+their+part&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We are not having enough babies and that's a problem for all of us, May 5
In response to Jaime Watt's recent opinion on why Canadian women are not having children, I'll agree part of the reason is economics. Children are expensive. But that is only a small part of why many women don't want children. Women do a disproportionate amount of housework. On average, having a male spouse results in six unpaid additional hours of household labour per week for women. Children add many more unpaid labour hours to women's lives. Women also do a disproportionate amount of child rearing. Taking time from paid work to raise children has a negative impact on upward mobility in the paid workforce, and time off of paid work suppresses women's wages over their lifetime. Men are not financially impacted in the same way because men generally fail to do their part rearing children, just as men fail to do their part regarding housework. Having children in your 20s and 30s can interfere with women's educational goals, travel, and relocation. Women often prioritize these things, finding them more personally gratifying than children. Obtaining rental housing can be very difficult for single women with children. Canada has a high divorce rate, resulting in women raising children alone, often without support. Having children can result in insecure housing for women. As a male and a Conservative, one expects Watt would view women as nothing more than human incubators. Women, however, know we are more than just incubators destined to birth the next generation of taxpayers. Women have lives, which we want to live as unencumbered as a Conservative male.
Deb Bernat, St. Catharines
The solution to affordable housing is social housing
Ten big problems define Toronto's housing crisis. Here's what every level of government is promising to do about it (and whether it will work, May 5
There is only one solution to create truly affordable long-term housing. The solution is "social housing" for rent delivered through non-profit, co-operative and municipal housing providers. This solution starts with free government land combined with no government entitlements such as taxes, fees and charges. These fees represents 35 per cent of a total development's costs. Governments will further have to subsidize costs based on the level of affordability. Government will guarantee and write down financing. Organizing the process and teams to pull this off is the next step. This is the path forward. We all have to push government to get on board.
Steven Kirshenblatt, Kirshenblatt Urban Architecture Inc.
Blue Jay fans deserve more from Rogers
You can hear the fans clamouring that heads should roll for what the current Blue Jays are producing. the current owners, Rogers Communication Inc. are not baseball people, their only concern is their bottom line. Spending more than $400 million on stadium renovations and increasing ticket prices are only ploys to increase revenues. Jays President Mark Shapiro and, in his ninth season as Blue Jays General Manager, Ross Atkins have done nothing to bolster the club to a championship calibre. Prior to coming to Toronto, their tenure at Cleveland was a bust. Atkins latest attempts to add high-profile players to the team are a joke, you need more than AAA no-names. The fans deserve more than just a fancy dressed-up ballpark. It shouldn't take a GM close to 10 years to create a winner, if he has the baseball smarts necessary. Heads should roll.
Gary Megaffin, Kitchener
Proportional representation would make any foreign interference ineffectiveWhat the foreign interference inquiry has to say about foreign election meddling, May 3
Frankly, I am far less concerned that "foreign interference" may persuade a few voters to change their vote than I am about the fact that in any given election, the majority of votes elect no one. This is a failure of all winner-take-all voting systems. Conversely, when proportional representation is used, typically 95 per cent of votes elect someone. That's why the vast bulk of industrial democracies use it. When people's votes actually count, "foreign interference" can't be targeted to altering a few votes in a handful of swing ridings. It becomes ineffective. Instead, we are left with the more pervasive problem of the rich and powerful abusing their positions to influence our governments for their own benefit.
Gary Dale, West Hill, ON
Put an end to corporate welfareTrudeau and Ford unveil Honda's plan to build four new factories in Ontario, April 25
Premier Doug Ford keeps giving your money to multinational corporations. Ford likes to say he's sticking up for the little guy. But he's taking your money and giving $2.5 billion to Honda, more than $4 billion to Volkswagen and $5 billion to Stellantis - the company that makes Maserati and Alfa Romeo. Does Ford really think Honda, Volkswagen and Maserati are the little guys? When Ford was first running to be premier, he said he would stop "picking winners and losers" and instead cut taxes for everyone. He said, "the party is over with the taxpayers' money." Does it look like the taxpayer party is over for corporate lobbyists?
George Kranz, Etobicoke
Maple Leafs were playing flat
The Maple Leafs' Cup drought hits 57 years with another Game 7 loss, and another one at the hands of the Bruins, May. 5
I blamed poor coaching for the Maple Leafs loss. The team was playing flat. The pond hockey play favoured by the coaching staff requires a high level of spirit and determination to fill in for body memory in a set play system. For games five, six and seven there was a different team. Less line mixing and a spirited desire to win is a coach's doing. When the need was greatest, Leafs coach Sheldon Keefe stepped up.
He deserves the credit. Lifted spirit makes lifted play and the middle of the road players have responded. Good work coach Keefe. You have likely dodged the headsman even though it was seven and out. One more season is likely all he will get as earned. Anything more should depend only on next season. Pond hockey has been given a fair chance. No more free lunch.
General Manager Brad Treliving gets credit for beefing up the team. Their hitting greatly increased.
John Wilkins, Toronto
Another well-earned Leafs banner
The Maple Leafs' Cup drought hits 57 years with another Game 7 loss, and another one at the hands of the Bruins, May. 5
It's time for us all to look ahead to the first Leafs home game next season. I can hardly wait to see the ceremonial unveiling of the team's latest participation banner as it hangs proudly over centre ice.
Ronald Wencer, Toronto
CAPTION: Women want to live their lives as unencumbered as a Conservative male, writesDeb Bernat.

Subject: Housework; Women; Professional hockey

Company / organization: Name: Volkswagen AG; NAICS: 336110, 336390

Document 378 of 500

Without justice there is no peace

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/without-justice-there-is-no-peace/docview/3051830983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Without+justice+there+is+no+peace&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Monday night, I was sitting at home watching the news. I heard that the Israeli government continued its strikes against Hamas with an assault on the southern Gaza city of Rafah.
On Sunday, it was reported that "(t)here is famine, full-blown famine, in the north, and it's moving its way south."
Last week, protest encampments popped up at universities across the province including the University of Toronto, McMaster, Western and the University of Ottawa.
As it seems necessary to highlight, the assault by Hamas on Oct. 7 was despicable and a terrorist act. The Israeli citizens who were kidnapped and are now hostages must be returned safely to their families.
I think we can agree about that, but we may disagree on a few points.
Premier Doug Ford said on Monday night, after the death toll in Gaza had surpassed 34,000 - the majority women and children - and some 1.7 million people displaced, that "people are fed up."
My God, man, how nice it is to be "fed up" with genocide and to encourage people to "move on" because we're safe in our homes.
Premier Ford said we live in "peaceful Ontario" and I agree and I'm glad.
I'm glad that I'm not being bombed and there are safe places for me and my family. I'm glad that my children aren't being starved because a government is withholding food as punishment. I'm glad that I'm not living in a war zone.
I think we can agree about this, but that's as far as my agreement can go.
This desire for order and peace above justice that will end a war is a problem.
The fight for justice is messy. It means that we, those of us who are blessed enough to not be in a war zone, must make others uncomfortable. This fight often means getting in people's face - never with hate speech or threats - and refusing to let anyone get complacent.
Because if there is no justice, it is impossible to have real peace.
This idea that we need to ensure order over justice is what the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King spoke about in his 1963 "Letters from a Birmingham Jail." And, just like today, too many people speak about the necessity of peace and order instead of the necessity of justice for all.
The Rev. Dr. King wrote: "I have almost reached the regrettable conclusion that the Negro's great stumbling block in his stride toward freedom is not the White Citizen's Counciler or the Ku Klux Klanner, but the white moderate, who is more devoted to 'order' than to justice; who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice; who constantly says: 'I agree with you in the goal you seek, but I cannot agree with your methods of direct action'; who paternalistically believes he can set the timetable for another man's freedom; who lives by a mythical concept of time and who constantly advises the Negro to wait for a 'more convenient season.' Shallow understanding from people of good will is more frustrating than absolute misunderstanding from people of ill will. Lukewarm acceptance is much more bewildering than outright rejection."
I am bewildered as well when people talk of peace but only want convenience. They say they want peace, but they don't like how or when you protest. Go over there in that corner and quietly protest and complain, but don't get in my way because that doesn't work for me.
Peace and order above justice.
How many more people must die for us to realize that order is only useful when it means justice for everyone? If disorder and discomfort on campus means justice for all, so be it.
Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator, and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Pro-Palestinian protesters make camp at the University of Toronto last week. If disorder and discomfort on campus means justice for all, so be it, Shellene Drakes-Tull writes.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Shellene Drakes-Tull OPINION Shellene Drakes-Tull is a Toronto-based storyteller, educator, and the founder of Sweet Lime Communications. She is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.;

Subject: Famine; Displaced persons; Peace; College campuses

Location: Gaza Strip

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310; Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940; Name: Lime Communications; NAICS: 541810

Title: Without justice there is no peace

Document 379 of 500

CAPTION: A small tent city has popped up on the front campus of the University of Toronto around King's College Circle in front of University College last week.

Title: I am bewildered when people talk of peace but only want convenience

Document 380 of 500

Health Canada waiting on details from Toronto: City officials say request is still in play but agency says application is incomplete

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-canada-waiting-on-details-toronto/docview/3051830758/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+Canada+waiting+on+details+from+Toronto%3A+City+officials+say+request+is+still+in+play+but+agency+says+application+is+incomplete&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health Canada sent back Toronto's application for a drug decriminalization program more than a year ago with a list of questions and has yet to receive a reply, the Star has learned, but city officials insist their request is still in play.
Whether it could ever pass muster with Ottawa remains in limbo as the federal government announced Tuesday it is scaling back Canada's lone pilot project that allows people to possess and use small amounts of otherwise-illegal hard drugs without fear of criminal charges.
Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks wouldn't say Tuesday whether her decision to grant British Columbia's request for a change to its exemption under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act would have any bearing on Toronto's application - in part because it has yet to reach her desk.
But after days of questions about why that is, officials have now told the Star that Health Canada sent the application back to Toronto Public Health seeking more information - and no one has replied.
"The request from Toronto Public Health was received more than two years ago and remains incomplete," Saks's office told the Star.
"Health Canada has not received anything for consideration in response to questions sent last year to determine whether the application adequately addressed the dual objectives of public health and public safety."
Saks's office declined to provide further details about the concerns Health Canada had expressed.
Questions about the status of Toronto's 2022 application for an exemption resurfaced over the last several days in the wake of B.C.'s decision to ask Ottawa for changes to its program.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Saks had suggested that Toronto's request would not be approved without buy-in from the Ontario government - and Premier Doug Ford has made it clear he does not support the bid.
But Trudeau and Saks also demurred when asked why Toronto's request had yet to be approved or denied, creating confusion as to the exact status of the application.
In a statement, Toronto Public Health spokesperson Dane Griffiths would only say "ongoing discussions" continue with Health Canada.
"TPH's role is to provide decision-makers with the best possible advice, supported by the best possible evidence, regarding this issue," Griffiths said, adding that Toronto's approach is consistent with the latest recommendations from both the top public health officials for Canada and Ontario.
"Decriminalization is fundamentally about recognizing that addiction is a health issue which, in the city of Toronto alone, causes hundreds of preventable deaths every year," Griffiths said.
While Ford has indicated he would not support the application, Griffiths said Toronto Public Health "has not received a formal indication from the province that it does not support the request currently under review by Health Canada."
Toronto Public Health first called for drug decriminalization for personal use in 2018, and submitted its formal application in January 2022 - a move backed by the city's police chief and leading hospitals.
The application was part of what city officials called a "made-in-Toronto" model to address the deadly toxic drugs crisis, and the added pressures created by arresting and charging people for the use of small amounts of illegal drugs.
By the end of 2022, more than 500 people in Toronto had died after using toxic drugs, and paramedics were responding to hundreds of calls about non-fatal overdoses each month.
Toronto's application was expanded in 2023 to, among other things, have the request for decriminalization apply to all public places except for primary and secondary schools, airports and child-care facilities. Toronto has also asked for the exemption to cover young people, and to be extended to all drugs.
The request makes Toronto's bid broader than British Columbia's, which had only asked for its approach to apply to adults, and only for some specific drugs.
Criminal charges also continued to apply in B.C. if drugs were used on school property or at child-care facilities, an approach B.C. modified last fall by asking for protections for other areas intended for use by young people.
The change approved to B.C.'s plan Tuesday will again make it illegal to use drugs in all public spaces.
B.C.'s request was prompted by mounting concerns of police and others - including many who support decriminalization - that allowing the use of illegal drugs in public was causing too much disorder.
Mayor Olivia Chow expressed frustration over the handling of Toronto's application, saying whenever she's raised the issue with the federal government, she's been told to talk to the provincial government, which in turn says the focus should be on drug treatment. Chow said she hasn't seen action on that front, either.
"What the best, ideal scenario is, is for the federal government and provincial government to stop pointing fingers at each other and do a comprehensive treatment program," she said.
CREDIT: Stephanie Levitz Deputy Toronto Star Alyshah Hasham Toronto Star
