Document 381 of 500

When Toronto asked to decriminalize drugs, Health Canada had questions. More than a year later, it's still waiting for answers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/when-toronto-asked-decriminalize-drugs-health/docview/3052197232/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=When+Toronto+asked+to+decriminalize+drugs%2C+Health+Canada+had+questions.+More+than+a+year+later%2C+it%27s+still+waiting+for+answers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Health Canada sent back Toronto's application for a drug decriminalization program more than a year ago with a list of questions and has yet to receive a reply, the Star has learned, but city officials insist their request is still in play.
Whether it could ever pass muster with Ottawa remains in limbo as the federal government announced Tuesday it is scaling back Canada's lone pilot project that allows people to possess and use small amounts of otherwise-illegal hard drugs without fear of criminal charges.
Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks wouldn't say Tuesday whether her decision to grant British Columbia's request for a change to its exemption under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act would have any bearing on Toronto's application - in part because it has yet to reach her desk.
But after days of questions about why that is, officials have now told the Star that Health Canada sent the application back to Toronto Public Health seeking more information - and no one has replied.
"The request from Toronto Public Health was received more than two years ago and remains incomplete," Saks's office told the Star.
"Health Canada has not received anything for consideration in response to questions sent last year to determine whether the application adequately addressed the dual objectives of public health and public safety."
Saks's office declined to provide further details about the concerns Health Canada had expressed.
Questions about the status of Toronto's 2022 application for an exemption resurfaced over the last several days in the wake of B.C.'s decision to ask Ottawa for changes to its program.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Saks had suggested that Toronto's request would not be approved without buy-in from the Ontario government - and Premier Doug Ford has made it clear he does not support the bid.
But Trudeau and Saks also demurred when asked why Toronto's request had yet to be approved or denied, creating confusion as to the exact status of the application.
In a statement, Toronto Public Health spokesperson Dane Griffiths would only say "ongoing discussions" continue with Health Canada.
"TPH's role is to provide decision-makers with the best possible advice, supported by the best possible evidence, regarding this issue," Griffiths said, adding that Toronto's approach is consistent with the latest recommendations from both the top public health officials for Canada and Ontario.
"Decriminalization is fundamentally about recognizing that addiction is a health issue which, in the city of Toronto alone, causes hundreds of preventable deaths every year," Griffiths said.
While Ford has indicated he would not support the application, Griffiths said Toronto Public Health "has not received a formal indication from the province that it does not support the request currently under review by Health Canada."
Toronto Public Health first called for drug decriminalization for personal use in 2018, and submitted its formal application in January 2022 - a move backed by the city's police chief and leading hospitals.
The application was part of what city officials called a "made-in-Toronto" model to address the deadly toxic drugs crisis, and the added pressures created by arresting and charging people for the use of small amounts of illegal drugs.
By the end of 2022, more than 500 people in Toronto had died after using toxic drugs, and paramedics were responding to hundreds of calls about non-fatal overdoses each month.
Toronto's application was expanded in 2023 to, among other things, have the request for decriminalization apply to all public places except for primary and secondary schools, airports and child-care facilities. Toronto has also asked for the exemption to cover young people, and to be extended to all drugs.
The request makes Toronto's bid broader than British Columbia's, which had only asked for its approach to apply to adults, and only for some specific drugs.
Criminal charges also continued to apply in B.C. if drugs were used on school property or at child-care facilities, an approach B.C. modified last fall by asking for protections for other areas intended for use by young people.
The change approved to B.C.'s plan Tuesday will again make it illegal to use drugs in all public spaces.
B.C.'s request was prompted by mounting concerns of police and others - including many who support decriminalization - that allowing the use of illegal drugs in public was causing too much disorder.
Opposition politicians in B.C. and on Parliament Hill had turned the issue into a political pressure point, accusing the provincial and federal governments of being responsible for skyrocketing rates of opioid deaths.
The federal Conservatives held fast to that line Tuesday, urging the Liberal government to reject Toronto's application and admit the policy is a failure.
"Canadians deserve to know what Trudeau is going to do next and whether he will impose his extreme policy elsewhere," the party said in a statement.
Saks pushed back against that narrative.
"Decriminalization is about ensuring that people don't have to face stigma and actually go to get help," she said.
"I really am frustrated that the opposition has made this a dehumanizing debate for those who actually need our help, and that they don't recognize we need a full suite of tools to actually save lives."
Mayor Olivia Chow expressed frustration over the handling of Toronto's application, saying whenever she's raised the issue with the federal government, she's been told to talk to the provincial government, which in turn says the focus should be on drug treatment. Chow said she hasn't seen action on that front, either.
"What the best, ideal scenario is, is for the federal government and provincial government to stop pointing fingers at each other and do a comprehensive treatment program," she said.
Chow said the services and supports that are currently needed alongside decriminalization are piecemeal and inadequate, as is access to stable housing.
The focus on decriminalization "masks the real need, the discussion of where are the drug treatment programs," she said.
CAPTION: Mental Health and Addictions Minister Ya'ara Saks speaks at a news conference in Ottawa on April 9, 2024.

Ottawa to send money to cities, bypassing PCs: Federal government says province is no longer willing to co-operate on deal for new homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-send-money-cities-bypassing-pcs/docview/3051314011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+to+send+money+to+cities%2C+bypassing+PCs%3A+Federal+government+says+province+is+no+longer+willing+to+co-operate+on+deal+for+new+homes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government is going over Premier Doug Ford's head and promising to send $357 million in affordable housing funding directly to regions and municipalities, arguing Ontario has failed to show how it plans to meet the targets set out by the federal government.
It means Ottawa will now decide how that money is dished out, and the Ontario government won't get expected reimbursements from the federal government for money already sent to municipalities for affordable housing.
It's the latest in a squabble between Ottawa and Queen's Park after the federal government warned Ontario in March it could miss out on the funding used to reimburse municipalities for affordable housing due to its failure to meet the target of 19,660 new affordable homes by 2028.
Ontario contended it was being treated unfairly because repairs to existing affordable units have come at the expense of building new ones as the province's housing stock is the oldest in the country. It also argued the threat puts the province's most vulnerable in jeopardy.
Ottawa had already given Ontario an extension from a September 2023 deadline to show how it will meet the targets, but the proposed plan did not satisfy the Trudeau government's demands. In a scathing letter in March, federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser provided conditional approval to Ontario's plan, but gave Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra until September 2024 to show how the province will meet the targets.
But in another letter last week, Fraser argued the province is no longer willing to co-operate and the federal government will go to municipalities directly as a result.
"The federal government has made every effort to reach an agreement with Ontario," Fraser said in his letter to Calandra. "I am disappointed that through your rejection of the conditional approval you have decided to forego the federal funding that would reimburse Ontario for investments it makes under our agreement."
During question period in Queen's Park on Monday, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles accused the Ford government of "abandoning its responsibility to fund and deliver new affordable homes in this province."
Calandra pushed back, downplaying Ottawa's decision and arguing it was what the province had called for all along: the distribution of affordable housing funding through service managers in Ontario - the administrators responsible for funding and the administration of all social housing.
"For weeks, we've been saying, 'It is distributed through our service managers.' Now, the big, bad federal minister of housing is going to punish Ontario. Do you know how? By distributing the money the same way we have done it for the last 35 years: through our service managers," he said.
But the move means Ottawa is once again bypassing a provincial government when it comes to housing. Ottawa is also increasingly attaching conditions to funding to the provinces in a bid to loosen restrictions and increase supply, facing backlash from premiers across the country.
But no other province or territory is falling behind as much as Ontario on its affordable housing targets, prompting this action, Fraser charged. In his letter to Calandra in March, he said the province's improved plan showed it was on track to achieve only 28 per cent of its plan by 2025, compared to 66 per cent on average by other provincial and territorial governments in Canada. And in response to Ontario's claims its repairs were taking priority, Fraser said other provinces have had no trouble meeting targets for both repairs and new units.
With files from Victoria Gibson and Kristin Rushowy
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star With files from Victoria Gibson and Kristin Rushowy

Ford says schools should clear out protesters: Premier points to safety concerns raised by parents

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-says-schools-should-clear-out-protesters/docview/3051313518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+says+schools+should+clear+out+protesters%3A+Premier+points+to+safety+concerns+raised+by+parents&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says it's time for universities to clear out pro-Palestinian encampments, saying parents are concerned if campuses are safe for students and that in general, "people are fed up" with the protests.
Schools "need to move them on," he told reporters at Queen's Park on Monday when asked about the ongoing protests, including one at the University of Toronto, whose sprawling downtown campus surrounds the Ontario legislature.
"I'm getting messages from parents, and not just parents from the Jewish community - parents as a whole. Are my kids safe at university? Are they going to be harassed, are they going to be bullied? It's unacceptable," the premier said.
"We all need to work together. You want to protest, protest - but protest peacefully. No hate speech of any type for any culture, not just one."
Ford also said he "can't stand if there's hate speech. I can't stand some of the nasty stuff I've been seeing out there I can tell you - I talk to more people than anyone in this province ... they're up to here with this stuff."
The protests are the latest events during the seven-month war in the Middle East that began with an attack by Hamas last Oct. 7. The protests have sparked fierce debate over the lines between free speech and intimidation, and over Canadian connections to Israel during a conflict that has seen thousands killed and a humanitarian disaster emerging in the Gaza Strip.
While demonstrators say they are protesting the actions of a foreign government, critics say those actions have repeatedly spilled over into antisemitism here at home.
The tents at U of T's downtown St. George campus were set up last week, and protesters are demanding the school cut ties with Israel and divest from Israeli companies. The University of Ottawa is among others to have been targeted by protesters.
U of T said Monday that some progress has been made.
"Our goal remains the same: to find a peaceful conclusion to the unauthorized encampment as soon as possible, without the risk of violent confrontation or physical injury. Members of the U of T administration met in good faith with U of T student representatives of the individuals in the encampment over the weekend," it said in a written statement from vice-provost of students Sandy Welsh, and Kelly Hannah-Moffat, vice-president of people strategy, equity and culture. "Our discussions have been constructive, and we see a way forward."
With summer school now underway, and convocations on the horizon, the school said it is "working with these student representatives to address our concerns about health and safety, sanitation, emergency and public access, and language that is threatening, hateful and discriminatory."
"We remain concerned about large numbers of the broader public coming to campus for rallies and marches. There have been several incidents of particular concern, including reported assaults and hate speech" and police have been notified, U of T said in its written statement posted online.
Mohammad Yassin, a University of Toronto student and one of the encampment's spokespeople, told The Canadian Press that allegations of hate or violence aren't true.
"People are walking around with their pets and their children," he said. "There are people drawing art and reading books and sitting down in prayer circles, and that's exactly what the space is supposed to be."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 384 of 500

Banning the kaffiyeh was the wrong call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/banning-kaffiyeh-was-wrong-call/docview/3051313514/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Banning+the+kaffiyeh+was+the+wrong+call&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The kaffiyeh remains under wraps in the Ontario legislature until further notice.
The traditional Arab headdress, popularized by the late Yasser Arafat during his struggle for a Palestinian homeland - and embraced by many protesters during the latest Gaza-Israel conflict - has been banned at Queen's Park since last month. But the kerfuffle over the kaffiyeh keeps evolving day by day.
Now, freewheeling MPPs are tying themselves in knots over the scarf, redolent in symbolism.
It is a reflexive impulse among politicians to be all things to all people. But this square piece of cloth has become a Rorschach blotter test that means different things to different people(s) at different times.
Amid student-led protest on the University of Toronto campus, demarcated by tents and tumult, the tempest at neighbouring Queen's Park has been largely overshadowed. Amid the pathos of student protesters, the posturing of politicians can seem anticlimactic.
Three weeks after imposing a ban throughout the legislative building (or precincts), Speaker Ted Arnott announced Monday that, upon reflection, he was narrowing - "clarifying" - the ban to the legislative chamber alone. Now, all are free to wear the kaffiyeh in offices and hallways, but MPPs cannot don them during debates on the floor of the house.
You could hear a pin drop after Arnott delivered his speech, every MPP hanging on his every word. But the compromise didn't satisfy two New Democrats and an independent MPP, who pointedly revealed their scarves after Arnott's ruling, daring him to act.
Two MPPs were promptly expelled (New Democrat Kristyn Wong-Tam, who sported a more modern interpretation of the traditional chequered kaffiyeh, went unnoticed but left unprompted). Whereupon all three unburdened themselves about the ban to the waiting media.
What does this all mean for Ontario?
Queen's Park is a place of rituals and rules, traditions and trade-offs. Understandably, the Speaker's rulings can be hard to understand when there's a moving target.
When is a scarf not a scarf?
It's complicated. As Arnott struggled to explain, parliaments around the world have a long tradition of prohibiting protests and props (including flags or printed slogans) - because the rules specify that debates are meant to be verbal, not visual.
Just as you can't wear a T-shirt demanding the release of Israeli hostages held by Hamas (banned even at MLSE sporting events), you can't wear a kaffiyeh that bespeaks solidarity with Palestinians. The Speaker stressed that he had researched his ruling carefully, and that precedent was on his side.
The NDP's Marit Stiles, who is leader of the opposition, countered that "the kaffiyeh is a traditional clothing item that is significant not only to (Palestinians) but to many members of Arab and Muslim communities." Not so simple.
Arnott pointed out that he'd permitted the kaffiyeh in the past, when few bothered to wear it. Now that so many were suddenly embracing the scarf in co-ordinated bursts, it had crossed the line into symbolic protests. Put another way, if it walks and talks like a political protest, it's a protest. When so many people of all backgrounds suddenly don the Palestinian kaffiyeh, it's no longer merely cultural or sartorial but political.
Yet even if the Speaker was speaking the truth - and Stiles was surely straining credulity by claiming the kaffiyeh isn't political at this point - Arnott made the wrong call. Technically, he's right, but practically his ruling was unenforceable and unsupportable.
Which is why no party leader supported him last month - not just Stiles but her Green, Liberal and Progressive Conservative counterparts asked him to reconsider. Yes, even Premier Doug Ford, mindful of a hard-fought byelection last week with many Muslim voters, echoed the NDP's call.
The Speaker reminded them all that he is merely their servant, and that they are free to overrule him. But when MPPs were asked to give unanimous consent to permit the kaffiyeh, a number of Tories demurred, leading to the present standoff.
But the Tories, no longer worried about byelections (they won both last week), are belatedly rallying to the Speaker's position. PC House Leader Paul Calandra said he is now onside with Arnott, and the premier - choosing his words carefully - implied Monday that he too supports the latest compromise.
At a time of increasingly poisonous partisan misconduct, where even the Speaker's rulings are routinely ignored (not least by the three MPPs Monday), it is hard to believe that banning the kaffiyeh is required to protect the delicacy of parliamentary decorum. Men no longer need ties in the house, why make a fuss over the kaffiyeh?
After all, when Arafat spoke at the General Assembly of the United Nations - the ultimate parliamentary deliberative body - he wore his headdress. When several New Democrat MPs wore the scarf in the House of Commons earlier this year, the Speaker turned a blind eye.
And when an Israeli politician complained about a Palestinian-Israeli parliamentarian daring to don the kaffiyeh while addressing the Knesset a few years ago, the speaker shot it down, saying: "We wear yarmulkes, he can come with a kaffiyeh."
Live and let live.
CAPTION: MPPs Sarah Jama, left, and Joel Harden were expelled from the legislative chamber for wearing the kaffiyeh on Monday, and Kristyn Wong-Tam, right, followed them out. Technically, Speaker Ted Arnott is right in his rationale for the ban, but practically his ruling was unenforceable and unsupportable, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Richard Lautens Toronto Star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 385 of 500

Speaker clarifies kaffiyeh ban: MPPs asked to leave chamber for wearing scarves

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/speaker-clarifies-kaffiyeh-ban/docview/3051312626/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Speaker+clarifies+kaffiyeh+ban%3A+MPPs+asked+to+leave+chamber+for+wearing+scarves&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Speaker Ted Arnott says the kaffiyeh can be worn in the Ontario legislative building - but not in the chamber itself.
Shortly after his remarks during Monday's question period - which he said were to clarify his previous ruling that banned the black-and-white scarves at Queen's Park - two MPPs were ordered to leave the chamber for donning them, while a third left in solidarity.
"Since the issue has become so controversial, I think it necessary to point out that there is not a blanket ban which singles out only the kaffiyeh for those who wish to wear it and enter the legislative precinct," Arnott said in the legislature before the three MPPs left the chamber.
"It has been our standard practice, again for many decades, to ask those who seek to enter the assembly not to wear any attire which appears to be intended to make a political statement of any sort. This is intended to promote order and decorum and mostly has had the desired effect through the years," he added.
"But in this case, which unfortunately became politicized, it has instead fostered division and discord, both in this House and in our communities in the province. As Speaker, my intent has always been to uphold the conventions and principles that were designed to bring us together to debate important issues. Diversity has been and remains one of Ontario's greatest strengths.
"Therefore, I wish to clarify that members, staff and visitors wearing the kaffiyeh will be permitted entry to the legislative precinct. However, in line with my previous statements in the House, the kaffiyeh is not permitted to be worn in the chamber or the galleries at this time without the unanimous consent of the House."
New Democrats Kristyn Wong-Tam (Toronto Centre), Joel Harden (Ottawa Centre) and Independent MPP Sarah Jama told reporters afterward that they disagree with the ban.
"As long as the ban is in existence, we will take whatever necessary actions and we will of course do so respectfully and peacefully to ensure that this unjust ban is reversed," said Wong-Tam.
"If this was a fair ban, if this was a just rule, we would not be here."
Following a complaint, Arnott said he had ruled the kaffiyeh was a political symbol and therefore not allowed to be worn, given long-standing legislative rules.
Almost two weeks ago, Jama refused to leave the legislature while wearing a kaffiyeh.
She said she left when asked on Monday because of the solidarity shown by the other two MPPs.
Kaffiyehs have a history dating back thousands of years, and have become a common sight at pro-Palestinian rallies since the Oct. 7 attack against Israel by Hamas and the subsequent retaliation by the Israeli military in Gaza.
The NDP has twice sought, unsuccessfully, to have the ban overturned, which would require unanimous consent from MPPs.
Even though overturning the ban has the support of all party leaders, including Premier Doug Ford, a handful of Progressive Conservative MPPs have refused to go along with the unanimous vote needed to overrule Arnott.
The Liberals and NDP are asking for a recorded vote on allowing the kaffiyeh back in the legislative chamber.
Ford has said he doesn't support the ban, but on Monday told reporters that "the Speaker was pretty clear. This is the legislature; I have to respect what the Speaker says."
House Leader Paul Calandra said he, too, has confidence in the Speaker, who has "the right and responsibility to organize his affairs the way he sees fit ... this is a Speaker's ruling, and that's what he is charged to do. That's why we have him in the chair."
Calandra said he was glad Arnott "made that distinction today" about where the kaffiyeh can be worn at Queen's Park. "I think that was certainly important," he said.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called the move "a step in the right direction. It's a good day for people wanting to wear their cultural attire here in the legislative assembly."
Stiles said she's "hopeful that we can overturn the full ban, and we will be looking at all the options."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Trudeau government bypasses Doug Ford, sending housing money straight to Ontario municipalities

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-bypasses-doug-ford-sending/docview/3051665256/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+bypasses+Doug+Ford%2C+sending+housing+money+straight+to+Ontario+municipalities&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-The federal government is going over Premier Doug Ford's head and promising to send $357-million in affordable housing funding directly to regions and municipalities, arguing Ontario has failed to show how it plans to meet the targets set out by the federal government.
It means Ottawa will now decide how that money is dished out, and the Ontario government won't get expected reimbursements from the federal government for money already sent to municipalities for affordable housing.
It's the latest in a squabble between Ottawa and Queen's Park after the federal government warned Ontario in March it could miss out on the funding used to reimburse municipalities for affordable housing due to its failure to meet the target of 19,660 new affordable homes by 2028.
Ontario contended it was being treated unfairly because repairs to existing affordable units have come at the expense of building new ones as Ontario's housing stock is the oldest in the country. The province also argued the threat puts the province's most vulnerable in jeopardy.
Ottawa had already given the province an extension from a September 2023 deadline to show how it will meet the targets, but the proposed plan did not satisfy the Trudeau government's demands. In a scathing letter in March, federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser provided conditional approval to Ontario's plan, but gave Ontario Housing Minister Paul Calandra until September 2024 to show how the province will meet the targets.
But in another letter to Calandra last week, Fraser argued the province is no longer willing to co-operate and the federal government will go to municipalities directly as a result.
"The federal government has made every effort to reach an agreement with Ontario," Fraser said in his letter to Calandra. "I am disappointed that through your rejection of the conditional approval you have decided to forego the federal funding that would reimburse Ontario for investments it makes under our agreement."
During question period in Queen's Park on Monday, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles accused the Ford government of "abandoning its responsibility to fund and deliver new affordable homes in this province."
Calandra pushed back, downplaying Ottawa's decision and arguing it was what the province had called for all along: the distribution of affordable housing funding through service managers in Ontario -the administrators responsible for funding and administration of all social housing.
"For weeks, we've been saying, 'It is distributed through our service managers.' Now, the big, bad federal minister of housing is going to punish Ontario. Do you know how? By distributing the money the same way we have done it for the last 35 years: through our service managers," he said.
"So I say, 'Thank you very much for listening to the province of Ontario. Thank you for continuing to distribute the money the way we have done it for 35 years.'"
But the move means Ottawa is once again bypassing a provincial government when it comes to housing. Ottawa is also increasingly attaching conditions to funding to the provinces in a bid to loosen restrictions and increase supply, facing backlash from premiers across the country.
But no other province or territory is falling behind as much as Ontario on its affordable housing targets, prompting this action, Fraser charged. In his letter to Calandra in March, he said the province's improved plan showed it was on track to achieve only 28 per cent of its plan by 2025, compared to 66 per cent on average by other provincial and territorial governments in Canada. And in response to Ontario's claims its repairs were taking priority, Fraser said other provinces have had no trouble meeting targets for both repairs and building new units.
"Although Ontario has exceeded the minimum repair target, this does not exempt Ontario from the other foundational commitment of creating new affordable housing," he said.
In a letter to the 47 service managers in Ontario, Fraser said this should not impact reimbursements to municipalities.
But this funding dispute has been felt already at the local level.
As of mid-April, Toronto city hall's head of homelessness initiatives had noted the city was still awaiting its latest batch of funding for a rental subsidy program called the Canada-Ontario Housing Benefit, which has been used to move people from crowded shelters and off city streets.
While last year's funding term had ended, this year's funding had not yet materialized amid the conflict between the two governments - and it's unclear whether this latest development will delay those payments further.
Fraser, speaking with the Star at the time, suggested the problem would be solved imminently, but declined to provide a specific date for when the money would flow.
"We are going to look for ways to deliver that money directly to the organizations or people who are relying on it," he said. "This is not going to sit on anyone's desks for months. This is going to be dealt with very, very soon."
With files from Victoria Gibson and Kristin Rushowy.
CAPTION: Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser speaks during a press conference at the National Press Theatre in Ottawa on April 30, 2024.

'People are fed up:' Doug Ford says it's time for universities to clear out pro-Palestinian encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/people-are-fed-up-doug-ford-says-time/docview/3051665249/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27People+are+fed+up%3A%27+Doug+Ford+says+it%27s+time+for+universities+to+clear+out+pro-Palestinian+encampments&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says it's time for universities to clear out pro-Palestinian encampments, saying parents are concerned if campuses are safe for students and that in general, "people are fed up" with the protests.
Schools "need to move them on," he told reporters at Queen's Park on Monday when asked about the ongoing protests, including one at the University of Toronto, whose sprawling downtown campus surrounds the Ontario legislature.
"I'm getting messages from parents, and not just parents from the Jewish community - parents as a whole. Are my kids safe at university? Are they going to be harassed, are they going to be bullied? It's unacceptable," the premier said.
"We all need to work together. You want to protest, protest - but protest peacefully. No hate speech of any type for any culture, not just one."
Ford also said he "can't stand if there's hate speech. I can't stand some of the nasty stuff I've been seeing out there I can tell you - I talk to more people than anyone in this province ... they're up to here with this stuff."
The protests are the latest events during the seven-month war in the Middle East that began with an attack by Hamas last Oct. 7. The protests have sparked fierce debate over the lines between free speech and intimidation, and over Canadian connections to Israel during a conflict that has seen thousands killed and a humanitarian disaster emerging in the Gaza Strip.
While demonstrators say they are protesting the actions of a foreign government, critics say those actions have repeatedly spilled over into antisemitism here at home.
The tents at U of T's downtown St. George campus were set up last week, and protesters are demanding the school cut ties with Israel and divest from Israeli companies. The University of Ottawa is among others to have been targeted by protesters.
U of T said Monday that some progress has been made.
"Our goal remains the same: to find a peaceful conclusion to the unauthorized encampment as soon as possible, without the risk of violent confrontation or physical injury. Members of the U of T administration met in good faith with U of T student representatives of the individuals in the encampment over the weekend," it said in a written statement from vice-provost of students Sandy Welsh, and Kelly Hannah-Moffat, vice-president of people strategy, equity and culture.
"Our discussions have been constructive, and we see a way forward."
With summer school now under way, and convocations on the horizon, the school said it is "working with these student representatives to address our concerns about health and safety, sanitation, emergency and public access, and language that is threatening, hateful and discriminatory.
"We remain concerned about large numbers of the broader public coming to campus for rallies and marches. There have been several incidents of particular concern, including reported assaults and hate speech. It is not clear how many of these involve individuals inside the encampment or members of the public" and police have been notified, U of T said in its written statement posted online.
Mohammad Yassin, a University of Toronto student and one of the encampment's spokespeople, told The Canadian Press that allegations of hate or violence aren't true.
"People are walking around with their pets and their children," he said. "There are people drawing art and reading books and sitting down in prayer circles, and that's exactly what the space is supposed to be."
At McGill, the ongoing encampment has prompted Quebec Premier François Legault to say police need to be called in.
In the U.S., hundreds of demonstrators on a number of campuses have been arrested.
Ford told reporters that "people are fed up with this."
"We live in Ontario, peaceful Ontario - we all need to get along," he said.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford talks to reporters at Queens Park following question period on May 6 2024.

Document 388 of 500

Israel and the UN have allowed the kaffiyeh. Why does Queen's Park need to ban it?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/israel-un-have-allowed-kaffiyeh-why-does-queens/docview/3051664977/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Israel+and+the+UN+have+allowed+the+kaffiyeh.+Why+does+Queen%27s+Park+need+to+ban+it%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The kaffiyeh remains under wraps in the Ontario legislature until further notice.
The traditional Arab headdress, popularized by the late Yasser Arafat during his struggle for a Palestinian homeland - and embraced by many protesters during the latest Gaza-Israel conflict - has been banned at Queen's Park since last month. But the kerfuffle over the kaffiyeh keeps evolving day by day.
Now, freewheeling MPPs are tying themselves in knots over the scarf, redolent in symbolism.
It is a reflexive impulse among politicians to be all things to all people. But this square piece of cloth has become a Rorschach blotter test that means different things to different people(s) at different times.
Amid student-led protest on the University of Toronto campus, demarcated by tents and tumult, the tempest at neighbouring Queen's Park has been largely overshadowed. Amid the pathos of student protesters, the posturing of politicians can seem anticlimactic.
Three weeks after imposing a ban throughout the legislative building (or precincts), Speaker Ted Arnott announced Monday that, upon reflection, he was narrowing - "clarifying" - the ban to the legislative chamber alone. Now, all are free to wear the kaffiyeh in offices and hallways, but MPPs cannot don them during debates on the floor of the house.
You could hear a pin drop after Arnott delivered his speech, every MPP hanging on his every word. But the compromise didn't satisfy two New Democrats and an independent MPP, who pointedly revealed their scarves after Arnott's ruling, daring him to act.
Two MPPs were promptly expelled (New Democrat Kristyn Wong-Tam, who sported a more modern interpretation of the traditional chequered kaffiyeh, went unnoticed but left unprompted). Whereupon all three unburdened themselves about the ban to the waiting media.
What does this all mean for Ontario?
Queen's Park is a place of rituals and rules, traditions and trade-offs. Understandably, the Speaker's rulings can be hard to understand when there's a moving target.
When is a scarf not a scarf?
It's complicated. As Arnott struggled to explain, parliaments around the world have a long tradition of prohibiting protests and props (including flags or printed slogans) - because the rules specify that debates are meant to be verbal, not visual.
Just as you can't wear a T-shirt demanding the release of Israeli hostages held by Hamas (banned even at MLSE sporting events), you can't wear a kaffiyeh that bespeaks solidarity with Palestinians. The Speaker stressed that he had researched his ruling carefully, and that precedent was on his side.
The NDP's Marit Stiles, who is leader of the opposition, countered that "the kaffiyeh is a traditional clothing item that is significant not only to (Palestinians) but to many members of Arab and Muslim communities." Not so simple.
Arnott pointed out that he'd permitted the kaffiyeh in the past, when few bothered to wear it. Now that so many were suddenly embracing the scarf in co-ordinated bursts, it had crossed the line into symbolic protests.
Put another way, if it walks and talks like a political protest, it's a protest. When so many people of all backgrounds suddenly don the Palestinian kaffiyeh, it's no longer merely cultural or sartorial but political.
Yet even if the Speaker was speaking the truth - and Stiles was surely straining credulity by claiming the kaffiyeh isn't political at this point - Arnott made the wrong call. Technically, he's right, but practically his ruling was unenforceable and unsupportable.
Which is why no party leader supported him last month - not just Stiles but her Green, Liberal and Progressive Conservative counterparts asked him to reconsider. Yes, even Premier Doug Ford, mindful of a hard-fought byelection last week with many Muslim voters, echoed the NDP's call.
The Speaker reminded them all that he is merely their servant, and that they are free to overrule him. But when MPPs were asked to give unanimous consent to permit the kaffiyeh, a number of Tories demurred, leading to the present standoff.
But the Tories, no longer worried about byelections (they won both last week), are belatedly rallying to the Speaker's position. PC House Leader Paul Calandra said he is now onside with Arnott, and the premier - choosing his words carefully - implied Monday that he too supports the latest compromise.
At a time of increasingly poisonous partisan misconduct, where even the Speaker's rulings are routinely ignored (not least by the three MPPs Monday), it is hard to believe that banning the kaffiyeh is required to protect the delicacy of parliamentary decorum. Men no longer need ties in the house, why make a fuss over the kaffiyeh?
After all, when Arafat spoke at the General Assembly of the United Nations - the ultimate parliamentary deliberative body - he wore his headdress. When several New Democrat MPs wore the scarf in the House of Commons earlier this year, the Speaker turned a blind eye.
And when an Israeli politician complained about a Palestinian-Israeli parliamentarian daring to don the kaffiyeh while addressing the Knesset a few years ago, the speaker shot it down, saying: "We wear yarmulkes, he can come with a kaffiyeh."
Live and let live.
CAPTION: MPPs Sarah Jama, left, Joel Harden, centre, and Kristyn Wong-Tam speak to reporters at the legislature on May 6 2024.

Location: Israel

Title: Israel and the UN have allowed the kaffiyeh. Why does Queen's Park need to ban it?

Document 389 of 500

Outrage in Ontario is the highest in the country, latest 'Rage Index' poll finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/outrage-ontario-is-highest-country-latest-rage/docview/3050988408/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Outrage+in+Ontario+is+the+highest+in+the+country%2C+latest+%27Rage+Index%27+poll+finds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's not just in our flag, Canadians across the country are seeing red.
Outrage throughout Canada, and in Ontario especially, is spiking according to the latest "Rage Index" from Pollara.
The poll measures anger and annoyance towards six factors: the federal government, the provincial government, the Canadian economy, their personal financial situation, the types of changes happening in Canada and the latest stories in the news.
Across the country, outrage is boiling over across those six measures.
The poll was conducted between April 22 and April 26 through an online survey of 1,507 Canadians over the age of 18. The poll has a margin of error of 2.5 per cent and was weighted to be representative of the general Canadian population.
The percentage of Canadians saying they were "very angry" was up four points since Pollara's last questionnaire in January, and is now the highest it's ever been since pollster Dan Arnold, Pollara's chief strategy officer, starting tracking the measure.
The new-found levels of fury across the country comes as affordability remains top of mind for Canadians who have been stretched thin with high rents, hefty grocery bills and high interest rates.
Ontarians are, generally, very outraged
Ontario has overthrown Alberta, taking the crown for the most contemptuous.
Usually, Arnold said, Ontarians trail Albertans for the most annoyed or angry, but that's changed.
"We're seeing a lot of anger among Ontarians, and it's directed in a lot of different directions," Arnold said.
Those surveyed in Ontario were most likely to have negative emotions towards both the federal and provincial governments.
"I don't think this is a partisan issue," Arnold said, adding that it's more likely economic frustration boiling over in all directions, with 70 per cent of Ontarians expressing frustration with the economy.
"A lot of the pressure is around inflation, around housing, hitting people in Ontario especially hard," he added, "and actually spilling over into a lot of different outlets."
B.C. goes berserk over its provincial government
Ontarians aren't the only ones outraged, our Western neighbours are as well.
In B.C., Arnold said, anger and frustration has jumped to record highs, particularly aimed at the provincial government.
"That's the first time in two years of tracking that we've seen the majority of British Columbians with negative emotions towards their provincial government," he said.
"(B.C. Premier David Eby) might be starting to slip a little bit from what looked like a cakewalk to a much more difficult reelection this year."
Eby will face a provincial election in October for the first time as head of the B.C. NDP. He took over for former premier John Horgan in 2022.
Federal budget deficit drew the most outrage
Arnold's latest poll also found that the majority of Canadians, more than two-thirds, either haven't heard about the latest federal budget or aren't familiar with its details.
Feelings toward the budget's new deficit calculations drew the most outrage, followed by anger toward the capital gains tax and building out Canada's artificial intelligence infrastructure.
In Ontario, the mental health care measures within the budget, new school food programs and new contraceptive programs were the most popular.
But even with popular measures, there's still going to outrage directed at the government, Arnold said.
"I think what's driving most of the negative feelings toward the federal government, and the provincial government to some extent, are things like high interest rates, high cost of housing, high cost at the grocery store."
"Until there's a change there," Arnold explained, "anything else the government does will really only have a marginal impact."
CAPTION: Economic frustrations have boiled over into outrage towards the federal and provincial government, Pollara's chief strategy officer told the Star.

Subject: Anger; Polls & surveys; Federal budget; Canadians; Interest rates; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: Federal budget Interest rates Budget deficits

Document 390 of 500

B.C. rethinks its policy on public drug use. Toronto should heed its lessons

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/b-c-rethinks-policy-on-public-drug-use-toronto/docview/3050988109/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=B.C.+rethinks+its+policy+on+public+drug+use.+Toronto+should+heed+its+lessons&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A global criminal market with an annual value of more than half a trillion dollars. Hundreds or thousands of people dead from gang wars and overdoses involving contaminated drugs, and millions more infected with HIV, hepatitis and other diseases. A permanent social and economic underclass of people unable to access employment and housing due to criminal convictions.
All of these consequences, it seems, constitute an acceptable state of affairs. On the other hand, an apparent uptick in public use of otherwise illicit drugs, well that's a "nightmare" and a "disaster."
Just take it from Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Federal Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre, both of whom used the word "nightmare" to describe British Columbia's experience with drug decriminalization. Or take it from Saskatchewan Attorney General Bronwyn Eyre, who prefers the term "disaster."
At issue is B.C.'s exemption under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act (CDSA), which decriminalized personal possession of small amounts of opioids, cocaine, methamphetamine and MDMA (ecstasy.) The exemption, a pilot program, took effect on Jan. 31, 2023, and is expected to run for three years.
Now the provincial government is seeking changes to that amendment. While there were concerns about resulting drug use in public spaces, it appears the government's request for an amendment was driven by what was happening in hospitals. The B.C. Nurses Union, which supports decriminalization, expressed concern about hospital nurses being subject to smoke from fentanyl and other dangerous substances. In one week in April, for example, five nurses landed "in emergency after being exposed by one patient."
That should come as no surprise, since people with addictions don't suddenly lose their cravings once they cross the threshold of the emergency room. But the threat to hospital staff is a legitimate concern, and the B.C. government responded by introducing an array of supports for hospital patients with substance use disorders.
The province also announced that a task force will investigate illicit drug use in hospitals, and in November, it passed a law prohibiting the public use of illicit drugs. But that law is now subject to a court challenge, and the courts enjoined the province from enforcing the law while the challenge proceeds.
Aside from hospitals, Vancouver Deputy Police Chief Fiona Wilson told the House of Commons Standing Committee on Health that "there have been several high-profile instances of problematic drug use at public locations, including parks, beaches and around public transit."
As a result, B.C. Premier David Eby has asked the federal government to amend its exemption under the CDSA to recriminalize drug use in public spaces, including hospitals, parks, beaches and on public transit, but not including drug consumption and overdose sites and among those legally sheltering.
And that, some of our political leaders would have you believe, means decriminalization has been a nightmare or a disaster.
In the real world, B.C.'s problems have virtually nothing to do with decriminalization, and virtually everything to do with its difficulties in regulating public spaces. It would, of course, have been wise had B.C. excluded public spaces from its original exemption request, but hindsight is 20/20.
And benefiting from hindsight is the entire purpose of the pilot project. By definition, a pilot project is a small-scale study to assess the effects of a measure before implementing it on a larger scale.
In keeping with that purpose, B.C.'s pilot project is being monitored by both the federal and provincial governments to see if it's achieving its objectives, or if changes are necessary. The evidence gathered will help B.C. - and other jurisdictions, including Toronto, which has applied for a similar exemption - to determine if longer-term decriminalization is advisable, and if so, to decide how best to structure it.
As the pilot project is still in its early stages, evidence is limited, but we have learned that public drug use must be carefully regulated. Beyond that, though, we don't know the effects of decriminalization, which is why the project should - nay, must - continue.
Above all, we must not let the remarks of a few opportunistic politicians persuade us to give up without even really trying, and return to drug prohibition and the cataclysm it has unleashed. That's already a nightmare. And a disaster.
CAPTION: The British Columbia government is asking Health Canada to "urgently change" the decriminalization policy to stop drug use in public. In announcing the change, B.C. Premier David Eby cited"street disorder that makes communities feel unsafe."

Subject: Emergency medical care; Decriminalization; Public spaces; Drug overdose; Public transportation; Ecstasy; Pilot projects; Nurses; Drug use; Contamination

Location: Canada; British Columbia Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/learning-b-c-s-lessons-on-public-drug-use/docview/3050809878/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 392 of 500

Ontario must recognize First Nations bylaws - failure to do so 'will result in harm to our citizens'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-must-recognize-first-nations-bylaws/docview/3050687160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+must+recognize+First+Nations+bylaws+-+failure+to+do+so+%27will+result+in+harm+to+our+citizens%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Lately, our neighbouring communities have been making headlines for all the wrong reasons: overdoses, homelessness and crime. While we have been working diligently to prevent the same crises in our community, Hiawatha First Nation, Premier Doug Ford's directive to police stating that the enforcement of First Nations bylaws is not mandatory has thrown wrench in our plans.
First Nations bylaws play a crucial role in keeping our community's safe by allowing us to address the unique realities we face, drawing from both our traditional natural laws and western laws. These bylaws allow us to keep drug dealers at bay using trespassing laws and to help nondangerous offenders break free from destructive cycles and reintegrate into the community.
We firmly believe Ontario could learn from us about how to deal with issues they're experiencing now but we've seen before.
Instead of collaborating with us for the safety and benefit of our citizens, Ontario is moving forward with the Community Safety and Policing Act (CSPA), which does not mandate the enforcement of First Nations bylaws as a part of "safe and adequate" policing.
This is a change from precedent, where our bylaws were recognized by Ontario and by Canada in the Indian Act, leaves our community in a more vulnerable position at a time when crisis is escalating around us.
We have every reason to believe that the CSPA will result in harm to our citizens. Inquests and inquiries into the deaths of vulnerable First Nations people, including women and girls, repeatedly emphasized that recognizing First Nations jurisdiction is essential for keeping people safe.
Our laws are preventative and focus on the community collaboration for the good of everyone. Relying solely on provincial laws has led us into the very situation that prompted the inquests and inquiries in the first place.
We want to be clear: we have an outstanding relationship with the Ontario Provincial Police. We do not want Ontario to get in the way of that relationship, which is precisely what the CSPA will do. We also fundamentally agree with Premier Ford that community safety is essential and policing needs modernization to address modern challenges. However, the CSPA does not adequately address the need for communities like Hiawatha.
The solution is clear: Ontario can pass a simple regulation under the CSPA recognizing our bylaws. We hope they do because we want our community to make headlines for a different reason: to demonstrate to our neighbours that it is possible to confront the challenges plaguing communities in Ontario and that we have a viable road map.
CAPTION: "First Nations bylaws play a crucial role in keeping our community's safe by allowing us to address the unique realities we face, drawing from both our traditional natural laws and western laws," writes Chief Laurie Carr.
CREDIT: Laurie Carr Contributor

Subject: Native North Americans

Failure to use First Nations bylaws will cause harm

Instead of collaborating with us for the safety and benefit of our citizens, Ontario is moving forward with the Community Safety and Policing Act (CSPA), which does not mandate the enforcement of First Nations bylaws as a part of "safe and adequate" policing.

Relying solely on provincial laws has led us into the very situation that prompted the inquests and inquiries in the first place. Laurie Carr is chief of
Hiawatha First Nation.

Document 394 of 500

Here is what's at stake in campus showdowns across Canada and the U.S.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/here-is-whats-at-stake-campus-showdowns-across/docview/3050544957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here+is+what%27s+at+stake+in+campus+showdowns+across+Canada+and+the+U.S.&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anyone paying attention to the news will notice that university and college campuses are the focus of impassioned debates over freedom of expression.
In the U.S., police have been mobilized in response to student and faculty actions demanding divestment from institutional relationships and funding that support Israel's militarization given the International Criminal Court's investigation of their conduct in Palestine.
Across North America, campus events have been cancelled, students have been suspended and faculty have been censured for speaking about Palestine and the violence of the ongoing Israeli occupation.
One might be tempted to dismiss campus-based protests as a symptom of students' entitlement or naïveté. Others might disregard it as a symptom of incivility, an inability to get along when political differences seem acute.
But what these struggles reveal is how deeply our systems of postsecondary education are embedded with corporate and political interests and how far governments will go to protect them under the illusion of the public good and neutrality.
The cost of political interference in the business of higher education - which is to produce knowledge and uphold a vibrant public sphere - is restrictions on speech. Such censorship always has the effect of privileging certain voices over others.
Political attempts to control the narrative around Palestine/Israel are evident in the ban, instituted by the Speaker of the Ontario Legislative Assembly, on wearing the kaffiyeh in the legislative building, something the Canadian Civil Liberties Association has called a "direct attack on freedom of expression"
Party leaders, including Premier Doug Ford, have called for the speaker to reverse this ban because of its divisive nature. Instead of supporting free expression, Jill Dunlop, the Minister of Colleges and Universities' championing of Bill 166, the Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, suggests a doubling down on attempts to direct how, and what, Ontarians should be allowed to speak about.
Now in its second reading, Bill 166 would empower ministerial directives on the content of antiracism and mental health policies at colleges and universities. This may seem positive - higher education institutions should have these policies. And they do.
The real problem is that the government has underfunded educational institutions to a point that undermines campus-based anti-racism and mental health initiatives.
The Ontario government's strategy with respect to higher education echoes its approach to publicly funded healthcare - underfunding to justify restructuring. Bill 166 is a trojan horse, invoking concerns about student mental health, racism, hate and educational affordability to warrant unprecedented interference in university autonomy.
University autonomy is crucial if a democracy based on public participation, freedom of expression and research innovation is to thrive. For research and education to be credible and sound, they must be free of political interference.
Introducing ministerial directives dictating the content of policies not only opens the door for unprecedented influence in educational affairs, it also means that policies can change whenever there is an election, cabinet shuffle or simply if the minister changes their mind. This fickle approach is not an effective use of public funding.
Further, Bill 166 will redirect resources from existing initiatives to establish new reporting structures and whatever else might be mandated by the minister. Removing support from existing practices grounded in evidence-based research conducted by experts in anti-racism and mental health will not benefit students and could cause harm.
Unlike post-secondary policies, Bill 166 would not require that the minister' directives be guided by research and best practices. Given this government's slashing of funding for public mechanisms mandated to combat racism- the Anti-Racism Directorate and the Ontario Human Rights Commission - we lack confidence in the minister's statements that Bill 166 is about supporting students.
Educational workers and students are rightly concerned about ministerial directives that will undercut university autonomy and interfere in best practices around campus anti-racism and mental health support and policies. Educators who teach, produce knowledge and create a vital public sphere are best positioned to do this work; after all, this is our job.
CAPTION: Law enforcement personnel clash with demonstrators protesting the war in Gaza as they work to remove a non-sanctioned encampment on the campus of UW-Madison in Madison, Wis. on Wednesday.
CREDIT: Eve Haque, Sara Matthews and Trish Salah Contributors

Subject: Higher education; Students; Mental health; Racism; Colleges & universities; Funding; Politics; Public good; College campuses; Censorship

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Location: United States--US; Israel

Title: Here is what's at stake in campus showdowns across Canada and the U.S.

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 4, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Document 395 of 500

Acting like a petulant child paid off for Pierre Poilievre. Canada may not be so lucky

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/acting-like-petulant-child-paid-off-pierre/docview/3050544931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Acting+like+a+petulant+child+paid+off+for+Pierre+Poilievre.+Canada+may+not+be+so+lucky&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was quite a week in Canadian politics.
On Monday, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who polls suggests could win a majority government with more than 200 seats in the next election, suggested he will invoke the notwithstanding clause if he becomes prime minister.
Speaking to the Canadian Police Association, Poilievre said a Conservative government would repeal Bill C-83, which guides the Correctional Service of Canada to use the "least restrictive measures" to ensure the protection of society, staff and offenders. Poilievre pledged multiple murders would serve consecutive sentences, rather than concurrent ones, and "multiple murderers will only ever come out of jail in a box."
"And all of my proposals are constitutional," he told the crowd. "We will make them constitutional, using whatever tools the Constitution allows me to use to make them constitutional. I think you know exactly what I mean. So they will happen and they will stay in place, and I will be the democratically elected prime minister, democratically accountable to the people. And they can then make the judgments themselves on whether they think my laws are constitutional because they will be."
Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which is know as the "notwithstanding clause," allows legislatures to suspend some rights protected by the Constitution. It has never been used by a prime minister.
But it has been used by premiers.
The check against the use of the clause was thought to be the ensuing public outcry that would erupt when political leaders attempted to trampled on Canadians' rights - and particularly those of minorities. In Ontario, a public outcry led by labour groups prompted Premier Doug Ford to reverse course on his pre-emptive use of the clause in back-to-work legislation. Outcry in Quebec, however, was not large enough to make Premier François Legault rethink his use of the clause. His controversial Bill 21, which prevents those wearing religious symbols from holding public-sector jobs such as teachers, police officers or judges, and Bill 96, which tramples on the language rights of the English-speaking minority, sailed through the provincial legislature with majority support.
If Poilievre were to invoke the notwithstanding clause, however, the norms that have prevented its use by a prime minister will have been shattered. Papers will be written questioning the value of the Charter, if federal legislators no longer regard the rights it lays out as sacred.
There would have been much public debate on the question were it not for the Leader of His Majesty's Loyal Opposition calling the Prime Minister of Canada a "wacko."
Poilievre and his caucus spent the better part of the week demanding to know the federal government's response to British Columbia Premier David Eby's request to amend a provincial pilot program, and thereby prevent the public use of decriminalized drugs. They called the Liberal government's acquiescence to B.C.'s original plan "wacko."
Then, Tuesday, Poilievre went too far.
"When will we put an end to this wacko policy by the wacko prime minister?" he asked during question period.
There were audible gasps in the House of Commons, and calls for Speaker Greg Fergus to make Poilievre retract his comments.
"No, that is not acceptable," Fergus said. "There are a couple of things going on here today that are not acceptable."
At that point, Trudeau had already accused Poilievre of "actively courting the support of groups with white nationalist views," and Poilievre had referred to Trudeau as a "practising racist." That comment earned a rebuke from the Speaker, who reminded MPs that making an "accusation directly at the character of a single person is not appropriate."
Poilievre had rephrased his question to talk about the "extremism" of the prime minister and Trudeau had countered that the Conservative leader was showing "spineless leadership."
But no one had ever been called a "wacko" in the Commons before.
When he was asked to withdraw his comment, Poilievre said he would withdraw and replace the term with "extremist." Asked again, he offered "radical." When Fergus asked him to withdraw the remark outright, not replace it, Poilievre countered with "extremist."
The Tory leader was acting like a petulant child trying to exert his dominance.
Fergus, as the Speaker elected by all MPs to enforce the rules of the House, had little choice but to expel him for the day. Not doing so would have set a dangerous precedent.
An hour-and-a-half later, the Conservative party sent out a fundraising note, in which it lied about the reason for Poilievre's expulsion.
The Conservative leader was not expelled for calling the policies of the government "wacko" - a word that has been used nearly 20 times before in the Commons.
He was expelled for calling the prime minister a name and refusing to withdraw his comments.
Planned or improvised, Poilievre's willingness to degrade the debate in chamber surely paid off financially for his party.
But it did not for Canadian democracy.
What a week indeed.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises in response to the Speaker asking him to withdraw language during question period in the House of Commons on Tuesday.

Document 396 of 500

Education is the better solution than a cellphone ban

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/education-is-better-solution-than-cellphone-ban/docview/3050544877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Education+is+the+better+solution+than+a+cellphone+ban&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario reveals details of cellphone ban in schools. Here are the new rules for students, April 28
History shows that prohibition doesn't work. Alcohol, gambling, and marijuana were banned, yet their use continued. Accordingly, prohibiting the use of misnamed cellphones, which are actually powerful computers, in classrooms will likely yield similar results. Depriving students of knowledge tools because they lack the social skills to use them in a classroom only compounds the problem.
Education is the better solution. Smoking was reduced despite great addictive pressure when people realized the danger to their health. Similarly, persuasive evidence justifying the utility of computational devices in class is available. Utilizing advancements in computation, including AI, should be the primary directive of schooling. Furthermore, increasing general knowledge will only be meaningful if it includes social knowledge. Proper respectful behaviour towards others can and should be taught. Teachers are there to ensure that individuals have the right to be educated without disruption from unco-operative students.
Consequently, instead of banning knowledge, innovative devices should be introduced in lower grades under controls that are gradually diminished as students exhibit mastery in higher grades. Teaching students' responsibility and holding them accountable for their choices is how we can make society smarter.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Leave enforcement to the administrators and allow teachers to teach
Doug Ford's school cellphone ban is too easy. There's a bigger education problem he needs to fix, April 30
Education Minister Stephen Lecce says teachers will be responsible for enforcing his new policies. What everyone needs to understand is that teachers, even elementary ones, have no tools to enforce these policies, and in many cases, they get very little if any administrative support. The misbehaving kids know this and take full advantage of it. That is why teachers are so stressed out.
Lecce needs to stop his fanciful political glad-handing and deal with the real issues. Teachers need to be given a clear path that allows them to teach. That's what we pay them to do and that's what our kids need. Our teachers need to be protected, not put on the policy enforcement front line to the point that they require stress leave.
Jeff Osborne, Toronto
It's the students who struggle who get distracted by their phones
Premier Doug Ford weighed in on the proposed cellphone ban saying, "It's not complicated: Don't use the phones." That's reminiscent of Nancy Reagan's advice in 1982 when questioned by a young student about drugs. The first lady responded, "Just say 'no'." So simple. But as Edward Keenan points out, enforcing a ban on mobile phones will prove "nearly impossible." They are very expensive devices, an amazing source of information at one's fingertips, a hand-held encyclopedia. As a very old supply teacher, I understand the concern that such gizmos may interfere with the learning process. The problem is not with the many students who have a work ethic, or academic aspirations. It's those who are struggling in school, or bored, or who just don't realize the importance of focusing on school assignments. So, Education Minister Stephen Lecce's bark better have some bite. But he shouldn't expect classroom teachers to do the dirty work. They have too many things on their plates now. Parents will have to show gumption in this contentious issue. After all, they're usually the ones who purchased the phone, or who are paying a monthly bill.
Garry Burke, Oro-Medonte, ON
Students know how to play with IT, they need to learn how to use it for workEdward Keenan states, "But this generation are digital natives: they were born online, their brains moulded by social media. They don't need to learn how to use it." But that's not quite true. According to the 2006 ECAR study of undergraduates and IT, students arrive at university with good recreational skills with IT, but are less skilled with instrumentally useful technologies. In other words, they are good at playing with IT, but aren't learning how to use it effectively for work or further education. That was almost 20 years ago, and IT use is even more critical today. This is a problem that needs to be addressed and is being avoided.
Donald Philip, Severn, ON
Lack of respect for others is the deeper societal problem
Here's what Ontario students say about Doug Ford's cellphone ban, April 29
The student comment about the ban being a deeper-rooted problem is probably a true statement. The biggest problem is not only in schools, it's everywhere. You see it in the lack of respect for other people, the "no one is going to tell me what to do" attitude. Shoplifting is epidemic because no one stops them. People drive like they are the only car on the roads because they can. Protesters stop people from going about their day, because no one intervenes. Kids vape, smoke, are late for school and dress like they just rolled out of bed because they can. I could go on and on. Bottom line is there is no respect for other people or anyone in authority. When something terrible happens, you hear politicians say "this isn't who we are." Sadly this is exactly who we have become.
Patricia Holland, Oshawa
CAPTION: UNESCO, the United Nations agency for education, science and culture, says that "the mere proximity of a mobile device can be a distraction" for young minds meant to be hitting the books, not their screens.

Subject: Bans; Cellular telephones; Students; Learning; Teachers; Education

Poilievre is acting like a petulant child

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-is-acting-like-petulant-child/docview/3050449460/se-2?accountid=15090
