Document 398 of 500

How Ford schooled rivals in Milton: Premier banked on his candidate's name recognition to win close byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-ford-schooled-rivals-milton/docview/3050449447/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Ford+schooled+rivals+in+Milton%3A+Premier+banked+on+his+candidate%27s+name+recognition+to+win+close+byelection&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, Doug Ford's Liberal candidate beat Bonnie Crombie's Liberal candidate.
"I was very excited to be part of the Ford Nation, I was very excited to join the team," Zee Hamid told reporters after his victory Thursday night in the Milton byelection as a Progressive Conservative candidate.
Hamid, who tried to run for the federal Liberals in 2015 and has donated $3,508 to the Grits over the past decade, defeated Crombie's standard-bearer Galen Naidoo Harris by 2,407 votes.
That's more than the 1,680 votes former PC cabinet minister Parm Gill won by in the Tories' 2022 landslide re-election.
Indeed, Hamid took 47 per cent of the popular vote compared to Gill's 43.1 per cent, increasing the margin of victory to 8.8 percentage points from 1.4 two years ago.
While local Tories privately grumbled about Ford anointing a Liberal former councillor and mayoral runner-up as the PC candidate, the premier, who often emphasizes that he doesn't "believe in political stripes," was convinced Hamid's name recognition would help in a close contest.
Voters proved him right, but he struck an uncharacteristically modest tone at the victory party, saying he was "very humbled" by the wins in Milton and in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, where Steve Pinsonneault triumphed.
Ford - who over the past six years has transformed the Tories into a more diverse, centrist party that can attract Liberal and NDP voters - was asked about his opposition to Speaker Ted Arnott's decision to ban the Palestinian kaffiyeh from the legislature because it breaks the rule against all "overtly political" attire.
While Ford, Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner all oppose Arnott's edict, some Tory MPPs have refused to agree to unanimous consent motions to allow the kaffiyeh at Queen's Park. "You can see it hasn't caused too much of a division," the premier insisted in Milton, home to a growing and influential Muslim population.
"We're one family, one team - sure, families disagree sometimes, but most importantly we all keep together," he said.
For Crombie, who won the Liberal leadership five months ago, Milton was a setback. "There were a lot of factors and we'll go back and take a look at them," she said at Naidoo Harris's postelection party.
Privately, one Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, admitted their candidate was hindered by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal party.
"But the NDP is cratering, which is a positive," the insider said gamely, echoing Crombie's contention the Liberals are the best alternative to Ford, having finished second in both ridings - albeit with 22.6 per cent of the vote in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex to 56.9 per cent for the Tories. Indeed, Stiles's Official Opposition New Democrats had a disappointing night, garnering just 8.6 per cent of the vote in Milton and 10.8 per cent in the London-area riding.
Only Schreiner's Greens, who had won the Kitchener Centre byelection just six months ago with 48 per cent of the vote, taking an NDP seat, fared worse Thursday. They were fifth - behind the right-wing New Blue Party - with 2.8 per cent in Milton, where opposition to reopening the Campbellville quarry was a major issue, and 1.6 per cent in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex.
If the byelections were a teachable moment for the opposition party leaders, it was Ford who ended up schooling them.
With a provincewide election 25 months away, there are plenty of lessons for them - and not much time left to learn.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative candidate Zee Hamid celebrates with Premier Doug Ford after winning the provincial byelection in Milton on Thursday.Hamid defeated Crombie's standard-bearer Galen Naidoo Harris by 2,407 votes.Roland Cilliers Metroland
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Title: How Ford schooled rivals in Milton:   Premier banked on his candidate's name recognition to win close byelection

Document 399 of 500

What's at stake in campus showdowns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whats-at-stake-campus-showdowns/docview/3050449223/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+at+stake+in+campus+showdowns&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anyone paying attention to the news will notice that university and college campuses are the focus of impassioned debates over freedom of expression.
In the U.S., police have been mobilized in response to student and faculty actions demanding divestment from institutional relationships and funding that support Israel's militarization given the International Criminal Court's investigation of their conduct in Palestine.
One might be tempted to dismiss campus-based protests as a symptom of students' entitlement or naïveté. Others might disregard it as a symptom of incivility, an inability to get along when political differences seem acute.
But what these struggles reveal is how deeply our systems of post-secondary education are embedded with corporate and political interests and how far governments will go to protect them under the illusion of the public good and neutrality.
The cost of political interference in the business of higher education - which is to produce knowledge and uphold a vibrant public sphere - is restrictions on speech. Such censorship always has the effect of privileging certain voices over others.
Political attempts to control the narrative around Palestine/Israel are evident in the ban, instituted by the Speaker of the Ontario Legislative Assembly, on wearing the kaffiyeh in the legislative building, something the Canadian Civil Liberties Association has called a "direct attack on freedom of expression"
Party leaders, including Premier Doug Ford, have called for the Speaker to reverse this ban because of its divisive nature. Instead of supporting free expression, Jill Dunlop, the minister of colleges and universities' championing of Bill 166, the Strengthening Accountability and Student Supports Act, suggests a doubling down on attempts to direct how, and what, Ontarians should be allowed to speak about.
Now in its second reading, Bill 166 would empower ministerial directives on the content of anti-racism and mental-health policies at colleges and universities. This may seem positive - higher education institutions should have these policies. And they do.
The real problem is that the government has underfunded educational institutions to a point that undermines campus-based anti-racism and mental-health initiatives.
The Ontario government's strategy with respect to higher education echoes its approach to publicly funded health care - underfunding to justify restructuring. Bill 166 is a Trojan horse, invoking concerns about student mental health, racism, hate and educational affordability to warrant unprecedented interference in university autonomy.
University autonomy is crucial if a democracy based on public participation, freedom of expression and research innovation is to thrive. For research and education to be credible and sound, they must be free of political interference.
Introducing ministerial directives dictating the content of policies not only opens the door for unprecedented influence in educational affairs, it also means that policies can change whenever there is an election, cabinet shuffle or simply if the minister changes their mind. This fickle approach is not an effective use of public funding.
Further, Bill 166 will redirect resources from existing initiatives to establish new reporting structures and whatever else might be mandated by the minister. Removing support from existing practices grounded in evidence-based research conducted by experts in anti-racism and mental health will not benefit students and could cause harm.
Unlike post-secondary policies, Bill 166 would not require that the minister' directives be guided by research and best practices. Given this government's slashing of funding for public mechanisms mandated to combat racism - the Anti-Racism Directorate and the Ontario Human Rights Commission - we lack confidence in the minister's statements that Bill 166 is about supporting students.
Educational workers and students are rightly concerned about ministerial directives that will undercut university autonomy and interfere in best practices around campus anti-racism and mental-health support and policies. Educators who teach, produce knowledge and create a vital public sphere are best positioned to do this work; after all, this is our job.
Eve Haque (York University), Sara Matthews (Wilfrid Laurier University) and Trish Salah (Queen's University) are university professors.
CREDIT: Eve Haque, Sara Matthews and Trish Salah Contributors Eve Haque (York University), Sara Matthews (Wilfrid Laurier University) and Trish Salah (Queen's University) are university professors.;

Document 400 of 500

Education a better solution: Ontario reveals details of cellphone ban in schools. Here are the new rules for students, April 28 Doug Ford's school cellphone ban is too easy. There's a bigger education problem he needs to fix, April 30 Here's what Ontario students say about Doug Ford's cellphone ban, April 29

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/education-better-solution/docview/3050449168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Education+a+better+solution%3A+Ontario+reveals+details+of+cellphone+ban+in+schools.+Here+are+the+new+rules+for+students%2C+April+28+Doug+Ford%27s+school+cellphone+ban+is+too+easy.+There%27s+a+bigger+education+problem+he+needs+to+fix%2C+April+30+Here%27s+what+Ontario+students+say+about+Doug+Ford%27s+cellphone+ban%2C+April+29&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario reveals details of cellphone ban in schools. Here are the new rules for students, April 28
History shows that prohibition doesn't work. Alcohol, gambling, and marijuana were banned, yet their use continued. Accordingly, prohibiting the use of misnamed cellphones, which are actually powerful computers, in classrooms will likely yield similar results. Depriving students of knowledge tools because they lack the social skills to use them in a classroom only compounds the problem.
Education is the better solution. Smoking was reduced despite great addictive pressure when people realized the danger to their health. Similarly, persuasive evidence justifying the utility of computational devices in class is available. Utilizing advancements in computation, including AI, should be the primary directive of schooling. Furthermore, increasing general knowledge will only be meaningful if it includes social knowledge.
Consequently, instead of banning knowledge, innovative devices should be introduced in lower grades under controls that are gradually diminished as students exhibit mastery in higher grades. Teaching students' responsibility and holding them accountable for their choices is how we can make society smarter.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Leave enforcement to administrators, let teachers teach

Doug Ford's school cellphone ban is too easy. There's a bigger education problem he needs to fix, April 30
Education Minister Stephen Lecce says teachers will be responsible for enforcing his new policies. What everyone needs to understand is that teachers, even elementary ones, have no tools to enforce these policies, and in many cases, they get very little if any administrative support. The misbehaving kids know this and take full advantage of it. That is why teachers are so stressed out.
Lecce needs to stop his fanciful political glad-handing and deal with the real issues. Teachers need to be given a clear path that allows them to teach. Our teachers need to be protected, not put on the policy enforcement front line to the point that they require stress leave.
Jeff Osborne, Toronto
Those struggling in class are the ones who get distracted
Premier Doug Ford weighed in on the proposed cellphone ban saying, "It's not complicated: Don't use the phones." That's reminiscent of Nancy Reagan's advice in 1982 when questioned by a young student about drugs. The first lady responded, "Just say 'no.' " So simple. But as Edward Keenan points out, enforcing a ban on mobile phones will prove "nearly impossible." As a very old supply teacher, I understand the concern that such gizmos may interfere with the learning process. The problem is not with the many students who have a work ethic, or academic aspirations. It's those who are struggling in school, or bored, or who just don't realize the importance of focusing on school assignments. So, Education Minister Stephen Lecce's bark better have some bite. But he shouldn't expect teachers to do the dirty work. Parents will have to show gumption in this contentious issue.
Garry Burke, Oro-Medonte, Ont.
Students need
to learn how to
use IT for work
Edward Keenan states, "But this generation are digital natives: they were born online, their brains moulded by social media. They don't need to learn how to use it." But that's not quite true. According to the 2006 ECAR study of undergraduates and IT, students arrive at university with good recreational skills with IT, but are less skilled with instrumentally useful technologies. In other words, they are good at playing with IT, but aren't learning how to use it effectively for work or further education. This is a problem that needs to be addressed and is being avoided.
Donald Philip, Severn, Ont.
Lack of respect for others is the deeper societal problem
Here's what Ontario students say about Doug Ford's cellphone ban, April 29
The student comment about the ban being a deeper-rooted problem is probably a true statement. The biggest problem is not only in schools, it's everywhere. You see it in the lack of respect for other people, the "no one is going to tell me what to do" attitude. Shoplifting is epidemic because no one stops them. People drive like they are the only car on the roads because they can. Protesters stop people from going about their day, because no one intervenes. Kids vape, smoke, are late for school and dress like they just rolled out of bed because they can. There's no respect for other people or anyone in authority.
Patricia Holland, Oshawa

Subject: Bans; Cellular telephones; Students; Learning; Teachers; Education

Document 401 of 500

How Doug Ford schooled his rivals in the Milton byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-doug-ford-schooled-his-rivals-milton/docview/3050570293/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Doug+Ford+schooled+his+rivals+in+the+Milton+byelection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the end, Doug Ford's Liberal candidate beat Bonnie Crombie's Liberal candidate.
"I was very excited to be part of the Ford Nation, I was very excited to join the team," Zee Hamid told reporters after his victory Thursday night in the Milton byelection as a Progressive Conservative candidate.
Hamid, who tried to run for the federal Liberals in 2015 and has donated $3,508 to the Grits over the past decade, defeated Crombie's standard-bearer Galen Naidoo Harris by 2,407 votes.
That's more than the 1,680 votes former PC cabinet minister Parm Gill won by in the Tories' 2022 landslide re-election.
Indeed, Hamid took 47 per cent of the popular vote compared to Gill's 43.1 per cent, increasing the margin of victory to 8.8 percentage points from 1.4 two years ago.
While local Tories privately grumbled about Ford anointing a Liberal former councillor and mayoral runner-up as the PC candidate, the premier, who often emphasizes that he doesn't "believe in political stripes," was convinced Hamid's name recognition would help in a close contest.
Voters proved him right, but he struck an uncharacteristically modest tone at the victory party, saying he was "very humbled" by the wins in Milton and in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, where Steve Pinsonneault triumphed.
Ford - who over the past six years has transformed the Tories into a more diverse, centrist party that can attract Liberal and NDP voters - was asked about his opposition to Speaker Ted Arnott's decision to ban the Palestinian kaffiyeh from the legislature because it breaks the rule against all "overtly political" attire.
The traditional black-and-white scarves are common at pro-Palestinian rallies, including at the ongoing University of Toronto encampment protest, after the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks against Israel and subsequent Israeli military retaliation in Gaza.
While Ford, Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles and Green Leader Mike Schreiner all oppose Arnott's edict, some Tory MPPs have refused to agree to unanimous consent motions to allow the kaffiyeh at Queen's Park.
"You can see it hasn't caused too much of a division," the premier insisted in Milton, home to a growing and influential Muslim population.
"We're one family, one team - sure, families disagree sometimes, but most importantly we all keep together," he said.
For Crombie, who won the Liberal leadership five months ago, Milton was a setback.
"There were a lot of factors and we'll go back and take a look at them," she said at Naidoo Harris's postelection party.
Privately, one Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, admitted their candidate was hindered by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal party.
"But the NDP is cratering, which is a positive," the insider said gamely, echoing Crombie's contention the Liberals are the best alternative to Ford, having finished second in both ridings - albeit with 22.6 per cent of the vote in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex to 56.9 per cent for the Tories.
Indeed, Stiles's Official Opposition New Democrats had a disappointing night, garnering just 8.6 per cent of the vote in Milton and 10.8 per cent in the London-area riding.
Only Schreiner's Greens, who had won the Kitchener Centre byelection just six months ago with 48 per cent of the vote, taking an NDP seat, fared worse Thursday.
They were fifth - behind the right-wing New Blue Party - with 2.8 per cent in Milton, where opposition to reopening the Campbellville quarry was a major issue, and 1.6 per cent in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex.
If the byelections were a teachable moment for the opposition party leaders, it was Ford who ended up schooling them.
With a provincewide election 25 months away, there are plenty of lessons for them - and not much time left to learn.
CAPTION: Progressive Conservative candidate Zee Hamid celebrates with Premier Doug Ford after winning a provincial byelection in Milton on May 2, 2024.

Subject: Leadership; Voters

Document 402 of 500

Doug Ford's Tories easily win byelections in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tories-easily-win-byelections-milton/docview/3050310003/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+Tories+easily+win+byelections+in+Milton+and+Lambton-Kent-Middlesex&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have easily won byelections in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, holding onto seats that had been represented by cabinet ministers.
In a major setback for rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Tory Zee Hamid, a three-term former local councillor who recently quit the Grits, was victorious in Milton on Thursday while councillor Steve Pinsonneault held the London-area riding.
"These byelection victories show that voters across Ontario know Bonnie Crombie will make their lives more expensive," crowed Ford.
"I want to congratulate Zee and Steve and I can't wait to welcome them to our team at Queen's Park," he said.
Crombie put a positive spin on a disappointing night for the Liberals, saying the results prove her party is the only alternative to Ford's Tories as the New Democrats sputtered.
Privately, Grit strategists suspect they were hindered by the plunging fortunes of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal Liberals.
The byelections were called after Parm Gill, the red-tape reduction minister and two-term Milton MPP, and Monte McNaughton, the labour minister, who had represented Lambton-Kent-Middlesex since 2011, resigned.
Chastened after losing byelections to the Liberals last July in Scarborough-Guildwood and Kanata-Carleton, Ford left nothing to chance, throwing everything but the kitchen sink into winning on Thursday night.
Scores of Tory political staffers at Queen's Park were ordered to take personal days off work to canvass and pull the vote in Milton.
Eager to hobble Crombie, who became Liberal leader on Dec. 2, the governing party spent millions on attack ads against her that have been airing in NHL playoff games.
During the byelection writ period, the Tories touted next year's construction start date for Highway 413, the proposed 52-km Milton-to-Vaughan freeway that was a cornerstone promise of their 2022 re-election campaign, and unveiled a classroom cellphone ban for students that polling suggests is popular with parents and educators.
Mindful of Milton's growing and influential Muslim vote, Ford also opposed a decision by Speaker Ted Arnott, who oversees the legislature, to ban the Palestinian kaffiyeh from the legislative precinct because it breaks the rule against all "overtly political" clothing.
The black-and-white scarves are frequently worn at pro-Palestinian rallies - including at the University of Toronto encampment protest - in the wake of the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks against Israel and the subsequent retaliation by the Israeli military in Gaza.
Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who is expected to revisit the kaffiyeh issue Monday at the legislature, and Green Leader Mike Schreiner also opposed Arnott's decree.
Hamid hailed Ford for doing "the right thing" on the ban and Liberal runner-up Galen Naidoo Harris, son of former education minister Indira Naidoo Harris, who represented Milton from 2014 until 2018, campaigned in a kaffiyeh at times.
Until a few months ago, Hamid was a prominent local Liberal. He won 47.2 per cent of the vote to 38.1 per cent for Naidoo Harris.
The NDP's Edie Strachan finished third with 6.8 per cent while New Blue's John Spina was a surprising fourth with four per cent and Green Kyle Hutton was fifth with 2.8 per cent.
It remains unclear what Thursday's result means for the proposed reopening of the Milton-area Campbellville quarry, which is opposed by many residents and the local council.
The Greens' Schreiner noted Ford "promised to cancel the quarry nearly four years ago," but has since appeared to waffle somewhat.
Asked about the issue in Tuesday in Caledon, the premier said: "It's in front of an environmental assessment right now and then after the EA is done, we'll sit down with the community.
"But I always believe if communities don't want something, then we don't do it; if they do, then we move forward with it," stressed Ford.
ACTION Milton, a local community group that congratulated Hamid on his win, warned the quarry could impact drinking water for 20,000 people in the area.
"Now it's time for MPP-elect Hamid to keep the promise the premier made in 2020: the proposed Campbellville quarry must not be licensed," the group said.
In the sprawling riding of Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, the Tories' Pinsonneault, who won 56.6 per cent of the vote, trounced Liberal Cathy Burghardt-Jesson, the mayor of Lucan Biddulph, who received 22.7 per cent. New Democrat Kathryn Shailer was third with 10.8 per cent while New Blue's Keith Benn was fourth with 5.5 per cent and Green Andraena Tilgner fifth with 1.6 per cent.
Turnout was low - just 27.5 per cent in Milton and 28.3 per cent in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex.
After the byelections, there are 80 Tories in the 124-member legislature, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, five Independents, and two Greens.
The next general provincial election is June 2026.
CAPTION: Tory Zee Hamid, a three-term former local councillor who recently quit the Grits, was victorious in Milton on Thursday.Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives won byelections in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex Thursday night in a setback for rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie.

Subject: Bans; Negative campaigning; Legislatures

Title: Doug Ford's Tories easily win byelections in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex

Document 403 of 500

Is it too much to expect MPs to set aside partisan showboating and engage in mature debate?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-too-much-expect-mps-set-aside-partisan/docview/3050239888/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+it+too+much+to+expect+MPs+to+set+aside+partisan+showboating+and+engage+in+mature+debate%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There have been times over the years, far too many times, that we have witnessed the antics of our elected representatives in Ottawa and shaken our heads in despair.
A nostalgic journey into the mud quickly conjures a litany of lowlights. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, while in opposition, once had to apologize to the House of Commons after calling then-Conservative Environment Minister Peter Kent "a piece of s--t." His father infamously shouted an obscenity across the aisle which he later amended to "fuddle duddle." In opposition, a Liberal quartet of backbenchers who would go on to become cabinet ministers became known as the "Rat Pack" for their disruptive behaviour and taunting of the Brian Mulroney government.
Former Reform Party MP Darrell Stinson twice challenged Liberals to fisticuffs and referred to then-Progressive Conservative leader Jean Charest as "a fat little chubby, little sucker," after Charest called him a bigot. One-time PC cabinet minister John Crosbie told Liberal Sheila Copps to "just quieten down, baby."
So, expectations for reasoned, mature debate are often low, but this week MPs in Ottawa managed to slither under the limbo bar one more time.
When Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was ejected from the Commons on Tuesday, he went far beyond the heat-of-battle indiscretions oft heard in the chamber. He three times refused entreaties from Speaker Greg Fergus to play by the rules and withdraw a word Fergus deemed unparliamentary. For Poilievre there seems to be no rules except his rules, no respect for decorum and a delight in saying anything, meeting with anybody and flouting convention in the name of ramping up anger. In Poilievre's world, everyone is a liar and only he has the truth. Just the day before that ugly day in the Commons, he told the Canadian Police Association he would set his own rules on bail reform by using the notwithstanding clause in the Constitution to override Charter rights. He also sets his own rules on civility. Time and again he has shown he will gladly meet with fringe supporters sporting Diagolon stickers and flying F-Trudeau flags, or convoy protesters illegally blockading Ottawa streets.
The point is not that Poilievre was booted for calling Trudeau a "wacko," a fairly tame term given what had already been tossed around Tuesday. Poilievre clearly wanted to be booted from the Commons as a challenge to Fergus, because the Speaker is one of those gatekeepers he clearly disrespects. Members of his caucus who followed him outside the chamber were live streaming their faux indignation and victimhood before they left the building.
None of this absolves Trudeau from blame. Indeed, the day after the drama, it was the prime minister who tossed barbs at Poilievre who in turn channelled his inner adult and was the model of decorum. The leader of the country should be elevating discourse and displaying maturity and calm, but he too often plays on Poilievre's turf. He had already helped inflame proceedings by referring to the "spineless" style of leadership shown by Poilievre. For his part, the opposition leader called Trudeau a racist and implied the prime minister was responsible for drug deaths in British Columbia.
Chaos in the House of Commons is something that rivets Ottawa insiders but is mostly ignored by Canadians outside the capital. It's easy - indeed fashionable - to point out that no one is watching daily question period and it is all vacuous political performance by MPs who seem more concerned with spewing over-the-top rants that are quickly posted to social media.
But we're headed to an election next year (if not sooner) and if this type of overheated rhetoric continues between two men who clearly dislike each other, voters will continue to be repelled and serious, quality candidates will run for cover. Indeed, Liberal MP Pam Damoff announced Wednesday she would not run again, citing the toxicity in Canadian politics and the erosion of national discourse.
More disconcerting is that, if the polls are correct, Poilievre is on track to become the next prime minister on a campaign stoked by anger and distrust of our institutions. Once elected, can he possibly put that anger back in the box? That would be a tall order for a man who has so far shown no appetite for compromise or calm.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises in response to the Speaker asking him to withdraw language during question period on Tuesday.

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Is it too much to expect MPs to set aside partisan showboating and engage in mature debate?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 3, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 404 of 500

Parliament's overheated rhetoric

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/parliaments-overheated-rhetoric/docview/3050053562/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Parliament%27s+overheated+rhetoric&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There have been times over the years, far too many times, that we have witnessed the antics of our elected representatives in Ottawa and shaken our heads in despair.

Former Reform Party MP Darrell Stinson twice challenged Liberals to fisticuffs and referred to then-Progressive Conservative leader Jean Charest as "a fat little chubby, little sucker," after Charest called him a bigot. One-time PC cabinet minister John Crosbie told Liberal Sheila Copps to "just quieten down, baby."

In Poilievre's world, everyone is a liar and only he has the truth. Just the day before that ugly day in the Commons, he told the Canadian Police Association he would set his own rules on bail reform by using the notwithstanding clause in the Constitution to override Charter rights. He also sets his own rules on civility. Time and again he has shown he will gladly meet with fringe supporters sporting Diagolon stickers and flying F--k Trudeau flags, or convoy protesters illegally blockading Ottawa streets.
The point is not that Poilievre was booted for calling Trudeau a "wacko," a fairly tame term given what had already been tossed around Tuesday. Poilievre clearly wanted to be booted from the Commons as a challenge to Fergus, because the Speaker is one of those gatekeepers he clearly disrespects. Members of his caucus who followed him outside the chamber were live streaming their faux indignation and victimhood before they left the building.
None of this absolves Trudeau from blame. Indeed, the day after the drama, it was the prime minister who tossed barbs at Poilievre who in turn channelled his inner adult and was the model of decorum. The leader of the country should be elevating discourse and displaying maturity and calm, but he too often plays on Poilievre's turf. He had already helped inflame proceedings by referring to the "spineless" style of leadership shown by Poilievre. For his part, the Opposition leader called Trudeau a racist and implied the prime minister was responsible for drug deaths in British Columbia.
Chaos in the House of Commons is something that rivets Ottawa insiders but is mostly ignored by Canadians outside the capital. It's easy - indeed fashionable - to point out that no one is watching daily question period and it is all vacuous political performance by MPs who seem more concerned with spewing over-the-top rants that are quickly posted to social media.
But we're headed to an election next year (if not sooner) and if this type of overheated rhetoric continues between two men who clearly dislike each other, voters will continue to be repelled and serious, quality candidates will run for cover. Indeed, Liberal MP Pam Damoff announced Wednesday she would not run again, citing the toxicity in Canadian politics and the erosion of national discourse.
More disconcerting is that, if the polls are correct, Poilievre is on track to become the next prime minister on a campaign stoked by anger and distrust of our institutions. Once elected, can he possibly put that anger back in the box? That would be a tall order for a man who has so far shown no appetite for compromise or calm.
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Document 405 of 500

Ford's Tories easily win byelections: PCs retain Milton, Lambton-Kent-Middlesex seats vacated by former cabinet ministers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-tories-easily-win-byelections/docview/3050053500/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+Tories+easily+win+byelections%3A+PCs+retain+Milton%2C+Lambton-Kent-Middlesex+seats+vacated+by+former+cabinet+ministers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives have easily won byelections in Milton and Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, holding onto seats that had been represented by cabinet ministers.
In a major setback for rookie Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, Tory Zee Hamid, a three-term former local councillor who recently quit the Grits, was victorious in Milton on Thursday while councillor Steve Pinsonneault held the London-area riding.
"These byelection victories show that voters across Ontario know Bonnie Crombie will make their lives more expensive," crowed Ford. "I want to congratulate Zee and Steve and I can't wait to welcome them to our team at Queen's Park."
Crombie put a positive spin on a disappointing night for the Liberals, saying the results prove her party is the only alternative to Ford's Tories as the New Democrats sputtered.
Privately, Grit strategists suspect they were hindered by the plunging fortunes of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal Liberals.
The byelections were called after Parm Gill, the red-tape reduction minister and two-term Milton MPP, and Monte McNaughton, the labour minister, who had represented Lambton-Kent-Middlesex since 2011, resigned.
Chastened after losing byelections to the Liberals last July in Scarborough-Guildwood and Kanata-Carleton, Ford left nothing to chance, throwing everything into winning on Thursday night.
Scores of Tory political staffers at Queen's Park were ordered to take personal days off work to canvass and pull the vote in Milton.
Eager to hobble Crombie, who became Liberal leader on Dec. 2, the governing party spent millions on attack ads against her that have been airing in NHL playoff games.
During the byelection writ period, the Tories touted next year's construction start date for Highway 413, the proposed 52-kilometre Milton-to-Vaughan freeway that was a cornerstone promise of their 2022 re-election campaign, and unveiled a classroom cellphone ban for students that polling suggests is popular with parents and educators.
Mindful of Milton's growing and influential Muslim vote, Ford also opposed a decision by Speaker Ted Arnott, who oversees the legislature, to ban the Palestinian kaffiyeh from the legislative precinct because it breaks the rule against all "overtly political" clothing.
The black-and-white scarves are frequently worn at pro-Palestinian rallies - including at the University of Toronto encampment protest - in the wake of the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks against Israel and the subsequent retaliation by the Israeli military in Gaza.
Crombie, NDP Leader Marit Stiles, who is expected to revisit the kaffiyeh issue Monday at the legislature, and Green Leader Mike Schreiner also opposed Arnott's decree.
Hamid hailed Ford for doing "the right thing" on the ban and Liberal runner-up Galen Naidoo Harris, son of former education minister Indira Naidoo Harris, who represented Milton from 2014 until 2018, campaigned in a kaffiyeh at times.
Until a few months ago, Hamid was a prominent local Liberal. He won 47 per cent of the vote to 38.3 per cent for Naidoo Harris.
The NDP's Edie Strachan finished third with 6.8 per cent while New Blue's John Spina was a surprising fourth with four per cent and Green Kyle Hutton was fifth with 2.8 per cent.
It remains unclear what Thursday's result means for the proposed reopening of the Milton-area Campbellville quarry, which is opposed by many residents and the local council. The Greens' Schreiner noted Ford "promised to cancel the quarry nearly four years ago."
Asked about the issue in Tuesday in Caledon, the premier said: "It's in front of an environmental assessment right now and then after the EA is done, we'll sit down with the community.
"But I always believe if communities don't want something, then we don't do it; if they do, then we move forward with it," stressed Ford.
ACTION Milton, a local community group that congratulated Hamid on his win, warned the quarry could impact drinking water for 20,000 people in the area.
"Now it's time for MPP-elect Hamid to keep the promise the premier made in 2020: the proposed Campbellville quarry must not be licensed," the group said.
In the sprawling riding of Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, the Tories' Pinsonneault, who won 56.9 per cent of the vote, trounced Liberal Cathy Burghardt-Jesson, the mayor of Lucan Biddulph, who received 22.6 per cent. New Democrat Kathryn Shailer was third with 10.8 per cent while New Blue's Keith Benn was fourth with 5.5 per cent and Green Andraena Tilgner fifth with 1.6 per cent.
Turnout was low - just 27.5 per cent in Milton and 28.3 per cent in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex.
There are now 80 Tories in the 124-member legislature, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, five Independents and two Greens. The next general election is June 2026.
CAPTION: PC candidate Zee Hamid was elected MPP for Milton on Thursday.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Bans; Negative campaigning; Legislatures; Special elections; Election results

People: Ford, Doug; Crombie, Bonnie

Document 406 of 500

Ottawa pins emissions on COVID recovery: Canada saw rise in greenhouse gas levels in 2021, 2022

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-pins-emissions-on-covid-recovery/docview/3050053159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+pins+emissions+on+COVID+recovery%3A+Canada+saw+rise+in+greenhouse+gas+levels+in+2021%2C+2022&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's annual greenhouse gas emissions increased for the second year in a row in 2022, a bump the federal government blames in part on how economic activity was still bouncing back from the closures of the COVID-19 pandemic.
According to the latest official government tally of national emissions that cause climate change, Canada's overall greenhouse gas output in 2022 was 708 megatonnes of pollution.
That's 1.3 per cent higher than the 698 megatonnes in 2021, and 2.6 per cent higher than the emissions reported in 2020.
Speaking to reporters just as the government released the figures, federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said the increase is partly because of a "rebound" from the pandemic, when businesses shut down and commuter traffic plummeted because of public-health measures. Guilbeault also said part of the increase comes from improved measurements of highly potent methane emissions, which were previously undercounted, in an oil and gas sector that remains responsible for the largest share of national greenhouse gas pollution.
Overall, however, Guilbeault stressed that the 2022 emissions are still below levels posted before the pandemic, which the report credited to the movement toward cleaner fuels, the "modernization" of industrial processes, and more.
Guilbeault also claimed Canada is "on track" to achieve its emissions reduction targets for 2026 and 2030, when the country has pledged in global negotiations to post greenhouse gas emissions that are at least 40 per cent lower than they were in 2005.
The government's most recent projections predict Canada's emissions will be at least 36 per cent lower in 2030 than in 2005, leaving a gap of at least 28 millions tons of greenhouse gas emissions.
Thursday's report calculated the country's total emissions in 2022 were 7.1 per cent lower than in 2005.
"We knew emissions were going to bounce back in 2022 as our economy burst back to life," Guilbeault said. "We need everyone to help drive down the emissions that are already fuelling costly climate impacts like wildfires that damaged so much in Canada last year, and threaten to do so again this year. That's why we cannot pause or start throwing environmental measures overboard in the pursuit of short-term partisan gain."
Environmental groups had mixed responses to the report. A statement from the David Suzuki Foundation called for stronger efforts to fight climate change, noting that the latest tally showed oil and gas emissions increased and remain Canada's largest source of planet-warming pollution. Meanwhile, a British Columbia-based think tank, Clean Energy Canada, said the report shows the country is "moving in the right direction" and that the 2030 target is "in reach."
Under the Liberal government, Ottawa has implemented a suite of policies designed to slash Canada's greenhouse gas emissions and contribute to the global effort to contain the damage of the climate crisis. Most contentiously, the policies include a legal requirement for all provinces to have a minimum carbon price that discourages emissions and rewards moves to reduce them. The price level goes up every year, and is currently set at $80 per tonne of emissions, which adds about 17 cents to the cost of a litre of consumer gasoline, according to federal stats.
Provinces that don't implement their own pricing systems are forced to adopt the federal scheme, which includes a consumer levy on fuels as well as rebates that get paid out to households every three months to cover extra costs from the system. In Ontario, where this system applies, a family of four will get $1,120 over the coming year.
Under Pierre Poilievre, the Conservatives have aggressively attacked the policy and have pledged to "axe the tax" on consumer fuel if they win power in the next federal election.
Alongside the carbon price, the Trudeau Liberals are also implementing a cap on emissions from the oil and gas production that will require the industry to reduce its overall greenhouse gas output by at least 35 per cent below 2019 levels by 2030 - a level that some argue is too little for Canada's heaviest-emitting sector.
Thursday's report showed the oil and gas sector remained responsible for the largest share of national emissions in 2022, at 31 per cent. The sector's emissions have also increased 21 per cent since 2005, the report said, with the biggest proportion of the industry's emissions coming from the Western oilsands.
When asked about this on Thursday, Guilbeault said draft regulations for the oil and gas cap will be released before the fall.
After requiring the phase out of coal-fired power plants, the government is also moving to restrict the amount of emitting fossil fuels used to create electricity after 2035, cement sales targets for zero-emission vehicles, and tighten regulations for cleaner fuel.
While Conservatives have railed against carbon pricing, the Liberals routinely accuse their opponents of failing to present credible alternatives to reduce emissions. A recent report from the Canadian Climate Institute said carbon pricing could be responsible for at least a third of the country's projected emissions reductions by 2030 - though the bulk of that would come from industrial pricing systems rather than the consumer levy on fuel.
Critics from the environmental movement, meanwhile, have denounced the Liberal government's use of $34 billion in public money to finance construction of the Trans Mountain expansion project, which tripled the carrying capacity of a decades-old oil pipeline from Alberta to the Pacific coast.
CAPTION: Federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said Wednesday that despite greenhouse gas emissions increasing in 2022, Canada is "on track" to achieve its emissions reduction targets for 2026 and 2030.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Subject: Greenhouse gases; Emission standards; Coal-fired power plants; Emissions; Carbon; Climate change; Industrial plant emissions; Pandemics; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Coal-fired power plants

Document 407 of 500

Is the job worth the sacrifice anymore?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-job-worth-sacrifice-anymore/docview/3050053090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+the+job+worth+the+sacrifice+anymore%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It used to be that when politicians announced they were stepping down, you got a glimpse of the kinder, gentler side of politics - the joy of public service, the friendships made across party lines.
Now, though, I've come to dread learning why many politicians are packing it in - the threats to their safety and mental health, and how the job may not be worth the sacrifice. No longer are we seeing the best side of politics; we're seeing the worst.
This week, Pam Damoff, the three-term MP from Oakville East, became the latest to announce the toll that her public service has taken on her life.
"The threats and misogyny I have experienced as a member of Parliament are such that I often fear going out in public, and that is not a sustainable or healthy way to live," Damoff wrote in a letter to her constituents. "Quite simply, politics is no longer for me and so it is time for me to turn the page on this chapter."
Charlie Angus, one of the New Democrats' best-known MPs, was one of three in his caucus who announced a month ago they wouldn't be seeking re-election.
Angus said his departure was triggered in part by the redrawing of electoral boundaries that would make his huge, Northern Ontario riding too tough to serve properly.
But only weeks before, Angus was talking to the National Observer about the torrent of abuse he was facing over his bill to crack down on fossil fuel advertising - abuse that included death threats.
Former Conservative leader Erin O'Toole used some of his final days as an MP to warn about the state of politics.
He had lots to say about the dangers of social media and wedge politics, but he also thought the pandemic had helped make everything worse.
"I think the pandemic was like a pressure cooker on some of the divisions that were already there, and it accelerated social media and this algorithmic descent into division that we're seeing," O'Toole said to CBC Radio host Catherine Cullen.
"I think politicians have an important role of making sure that doesn't become a permanent state of chaos."
Certainly the spectacle in the Commons this week, culminating in the ouster of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, made O'Toole's words look prescient.
But what's more disturbing than the chaos on Parliament Hill is how the toxicity of politics has crept deep down to the riding level.
Damoff talked about not feeling safe in her own hometown of Oakville; other MPs have had violence and threats right at their very doorstep.
In March, an "axe the tax" protest outside the constituency office of Gord Johns, an NDP MP from Port Alberni, B.C., turned menacing when one protester physically threatened the MP and the police had to be called in.
This week, in an internal memo circulated on Parliament Hill and reported by Global News, MPs were warned that another round of co-ordinated, "axe the tax" protests were coming to constituency offices on Thursday, and all were advised to not engage with the protesters and to their keep their "duress alarms" at hand.
Several years back, I started doing an informal poll with MPs, asking them which part of their jobs they liked better - the time they spend in Ottawa, or the time back in their ridings.
With only a few exceptions, they said they preferred the riding work - the face-to-face dealings with constituents and the chance to help solve their problems.
Many said Ottawa was just too toxic.
This is why, perhaps, Damoff's tales of riding-level abuse leapt out at me when she wrote her letter to constituents.
It was Pierre Trudeau who famously said that MPs were "nobodies" when they got 50 yards from Parliament Hill. (It was in the days before we said "metres.")
I'm betting that some of them now only wish they could stay nobodies off Parliament Hill, if it meant shielding them from in-person threats and abuse in their own ridings.
Nor is this political abuse limited to federal or provincial politics; we've seen a flurry of mayors stepping down too, including across the river from Ottawa, in Gatineau, Que., where mayor France Bélisle said she couldn't handle the toxicity.
CBC Radio devoted an hour of its daily call-in show in Ontario on Thursday to the question of whether Parliament is broken - prompted by the raucous day we saw on the Hill this week.
It was a slam-dunk; almost all the callers talked in varying degrees of frustration about the mess all around.
Shachi Kurl of the Angus Reid Institute helpfully reminded the show's listeners that the coarsening of public debate wasn't limited to politics - that it was being felt in all areas of life. So it may well be that politics looks so ugly now because it is a mirror of something larger out there.
But it raises real questions about who will want to run if public service has now become a job more dangerous than it's worth.
CAPTION: Liberal MP Pam Damoff says she will not run again in the next federal election due to the toll public service has taken on her life. It may well be that politics looks so ugly now because it is a mirror of something larger out there, writes Susan Delacourt.Pam Damoff via X
CREDIT: Susan Delacourt OPINION

Subject: Members of Parliament; Demonstrations & protests; Parliaments; Politics; Politicians; Pandemics; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Broadcasting Corp; NAICS: 516110, 516120

Document 408 of 500

Death threats. A toxic culture. Is it any wonder MPs are saying, ''politics is no longer for me'?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/death-threats-toxic-culture-is-any-wonder-mps-are/docview/3050053089/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Death+threats.+A+toxic+culture.+Is+it+any+wonder+MPs+are+saying%2C+%27%27politics+is+no+longer+for+me%27%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

CAPTION: Liberal MP Pam Damoff says she will not run in the next federal election and blames disrespectful dialogue in politics, threats to her life and misogyny.

Subject: Death threats; Members of Parliament; Misogyny; Demonstrations & protests; Parliaments; Politics; Politicians; Pandemics; Social networks

Business indexing term: Subject: Social networks

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Broadcasting Corp; NAICS: 516110, 516120

Title: Death threats. A toxic culture. Is it any wonder MPs are sayi ng, ''politics is no longer for me'?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 3, 2024

Section: politics

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 409 of 500

Trudeau government blames post-pandemic recovery as Canada's greenhouse emissions rise for a second straight year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-government-blames-post-pandemic-recovery/docview/3050310005/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government+blames+post-pandemic+recovery+as+Canada%27s+greenhouse+emissions+rise+for+a+second+straight+year&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Canada's annual greenhouse gas emissions increased for the second year in a row in 2022, a bump the federal government blames in part on how economic activity was still bouncing back from the closures of the COVID-19 pandemic.
According to the latest official government tally of national emissions that cause climate change, Canada's overall greenhouse gas output in 2022 was 708 megatonnes of pollution.
That's 1.3 per cent higher than the 698 megatonnes in 2021, and 2.6 per cent higher than the emissions reported in 2020.
Speaking to reporters just as the government released the figures, federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said the increase is partly because of a "rebound" from the pandemic, when businesses shut down and commuter traffic plummeted because of public health measures. Guilbeault also said part of the increase comes from improved measurements of highly potent methane emissions, which were previously undercounted, in an oil and gas sector that remains responsible for the largest share of national greenhouse gas pollution.
Overall, however, Guilbeault stressed that the 2022 emissions are still below levels posted before the pandemic, which the report credited to the movement toward cleaner fuels, the "modernization" of industrial processes, and more.
Guilbeault also claimed Canada is "on track" to achieve its emissions reduction targets for 2026 and 2030, when the country has pledged in global negotiations to post greenhouse gas emissions that are at least 40 per cent lower than they were in 2005.
The government's most recent projections predict Canada's emissions will be at least 36 per cent lower in 2030 than in 2005, leaving a gap of at least 28 millions tons of greenhouse gas emissions.
Thursday's report calculated the country's total emissions in 2022 were 7.1 per cent lower than in 2005.
"We knew emissions were going to bounce back in 2022 as our economy burst back to life," Guilbeault said.
"We need everyone to help drive down the emissions that are already fuelling costly climate impacts like wildfires that damaged so much in Canada last year, and threaten to do so again this year. That's why we cannot pause or start throwing environmental measures overboard in the pursuit of short-term partisan gain."
Environmental groups had mixed responses to the report. A statement from the David Suzuki Foundation called for stronger efforts to fight climate change, noting that the latest tally showed oil and gas emissions increased and remain Canada's largest source of planet-warming pollution. Meanwhile, the British Columbia-based think tank, Clean Energy Canada, said the report shows the country is "moving in the right direction" and that the 2030 target is "in reach."
Under the Liberal government, Ottawa has implemented a suite of policies designed to slash Canada's greenhouse gas emissions and contribute to the global effort to contain the damage of the climate crisis. Most contentiously, the policies include a legal requirement for all provinces to have a minimum carbon price that discourages emissions and rewards moves to reduce them. The price level goes up every year, and is currently set at $80 per tonne of emissions, which adds about 17 cents to the cost of a litre of consumer gasoline, according to federal stats.
Provinces that don't implement their own pricing systems are forced to adopt the federal scheme, which includes a consumer levy on fuels as well as rebates that get paid out to households every three months to cover extra costs from the system. In Ontario, where this system applies, a family of four will get $1,120 over the coming year.
Under Pierre Poilievre, the Conservatives have aggressively attacked the policy and have pledged to "axe the tax" on consumer fuel if they win power in the next federal election.
Alongside the carbon price, the Trudeau Liberals are also implementing a cap on emissions from the oil and gas production that will require the industry to reduce its overall greenhouse gas output by at least 35 per cent below 2019 levels by 2030 - a level that some argue is too little for Canada's heaviest-emitting sector.
Thursday's report showed the oil and gas sector remained responsible for the largest share of national emissions in 2022, at 31 per cent. The sector's emissions have also increased 21 per cent since 2005, the report said, with the biggest proportion of the industry's emissions coming from the Western oilsands.
When asked about this on Thursday, Guilbeault said draft regulations for the oil and gas cap will be released before the fall.
After requiring the phase out of coal-fired power plants, the government is also moving to restrict the amount of emitting fossil fuels used to create electricity after 2035, cement sales targets for zero-emission vehicles, and tighten regulations for cleaner fuel.
While Conservatives have railed against carbon pricing, the Liberals routinely accuse their opponents of failing to present credible alternatives to reduce emissions. A recent report from the Canadian Climate Institute said carbon pricing could be responsible for at least a third of the country's projected emissions reductions by 2030 - though the bulk of that would come from industrial pricing systems rather than the consumer levy on fuel.
Critics from the environmental movement, meanwhile, have denounced the Liberal government's use of $34 billion in public money to finance construction of the massive Trans Mountain expansion project, which tripled the carrying capacity of a decades-old oil pipeline from Alberta to the Pacific coast.
CAPTION: Steven Guilbeault, federal minister of environment and climate change, answers questions on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on May 2, 2024.

Document 410 of 500

Monday's highway carnage is yet more proof that police chases are never worth the risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mondays-highway-carnage-is-yet-more-proof-that/docview/3049789490/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Monday%27s+highway+carnage+is+yet+more+proof+that+police+chases+are+never+worth+the+risk&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unacceptable and incomprehensible tragedy.
There's no other way to describe the scene that unfolded on Highway 401 in Whitby Monday night, when a wrong-way police pursuit ended in a multi-vehicle collision that claimed the lives of four people, including an infant and the child's grandparents. An occupant in the van was also killed. Another person suffered serious injuries.
It's hard to imagine a riskier scenario - a suspect in a cargo van, followed by a phalanx of cop cars, all speeding head-on into unsuspecting traffic.
For what? A robbery at an LCBO store? Even the report that the suspect may have brandished a knife at an off-duty Durham Region officer who was at the store does not appear to justify the decision to race off in hot pursuit.
This chase should never have been started. It certainly should have been called off when it became apparent the suspect vehicle was headed onto the highway.
Viewing bystander video of the chase on the highway and it's apparent that it was certain to end badly. Motorists described the terrifying scene of vehicles rushing at them. The dangers were obvious, at least to some officers. "Someone's going to get hurt," an unidentified OPP officer commented over the radio as multiple Durham police cars gave chase after the van.
Yes, the suspect made the reckless decision to put lives at risk. But the wanton recklessness of a suspected criminal does not compel police to follow suit. Officers, after all, are the ones with the responsibility to weigh the bigger picture and the threat to broader public safety. They are the ones trained for such scenarios, to stay cool and clear-headed in such adrenaline-fueled situations.
Indeed, revised provincial regulations, which came into force just a month ago, make clear the obligations of police officers around pursuits. They can only be started if a criminal offence has been committed or is about to be committed and no alternatives can be "readily" implemented. Critically, the regulations underscore the responsibility of officers to continually assess the risks to public safety and to discontinue a pursuit if that risk becomes greater than the dangers of a suspect not being immediately apprehended.
Such regulations are born out of past tragedies. There's a lengthy list of innocent bystanders killed in Toronto-area police chases, which underscores the folly of such actions. These incidents in the late 1990s prompted the previous revision of the rules more than a decade ago.
Based on what we know so far, it's difficult to think that the apprehension of an alleged liquor store robber met the high bar for initiating a chase. In this era of security and traffic cameras -- Highway 401 in particular is dotted with them -- surely there were other avenues to track the van and help ensure the apprehension of the suspect.
Premier Doug Ford called the incident a "tragedy" and suggested that the province's funding of four additional police helicopters for the Greater Toronto region would avoid such chases in the future. They can certainly help but they are no panacea given that they are not always airborne and available when needed. In this case, Durham does have a police helicopter. It's not known if it was called on to assist Monday.
That's one of the questions that hang over this tragedy. There are many others. What was the justification for the pursuit? How was it supervised?
The Special Investigations Unit, which investigates deaths or serious injuries in incidents that involve police, is investigating the circumstances around this fatal pursuit. An inquest will also be needed to get at the broader issues.
A month after new guidelines took effect and already it's apparent more explicit guidance is needed to make clear what was underscored by Monday's tragedy: Police chases are never worth the risk.
CAPTION: Four people were killed Monday, including an infant, when a cargo van pursued by police drove the wrong-way on Highway 401 in Whitby and collided with oncoming cars.

Subject: Automobiles; Criminal investigations; Public safety; Robbery

Title: Monday's highway carnage is yet more proof that police chases are never worth the risk

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 2, 2024

Grocery store produce rots while food banks can't keep up with the demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/grocery-store-produce-rots-while-food-banks-cant/docview/3049789250/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grocery+store+produce+rots+while+food+banks+can%27t+keep+up+with+the+demand&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'Deeply unhappy' grocery shoppers plan to boycott Loblaw-owned stores in May, April 29
There is no shortage of food, the supermarkets are full. We know the problem is affordability. When is the government going to take on these companies with their million-dollar bonuses at the expense of the consumers? No Frills is filled with products local and foreign. I notice most people have shopping lists and are carefully checking off items not even looking at anything off their list. I see employees discarding food by the box load. Meanwhile the food banks are experiencing an almost impossible task of keeping up with demand. Stop corporate greed.
Frances Sedgwick, Toronto
Small steps can conquer Toronto's traffic problemsA behind-the-scenes peek at the control centre trying to keep Toronto's clogged streets moving, April 30
The city should consider taking small steps rather than huge ones to fixing Toronto's clogged streets. When it comes to vehicles blocking intersection between lights, hindering the flow of traffic in the other direction, the city should identify the city core then select 10 intersections a month to be monitored during the busiest times by police officers. Any vehicle caught in the intersection blocking the flow of traffic gets a $500 fine. The police should rotate intersections each month. No bylaw for this? Amend the City of Toronto Act to reflect the new rules. It is not the entire solution, but it is a step in the right direction and in combination with other small steps will ultimately lead to success.
Richard Bino, Singhampton, ON
Even in light of the Zameer case, Poilievre still calls for tough on crime bail reforms'I should have been more clear': Toronto police chief apologizes for saying he hoped for 'different outcome' at Umar Zameer trial, April 30
The criminal justice system in Canada does have its problems but the after the fact consensus is that the jury came with the right decision in the trial of Umar Zameer. Premier Doug Ford, former Toronto Mayor John Tory and Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw are all walking back comments made at the time of Zameer's release from custody on bail, and in Demkiw's case, after the jury made its decision that Zameer was in fact, innocent. However, at the federal political level, there is Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and others denouncing the justice system, the Charter and the Liberal government for being soft on crime.
It is ironic that almost simultaneously with the conclusion of Zameer's trial and the personal hardships he and his family endured, there is again the renewed, or perhaps continuously recycled, calls by Poilievre for bail reform and a tough on crime reforms. Similarly, are Ford's musings of having like-minded judges appointed, reflecting the same tough on crime political stance. Perhaps Poilievre and Ford should comment in a meaningful manner on what the lessons are of Zameer's trial and criminal prosecution of the first-degree murder charges. Just imagine the injustice to Zameer if he had been denied bail.
David Maltby, Toronto
Ontario should be pushing for its share of MPs New federal ridings will create big changes in the GTA. Here's where the drama will be, April 28
Toronto should not be losing a riding, but our MPs did not do enough to fight this.
First, the changes are based on the mid-2020 Census. But because of the pandemic, Toronto has a remarkably high five per cent apartment vacancy rate and fewer people here as people fled the city during the pandemic, immigration was low, and few students were here for school.
But the bigger problem is that Toronto and Ontario MPs will not fight for representation by population, so Saskatchewan and Quebec do not lose MPs when their proportion of the population shrinks, while of course the Atlantic provinces are grossly overrepresented, so they have the same number of MPs as Senators.
Ironically, one of the reasons for Confederation was that Ontario (Canada West) was under-represented in the previous union with Quebec. In future, Ontario should be pushing for its share of MPs with as much force as other provinces push against it, and maybe over time we can slowly return closer to representation by population.
Brian Graff, Toronto
Who is going to police the student cellphone ban?
Ontario reveals details of cellphone ban in schools. Here are the new rules for students, April 28
The Doug Ford government's latest plan to ban cellphone use in classrooms has theoretical merit. But on a practical level, there are serious flaws. What's to prevent students from excusing themselves from class to use the washroom, and their cellphones, for extended periods of time, and perhaps during every class? What impact will there be on punctuality to class if students are told they can use their cellphones in the hallways between classes? What's to prevent a student from surreptitiously using a cellphone under cover of a desk or textbook? What happens if a confrontation occurs when a teacher tries to take away a student's cellphone?
Policing of the cellphone ban will fall heavily on teachers who already have a full plate of responsibility in delivering an educational experience to students of a variety of abilities, interests and attention spans.Perhaps the onus should then fall on parents to restrict the use of cellphones by their children while they attend school. The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario has said it would reserve judgment" on this plan until "it had the opportunity to review the full range of changes," a position that the Ford government often neglects to take when making sweeping changes in vital social structures. A majority of Ontario students currently have generous cellphone access. The horse is already out of the barn. Why not involve those most affected by the proposed ban to help decide how a problem seen by many can be sensibly resolved?
Pam Okihiro, Oakville
We applaud heroic efforts to rescue an orca calf while we continue to abuse primates
Consciousness a 'realistic possibility' in birds, fish, squid and bees, April 22
A company stopped importing monkeys to the U.S. for lab tests over smuggling concerns. Now they're flooding into Canada, April
A couple of weeks ago, The Star ran an article about monkeys being shipped to Canadian laboratories. This was followed by a separate article that finally acknowledges that animals have "consciousness" and can feel. I have sat among macaques and watched them in their private lives, impressed by what they must learn to manage in their environment. It is a far cry from the miserable animal in a cage, biting the bars till their mouths bleed to get out. The simple reality is that monkeys are sentient, cognitive beings. They feel what we feel and being dragged from their group, roughly handled and tested for something about which they have no idea nor understanding is beyond cruel. It is unnecessary. The heroic efforts made to rescue the orca calf in British Columbia contrast strongly with a country that promotes the abuse of primates. Canada should be a leader in stopping the trade in animals, not promoting it.
Frances Burton, Cobourg

CAPTION: A customer walks through the produce section at a grocery store In Toronto on Friday, Feb. 2, 2024.
